title: SWORD: Spectral Wasserstein Online Regime Detection in Dynamic Networks
summary: Online change point detection in dynamic graphs requires comparing graphs as they arrive, in time linear in the number of edges, without parametric assumptions. Recent spectral methods address scale via the Kernel Polynomial Method (KPM): SCPD computes Chebyshev moments of the normalized Laplacian, discretizes them into a density-of-states histogram, and scores the histogram with SVD plus cosine similarity. We introduce SWORD, which computes the same moments and instead compares their mean across two adjacent time windows by their $L_1$ distance. On three real-world benchmarks (MIT Reality, AskUbuntu, Enron), this lifts mean $F_1$ from SCPD's $0.27$ to $0.79$, with SCPD failing to detect any change on Enron. A controlled cascading ablation attributes the gap to two design choices: the two-window mean structure (dominant on MIT) and the $L_1$ metric on those mean vectors (dominant on Enron). A bin-width sweep rules out histogram discretization -- SCPD's most visible architectural choice -- as the driver. SWORD inherits SCPD's KPM core, so per-graph cost is $O(KRm)$ with no eigendecomposition, scaling to $86{,}000$-node networks. With per-dataset tuning it matches the offline TIRE autoencoder on mean $F_1$ and attains the highest precision among online methods ($0.91$, only $2$ false positives across the three benchmarks).

AI.tldr: A simple two-window mean comparison of Chebyshev moments, via Kernel Polynomial Method (KPM), yields superior online change point detection in dynamic graphs (SWORD) compared to SCPD, with strong scalability and precision.
AI.motivation: Online change point detection in evolving networks requires fast, parameter-free methods that scale with graph size; SCPD's histogram+SVD approach may miss changes and be sensitive to discretization or require complex processing.
AI.method: Compute Chebyshev moments of the normalized Laplacian with KPM for consecutive time windows, derive mean moment vectors per window, and detect changes by the L1 distance between these means; maintain a two-window structure, avoid eigendecomposition, and analyze three benchmarks (MIT Reality, AskUbuntu, Enron) with ablations to identify key design choices; perform a bin-width sweep to assess histogram discretization effects.
AI.result: SWORD raises mean F1 from SCPD's 0.27 to 0.79 across benchmarks; SCPD fails on Enron; ablations indicate the two-window mean structure and L1 distance drive performance; histogram discretization is not the primary driver; per-graph cost is O(KRm) with no eigendecomposition, enabling scalability to 86k-node networks; with tuning it matches offline TIRE on mean F1 and achieves high precision (0.91) with only 2 false positives across datasets.
AI.conclusion: A two-window mean-based L1 comparison of KPM moments is an effective, scalable online CPD method for dynamic graphs, outperforming prior histogram-based spectral methods and offering robust detection across diverse datasets.

title: Resolving Endpoint Underfitting in Diffusion Bridges via Noise Alignment
summary: Diffusion bridge models offer a powerful framework for connecting two data distributions, such as in image restoration and translation. Many existing methods learn this bridge by mimicking the score-matching formulation of standard diffusion models. In this work, we find that this way leads to an anomalous underfitting phenomenon near the target endpoint, as the process approaches the target distribution ($t \to 0$). This underfitting, characterized by significant drift in the predicted variance and direction, results from an excessively large discrepancy in noise levels between the network's input and its regression target.To resolve this issue, we propose the Noise-Aligned Diffusion Bridge (NADB).Our approach reformulates the diffusion bridge by first employing a mean network to provide a cleaner conditional target, and then introducing a novel, noise-aligned mapping relationship. This new formulation resolves the noise mismatch and corrects the underfitting near the target endpoint. Experimental validation across multiple image restoration and image translation tasks demonstrates the effectiveness of our approach. Code is available at https://github.com/gyr02/NADB.

AI.tldr: Noise-Aligned Diffusion Bridge (NADB) mitigates underfitting near the target distribution by decoupling target conditioning via a mean network and introducing a noise-aligned mapping; shows improved results on image restoration and translation; code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.motivation: Diffusion bridge models trained via score-matching exhibit anomalous underfitting as t approaches 0 (target endpoint) due to a large noise-level mismatch between the network input and its regression target; this degrades drift direction and variance near the target distribution.
AI.method: Propose NADB: (1) use a mean network to provide a cleaner conditional target, (2) introduce a novel noise-aligned mapping that aligns the noise levels between input and regression target, thereby reformulating the diffusion bridge training.
AI.result: Experimental validation across multiple image restoration and image translation tasks demonstrates the effectiveness of NADB, with code released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: NADB resolves the noise mismatch responsible for underfitting near the target endpoint, improves the training stability and performance of diffusion-bridge models, and broadens applicability to restoration and translation tasks.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Swarms: A Bionic Swarm Approach to Real-World Soil Mapping
comment: 27 pages, 15 figures. Submitted to Advanced Intelligent Systems
summary: Swarm and field robotics face significant barriers to real-world validation due to the high cost and development time to deploy hardware. This paper introduces the ``Bionic Swarm,'' a novel system that lowers these barriers by abstracting away many of the tasks that are difficult to implement on robots but which do not contribute to the overall algorithm evaluation, giving these tasks to human users. These human users take directions from a smartphone web-app that takes measurements from Bluetooth-connected sensors and relays them to a centralized server. This server runs the swarm algorithm and directs actions to the human users. We evaluate this system through the experimental validation of a geotechnically-focused search algorithm named Score-Biased-Search, which functions by assigning a ``score'' to each location on a reconstructed map, then biases search patterns through areas of higher expected scores, and which exhibits superlinear map reconstruction relative to the number of search agents. After presenting simulation results for the algorithm, we then apply the algorithm on the Bionic Swarm platform to validate its function in a real-world, outdoor setting. This work demonstrates that this human-in-the-loop approach significantly lowers the barrier to entry for field and swarm robotics research.

AI.tldr: Bionic Swarm enables human-in-the-loop validation of swarm algorithms by delegating non-essential tasks to humans via a smartphone app, reducing hardware costs and development time; demonstrated with Score-Biased-Search showing superlinear map reconstruction and real-world outdoor tests.
AI.motivation: Reduce the high costs and long development cycles of hardware-based field validation in swarm/field robotics by offloading mundane tasks to humans.
AI.method: Developed the Bionic Swarm platform where human users with Bluetooth sensors and a smartphone web-app are guided by a central server running the swarm algorithm; evaluated via a geotechnically-focused Score-Biased-Search algorithm through simulations and outdoor experiments.
AI.result: Score-Biased-Search achieves superlinear map reconstruction as a function of the number of search agents; platform validation in real-world outdoor settings.
AI.conclusion: The human-in-the-loop approach significantly lowers barriers to entry for field and swarm robotics research and enables faster algorithm validation.

title: One Mask to Rule Them All: On Hidden Facts after Editing and How to Find Them
summary: Knowledge editing methods such as ROME and MEMIT update factual associations in transformer models by modifying MLP weights. While evaluated mainly by output behavior, their internal mechanism remains underexplored. We investigate whether edits rely on a common mechanism, regardless of which fact is modified. Despite fact-specific weight changes, we argue that ROME and MEMIT target the same subset of weights critical for maintaining edits. To isolate this subset, we train a compact binary mask over the edited weights. The mask reverses 80% of edits on the training set and over 70% on the test set, confirming that diverse edits share a common functional structure. Our analysis reveals that the mask reverses edits by eliminating overattention in later layers. Additionally, we show that injecting the mask during editing drops editing success from 98% to 38%, demonstrating that this mechanism is necessary for edits to succeed. Our finding that edits suppress rather than overwrite knowledge explains why ROME and MEMIT fail to propagate changes to related facts. The identified common functional subspace informs detection and defense against unwanted edits.

AI.tldr: Knowledge edits in LLMs (ROME/MEMIT) share a common functional subspace; a compact binary mask over edited weights can reverse edits, implying a common mechanism (elimination of overattention in later layers) and explaining why edits do not propagate to related facts.
AI.motivation: Understand whether diverse factual edits in transformer models rely on a shared mechanism beyond fact-specific weight changes and elucidate the internal dynamics of editing methods.
AI.method: Train a compact binary mask over edited weights to identify the subset responsible for edits; evaluate its ability to reverse edits on training and test sets; analyze how the mask affects editing (e.g., by removing overattention in later layers); test the necessity of the mechanism by injecting the mask during editing and measuring changes in success; interpret why edits fail to propagate to related facts.
AI.result: The mask reverses 80% of edits on the training set and 70% on the test set, indicating a shared functional structure; edits appear to operate by suppressing overattention rather than overwriting knowledge; injecting the mask during editing reduces success from 98% to 38%, demonstrating the mechanism’s necessity; these findings explain why edits do not propagate to related facts and point to a common subspace for detection and defense.
AI.conclusion: There exists a common functional subspace governing knowledge edits across facts; this mechanism (elimination of overattention) is essential for edits and explains suppression rather than overwriting; this insight can inform detection/defense against unwanted edits and guide future editing methods.

title: Behavior-Induced Mirror-Prox Temporal-Difference Learning for Faster Off-Policy Prediction
summary: Gradient temporal-difference methods provide stable off-policy prediction with linear function approximation, but their practical performance is strongly affected by the geometry induced by the auxiliary-variable metric. Existing Mirror-Prox TD methods typically use the feature covariance metric, whereas hybrid TD methods suggest that behavior-policy transition information can provide a more informative update geometry. This paper proposes a behavior-induced Mirror-Prox temporal-difference method, called STHTD-MP, which replaces the covariance metric in the primal-dual saddle-point formulation with the symmetric part of the behavior-policy Bellman matrix. The method keeps a single learning rate for the primal and auxiliary variables and applies a Mirror-Prox prediction-correction step to the resulting hybrid saddle-point operator. We provide a formal convergence analysis for fixed-policy linear prediction under standard stochastic approximation assumptions: the behavior-induced metric is positive definite, the joint mean system is Hurwitz, boundedness follows from a Lyapunov argument, and the stochastic recursion converges by the ODE method. We further derive projected-oracle ergodic gap bounds and an exact mean-operator comparison with GTD2-MP based on the spectral radius of the deterministic Mirror-Prox error matrix. The analysis shows that STHTD-MP can have a smaller mean contraction factor than GTD2-MP when the behavior-induced metric improves the saddle-point geometry. Exact numerical mean-operator analysis on two-state, Random Walk, and Boyan Chain benchmarks supports this condition, while Baird's counterexample is identified as a singular boundary case where the strict assumptions fail.

AI.tldr: Introduces STHTD-MP, a behavior-induced Mirror-Prox TD method that replaces the covariance metric with the symmetric part of the behavior-policy Bellman matrix; provides convergence analysis, ergodic gap bounds, and a mean-operator comparison with GTD2-MP; shows potential contraction improvement in favorable geometry, with Baird’s counterexample as a boundary case.
AI.motivation: Address sensitivity of gradient TD methods to the geometry induced by auxiliary-variable metrics; leverage behavior-policy transition information to obtain a more informative saddle-point geometry for stable off-policy prediction with linear function approximation.
AI.method: Replace the primal-dual metric (covariance) with the symmetric part of the behavior-policy Bellman matrix in the saddle-point formulation; use a single learning rate for primal and auxiliary variables; apply a Mirror-Prox prediction-correction step to the resulting hybrid saddle-point operator; analyze convergence under fixed-policy linear prediction with standard stochastic approximation assumptions; derive projected-oracle ergodic gap bounds and an exact mean-operator comparison against GTD2-MP based on the spectral radius of the deterministic error matrix.
AI.result: Formal convergence guarantees: metric positive definite, joint mean system Hurwitz, Lyapunov-based boundedness, convergence via ODE method; ergodic gap bounds; exact mean-operator comparison showing potential contraction improvement over GTD2-MP when the behavior-induced metric improves geometry; empirical support on two-state, Random Walk, and Boyan Chain benchmarks; Baird’s counterexample identified as a singular boundary case where assumptions fail.
AI.conclusion: STHTD-MP canonically integrates behavior-policy information into the Mirror-Prox TD framework, yielding possible improvements in contraction and stability for off-policy linear TD; theoretical guarantees and exact mean-operator comparisons underpin its advantages, while edge cases like Baird’s example highlight sensitivity to assumptions and geometry.

title: GAP3D: Generative Alignment of VLM Latents to Patch-Level Embeddings for 3D Generation
summary: Recent approaches integrating vision-language models (VLMs) as prompt encoders for generative model conditioning typically rely on expensive end-to-end training or map features to compressed representations, discarding the dense spatial structure required for geometry-aware tasks like 3D asset generation. To address this, we propose GAP3D, a modular, diffusion-based approach that aligns VLM-generated latents directly to the complete, patch-level feature space of a pre-trained image encoder, enabling a frozen downstream generative model to utilize a VLM as prompt encoder while maintaining a spatially structured conditioning signal. Evaluated on 3D asset generation, our method bypasses the need for large-scale 3D data by training mainly on general-domain image-text pairs. It also exhibits emergent zero-shot capabilities for multimodal prompts, despite being trained exclusively on text input. Finally, while currently prioritizing high-level semantics over fine-grained detail, GAP3D demonstrates that the representation gap between VLM and image-encoder feature spaces can be partially bridged through diffusion-based alignment, taking the first steps towards a modular integration of foundation models through generative alignment to dense embedding spaces.

AI.tldr: GAP3D is a diffusion-based method that aligns VLM latents to the patch-level features of a pre-trained image encoder, enabling a frozen generative model to use a VLM as a prompt encoder with spatially structured conditioning for 3D asset generation, even with limited 3D data and with emergent zero-shot multimodal prompts.
AI.motivation: Preserve dense spatial structure essential for geometry-aware tasks (e.g., 3D asset generation) while leveraging vision-language models as prompts; avoid end-to-end expensive training and information loss from compressed representations; enable modular integration of foundation models through dense embedding alignment.
AI.method: A modular diffusion-based alignment (GAP3D) that maps VLM-generated latents directly to the full, patch-level feature space of a pre-trained image encoder, allowing a frozen downstream generator to condition on VLM prompts with spatial structure; trained primarily on general image-text pairs; yields zero-shot multimodal prompting despite text-only training; emphasizes semantic guidance over fine-grained detail while bridging VLM and image-encoder spaces.
AI.result: On 3D asset generation, GAP3D reduces dependence on large-scale 3D data by leveraging general-domain image-text data; exhibits emergent zero-shot multimodal prompting; demonstrates partial bridging of the representation gap between VLM and image-encoder spaces via diffusion-based alignment; marks initial steps toward modular integration of foundation models through dense embedding alignment.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based alignment can reconcile VLM and image-encoder feature spaces to provide spatially structured conditioning for a frozen generator, enabling VLM-as-prompt encoding in 3D tasks with reduced data requirements; the approach prioritizes high-level semantics currently, with further work needed to enhance fine-grained detail and extend modular integration to broader tasks.

title: Multi-Resolution End-to-End Deep Neural Network for Optimizing Latency-Accuracy Tradeoff in Autonomous Driving
summary:
Latency-accuracy tradeoffs are fundamental in real-time applications of deep neural networks (DNNs) for cyber-physical systems. In autonomous driving, in particular, safety depends on both prediction quality and the end-to-end delay from sensing to actuation. We observe that (1) when latency is accounted for, the latency-optimal network configuration varies with scene context and compute availability; and (2) a single fixed-resolution model becomes suboptimal as conditions change.
  We present a multi-resolution, end-to-end deep neural network for the CARLA urban driving challenge using monocular camera input. Our approach employs a convolutional neural network (CNN) that supports multiple input resolutions through per-resolution batch normalization, enabling runtime selection of an ideal input scale under a latency budget, as well as resolution retargeting, which allows multi-resolution training without access to the original training dataset.
  We implement and evaluate our multi-resolution end-to-end CNN in CARLA to explore the latency-safety frontier. Results show consistent improvements in per-route safety metrics - lane invasions, red-light infractions, and collisions - relative to fixed-resolution baselines.

AI.tldr: A multi-resolution end-to-end CNN for autonomous driving in CARLA uses per-resolution batch normalization to adapt input scale at runtime under a latency budget, plus resolution retargeting to enable multi-resolution training without the original dataset, achieving improvements over fixed-resolution baselines in lane invasion, red-light, and collision metrics.
AI.motivation: To address latency-accuracy tradeoffs in real-time DNNs for cyber-physical systems, where safety depends on end-to-end delay and prediction quality; fixed-resolution models are suboptimal as scene context and available compute vary.
AI.method: A CNN that supports multiple input resolutions via per-resolution batch normalization, enabling runtime selection of the ideal input scale to meet latency budgets and resolution retargeting to allow multi-resolution training without access to the original training data; evaluated end-to-end on monocular camera input in the CARLA urban driving simulator.
AI.result: The approach yields consistent improvements in per-route safety metrics (lane invasions, red-light infractions, collisions) relative to fixed-resolution baselines.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive multi-resolution end-to-end models can push the latency-safety frontier; runtime resolution selection and resolution retargeting enable safer, more efficient autonomous driving under varying scene conditions and compute availability, without requiring original training data.

title: Representation Signatures and Risk-Feedback Alignment in LLM Trading Agents
summary: We study behavioral alignment and representation dynamics of large language model (LLM) agents in financial decision environments. Using TradeArena, an auditable trading-agent testbed with risk reports, execution simulation, memory, and replayable trajectories, we analyze how rationales, positions, and interventions evolve under market stress. We find measurable pre-failure signatures: planning embeddings drift from normal-state centroids, fused plan-risk representations separate normal from pre-drawdown states, and manifold diagnostics show effective-rank contraction before failures. To address small-sample and embedding-choice concerns, we use 80 rolling failure anchors across eight LLM trajectories and show that contraction persists across hash, LSA, Transformer, and white-box hidden-state probes. Stress tests with CoT-free target weights, lexical controls, OHLCV noise, and false-audit reports indicate that rationale-level contraction can vanish without rationales, while intent-space contraction may remain; lexical diversity does not collapse; and fused signatures remain informative under noise. We also find that structured risk feedback can act as an external alignment signal without fine-tuning, but not as a universal performance enhancer: true audit feedback improves calibration for some models, return and drawdown for others, and reveals cases where hidden or placebo feedback has higher short-horizon return but weaker alignment diagnostics. Finally, a 51-stock intraday experiment reveals a correlation blind spot: LLM rationales often justify concentrated exposure to coupled assets that the risk layer repeatedly clips, with a rolling Markowitz baseline as a covariance reference. These results support a research claim rather than a profitability claim: auditable risk feedback and representation trajectories reveal when LLM financial reasoning is aligning, drifting, or failing.

AI.tldr: Auditable risk feedback and representation dynamics reveal when LLM-based trading reasoning aligns, drifts, or fails under stress.
AI.motivation: Investigate how LLM agents in financial environments align their reasoning with risk signals, and identify pre-failure signatures and robustness of alignment diagnostics across perturbations and data noise.
AI.method: Analyze TradeArena-based LLM trading agents using risk reports, memory, and replayable trajectories; examine planning embeddings, plan-risk fusion representations, and manifold diagnostics; use 80 rolling failure anchors across eight trajectories; test across hash, LSA, Transformer, and hidden-state probes; perform stress tests (CoT-free weights, lexical controls, OHLCV noise, false-audit reports); conduct a 51-stock intraday experiment.
AI.result: Detected pre-failure signatures such as drift of planning embeddings away from normal-state centroids; separation of fused plan-risk representations between normal and pre-drawdown states; effective-rank contraction before failures; contraction persists across probes; some stress tests remove rationale-level contraction but not intent-space; lexical diversity remains; external risk feedback can help calibration for some models; not universal performance enhancer; correlation blind spot where rationales justify exposure to clustered assets that risk layer clips.
AI.conclusion: Auditable risk feedback and representation dynamics are informative diagnostics of when LLM financial reasoning is aligning, drifting, or failing; they do not guarantee profitability but reveal alignment states and can guide risk-aware interventions.

title: Behavior-Aware Auxiliary Corrections for Off-Policy Temporal-Difference Prediction
summary: Temporal-difference learning with function approximation can be unstable under off-policy sampling. TDC stabilizes off-policy TD through an auxiliary covariance correction, and TDRC further regularizes this correction in a single-timescale recursion. This paper studies a behavior-aware replacement of the auxiliary covariance geometry in the linear prediction setting, which is the standard local model for understanding the feature-space dynamics of value-function approximation. We first replace the TDC auxiliary matrix (C) by the behavior Bellman matrix (A_μ), yielding BA-TDC, and then regularize the same behavior-aware equation to obtain BA-TDRC. This two-step construction separates the contribution of behavior-aware geometry from the contribution of regularization. The linear analysis also provides a tractable model for an auxiliary-geometry design question that arises in neural-network value approximation, where feature covariances and temporal transition matrices jointly shape the last-layer correction dynamics. We give a finite-state mean-system formulation, prove fixed-point preservation and almost-sure convergence under a Hurwitz stability condition on the instantiated mean system, and compare deterministic mean rates through the spectral radius of the exact linear error recursion. Experiments on the two-state counterexample, Baird's counterexample, Random Walk, and Boyan Chain show that the behavior-aware replacement can be highly beneficial by itself on some tasks, but that regularization is necessary for robust performance across harder settings.

AI.tldr: The work introduces BA-TDC and BA-TDRC by replacing the TDC auxiliary covariance with the behavior Bellman matrix, analyzes their linear dynamics via a finite-state mean-system, and shows that behavior-aware geometry can help but regularization is required for robust off-policy stability; provides convergence guarantees and empirical evidence on standard RL benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Off-policy TD with function approximation is prone to instability; existing TDC/TDRC corrections rely on covariance geometry that interacts with features. Understanding a behavior-aware geometry in the linear setting clarifies how feature covariances and transition dynamics shape last-layer corrections and informs neural-network designs.
AI.method: Replace TDC's auxiliary matrix C with the behavior Bellman matrix A_μ to obtain BA-TDC, then regularize this equation to obtain BA-TDRC. Analyze in a finite-state mean-system, prove fixed-point preservation and almost-sure convergence under a Hurwitz stability condition, and compare deterministic mean rates via the spectral radius of the exact linear error recursion. Empirical evaluation on two-state counterexample, Baird's counterexample, Random Walk, and Boyan Chain.
AI.result: BA-TDC can be beneficial on some tasks by leveraging behavior-aware geometry, but regularization is necessary for robust performance across harder settings. The linear analysis yields a tractable model for auxiliary-geometry design in neural networks and provides convergence guarantees under Hurwitz stability. Empirical results illustrate the potential gains and the need for regularization.
AI.conclusion: Behavior-aware geometry matters for off-policy value function learning with function approximation. The two-step BA-TDC/BA-TDRC construction separates geometry from regularization, offering a framework to design auxiliary corrections in linear and neural settings. Regularization remains essential to ensure stability and robustness across a range of tasks.

title: Auditing Training-Free 3D Shape Retrieval with Diffused Geodesic Moments
summary: Reported retrieval scores for training-free shape descriptors conflate local signal design, normalization, aggregation, codebook fitting, and metric choices, making isolated component evaluation difficult. This paper reframes descriptor evaluation as a {\em protocol audit}. We introduce Diffused Geodesic Moments (DGM), a seed-conditioned descriptor that computes sparse implicit heat responses, converts them to distance-like fields, and summarizes each vertex by low-order moments across seeds and scales. DGM is used both as a practical non-spectral baseline and as an instrument for isolating protocol effects. On the registered FAUST benchmark split (FAUST-Reg) and the TOSCA shape collection, aggregation-matched experiments show that an independent Geometric Moment Shape Descriptor baseline built on Heat Kernel Signature features (GMSD-HKS) obtains the highest scores in this implementation ($0.621/0.820$ and $0.865/0.963$ mean average precision (mAP)/top-1), Wave Kernel Signature (WKS) remains a strong classical signal, and DGM is useful mainly when sparse solves, non-spectral deployment, or symmetry-informative seed frames are priorities. The broader finding is methodological: the input field and aggregation protocol can dominate the moment formula. The paper contributes a reproducible protocol-cascade analysis, a cross-shape alignment diagnostic for functional-map compatibility, and concrete recommendations for designing and reporting training-free shape descriptors.

AI.tldr: A protocol-audit approach to evaluate training-free shape descriptors; introduces Diffused Geodesic Moments (DGM) and cross-shape protocol cascade; finds GMSD-HKS as strongest baseline on FAUST-Reg and TOSCA; DGM helpful mainly for sparse/non-spectral use or symmetry-aware seeds; highlights that input fields and aggregation protocols can dominate moment formulas; provides reproducible protocol analysis and practical reporting recommendations.
AI.motivation: To disentangle confounded components in reported retrieval scores for training-free shape descriptors by standardizing evaluation protocols and enabling isolation of component effects.
AI.method: Propose DGM: a seed-conditioned descriptor that yields sparse implicit heat responses, converts them to distance-like fields, and summarizes vertices via low-order moments across seeds and scales. Use DGM both as a practical non-spectral baseline and as an instrument to audit protocol effects. Compare against GMSD-HKS (Geometric Moment Shape Descriptor using Heat Kernel Signatures) and Wave Kernel Signature (WKS) on FAUST-Reg and TOSCA. Conduct aggregation-matched experiments, cross-shape alignment diagnostics for functional-map compatibility, and derive reporting/design recommendations.
AI.result: On FAUST-Reg and TOSCA, GMSD-HKS achieves the highest scores in this implementation (mAP/top-1: 0.621/0.820 on FAUST-Reg and 0.865/0.963 on TOSCA). WKS remains a strong classical signal. DGM provides benefits mainly when sparse solves, non-spectral deployment, or symmetry-informed seed frames are prioritized. The broader finding is that the input field and the aggregation protocol can dominate the moment formula. The paper delivers a reproducible protocol-cascade analysis, a cross-shape alignment diagnostic for functional-map compatibility, and concrete recommendations for designing/reporting training-free shape descriptors.
AI.conclusion: Advocate for protocol-aware evaluation in training-free shape descriptors, emphasizing protocol-cascade analyses, and providing practical guidelines and diagnostics to separate descriptor design from evaluation protocol. Recommend reporting standards and diagnostic tools to improve comparability across works.

title: Learning and Adaptation in Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing Bead Geometry Control
summary: Robotics Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM) is governed by complex and nonlinear process dynamics coupling thermal field to the build geometry. The process may be regarded as a multi-input/multi-output dynamical system with welding torch speed and wire feed rate as inputs and weld bead deposition height and width as outputs. In this paper, we use the input/output data to learn a data-driven model and use it for weld planning and control. We show that a simple recurrent neural network architecture and one-step-ahead predictive control can improve the process performance in terms of height and width consistency. To account for the changing thermal conditions during the printing process, we update the learning model using prediction error from the previous layer. This adaptation step further improves the prediction accuracy and controller performance. Experiments on a robotic WAAM testbed with integrated line-scanner feedback significant improvements in height and width consistency compared to constant input and static model baselines. The proposed learning and adaptation framework provides a practical pathway toward robust, data-driven regulation of additive manufacturing processes.

AI.tldr: Data-driven RNN model with online adaptation for WAAM to control deposition height/width via torch speed and wire feed, improving consistency with one-step-ahead control and line-scanner feedback.
AI.motivation: WAAM exhibits nonlinear, thermal-geometry coupling that makes regulation challenging; a data-driven, adaptive approach using process data and sensor feedback can improve dimensional control (height/width) of weld beads.
AI.method: Model a multi-input/multi-output WAAM process with a simple recurrent neural network using torch speed and wire feed rate as inputs and height/width as outputs. Implement one-step-ahead predictive control. Update the model online using the prediction error from the previous layer to adapt to changing thermal conditions. Leverage integrated line-scanner feedback during experiments on a robotic WAAM testbed.
AI.result: The approach yields significant improvements in height and width consistency compared with constant input and static-model baselines, demonstrated on a robotic WAAM testbed with line-scanner feedback.
AI.conclusion: A practical learning-and-adaptation framework enables robust, data-driven regulation of additive manufacturing processes, improving dimensional consistency of WAAM deposits.

title: Mechanistic origins of catastrophic forgetting: why RL preserves circuits better than SFT?
summary: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) frequently induces catastrophic forgetting of prior capabilities. Recent work has shown that reinforcement learning (RL) retains prior capabilities more effectively than supervised fine-tuning (SFT), attributing this to policy-gradient updates remaining closer to the base policy \cite{shenfeld2025rl}. We extend this behavioral account to the mechanistic level and ask whether RL's advantage is mirrored by stronger preservation of internal computational circuits. We introduce differential circuit vulnerability, a head-level measure of how much a circuit degrades under fine-tuning, and use it to compare RL and SFT on Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct adapted to scientific question-answering. We find a clear mechanistic trade-off: SFT adapts more rapidly to the target task but produces substantially greater circuit disruption and forgetting of prior capabilities, whereas RL preserves a larger fraction of the base circuit at the cost of slower task adaptation. These findings suggest that circuit preservation may help explain why RL is more robust to catastrophic forgetting. We released our code here: https://github.com/rl-sft-circuit-research/differential-circuit-vulnerability.

AI.tldr: RL-fine-tuning preserves internal circuit structure better than supervised fine-tuning, trading slower task adaptation for reduced forgetting; a head-level 'differential circuit vulnerability' metric reveals a mechanistic trade-off under fine-tuning on scientific QA tasks.
AI.motivation: To extend the behavioral finding that RL reduces forgetting to a mechanistic level by examining internal computational circuits in LLMs under RL vs SFT.
AI.method: Introduce differential circuit vulnerability, a head-level measure; apply to Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct adapted for scientific QA; compare RL vs SFT; analyze impact on base circuit and new task adaptation; provide code.
AI.result: SFT yields faster adaptation but greater disruption of base circuits and forgetting of prior capabilities; RL preserves more of the base circuit at the cost of slower adaptation; supports the idea that circuit preservation underlies robustness to catastrophic forgetting.
AI.conclusion: Circuit preservation offers a mechanistic explanation for RL’s robustness, and the differential circuit vulnerability metric provides a tool for evaluating fine-tuning methods; code released.

title: The Cognitive Categorical Transformer: Category-Theoretic Inductive Biases for Language Modeling
summary: The Cognitive Categorical Transformer (CCT) is a 306M-parameter architecture that augments a pretrained GPT-2 Small backbone with cognitively grounded components derived from category theory and several inspirations from cognitive science. Under a matched-step protocol (215,000 optimizer steps, matched data, matched optimizer and schedule) on WikiText-103, CCT reaches 21.27 validation perplexity, compared with 24.19 for an identically fine-tuned GPT-2 Small baseline. The architecture therefore contributes a 2.92 PPL (12% relative) reduction beyond what in-domain fine-tuning alone provides. A retrain-from-scratch ablation that holds GT-Full simplicial message passing bypassed across the entire seven-phase activation schedule reaches 23.72 PPL, localizing 84% of the architectural improvement (2.45 of 2.92 PPL) to GT-Full. We present the first ablation-validated evidence that simplicial message passing improves language-model perplexity at the 306M-parameter scale on WikiText-103. Published GPT-2 Large reaches 22.05 zero-shot PPL on WikiText-103 with 6.2x more parameters than GPT-2 Small; this paper treats that number as an external published reference, not as the architectural benchmark. Three negative results on consistency-style categorical priors (sheaf smoothing, adjunction round-trip, curvature regularization) and the joint structural-prior result for GT-Full and PrecisionWeightedPP together support an empirical pattern termed the *structure/consistency distinction*, in which categorical priors that add new topology improve language modeling and those that enforce a consistency identity do not.

AI.tldr: A 306M-CCT augments GPT-2 Small with category-theoretic and cognitive-inspired components, achieving a notable perplexity reduction on WikiText-103 via simplicial message passing; ablations attribute most gains to GT-Full; negative results for some consistency priors; supports a structure/consistency distinction.
AI.motivation: To improve language modeling by integrating cognitively grounded, topology-aware priors derived from category theory, testing whether such priors help at 306M parameters and how they compare to larger models and standard fine-tuning.
AI.method: Fine-tune a 306M GPT-2 Small backbone with cognitively grounded components, including simplicial message passing (GT-Full) across a seven-phase activation schedule; evaluate under matched-step protocol (215k steps) on WikiText-103; conduct retrain-from-scratch ablations; compare to GPT-2 Large zero-shot and report negative results for certain priors.
AI.result: CCT achieves 21.27 PPL vs 24.19 baseline, a 2.92 PPL (12%) improvement; GT-Full accounts for ~84% of improvement (2.45 PPL); ablation shows simplicial message passing improves PPL at 306M scale; GPT-2 Large zero-shot PPL is 22.05 with 6.2x more params; three negative results for consistency priors; joint structural-prior results with GT-Full and PrecisionWeightedPP; supports structure/consistency distinction.
AI.conclusion: Simplicial message passing and topology-aware priors can meaningfully improve large-scale language modeling at moderate parameter counts; not all categorical priors help—consistency-enforcing priors may hurt or be neutral; the data support a dichotomy between adding topology (structure) and enforcing consistency (consistency).

title: Trajectory Constraints for Imaging Inverse Problems
summary: Diffusion-based and iterative methods have become effective tools for solving imaging inverse problems. Their reconstruction process naturally forms a trajectory of intermediate estimates. Although these intermediate estimates define a reconstruction trajectory, most methods do not explicitly regularize the transitions between consecutive states. To address this limitation, we introduce TRACE, a training-free TRAjectory-Constrained rEconstruction framework that stabilizes the reconstruction path by coupling adjacent states along the trajectory. This gives a trajectory-level model that can be interpreted as a sequence of proximal updates. Since the exact proximal update is generally intractable, we approximate it with a neural mapping. This yields a diffusion-like reconstruction process with an explicit coupling between neighboring states. We provide a stability analysis showing that temporal coupling bounds trajectory variation and that this control is preserved under untrained network updates. Experiments on linear and nonlinear image reconstruction tasks show that TRACE improves reconstruction quality. Trajectory-level analyses and ablations confirm that temporal coupling directly affects state transitions along the reconstruction path.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a training-free trajectory-constrained reconstruction framework for diffusion-based and iterative imaging inverse problems. It stabilizes the reconstruction path by explicitly coupling adjacent trajectory states via a neural mapping that approximates proximal updates, resulting in a diffusion-like process with bound trajectory variation. Stability holds under untrained network updates. Empirical results on linear and nonlinear problems show improved reconstruction quality, with ablations confirming temporal coupling directly influences state transitions along the path.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based/iterative reconstructions form trajectories but typically do not regularize transitions between consecutive states, leading to potential instability or incoherent trajectories. There is a need for a training-free mechanism to enforce stable, smooth trajectory evolution without learning a proximal operator.
AI.method: Introduce TRACE, which treats reconstruction as a trajectory and imposes temporal coupling between neighboring states. The exact proximal update is intractable, so a neural mapping approximates it, yielding a diffusion-like process with explicit state coupling. Provides a stability analysis showing that coupling bounds trajectory variation and that this control persists under updates of untrained networks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on linear and nonlinear imaging tasks show TRACE improves reconstruction quality. Trajectory-level analyses and ablations demonstrate that temporal coupling directly affects state transitions along the reconstruction path.
AI.conclusion: TRACE delivers a training-free, trajectory-level regularization for diffusion-based and iterative reconstructions, offering stability guarantees and improved reconstruction quality by enforcing explicit coupling between consecutive states.

title: CA-AC-MPC: CUDA-Accelerated Actor-Critic Model Predictive Control
comment: Accepted for presentation at the 2026 International Conference on Unmanned Aircraft Systems, ICUAS 2026
summary: In the literature, actor-critic model predictive control (AC-MPC) integrates MPC with reinforcement learning to enable high-performance control of complex dynamical systems. However, its differentiable MPC layer requires repeatedly solving an optimization problem in both the forward and backward passes, leading to substantial training and inference latency. This paper tackles this bottleneck introducing a CUDA-accelerated variant that significantly reduces end-to-end execution time while preserving the control performance of the baseline formulation. Simulation results on an agile drone racing task show that our approach achieves state-of-the-art lap times and near-limit dynamic behaviour with markedly reduced training and inference time.

AI.tldr: CUDA-accelerated AC-MPC drastically lowers latency without sacrificing control quality, achieving state-of-the-art drone racing lap times in simulation.
AI.motivation: Overcome the high computational burden of differentiable MPC layers in AC-MPC that slows training and inference due to repeated forward/backward optimization.
AI.method: Develop a CUDA-accelerated variant of the differentiable MPC layer within AC-MPC and evaluate on a drone racing simulation, comparing end-to-end latency and control performance to the baseline.
AI.result: End-to-end execution time is significantly reduced while preserving baseline control performance; simulation results show state-of-the-art lap times and near-limit dynamic behavior with substantially lower training and inference time.
AI.conclusion: GPU-accelerated differentiable MPC within AC-MPC is effective for real-time high-performance control, offering substantial speedups without compromising control quality.

title: Molecular Lead Optimization via Agentic Tool Planning
summary: Drug discovery is a lengthy and resource-intensive process composed of multiple stages. Among these stages, lead optimization plays a critical role in transforming early hit compounds into viable drug candidates. This stage requires improving ADMET-related properties through subtle structural refinement while preserving key molecular substructures responsible for binding affinity to disease targets. Recent advances in artificial intelligence have shown promise in accelerating various aspects of drug discovery; however, most existing approaches to lead optimization rely on one-step molecular optimization, which fail to account for the long-term consequences of sequential design decisions. To address this limitation, we propose TRACE, a trajectory-aware, LLM-reasoning agent for molecular lead optimization that formulates tool selection as a sequential decision-making problem over action trajectories. Given a lead molecule and an optimization objective, TRACE makes trajectory-aware decisions over molecular optimization tools, enabling forward-looking refinement under structural constraints. Experiments on multiple ADMET optimization tasks show that our agent achieves higher optimization success, larger property improvements, and higher validity, while preserving molecular similarity compared to baseline models.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a trajectory-aware LLM-driven agent for multi-step molecular lead optimization that plans tool-use over action trajectories, addressing the shortcomings of one-step optimization and improving ADMET property gains, validity, and similarity preservation compared with baselines.
AI.motivation: Conventional lead optimization often uses single-step molecular edits, neglecting long-term consequences of sequential design choices; there is a need to account for trajectory planning to balance ADMET improvements with binding stability and similarity constraints.
AI.method: Proposes TRACE, an LLM-based reasoning agent that formulates tool selection as a sequential decision-making problem over action trajectories. Given a lead and optimization objective, TRACE selects optimization tools in a trajectory-aware manner to enforce forward-looking refinement under structural constraints.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple ADMET optimization tasks shows TRACE achieves higher optimization success, larger property improvements, higher chemical validity, and better preservation of molecular similarity than baseline one-step models.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-aware, LLM-guided decision making can effectively optimize lead compounds across multiple steps, outperforming traditional one-step approaches and offering a path toward more predictive, constraint-aware drug design.

title: Ultra-Reduced-Impact-Encased-Logging (URIEL): propose a new method for selective sustainable logging and post-harvest silvicultural treatment in tropical forest using airborne robotics systems
comment: 196 pages, 40 figures, A revolutionary technology to help protect tropical forests. It was developed, scaled, detailed, calculated, and simulated in an advanced computational environment, com viabilidade econômica e social. "E pur si muove"
summary: Tropical forests worldwide are under intense deforestation pressure driven by economic and political interests, and scientific evidence suggests this deforestation contributes to climate change. This paper proposes a novel logging method for tropical forests, Ultra-Reduced-Impact-Encased-Logging (URIEL). This new method is based on heli-logging techniques combined with intensive use of robotics and AI integrated with post-harvest silvicultural treatments performed by drones. The concept of appropriate equipment for this method was developed, dimensions were determined, details were completed in a digital proof of concept, and an effective digital simulation and economic feasibility analysis were carried out for various helicopter-timber-distance combinations. The results demonstrated that a URIEL method has high economic viability and makes it possible to virtually eliminate collateral damage to forests while maintaining ecosystem services. The main conclusion of this paper is that, despite the satisfactory scientific and technological results, the feasibility of a Uriel method depends on the integration of stakeholders intrinsic to the context: high-tech industry; political governments; certified logging companies; and native populations.

AI.tldr: Ultra-Reduced-Impact-Encased-Logging (URIEL) is proposed: a high-tech, helicopter-based logging method using robotics, AI, and drone-based silviculture to minimize forest damage; digital proof of concept and economic feasibility show viability, but success depends on integrating stakeholders (industry, government, certified loggers, indigenous communities).
AI.motivation: Address tropical deforestation and its climate impacts by developing a logging method with minimal collateral damage that preserves ecosystem services, while leveraging advanced technologies and stakeholder collaboration.
AI.method: Concept development of URIEL, defining equipment dimensions, digital proof of concept, and comprehensive digital simulation and economic feasibility analyses across various helicopter-timber-distance configurations; integration of heli-logging with robotics, AI, and drone-enabled post-harvest silvicultural treatments.
AI.result: UR IEL demonstrates high economic viability and potential to virtually eliminate collateral damage while sustaining ecosystem services; however, real-world feasibility hinges on the alignment and cooperation of multiple stakeholders—high-tech industry, governments, certified logging firms, and native populations.
AI.conclusion: Technical and economic gains exist, but practical deployment requires broad stakeholder integration and contextual adaptation to local political and socio-economic conditions.

title: Embodied3DBench: Benchmarking Low-Level Embodied Spatial Intelligence of Vision Language Models
summary: Are current Vision Language Models (VLMs) ready to comprehend and reason about complex embodied interactions in 3D environments? We introduce Embodied3DBench, a robot-centric benchmark targeting low-level spatial intelligence in embodied 3D environments. To systematically evaluate these foundational perceptual capabilities, the benchmark includes 6 task categories divided into two core groups: Spatial Structural Understanding (Grounding, Spatial Relation Prediction, and Multi-view Correspondence) and Interaction-Oriented Perception (Affordance Prediction, Grasp Point Prediction, and Trajectory Prediction). The benchmark spans 12 subcategories and contains over 21k high-quality question-answer pairs. We evaluate 13 state-of-the-art models, and the results show that while current models exhibit relatively strong high-level spatial reasoning, such as understanding object-to-object positional relations, they remain fragile in interaction-oriented perception, highlighting a significant lack of robust 3D-aware interaction priors. To actively bridge this capability gap revealed by our benchmark, we further synthesize a large-scale training dataset comprising 1.3M QA pairs. Notably, fine-tuning on this dataset yields significant improvements in low-level spatial intelligence. Ultimately, Embodied3DBench fills a critical gap by providing both a systematic evaluation framework and a scalable data solution, setting a clear target for the development of interaction-aware multimodal systems.

AI.tldr: Embodied3DBench provides a robot-centric benchmark and a large-scale training dataset to evaluate and boost low-level, 3D-aware embodied perception in VLMs, revealing a gap in interaction-oriented capabilities that can be mitigated with fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Motivated by the mismatch between current VLMs' high-level spatial reasoning and the needs of embodied 3D interaction understanding; aims to establish a standardized evaluation for low-level spatial intelligence and to supply scalable data to bridge this gap.
AI.method: Benchmark design with 6 task categories (Spatial Structural Understanding and Interaction-Oriented Perception), spanning 12 subcategories and 21k+ QA pairs; evaluation of 13 state-of-the-art models; creation of a 1.3M QA synthetic training dataset for fine-tuning; analysis of model performance across tasks.
AI.result: Models show solid high-level spatial reasoning but fragile for interaction-oriented perception (affordances, grasp points, trajectories) due to lacking 3D-aware interaction priors; fine-tuning on the 1.3M QA dataset yields significant improvements in low-level spatial intelligence.
AI.conclusion: Embodied3DBench fills a critical gap by offering an evaluation framework and scalable data to drive the development of interaction-aware multimodal systems in embodied 3D environments.

title: Extreme dynamic symmetry enables omnidirectional and multifunctional robots
comment: Published in Science Robotics (2026). Our project website is at:https://generalroboticslab.com/Argus
summary: Symmetry is a central organizing principle in natural systems, yet its use as a unifying design strategy in robotics has largely remained limited to geometric form. We show that symmetry can instead be leveraged at the level of dynamic actuation capability. We introduce dynamic symmetry, the uniformity of a robot's attainable center-of-mass accelerations, and formalize it through a measure coined as dynamic isotropy. Across more than 1000 simulated morphologies, we found that higher dynamic symmetry consistently improved trajectory tracking, task success, robustness, resiliency, and energy efficiency, with the benefits becoming most pronounced as dynamic isotropy approached its theoretical limit. To study this regime systematically, we developed Argus, a family of spherical robots designed to explore the effects of increasing dynamic symmetry. Members of the Argus family vary in their actuation geometry and dynamic symmetry level while sharing a common architectural principle: radially oriented linear actuators that directly shape the robot's center-of-mass dynamics. Among them, we built a physical 20-leg Argus variant that achieved near-extreme dynamic isotropy and demonstrated orientation-invariant locomotion, agile traversal of cluttered and deformable terrain, rapid self-stabilization, and resilience to partial actuator failures. Its distributed sensing further enabled omnidirectional perception and object interaction during continuous motion. These results show that designing robots for symmetry not only in morphology but also in their attainable dynamics provides a powerful and general pathway toward agility, robustness, and multifunctionality in uncertain terrestrial and extraterrestrial environments.

AI.tldr: The abstract introduces dynamic symmetry and dynamic isotropy as a new design principle for robotics, showing that uniform attainable center-of-mass accelerations (dynamic isotropy) improves locomotion performance across many morphologies; it validates the concept with a physical Argus robot family and demonstrates robust, energy-efficient, and adaptive behavior in uncertain terrains, suggesting symmetry in dynamics as a general pathway for agility and resilience.
AI.motivation: Seeks to extend symmetry from static morphology to dynamic actuation, proposing dynamic isotropy as a measure of uniform COM acceleration capabilities to enhance robustness, agility, and multifunctionality in robotics, including extraterrestrial environments.
AI.method: Define dynamic symmetry via dynamic isotropy; perform large-scale simulations (>1000 morphologies) to correlate isotropy with performance metrics; design and build Argus family of robots with radially oriented linear actuators; implement a 20-leg Argus variant to achieve near-extreme isotropy; evaluate orientation-invariance, terrain traversal, self-stabilization, and actuator fault tolerance; leverage distributed sensing for omnidirectional perception.
AI.result: Higher dynamic symmetry consistently improves trajectory tracking, task success, robustness, resiliency, and energy efficiency; benefits peak near the theoretical limit of dynamic isotropy; the physical Argus 20-leg robot demonstrated orientation-invariant locomotion, agile terrain handling, rapid self-stabilization, and fault tolerance; distributed sensing enabled omnidirectional perception and interaction during motion.
AI.conclusion: Designing robots for symmetry should encompass not only morphology but also the symmetry of attainable dynamics, providing a general strategy for agility, robustness, and multifunctionality in uncertain terrestrial and extraterrestrial environments.

title: Self-Play Reinforcement Learning under Imperfect Information in Big 2
summary: Imperfect-information multiplayer games test whether agents can act under hidden information, sparse rewards, and non-stationary opponents. We study these challenges in Big 2, a four-player imperfect-information card game. We develop a self-play RL framework for Big 2 that enables controlled comparisons between policy-gradient and value-approximating agents. Under a common environment, input representation, training budget, and evaluation protocol, PPO outperforms Monte Carlo Q approximation, SARSA, and Q-learning against random, greedy, and heuristic Big 2 opponents. We further find that moderate entropy regularization improves PPO by preventing the policy from becoming overly deterministic, and that current-policy self-play provides a stronger finite-budget curriculum than checkpoint self-play or fixed-opponent training. Together, these results show that Big 2 is a useful controlled setting for studying deep RL under imperfect information, multiplayer interaction, delayed rewards, and variable action sets.

AI.tldr: PPO with entropy regularization, trained via current-policy self-play, outperforms value-based methods (Monte Carlo Q, SARSA, Q-learning) in Big 2, a four-player imperfect-information card game; a controlled self-play framework shows Big 2 is a valuable testbed for deep RL under imperfect information, multiplayer dynamics, delayed rewards, and varying action sets.
AI.motivation: Investigate how deep RL agents perform under hidden information, sparse rewards, and non-stationary opponents; compare policy-gradient and value-based methods; establish a controlled, standardized environment (Big 2) to study these challenges in a multiplayer imperfect-information setting.
AI.method: Develop a self-play RL framework for Big 2 with standardized environment, input representation, training budget, and evaluation protocol; implement and compare PPO (policy-gradient) against Monte Carlo Q approximation, SARSA, and Q-learning against diverse opponents (random, greedy, heuristic); explore entropy regularization; compare curricula: current-policy self-play, checkpoint self-play, and fixed-opponent training.
AI.result: PPO outperforms the listed value-based methods across the tested opponents. Moderate entropy regularization improves PPO by preventing over-deterministic policies. Current-policy self-play provides a stronger finite-budget curriculum than checkpoint self-play or fixed-opponent training.
AI.conclusion: Big 2 provides a controlled, realistic platform for studying deep RL under imperfect information, multiplayer interaction, delayed rewards, and variable action sets; the results support the effectiveness of policy-gradient methods with proper regularization and curriculum design in such settings.

title: Review Arcade: On the Human Alignment and Gameability of LLM Reviews
summary: LLM-generated reviews for scientific papers are gaining considerable traction and are even being officially piloted by major conferences. We have to assume that not only reviewers are using LLM-assistance, but also that authors use LLMs to revise their papers before submitting. In this work, we perform empirical experiments on papers from the 2025 ACL Rolling Review (ARR) to evaluate LLM reviews from both the author and the reviewer perspective. First, we identify a limited alignment of LLM reviews with human ones. In the best-case scenario, the alignment is reasonable. However, we also find that LLM-human alignment varies substantially across prompts and models. Finally, we investigate the scenario in which the author uses an iterative draft-revise workflow to improve the submission according to the LLM review. We find that this "gaming" of LLM reviews can be effective in specific scenarios, leading to a statistically significant increase of overall scores for up to 35\% of papers. We publish our code: https://github.com/uhh-hcds/reviewarcade.

AI.tldr: LLM-generated reviews show limited and inconsistent alignment with human reviews, and authors can sometimes improve scores via iterative, LLM-guided revisions; results depend on prompts/models; code released for replication.
AI.motivation: The surge of LLM-assisted reviewing by both reviewers and authors in top venues raises concerns about reliability, fairness, and susceptibility to gaming; empirical evaluation is needed.
AI.method: Empirical study on ACL Rolling Review 2025 papers. Compare LLM-generated reviews from author and reviewer perspectives, assess alignment across prompts and models, and simulate an iterative draft-revise workflow by authors based on LLM reviews. Measure impact on overall scores and report variability; release code at GitHub.
AI.result: LLM reviews align with human reviews only modestly; best-case alignment is reasonable but highly variable across prompts and models. Author-side gaming of revisions in response to LLM reviews can yield significant score increases for up to 35% of papers in certain scenarios.
AI.conclusion: LLM-assisted reviewing has potential but shows limited reliability and notable risk of gaming; results underscore need for safeguards, standardization, and further research; code for replication provided.

title: MonoDuo: Using One Robot Arm to Learn Bimanual Policies
comment: Accepted to appear in the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA), Vienna, Austria, 1-5 June 2026
summary: Bimanual coordination is essential for many real-world manipulation tasks, yet learning bimanual robot policies is limited by the scarcity of bimanual robots and datasets. Single-arm robots, however, are widely available in research labs. Can we leverage them to train bimanual robot policies? We present MonoDuo, a framework for learning bimanual manipulation policies using single-arm robot demonstrations paired with human collaboration. MonoDuo collects data by teleoperating a single-arm robot to perform one side of a bimanual task while a human performs the other, then swapping roles to cover both sides. RGB-D observations from a wrist-mounted and fixed camera are augmented into synthetic demonstrations for target bimanual robots using state-of-the-art hand pose estimation, image and point cloud segmentation, and inpainting. These synthetic demonstrations, grounded in real robot kinematics, are used to train bimanual policies. We evaluate MonoDuo on five tasks: box lifting, backpack packing, cloth folding, jacket zipping, and plate handover. Compared to approaches relying solely on human bimanual videos, MonoDuo enables zero-shot deployment on unseen bimanual robot configurations, achieving success rates up to 70%. With only 25 target robot demonstrations, few-shot finetuning further boosts success rates by 65-70% over training from scratch, demonstrating MonoDuo's effectiveness in efficiently transferring knowledge from single-arm robot data to bimanual robot policies.

AI.tldr: MonoDuo synthesizes bimanual manipulation demonstrations from single-arm teleoperation with human collaboration, generating synthetic data to train bimanual policies, enabling zero-shot transfer to unseen bimanual robots and efficient few-shot finetuning.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of bimanual robots/datasets motivates leveraging abundant single-arm robots. Bridge the gap between single-arm demonstrations and bimanual policy learning by synthesizing realistic training data grounded in real kinematics.
AI.method: Data collection: teleoperate one side of a bimanual task while a human performs the other, then swap roles to cover both sides. Capture RGB-D from a wrist-mounted and a fixed camera. Apply hand pose estimation, image/point-cloud segmentation, and inpainting to create synthetic demonstrations for target bimanual robots. Train policies on synthetic demonstrations anchored to real kinematics; evaluate zero-shot on unseen robots and fine-tune with ~25 target demonstrations (few-shot).
AI.result: Zero-shot deployment achieves up to 70% success on five tasks (box lifting, backpack packing, cloth folding, jacket zipping, plate handover). With 25 target demonstrations, finetuning yields a further 65–70% improvement over training from scratch.
AI.conclusion: MonoDuo demonstrates effective transfer from single-arm data to bimanual policies, reducing data requirements and enabling practical learning for bimanual manipulation. Stronger perception/segmentation and closer alignment between synthetic and real demonstrations could further improve performance.

title: Emergent Semantic Representations in World Models through Physical Interaction without Linguistic Supervision
summary: What does a world model learn from physical exploration, without any linguistic supervision? We argue the answer is organized by a single principle: the geometric structure of the physical world. Training a VAE-based world model on random embodied exploration, we find that its latent space develops spatial semantic structure that mirrors physical geometry -- direction accuracy 0.677+-0.029 versus 0.547 for a randomly initialized encoder, and position RSA 0.192+-0.047 versus 0.029 for random encoders (6.6x improvement), showing that training induces genuine structural organization beyond CNN inductive bias. Across 20 temporal checkpoints, prediction performance and semantic alignment co-improve (Spearman r=-0.61, p=0.004), consistent with the shared-driver account. We confirm this through a double knockout: standard KL regularization (beta=0.1) forces the encoder away from geometric structure, and both prediction performance and semantic alignment collapse simultaneously to near-chance by step 50,000 -- exactly as the shared-driver account predicts. Reducing beta to 0.001 restores geometric access and recovers both capabilities together. These findings establish physical world geometry as the organizing principle of world model representations, with direct implications for the design of semantically grounded embodied agents.

AI.tldr: A VAE-based world model trained on random embodied exploration organizes its latent space around physical geometry; predictive performance and semantic alignment co-improve and are causally tied to a geometry-preserving KL objective.
AI.motivation: To determine whether a language-free world model’s internal representations become structured by the geometry of the physical world, and whether this geometry governs both predictive quality and semantic alignment.
AI.method: Train a VAE-based world model on random embodied exploration. Assess latent geometry via direction accuracy and position RSA; track prediction performance and semantic alignment over 20 temporal checkpoints; test causality with KL regularization (beta) to enforce or suppress geometric structure; perform a double knockout (standard beta=0.1) and rescue (beta=0.001).
AI.result: Latent representations exhibit geometry-aligned structure: direction accuracy 0.677±0.029 vs 0.547 for random encoder; position RSA 0.192±0.047 vs 0.029. 6.6× improvement. Prediction and semantic alignment co-improve across checkpoints (Spearman r=−0.61, p=0.004). With beta=0.1, KL regularization drives encoders away from geometry and both metrics collapse; reducing beta to 0.001 restores geometric access and both metrics recover.
AI.conclusion: Physical world geometry is the organizing principle of world-model representations, suggesting designs for semantically grounded embodied agents.

title: Orthogonal Concept Erasure for Diffusion Models
summary: Concept erasure has emerged as a promising approach to mitigate undesired or unsafe content in diffusion models, yet existing methods still face significant limitations. While training-based methods are effective, their high computational cost limits scalability. Editing-based methods are more efficient and deployment-friendly, yet they struggle to simultaneously achieve precise concept erasure and preserve overall generative capacity. We identify this core limitation of the editing-based methods as reliance on additive parameter updates. Our empirical analysis reveals that concept semantics primarily depend on neuron direction rather than neuron magnitude, while overall generative capacity relies on the angular geometry of neurons. As additive updates inherently entangle direction, magnitude, and angular geometry, they inevitably introduce unintended interference between concept erasure and overall generation performance. To address this, we propose Orthogonal Concept Erasure (OCE), which reformulates editing-based erasure as multiplicative parameter updates from a geometric perspective. Specifically, OCE applies layer-wise orthogonal transformations derived from a closed-form solution to the parameters, enabling precise concept erasure while preserving the neuron magnitude and angular geometry. Furthermore, to address conflicting constraints in multi-concept erasure, OCE introduces a subspace-level objective with structured subspace manipulation, yielding a more effective and scalable erasure. Extensive experiments on single- and multi-concept erasure demonstrate that OCE outperforms existing methods in concept erasure and non-target preservation, erasing up to 100 concepts in 4.3 s. Code: https://github.com/HansSunY/OCE.

AI.tldr: Orthogonal Concept Erasure (OCE) introduces multiplicative, layer-wise, orthogonal parameter updates to diffusion models to erase concepts precisely while preserving generative capacity. It addresses the shortcomings of additive edits by decoupling concept direction (semantics) from angular geometry (capacity) and uses subspace-level objectives to scale to multiple concepts. Demonstrates faster, scalable erasure (up to 100 concepts in 4.3s) with strong non-target preservation; code released.
AI.motivation: Existing training-based concept erasure methods are computationally expensive and not scalable. Editing-based approaches rely on additive parameter updates, which entangle neuron direction (concept semantics) with magnitude and angular geometry (generative capacity), causing interference between erasure and overall performance.
AI.method: Orthogonal Concept Erasure (OCE) reformulates edits as multiplicative, layer-wise orthogonal transformations derived from a closed-form parameter solution. This preserves neuron magnitude and angular geometry while enabling precise erasure. A subspace-level objective with structured subspace manipulation addresses conflicts in multi-concept erasure.
AI.result: OCE outperforms prior editing methods in both single- and multi-concept erasure, maintaining non-target generation quality and erasing up to 100 concepts in 4.3 seconds.
AI.conclusion: OCE provides precise concept erasure with minimal interference to overall generative capacity, decoupling semantic direction from geometric capacity. The approach scales to multiple concepts and offers a practical, fast solution for deployment; code is available.

title: Lightweight Complementary-Cue Fusion for Robust Video Face Forgery Detection
summary: Current face video forgery detectors use wide or dual-stream backbones. We show that a single, lightweight fusion of two handcrafted cues can achieve higher accuracy with a much smaller model. Based on the Xception baseline model (21.9 million parameters), we build two detectors: LFWS, which adds a 1x1 convolution to combine a low-frequency Wavelet-Denoised Feature (WDF) with a phase-spectrum channel derived from Spatial-Phase Shallow Learning (SPSL), and LFWL, which merges WDF with Local Binary Patterns (LBP) in the same way. This extra module adds only 292 parameters, keeping the total at 21.9 million, smaller than F3Net (22.5 million) and less than half the size of SRM (55.3 million). Even with this minimal overhead, the fused models increase the average area under the curve (AUC) from 74.8% to 78.6% on FaceForensics++ and from 70.5% to 74.9% on DFDC-Preview, gains of 3.8% and 4.4% over the Xception baseline. They also consistently outperform F3Net, SRM, and SPSL in eight public benchmarks, without extra data or test-time augmentation. These results show that carefully paired, handcrafted features, combined through the lightweight fusion block, can provide competitive robustness at a significantly lower cost than comparable frequency-based detectors. Our findings suggest a need to reevaluate scale-driven design choices in face video forgery detection.

AI.tldr: A lightweight fusion of two handcrafted cues (WDF with SPSL phase-spectrum or LBP) added to Xception yields higher AUC with only 292 extra parameters, outperforming larger frequency-based detectors across eight benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reduce model size and computation in face video forgery detection while achieving competitive or superior accuracy; question scale-driven architectural choices.
AI.method: Implement two detectors, LFWS and LFWL, by adding a 1x1 fusion layer that combines WDF with a phase-spectrum channel from SPSL (LFWS) or with Local Binary Patterns (LBP) on top of Xception baseline (21.9M). The fusion adds 292 parameters, total remains 21.9M.
AI.result: AUC improved from 74.8% to 78.6% on FaceForensics++, and from 70.5% to 74.9% on DFDC-Preview. The models outperform F3Net, SRM, and SPSL on eight benchmarks, without extra data or test-time augmentation. The approach demonstrates competitive robustness at lower cost than frequency-based detectors.
AI.conclusion: Carefully paired handcrafted features fused via a lightweight block can rival larger frequency-based detectors, prompting reevaluation of scale-driven design in face video forgery detection.

title: The Open Motion Planning Library 2.0
summary: The Open Motion Planning Library (OMPL), first released in 2008, has become a cornerstone of the motion planning community, providing implementations of a wide range of state-of-the-art sampling-based algorithms. Over almost two decades of continuous development, we have steadily expanded the library with new planners, state spaces, and problem formulations. These additions range from asymptotically optimal and lazy planners to constrained motion planning and planning with temporal-logic goals. Building on this foundation, we introduce OMPL 2.0, a major evolution of the library that targets real-time motion planning through hardware acceleration and integrates seamlessly with modern AI research workflows. We also reflect on how OMPL and the field of motion planning have grown together over the years, and discuss the library's broader impact on the research community.

AI.tldr: OMPL 2.0 marks a major evolution of the Open Motion Planning Library to enable real-time planning through hardware acceleration and AI-friendly workflows, expanding planners, spaces, and problem formulations built over two decades, and reflecting on the field’s growth and impact.
AI.motivation: Meet the demand for real-time motion planning and seamless integration with modern AI research workflows; extend capabilities across asymptotically optimal, lazy, constrained, and temporal-logic planning; leverage hardware acceleration to reduce planning times.
AI.method: Extend OMPL with new planners, diversified state spaces, and richer problem formulations; integrate hardware acceleration and design for compatibility with AI-centric research pipelines; provide reflection on historical growth and community impact.
AI.result: OMPL 2.0 enables real-time planning capabilities and AI-compatible workflows, with broader library features and improved performance through hardware acceleration; reinforces OMPL’s role as a foundational tool in motion planning.
AI.conclusion: OMPL has grown in tandem with the motion planning field, shaping and being shaped by research trends; the 2.0 release solidifies real-time capabilities and AI integration as central themes for future development and community impact.

title: Continuity and Ordinality Matter: Constraining Time Series Tokens for Effective Time Series Analysis with Large Language Models
summary: Token-based time series large language models (TS-LLMs) have emerged as a promising direction for time series analysis and reasoning. However, prior studies largely overlook the inherent continuity and ordinality of time series tokens, which substantially limits model performance. In this paper, we argue that preserving these properties in time series token embeddings is crucial for the effectiveness of token-based TS-LLMs. To this end, we propose COM (Continuity and Ordinality Matter), a continuity- and ordinality-aware strategy that integrates geometric constraints into both the initialization and training stages. Empirical results on multiple time series analysis benchmarks demonstrate that COM consistently improves the performance of token-based TS-LLMs, achieving competitive results and strong generalizability. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/COM .

AI.tldr: COM enforces continuity and ordinality in time-series token embeddings via geometric constraints during initialization and training, yielding consistent improvements for token-based TS-LLMs.
