AI.motivation: Time series tokens are inherently continuous and ordinal; ignoring these properties limits model capability. Embedding geometry can preserve these relationships.
AI.method: Proposes COM: a continuity- and ordinality-aware strategy applying geometric constraints in both initialization and training phases of token-based TS-LLMs.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multiple time series benchmarks shows consistent performance gains, competitive results, and strong generalization.
AI.conclusion: Preserving continuity and ordinality in token embeddings is beneficial; COM provides an effective, general mechanism; code is available.

title: Frontier LLM-based agents can overcome the ontology curation bottleneck for natural phenotypes
summary: Linking free-text phenotype descriptions to ontology terms, typically referred to as phenotype annotation, is essential for the cross-study integration of comparative morphological data. This labor intensive process has heavily relied on highly trained human experts, which makes it challenging to scale and thus a key bottleneck. Dahdul et al. (2018) established a Gold Standard (GS) of Entity-Quality (EQ) annotations across seven phylogenetic studies and used it to evaluate three human curators and the Semantic CharaParser NLP tool with ontology-based semantic similarity metrics; they reported that machine-human consistency was significantly lower than inter-curator (human-human) consistency. Here we revisit that benchmark with five frontier hosted LLMs from Anthropic and OpenAI, each operating as an "agentic curator" within a self-contained workspace that supplies the source publication PDF, the same annotation guide used by the original human curators, the four project ontologies (UBERON, PATO, BSPO, GO), and a validation script. Evaluated against the same Gold Standard, every agent fell within the range of inter-curator variability of the three trained human biocurators of the original study; the best performing agents approached but did not reach the best performing human curator. Agents substantially outperformed Semantic CharaParser on all four metrics.

AI.tldr: Five frontier LLMs used as agentic curators achieved performance within human inter-curator variability and surpassed Semantic CharaParser in four ontology-based metrics for phenotype annotation against the Dahdul et al. Gold Standard, approaching top human curator but not surpassing them.
AI.motivation: Phenotype annotation links free-text descriptions to ontology terms; scaling relies on labor-intensive human curators; prior study showed machine-human consistency lag; evaluate if LLMs can bridge gap.
AI.method: Replicated the Gold Standard evaluation with five LLMs (Anthropic/OpenAI) as autonomous curators within a self-contained workspace containing source PDFs, the original annotation guide, four ontologies (UBERON, PATO, BSPO, GO), and a validation script; used four ontology-based semantic similarity metrics to compare against the GS across seven phylogenetic studies; compared to three trained human curators and Semantic CharaParser.
AI.result: Agentic LLMs’ performance fell within the inter-curator variability of the original human curators; best LLMs approached but did not beat the top human curator; all LLMs outperformed Semantic CharaParser across all four metrics.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can match human-level consistency in phenotype annotation to a significant extent and outperform a leading NLP-based tool, suggesting scalable, near-human curation capability while leaving some gap to top human performance.

title: GeRaF: Neural Geometry Reconstruction from Radio Frequency Signals
summary: GeRaF is the first method to use neural implicit learning for near-range 3D geometry reconstruction from radio frequency (RF) signals. Unlike RGB or LiDAR-based methods, RF sensing can see through occlusion but suffers from low resolution and noise due to its lensless imaging nature. While lenses in RGB imaging constrain sampling to 1D rays, RF signals propagate through the entire space, introducing significant noise and leading to cubic complexity in volumetric rendering. Moreover, RF signals interact with surfaces via specular reflections, requiring fundamentally different modeling. To address these challenges, GeRaF (1) introduces filter-based rendering to suppress irrelevant signals, (2) implements a physics-based RF volumetric rendering pipeline, and (3) proposes a novel lensless sampling and lensless alpha blending strategy that makes full-space sampling feasible during training. By learning signed distance functions, reflectiveness, and signal power through MLPs and trainable parameters, GeRaF takes the first step towards reconstructing millimeter-level geometry from RF signals in real-world settings.

AI.tldr: GeRaF pioneers neural implicit learning for near-range RF 3D geometry using signed distance fields, addressing occlusion, noise, and full-space sampling with filter-based rendering, a physics-based RF rendering pipeline, and lensless sampling/alpha blending.
AI.motivation: RF sensing can see through occlusions but suffers from low resolution, high noise, and lensless imaging; unlike RGB/LiDAR with sampling constrained to rays, RF propagates through space and experiences strong specular reflections, necessitating a new modeling approach for high-resolution geometry reconstruction.
AI.method: (1) filter-based rendering to suppress irrelevant RF signals; (2) physics-based RF volumetric rendering pipeline; (3) lensless sampling and lensless alpha blending enabling full-space sampling during training; uses MLPs to learn signed distance functions, reflectiveness, and signal power with trainable parameters.
AI.result: First step towards reconstructing millimeter-level geometry from RF signals in real-world settings, demonstrating feasibility of neural implicit representations for RF-based near-field 3D reconstruction and presenting a complete pipeline addressing noise, sampling, and specular reflections.
AI.conclusion: GeRaF introduces a novel framework that enables millimeter-level geometric reconstruction from RF signals by combining filter-based rendering, physics-based RF volume rendering, and lensless sampling techniques; it opens avenues for robust RF-based 3D sensing but requires further validation across environments, efficiency improvements, and investigation of generalization to dynamic scenes.

title: PrismFlow: Residual Dynamics for Flow Matching in Time-Series Generation
summary: Generating high-quality time-series data is challenging because real-world signals often exhibit multimodal patterns and multiscale dynamics, including oscillations and high-frequency variations. Flow Matching (FM) offers an efficient alternative to diffusion models, but practical implementations typically rely on a single finite-capacity global vector-field estimator. In such heterogeneous temporal distributions, distinct regimes may pass through nearby flow states while requiring incompatible conditional velocities. A monolithic estimator trained with the standard $\ell_2$ velocity-matching objective may therefore learn an overly smoothed approximation of the local transport field. This estimator-level smoothing can attenuate branch-specific dynamics, leading to spectral distortion and poor mode coverage. To address this, we propose PrismFlow, a new FM method with Koopman-inspired dynamical experts. Each expert learns residual corrections in a latent space where local nonlinear temporal evolution can be approximated by linear transitions. We further propose a confidence-aware Winner-Take-All (WTA) objective that updates only the expert best aligned with each sample while masking gradients to the others, encouraging mode-specific specialization. During sampling, the selected expert adds a residual dynamical correction to the global transport field, preserving FM stability while recovering fine-grained and high-frequency temporal structures. Across various benchmarks, PrismFlow effectively mitigates the spectral contraction in standard FM and achieves state-of-the-art performance, with a 15.6% gain in Context-FID and a 38.6% improvement in Discriminative Score, while remaining robust in low-data settings and effective for forecasting and imputation.

AI.tldr: PrismFlow introduces Koopman-inspired dynamical experts for Flow Matching to address multimodal, multiscale time-series by learning residual corrections in latent space with a confidence-aware Winner-Take-All objective. This yields mode-specific dynamics, improves spectral fidelity, and achieves state-of-the-art scores on benchmarks while remaining robust in low-data or forecasting/imputation tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard Flow Matching uses a single global vector-field estimator, which can overly smooth local transport fields in heterogeneous temporal distributions, leading to spectral contraction and poor mode coverage. A modular, expert-based approach could capture regime-specific dynamics.
AI.method: PrismFlow employs multiple dynamical experts that learn residual corrections in a latent space where local evolution is approximately linear (Koopman-inspired). A confidence-aware Winner-Take-All objective updates only the best-aligned expert for each sample, masking gradients for others to promote mode specialization. During sampling, the chosen expert applies a residual correction to the global transport field, preserving FM stability while recovering high-frequency structures.
AI.result: Across benchmarks, PrismFlow reduces spectral contraction, achieves a 15.6% gain in Context-FID and a 38.6% improvement in Discriminative Score, and demonstrates robustness in low-data settings as well as effectiveness for forecasting and imputation.
AI.conclusion: PrismFlow offers a scalable, expert-based FM framework that can capture multimodal and multiscale temporal dynamics without sacrificing stability, delivering improved mode coverage and finer temporal fidelity over standard FM.

title: VFEAgent: A Multimodal Agent Framework for End-to-End Automated Finite Element Analysis
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables. Equal contribution: Jiachen Zhang and Junyi Lao. Corresponding author: Songfang Huang. Preprint
summary: Finite Element Analysis (FEA) serves as the cornerstone of modern engineering design. However, its workflow is inherently complex and relies heavily on domain expertise. Although recent efforts have integrated Large Language Models (LLMs) into FEA, existing approaches face limitations in handling multimodal inputs and executing complex tasks. To address these limitations, we propose VFEAgent, an end-to-end multi-agent system designed to automate FEA modeling and simulation directly from input images and problem descriptions. Our methodology integrates two core components: (1) a multimodal vision-language multi-agent pipeline that employs ReAct-driven reasoning to extract structured FEA specifications from heterogeneous inputs and (2) a verification-first code synthesis framework, incorporating robust self-debugging and fallback mechanisms to ensure executability and physical validity. We systematically evaluated the system across various engineering mechanics scenarios. The results demonstrate that VFEAgent achieves a high success rate in generating complete and physically valid simulations, outperforming LLM-based baseline methods in reliability and correctness. These findings validate the feasibility of automating the complete FEA workflow, highlighting the framework's potential to liberate engineers from tedious manual analysis.

AI.tldr: VFEAgent is an end-to-end multimodal, multi-agent system that automates FEA modeling and simulation from images and problem descriptions, using ReAct-inspired reasoning and a verification-first code synthesis framework with self-debugging, achieving high reliability and physical validity compared to LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: FEA workflows are complex, idiosyncratic, and demand domain expertise; existing LLM-based approaches struggle with multimodal inputs and executing complete, valid simulations; there is a need for automated, end-to-end solutions to reduce manual effort.
AI.method: Two main components: (1) a multimodal vision-language multi-agent pipeline employing ReAct-style reasoning to extract structured FEA specifications from heterogeneous inputs; (2) a verification-first code synthesis framework with robust self-debugging and fallback mechanisms to ensure executable, physically valid simulations.
AI.result: Systematic evaluation across engineering mechanics scenarios shows a high success rate in producing complete and physically valid simulations, with better reliability and correctness than LLM-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of fully automating the complete FEA workflow and highlights potential to free engineers from tedious manual analysis.

title: Seeing through boxes: Non-Line-of-Sight 3D Reconstruction from Radar Signals
comment: Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR), 2026
summary: Reconstructing object geometry from radio frequency (RF) signals is fundamentally challenging due to the lensless imaging nature of RF sensing, which leads to low spatial resolution and high noise. Unlike light signals, RF signals can penetrate occlusions and thus capture information about hidden scenes. Existing Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS) 3D neural reconstruction methods can recover coarse surfaces inside enclosed environments but often suffer from unstable optimization, noisy surface geometry, and surface ambiguity, failing to produce accurate zero-level sets from the signed distance field (SDF). These limitations largely stem from neglecting the role of Line-of-Sight (LoS) geometry outside the enclosed region, which provides valuable physical constraints for modeling signal propagation. In this paper, we introduce a Unified LoS and NLoS neural geometry reconstruction framework GeRaF 2.0 that leverages the outside LoS geometry to model and guide RF propagation from the LoS region into the NLoS region. By integrating visual LoS priors into the neural field formulation, GeRaF 2.0 achieves stable training and physically consistent reconstruction of both visible and hidden geometry, setting a new state-of-the-art in RF-based geometry reconstruction.

AI.tldr: A unified framework GeRaF 2.0 leverages outside line-of-sight geometry to guide RF-based reconstruction, achieving stable, physically consistent, state-of-the-art 3D geometry from RF signals.
AI.motivation: RF sensing has lensless imaging with low resolution and high noise; NLoS methods lack LoS geometric constraints outside the scene, causing instability and surface ambiguity.
AI.method: GeRaF 2.0 integrates outside LoS geometry and visual LoS priors into a neural field to model RF propagation from LoS to NLoS regions, stabilizing training and improving reconstruction.
AI.result: Stable training and physically consistent geometry for both visible and hidden regions; state-of-the-art performance in RF-based geometry reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating external LoS geometry provides valuable physical constraints for RF propagation, enabling accurate zero-level sets of the SDF and robust NLoS geometry recovery.

title: Phase-Conditioned Imitation Learning with Autonomous Failure Recovery for Robust Deformable Object Manipulation
comment: Accepted to IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
summary: This paper presents a phase-conditioned, force-aware framework for robust deformable object manipulation. Standard imitation learning policies such as Action Chunking with Transformers (ACT) rely on a Markovian assumption at inference, causing state aliasing when visually similar observations require contradictory actions and preventing autonomous recovery from execution failures. We address this with a closed-loop hierarchical architecture. A FiLM-conditioned ACT encoder modulates feature extraction based on the current task phase, enabling a single unified policy to produce phase-specific behaviors while sharing action dynamics across phases. A multi-modal phase predictor fusing visual, force, and pose feedback estimates the phase in real time, detecting contact failures that are invisible to vision alone and autonomously triggering recovery trajectories. The system is completed by a hybrid impedance controller for compliant execution and a haptic teleoperation interface for force-aware data collection. Ablation studies show that FiLM-based modulation significantly outperforms both unconditioned and token-level conditioned baselines, and t-SNE analysis confirms that FiLM induces well-separated, phase-specific feature representations. Validated on hanging and removing a T-shirt with dual arms, the closed-loop system improves the hanging success rate from 56\% to 87\% through autonomous error recovery. Code and videos: https://leledeyuan00.github.io/phaser/

AI.tldr: Phase-conditioned, force-aware hierarchical policy for robust deformable object manipulation using FiLM-modulated ACT encoder, real-time multi-modal phase prediction, and autonomous error recovery; demonstrates improved performance on hanging/removing a T-shirt.
AI.motivation: Address Markovian limitations and state aliasing in standard imitation-learning policies (ACT) and enable autonomous recovery from execution failures in deformable object manipulation.
AI.method: A FiLM-conditioned ACT encoder modulates feature extraction by task phase; a multi-modal phase predictor fusing vision, force, and pose estimates phase in real time; a hybrid impedance controller enables compliant execution; a haptic teleoperation interface collects force data; ablations compare FiLM to unconditioned and token-conditioned baselines; t-SNE analyzes feature separation.
AI.result: FiLM modulation significantly outperforms unconditioned and token-level baselines; phase-specific features emerge; system raises hanging success from 56% to 87% via autonomous error recovery on a T-shirt task.
AI.conclusion: Integrating phase-conditioned perception with a real-time phase predictor and compliant control yields robust, autonomous deformable-object manipulation, mitigating state aliasing and enabling recovery from failures.

title: TaxDistill: Improving Metagenomic Taxonomic Annotation via Distilled Genomic Foundation Models
comment: The manuscript contains 14 pages, 7 figures, and 3 tables
summary: Metagenomic taxonomic annotation aims to identify the microbial origins of DNA fragments in environmental samples. Traditional methods that rely on sequence similarity are often constrained by the high microbial diversity and the incompleteness of reference databases, which has motivated the development of learning approaches such as Taxometer that perform post hoc correction to learn more informative metagenomic sequence representations. However, these methods typically rely on labels derived from similarity search tools during training, which inevitably introduces noise that can impair representation learning and degrade classification performance. To address this issue, we propose TaxDistill, a knowledge distillation framework for metagenomic classification. We introduce GenomeOcean, a 500M parameter genomic foundation model, as the teacher network to extract deep semantic features and generate soft labels based on confidence. By distilling this soft label information into a lightweight student network, TaxDistill effectively reduces the label noise introduced by initial retrieval tools. Comprehensive experiments on seven diverse CAMI2 datasets demonstrate that TaxDistill outperforms existing baselines in most scenarios. For instance, on the Gastrointestinal dataset, it improves the F1 score of MMseqs2 from 0.763 to 0.941, outperforming the Taxometer baseline. Overall, TaxDistill provides a reliable method for label correction in complex metagenomic analysis.

AI.tldr: A knowledge-distillation framework for metagenomic taxonomic classification (TaxDistill) uses a large genomic foundation model (GenomeOcean, 500M parameters) as teacher to generate soft labels, which are distilled into a lightweight student to mitigate label noise from similarity-based retrieval. It achieves improved performance across CAMI2 datasets, notably raising the Gastrointestinal dataset F1 from 0.763 to 0.941.
AI.motivation: Label noise from similarity-based retrieval and incomplete reference databases hampers representation learning in metagenomic classifiers; a robust learning paradigm is needed to improve accuracy and generalization.
AI.method: A teacher–student distillation pipeline where GenomeOcean provides deep semantic features and confidence-based soft labels; the student network learns from these soft labels to reduce reliance on noisy retrieval-derived labels; evaluated on seven CAMI2 datasets, with comparisons to MMseqs2 and Taxometer baselines.
AI.result: TaxDistill outperforms existing baselines in most scenarios; on the Gastrointestinal dataset, it elevates the F1 score from 0.763 (MMseqs2) to 0.941, surpassing Taxometer; overall, demonstrates effective label correction and improved metagenomic classification performance across diverse datasets.
AI.conclusion: TaxDistill provides a reliable approach for label correction in complex metagenomic analysis by distilling soft information from a genomic foundation model into a lightweight student, yielding robust representations and improved classification accuracy.

title: BEAMS: Benchmarking and Evaluating AI for Modeling and Simulation
summary: AI tools to support real world decision making must be able to build simulation models that inform their recommendations and render them interpretable. Tools that can automate aspects of modeling practice must complement human expertise, not replace it. The BEAMS Initiative aims to guide the development of AI tools for modeling and simulation toward forms that are responsible and ethical by establishing benchmarks for human centered modeling and simulation practices. The initiative uses open digital and organizational infrastructure to collaboratively evaluate AI tools for modeling and simulation. The open source sd ai project hosted by the initiative establishes transparency and enables contributions to be shared broadly. A steering group focuses on prioritizing potential benchmarks, while a technical group focuses on implementing the benchmarks in the form of automated tests. Tests for several distinct categories of evaluation have been implemented and applied to AI tools that support qualitative model building, quantitative model building, and model discussion. These include tests for causal translation, model iteration, causal reasoning, conformance, model behavior explanation, suggested model building steps, and suggested model fixes. When engines from the sd ai project are coupled with different LLMs, their performance on these evaluations reveals variability across different AI tools. The evaluations implemented by the initiative demonstrate that AI enabled modeling tools perform better at discussion and basic qualitative tasks than with causal reasoning and quantitative error fixing. No single LLM dominates across engine types, highlighting the importance of specific tasks and tradeoffs between speed and accuracy. Ongoing efforts of the initiative aim to incorporate benchmarks that address concerns about bias by considering alternative perspectives and human centered use cases.

AI.tldr: BEAMS develops benchmarks and open infrastructure to guide human-centered, ethical AI-enabled modeling; evaluations show variability across LLMs and better performance for qualitative discussion tasks than for causal reasoning or quantitative fixes; no single model dominates; ongoing work addresses bias and broader use cases.
AI.motivation: To ensure AI tools for real-world decision making are interpretable, trustworthy, and augment rather than replace human expertise; establish benchmarks for human-centered modeling and simulation.
AI.method: Establish governance (steering and technical groups) to prioritize and implement benchmarks as automated tests. Use the sd ai open-source project to couple engines with various LLMs. Apply a suite of tests across qualitative model building, quantitative model building, and model discussion (e.g., causal translation, model iteration, causal reasoning, conformance, model behavior explanation, suggested steps, suggested fixes).
AI.result: AI-enabled modeling tools show stronger performance in discussion and basic qualitative tasks than in causal reasoning and quantitative error fixing. Performance varies across engines/LLMs; no single LLM dominates across engine types.
AI.conclusion: Ongoing benchmark development should address bias by incorporating alternative perspectives and human-centered use cases, while continuing to balance speed and accuracy in AI-assisted modeling.

title: Robust Cross-Domain Generalization Using Unlabeled Target Data with Source-Domain Supervision
summary: It is often desirable to generalize medical imaging AI models trained with dense annotations to data acquired from different ultrasound scanners or clinical sites; however, retraining these models with new annotations is often difficult and costly. We examine this challenge in pediatric wrist fracture assessment using point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS), where fractures are common and can be effectively triaged via ultrasound. AI has shown radiologist-level performance for fracture detection, often aided by high-quality bony structure segmentation. However, due to significant domain shifts, models perform poorly on data from other centers or probes, and obtaining segmentation labels across devices is impractical due to manual annotation effort and data privacy concerns. To address this, we propose a target-informed self-supervised pretraining and model-ensemble strategy. Specifically, our approach combines masked image modeling (MIM) and contrastive learning to learn target-domain structural representations without labels, and introduces a confidence-aware infusion head to adaptively integrate predictions. The source dataset, collected with a Philips Lumify probe, contained dense labels, while the target dataset, acquired with a TeleMED portable probe, was unlabeled. The datasets were kept strictly separate throughout the entire process. Our method used labeled source data for supervised training and leveraged target-domain pretraining to improve generalization. On 318 images from 62 pediatric POCUS videos, this approach significantly improved cross-device performance, achieving over 6% Dice improvement on the target domain versus the baseline. These results demonstrate a label-efficient and privacy-preserving approach for cross-device-robust ultrasound AI, offering a framework that can be extended to multi-center studies or federated learning setups.

AI.tldr: A label-efficient, privacy-preserving strategy improves cross-device ultrasound fracture detection/segmentation by pretraining on the target domain with self-supervised learning (MIM + contrastive) and fusing predictions with a confidence-aware infusion head, achieving >6% Dice gain on a target device.
AI.motivation: Medical ultrasound models suffer from domain shift when applied across different scanners/probes and sites. Annotating segmentation labels for every device is costly and privacy-sensitive. A data-efficient, cross-device learning approach is needed that leverages unlabeled target data and dense-labeled source data while keeping datasets separate.
AI.method: Source-domain supervised training using densely labeled data (Philips Lumify). Target-domain pretraining with unlabeled TeleMED data using a combination of masked image modeling (MIM) and contrastive learning to learn target-specific structural representations. A confidence-aware infusion head computes adaptive fusion of predictions across models. Data splits kept strictly separate; target-domain pretraining informs generalization without sharing labels.
AI.result: On 318 images from 62 pediatric POCUS videos, the approach yielded >6% Dice improvement on the target domain compared with a baseline, demonstrating improved cross-device robustness without target labels.
AI.conclusion: The proposed target-informed self-supervised pretraining plus model-ensemble strategy offers a label-efficient, privacy-preserving path toward cross-device-robust ultrasound AI, with potential applicability to multi-center studies or federated learning.

title: A Progress-Aware Leader-Follower Midair Docking System for Dual-Drone Aerial Manipulation
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the 2026 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Automation Science and Engineering (CASE 2026), August 17-21, 2026, Shenyang, China
summary: Reliable midair docking between small unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) is essential for modular aerial cooperation and manipulation, but it requires precise relative-pose control and repeatable platform under tight thrust and payload constraints. We present a dual-drone docking platform where two quadrotors operate in a leader-follower formation and dock using a lightweight modular frame with passive magnetic latching. A progress-aware mission supervisor manages phase transitions: approach, alignment, capture, and settle. This platform integrates a complete hardware-software stack (ROS 2 with Crazyflie/PX4 interfaces) and synchronized logging for benchmark evaluation. We evaluate the platform in simulation and real-world experiments using quantitative metrics such as formation error, baseline and yaw consistency, docking success rate, time-to-dock, and failure-mode statistics. The platform enables statistically grounded comparison of docking supervision and synchronization strategies and provides a practical testbed for modular aerial cooperation and repeatable midair aerial manipulation.

AI.tldr: A dual-quadrotor docking platform uses a leader-follower setup with a passive magnetic latch and a phase-based mission supervisor to enable reliable midair docking under tight thrust/payload constraints; integrated ROS 2/Crazyflie/PX4 stack with synchronized logging; evaluated in simulation and on real hardware with quantitative docking metrics; serves as a benchmark/testbed for modular aerial cooperation and repeatable midair manipulation.
AI.motivation: Reliable midair docking is essential for modular aerial cooperation/manipulation, but is challenging under harsh thrust/payload constraints; there is a need for repeatable, benchmarkable platforms to compare docking strategies.
AI.method: Two quadrotors operate in a leader-follower formation, docking via a lightweight modular frame with passive magnetic latching. A progress-aware mission supervisor manages phase transitions: approach, alignment, capture, and settle. The system includes a complete hardware-software stack (ROS 2 with Crazyflie/PX4 interfaces) and synchronized logging. Evaluation is performed in both simulation and real-world experiments, using quantitative metrics (formation error, baseline/yaw consistency, docking success rate, time-to-dock, failure-mode statistics) to benchmark docking supervision and synchronization strategies.
AI.result: The platform enables statistically grounded comparison of docking supervision and synchronization strategies and provides a practical testbed for modular aerial cooperation and repeatable midair manipulation; benchmarking data include formation and alignment metrics, docking success, and timing statistics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed platform offers a practical, benchmarkable testbed for modular aerial cooperation and repeatable midair manipulation, facilitating rigorous evaluation of docking supervision and synchronization approaches in both simulated and real-world settings.

title: Balancing Multimodal Learning through Label Space Reshaping
summary: Multimodal learning often suffers from modality imbalance, where modalities that converge faster dominate optimization while others remain undertrained. Existing approaches typically mitigate this issue by strengthening the weak modality or adjusting optimization gradients. However, such strategies mainly compensate for optimization rate discrepancies, often at the expense of the strong modality's optimization capacity, without analyzing how these discrepancies arise at the modality level. Based on theoretical insights and empirical observations, we argue that the discrepancy of learning pace arises from differences in the mapping difficulty between modality-specific feature space and the shared label space. To address this issue, we propose Balanced Multimodal Label Reshaping (BMLR), the first method that promotes multimodal balance from the label-side design. BMLR reshapes the cross-modal label space to equalize mapping difficulty across modalities, thereby facilitating modality interaction and injecting richer inter-class information into each modality. Extensive experiments across multiple architectures demonstrate that BMLR consistently improves multimodal performance and exhibits strong compatibility with diverse model designs. The source code will be released soon.

AI.tldr: Proposes Balanced Multimodal Label Reshaping (BMLR) to equalize mapping difficulty across modalities by reshaping the cross-modal label space, improving multimodal interaction and performance across architectures; code forthcoming.
AI.motivation: Modality imbalance in multimodal learning stems from differences in the difficulty of mapping modality-specific features to the shared label space, not solely from optimization rate differences. Existing methods focus on compensating optimization pace, which can hurt the strong modality; there is a need to address label-space design to balance learning.
AI.method: Introduce BMLR, a label-side design that reshapes the cross-modal label space to equalize the mapping difficulty across modalities. This reshaping injects richer inter-class information into each modality and promotes cross-modal interaction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple architectures show consistent improvements and strong compatibility with diverse model designs.
AI.conclusion: BMLR is the first method to promote multimodal balance from the label side, enabling better modality interaction and information sharing; code will be released soon.

title: Adopt $\neq$ Adapt: Longitudinal Analyses of LLM Conversations in the Wild
summary: Although a growing body of research has begun to describe user--LLM interactions, the picture it paints is largely static; little is known about how individual users change their behavior over time. To address this gap, we analyze the conversational trajectories of $\sim$12,000 randomly sampled Microsoft Bing Copilot users and compare these with data from WildChat-4.8M. While the Copilot data contains significant population-level trends, we find that trends in individual user trajectories are much weaker; user habits prove to be overwhelmingly sticky. We also find stark differences between users of different activity levels: more active users have more successful conversations and use the LLM for more complex and professionally oriented tasks. Some user trends also appear in WildChat-4.8M, but we find evidence that this dataset is significantly skewed towards highly proficient "power" users. Ultimately, our results suggest that existing user behavior is difficult to change and demonstrate the extent of user heterogeneity. Our comparison between datasets highlights that WildChat does not represent typical user-AI interactions, an important caveat for downstream uses of the data.

AI.tldr: Individual user trajectories in AI-assisted interactions are highly sticky and heterogeneous; population trends do not generalize to individuals; WildChat data are biased toward expert users and may not reflect typical user behavior; more active users perform better and use LLMs for complex tasks; dataset choice critically affects downstream inferences.
AI.motivation: To move beyond static, population-level descriptions of user–LLM interactions and understand how individual users evolve over time, including heterogeneity and dataset representativeness.
AI.method: Longitudinal analysis of conversational trajectories from ~12,000 randomly sampled Microsoft Bing Copilot users, with a comparative analysis against WildChat-4.8M. The study assesses trajectory strength, stability (habit persistence), activity-level segmentation, conversation success, task complexity, and professional orientation, and contrasts findings across datasets.
AI.result: Individual user trajectories exhibit weak trends and strong habit stickiness; user behavior is highly heterogeneous. More active users achieve more successful conversations and engage in more complex/professional tasks. WildChat-4.8M shows similar trends but is skewed toward high-skill, “power” users, limiting representativeness. Overall, cross-dataset differences reveal that WildChat may not reflect typical user–AI interactions.
AI.conclusion: Temporal dynamics and population heterogeneity are essential for understanding user–LLM interactions. Behavioral change is difficult, and dataset biases must be accounted for in downstream applications and evaluations.

title: Eulerian Gaussian Splatting using Hashed Probability Pyramids
summary: We introduce a probabilistic splat-based radiance field framework that retains the fast rasterization and test-time efficiency of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) while replacing heuristic primitive manipulation with gradient-based optimization of a volumetric probability density. Rather than relocating, splitting, or culling Gaussians via hand-tuned densification (e.g., ADC), we treat primitive locations as samples drawn from a persistent, learnable density. We instantiate this density using a novel, memory-efficient multi-scale hierarchical grid that enables end-to-end gradient-based optimization. To stabilize the optimization, we derive an unbiased gradient estimator with control variates that markedly reduces variance. By allowing probability mass to flow to where the loss demands, our framework eliminates brittle priors and naturally explores the volume, achieving state-of-the-art reconstruction quality on mip-NeRF 360 while preserving 3DGS-level rendering speed.

AI.tldr: A gradient-based probabilistic splat framework uses a learnable density on a memory-efficient multi-scale grid to replace hand-tuned Gaussian manipulation, achieving SOTA on mip-NeRF 360 with 3DGS-level speed.
AI.motivation: Eliminate brittle priors and manual Gaussian operations in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) by learning a persistent density for primitive locations, enabling end-to-end optimization.
AI.method: Introduce a memory-efficient multi-scale hierarchical grid to instantiate a differentiable density; treat primitive locations as samples from this density; derive an unbiased gradient estimator with control variates to reduce variance; allow probability mass to flow toward loss regions for end-to-end gradient optimization, preserving fast rendering.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality on mip-NeRF 360 while maintaining rendering speed comparable to 3D Gaussian Splatting.
AI.conclusion: The approach removes brittle priors, enables natural exploration of the volumetric space, and combines fast rendering with high-quality radiance-field reconstruction through probabilistic splats and gradient-based optimization.

title: 3DVLA: Enhancing Vision-Language-Action Models via 3D Spatial and Instance Understanding
summary: Vision-Language-Action models have achieved remarkable progress in robotic manipulation, yet they suffer from a critical limitation: a lack of 3D scene understanding. This deficiency manifests as three intertwined challenges: weak extraction of 3D spatial positions without enforcing multi-view consistency, inadequate 3D instance understanding, and fragile reasoning under occlusion. Although mature 3D perception methods exist, their direct integration into VLA pipelines is hindered by architectural incompatibility and by heavy reliance on costly instance-level annotations. To address the above challenges, we propose 3DVLA, a plug-and-play framework that injects robust 3D reasoning into pretrained VLAs without requiring extra manual labels or discarding VLM priors. Specifically, 3DVLA tackles the three challenges through: (1) pervasive 3D feature encoding with explicit multi-view consistency constraints across all modalities and a Spatially-Conditioned Geometry Aggregation method, (2) an instance estimation module with high-level instance tokens for 3D instance awareness, and (3) a masked self-supervised 3D encoding branch that retains its predictor for visual token completion to handle occlusions. We integrate 3DVLA with multiple VLA baselines and evaluate on LIBERO-Plus and RoboTwin 2.0. Results show consistent and significant gains in manipulation performance, validating both the effectiveness and plug-and-play compatibility of our approach.

AI.tldr: 3DVLA injects robust 3D reasoning into pretrained Vision-Language-Action models via pervasive 3D feature encoding with multi-view consistency, an instance estimation module for 3D instance awareness, and a masked self-supervised 3D encoding branch; it plugs into existing VLA baselines and yields significant manipulation gains on LIBERO-Plus and RoboTwin 2.0 without extra labels.
AI.motivation: Overcome three intertwined 3D understanding gaps in VLA: (i) weak 3D spatial positioning without multi-view consistency, (ii) poor 3D instance recognition, (iii) fragile reasoning under occlusion; while avoiding heavy instance-level annotations and architectural incompatibilities.
AI.method: Three components: (1) pervasive 3D feature encoding with explicit multi-view consistency constraints across modalities and Spatially-Conditioned Geometry Aggregation; (2) high-level instance tokens for 3D instance awareness; (3) masked self-supervised 3D encoding branch preserving a predictor for visual token completion to handle occlusion. Plug-and-play integration with pretrained VLA baselines.
AI.result: Demonstrates consistent, significant performance gains across LIBERO-Plus and RoboTwin 2.0 baselines, validating effectiveness and compatibility.
AI.conclusion: 3DVLA provides a practical pathway to endow VLAs with robust 3D reasoning without extra manual labels or discarding VLM priors, achieving improved 3D perception, instance awareness, and occlusion handling in robotic manipulation.

title: Representation Alignment Rests on Linear Structure
summary: We investigate the Platonic Representation Hypothesis (PRH) through a tripartite statistical framework of representations: signal, bias, and noise. {1) Signal:} We propose that Platonic alignment arises from the universal relationship between objects and attributes, which is encoded linearly in representations according to the Linear Representation Hypothesis (LRH). We provide evidence that LRH helps explain PRH by extracting linear object-attribute features with sparse autoencoders and showing that these sparse representations often exhibit stronger cross-modal alignment than their dense counterparts. {2) Bias:} Models have different implicit biases due to the diverse architectures and training procedures used. We show that this difference can be partially mitigated. Centering and normalization consistently improve cross-model alignment. {3) Noise:} Finite-sample training leads to noise in representations. We provide evidence that representational noise is driven by data scarcity by revealing a strong and consistent positive correlation between word frequency and alignment in LLMs and text embedding models. Synthesizing signal, bias, and noise, we propose a statistical model that refines the Linear Representation Hypothesis and explains further phenomena related to the alignment of representations emerging from diverse modern AI architectures.

AI.tldr: Tripartite signal–bias–noise framework for Platonic Representation Hypothesis (PRH) with Linear Representation Hypothesis (LRH). Evidence that sparse linear features yield stronger cross-modal alignment; bias mitigation via centering/normalization; noise tied to data scarcity (word frequency correlates with alignment); a refined LRH–PRH statistical model is proposed.
AI.motivation: To explain cross-modal and cross-architecture representation alignment by decomposing factors into signal, bias, and noise, and to extend the LRH to account for observed PRH phenomena.
AI.method: (1) Formalize LRH as the linear encoding of object–attribute relations; (2) extract linear object–attribute features with sparse autoencoders and compare with dense representations in terms of cross-modal alignment; (3) assess how architecture and training procedures (bias) affect alignment and test centering/normalization as mitigation; (4) analyze finite-sample noise by correlating word frequency with alignment in LLMs/text embeddings; (5) integrate findings into a refined statistical model of representation alignment.
AI.result: Sparse linear representations show stronger cross-modal alignment than dense ones, supporting LRH as a mechanism for PRH; centering and normalization improve cross-model alignment, indicating bias can be mitigated; a positive correlation between word frequency and alignment reveals data-scarcity-driven noise; these findings are unified into a refined LRH–PRH model.
AI.conclusion: A coherent tripartite framework—signal, bias, and noise—captures alignment phenomena across modern AI representations. The refined LRH clarifies how intrinsic object–attribute structure, architectural biases, and data scarcity together shape alignment, offering practical guidance (normalization) and directions for empirical validation and theoretical development.

title: When Models Disagree: Rethinking LLM Evaluation for Public Comment Analysis
summary: Federal agencies are deploying large language models (LLMs) to categorize public comment corpora, where the model's organization of the record shapes what policymakers see and which arguments register. Standard evaluation, anchored on stance accuracy against a small validated set, cannot detect when different models produce materially different categorizations of the same public input. We propose an Interpretive Audit Pipeline that treats multi-model disagreement as diagnostic of interpretive complexity and directs human review toward genuinely ambiguous public input. Analyzing 1,260 public comments on a federal USDA docket across four LLMs, we find that inter-model thematic divergence exceeds within-model prompt variation, and that an expert rubric suppresses deep interpretive disagreement without resolving it. In a two-stage labeling study on a stratified 40-comment subsample, four LLMs and a human annotator labeled independently and then revised after seeing the others' labels. Revision behavior varied across labelers, and the human annotator's revisions frequently introduced framings absent from the ensemble's collective output. We argue disagreement-based evaluation is a necessary complement to accuracy metrics for LLM-assisted interpretive coding.

AI.tldr: Disagreement among multiple LLMs reveals interpretive complexity in public-comment coding; proposes an Interpretive Audit Pipeline and argues disagreement-based evaluation complements stance-accuracy metrics.
AI.motivation: To address the limitation that stance-accuracy evaluation misses systematic interpretive divergence when LLMs categorize public input for policy decisions; aims to align evaluation with the interpretive nature of policy-relevant coding.
AI.method: Introduce an Interpretive Audit Pipeline; analyze 1,260 USDA docket comments across four LLMs; conduct a two-stage labeling study on a stratified subsample of 40 comments with four LLMs and a human annotator, including iterative revisions after viewing others’ labels.
AI.result: Inter-model thematic divergence exceeds within-model prompt variation; expert rubrics suppress deep interpretive disagreement without resolution; human annotator revisions introduce framings absent from the ensemble; disagreement-based evaluation proves a necessary complement to accuracy metrics in LLM-assisted interpretive coding.
AI.conclusion: Disagreement-based evaluation is essential alongside accuracy metrics for assessing LLM-assisted interpretive coding, guiding human review toward genuinely ambiguous inputs and improving policy-relevant interpretive outcomes.

title: Guidance Contrastive Token Credit Assignment for Discrete Policy Optimization
summary: Group-advantage-based reinforcement learning methods, such as GRPO and DAPO, have demonstrated strong performance across diverse domains, including mathematical reasoning and text-to-image generation. However, their reliance on sample-level rewards introduces a key limitation as uniform credit assignment across all tokens fails to capture fine-grained, token-level contributions. To address this issue, we propose Guidance Contrastive Policy Optimization (GCPO), a novel algorithm that enables per-token credit assignment by contrasting model predictions under positive and negative prompts. Rather than uniformly broadcasting sample-level advantages, GCPO assigns token-level advantages proportional to the difference between these contrastive predictions, allowing more precise and informative learning signals. Empirically, we find that GCPO emphasizes semantically relevant regions such as visual areas aligned with textual prompts in text-to-image generation, and critical keywords within reasoning traces for chain-of-thought tasks. Through extensive experiments, GCPO consistently outperforms GRPO and DAPO baselines on both text-to-image generation and chain-of-thought reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating its effectiveness as a general and scalable optimization strategy for discrete policy learning.

AI.tldr: GCPO introduces per-token credit assignment by contrasting model outputs under positive vs negative prompts, addressing the token-level credit issue in group-advantage RL methods like GRPO/DAPO. It improves text-to-image generation and chain-of-thought reasoning over baselines, and is presented as a general, scalable discrete-policy optimization strategy.
AI.motivation: To overcome uniform credit assignment from sample-level rewards in group-advantage reinforcement learning, which obscures token-level contributions critical for structured tasks such as reasoning traces and image-conditional generation.
AI.method: GCPO computes token-level advantages by contrasting predictions under positive and negative prompts. The per-token advantages are proportional to the difference between these contrastive predictions, replacing uniform sample-level broadcasting with token-differentiated signals and applying this to discrete policy learning.
AI.result: Empirically, GCPO emphasizes semantically relevant regions in text-to-image generation and key keywords in reasoning traces for chain-of-thought tasks. Across experiments, GCPO outperforms GRPO and DAPO baselines on both domains, indicating improved learning signals and performance.
AI.conclusion: GCPO provides a general, scalable approach for discrete policy learning by enabling fine-grained, token-level credit assignment through contrastive prompts, with strong empirical gains in both multimodal generation and reasoning benchmarks.

title: ElegantVLA: Learning When to Think for Efficient Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are a powerful paradigm for generalist robotic control. However, their high computational cost and limited control frequency hinder real-time robotic manipulation, especially when large vision-language backbones and iterative action heads run at every control step. Existing VLA acceleration methods often optimize individual components or rely on fixed acceleration rules, treating different control steps with largely fixed computation and overlooking the non-uniform reasoning demands of sequential embodied control. Inspired by human motor control, where cognitive and feedback resources concentrate on goal-sensitive stages, we argue that VLA models should learn when to invest full computation and when to reuse prior computation. We propose ElegantVLA, a plug-in phase-adaptive inference framework that accelerates VLA models through intra-model dynamic compute scheduling. ElegantVLA introduces a lightweight scheduler that observes temporal representation similarity, robot-motion cues, and episode progress to jointly allocate computation across the vision encoder, LLM, and action head. For perception-language reasoning, the scheduler selects a five-level Vision-LLM compute mode, from full recomputation to multi-step temporal reuse, based on visual-language representation stability. For action generation, it selects a three-level denoising mode, reusing intermediate denoising states during stable motion while preserving full refinement for goal-sensitive stages. By coordinating these decisions, ElegantVLA offers a general acceleration framework for modern VLA pipelines with explicit action-generation modules, without modifying or retraining the base model. Experiments on GR00T and CogACT achieve up to 2.55x and 3.77x speedup, and on six real-world GR00T tasks ElegantVLA cuts computation by 2.18x while raising control frequency from 13.8 Hz to 26.3 Hz.

AI.tldr: ElegantVLA introduces a phase-adaptive, plug-in scheduler that dynamically allocates compute across vision encoder, LLM, and action head in Vision-Language-Action models, enabling substantial speedups without model retraining.
AI.motivation: VLA models incur high computational cost and operate at limited control frequencies; sequential embodied control exhibits non-uniform, goal-sensitive reasoning demands. A phase-adaptive, resource-aware approach can allocate compute where and when it matters most.
AI.method: A lightweight, plug-in scheduler observes temporal representation similarity, robot-motion cues, and episode progress to allocate computation across components. It defines a five-level Vision-LLM compute mode (full recomputation to multi-step reuse) for perception-language reasoning and a three-level denoising mode for action generation, enabling reuse of intermediate states during stable motion and full refinement for goal-sensitive stages. No retraining of the base model is required.
AI.result: Up to 2.55x speedup on GR00T and 3.77x on CogACT. On six real-world GR00T tasks, computation is reduced by 2.18x and control frequency doubles from 13.8 Hz to 26.3 Hz.
AI.conclusion: ElegantVLA provides a general, plug-in acceleration framework for modern VLA pipelines with explicit action-generation modules, achieving substantial speedups without modifying the base model.

title: Pre-Registering the Detectable Effect: A Paired-MDE Budget for 4-bit Quantization Benchmarks, with a Pilot Audit
summary: This is a planning-method note with an unpaired pilot audit. We adapt the classical paired-binary sample-size calculation (Miettinen, 1968) to quantization benchmarks, giving a conservative minimum detectable effect (MDE) bound $δ^{*} \le (z_{1-α/2}+z_{1-β})\sqrt{ρ_d/m}$ in the paired item count $m$ and the FP16-NF4 disagreement rate $ρ_d$. The bound turns "how reliable is my quantization claim?" into a one-line budget a benchmark designer can commit to before running. We illustrate the bound on four models and four benchmarks ($k=5$ splits of $n=100$), and add a parallel MMLU prompt-template study to put the bound's quantization-noise scale alongside the prompt-noise scale. Assuming $ρ_d=0.10$ (an unmeasured planning value), all observed NF4-FP16 deltas fall below the implied MDE, and most cross-split SDs lie within $\pm 1.5$ pp of the binomial reference $\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$, so much of the variance reported as "benchmark unreliability" on $n=100$ subsamples is binomial sampling noise. The single borderline cell (OPT-WinoGrande, $|Δ|=3.2$ pp) is below the implied MDE at $ρ_d=0.10$ but above it at $ρ_d=0.05$, illustrating the planning trade-off the bound makes explicit. On MMLU, prompt-template ranges of 2-10 pp meet or exceed the largest observed quantization delta (3.2 pp), so a quantization audit that does not first fix the prompt template absorbs template variance into its noise floor. We complement the bound with a five-line pre-registration template.

AI.tldr: A planning-method note that adapts the classical paired-binary sample-size bound to quantization benchmarks, deriving a conservative minimum detectable effect (MDE) δ* ≤ (z_{1-α/2}+z_{1-β})√(ρ_d/m) based on a disagreement rate ρ_d and item count m; applies it to NF4-FP16 quantization across four models/benchmarks and an MMLU prompt-template study; finds most deltas below the MDE with some trade-offs, and advocates fixing prompt templates and preregistration to disentangle noise.
AI.motivation: Provide a practical budgeting tool for evaluating the reliability of quantization claims in benchmarks, by turning reliability into a quantifiable MDE bound that practitioners can commit to before running experiments; incorporate both quantization noise (ρ_d) and prompt-template noise.
AI.method: Adapt Miettinen (1968) paired-binary sample-size calculation to the setting of quantization benchmarks. Derive a bound δ* ≤ (z_{1-α/2}+z_{1-β})√(ρ_d/m) where m is the paired item count and ρ_d the FP16-NF4 disagreement rate. Apply the bound to four models across four benchmarks (k=5 splits of n=100), and to a parallel MMLU prompt-template study to compare quantization-noise with prompt-noise. Use assumed ρ_d (e.g., 0.10) to compare observed NF4-FP16 deltas against the MDE, and analyze cross-split SDs relative to the binomial reference √(p(1-p)/n). Include a five-line pre-registration template as complement.
AI.result: The bound provides a conservative MDE for planning. With ρ_d=0.10, most NF4-FP16 deltas stay below δ*; cross-split SDs cluster within about ±1.5 percentage points of the binomial reference; a single borderline cell (OPT-WinoGrande, |Δ|=3.2 pp) is below the MDE at ρ_d=0.10 but above it at ρ_d=0.05, illustrating the planning trade-off. On MMLU, prompt-template ranges of 2–10 pp meet or exceed the largest observed quantization delta (3.2 pp), indicating that not fixing the prompt template would absorb template variance into the noise floor.
AI.conclusion: The bound translates reliability questions into a concrete planning budget, enabling bench-designers to anticipate whether observed quantization differences are detectable. It supports the use of a preregistration template and highlights the importance of fixing prompt templates to avoid confounding quantization with template variance.

title: Mind Your Tone: Does Tone Alter LLM Performance?
comment: 10 pages, 6 tables, 1 figure. Accepted as a full paper at the Thirty-second Americas Conference on Information Systems (AMCIS 2026), Reno. Follow-up to arXiv:2510.04950
summary: The use of Large Language Models (LLMs) is proliferating, yet their performance is observed to vary based on prompting styles and tones. In this study, we investigate both whether and how tonal variations in prompts lead to disparate LLM accuracy for objective multiple-choice questions. We use two datasets: a 50-base question dataset with five tone variants and a 570-base question MMLU subset spanning 57 subjects with seven tone variants. Experiments were conducted to evaluate the performance of four cost-efficient, popular LLMs: ChatGPT-4o, ChatGPT-5-nano, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite. Across models, tonal effects are systematic but highly model-dependent. Some models show small, yet statistically significant, shifts, while others exhibit large accuracy swings across tones. Further, we identify subject-level differences in tone sensitivity and present a routing framework to explain how tones may attune internal reasoning modes. Our findings caution users against assuming tone-robust reliability in LLM deployments.

AI.tldr: Tone variations in prompts systematically affect LLM accuracy on objective MCQs; effects are model-dependent and vary across questions and subjects; a routing framework is proposed to explain how tones tune internal reasoning modes.
AI.motivation: To understand whether and how tonal prompt variations influence LLM performance across multiple models and datasets, informing robust deployment.
AI.method: Evaluate four cost-efficient LLMs (ChatGPT-4o, ChatGPT-5-nano, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite) on two datasets: a 50-question base with five tone variants; a 570-question MMLU subset spanning 57 subjects with seven tone variants. Measure accuracy, tone sensitivity, and statistical significance; develop a routing framework to explain tone-induced changes in internal reasoning modes.
AI.result: Tonal effects are systematic but model-dependent. Some models show small but significant shifts; others exhibit large accuracy swings across tones. There are subject-level differences in tone sensitivity. A routing framework is proposed to explain how tones tune internal reasoning modes.
AI.conclusion: Tone sensitivity undermines tone-robust claims for LLM deployments; users should consider tonal effects in prompting and potentially use routing frameworks to mitigate risk.

title: MetaRanker: Human-in-the-loop Active Ranking for Metalens Image Quality
summary: Image quality in modern imaging systems emerges from the coupled effects of the sensor, optics, and computational reconstruction. Ultra-thin metalenses offer a path toward substantial miniaturization of optical modules, but practical designs often exhibit pronounced chromatic and field-dependent aberrations that necessitate computational reconstruction. In current metalens pipelines, reconstruction models are commonly trained and selected using distortion-based fidelity objectives, such as PSNR, yet these proxies can be weakly correlated with human preference and downstream utility, reflecting the well-known perception--distortion trade-off. We introduce MetaRanker, a human-in-the-loop active ranking framework that formalizes metalens image quality in terms of semantic interpretability, defined as the degree to which humans can reliably recognize objects and structures in the presence of optical artifacts. MetaRanker combines a probabilistic preference model with uncertainty-aware query selection, and leverages vision--language models to provide lightweight semantic priors. Importantly, these priors are used only to guide the sampling of informative comparisons; human judgments remain the primary supervision signal throughout. Across real-world and synthetic metalens datasets with distinct degradation profiles, MetaRanker produces rankings that align most closely with human assessments, while reducing the number of pairwise annotations required by approximately 80% relative to exhaustive pairwise evaluation. Finally, we show that standard image quality assessment metrics exhibit limited alignment with human interpretability in the metalens domain, positioning MetaRanker as a practical step toward perceptually grounded metalens evaluation and co-design.

AI.tldr: MetaRanker uses human-in-the-loop active ranking to assess metalens image quality by semantic interpretability, combining probabilistic preferences, uncertainty-aware query selection, and vision-language priors; achieves near-human alignment with ~80% reduction in pairwise annotations; reveals perceptual metrics matter more than PSNR for metalenses.
AI.motivation: Current metalens pipelines rely on distortion-based metrics (e.g., PSNR) that poorly predict human perception and downstream utility; metalenses exhibit strong chromatic/field aberrations requiring perceptually grounded evaluation and co-design.
AI.method: Propose MetaRanker: a probabilistic preference model + uncertainty-aware query selection + lightweight vision-language priors; human judgments remain the supervision; use semantic interpretability as the objective; apply to real and synthetic metalens datasets; sample informative pairwise comparisons to reduce annotations.
AI.result: Rankings produced by MetaRanker align most closely with human assessments; reduces pairwise annotations by ~80% relative to exhaustive evaluation; outperforms distortion-based metrics in correlating with human interpretability; validated on datasets with different degradation profiles.
AI.conclusion: Perceptually grounded evaluation via human-in-the-loop ranking is practical for metalens co-design; MetaRanker demonstrates that semantic interpretability better captures image quality than PSNR; priors guide sampling with minimal reliance on large labels; opens path for perceptual-metalens optimization.

title: VLA-Pro: Cross-Task Procedural Memory Transfer for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action~(VLA) models have shown strong potential for general-purpose robotic manipulation, yet they still struggle to generalize to unseen tasks that necessitate transferring relevant experience across objects, scenes, and action patterns. This paper proposes VLA-Pro, a plug-and-play framework designed to enhance cross-task generalization by storing task-relevant procedural memories at training time and transferring these memories during inference. Specifically, VLA-Pro stores task-specific LoRA adapters as parameterized procedural memories during training. At inference time, VLA-Pro retrieves relevant procedural memories based on the current multi-modal context and dynamically fuses these memories for generating the current action chunk. Experiments on RoboTwin, RLBench, and real-world manipulation tasks show that VLA-Pro consistently improves cross-task generalization across multiple backbones, achieving up to a 207% relative improvement in simulation and increasing real-world success rate from 5.8% to 65.0%. These results suggest that procedural memory retrieval and adaptation provide an effective mechanism for transferring manipulation experience to novel tasks while preserving modularity and execution stability.

AI.tldr: VLA-Pro enhances cross-task generalization in Vision-Language-Action models for robotic manipulation by storing and retrieving task-specific LoRA adapters as procedural memories, dynamically fusing them at inference to guide action generation, achieving large gains in simulation and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: VLA models struggle to generalize to unseen tasks requiring cross-task transfer of experience across objects, scenes, and action patterns; a modular, memory-based approach could reuse task-relevant experience without full retraining.
AI.method: During training, learn and store task-specific LoRA adapters as parameterized procedural memories. At inference, retrieve relevant memories using the current multi-modal context and fuse them to generate the current action chunk. Evaluated on RoboTwin, RLBench, and real-world manipulation tasks across multiple backbones.
AI.result: Significant cross-task generalization improvements across backbones; up to 207% relative improvement in simulation; real-world success rate increases from 5.8% to 65.0%. Demonstrates that procedural memory retrieval/adaptation supports transferring manipulation experience while preserving modularity and stability.
AI.conclusion: Procedural memory retrieval and adaptation provide an effective mechanism for transferring manipulation experience to novel tasks with maintained modularity and execution stability.

title: Towards Continuous-time Causal Foundation Models
comment: ICML 2026 2nd Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data (FMSD)
summary:
Extending discrete-time causal Prior-data Fitted Networks for time series to continuous time invites writing the mechanism as a stochastic differential equation (SDE) -- but if the SDE is integrated \emph{once per observation gap}, the trajectory law depends on when it is observed, and the prior remains a discrete-time Markov model in SDE clothing.
  We propose a precise continuity criterion -- trajectory-law invariance to the observation schedule -- together with a three-tier taxonomy (discrete; naive observation-grid integration; fine-grid integration with decoupled observation) and a construction realising the top tier on a random DAG with OU or small-MLP nonlinear drifts, irregular observation schedules, and hard / soft / time-varying interventions.
  A $2 \times 2$ encoder $\times$ integrator ablation, run independently on a linear and a nonlinear prior, finds fine-grid integration beats naive on 8/8 cells (sign-consistency $p < 1/256$) with the gap growing as the eval grid refines; the encoder axis is null with fine integration but time-aware-leading with naive.
  We release the prior and a preliminary zero-shot protocol on pharmacokinetic and physical-system data.

AI.tldr: Extends discrete-time causal prior-data fitted networks to continuous time via precise trajectory-law continuity; introduces a three-tier integration taxonomy and shows fine-grid integration outperforms naive integration; releases priors and zero-shot protocol for PK and physical systems.
AI.motivation: To resolve dependence of trajectory distribution on observation schedule when integrating SDEs once per gap, ensuring schedule-invariant trajectory laws and enabling robust inference with irregular observations and interventions.
AI.method: Proposes a continuity criterion (trajectory-law invariance to observation schedule), defines three-tier taxonomy (discrete; naive-grid integration; fine-grid integration with decoupled observation), and constructs a top-tier realization on a random DAG with OU or small-MLP drifts, accommodating irregular schedules and hard/soft/time-varying interventions; performs a 2x2 encoder×integrator ablation on linear and nonlinear priors; evaluates across grids; reports sign-consistency and schedule dependence.
AI.result: Fine-grid integration beats naive integration on all tested cells (8/8), with sign-consistency p<1/256; the performance gap grows as the evaluation grid refines; the encoder axis becomes null under fine integration but time-aware-leading under naive integration.
AI.conclusion: Provides a complete framework for continuous-time PD-FN via trajectory-law invariant integration, with a realizable top-tier model on DAGs; demonstrates practical gains and releases priors and a zero-shot protocol for pharmacokinetic and physical-system applications.

title: Practitioner Beliefs and Behaviors in AI-Enhanced Education: DOT Framework Survey Evidence
summary: This study reports findings from a cross-sectional survey (n = 72) of higher education practitioners examining beliefs, behaviors, and institutional conditions related to artificial intelligence (AI) integration in teaching and learning. Grounded in the DOT Framework, which integrates design thinking and open systems theory, the study investigates AI familiarity, usage patterns, design-oriented practices, and pedagogical beliefs. Exploratory factor analysis of 19 belief items identified a three-factor structure: AI Functional Capabilities, Oversight and Governance, and Instructor Collaboration and Planning (α = .90). Results indicate that practitioners hold favorable views of AI as a pedagogical support while maintaining strong commitments to human oversight and critical evaluation. Reported practices emphasize iterative prompting and content generation, with less consistent use of needs assessment and feedback loops. Institutional barriers including limited policy, training, and infrastructure were widely reported. These findings provide preliminary empirical support for the DOT Framework as a descriptive model of practitioner beliefs and practices, while also highlighting gaps between design-oriented theory and current implementation. The study contributes an initial measurement structure and identifies directions for confirmatory validation and outcome-based research linking AI-supported design practices to instructional quality.

AI.tldr: Cross-sectional survey (n=72) of higher-ed practitioners examining beliefs, behaviors, and institutional conditions for AI integration in teaching, grounded in the DOT Framework; identifies a three-factor belief structure and a gap between design-oriented theory and practice; contributes a preliminary measurement structure with directions for validation and outcome-based research.
AI.motivation: Fill empirical validation gaps for the DOT Framework in AI-enabled pedagogy, characterize practitioner beliefs, usage, and institutional barriers, and develop an initial measurement model.
AI.method: Cross-sectional survey (n=72) grounded in the DOT Framework; Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) on 19 belief items revealed a three-factor structure (AI Functional Capabilities, Oversight and Governance, Instructor Collaboration and Planning) with Cronbach's alpha α = .90. Assessed AI familiarity, usage patterns, design-oriented practices, pedagogical beliefs, and reported institutional barriers such as policy, training, and infrastructure.
AI.result: Practitioners hold favorable views of AI as pedagogical support while prioritizing human oversight and critical evaluation. Practices emphasize iterative prompting and content generation; needs assessment and feedback loops are less consistently used. Institutional barriers (policy, training, infrastructure) are widely reported. Findings preliminarily support the DOT Framework as a descriptive model for beliefs and practices and highlight gaps between design-oriented theory and actual implementation. A measurement structure is proposed, with avenues for confirmatory validation and outcome-based research linking AI-supported design practices to instructional quality.
AI.conclusion: DOT Framework shows promise as a descriptive model of practitioner beliefs and practices in AI-integrated teaching. The study provides an initial measurement structure and outlines next steps for validation and research linking design-oriented AI practices to instructional quality.

title: Towards the automated segmentation of epicardial and mediastinal fats: A multi-manufacturer approach using intersubject registration and random forest
summary: The amount of fat on the surroundings of the heart is correlated to several health risk factors such as carotid stiffness, coronary artery calcification, atrial fibrillation, atherosclerosis, cancer incidence and others. Furthermore, the cardiac fat varies unrelated to the overall fat of the subject, and, therefore, it reinforces the quantitative analysis of these adipose tissues as being essential. Clinical decision support systems are computer programs capable of evaluating information and providing a corresponding diagnosis or data to complement the physicists' analyses. The aim of this work is to propose a method capable of fully automatically segmenting two types of cardiac adipose tissues that stand apart from each other by the pericardium on CT images obtained by the standard acquisition protocol used for coronary calcium scoring. Much effort was devoted to promote minimal user intervention and ease of reproducibility. The methodology proposed in this work consists of a registration, which will roughly adjust input images to a standard, an extraction of features related to pixels and their surrounding area and a segmentation step based on data mining classification algorithms that define if an incoming pixel is of a certain type. Experimentations showed that the achieved mean accuracy for the epicardial and mediastinal fats was 98.4% with a mean true positive rate of 96.2%. In average, the Dice similarity index was equal to 96.8%.

AI.tldr: Automatic segmentation of two cardiac fat types (epicardial and mediastinal) on CT images for coronary calcium scoring using registration, feature-based pixel analysis, and data-mining classifiers; high accuracy and Dice similarity.
AI.motivation: Cardiac adipose tissue correlates with multiple health risk factors and varies independently from overall body fat, necessitating automated, reproducible quantification to aid clinical decision support.
AI.method: A pipeline consisting of image registration to a standard space, extraction of pixel-level and neighborhood features, followed by a data-mining classifier to label each pixel as a fat type.
AI.result: Mean accuracy for epicardial and mediastinal fats: 98.4%; mean true positive rate: 96.2%; Dice similarity index: 96.8%.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables accurate, automated separation of cardiac adipose tissue compartments on standard coronary calcium scoring CTs, with high overlap to ground truth and low user intervention.

title: VE2VF: Vision-Enabled to Vision-Free Distillation via Real-world Reinforcement Learning for Robust Contact-Rich Manipulation
summary: When using reinforcement learning (RL) for contact-rich robotic manipulation, vision can provide task-relevant information that accelerates learning beyond what proprioception alone can achieve. However, vision-enabled policies tend to overfit to the visual conditions seen during training, limiting their robustness and transferability. We present a human-in-the-loop RL framework that employs teacher-student distillation to achieve robust performance across multiple task variants, trained entirely in the real world without requiring domain randomization or data augmentation. A vision-enabled teacher distills its knowledge into a vision-free student that relies solely on pose, twist, and wrench sensing, combining fast training with strong task generalization. On the real-world NIST assembly benchmark board, our approach achieves 95\% overall success after approximately 50 minutes of training on 3 representative tasks, including robust generalization to 8 unseen task variants. Fine-tuning with distillation achieves full success on the most challenging task. We demonstrate that the resulting policies outperform baselines in both robustness and adaptability.

AI.tldr: A real-world, human-in-the-loop RL framework using teacher-student distillation to obtain robust, vision-free manipulation policies; achieves 95% success on the NIST board across 3 tasks and 8 unseen variants with ~50 minutes of training, outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Vision-based policies often overfit to training visual conditions, reducing robustness and transferability. The goal is robust, transferable manipulation under real-world constraints without heavy data augmentation or domain randomization.
AI.method: Train a vision-enabled teacher in the real world; distill its knowledge into a vision-free student that relies on pose, twist, and wrench sensing; employ a human-in-the-loop RL framework with teacher-student distillation; no domain randomization or augmentation required; fine-tuning with distillation for challenging tasks.
AI.result: Achieves 95% overall success after about 50 minutes of training on 3 representative tasks; demonstrates robust generalization to 8 unseen variants; fine-tuning with distillation yields full success on the most challenging task; outperforms baselines in robustness and adaptability.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that vision-free policies distilled from vision-enabled teachers can retain task knowledge while improving robustness and generalization in real-world, contact-rich manipulation, with efficient training and strong adaptability across variants.

title: Context Distillation as Latent Memory Management
