summary: Context distillation compresses contextual information into model parameters, yet existing methods often ignore how multiple distilled latent memories should be stored, retrieved, and safely activated in non-oracle settings. We formulate context distillation as a latent memory management problem. We distill each context into an independent LoRA adapter, forming a modular memory bank that enables explicit memory selection. Given a query, our framework retrieves candidate memories, routes the query to the most suitable adapter, and uses a Self-Gating mechanism to decide whether latent memory should be activated. To improve efficiency, we further introduce cache sharing to reduce management overhead during inference. Experiments show that our method substantially outperforms baselines with retrieval, while Self-Gating improves robustness by deactivate unnecessary latent memories.

AI.tldr: Proposes a modular memory-bank for context distillation where each context is distilled into an independent LoRA adapter. A retrieval-routing-self-gating pipeline selects and activates relevant memories, with cache sharing to reduce inference overhead; shows improved retrieval performance and robustness over baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of explicit memory management for multiple distilled latent memories in non-oracle settings. Treat context distillation as latent memory management to enable selective storage, retrieval, and activation of memories.
AI.method: Distill each context into independent LoRA adapters forming a modular memory bank. For a given query, retrieve candidate memories, route the query to the most suitable adapter, and use a Self-Gating mechanism to decide whether to activate latent memory. Introduce cache sharing to lower inference overhead.
AI.result: Experiments show substantial improvement over baselines that incorporate retrieval. Self-Gating improves robustness by deactivating unnecessary latent memories.
AI.conclusion: A scalable approach to context distillation via explicit latent memory management, enabling efficient and robust utilization of multiple distilled memories through retrieval, routing, gating, and caching.

title: Differentiable Belief-based Opponent Shaping
summary: Human coordination often relies on the ability to influence the beliefs of others through strategic action. In multi-agent reinforcement learning, opponent shaping attempts to replicate this influence, though existing methods typically operate within an opponent's parameter, policy, or value space. Meanwhile, belief-manipulation techniques in hidden-role games often rely on hard-coded objectives, such as deception or belief saturation. We propose Differentiable Belief-based Opponent Shaping (D-BOS), a first-order method that treats each observer's belief as the shaped opponent state and differentiates through $k$-step softmax-Bayes belief dynamics. Rather than explicitly rewarding deceptive or cooperative behavior, our method treats the belief state as the target for shaping. This allows the optimal strategy to emerge naturally from the environment's reward structure. This belief-space formulation provides an opponent-shaping signal by differentiating through opponent belief updates, and naturally extends to multiple observers by aggregating gradients over their individual inferred belief trajectories. Empirically, D-BOS outperforms PPO and BBM in hidden-role games, with the largest gains in mixed-motive settings.

AI.tldr: D-BOS is a differentiable belief-based opponent shaping method for multi-agent RL that uses observer belief dynamics as shaping targets; it outperforms PPO and BBM in hidden-role games, especially in mixed-motive settings.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap between belief-manipulation in hidden-role games and differentiable ML by treating belief states as controllable objectives; avoid hard-coded deception by letting optimal behavior emerge from environment rewards.
AI.method: Model observers' beliefs as a state to be shaped; differentiate through k-step softmax-Bayes belief updates; a first-order method; aggregate gradients across multiple observers to handle multiple beliefs.
AI.result: Empirically superior to PPO and BBM in hidden-role games; largest gains in mixed-motive settings.
AI.conclusion: Belief-space shaping via differentiating belief updates provides a natural, scalable signal for opponent shaping, enabling effective strategies under standard reward structures.

title: SalsaAgent: A multimodal embodied language model for interactive dance generation
summary: Interaction between humanoids involves bidirectional and nonverbal reactivity, coordination and synchrony. Toward socially aware robots and interactive virtual agents, we present SalsaAgent, a language model that generates expressive, full-body salsa dance motions in reaction to a human leader and against a contextual music backdrop. We formulate interaction as nonverbal motion token passing, extending the vocabulary of a large language model (LLM) to process discrete motion tokens, pairwise relation tokens, and audio. Our contributions include new tokens for full-body and motion relations, LLM fine-tuning using automatically derived text descriptions of skeleton dynamics for token grounding, and a two-stage token-to-diffusion pipeline. Subjective and objective evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in terms of motion quality, music and partner coordination, and consistent two-person spatial behavior, with significant improvements over baselines.

AI.tldr: A SalsaAgent system uses an LLM to generate bidirectional, expressive full-body salsa dance motions in response to a human leader, via nonverbal motion token passing and a token-to-diffusion pipeline, with automatic skeleton-descriptions for grounding; evaluations show improved motion quality and coordination over baselines.
AI.motivation: Enable socially aware robots and interactive agents to engage in coherent nonverbal interaction with humans under music context, by extending LLMs with gesture-oriented tokens and diffusion-based motion synthesis.
AI.method: Introduce new tokens for full-body motion and motion relations; process discrete motion tokens, pairwise relation tokens, and audio; fine-tune the LLM using automatically derived text descriptions of skeleton dynamics for token grounding; implement a two-stage token-to-diffusion pipeline that converts tokens into motion.
AI.result: Subjective and objective evaluations demonstrate improved motion quality, music/partner coordination, and consistent two-person spatial behavior, with significant improvements over baselines.
AI.conclusion: Nonverbal motion token passing with LLM grounding and diffusion-based synthesis is a viable approach to producing expressive, socially aware danceable motions in human-robot interaction.

title: Learning to Feel Materials from Multisensory Tactile Data via Interpretable Models
summary: Human tactile perception of materials relies on complex multisensory touch cues, yet the relationship between low-level tactile signals and perceptual representations remains poorly understood. This knowledge gap hinders the integration of touch in digital environments and the development of robots capable of human-like tactile perception. Here, we present an interpretable computational framework for modeling human material perception and recognition using multisensory touch data. Our framework comprises three interconnected models: Model 1 maps finger-surface interaction features to psychophysical sensory attributes, Model 2 classifies materials based on these perceptual representations, and Model 3 directly classifies materials from tactile features. The results showed that combining information from pressing, static contact, and sliding interactions improves prediction accuracy, and that thermal cues are particularly informative for both perceptual modeling and material classification. These findings highlight the importance of thermal and compliance cues, which remain underrepresented in current robotic fingers and haptic displays. Incorporating such cues may enhance artificial systems' ability to approximate human material perception and guide the design of more perceptually grounded haptic interfaces.

AI.tldr: An interpretable, three-model framework links low-level multisensory tactile signals to perceptual attributes and material categories, highlighting the value of thermal and compliance cues for improved material perception in robotics.
AI.motivation: To elucidate how basic tactile signals translate into perceptual representations for material recognition, enabling more human-like tactile perception in digital environments and robots.
AI.method: Three interconnected models: Model 1 maps finger-surface interaction features to psychophysical sensory attributes; Model 2 uses these perceptual representations to classify materials; Model 3 classifies materials directly from tactile features. The study leverages multisensory touch data (pressing, static contact, sliding) and emphasizes thermal cues.
AI.result: Integrating information across pressing, static contact, and sliding improves prediction accuracy. Thermal cues are particularly informative for both perceptual modeling and material classification. Thermal and compliance cues are underrepresented in current robotic fingers and haptic displays, and incorporating them can enhance perceptually grounded tactile systems.
AI.conclusion: An interpretable, multisensory framework that emphasizes thermal and compliance cues can improve human material perception modeling and material recognition, guiding the design of more perceptually grounded tactile interfaces and robotic sensing.

title: Feature Geometry of LoRA Adapters: A Sparse Autoencoder Analysis of Representational Divergence in Fine-Tuned Language Models
summary:
Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) has emerged as a widely adopted approach for adapting large language models, yet the internal representational changes induced by LoRA fine-tuning remain insufficiently understood. In this work, we investigate the geometry of LoRA-induced representations using Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs). We introduce a delta activation framework that isolates the adapter-specific contribution to the residual stream.
  Using Gemma-2-9B with LoRA ranks 4, 8, 16, and 32, we train adapter-specific SAEs across multiple transformer layers and compare their learned feature spaces with pretrained SAE dictionaries. We evaluate representational alignment using cosine similarity between decoder directions, principal-angle analysis of feature subspaces, and Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) between activation representations.
  Across layers and ranks, we consistently observe comparatively weak geometric alignment between LoRA-induced feature dictionaries and pretrained SAE features. Adapter-specific SAEs also reconstruct delta activations more effectively than pretrained SAEs, suggesting that LoRA updates occupy partially distinct representational structure within the residual stream. Additionally, feature density increases with rank and depth, while geometric divergence remains relatively stable across ranks.
  These findings provide empirical evidence that LoRA fine-tuning can induce feature structures that are not fully captured by pretrained interpretability dictionaries, with implications for mechanistic interpretability, adaptation analysis, and safety auditing of fine-tuned language models.

AI.tldr: LoRA-induced representations diverge from pretrained SAEs, with adapter-specific structures occupying partially distinct subspaces in the residual stream; higher rank/depth increases feature density but not alignment.
AI.motivation: Understand how LoRA fine-tuning reshapes internal representations and whether pretrained interpretability dictionaries capture these changes.
AI.method: Train adapter-specific Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) on Gemma-2-9B with LoRA ranks 4, 8, 16, 32; compare to pretrained SAE dictionaries; evaluate alignment via cosine similarity of decoder directions, principal-angle subspace comparisons, and Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) across multiple transformer layers and ranks.
AI.result: Consistent weak geometric alignment between LoRA-induced feature dictionaries and pretrained SAE features; adapter-specific SAEs reconstruct delta activations more effectively than pretrained SAEs; feature density increases with rank and depth; geometric divergence remains relatively stable across ranks.
AI.conclusion: LoRA fine-tuning can create representational structures not fully captured by pretrained interpretability dictionaries, impacting mechanistic interpretability, adaptation analysis, and safety auditing.

title: Hallucination Mitigation with Agentic AI, Nested Learning, and AI Sustainability via Semantic Caching
summary: Hallucination remains a major reliability barrier for production LLM systems, particularly in multi-agent pipelines where unsupported claims can propagate unchecked across stages. This paper adapts a HOPE-inspired Nested Learning architecture with Continuum Memory Systems (CMS) and semantic similarity caching to a hybrid benchmark of 310 prompts combining 217 epistemic-uncertainty prompts and 93 fabrication-induction stress-test prompts. A three-stage agentic pipeline orchestrated via the Open Floor Protocol (OFP) is evaluated with five KPIs -- FCD (Factual Claim Density), FGR (Factual Grounding References), FDF (Fictional Disclaimer Frequency), ECS (Explicit Contextualization Score), and OSR (Observability Score Ratio) -- aggregated into THS (Total Hallucination Score) across five weighting configurations to study mitigation-observability trade-offs. FDF, ECS, OSR, and FGR are subtracted as mitigation signals, so that a more negative THS indicates stronger mitigation. The FrontEndAgent is configured as a high-stochasticity generator (temperature = 1.0) to produce a realistic hallucination baseline, while the SecondLevelReviewer and ThirdLevelReviewer operate as progressive correctors. This asymmetric design yields end-to-end THS reductions of -31.3% to -35.9% across five weighting configurations. Semantic caching achieves 440 cache hits over 930 potential calls (47.3% hit rate), reducing LLM invocations to 490, lowering energy and CO2e footprint, and making multi-stage review pipelines operationally viable at production scale. ExtremeObservability attains the most negative final THS (-0.0709), confirming that observability-heavy configurations reinforce rather than compromise mitigation. These findings suggest that memory-augmented multi-agent designs can jointly improve factual reliability, operational efficiency, and auditability without model retraining.

AI.tldr: A memory-augmented, multi-agent LLM pipeline using HOPE-inspired Nested Learning with Continuum Memory Systems (CMS) and semantic caching to mitigate hallucinations. Evaluated on 310 prompts (217 epistemic-uncertainty; 93 fabrication-induction stress tests) via a three-stage OFP-driven pipeline. Across five weighting schemes, THS reductions ranged from -31.3% to -35.9%; semantic caching achieved a 47.3% hit rate (440/930 calls), reducing LLM invocations to 490. ExtremeObservability yielded the most negative THS (-0.0709). The results suggest memory-augmented designs can improve factual reliability, efficiency, and auditability without retraining."
AI.motivation: Mitigate hallucinations and improve reliability in production multi-agent LLM systems, where errors propagate across stages, while also reducing compute and enabling auditability without retraining.
AI.method: Three-stage agentic pipeline orchestrated via Open Floor Protocol (OFP). FrontEndAgent operates at high stochasticity (temperature=1.0); SecondLevelReviewer and ThirdLevelReviewer act as progressive correctors. The architecture employs HOPE-inspired Nested Learning with Continuum Memory Systems (CMS) and semantic similarity caching. Evaluation uses 310 prompts (217 epistemic-uncertainty; 93 fabrication-induction stress-test). Five KPIs (FCD, FGR, FDF, ECS, OSR) are combined into THS under five weighting configurations.
AI.result: End-to-end THS reductions between -31.3% and -35.9% across weightings. Semantic caching: 440 cache hits of 930 calls (47.3% hit rate); LLM invocations reduced to 490. Observability-Heavy (ExtremeObservability) yields THS = -0.0709, indicating mitigation benefits. Energy and CO2e footprints are reduced; approach enables production-scale multi-stage review without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Memory-augmented multi-agent designs can jointly improve factual reliability, operational efficiency, and auditability without retraining, with semantic caching contributing to compute and energy savings and high observability further strengthening mitigation.

title: Motion-guided sparse correction enables expert-quality point tracking across diverse microscopy regimes
summary: Tracking the dynamics of non-canonical biological systems in microscopy videos remains a persistent challenge. Both classical and learning-based trackers depend on expert-reviewed data to be evaluated and adapted, yet exhaustive manual annotation rarely scales to the videos where these tools are needed most. We developed RIPPLE (Refinement Interpolation Platform for Point Location Estimation), which recasts annotation as sparse correction: a user clicks a starting point, RIPPLE proposes a full trajectory, and the user intervenes only where the trajectory drifts. We tested RIPPLE on five challenging microscopy datasets from our laboratories, four from the transparent jellyfish Clytia hemisphaerica and one tracking landmarks on rapidly moving sperm. Across these, RIPPLE matched the quality of exhaustive manual annotation while reducing manual clicks by 3 to 25 times across datasets. RIPPLE thereby fills a missing layer between manual annotation and fully automated tracking, enabling immediate quantification of biological dynamics, method benchmarking, and the production of the gold-standard data needed to adapt future automated microscopy trackers.

AI.tldr: RIPPLE reduces manual annotation effort in microscopy tracking by reframing annotation as sparse corrections, producing full trajectories from a single starting point with targeted user edits.
AI.motivation: Manual annotation for tracking in microscopy is time-consuming and scales poorly; a scalable, accurate, and benchmark-ready annotation workflow is needed.
AI.method: Sparse correction platform: user clicks a starting point; RIPPLE generates a full trajectory; user intervenes only where the trajectory drifts; iterative refinement.
AI.result: RIPPLE matched exhaustive manual annotation quality while reducing manual edits by 3–25× across five challenging datasets (four Clytia hemisphaerica jellyfish, one sperm landmark dataset).
AI.conclusion: Bridges the gap between manual and automated tracking, enabling immediate quantification, method benchmarking, and gold-standard data to guide future trackers.

title: How to Relieve Distribution Shifts in Semantic Segmentation for Off-Road Environments
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures. Accepted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L). \c{opyright} 2025 IEEE. Personal use of this material is permitted. Permission from IEEE must be obtained for all other uses
summary: Semantic segmentation is crucial for autonomous navigation in off-road environments, enabling precise classification of surroundings to identify traversable regions. However, distinctive factors inherent to off-road conditions, such as source-target domain discrepancies and sensor corruption from rough terrain, can result in distribution shifts that alter the data differently from the trained conditions. This often leads to inaccurate semantic label predictions and subsequent failures in navigation tasks. To address this, we propose ST-Seg, a novel framework that expands the source distribution through style expansion (SE) and texture regularization (TR). Unlike prior methods that implicitly apply generalization within a fixed source distribution, ST-Seg offers an intuitive approach for distribution shift. Specifically, SE broadens domain coverage by generating diverse realistic styles, augmenting the limited style information of the source domain. TR stabilizes local texture representation affected by style-augmented learning through a deep texture manifold. Experiments across various distribution-shifted target domains demonstrate the effectiveness of ST-Seg, with substantial improvements over existing methods. These results highlight the robustness of ST-Seg, enhancing the real-world applicability of semantic segmentation for off-road navigation.

AI.tldr: ST-Seg proposes an explicit distribution-shift handling mechanism for semantic segmentation in off-road environments through style expansion (SE) and texture regularization (TR). It treats distribution shift as a controllable factor by broadening source styles and stabilizing textures, leading to improved robustness across shifted domains.
AI.motivation: Off-road semantic segmentation faces significant distribution shifts due to domain discrepancies and sensor corruption from rough terrain, which degrade prediction accuracy and navigation reliability. Existing methods largely rely on fixed source distributions and generalization tricks, lacking explicit expansion of style diversity to cover target variations.
AI.method: ST-Seg expands the source distribution via style expansion (SE) to generate diverse realistic styles, augmenting limited source style information. It uses texture regularization (TR) through a deep texture manifold to stabilize local texture representations during style-augmented learning, aiming to reduce sensitivity to texture changes.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple distribution-shifted target domains show substantial improvements over baseline and existing methods, demonstrating enhanced robustness of semantic segmentation for off-road navigation.
AI.conclusion: ST-Seg provides a practical framework to handle distribution shifts in off-road semantic segmentation by explicitly expanding style variations and enforcing texture stability, improving real-world applicability and navigation reliability.

title: Spectral Guidance for Flexible and Efficient Control of Diffusion Models
summary: We introduce Spectral Guidance, a framework for controlling diffusion models by leveraging the intrinsic geometry of the generative process. As data is progressively corrupted by noise, only a small number of features remain informative for control. We characterize them as the singular functions of a conditional expectation operator and show that they can be learned via a self-supervised objective. Once recovered, this basis enables the projection of arbitrary guidance signals, such as labels, CLIP embeddings, or masks, directly onto the sampling trajectory. This approach allows for stable, high-fidelity control without retraining or denoiser backpropagation during sampling. Empirically, we improve conditional accuracy on CIFAR-10 by 37 percentage points over the strongest training-free baseline while offering $4\times$ faster sampling. Moreover, the same representations that support label and CLIP guidance also enable spatial control, such as mask-based guidance, without auxiliary models. Finally, our framework reveals a phase transition in the generative process, pinpointing the optimal time window for effective guidance.

AI.tldr: Spectral Guidance learns a spectral basis from diffusion processes to enable flexible, training-free, high-fidelity control of diffusion models via projection of guidance signals onto the sampling trajectory.
AI.motivation: To enable stable, high-fidelity, training-free control of diffusion models by exploiting the intrinsic geometry of the generative process.
AI.method: Identify singular functions of a conditional expectation operator corresponding to the noisy data; learn this basis via self-supervised objective; project arbitrary guidance signals onto sampling trajectory using this basis; avoid retraining or backprop during sampling; enable both label/CLIP guidance and spatial mask guidance; analyze phase transition to identify optimal guidance window.
AI.result: Achieves 37 percentage-point improvement in conditional accuracy on CIFAR-10 over training-free baseline; 4x faster sampling; supports mask-based guidance without extra models; reveals optimal time window for guidance.
AI.conclusion: Spectral Guidance provides a principled, efficient framework for controllable diffusion using learned spectral basis, enabling flexible guidance and stable sampling with improved performance and speed, and suggests a phase-transition-based guidance window.

title: Robust and Efficient Guardrails with Latent Reasoning
summary: Maintaining the safety of large language models (LLMs) is crucial as they are increasingly deployed in real-world applications. Existing safety guardrails typically rely on single-pass classification or, more recently, distilled reasoning. Reasoning-based guardrails significantly outperform classification-only baselines, but they incur substantial query latency and token overhead that make them impractical for highthroughput deployment. To address this challenge, we propose COLAGUARD, a guardrail model that transfers multi-step safety reasoning into a continuous latent space through a stage-wise training curriculum, enabling direct hidden-state propagation at inference. Evaluated on ten prompt- and response-moderation settings spanning eight safety benchmarks, COLAGUARD improves macro-F1 by 8.24 points over Llama Guard 3 and matches our explicit reasoning baseline, GuardReasoner, in macroF1 while delivering a 12.9X speedup and 22.4X reduction in token usage. Our results suggest that latent reasoning offers a practical alternative to explicit rationale generation for deployable guardrails, jointly improving safety robustness and inference efficiency rather than treating them as competing objectives.

AI.tldr: COLAGUARD transfers multi-step safety reasoning into a latent space to enable fast inference, achieving comparable safety to explicit reasoning approaches with large throughput gains.
AI.motivation: Current safety guardrails rely on single-pass classification or explicit reasoning; reasoning-based approaches improve safety but incur high latency and token cost, hindering deployment in high-throughput settings.
AI.method: Stage-wise training curriculum that embeds multi-step safety reasoning into a continuous latent representation, enabling hidden-state propagation during inference and avoiding explicit rationale generation.
AI.result: COLAGUARD yields a macro-F1 improvement of 8.24 over Llama Guard 3, matches GuardReasoner in macro-F1, and provides 12.9x speedup with 22.4x fewer tokens across ten prompt/response moderation settings spanning eight benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Latent reasoning is a practical and efficient alternative to explicit rationale generation for deployable guardrails, improving both safety robustness and inference efficiency.

title: An Approach for Thyroid Nodule Analysis Using Thermographic Images
summary: Thyroid cancer is said to be the second most common type of cancer in female individuals and the third in males by 2030, according to projections. In general, detecting cancer in its early stages improves the chance of survival of the individual. Thermography is a diagnostic tool that has been increasingly used to detect cancer and abnormalities, including that of thyroid. Various methods to segment and detect hot regions in thermograms and, consequently, to detect suspicious tissues present in these images have been proposed. It is well known that medical diagnosis yields a great deal of information. Thus, physicians have to comprehensively analyse and evaluate this information in a short period of time, which is infeasible in most cases. In this work, we perform a general review of thermography , focusing on the thyroid analysis. We propose protocols for image acquisiton and an autonomous registration for thyroid images. We also perform analyses of the image data, which include feature extraction, image processing, and a possible approach for classification of healthy or unhealthy patients. In summary, this work presents a pilot project for detection of tumors in our university hospital, which is part of an effort to support preventive medical actions in our endocrinology department. Under some future adjustments, this project will be submitted for approval by the ethics and research committee of Hospital Universitário Antonio Pedro at Universidade Federal Fluminense (HUAP-UFF) and to the Brazilian Ministry of Health Ethical committee under the name: Evaluation of the importance of thermography to aid diagnosis of thyroid nodules of patients in HUAP-UFF (in Portuguese: Avaliação da importância da termografia no auxílio à investigação diagnóstica de nódulos tireoidianos em pacientes acompanhados no HUAP-UFF).

AI.tldr: A narrative review and pilot study on thyroid thermography, outlining acquisition protocols, autonomous registration, feature extraction/processing, and a proposed classification framework for healthy vs unhealthy patients, with a pilot deployment at a Brazilian university hospital to support preventive care.
AI.motivation: Early cancer detection improves survival; thermography is increasingly used for thyroid abnormalities. There is a need for standardized imaging protocols, automated image registration, and efficient data analysis pipelines to aid clinicians facing information overload, along with ethics-compliant evaluation in a clinical setting.
AI.method: General literature review on thermography for thyroid analysis; proposal of image acquisition protocols; proposal of autonomous registration for thyroid thermograms; analysis of image data including feature extraction and processing; discussion of a possible classifier to distinguish healthy from unhealthy patients; description of a pilot project for tumor detection at HUAP-UFF with plans for ethical approvals.
AI.result: The paper provides a conceptual framework and a pilot implementation outline, including proposed protocols and analytic steps; no quantitative results are reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Thermography could support thyroid nodule evaluation if standardized acquisition/registration and robust analytics are in place; the pilot project aims to translate this into preventive clinical practice pending ethical approvals.

title: VLAConf: Calibrated Task-Success Confidence for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Confidence estimation for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models is essential for robots to perform manipulation tasks in the open world, providing crucial signals for risk-sensitive decision-making and failure anticipation. Existing confidence estimation methods typically rely on ensemble-based paradigms or action-token probabilities to predict the likelihood of task success. However, they still encounter challenges in computational efficiency and cross-architecture generalizability. These methods usually require repeated sampling, leading to inference inefficiency, and are restricted to VLA models with discrete action outputs, making them difficult to apply to continuous action spaces. To address this issue, we propose VLAConf, a one-class discriminative confidence framework. By leveraging frozen pretrained VLA internal representations, VLAConf directly estimates step-wise anomaly scores in a single forward pass using a lightweight confidence head, thereby eliminating the overhead of exhaustive resampling. We additionally use step-conditioned modeling to encode rollout-phase information along the manipulation trajectory. Experiments on the LIBERO benchmark demonstrate that VLAConf significantly improves the quality of the confidence signal constructed for post-hoc calibration, outperforming existing baselines by a large margin in inference efficiency. The effectiveness of VLAConf is further validated in real-robot experiments. To access the source code and supplementary videos, visit https://sites.google.com/view/vlaconf.

AI.tldr: VLAConf introduces a one-class discriminative confidence estimator for Vision-Language-Action models that achieves efficient, single-pass confidence estimation using frozen VLA representations and step-conditioned rollout information, improving calibration quality and inference efficiency on LIBERO and in real-robot tests.
AI.motivation: Confidence estimation is critical for risk-aware robotic manipulation in open-world settings. Existing ensemble or action-probability methods are computationally expensive and struggle to generalize to continuous action spaces, hindering real-time deployment.
AI.method: VLAConf uses frozen pretrained VLA representations and a lightweight confidence head to produce step-wise anomaly scores in a single forward pass. It incorporates step-conditioned modeling to encode rollout-phase information along the manipulation trajectory, adopting a one-class discriminative framework to detect anomalies without heavy sampling.
AI.result: On the LIBERO benchmark, VLAConf substantially improves the quality of the confidence signal for post-hoc calibration and achieves higher inference efficiency than baselines. Real-robot experiments validate its effectiveness; source code is provided.
AI.conclusion: VLAConf offers an efficient, architecture-agnostic confidence estimation approach for VLA models, enabling better risk-sensitive decision-making in manipulation tasks and extending applicability to continuous action spaces through single-pass anomaly scoring.

title: Sequential Physics-Constrained Neural Operator Forward Modeling for the $\textit{Norne}$ Reservoir System
comment: 22 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Code available at https://github.com/clementetienam/physicsnemo/tree/801a85bc08aa9caa0d54027a145b88c68e5e5f36/examples/reservoir_simulation/norne
summary: We develop a comprehensive mathematical and computational framework for sequential surrogate modeling of three-phase black-oil reservoir dynamics using neural operators, with particular emphasis on Fourier Neural Operators (FNO) and their physics-informed variant (PINO). The application focus is the Norne benchmark reservoir, defined on a heterogeneous $46\times112\times22$ grid ($N=113,344$ cells), with a production history spanning $T=30$ timesteps covering 3298 days. Our theoretical contributions are organized around four interlocking problems: (1) functional-analytic formulation in a product-Sobolev-space setting, including well-posedness of the implicit timestep map and sharp local Lipschitz estimates; (2) covariate shift quantification, proving that the Wasserstein-2 distance grows as $W_2 \leq \varepsilon(L^n-1)/(L-1)$, with exponential population-risk discrepancy for $L>1$; (3) physics-constrained spectral stability, showing PINO training with $λ_R \geq λ^*_R$ reduces the learned Jacobian spectral radius to $ρ_F + Cλ_R^{-1/2}$, yielding uniform-in-time rollout error $|δ_n| \leq \varepsilon/(1-ρ)$; and (4) $K$-step TBPTT gradient analysis, deriving geometric bias decay $O(ρ^K)$, optimal window $K^ = O(\log(T/σ^2))$, and Adam convergence $O(1/\sqrt{t}) + O(ρ^{K^*})$. Empirical validation confirms all theoretical predictions: autoregressive PINO surrogates sustain $R^2>0.99$ (oil), $R^2>0.90$ (gas), $R^2\approx 0.80$ (pressure), and monotonically improving $R^2$ (water) across the full 3298-day horizon, trained on eight NVIDIA B200 GPUs in under one hour. A 1000-member ensemble runs in under one minute on a single B200 GPU, giving a ${\sim}10^4\times$ wall-clock speedup over the OPM finite-volume simulator.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed neural-operator surrogate framework (FNO/PINO) for sequential three-phase black-oil reservoir dynamics on the Norne benchmark, with rigorous theory (well-posedness, covariate shift, spectral stability, TBPTT) and strong empirical validation including large speedups and high predictive accuracy across oil, gas, pressure, and water states.
AI.motivation: To enable accurate, stable, and fast sequential forecasting of computationally expensive three-phase reservoir simulations, addressing nonlinearity, heterogeneity, and long-time horizons while mitigating covariate shift and ensuring physics-consistency through PINO regularization.
AI.method: Develop a four-part framework: (1) functional-analytic formulation in product-Sobolev spaces with well-posed implicit timestep maps; (2) covariate-shift quantification with Wasserstein-2 bounds; (3) physics-constrained spectral stability showing PINO reduces Jacobian spectral radius and yields uniform-in-time errors; (4) K-step TBPTT gradient analysis for bias decay and convergence, coupled with TBPTT-accelerated training of FNO/PINO surrogates on Norne (46×112×22 grid, T=30, 3298 days). Empirical evaluation uses eight NVIDIA B200 GPUs; ensembles of 1000 members achieve massive wall-clock speedups over OPM finite-volume simulator.
AI.result: Theoretical anchors: W2 covariate-shift bound with exponential population-risk for L>1; spectral-stability bound λ_R≥λ_R^* leading to ρ_F+O(λ_R^{-1/2}) bound and uniform rollout error; TBPTT bias decays geometrically with rate ρ^K; optimal window K*=O(log(T/σ^2)); Adam convergence O(1/√t) + O(ρ^{K*}). Empirically: autoregressive PINO attains R^2>0.99 (oil), >0.90 (gas), ~0.80 (pressure), improving R^2 (water) across 3298 days; 8-GPU training in under 1 hour; 1000-member ensemble in under 1 minute on a single GPU; ~10^4× speedup versus OPM finite-volume simulator.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, theoretically grounded, physics-informed neural-operator surrogate framework for long-horizon, three-phase reservoir dynamics that delivers high predictive accuracy, strong stability guarantees, and dramatic runtime speedups, making large-ensemble forecasting feasible.

title: Bridging the Sim-to-Real Gap in Reinforcement Learning-Based Industrial Dispatching through Execution Semantics
comment: Accepted for publication at the 24th IEEE International Conference on Industrial Informatics (INDIN 2026), held from 26 to 29 July 2026 in Melbourne, Australia
summary:
Event-driven scheduling policies are increasingly deployed in industrial environments, where decisions are made under asynchronous and partially observed system states. As a result, decision states are not temporally consistent, action admissibility is not explicitly defined, and the origin of execution errors remains ambiguous. These issues limit both reliability and interpretability.
  To address this gap, a policy-neutral execution and measurement layer is proposed to mediate between scheduling policies and the industrial execution environment. The layer constructs decision-valid snapshots from asynchronous event streams, defines a standardized execution contract with explicit action admissibility, and records outcomes as divergences between policy intent, transactional outcomes, physical execution, and human intervention. This enables a separation between decision semantics and execution behavior and makes deployment mismatch observable and structurally attributable.
  The proposed framework is evaluated using a discrete-event simulation. The results show analytical benefits across all observation lag regimes, as undifferentiated execution failures are transformed into structured, typed outcomes with full attribution coverage. Operational benefits are strongest under low observation lag, where avoidable execution errors can be prevented before commitment. Overall, the layer turns execution uncertainty into supervisory data for evaluation and policy refinement.

AI.tldr: A policy-neutral execution/measurement layer mediates between scheduling policies and the industrial environment, enabling decision-valid snapshots, explicit action admissibility, and structured attribution of divergences; validated via discrete-event simulation with improvements in interpretability and reliability, especially at low observation lag.
AI.motivation: To address gaps in event-driven scheduling under asynchronous/partially observed states, where decision states lack temporal consistency, action admissibility is not defined, and execution errors have ambiguous origins, harming reliability and interpretability.
AI.method: Propose a policy-neutral execution/measurement layer that mediates between policies and the industrial environment; constructs decision-valid snapshots from asynchronous event streams; defines a standardized execution contract with explicit action admissibility; records outcomes as divergences among policy intent, transactional outcomes, physical execution, and human intervention; evaluate the framework with a discrete-event simulation.
AI.result: Across all observation lag regimes, undifferentiated execution failures are transformed into structured, typed outcomes with full attribution; operational benefits are strongest at low observation lag where preventable errors are avoided before commitment; the layer turns execution uncertainty into supervisory data for evaluation and policy refinement.
AI.conclusion: The layer enables separation between decision semantics and execution behavior, making deployment mismatch observable and structurally attributable, and providing a basis for evaluation, attribution, and policy refinement.

title: Toward Ethical Facial Age Estimation: A Generalized Zero-Shot Benchmark Without Training on Children's Data
summary: Age estimation from facial images typically relies on training data that includes images of minors, a practice that raises serious ethical, legal, and privacy concerns. In this work, we propose a generalized zero-shot benchmark for facial age estimation that explicitly excludes children's data during training while still assessing model performance on younger populations. We revisit six widely used datasets and introduce standardized splits with strict age-group separation: samples aged 18-59 for training, validation, and testing; samples under 18 reserved exclusively for zero-shot evaluation; and samples 60+ as an unseen validation set for model selection under distribution shift. For datasets with identity annotations, subject-exclusive splits prevent identity leakage and better reflect real-world deployment conditions. Evaluating nine state-of-the-art age estimation methods under this protocol reveals that all evaluated methods consistently fail to generalize to unseen age groups, suffering substantial performance degradation -- on average 46.4%, and up to 52.8% -- relative to the supervised baseline. Moreover, models do not simply degrade: they systematically anchor predictions for unseen ages to nearby seen classes, a manifestation of the well-known seen-class bias in generalized zero-shot learning. By formalizing age estimation without children's data as a generalized zero-shot benchmark on existing datasets, this work highlights a critical gap between current modeling practices and real-world ethical constraints. Our benchmark provides a principled basis for evaluating models under restricted data regimes and encourages the development of methods that are robust to distribution shift and aligned with responsible data use.

AI.tldr: A generalized zero-shot benchmark for facial age estimation excluding children's data reveals poor generalization to unseen ages and a pronounced seen-class bias; proposes standardized data splits and evaluation protocol.
AI.motivation: Ethical, legal, and privacy concerns motivate removing minor data from training and evaluating performance on younger populations under a controlled protocol.
AI.method: Revisit six widely used datasets; introduce standardized splits with strict age-group separation: 18-59 for training/validation/testing; under-18 reserved exclusively for zero-shot evaluation; 60+ as unseen validation set for model selection under distribution shift; subject-exclusive splits for datasets with identity annotations to prevent leakage; evaluate nine state-of-the-art age estimation methods under this protocol.
AI.result: All evaluated methods fail to generalize to unseen age groups, with substantial performance degradation averaging 46.4% and up to 52.8%; predictions for unseen ages anchor near seen classes, indicating a seen-class bias typical in generalized zero-shot learning.
AI.conclusion: Formalizes age estimation without children data as a generalized zero-shot benchmark on existing datasets; highlights a gap between current modeling practices and ethical constraints; provides a principled basis for evaluating models under restricted data regimes and fosters development of robustness to distribution shift and responsible data use.

title: EXACT-MPPI: Exact Signed-Distance Navigation for Arbitrary-Footprint Robots from Point Clouds via Path Integral Control
summary: Ground robots often carry payloads, implements, or other attachments that turn their effective footprint into complex, non-convex shapes. Navigating safely through clutter then requires reasoning about this true geometry, yet most local planners simplify it with convex or inflated proxies and rasterize sensor data into occupancy grids or distance fields. Both choices eliminate feasible motions when clearance is comparable to the footprint geometry. We present EXACT-MPPI, a training-free local navigation framework that maps local point-cloud observations and sparse guidance directly to motion commands, without any intermediate map representation. The framework embeds an analytic, exact signed-distance evaluator into a Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) controller. The footprint is represented as a simple polygon for general convex or concave planar shapes, with a rectangle-cover specialization for faster evaluation of rectilinear footprints, enabling footprint-aware collision costs without convex decomposition, inflation, or learned encoders. During each MPPI rollout, observed obstacle points are transformed into the predicted body frame and evaluated against the footprint. All operations are batched in JAX, leveraging GPU parallelism for real-time receding-horizon control. Experiments show that EXACT-MPPI accelerates batched distance evaluation over a learned point-to-robot baseline, preserves feasible motion where convex-footprint planners fail, and remains robust under dense static and moving obstacles. The same framework deploys on differential-drive, Ackermann, omnidirectional, and hybrid-mode platforms by changing only the footprint description and motion model without per-platform training. Pairing exact footprint geometry with sampling-based predictive control thus offers a practical, training-free path to footprint-aware local navigation across diverse robots.

AI.tldr: Introduces EXACT-MPPI, a training-free local navigation framework that uses exact signed-distance evaluation of complex footprints within an MPPI controller, enabling footprint-aware, map-free navigation across diverse robot platforms.
AI.motivation: Conventional local planners rely on convex proxies or occupancy-based maps, which can discard feasible motions when footprint geometry is non-convex or clearance is tight; a footprint-aware, training-free approach is needed to enable robust, real-time navigation across varied robots.
AI.method: Embed an analytic exact signed-distance evaluator into a Model Predictive Path Integral controller. Represent the footprint as a simple polygon (with a rectangle-cover specialization for rectilinear shapes). During each MPPI rollout, transform observed obstacle points into the predicted body frame and evaluate them against the footprint. All operations are batched in JAX for GPU acceleration. No intermediate map representations or learning required. The approach adapts to different platforms by changing only the footprint description and motion model.
AI.result: The method accelerates batched distance evaluation compared to a learned point-to-robot baseline, preserves feasible motions where convex-footprint planners fail, and remains robust under dense static and moving obstacles.
AI.conclusion: Pairing exact footprint geometry with sampling-based predictive control provides a practical, training-free path to footprint-aware local navigation that applies across diverse robots without per-platform training.

title: Cycle-Space Informed Detection of Autoencoded Blind False Data Injection Attacks on Power Systems
summary: The rapid growth of AI-driven data centers and large-scale energy storage systems is increasing the reliance of power system operation on real-time measurement data and automated decision-making. However, many existing detection methods rely on statistical or data-driven analysis of measurements and can fail when attackers exploit the same data structure to craft stealthy perturbations. To illustrate this limitation, we demonstrate a blind False Data Injection Attack (FDIA) in which an Autoencoder learns the measurement manifold and generates perturbations aligned with the Jacobian null space, thereby allowing the attack to evade both residual-based baddata detectors and time-series anomaly detectors. To mitigate data-driven FDIAs which exploit the null space, we propose a topology-informed Cycle-Space Detector (CSD) that leverages the Cycle-Space of the network to impose structural constraints that enhance null space estimation. In addition, we prove that by using the Minimum Cycle Basis (MCB), the proposed CSD achieves the optimal generalization error for attack detection. By exploiting topology-derived cycle constraints rather than relying solely on numerical null space estimation, the proposed method does not require precise line parameters and improves the separation between normal and attacked measurements. Simulation results on IEEE 14-, 30-, 57-, and 118-bus systems demonstrate that the proposed method effectively detects data-driven FDIAs under realistic measurement noise.

AI.tldr: Topology-informed Cycle-Space Detector (CSD) using Minimum Cycle Basis to detect data-driven false data injection attacks in power systems; reduces reliance on precise line parameters by leveraging network cycle constraints.
AI.motivation: FDIAs can exploit data structures to evade conventional residual/time-series detectors. A topology-aware detector aims to impose structural constraints via cycle space to improve null-space estimation and detection robustness without requiring exact line parameters.
AI.method: Construct the network's cycle-space and apply a Minimum Cycle Basis (MCB) to impose topology-derived constraints. The detector uses these constraints to identify perturbations aligned with the Jacobian null space but restricted by cycle structure, enabling improved separation between normal and attacked measurements. Evaluation on IEEE 14-, 30-, 57-, and 118-bus systems under realistic measurement noise.
AI.result: Demonstrates effective detection of data-driven FDIAs under measurement noise; enhanced discrimination between attacked and normal data across multiple IEEE test systems without relying on precise line parameters.
AI.conclusion: Topology-derived cycle constraints, particularly via MCB, yield improved generalization for attack detection and reduce dependence on exact network parameters, offering robust FDIAdetection against data-driven perturbations.

title: The Importance of Out-of-Band Metadata for Safe Autonomous Agents: The Redpanda Agentic Data Plane
comment: 6 pages, 1 figure. Published at SAO '26 (co-located with ACM CAIS '26)
summary:
AI agents are increasingly expected to operate as digital employees: accessing enterprise data, making decisions, and taking actions autonomously. But agents are simultaneously less predictable than humans -- prone to hallucination, misinterpretation, and adversarial manipulation -- and more technically capable: with deep system knowledge and high-throughput interfaces cascading damage at machine speed. This combination makes it unsafe to rely on agents to faithfully interpret or propagate security-critical metadata such as access policies, data classifications, and behavioral constraints.
  We present the Redpanda Agentic Data Plane (ADP), an architecture built around out-of-band metadata channels: infrastructure pathways that carry security context, policy signals, and audit trails deterministically, entirely outside the agent's read and write path and across heterogeneous infrastructure. These channels enforce governance at every stage of the agent lifecycle -- scoping data access on the way in, constraining actions during execution, and capturing tamper-proof transcripts on the way out.
  We demonstrate ADP with a multi-agent portfolio rebalancing system in which autonomous agents monitor markets, make trade decisions, and execute orders across isolated client accounts -- with per-client data scoping, trade approval thresholds, and tamper-proof audit trails all enforced by out-of-band channels the agents can neither see nor bypass.

AI.tldr: ADP architecture using out-of-band metadata channels to enforce security governance for autonomous AI agents, preventing leakage and tampering of policies while enabling safe multi-agent operations.
AI.motivation: Mitigate safety and security risks from autonomous agents (hallucination, misinterpretation, adversarial manipulation) by isolating security metadata from agents’ read/write paths.
AI.method: Propose Redpanda Agentic Data Plane (ADP) that uses out-of-band metadata channels carrying security context, policy signals, and audit trails; channels operate outside the agent’s data path to scope data access, constrain actions, and capture tamper-proof transcripts. Demonstrated via a multi-agent portfolio rebalancing system with per-client data scoping and trade-approval thresholds.
AI.result: Demonstration that agents are constrained by out-of-band channels, enabling per-client data scoping, enforceable trade-approval thresholds, and tamper-proof audit trails that agents cannot see or bypass.
AI.conclusion: Out-of-band metadata channels provide deterministic governance across the agent lifecycle, reducing risk from autonomous agents and enabling safer deployment; architecture is applicable beyond the financial domain.

title: Deep Psychovisual Image Representations
summary: Psychovisual models suggest human vision decouples low-level feature extraction from higher cognition by first forming intermediate abstractions. In contrast, deep learning-based vision models routinely extract and aggregate features using homogeneous stacks of spatial layers, rendering their decision-making processes opaque. In this paper, we propose Deep Visual Coding, a learned frequency-domain representation inspired by 1990s image codes that quantised perceptually salient frequencies, which together with complex-valued image representations produces psychovisual-style abstractions. This approach enables the first psychovisual-based deep learning framework, utilizing data-driven spectral filters that learn to encode task-relevant semantic structures within distinct frequency sub-bands. Salience analyses reveal that our psychovisual models extract highly interpretable object parts compared to the amorphous regions produced by regular Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). Furthermore, we find that our models are less depth dependent than CNNs for model scaling, since our complex-valued representations and learned abstractions subsume the role of the deep spatial layers. Together, these findings demonstrate that psychovisual coding provides a promising path toward more efficient and transparent vision models.

AI.tldr: A psychovisual-inspired deep learning framework (Deep Visual Coding) uses learned frequency-domain representations and complex-valued images to create interpretable, semantically structured features, reducing depth-dependence and improving transparency over standard CNNs.
AI.motivation: Address the opacity of deep CNNs by incorporating psychovisual principles to form intermediate abstractions via frequency-domain representations that align with perceptual salience.
AI.method: Propose Deep Visual Coding with learned spectral filters operating in the frequency domain and complex-valued image representations; quantize perceptually salient frequencies; train to encode task-relevant semantic structure within distinct frequency sub-bands; perform salience analyses and compare to CNN baselines; assess depth dependence.
AI.result: Models exhibit psychovisual-style abstractions with interpretable object parts; spectral filters learn task-relevant semantics; complex-valued representations and learned abstractions reduce reliance on deep spatial layers, yielding more efficient and transparent vision models.
AI.conclusion: Psychovisual coding offers a promising path toward more efficient and transparent vision systems by integrating perceptual salience into learned frequency-domain representations.

title: FLIP: Real-Time and Resilient Formation Planning for Large-Scale DIstributed Swarms via Point Cloud Registration
summary: Traditional large-scale formation planning either oversimplify the formation representation which leads to poor performance, or they employ complete collaborative relationships, which results in excessive computational load. To achieve high-performance and large-scale formation planning, we transform the Optimal Formation Position Sequence \cite{c1} (OFPS) calculation problem into a spatiotemporal Point Cloud Registration (PCR) problem. Each agent derives its OFPS by distributively computing the matching result between current positions and the desired formation positions of all other agents. Then each agent optimizes the cooperative formation trajectory by using OFPS. We leverage the PCR method with outlier rejection to rapidly perform large-scale formation position registration. This prevents suboptimal trajectories and failed agents from propagating through the cooperative network and affecting more agents. Consequently, we uniformly achieve resilient, efficient, and distributed trajectory planning for large-scale swarms. The effectiveness and the superiority of the proposed method are demonstrated through large-scale simulations of 120-drone formation, and rigorous benchmarking against state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods.

AI.tldr: Recast OFPS as a spatiotemporal Point Cloud Registration (PCR) problem with distributed, outlier-robust matching to enable scalable, resilient formation planning for large swarms; validated on 120-drone simulations with superior performance vs SOTA.
AI.motivation: Address the trade-off between overly simplistic formation representations (poor performance) and fully collaborative approaches (high computational load), aiming for high-performance, large-scale formation planning.
AI.method: Formulate OFPS as a spatiotemporal PCR problem. Each agent distributively matches its current positions to the desired formation positions of all agents, uses PCR with outlier rejection to rapidly register large-scale formations, and derives cooperative trajectories from the registration results.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness and superiority over SOTA via large-scale simulations (120 drones) and rigorous benchmarking, showing resilient, efficient, and distributed trajectory planning.
AI.conclusion: PCR-based, outlier-robust distributed framework enables high-performance, scalable trajectory planning for large-scale swarms, preventing propagation of suboptimal trajectories and supporting large-scale deployment.

title: When LLM Reward Design Fails: Diagnostic-Driven Refinement for Sparse Structured RL
summary: For sparse, structured reinforcement-learning tasks with semantic reward-function interfaces, LLM-generated reward shaping is better framed as debugging than one-shot generation. We study PPO-trained agents using MiniGrid as core evaluation and MuJoCo as boundary stress test. Our audit finds two dominant one-shot failure modes -- reward flooding and semantic/API misunderstanding -- plus a rarer weak-shaping case. We propose diagnostic-driven iterative refinement, where training diagnostics and a failure-mode taxonomy guide targeted reward-function revision. Refinement improves DoorKey-8x8 from 2.3% to 97.6% and KeyCorridor from 31.2% to 86.7% with high seed-to-seed variance. Controls show these gains are not from retrying or extra training: metrics-only re-prompting yields large drops, while a static-vocabulary control recovers much of the gap (87.6%; 70.7%), showing the taxonomy prompt is a major mechanism and dynamic labels provide only partially isolated incremental evidence. Budget-matched and Best-of-3 comparisons separate refinement from selection and training-time effects. Component-removal tests, sensitivity analyses, and an audit against author labels provide converging evidence for the debugging interpretation while revealing calibration limits. Continuous-control results show the boundary: success-based diagnostics can misfire in dense-reward locomotion, and return-trend feedback removes one false-positive mechanism without robust gains. The low-call protocol is a cost contrast with population-based reward search, not a benchmark comparison. In four crossed-variance-design environments, point estimates suggest larger gains when LLM reward-function variance dominates but bootstrap intervals are wide. The method is bounded to sparse structured tasks with reliable interfaces under PPO; fields like event_text may help, hurt, or be neutral.

AI.tldr: LLM-based reward shaping for sparse, structured RL is best framed as debugging rather than one-shot generation; diagnostic-driven iterative refinement with a failure-mode taxonomy yields large performance gains in PPO-driven MiniGrid and MuJoCo tasks, though gains are seed- and calibration-sensitive and bounded by task sparsity and interface reliability.
AI.motivation: Address failures in one-shot LLM reward shaping for sparse, structured reinforcement learning. The work identifies dominant failure modes (reward flooding, semantic/API misunderstanding) and proposes diagnostic-driven iterative refinement guided by a taxonomy of failure modes to produce robust reward functions.
AI.method: Audit PPO-trained agents on MiniGrid and MuJoCo; identify one-shot failure modes; develop diagnostic-driven iterative refinement using a failure-mode taxonomy to revise reward functions; evaluate on DoorKey-8x8 and KeyCorridor with multiple controls (metrics-only re-prompting, static vocabulary, etc.); conduct budget-matched and Best-of-3 comparisons; perform component-removal tests, sensitivity analyses, and author-label audits; test continuous control; examine cross-variance designs across four environments.
AI.result: DoorKey-8x8 improved from 2.3% to 97.6%; KeyCorridor from 31.2% to 86.7% with substantial seed-to-seed variance. Gains largely attributable to taxonomy-guided prompts rather than retraining; static-vocabulary control recovers much of the gap (87.6%; 70.7%), suggesting the taxonomy prompt is a major mechanism; dynamic labels provide partial incremental evidence. Boundaries revealed calibration limits and potential misfires in dense-reward tasks; low-call protocol contrasts with population-based reward search. Four crossed-variance designs show larger gains when LLM reward-function variance dominates, but bootstrap intervals are wide.
AI.conclusion: Framing LLM reward shaping as debugging with diagnostic-driven, taxonomy-guided refinement yields robust improvements for sparse, structured RL under PPO. The approach clarifies calibration limits and the role of dynamic labels and diagnostic prompts, and is applicable to interfaces with sparse signals, subject to task sparsity and interface reliability.

title: Turbulence-Robust Dynamic Object Segmentation with Multi-Signal Priors and SAM2 Refinement
summary:
This technical report presents our solution for the CVPR 2026 UG2+ Challenge Track 3: Dynamic Object Segmentation in Turbulence (DOST). We design a training-free multi-signal segmentation pipeline that combines pretrained motion estimation, self-supervised semantic priors, background anomaly modeling, manually calibrated proposal fusion, and SAM2-based mask refinement. The method uses RAFT for dense motion responses, DINOv2 for semantic objectness priors, ViBe for training-free background modeling, and pretrained SAM2 for box-prompt mask refinement.
  Instead of optimizing an end-to-end segmentation network, our system operates entirely in inference mode. This design is suitable for the DOST setting, where severe atmospheric turbulence produces pseudo-motion, blur, and intermittent target visibility, making a single motion cue unreliable. The final submitted masks are evaluated by the official leaderboard, which reports 0.425041 mIoU and 0.457206 mDice. Since no task-specific model training or fine-tuning is performed, stronger learned temporal association, adaptive proposal selection, or task-specific adaptation may further improve the system.

AI.tldr: Inference-only, training-free multi-signal segmentation for DOST; combines RAFT, DINOv2, ViBe, SAM2; achieves ~0.425 IoU and ~0.457 Dice; notes potential gains from temporal association and task-specific adaptation.
AI.motivation: Handle turbulence-induced pseudo-motion and intermittent visibility without task-specific training.
AI.method: Inference-only pipeline using pretrained motion estimation (RAFT), semantic priors (DINOv2), background modeling (ViBe), and SAM2 mask refinement with manual proposal fusion; no end-to-end optimization.
AI.result: Leaderboard metrics: mIoU 0.425041 and mDice 0.457206; claims no training or fine-tuning; suggests potential improvements with temporal association and adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of a fully inference-time approach for DOST; future gains possible with stronger temporal modeling and adaptation.

title: PhAIL: A Real-Robot VLA Benchmark and Distributional Methodology
comment: 22 pages, 10 figures, 8 tables. Dataset, analysis pipeline, and paper source: https://phail.ai and https://github.com/Positronic-Robotics/phail-paper
summary: Real-world evaluation of vision-language-action (VLA) policies still rests on binary success rate at a fixed timeout with $N \le 25$ rollouts per condition, almost always without confidence intervals or paired statistical comparison; these cohort sizes struggle to resolve close comparisons reliably. We introduce PhAIL (Physical AI Leaderboard, https://phail.ai), an open real-robot benchmark on a Franka FR3 (dataset, per-rollout artifacts, and end-to-end reference implementation) of a distributional evaluation methodology: the time-to-success cumulative distribution function (CDF) as the evaluation primitive, with two separated jobs. The first is scoring via Human-Relative Throughput (HRT), a dimensionless scalar with bootstrap confidence intervals, anchored to same-fixture human teleoperation. The second is a significance test (Kolmogorov-Smirnov, computed per-object and macro-averaged across objects). On four publicly-available VLAs, the macro-averaged KS test resolves two close comparisons (GR00T vs. ACT, OpenPI vs. ACT) at $N \le 30$ rollouts per (model, object) cell where binary-threshold metrics do not; the closest pair (OpenPI vs. GR00T) remains unresolved within our budget. The best evaluated VLA is $\sim 7\times$ slower per operation (RMST ratio) than the human reference.

AI.tldr: PhAIL: a real-robot benchmark for vision-language-action policies using time-to-success CDF with bootstrap CI and KS tests; improves discrimination over binary metrics; best VLA ~7x slower than human.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of VLA policies relies on binary success rates with small sample sizes, lacking confidence intervals or statistical comparisons, which hinders reliable judgments between near-equal methods.
AI.method: Two-stage evaluation: (1) distributional time-to-success CDF; (2) Human-Relative Throughput (HRT) with bootstrap CIs anchored to a fixed human teleoperation baseline; KS significance test per object and macro-averaged across objects.
AI.result: On four VLAs, KS resolves two close comparisons at N ≤ 30 per (model, object); the closest pair remains unresolved within the budget; the best VLA is about 7× slower per operation (RMST ratio) than the human reference.
AI.conclusion: PhAIL provides an open, distributional evaluation framework with artifacts to enable more reliable, statistically grounded comparisons of VLA policies; demonstrates improved resolution over binary metrics, though some comparisons remain indistinguishable at modest rollout budgets.

title: CosmicFish-HRM: Adaptive Reasoning via Hierarchical Recurrent Mechanisms in Compact Language Models
comment: 17 pages, 4 figures. Exploratory study of adaptive reasoning depth in compact autoregressive language models. Code available at https://github.com/MistyozAI/CosmicFish-HRM
summary: Large language models have achieved strong reasoning capabilities, though often at the cost of massive parameter counts and expensive inference. In this work, we explore a different direction: adaptive reasoning depth in compact language models. We present CosmicFish-HRM, a compact language model built around a Hierarchical Reasoning Module (HRM) that dynamically allocates computational effort during inference. Instead of applying fixed computation to every input, the model iterates through high-level and low-level reasoning cycles and learns when to halt based on input complexity. CosmicFish-HRM combines this adaptive reasoning core with modern transformer components including Grouped Query Attention, RoPE, and SwiGLU activations. While the additional reasoning infrastructure introduces overhead at small scale, we hypothesize that this tradeoff becomes increasingly favorable as model size grows and the relative cost of the HRM core diminishes. Our results show that the model learns non-uniform reasoning behavior, allocating different numbers of reasoning steps across tasks and inputs. These findings suggest that adaptive reasoning depth may offer a promising alternative to relying solely on parameter scale for reasoning capability.

AI.tldr: Adaptive reasoning depth in a compact LLM with a Hierarchical Reasoning Module (HRM) that halts dynamically based on input complexity; demonstrates non-uniform reasoning across tasks and inputs, with potential scalability benefits as model size grows.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on massive parameter counts for reasoning by introducing adaptive computational depth in compact language models.
AI.method: CosmicFish-HRM uses a Hierarchical Reasoning Module to alternate high-level and low-level reasoning cycles with a learned halting mechanism sensitive to input complexity; integrates modern Transformer components (Grouped Query Attention, RoPE, SwiGLU).
AI.result: The model learns non-uniform reasoning behavior, allocating different numbers of reasoning steps across tasks and inputs; overhead from the HRM exists at small scale, but the approach may become more favorable as model size increases; supports adaptive reasoning as a viable alternative to pure parameter scaling.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive reasoning depth via HRM in compact LMs is a promising direction, potentially offering scalable advantages and more efficient use of compute by allocating effort where needed rather than uniformly scaling parameters.

title: Trends in AI and Human-AI Interaction in Clinical Trials -- A Hybrid Human-AI Exploration
comment: 8 pages plus 2 pages references and appendix
summary: This paper examines records retrieved from the ClinicalTrials.gov registry to characterize temporal trends in AI terminology and the geographical distribution of AI trials. The work also reports on an exploratory hybrid human-AI approach to analyzing human-AI interaction trends in registered clinical trials. The hybrid workflow comprised a frontier generative AI model (GPT-5.5) and human review to screen and categorize records returned by an AI-focused search. The findings indicate a marked increase in AI-related trials over time, with recent growth in references to machine learning, deep learning, chatbots, GPTs, and large language models. Geographically, China and the United States accounted for the largest numbers of AI-related trials, with notable recent increases in several other countries including Italy, France, Spain, the UK and Turkey (Türkiye). In a random sample of 100 records, human and AI classifiers showed good agreement in identifying studies not substantively using AI, but lower agreement in classifying human-AI interaction, particularly where health professional interaction was ambiguous or insufficiently described. Overall, the results suggest that hybrid human-AI screening of clinical trial records is potentially viable, but clearer trial reporting and more precise interaction definitions will benefit the process.

AI.tldr: The abstract analyzes AI terminology and geographic distribution in ClinicalTrials.gov trials and evaluates a hybrid human-AI workflow for classifying human-AI interaction in trial records.
AI.motivation: Map how AI is described in clinical trial records over time and geography, and assess the feasibility of AI-assisted screening to categorize studies by AI use and human-AI interaction.
AI.method: Data extraction from ClinicalTrials.gov; trend and geographic analyses of AI-related terms (e.g., ML, DL, chatbots, GPTs, LLMs); exploratory hybrid workflow combining a frontier generative AI model (GPT-5.5) with human reviewers to screen and categorize AI-focused records; a random sample of 100 records to compare AI vs. human classifications, focusing on substantive AI usage and human-AI interaction.
AI.result: AI-related trials rise over time; growing references to ML, DL, chatbots, GPTs, and LLMs; China and the US host the largest trial counts, with notable increases in Italy, France, Spain, the UK, and Türkiye; in a 100-record sample, humans and AI agreed well on identifying studies not substantively using AI but showed lower agreement for classifying human-AI interaction when descriptions were ambiguous; hybrid screening appears viable but requires clearer reporting and more precise interaction definitions.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid human-AI screening of clinical-trial records shows promise for scalable analysis, contingent on improved reporting clarity and standardized definitions of human-AI interaction.

title: Pocket-Dentist: On-Device Dental Image Understanding via Efficient Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Evaluations of dental vision-language models remain fragmented across datasets, task definitions and metrics, and often ignore their computational cost. This limits their widespread deployment for dental screening outside specialist centres, where timely inference, limited hardware, and local handling of patient images are vital for practical, privacy-preserving clinical prescreening. Here we present Pocket-Dentist, an efficiency-aware benchmark for dental multimodal question answering that brings together three datasets spanning approximately 1,159 patients, five task types and seven metrics. Across typical 14 VLMs, our results reveals an interesting observation: compact VLMs (e.g., 2B-parameter models) outperform larger VLMs in accuracy while requiring substantially lower computational costs in dental image understanding. Deployed locally on an iPhone 17 Pro, our finetuned compact VLM Pocket-Dentist-2B processed each sample in 4.31 s, reducing latency by 4.9-fold and memory use by 2.3-fold compared with a 7B baseline.

AI.tldr: Pocket-Dentist is an efficiency-aware benchmark for dental multimodal question answering. It shows compact 2B-parameter vision-language models outperform larger models in accuracy while using far less compute, enabling on-device, privacy-preserving prescreening. Deployed on an iPhone 17 Pro, Pocket-Dentist-2B processes samples in 4.31 s, achieving ~4.9× latency reduction and ~2.3× memory reduction versus a 7B baseline.
AI.motivation: Current dental VLM evaluations are fragmented across datasets, tasks, and metrics and often overlook computational cost, hindering deployments for private, on-device dental prescreening with limited hardware.
AI.method: Create a unified efficiency-aware benchmark (Pocket-Dentist) by aggregating three dental datasets (~1,159 patients), five task types, and seven metrics. Evaluate ~14 VLMs; finetune compact models; demonstrate on-device performance (iPhone 17 Pro).
AI.result: Across typical 14 VLMs, compact 2B-parameter models outperform larger models in accuracy while requiring substantially lower computational costs for dental image understanding. Pocket-Dentist-2B processed each sample in 4.31 s on-device, reducing latency by 4.9× and memory usage by 2.3× compared with a 7B baseline.
AI.conclusion: Efficient, compact VLMs can deliver accurate dental multimodal QA with on-device privacy-preserving inference. The benchmark exposes the practical trade-offs between model size, accuracy, and compute, advocating for deployment-ready, efficiency-aware evaluation in dental AI.

title: MARS Policy: Multimodality Only When It Matters
summary: Imitation learning has become a cornerstone for solving complex robotic manipulation tasks. In particular, multimodality, which enables robots to capture diverse yet valid behavioral patterns, has driven the rapid emergence of generative policies as a dominant paradigm in robot learning. However, achieving such multimodality typically relies on stochastic noise initialization and iterative denoising procedures, resulting in substantial training complexity and low inference efficiency. Meanwhile, not all phases of a robotic task inherently require behavioral diversity. Motivated by this insight, we propose the Modality-Adaptive Robot Sampling (MARS) policy, which adaptively invokes tailored stochasticity only when it is truly beneficial, while reverting to an efficient deterministic learning during single-modal phases. In other words, the proper amount of noise is injected only at the proper time. By selectively activating multimodal generation, MARS policy bridges the gap between the multimodal capability of generative policies and the superior training and inference efficiency of deterministic models. Empirical studies across 8 simulated and 4 real-world tasks demonstrate that MARS exhibits robust multimodal expressivity and high efficiency, with a 16.67% success rate improvement and an 83.20% inference latency reduction in real-world tests. Counterintuitively, MARS also outpaces deterministic policies in training efficiency on near-deterministic tasks by more effectively modeling nuanced action diversity.

AI.tldr: A policy (MARS) adaptively injects stochasticity to enable multimodal behavior only when beneficial, achieving multimodal expressivity with near-deterministic efficiency.
AI.motivation: Conventional multimodal generative policies rely on heavy stochasticity and iterative denoising, causing training/inference inefficiency. There is a need to selectively employ multimodality, only during phases where it improves performance.
AI.method: Modality-Adaptive Robot Sampling (MARS): a policy that switches between stochastic multimodal generation and deterministic learning, activating noise only at appropriate times to preserve efficiency while retaining multimodal capabilities.
AI.result: Across 8 simulated and 4 real-world tasks, MARS yields 16.67% higher success rate and 83.20% lower inference latency; it also outperforms purely deterministic policies on near-deterministic tasks by better capturing action diversity.
