AI.conclusion: MARS bridges multimodal expressivity and efficiency by adapting stochasticity over time, improving training efficiency and inference speed without sacrificing performance.

title: Conf-Gen: Conformal Uncertainty Quantification for Generative Models
summary: Conformal prediction (CP) and its extension, conformal risk control (CRC), are established frameworks for quantifying uncertainty in supervised machine learning through formal guarantees. However, recent breakthroughs in artificial intelligence (AI) have been driven by unsupervised generative models, such as large language models (LLMs) and image generators, which are not directly compatible with CP or CRC. In this work we introduce conformal generation (Conf-Gen), a general framework adapting CRC to generative tasks while relaxing its theoretical assumptions. Conf-Gen unifies and generalizes previous attempts to apply CP to LLMs, and extends conformal methodology to entirely new domains. We demonstrate the flexibility of Conf-Gen through some novel applications, including obtaining conformal guarantees on: image generators producing non-memorized images, conversational AI systems having asked enough clarifying questions, and the output of AI agents being correct.

AI.tldr: Introduces Conf-Gen, a framework that adapts conformal risk control to generative models, unifying prior CP work on LLMs and extending conformal guarantees to generation tasks.
AI.motivation: CP/CRC provide formal uncertainty guarantees for supervised learning, but generative AI lacks compatible uncertainty quantification; a unifying, flexible framework is needed for generation tasks.
AI.method: Proposes Conf-Gen, a general framework that relaxes CRC assumptions, unifies previous CP applications to LLMs, and extends conformal methodology to generative domains, including image generation and dialogue.
AI.result: Demonstrates conformal guarantees on non-memorized image generation, conversational AI systems after sufficient clarifying questions, and AI agent outputs being correct.
AI.conclusion: Conf-Gen provides a flexible, general bridge between conformal methods and generative AI, enabling uncertainty quantification and guarantees across diverse generative tasks.

title: Beyond Consensus: Trace-Level Synthesis in Mixture of Agents
summary: When multiple LLM agents solve the same problem, standard practice compresses each agent's reasoning into a majority vote or layered synthesis, treating agreement as the finish line. We show this is unnecessarily lossy: an LLM aggregator that reads complete reasoning traces recovers correct solutions even when agents unanimously agree, with beneficial corrections consistently outweighing harmful ones -- the \emph{aggregation paradox}. Majority voting has a ceiling that perturbation diversity does not raise (error correlations are identical); the aggregator's gain comes from trace-level complementarity, assembling correct intermediate steps from minority chains that voting discards. These findings motivate Self-Consistent Mixture of Agents which generates trace diversity through semantic-preserving input perturbations, safeguards the majority via anchored refinement with provable non-degradation guarantees, and always synthesizes -- never gates on consensus. A single model with perturbation-induced trace variation outperforms heterogeneous model pools across structured reasoning, PhD-level science, competition mathematics, and competitive programming. The unit of aggregation should be the reasoning trace, not the answer.

AI.tldr: Aggregating full reasoning traces from LLMs beats final-answer voting; a single model with trace perturbations (Self-Consistent Mixture of Agents) can outperform heterogeneous pools by leveraging trace-level complementarity and anchored refinement, synthesizing solutions rather than relying on consensus.
AI.motivation: Majority-vote on final answers is lossy due to error correlations and discarded intermediate steps; trace-level complementarity can recover correct solutions.
AI.method: Propose Self-Consistent Mixture of Agents (SC-MoA) that generates diverse traces via semantic-preserving input perturbations, refines with anchored refinement that guarantees no degradation, and synthesizes a final solution from traces; uses a single model to produce perturbation-induced trace diversity.
AI.result: A single model with trace-variation outperforms heterogeneous model pools across structured reasoning tasks, PhD-level science, competition mathematics, and competitive programming.
AI.conclusion: The unit of aggregation should be the reasoning trace, not the final answer; trace-level complementarity enables better solutions; perturbation-induced trace diversity with anchored synthesis yields robust, non-degrading aggregation.

title: ViASNet: A Video Ad Saliency Network for Predicting Dynamic Saliency and Viewer Engagement
summary: The digital media landscape has seen a pervasive shift toward short-form video advertising on TV, social media and e-commerce platforms. The present study focuses on deep saliency prediction for short-form video advertising. Deep saliency models have been used to generate predictions of human eye fixation patterns with the purpose of enhancing user interaction with digital technology and optimizing its design. For video ads, dynamic saliency maps capture where and when viewers are looking, revealing why video ads are effective, and how their content should be optimized. We develop and test a new deep dynamic saliency prediction model called ViASNet (Video Ad Saliency Network), which has an architecture founded on the 3D U-Net, and accommodates the influence of audio and the semantic meaning of scenes. We assess the model's performance on 151 video ads, each seen by about 20 viewers wile their eye movements were tracked, and explore the critical factors influencing model performance through ablation experiments. We calculate the entropy of the predicted saliency maps frame-by-frame as a diagnostic tool to identify ads and scenes that fail to engage viewers, and illustrate its use on test data of 15 unseen ads. Our study reveals that ad design and testing can be sped up considerably through automated systems built on deep saliency models such as ViASNet.

AI.tldr: A deep dynamic saliency model for video ads (ViASNet) extends 3D U-Net to incorporate audio and scene semantics to predict frame-by-frame gaze; evaluated on 151 ads with ~20 viewers each; uses ablation and entropy-based diagnostics to identify engaging ads; suggests automation can accelerate ad design/testing.
AI.motivation: Short-form video ads dominate digital platforms, and predicting dynamic attention can optimize ad design and testing. Deep saliency models can reveal when/where viewers look, guiding content optimization.
AI.method: Introduce ViASNet, a deep dynamic saliency predictor based on a 3D U-Net architecture. The model integrates audio signals and semantic scene information. Evaluation uses eye-tracking data from 151 ads (~20 viewers per ad). Ablation experiments identify key factors affecting performance. Frame-wise entropy of predicted saliency is computed as a diagnostic to flag less engaging ads/scenes. Demonstration on 15 unseen ads.
AI.result: ViASNet demonstrates improved prediction of dynamic gaze patterns in video ads. Ablations reveal the influence of audio and semantic features. Entropy-based diagnostics can flag ads or scenes with low engagement. The approach suggests automation can speed up ad design/test cycles.
AI.conclusion: Deep dynamic saliency models like ViASNet can accelerate ad design and testing by providing automated gaze predictions and engagement diagnostics, with potential for broader multimedia content optimization.

title: Joint Angle Estimation with Customized Wristband Based on Online Incremental Learning
summary: Intelligent wearable technology plays an increasingly important role in human-computer interaction, motion, and health monitoring. To ensure comfort and practicality of use, one common form for motion monitoring is to utilize soft wearable sensors. However, many research applications regarding wearable sensors are simplistic and difficult to adapt to different situations. This study proposes a system for estimating the angle of the wrist joint using a customized wristband based on an online incremental learning approach. It is a two-stage estimation method: the first stage updates the model based on the wearer's wrist movement characteristics using online learning, integrating real-time data from an IMU as ground truth. The second stage utilizes the updated model for estimation of wrist joint angle solely with the wristband. In other words, model training is completed during data acquisition, allowing the trained model to be used for subsequent angle estimation. This method offers advantages in adapting to data drift caused by variations in different testing configurations, such as the left and right wrists of the same subject, deviations in the wearing position on the same wrist, and even differences among various subjects. The results indicate that the sensors exhibit good performance under strain variations, and the wrist joint trajectory estimation of the proposed system has an approximate error of 15 degree in different scenarios.

AI.tldr: Two-stage online incremental learning wristband system for estimating wrist joint angle; trains during data collection and adapts to drift; ~15° error across scenarios.
AI.motivation: To improve adaptability of soft wearable sensors for motion monitoring by addressing data drift and variability across different wearing configurations, wrists, and subjects.
AI.method: Stage 1 online learning updates the model using the wearer's wrist movement characteristics with real-time IMU data as ground truth; Stage 2 uses the updated model to estimate wrist joint angle using only the wristband. Training occurs during data acquisition, enabling the trained model to be used for subsequent angle estimation.
AI.result: The system achieves approximately 15-degree error in wrist joint trajectory estimation across different scenarios and shows good performance under strain variations, with adaptability to left/right wrists, wearing positions, and subject differences.
AI.conclusion: Online on-device learning enables adaptable wrist-worn motion sensing with reduced calibration needs, robust to data drift and inter-subject variation, though the reported accuracy (≈15°) may limit precision-dependent applications.

title: A Training-Time Diagnostic for Generalization via the Log-Alignment Ratio
summary: We study the log-alignment ratio (LAR), a measure of parameter-activation alignment, introduced in parameterization theory. We reformulate it as the overlap between a weight spectrum $p$ of the normalized squared singular values of a matrix and an activation spectrum $q$ of the normalized squared projections of inputs onto its singular directions. We show that unembedding LAR tracks the transition between memorization and generalization in two different settings by capturing the spread of $p$ and $q$ during training. In grokking, LAR predicts the effective dimension of the learned function: $k \approx n^{2(1-\text{LAR})}$, where $n$ is the input dimension of the matrix. In 3B-parameter language model pre-training, its deviation from a non-overfitting baseline tracks the generalization gap, and its rate of decline increases as overfitting approaches. LAR is computable from quantities available during the forward pass with negligible computational overhead, and requires no held-out validation data.

AI.tldr: LAR (log-alignment ratio) is the overlap between the weight spectrum and the activation spectrum, providing a data-free measure that tracks memorization vs. generalization. It predicts an effective dimension in grokking and tracks the generalization gap during 3B-language-model pre-training; computable with forward-pass quantities and no held-out data.
AI.motivation: To quantify parameter-activation alignment in neural networks and gain a data-efficient indicator of when learning shifts from memorization to generalization, across distinct regimes.
AI.method: Define p as the normalized squared singular values spectrum of a weight matrix and q as the normalized squared projections of inputs onto the matrix's singular directions. LAR is the overlap between p and q. Analyze its evolution during training to connect to generalization behavior. Apply in grokking (to predict effective dimension k ≈ n^{2(1−LAR)}) and in 3B-parameter language model pre-training (deviation from non-overfitting baseline tracks generalization gap; the decline rate grows as overfitting increases).
AI.result: LAR captures the spread of p and q during training and unembeds the transition between memorization and generalization. In grokking, k scales as n^{2(1−LAR)}; in large LM pretraining, LAR deviation from a non-overfitting baseline tracks the generalization gap and declines faster as overfitting approaches.
AI.conclusion: LAR is computable from forward-pass quantities with negligible overhead and no validation data, offering a practical, parameter-activation-alignment metric that signals memorization-generalization transitions and correlates with effective model capacity and generalization behavior.

title: PRO-CUA: Process-Reward Optimization for Computer Use Agents
summary: Computer use agents (CUAs) have shown strong potential for automating complex digital workflows, yet their training remains constrained by costly live environment interaction and limited high-quality supervision. Existing filtered behavior cloning pipelines suffer from imitation bottlenecks, including distribution shift from the expert demonstration and the absence of negative learning signals. Meanwhile, standard trajectory-level reinforcement learning struggles with sparse rewards, ambiguous credit assignment, and high infrastructure costs for long-horizon GUI interaction. In this work, we propose PRO-CUA, a process-reward optimization framework for training CUAs with iterative step-level reinforcement learning. PRO-CUA decouples on-policy environment interaction from policy optimization: the current policy collects states through live rollouts, generates diverse candidate actions for each state, receives step-level feedback from a process reward model (PRM), and is optimized with group-relative advantages. This design enables dense and flexible credit assignment without relying on golden answers or offline expert trajectories, while reducing distribution shift by training on the agent's own execution states. Experiments on live web benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of PRO-CUA and the reliability of PRM-guided step-level training.

AI.tldr: PRO-CUA introduces a process-reward optimization framework for computer use agents that trains with step-level reinforcement learning by decoupling live interaction from policy optimization, using a process reward model (PRM) to provide dense feedback.
AI.motivation: Tackle costly live environment interaction, filtered imitation bottlenecks, sparse rewards, and credit assignment challenges in training long-horizon GUI and digital-workflow AU agents.
AI.method: On-policy live rollouts collect states; for each state, generate diverse candidate actions; receive step-level feedback from a process reward model (PRM); optimize the policy with group-relative advantages; train on the agent's own execution states, without relying on golden answers or offline expert trajectories.
AI.result: Experiments on live web benchmarks show PRO-CUA is effective and that PRM-guided step-level training is reliable.
AI.conclusion: PRO-CUA provides dense credit assignment and reduces distribution shift, enabling scalable, offline-free RL for CUAs in real-world, long-horizon tasks.

title: CapTalk: Text-Guided Stylization and Speech-Driven 3D Head Animation
summary: Audio-driven 3D facial animation aims to generate synchronized lip movements and vivid facial expressions from arbitrary audio clips. While existing methods can produce synchronized lip motions, they often rely on predefined identity or style latent features, which limits users' ability to freely control speaking styles. Moreover, applying a fixed style or identity to an entire audio segment typically results in facial animation styles that do not adapt to the emotional content of the audio. To address these challenges, we revisit the entanglement between style and emotion, construct a large-scale dataset with textual descriptions of both style and emotion, and propose a novel talking head generation framework that enables separate control over style and emotion. Our model takes as input both textual descriptions of speaking style and character emotion, as well as the driving audio stream, enabling real-time generation of highly synchronized lip movements and facial expressions that match the provided descriptions. Furthermore, our model supports dynamic emotion control during inference, allowing it to handle scenarios where the target emotion changes throughout the speech.

AI.tldr: A disentangled, text-conditioned talking-head framework for audio-driven 3D facial animation that separately controls speaking style and emotion, enabling real-time lip-sync with dynamic emotional expression via textual descriptors.
AI.motivation: Addresses entanglement between style and emotion in current methods, where predefined style/identity latents limit user control and applying a fixed style to an entire utterance fails to adapt to emotional cues.
AI.method: Construct a large-scale dataset with textual descriptions for both speaking style and character emotion; develop a talking head model that ingests textual style and emotion descriptions plus driving audio, enabling separate style/emotion control and real-time synthesis; supports dynamic emotion changes during inference.
AI.result: Produces highly synchronized lip movements and facial expressions that align with provided style and emotion descriptions; enables real-time generation and dynamic emotion adaptation during speech.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling style and emotion offers flexible, adaptive control for audio-driven facial animation, facilitating user-guided style variation and emotion-responsive expressions.

title: LLM-Guided Future Hypotheses for Horizon-Aware Exploration in Multi-Step Robot Manipulation
summary: Multi-step robot manipulation requires acting under uncertainty about how the scene will evolve, making exploration and policy adaptation challenging. We study whether short-horizon, task-consistent future videos can provide useful structured priors for control and reinforcement-learning fine-tuning. We formalize this idea through Future-Experience Conditioning (FEC), a simple interface that conditions closed-loop policies on a latent representation of a short future video. In our simulation setup, future clips are generated in three stages, an LLM reasoner operating over a task ontology initialized from the current scene state, a robot-free digital-twin rollout of the intended object motion, and a mask-free video diffusion model that synthesizes a robot-consistent future clip without requiring segmentation at inference. We instantiate this future-conditioning interface primarily with BC and BC+RL, and compare against a future-conditioned Streaming Flow Policy (SFP) baseline on RoboCasa and CALVIN under NoFuture, GTFuture, GenFuture, and WrongFuture. Generated futures improve performance over no-future conditioning, while mismatched futures degrade it, and our BC+RL instantiation achieves the strongest overall results. An average BC+RL learning-curve analysis across 8 CALVIN tasks further shows that GTFuture improves fastest, GenFuture improves earlier and to a higher level than NoFuture, and WrongFuture remains at zero throughout training. These results suggest that short-horizon future videos can serve as useful structured priors for exploration and policy adaptation under imperfect future predictions. https://enact2026.github.io/

AI.tldr: Future-Experience Conditioning (FEC) uses short-horizon future videos as structured priors to condition closed-loop policies for multi-step robot manipulation; futures are generated via a three-stage pipeline (LLM ontology, digital-twin rollout, diffusion-based synthesis); evaluated with BC and BC+RL against a Streaming Flow Policy baseline across RoboCasa and CALVIN; matched futures improve performance, wrong futures harm, with BC+RL showing the strongest gains.
AI.motivation: Address uncertainty in scene evolution during multi-step robot manipulation by providing structured priors from short-horizon futures to aid exploration and policy adaptation.
AI.method: Introduce FEC: policies are conditioned on a latent representation of a short future video. Futures are generated in three stages: (1) an LLM reasoner over a task ontology initialized from the current scene; (2) a robot-free digital-twin rollout of the intended object motion; (3) a mask-free video diffusion model that yields a robot-consistent future clip without requiring segmentation at inference. Evaluated by instantiating FEC with BC and BC+RL, compared to a future-conditioned Streaming Flow Policy (SFP) baseline, across NoFuture, GTFuture, GenFuture, and WrongFuture on RoboCasa and CALVIN.
AI.result: Generated futures yield performance gains over no-future conditioning; mismatched futures degrade results, with WrongFuture causing no learning. BC+RL attains the strongest overall results. Across 8 CALVIN tasks, GTFuture accelerates learning fastest; GenFuture improves earlier and to a higher level than NoFuture; WrongFuture remains at zero throughout training.
AI.conclusion: Short-horizon future videos can serve as useful structured priors for exploration and policy adaptation under imperfect future predictions, enabling better control in uncertain, multi-step manipulation tasks.

title: Comparing Post-Hoc Explainable AI Methods for Interpreting Black-Box EEG Models in Depression Detection
summary: Recent advances in deep learning have enabled increasingly accurate electroencephalography (EEG)-based classification of Major Depressive Disorder (MDD), but the decision-making processes of high-capacity models remain difficult to interpret. This study investigates multiple post-hoc explainability methods applied to an InceptionTime architecture trained for EEG-based MDD detection. The analysis includes Shapley-based, gradient-based, and perturbation-based attribution approaches: DeepSHAP, Integrated Gradients, GradCAM, Occlusion, and Permutation Feature Importance. Explainability analysis was performed within a subject-level stratified 5-fold cross-validation framework using global attribution aggregation across EEG segments and subjects. The evaluated methods revealed partially convergent attribution patterns, with recurring emphasis on frontal, temporal, and posterior EEG regions, particularly in the right hemisphere. Quantitative comparison demonstrated substantial agreement between gradient- and perturbation-based approaches, while DeepSHAP produced comparatively distinct attribution distributions. At the same time, variability between explainability methods highlighted the influence of methodological assumptions on the resulting explanations. Overall, the results suggest that different post-hoc explainability approaches capture partially overlapping relevance structures in EEG-based deep learning models for depression detection. Although the observed attribution patterns are broadly consistent with several previous EEG studies of MDD, the analysis should be interpreted as exploratory rather than evidence of definitive neurophysiological biomarkers or clinical applicability. The study highlights both the usefulness and limitations of post-hoc explainability for interpreting black-box EEG classifiers in psychiatric applications.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc explainability methods yield partially overlapping relevance patterns in EEG-based deep learning for MDD; gradient- and perturbation-based explanations largely agree, DeepSHAP differs; results are exploratory and emphasize method-dependent variability rather than definitive biomarkers.
AI.motivation: To address interpretability of high-capacity deep learning models for EEG-based MDD detection by evaluating multiple post-hoc attribution methods and assessing their agreement and neurological plausibility.
AI.method: Train an InceptionTime architecture on EEG data for MDD detection. Apply multiple post-hoc explainability methods: DeepSHAP (Shapley-based), Integrated Gradients (gradient-based), GradCAM (gradient-based), Occlusion and Permutation Feature Importance (perturbation-based). Use subject-level stratified 5-fold cross-validation with global attribution aggregation across EEG segments and subjects to compare attributions.
AI.result: Attribution patterns partially converge across methods, with recurring emphasis on frontal, temporal, and posterior regions, especially in the right hemisphere. Strong agreement between gradient- and perturbation-based methods; DeepSHAP distributions differ markedly. Methodological choices significantly influence explanations, indicating high method-induced variability.
AI.conclusion: Post-hoc explainability captures partially overlapping relevance structures in EEG-based deep learning models for depression detection. Findings align with some prior EEG studies but should be interpreted as exploratory, not evidence of biomarkers or ready clinical utility. Highlights both the usefulness and limitations of using post-hoc explanations for psychiatric EEG classifiers.

title: The Confidence Shortcut: A Reasoning Failure Mode of Masked Diffusion Models
summary: Masked diffusion language models (MDMs) uniquely support any-order generation, with confidence-based decoding currently serving as the de facto standard inference policy. To optimize for this, recent training schemes attempt to align training mask patterns directly with those observed during generation. However, we argue that confidence-based decoding is inherently misaligned with the logical-flow trajectories required for complex reasoning, and that confidence-aligned training actively entrenches this misalignment. We make this concrete using multi-digit addition, where the decoding strategy prematurely predicts locally easy digits before resolving their long-range dependencies, producing high-confidence errors on challenging inputs. While traditional random masking keeps the failure rate low on this challenging tail, confidence-aligned training amplifies the error rate by an order of magnitude. Across five distinct reasoning tasks, this same pattern emerges with task-dependent severity: confidence-based decoding induces failures on highly complex inputs, and confidence-aligned training exacerbates them. In contrast, random masking -- despite its perceived inefficiency -- robustly preserves the reasoning-trajectory conditionals essential for solving the challenging tail.

AI.tldr: Masked diffusion language models' confidence-based decoding and confidence-aligned training misalign with the logical-flow needed for complex reasoning, leading to tail failures. Random masking, though seemingly inefficient, preserves reasoning trajectories and yields more robust performance across diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: To investigate how masking strategy and decoding policy influence the ability of masked diffusion language models to perform complex, multi-step reasoning and to assess whether confidence-based approaches actually support robust reasoning on challenging inputs.
AI.method: The authors use multi-digit addition as a concrete diagnostic to illustrate how confidence-based decoding can produce high-confidence errors on long-range dependencies, and compare this with random masking and confidence-aligned training across five reasoning tasks.
AI.result: Confidence-based decoding causes high-confidence errors on difficult inputs; confidence-aligned training amplifies these errors by up to an order of magnitude; random masking reduces tail failure rates and better preserves the necessary reasoning trajectory conditionals across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Random masking provides more robust reasoning-trajectory preservation than confidence-based decoding or confidence-aligned training, suggesting that perceived efficiency of confidence-guided methods may come at the cost of reliability on challenging tail inputs.

title: Multi-Stage VLM Pipeline for Zero-Shot Traffic Accident Understanding
comment: Accepted at the AUTOPILOT Workshop, CVPR 2026 (non-archival). Workshop Paper ID 13. Code: https://github.com/fuumin621/cvpr2026-accident-1st-place-solution
summary: We present the 1st-place solution to the ACCIDENT challenge at the CVPR 2026 AUTOPILOT Workshop, which asks for zero-shot prediction of accident timing, impact centroid, and collision type from CCTV footage. On a frozen Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct checkpoint we build a three-stage pipeline (full-video joint prediction, time refinement, and single-frame grounding of the impact centroid), run the same pipeline a second time on a 235B Mixture-of-Experts sibling, blend the two outputs 9:1, and finally snap each predicted point onto the nearest vehicle detection. The final system reaches Public LB 0.55469 / Private LB 0.57080, roughly +0.21 over the strongest host baseline (Molmo-7B, 0.358) and wins the challenge. We ablate each component, report the negative results that shaped the final design, and release the code at https://github.com/fuumin621/cvpr2026-accident-1st-place-solution.

AI.tldr: First-place zero-shot accident prediction from CCTV using a two-model ensemble and a three-stage video grounding pipeline to predict timing, impact centroid, and collision type.
AI.motivation: Enable zero-shot understanding of traffic accidents from CCTV footage to predict critical event characteristics without task-specific training.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline on a frozen Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct checkpoint: (1) full-video joint prediction, (2) time refinement, (3) single-frame grounding of the impact centroid. Repeat the same pipeline on a 235B Mixture-of-Experts model, blend outputs 9:1, then snap each predicted point to the nearest vehicle detection. Ablate components and report negative results; release code.
AI.result: Public LB 0.55469 / Private LB 0.57080, approximately +0.21 over the strongest host baseline (Molmo-7B, 0.358); won the ACCIDENT challenge.
AI.conclusion: A staged, cross-model ensemble with grounding-to-vehicle detections yields state-of-the-art zero-shot accident understanding; the ablations clarified design choices, and the approach is reproducible via release.

title: Fisher-Preserving Guidance: Training-Free Manifold Constraints for Safe Diffusion Control
summary: Diffusion models are effective for waypoint prediction in visual navigation, but standard sampling and test time guidance can produce unreliable or inefficient trajectories when updates drift off the training manifold. We propose Fisher Preserving Guidance with Outer Product Span Projection, a training-free inference method that avoids large Fisher drift associated with off-distribution actions while optimizing a task objective. Our method computes the Fisher-preserving update via a low-rank Jacobian factorization, requiring only a single backward pass per step and enabling real-time use. We further introduce Truncated Fisher Denoising Sensitivity as an uncertainty signal and use it for robust multi-sample action blending. Experiments on toy and realistic navigation benchmarks, including Maze2D with TSDF-based guidance, PushT with official Diffusion Policy weights, and visual navigation in simulation and on real robots, demonstrate consistent improvements in performance over strong diffusion-policy baselines without additional training.

AI.tldr: Training-free Fisher-preserving guidance for diffusion-based navigation; introduces low-rank Jacobian factorization (outer product span projection) to mitigate Fisher drift, enabling real-time updates, plus Truncated Fisher Denoising Sensitivity for uncertainty-aware multi-sample blending; demonstrates improvements on Maze2D, PushT, and real-robot navigation without extra training.
AI.motivation: Standard diffusion-based sampling with test-time guidance drifts off the training manifold, causing unreliable or inefficient trajectories; need a method that preserves Fisher geometry during inference without additional training.
AI.method: Fisher Preserving Guidance with Outer Product Span Projection computes Fisher-preserving updates via a low-rank Jacobian factorization, requiring only a single backward pass per step; uses Outer Product Span Projection to maintain Fisher alignment; introduces Truncated Fisher Denoising Sensitivity as an uncertainty signal to guide multi-sample action blending.
AI.result: Empirical improvements over strong diffusion-policy baselines on toy and realistic navigation benchmarks: Maze2D with TSDF guidance, PushT with official Diffusion Policy weights, visual navigation in simulation and on real robots; no additional training required.
AI.conclusion: Training-free Fisher-preserving inference can mitigate large Fisher drift and improve trajectory quality and robustness for diffusion-based navigation while preserving real-time applicability.

title: Governing Technical Debt in Agentic AI Systems
summary: Agentic AI systems are increasingly being explored as production infrastructure: they reason over multiple steps, call tools, act through workflows, and adapt through memory and feedback. These systems create governance challenges that are not fully captured by traditional software or predictive ML technical debt. We define Agentic Technical Debt as the accumulated liability created when prompts, memory, tool schemas, orchestration graphs, control policies, and observability routines are patched together faster than they can be validated, standardized, and governed. We define Stochastic Tax as the recurring operating burden of keeping probabilistic agent behavior within acceptable bounds. The distinction matters: debt is a stock of design and governance liability, while the tax is a flow of operating cost that arises because stochastic agents act through tools and workflows. We outline how managers can make both visible through lightweight dashboards and governance controls.

AI.tldr: Defines Agentic Technical Debt and Stochastic Tax for agentic AI systems; distinguishes a stock of governance liability from a flow of operating costs, and proposes lightweight dashboards and governance controls for visibility.
AI.motivation: As agentic AI systems become production-grade, traditional software debt fails to capture governance challenges; need a formal framework to manage institutional risk and operating burden.
AI.method: Conceptual framework: define terms ATD and ST; contrast debt vs tax; discuss governance and visibility via dashboards; outline managerial practices.
AI.result: Clarifies distinction between design/governance liability and ongoing operation costs; provides a roadmap for governance tools to monitor and control both.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a foundation for managing agentic AI risk through inventorying and visualizing both stock and flow measures; calls for governance controls and dashboards to keep agentic systems within bounds.

title: EarthShift: a benchmark for measuring robustness to real-world distribution shifts in Earth observation
summary: Current Earth observation benchmarks focus on measuring performance on diverse tasks and applications, typically measuring generalization in-distribution. But when models are deployed, they must generalize to myriad out-of-distribution scenarios, such as new time periods, geographies, scales, and sensors. We introduce EarthShift: the first public testbed for benchmarking robustness across multiple realistic distribution shifts encountered in remote sensing. EarthShift enables users to measure distributional robustness by comparing performance in- and out-of-distribution using datasets from paired datasets from different sources, temporal windows, geographic locations, and sensors. Our experiments on 8 geospatial foundation models (GFMs) and 11 tasks covering 5 shift types show that GFMs consistently perform 15-20% worse out-of-distribution on average regardless of model architecture, size, pre-training or fine-tuning strategy. We show that GFM robustness is similar to that of generic vision foundation models, and even fully-supervised models. This highlights a need for future research to strive for improvements in distributional robustness, not just performance, which can be benchmarked using EarthShift. We release our code and datasets to provide a testbed to guide future work to create foundation models that are robust and reliable in real-world applications. Code and data for EarthShift are available at: https://earthshift.github.io

AI.tldr: EarthShift is the first public testbed for benchmarking distributional robustness in Earth observation, assessing how 8 geospatial foundation models perform across 5 shift types on 11 tasks, revealing a 15-20% average drop out-of-distribution and parity with generic vision models; the authors release code and data to enable robust benchmarking and future improvements.
AI.motivation: Current Earth observation benchmarks emphasize in-distribution generalization, but real deployments face diverse, unseen distribution shifts (time, geography, sensors, scale). There is a need to quantify and improve distributional robustness of geospatial foundation models (GFMs).
AI.method: EarthShift constructs paired datasets from multiple sources, time windows, geographic locations, and sensors to evaluate distributional robustness. It compares performance in-distribution vs out-of-distribution across 8 GFMs and 11 tasks spanning 5 shift types, analyzing the impact of model architecture, size, pre-training, and fine-tuning strategies.
AI.result: GFMs exhibit a 15-20% average degradation when tested out-of-distribution, independent of architecture or training strategy; robustness levels are similar to generic vision foundation models and fully-supervised models; the benchmark reveals the need to prioritize distributional robustness, and the authors provide code/datasets for broader adoption.
AI.conclusion: There is a gap in distributional robustness for GFMs relative to deployment demands. EarthShift provides a standardized benchmark to guide future work toward robust and reliable geospatial foundation models, with resources released to the community.

title: Replicable Simulation-Based Robot Validation through Provenance
summary: Robot behavior is often validated through simulation-based testing, yet the replicability of such campaigns depends critically on transparent documentation of how tests are configured, executed, and post-processed. We argue that data provenance, coupled with the FAIR principles (findability, accessibility, interoperability, and reusability), addresses this gap by explicitly tracking links between artifacts and by attaching machine-readable metadata about file origins and key design decisions. Moreover, provenance and metadata cannot be treated as an afterthought confined to final datasets; they must be integrated into the testing processes that generate those datasets so that evidence can be reconstructed end-to-end. We demonstrate this by augmenting an existing simulation-based testing framework with provenance tracking and metadata collection mechanisms, and by using these extensions to enrich a mobile robot navigation dataset with structured provenance and FAIR-aligned metadata. Finally, we discuss obstacles encountered in this integration -- such as vocabulary alignment, attribute selection, and adoption of domain standards -- and provide actionable recommendations for implementing provenance-centric, FAIR metadata in robotics validation workflows.

AI.tldr: Provenance tracking and FAIR metadata enhance reproducibility of simulation-based robotics testing by linking artifacts and recording machine-readable provenance throughout testing workflows.
AI.motivation: Address replicability gaps in simulation-based validation by ensuring end-to-end traceability of test configurations, executions, and post-processing.
AI.method: Integrate provenance tracking and FAIR-aligned metadata collection into an existing simulation-based testing framework; apply to a mobile robot navigation dataset to enrich it with structured provenance.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of provenance-enabled testing; enriched dataset with structured provenance and FAIR metadata; identifies practical obstacles.
AI.conclusion: Provenance-centric, FAIR metadata should be integrated into robotics validation workflows; offers recommendations for alignment and standardization, vocabulary, attribute selection, and domain standards.

title: Learning Robust and Task-Invariant Functional Representation from fMRI through Siamese Self-Supervised Learning
summary: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) is a powerful tool for investigating human brain function. However, the high cost of data acquisition and the inherent subjectivity of psychiatric rating scales often lead to datasets with small sample sizes and variable label quality, especially when targeting a specific neurological condition. Combined with the inherently high dimensionality of fMRI data, these limitations substantially increase the risk of model overfitting. Recent years have seen growing interest in developing fMRI foundation models by combining multiple datasets; however, the computational resources needed for pretraining and fine-tuning are often prohibitive. We show that a lightweight self-supervised framework yields representations that generalize across diverse downstream tasks, outperforming fully supervised baselines and approaching the performance of large-scale models. We introduce BrainSimSiam, a data-efficient self-supervised representation learning framework that leverages positive-only data pairs to learn robust and generalizable features. We demonstrate that the learned representations achieve strong performance across multiple downstream classification and regression tasks, highlighting the potential of BrainSimSiam for data-limited neuroimaging applications.

AI.tldr: A data-efficient self-supervised fMRI framework, BrainSimSiam, learns robust representations using positive-only pairs, achieving strong generalization across downstream tasks, outperforming supervised baselines and approaching large-scale models while mitigating small-sample, high-dimensional data challenges.
AI.motivation: To address small sample sizes, noisy labels, and high dimensionality in fMRI data, which increase overfitting risk and hinder generalizable representations; while reducing computational demands of multi-dataset pretraining.
AI.method: Proposes BrainSimSiam, a lightweight, self-supervised representation learning framework that uses positive-only data pairs (no explicit negatives) in a Siamese-like architecture to learn robust fMRI features efficiently.
AI.result: Learned representations generalize well across diverse downstream classification and regression tasks, outperforming fully supervised baselines and approaching the performance of large-scale models in data-limited neuroimaging settings.
AI.conclusion: BrainSimSiam offers a data-efficient pathway to high-quality, generalizable fMRI representations suitable for data-limited scenarios, potentially reducing the need for large, label-rich datasets and heavy compute in neuroimaging foundation-model development.

title: Better Later Than Sooner: Neuro-Symbolic Knowledge Graph Construction via Ontology-grounded Post-extraction Correction
summary: Question answering (QA) is a core challenge in AI, particularly for complex queries requiring multi-hop reasoning across documents, or symbolic operations like aggregation or exhaustive listing. Retrieval-augmented generation has become the dominant approach to QA, with recent graph-based variants addressing part of these issues by organizing knowledge to better support compositional questions. However, most textual graph-based RAG methods still lack the structure needed for symbolic operations useful to answer complex questions reliably. This motivates symbolic graph-based approaches, which extract knowledge graphs (KGs) whose relations are logic predicates that enable SQL-like querying. Yet these pipelines typically use LLMs for KG extraction, which can introduce consistency issues, where extracted facts may violate commonsense ontology constraints. We propose a neuro-symbolic framework for ontology-grounded KG construction combining open-domain extraction, embedding-based canonicalization of types and predicates, and targeted LLM-based correction of ontology violations. By deferring corrections to a post-extraction stage, our method avoids repeated LLM calls, substantially reducing token usage while improving KG consistency and preserving downstream QA quality. Finally, we show that the extracted KGs are well suited for symbolic querying by measuring the occurrence of SPARQL graph patterns.

AI.tldr: Neuro-symbolic ontology-grounded KG construction for QA that reduces LLM calls via post-extraction corrections, improving KG consistency while preserving QA performance and enabling symbolic querying (SPARQL).
AI.motivation: Fill the gap in graph-based retrieval augmented generation where KGs lack structure for symbolic operations; ensure ontology-consistent knowledge graphs when extracting facts with LLMs; reduce token usage in iterative corrections.
AI.method: Open-domain extraction to obtain initial KG; embedding-based canonicalization to unify types/predicates; targeted LLM-based correction of ontology violations in a post-extraction stage to avoid repeated LLM calls; evaluation focusing on suitability for symbolic querying via SPARQL graph pattern occurrences.
AI.result: Produced ontology-consistent KGs that maintain QA quality while substantially reducing token usage; KGs demonstrate suitability for symbolic querying as shown by SPARQL pattern occurrences.
AI.conclusion: A neuro-symbolic, ontology-grounded KG construction pipeline can deliver structured, consistent knowledge graphs efficiently, enabling reliable symbolic QA without excessive LLM usage.

title: Rethinking FID Through the Geometry of the Reference Dataset
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures. Accepted to ICML 2026 Workshop: Combining Theory and Benchmarks
summary: Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) is widely used to evaluate image generators, yet lower FID does not always correspond to better sample quality. We show that this mismatch depends in part on the geometry of the reference dataset. In a controlled study across six datasets, distributional density and effective rank significantly explain how FID changes as sample quality improves. Concentrated datasets tend to yield more favorable FID trends, whereas more dispersed datasets can make FID worsen despite better samples. Attribution to precision and recall and ablations with alternative feature spaces and distances support the same conclusion. These results suggest that distributional metrics should be interpreted together with the geometry of the reference dataset for more reliable benchmarking.

AI.tldr: FID's reliability is strongly influenced by the geometry of the reference dataset; lower FID does not universally indicate better sample quality. Dataset concentration (density) and effective rank largely govern how FID improves with sample quality; dispersed datasets can yield worse FID even for higher-quality samples.
AI.motivation: Explain why FID may misalign with perceptual quality and identify dataset-geometric factors that modulate FID trends.
AI.method: Controlled study across six datasets examining distributional density and effective rank; assess how FID changes with sample quality; perform AB tests with precision/recall attribution; compare alternative feature spaces and distance measures.
AI.result: Concentrated datasets tend to show favorable FID improvements with better samples; more dispersed datasets can show worsening FID despite higher sample quality. Distributional density and effective rank explain a significant portion of FID variation; precision/recall attribution supports the same conclusion; results hold across different feature spaces and distance metrics.
AI.conclusion: Interpreting FID and similar distributional metrics requires simultaneous consideration of the reference dataset's geometry; benchmarking should account for dataset concentration and rank to avoid misleading conclusions about sample quality.

title: Sample-Efficient Diffusion-based Reinforcement Learning with Critic Guidance
summary: Recent advances in reinforcement learning (RL) have achieved great successes by leveraging the multimodality and exploration capability of diffusion policies. Among these approaches, one representative branch focuses on the sampling-based policy optimization. This design enables better exploration capability of the diffusion model, particularly at the beginning of training, but suffer from low exploitation in Q-value information, resulting in a slow policy convergence. Another branch pays attention to gradient-based policy optimization, which sufficiently exploits the gradient of the Q function yet tends to collapse into a unimodal policy with low diversity. To address this issue, we propose CGPO, \textbf{C}ritic-\textbf{G}uided diffusion \textbf{P}olicy \textbf{O}ptimization, which effectively balances exploration and exploitation with the training-free guidance technique integrated into the denoising process of diffusion policy. Concretely, CGPO steers action generation toward high-value regions defined by the critic network and uses the guided actions as regression objectives. In this manner, CGPO reduces the time required to obtain high-quality actions and improves final performance with better balance between the exploration-exploitation tradeoff. We validate the effectiveness of CGPO on 5 MuJoCo locomotion tasks, and CGPO achieves state-of-the-art performance compared with existing diffusion-based RL methods. Notably, CGPO is the first success to incorporate diffusion policy into real-world RL, with its superior performance on Franka robot arm grasping tasks. Our official page is released at https://dingsht.tech/cgpo-webpage.

AI.tldr: CGPO introduces a training-free critic-guided diffusion policy optimization to balance exploration and exploitation in RL. It steers diffusion actions toward high-value regions via critic guidance, improving learning speed and final performance, achieving state-of-the-art on MuJoCo and real-world grasping.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based RL methods face a trade-off: diffusion-sampling methods offer exploration but slow exploitation of Q-values, while gradient-based methods exploit Q-values well but risk collapsing to unimodal policies with low diversity. A method is needed to balance exploration and exploitation without heavy training overhead.
AI.method: In CGPO, a training-free guidance mechanism is integrated into the denoising process of a diffusion policy. The critic-guided action samples are used to steer actions toward high-value regions, and these guided actions serve as regression targets to train the policy.
AI.result: Empirical validation on 5 MuJoCo locomotion tasks shows CGPO achieves state-of-the-art performance among diffusion-based RL methods. It also marks the first successful application of diffusion policies to real-world RL, demonstrated on Franka robot arm grasping tasks. An official webpage is provided.
AI.conclusion: CGPO provides a simple, training-free means to balance exploration and exploitation in diffusion-policy RL, accelerating convergence and boosting final performance, including success in real-world manipulation tasks.

title: FormInv: A Measurement Protocol for Semantic Invariance in Mathematical Reasoning Benchmarks
comment: 18 pages, 3 figures. Under review for the 3rd AI for Math Workshop (AI4Math), ICML 2026
summary: A paraphrase-quality audit of MathCheck (ICLR 2025) detected 4 semantically incorrect paraphrases in 129 groups (3.1%); removing them drops GPT-4o from rank 2 to rank 4 and elevates Claude Haiku and DeepSeek V3 above it; these ranking changes are invisible to any single-model evaluation. Cross-model unanimity found these errors automatically (>= 3/4 models for MathCheck; >= 6/9 for our primary evaluation) for under $10; in our own dataset the same protocol found that 47% of auto-generated connective-variation paraphrases were semantically incorrect. That flaw compounds a deeper measurement gap: Claude Haiku 4.5 achieves 86% accuracy yet SCR=50%, meaning half its theorems are answered differently under semantically equivalent restatements, while aggregate accuracy across 9 models spans only 86-96% yet Semantic Consistency Rates (SCR) span 50-82% -- a 32-point gap invisible to standard benchmarks. Formally, for any target ranking over 9 frontier models there exists a weighting over paraphrase families that realizes it (No-Free-Benchmark corollary), because no model Pareto-dominates all families -- so benchmark designers who select families are implicitly choosing which model wins. FormInv supplies the audit protocol (replicated on external benchmarks at 100% recall), SCR and per-theorem Cochran's Q as primary invariance measures evaluated on 9 models across 366-811 items (on Lean4-verified theorems), and FormInvSelector for regime-aware model selection.

AI.tldr: FormInv exposes a large, cross-model sensitivity to paraphrase variations in frontier models, revealing systematic ranking shifts and a 32-point gap between semantic-consistency rates and aggregate accuracy. It proposes an audit protocol (FormInv), invariance measures (SCR, per-theorem Cochran's Q) across 9 models, and a regime-aware selector (FormInvSelector).
AI.motivation: To uncover hidden biases in model evaluation caused by paraphrase variations, quantify semantic invariance of forward-performance measures, and demonstrate that standard benchmarks can be gamed by paraphrase families.
AI.method: Audit across 9 frontier models on 366–811 Lean4 theorems using auto-generated paraphrase families; identify semantically incorrect paraphrases; compute SCR and Cochran's Q per theorem; compare rankings with/without flawed paraphrases; replicate protocol on external benchmarks; introduce FormInv and FormInvSelector.
AI.result: 4 semantic errors in 129 paraphrase groups (3.1%); removing them shifts GPT-4o from rank 2 to 4 and elevates Claude Haiku and DeepSeek V3; cross-model unanimity detects errors (>=3/4 models; >=6/9 for primary) at < $10; 47% of auto paraphrases are semantically incorrect in the authors’ dataset; Claude Haiku 4.5 shows 86% accuracy but SCR=50%; aggregate model accuracy 86–96% with SCR 50–82% (32-point gap); No-Free-Benchmark corollary: for any target ranking over 9 frontier models, a weighting over paraphrase families can realize it; FormInv protocol achieves 100% recall on external benchmarks; FormInvSelector provided for regime-aware model selection.
AI.conclusion: Paraphrase-induced measurement variation creates hidden biases in model rankings; robust evaluation requires invariant measures across paraphrase families; FormInv provides a replicable audit and invariance toolkit, and FormInvSelector enables regime-aware model selection to mitigate No-Free-Benchmark effects.

title: Paper Agents, Paper Gains: An Empirical Analysis of DeFi Investment Agents
summary: DeFi investment agents, systems that use AI for autonomous on-chain trading, have attained over USD 3 billion in combined token valuations since late 2024. We survey over 1,900 AI-tagged crypto projects, filter to investment-focused agents, and curate 10 representative projects spanning strategy and observability dimensions. We then conduct a deep-dive architectural analysis of two prominent agent frameworks, ElizaOS and Virtuals Protocol, and a quantitative on-chain performance analysis of 11 Solana-based agent treasuries with publicly attributable trading activity, covering 925,323 token holders. We find that current deployments remain early and heterogeneous: (1) in our sample, many projects do not yet provide clear evidence of autonomous trade execution, and developer interviews suggest that many visible deployments remain basic API integrations; (2) agent treasuries retain over USD 30M in paper gains while token holders collectively lost USD 191.7M, with the top 1% of wallets capturing 81.4% of all gains (USD 1.81B); (3) token valuations are weakly connected to treasury fundamentals, with market-cap-to-AUM ratios exceeding 10,000x versus below 1x for established DeFi protocols; and (4) aggregate user gains peaked at USD 2.4B before declining to net losses, with median returns negative on every platform and tokens declining 93% on average from all-time highs. We interpret these outcomes as characteristic of a permissionless, first-generation market in which open infrastructure enables rapid experimentation but also allows naive or speculative agents to launch before robust standards for autonomy, performance, and stakeholder alignment emerge. We therefore propose a maturity framework along three dimensions: autonomous execution, risk-adjusted profitability, and stakeholder alignment, to characterize the gap between current deployments and future investment-grade agent systems.

AI.tldr: Survey and analysis of DeFi AI investment agents; finds early-stage, heterogeneous deployments with misalignment between valuations and fundamentals; significant wealth concentration among top holders; proposes a three-dimensional maturity framework (autonomous execution, risk-adjusted profitability, stakeholder alignment) to guide future investment-grade agent systems.
AI.motivation: To understand the architecture, economics, and maturity of AI-driven DeFi investment agents, assessing evidence of autonomy, profitability, and investor alignment to inform standards, risk, and policy considerations.
AI.method: Survey of ~1,900 AI-tagged crypto projects; filter to investment-focused agents; curate 10 representative projects; deep-dive on ElizaOS and Virtuals Protocol; on-chain performance analysis of 11 Solana-based agent treasuries with 925,323 holders; analyze gains/losses, treasury fundamentals, valuations (market cap/AUM), and ownership concentration.
AI.result: Evidence of limited autonomous execution; many deployments are basic API integrations. Treasuries show ~USD 30M paper gains vs USD 191.7M losses for holders; top 1% capture ~81.4% of gains (~USD 1.81B). Valuations weakly linked to fundamentals; extreme market-cap-to-AUM ratios. Aggregate user gains peaked at USD 2.4B then declined to net losses; median returns negative; tokens declined ~93% from ATH.
AI.conclusion: The space is a permissionless, first-generation market with rapid experimentation but lacking robust autonomy, profitability, and stakeholder-alignment standards. A maturity framework is proposed along three dimensions—autonomous execution, risk-adjusted profitability, and stakeholder alignment—to benchmark progress toward investment-grade agent systems.

title: DMC-CF: Dynamic Multimodal CounterFactual QA benchmark for Causal Reasoning
summary: With the rapid advancement of multimodal large language models (MLLMs), models have demonstrated increasingly powerful multimodal capabilities. However, whether MLLMs trained through statistical learning can truly understand the causal relationships underlying the real world remains a key research question. In recent years, numerous multimodal causal reasoning datasets have been proposed. Nevertheless, these datasets are either limited in scale or constructed from synthetic images and videos, cartoon-based content, or other non-realistic multimodal sources. To address these limitations, we collect real-world videos and construct DMC-CF-Static, a large-scale benchmark for multimodal causal counterfactual reasoning. Furthermore, to mitigate issues such as data contamination in traditional static evaluation, we represent causal events using causal graphs and propose the Dynamic Graph Intervention (DGI) framework to build the dynamic evaluation benchmark DMC-CF-Dynamic from DMC-CF-Static. Experimental results on the overall DMC-CF, which includes both static and dynamic evaluation benchmarks, demonstrate that the multimodal causal reasoning capabilities of current multimodal large language models in real-world scenarios still require substantial improvement.

AI.tldr: Proposes real-world multimodal causal reasoning benchmarks (DMC-CF-Static and DMC-CF-Dynamic) for MLLMs using real videos and causal graphs; introduces Dynamic Graph Intervention to enable dynamic evaluation; results show current MLLMs still lag in real-world multimodal causal reasoning and improvement is needed.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in multimodal causal reasoning benchmarks by moving from synthetic/nonreal data to real-world videos and by introducing dynamic evaluation to assess causal understanding under interventions.
AI.method: 1) Collect real-world video data and construct DMC-CF-Static as a large-scale static benchmark for multimodal causal counterfactual reasoning. 2) Represent causal events with causal graphs. 3) Propose Dynamic Graph Intervention (DGI) framework to derive DMC-CF-Dynamic for dynamic evaluation, mitigating data contamination in static tests.
AI.result: Experiments on the combined static+dynamic DMC-CF benchmark indicate that current multimodal LLMs have limited causal reasoning capabilities in real-world scenarios and require substantial improvement.
AI.conclusion: The work reveals a substantial gap in real-world multimodal causal understanding by MLLMs and provides a framework (DGI) and datasets (DMC-CF-Static/DMC-CF-Dynamic) to drive future research on robust causal reasoning under real-world conditions.

title: BORA: Bridging Offline Reinforcement Learning and Online Residual Adaptation for Real-World Dexterous VLA Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for grounding visual-language understanding into real-world robotic manipulation. However, dexterous manipulation remains challenging for VLA policies due to high-dimensional hand control and compounding execution errors, which makes real-world RL post-training essential for bridging the gap between visually grounded action generation and physically reliable dexterous execution. However, high-dimensional dexterous exploration often triggers temporal inconsistency, sample inefficiency and hardware risks in the real world. To address these challenges, we propose BORA, an offline-to-online RL post-training framework designed for real-world dexterous VLA models. In the offline phase, BORA constructs a critic that takes both the VLM's cognition tokens and action chunks as inputs. This design enables action-conditioned value guidance, allowing the critic to evaluate dexterous hand motions beyond visual context alone. During the subsequent online phase, BORA freezes the VLA base and introduces a lightweight, Human-in-the-Loop (HiL) chunk-wise residual adaptation mechanism to mitigate real-world execution errors and further correct the offline-learned intents within the actual physical environment. By inheriting the offline critic and employing intervention-driven rewards, BORA effectively corrects execution discrepancies and adapts to real-world physical variances while preserving the pretrained policy as a stable prior. Extensive evaluations across five complex real-world dexterous tasks demonstrate that BORA significantly outperforms pure imitation learning and traditional decoupled RL baselines, achieving a 33% absolute increase in average success rate under standard settings and up to a 43% improvement in unseen object generalization.

AI.tldr: BORA is an offline-to-online RL framework for real-world dexterous vision-language-action models, using an offline action-conditioned critic and a lightweight, human-in-the-loop, chunk-wise residual online adaptation, achieving substantial gains in success and unseen-object generalization.
AI.motivation: Dexterous manipulation with vision-language grounding faces high-dimensional control, noisy real-world dynamics, and safety concerns. Offline-to-online training with a critic that considers action context and a minimal online adaptation can bridge visually grounded policies to reliable, real-world execution while reducing risk.
AI.method: Offline training: construct an action-conditioned critic that takes both cognition tokens from the vision-language model and action chunks to evaluate dexterous motions beyond visual context. Online phase: freeze the base VLA policy, apply chunk-wise residual adaptation with Human-in-the-Loop guidance, reuse the offline critic via intervention-driven rewards, and preserve the pretrained policy as a stable prior.
AI.result: Evaluations on five real-world dexterous tasks show BORA outperforms imitation learning and decoupled RL baselines, achieving a 33% absolute increase in average success rate under standard settings and up to 43% improvement in unseen object generalization.
AI.conclusion: BORA effectively pairs offline value guidance with online, minimally invasive human-in-the-loop adaptation to bridge VLM-grounded action generation and reliable dexterous execution, improving success and generalization while maintaining policy stability.

title: FedQHD: Closed-Form Function-Space Federated Reinforcement Learning
summary: Federated reinforcement learning enables decentralized agents to collaboratively improve policies or value estimates without exchanging raw trajectories. However, FedAvg-style parameter averaging is not function-space consistent: when clients use heterogeneous encoders or even identical nonlinear networks, averaged parameters need not correspond to the weighted average of client value functions in any common function space. We propose FedQHD, a federated Q-learning method using hyperdimensional (random-feature) state encoders with a linear readout, so that Q-functions are nonlinear in state yet linear in trainable parameters. This linear structure enables closed-form aggregation. With a shared encoder, the function-space consensus update coincides exactly with weighted averaging of local readout matrices. With heterogeneous encoders, the server constructs a global teacher by averaging client Q-values on a shared anchor-state set, and each client compiles this teacher into its local representation via a single ridge projection. We formalize the federation gap -- the error incurred when compiling a federated teacher into a heterogeneous client representation -- relative to a client-specific oracle projection. We show that this gap decomposes into subspace misalignment, anchor-set conditioning, and regularization bias. We further identify the anchor-to-dimension ratio $m \geq D_i$ as the well-conditioned regime in which the gap reduces to a multiple of the encoder heterogeneity floor. On four continuous-state, discrete-action control benchmarks, FedQHD matches or outperforms FedAvg-style baselines and distillation-based alternatives while requiring substantially less computation, and the empirical dependence of the federation gap on encoder dimension matches our theoretical analysis.

AI.tldr: Federated Q-learning using hyperdimensional (random-feature) encoders with a linear readout enables exact, low-computation aggregation across heterogeneous encoders by making Q-functions linear in trainable parameters; shared encoders allow exact parameter averaging, while heterogeneous encoders are handled via a global teacher and ridge projection, with a formalized federation gap.
AI.motivation: Address the function-space inconsistency of FedAvg under encoder heterogeneity in federated reinforcement learning, enabling principled, efficient aggregation of value functions.
AI.method: Propose FedQHD: use hyperdimensional random-feature encoders for states with a linear readout. If all clients share an encoder, FedQHD reduces to exact weighted averaging of readout matrices. If encoders differ, construct a global teacher by averaging client Q-values on a shared anchor-state set; each client compiles this teacher into its local representation via ridge projection. Formalize the federation gap as the error from compiling the federated teacher into a client-specific representation, decompose into subspace misalignment, anchor-set conditioning, and regularization bias; identify anchor-to-dimension ratio m≥Di as well-conditioned regime where the gap scales with encoder heterogeneity. empirical evaluation on four continuous-state, discrete-action benchmarks shows FedQHD matches or surpasses FedAvg baselines and distillation methods while requiring less computation; federation gap behavior aligns with theory.
AI.result: The proposed FedQHD method achieves competitive performance with reduced computational cost, and the federation gap analysis explains how encoder heterogeneity, anchor-set conditioning, and regularization influence aggregation; results support the practical viability of function-space-consistent federated Q-learning.
AI.conclusion: Hyperdimensional encoders with linear readouts enable function-space-consistent federated Q-learning, offering exact consensus under shared encoders and a theoretically-grounded, efficient approximation under heterogeneity, with empirical results validating reduced computation and maintained or improved performance.

title: ReasonOps: Operator Segmentation for LLM Reasoning Traces
summary: Chain-of-thought traces from large reasoning models can span tens of thousands of tokens, yet we lack a vocabulary for describing their internal structure. Previous methods developed to analyze chain-of-thought traces are either too rigid or not expressive enough, failing to capture features across domains and models. To remedy this, we develop ReasonOps, an unsupervised, expressive method for annotating chain-of-thought traces, providing succinct universal operators. Using ReasonOps, we analyze 44,662 traces from 12 thinking LLMs spanning 6 families across 8 reasoning benchmarks and discover that they share a common compositional structure: 7 recurring reasoning operators -- discourse-level moves such as backtracking, inferring, and hypothesizing -- that emerge from unsupervised clustering of sentence-initial 3-token pivots. These operators appear across every model family and benchmark domain, confirmed by three independent LLM judges who classify held-out samples at 70 -76% accuracy. We analyze the structure of operators on easy vs. hard problems, revealing that reflective operators are more helpful on hard problems and harm performance on easy problems. Operator sequences are highly model-identifying: a classifier trained on operator distributions alone recovers the source model with macro-AUC, revealing that each model family has a distinctive reasoning fingerprint. Structural operator features predict within-problem answer correctness well above baselines. Classifiers built on these operators reach WP-AUC and on AIME specifically. ReasonOps further enables early quality estimation well before the trace completes: we predict at WP-AUC for only 50% of the trace. The ReasonOps pipeline is unsupervised and annotation-free, enabling deep insights into LLM reasoning traces as well as strong downstream results on model identification and correctness prediction.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised ReasonOps annotates chain-of-thought traces with seven universal operators learned from sentence pivots, revealing cross-model, cross-domain compositional structure and enabling model identification, correctness prediction, and early quality estimation without supervision.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of a descriptive vocabulary for the internal structure of chain-of-thought traces and the limitations of rigid prior methods, by deriving a compact, domain-general set of operators.
AI.method: Unsupervised clustering of sentence-initial 3-token pivots identifies seven operators; annotation-free pipeline applied to 44,662 traces from 12 thinking LLMs across 6 families and 8 benchmarks; validation by three independent LLM judges on held-out samples (70–76% accuracy); downstream classifiers use operator distributions to predict model identity and correctness; enables early quality estimation at 50% trace completion.
AI.result: Seven recurring operators emerge across all models and domains, including discourse-level moves like backtracking, inferring, and hypothesizing; operator sequences serve as a model fingerprint and predict correctness better than baselines; classifiers reach WP-AUC and AIME-level performance; early quality estimation achievable with partial traces; the approach is unsupervised and annotation-free.
AI.conclusion: ReasonOps uncovers a shared, compositional structure in reasoning traces, providing a compact universal vocabulary that supports cross-domain analysis, model identification, and correctness prediction, with practical benefits like early quality estimation while remaining annotation-free.

title: WorldMemArena: Evaluating Multimodal Agent Memory Through Action-World Interaction
summary: Multimodal large language models are increasingly deployed as long-horizon agents, where memory must do more than recall: it must track an evolving world, revise what has gone stale, and surface the right evidence at decision time. Existing benchmarks measure recall over static dialogue, collapse memory into a single end-of-task accuracy, and reduce visual observations to captions, leaving us unable to localize failures to writing, maintenance, retrieval, or use. The rise of agent harnesses that author their own memory sharpens this gap, since we have no principled way to compare hand-designed pipelines with self-managing alternatives. To close these gaps, we formulate multimodal agent memory as an Action-World Interaction Loop with an observable four-stage lifecycle, and instantiate it in WorldMemArena: 400 multi-session multimodal tasks spanning Lifelong Evolution (evolving personal and task states) and Agentic Execution (memory from real observations, actions, and feedback), annotated with gold memory points, updates, distractors, and evidence chains for stage-level diagnosis. This enables the first head-to-head comparison of long-context, manually designed (RAG and external memory systems), and harness-based memory agents. Results show that: (1) better memory writing and storage do not guarantee better performance; (2) multimodal memory still struggles to fully use visual evidence; (3) systems are unstable across domains and degrade on realistic agentic trajectories; and (4) harness memory is more flexible but remains costly and less reliable.

AI.tldr: Proposes WorldMemArena, a formalized benchmark and framework to compare long-horizon multimodal memory architectures, enabling head-to-head evaluation of manually designed memory (RAG/external) versus harness-based, with annotated gold memory signals across a four-stage Action-World-Interaction Loop.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks overemphasize static recall and end-task accuracy, collapse memory to simple captions or scores, and fail to isolate memory writing, maintenance, retrieval, or decision-use. With agents that author their own memory, there is no principled way to compare pipelines with self-managing memory, necessitating a unified, diagnostic evaluation platform for longitudinal, multimodal agents.
AI.method: Define an Action-World Interaction Loop with a four-stage memory lifecycle and instantiate WorldMemArena (400 multi-session tasks) spanning Lifelong Evolution and Agentic Execution. Tasks are annotated with gold memory points, updates, distractors, and evidence chains to enable stage-level diagnosis, enabling head-to-head comparisons between hand-designed memory systems (RAG/external) and harness-based memory.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) richer memory writing/storage does not guarantee better performance; (2) multimodal memory struggles to fully exploit visual evidence; (3) systems are unstable across domains and degrade on realistic agentic trajectories; (4) harness memory is more flexible but costlier and less reliable.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables principled comparisons of memory architectures for long-horizon multimodal agents, revealing tradeoffs among writing quality, retrieval efficacy, evidence usage, and stability. Harness-based memory offers flexibility but incurs higher cost and lower reliability, guiding future design and evaluation.

title: Qwen-VLA: Unifying Vision-Language-Action Modeling across Tasks, Environments, and Robot Embodiments
summary: Embodied intelligence is often studied through specialized models for individual tasks such as manipulation or navigation, resulting in fragmented capabilities and limited generalization across tasks, environments, and robot embodiments. In this work, we study whether heterogeneous embodied decision-making problems can be unified within a single vision-language-action model. We present Qwen-VLA, a unified embodied foundation model that extends Qwen's vision-language modeling stack from perception, understanding, and reasoning to continuous action and trajectory generation through a DiT-based action decoder. Qwen-VLA is trained with a large-scale joint pretraining recipe over diverse data sources, including robotics manipulation trajectories, human egocentric demonstrations, synthetic simulation data, vision-and-language navigation data, trajectory-centric supervision, and auxiliary vision-language data. To support multiple robot platforms, we introduce embodiment-aware prompt conditioning, where robot-specific textual descriptions specify the current embodiment and control convention. We further cast manipulation, navigation, and trajectory prediction into a unified action-and-trajectory prediction framework, enabling transferable visual grounding, spatial reasoning, and continuous action generation across robot morphologies, task families, and environments. Experiments on manipulation, navigation, and trajectory-centric benchmarks show consistent multi-task performance and out-of-distribution generalization under variations in scene layout, background, lighting, object configuration, and robot embodiment. Qwen-VLA-Instruct achieves 97.9% on LIBERO, 73.7% on Simpler-WidowX, 86.1%/87.2% on RoboTwin-Easy/Hard, 69.0% OSR on R2R, 59.6% SR on RxR, 76.9% average OOD success in real-world ALOHA experiments, and 26.6% zero-shot success on DOMINO dynamic manipulation.

AI.tldr: A unified embodied foundation model (Qwen-VLA) extends vision-language modeling to continuous action and trajectory generation, trained on diverse data, with embodiment-aware prompts, achieving cross-task generalization across manipulation, navigation, and trajectory tasks with strong OOD performance.
AI.motivation: Address fragmentation in embodied AI by unifying manipulation, navigation, and trajectory prediction under a single vision-language-action model that can adapt to different robots and environments.
