AI.method: A DiT-based action decoder atop Qwen's vision-language stack; large-scale joint pretraining over diverse data sources (robotics trajectories, human egocentric demonstrations, synthetic simulations, vision-language navigation data, trajectory-centric supervision, and auxiliary V-L data); embodiment-aware prompt conditioning to specify the current embodiment and control conventions; unifies manipulation, navigation, and trajectory prediction into an actionable framework enabling transferable grounding and continuous control across morphologies; includes Qwen-VLA-Instruct with instruction tuning.
AI.result: Demonstrates multi-task performance and out-of-distribution generalization across scene variations and robot embodiments; benchmark scores include LIBERO 97.9%, Simpler-WidowX 73.7%, RoboTwin-Easy 86.1% / Hard 87.2%, R2R OSR 69.0%, RxR SR 59.6%, 76.9% average OOD success in real-world ALOHA experiments, and 26.6% zero-shot on DOMINO dynamic manipulation.
AI.conclusion: Qwen-VLA presents a promising unified approach for embodied AI with transferable visual grounding and continuous control across morphologies, outlining a path toward generalizable, multi-task embodied agents; practical deployment will require further data-efficiency and real-world evaluation considerations.

title: LoRe: Adaptive Interaction-Evaluation Routing with Per-Step Interaction Budgets for Iterative Graph Solvers
summary: Diffusion-based neural solvers for combinatorial optimization repeatedly re-evaluate dense edge/factor interactions, making inference expensive in wall-clock time and often memory-bound at scale. Inspired by the computational methodologies of many-body physics, we introduce LoRe, a training-free, inference-time drop-in wrapper that enforces per-step interaction-evaluation budgeting: at each iteration, it evaluates only a fixed fraction of interactions by dynamically routing computation to high-conflict or high-uncertainty interactions, instead of using a fixed sparsification (e.g., static kNN graphs or static masks). Under fully inclusive end-to-end wall-clock accounting, LoRe substantially improves scalability on the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem, extending feasible inference more than $3\times$ beyond the baseline's out-of-memory limit, delivering a $\sim 8\times$ speedup and a $\sim 12\times$ peak-memory reduction, with solution quality preserved in this regime. Demonstrating cross-task generality on the large-scale Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) and zero-shot robustness to topology shifts, LoRe achieves a $\sim 15\times$ speedup at $n=1000$ with a $44\times$ memory reduction and competitive tour quality.

AI.tldr: LoRe is a training-free inference-time wrapper for diffusion-based neural solvers in combinatorial optimization that budgets per-step interaction evaluations by routing compute to high-conflict/uncertainty interactions, avoiding fixed sparsification. It yields large wall-clock and memory wins on MIS, with maintained solution quality, and shows cross-task generality and robustness on large-scale TSP.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based solvers repeatedly evaluate dense edge/factor interactions, causing expensive inference that is often memory-bound at scale. There is a need for a training-free mechanism to reduce per-iteration interaction costs without sacrificing performance or requiring model retraining.
AI.method: LoRe acts as an inference-time, training-free wrapper that enforces per-step interaction-evaluation budgeting by dynamically routing computation to the subset of high-conflict or high-uncertainty interactions. Unlike fixed sparsification (static kNN or masks), it adaptively selects interactions at each iteration, inspired by many-body physics concepts, and operates in end-to-end, wall-clock accounting.
AI.result: On MIS, LoRe extends feasible inference more than 3× beyond the baseline's out-of-memory limit, delivering ~8× speedup and ~12× peak-memory reduction while preserving solution quality. On large-scale TSP, and zero-shot to topology shifts, LoRe achieves ~15× speedup at n=1000 with ~44× memory reduction and competitive tour quality.
AI.conclusion: LoRe provides a training-free, inference-time technique that substantially improves scalability of diffusion-based solvers for combinatorial optimization, with strong cross-task generality and robustness, enabling larger-scale problems to be solved within memory and time budgets without retraining.

title: GTA: Generating Long-Horizon Tasks for Web Agents at Scale
comment: Published at Proceedings of the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
summary: Web agents, which couple language models with browsing and tool-use capabilities, show promise as open web assistants. Yet progress is increasingly limited by the lack of scalable, process-level supervision. Existing benchmarks are largely manually constructed, providing only coarse start-goal annotations without intermediate trajectories, while recent automatic generation efforts remain expensive, biased, and shallow. These limitations prevent reliable training and evaluation of agents that must generalize to realistic, multi-hop, cross-page tasks. We introduce a scalable framework, GTA, that integrates crawling, retrieval-based seeding, in-context generation, and automated quality control to produce realistic tasks paired with executable trajectories. This design decouples crawling from generation for greater efficiency, grounds tasks in the site graph to enforce compositionality, and ensures dense supervision through deterministic replays and systematic validation. We instantiate the pipeline on over 50 websites covering e-commerce, government, forums, and news, with multilingual and multi-hop coverage. The resulting benchmark reveals a significant human-agent performance gap and enables detailed diagnostics. Our contributions are three-fold: (i) formalizing multi-hop web-agent task generation, (ii) proposing an efficient and validated pipeline for automatic data creation, and (iii) releasing a dynamic benchmark with reproducible evaluation.

AI.tldr: GTA provides a scalable, end-to-end framework to generate realistic, multi-hop web-task trajectories with automated quality control, grounded in a site graph, enabling dense supervision and reproducible benchmarks across 50+ multilingual sites; it reveals a human–agent performance gap.
AI.motivation: Overcomes the lack of scalable, process-level supervision and reliable benchmarks for web agents, addressing reliance on coarse manual annotations and expensive, biased automatic generation.
AI.method: A decoupled pipeline: crawling and generation; retrieval-based seeding; in-context generation; automated quality control; grounding tasks in a site graph to enforce compositionality; deterministic replays and systematic validation; deployed on 50+ sites with multilingual and multi-hop coverage.
AI.result: A scalable GTA pipeline and a dynamic, reproducible benchmark; significant human–agent performance gap identified; enables detailed diagnostics and analysis.
AI.conclusion: Formalizes multi-hop web-agent task generation, presents an efficient data-creation pipeline, and releases a dynamic benchmark with reproducible evaluation.

title: Orthogonal Negative Guidance in Attention Feature Space for Text-to-Image Generation
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) models have become increasingly capable of generating high-quality images. Yet, enforcing the explicit absence of a specified object or attribute remains a fundamentally challenging problem. Existing approaches, including prompt negation, post-hoc editing, and negative guidance, remain insufficient for explicit concept suppression, often failing to remove the target concept or degrading overall image quality. To this end, we propose Orthogonal Negative Guidance in attention feature space, a training-free method that operates in the attention output space of MM-DiT-based T2I transformers. Our method orthogonalizes negative-prompt attention features with respect to positive-prompt features and subtracts only the orthogonal component, suppressing unwanted concepts while preserving desired semantics. Experiments on FLUX-dev and FLUX-schnell show that our method achieves favorable trade-offs between concept suppression, prompt alignment, and image quality. In human evaluation, our method outperforms the second-best baseline by 18.78%. We further show that our method supports multi-concept suppression and adjustable concept suppression.

AI.tldr: A training-free method for suppressing unwanted concepts in text-to-image generation by orthogonalizing negative-prompt attention features against positive-prompt features in MM-DiT-based transformers, selectively subtracting the orthogonal component to improve concept removal while preserving image quality; demonstrated on FLUX-dev/schnell with multi-concept suppression and adjustable levels, outperforming baselines in human evaluation (18.78%).
AI.motivation: Explicitly eliminating a specified concept in T2I outputs remains challenging with existing prompt negation, post-editing, and negative guidance. A training-free, attention-feature-space approach could provide robust suppression without retraining.
AI.method: Compute attention output features in MM-DiT-based T2I transformers for both positive (desired) and negative (to be suppressed) prompts. Orthogonalize the negative-prompt attention features with respect to the positive-prompt features, and subtract only the orthogonal component from the attention signals to suppress the target concept while preserving the rest of the semantics. No additional training required.
AI.result: The method achieves favorable trade-offs among concept suppression, prompt alignment, and image quality. Human evaluation shows the approach outperforms the second-best baseline by 18.78%. It also supports multi-concept suppression and adjustable suppression levels, with empirical validation on FLUX-dev and FLUX-schnell datasets.
AI.conclusion: Orthogonal Negative Guidance in attention feature space is an effective, training-free strategy for explicit concept suppression in T2I models, enabling controlled, multi-concept suppression without compromising image quality.

title: Gaze2Act: Gaze-Conditioned Vision-Language-Action Policies for Interactive Robot Manipulation
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have recently shown strong potential for robot learning by following language instructions. However, in practice, language alone is often insufficient to precisely convey human intent. It is difficult to describe which exact object to interact with among similar candidates, where to act on the object, or how the target may change during execution. To address this limitation, we propose Gaze2Act, a novel VLA framework that leverages human gaze as a dynamic and intuitive intent signal for complex interactive manipulation. Gaze2Act first bridges the ego-exo view gap by mapping first-person gaze into the robot's perspective through cross-view semantic matching, producing both an object mask and a gaze point for coarse-to-fine target specification. These cues are then integrated into the policy through perception-level prompting and action-level conditioning, allowing the robot to attend to relevant regions and execute precise interactions under dynamic intent. In a systematic evaluation across seven task categories and 16 real-robot tasks on a Unitree G1 humanoid, Gaze2Act achieves state-of-the-art performance in both intent accuracy and task success rate. It notably outperforms baselines in object disambiguation, fine-grained interaction, and dynamic intent steering. These results demonstrate that human gaze provides a natural, low-burden, and highly expressive modality for human-in-the-loop VLA control.

AI.tldr: Gaze2Act introduces a gaze-driven VLA framework for robot manipulation, mapping first-person gaze to the robot view to provide object masks and gaze points, which are integrated via perception-level prompting and action-level conditioning to handle dynamic intent; evaluated on 16 real-robot tasks with Unitree G1, achieving state-of-the-art intent accuracy and task success, especially in disambiguation and fine-grained interaction.
AI.motivation: Language instructions alone often fail to precisely convey which object to interact with, where to act, or how targets change during execution. Human gaze offers a natural, low-burden, and expressive signal to specify intent in interactive manipulation.
AI.method: Cross-view semantic matching translates first-person gaze into the robot’s perspective to produce an object mask and gaze point. These cues are fused into the policy through perception-level prompting and action-level conditioning to focus attention and guide precise interactions under dynamic intent.
AI.result: Systematic evaluation over seven task categories and 16 real-robot tasks on a Unitree G1 humanoid shows state-of-the-art performance in intent accuracy and task success rate, outperforming baselines in object disambiguation, fine-grained interaction, and dynamic intent steering.
AI.conclusion: Human gaze provides a natural and effective modality for human-in-the-loop VLA control, enabling dynamic intent specification and improved manipulation performance.

title: Causal Intelligence for Constraint-Aware Intervention Design to Induce State Transitions
summary: Driving a system from one state to another through targeted interventions is a fundamental challenge in science, yet most predictive models offer limited mechanistic insight and no principled framework for decision-making. Here we present COAST (Causally Optimal Actions for State Transitions), a causal-intelligence approach for the in-silico design of constrained interventions that induce user-defined state transitions. Given data characterizing source and target states, COAST learns context-specific causal graphs and structural causal models, attributes observed distributional shifts to mechanism-level causal drivers, and introduces a novel constraint-aware multi-objective optimization formulation that balances transition efficacy, intervention complexity, and target-state stability. The approach is modular and domain-agnostic, integrating feature selection, causal discovery, causal modeling, and intervention identification and evaluation through interchangeable components. Across synthetic benchmarks and real biological datasets, COAST recovers key causal drivers and identifies robust single- and multi-target intervention strategies that achieve desired state transitions, accompanied by transparent mechanistic rationales to guide experimental validation.

AI.tldr: COAST designs user-defined state transitions via a modular causal-intelligence pipeline that learns context-specific causal graphs and SCMs, attributes distributional shifts to causal drivers, and solves a constraint-aware multi-objective optimization to identify robust, interpretable interventions.
AI.motivation: There is a need for principled, mechanistic decision-making to steer complex systems from an initial to a target state; predictive models alone offer limited insight and no framework for actionable interventions.
AI.method: Modular pipeline: feature selection, causal discovery, causal modeling (structural causal models), and intervention identification/evaluation. Learns context-specific causal graphs from source/target state data, attributes shifts to mechanism-level drivers, and solves a constrained multi-objective optimization balancing efficacy, intervention complexity, and target-state stability; applicable across domains with interchangeable components.
AI.result: In synthetic benchmarks and real biological datasets, COAST recovers key causal drivers and identifies robust single- and multi-target interventions that realize the desired state transitions, with transparent mechanistic rationales to guide validation.
AI.conclusion: COAST offers a principled, modular approach for data-driven, interpretable intervention design in complex systems, enabling robust state transitions and experimental guidance across domains.

title: BenchTrace: A Benchmark for Testing Reflection Ability and Controlled Evolution in LLM Agents
summary: Self-evolving agents improve over time by reflecting on past failures, but existing evaluation is limited in two ways: it measures only task scores, leaving reflection quality unknown, and it relies on agents' own episode runs, offering no mechanism to target specific failure patterns. We present \textbf{BenchTrace}, a benchmark for evaluating self-evolution ability in LLM agents. BenchTrace is built on a snapshot-reflection dataset of 1,821 annotated episodes spanning six diverse tasks, and comprises a \textbf{Reflection Evaluation} that probes failure identification through targeted QA tasks, and an \textbf{Evolution Evaluation} that tests whether past failure experience translates into avoidance behavior in a controlled self-evolution simulation. Building on BenchTrace, we propose \textbf{failure avoidance rate (FAR)}, a new evaluation metric measuring the fraction of test cases in which the agent successfully avoids the target failure instance. Experiments with Qwen3-32B and GPT-4.1 reveal that both models fall below a 30\% end-to-end pass rate on reflection evaluation, with diagnosis as the primary bottleneck. Evolution evaluation shows that self-evolution methods generally improve FAR over the non-evolving baseline, but agents forget early lessons as noise episodes accumulate, and agents fail to generalize their reflections beyond the specific context, causing negative transfer across task contexts. Our correlation analysis further reveals that only a fully correct reflection is strongly associated with higher FAR. BenchTrace exposes concrete limits of current self-evolution approaches and provides a controlled, model-agnostic framework for targeted evaluation.

AI.tldr: BenchTrace: a benchmark to evaluate self-evolution in LLM agents, introducing Reflection Evaluation and Evolution Evaluation with a new metric FAR; findings show limited reflection quality and that self-evolution partially improves failure avoidance but suffers from forgetting and negative transfer; only fully correct reflections strongly predict higher FAR.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of self-evolution in LLM agents measures only task scores and relies on agents' own runs, missing reflection quality and targeted failure handling. A model-agnostic benchmark is needed to diagnose reflection capability and the translation of past failures into safer behavior.
AI.method: Construct a snapshot-reflection dataset of 1,821 annotated episodes across six tasks; develop Reflection Evaluation with targeted QA tasks to probe failure identification; develop Evolution Evaluation to test translation of past failures into avoidance behavior in a controlled self-evolution simulation; introduce Failure Avoidance Rate (FAR) as the fraction of test cases where the agent avoids the target failure; perform experiments on Qwen3-32B and GPT-4.1.
AI.result: Both models show <30% end-to-end pass rate on the reflection evaluation, with diagnosis being the primary bottleneck; evolution evaluation indicates self-evolution methods improve FAR relative to non-evolving baselines, but performance degrades over noise episodes (forgetting) and reflections do not generalize across contexts (negative transfer); fully correct reflections correlate strongly with higher FAR.
AI.conclusion: BenchTrace exposes concrete limits of current self-evolution approaches and provides a controlled, model-agnostic framework for targeted evaluation.

title: Semantic and Visual Evidence for Efficient Long-Video Reasoning: A Solution for the HD-EPIC VQA Challenge
summary: Understanding long-form egocentric videos remains challenging for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) due to limited context length and insufficient grounding of fine-grained visual details. The recently proposed HD-EPIC benchmark highlights these limitations: even strong long-context models achieve relatively low performance across diverse video question answering tasks. In this paper, we propose a unified framework that decouples long-video reasoning into two complementary forms of evidence: semantic evidence and visual evidence. Semantic evidence captures global procedural structure through a coarse-to-fine extraction pipeline, while object-centric visual evidence preserves fine-grained grounding through bounding boxes and visual embeddings. During inference, we formulate reasoning as a query-conditioned evidence retrieval and integration process, dynamically selecting relevant information from both sources. Our approach achieves competitive performance in the HD-EPIC-VQA Challenge across multiple task categories. More broadly, our results demonstrate that explicitly structuring, retrieving, and integrating semantic and visual evidence is critical for effective long-video understanding with MLLMs.

AI.tldr: A unified framework decouples long-video reasoning into two evidence streams: semantic evidence (global procedural structure via a coarse-to-fine pipeline) and visual evidence (object-centric grounding with bounding boxes and visual embeddings). Inference uses query-conditioned evidence retrieval and integration from both sources, yielding competitive HD-EPIC-VQA results and demonstrating that explicit structuring of semantic and visual evidence is key for long-video MLLMs.
AI.motivation: Long-form egocentric videos pose context-length and grounding challenges for multimodal LLMs. The HD-EPIC benchmark reveals persistent deficiencies in long-context reasoning and fine-grained grounding, motivating a system that can separately model global structure and local details.
AI.method: Propose two complementary evidence streams: semantic evidence capturing global procedural structure via a coarse-to-fine extraction pipeline; visual evidence preserving fine-grained grounding through bounding boxes and visual embeddings. Inference treats reasoning as query-conditioned evidence retrieval and integration, dynamically selecting relevant information from both sources.
AI.result: The approach achieves competitive performance in the HD-EPIC-VQA Challenge across multiple task categories, indicating strong practicality. It demonstrates that explicit structuring, retrieving, and integrating semantic and visual evidence improves long-video understanding with MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly structuring, retrieving, and integrating semantic and visual evidence is critical for effective long-video understanding with multimodal LLMs.

title: A Heterogeneous Architecture for Robot RL Beyond GPU-Dominant Paradigms
summary: Simulation-based RL for contemporary robot control is increasingly organized around GPU-resident simulation: physics, rollout collection, and learning are placed on a single GPU-centric execution path. This paradigm has greatly improved training speed, but it has also encouraged a default assumption that efficient training requires physics to reside on the GPU. We revisit this assumption. Our view is that, in simulation-dominated robot control, the essential question is not which processor runs physics, but whether simulation throughput, policy learning, and runtime synchronization form an efficient end-to-end loop. We present UniLab, a heterogeneous CPU-simulation / GPU-learning architecture that decouples CPU-parallel simulation from GPU policy updates through a unified runtime for data movement, buffering, and synchronization. UniLab is implemented as a complete and extensible training system using MuJoCoUni and MotrixSim CPU-batched physics backends, supporting PPO, SAC, FlashSAC, TD3, and APPO. On representative simulation-based robot control tasks, UniLab improves end-to-end training efficiency by 3--10$\times$ under the same hardware configuration, while reducing dependence on the NVIDIA CUDA-based software stack and supporting cross-platform execution on the Apple macOS platform and the AMD ROCm and Intel XPU accelerator backends. These results show that GPU simulation is an effective path to efficient training, but not a necessary one, broadening the practical system choices available for robot RL training. Project page: https://github.com/unilabsim/UniLab.

AI.tldr: UniLab introduces a CPU-simulation / GPU-learning, heterogeneous architecture for simulation-based robot RL that decouples CPU-parallel physics from GPU policy updates via a unified data-movement runtime; it achieves 3–10× end-to-end training efficiency on representative tasks, with cross-platform support and reduced CUDA dependence.
AI.motivation: Challenging the assumption that physics must reside on the GPU for efficient RL training; instead, optimize the end-to-end training loop (throughput, learning, and synchronization) using a heterogeneous architecture to widen platform options and potentially reduce NVIDIA tooling reliance.
AI.method: Develop a unified runtime that buffers and synchronizes data between CPU-based batched physics backends (MuJoCoUni, MotrixSim) and GPU-based learning, decoupling simulation from policy updates. Implemented in UniLab with support for PPO, SAC, FlashSAC, TD3, and APPO, and enabling cross-platform backends (macOS, ROCm, Intel XPU).
AI.result: Achieves 3–10× improvements in end-to-end training efficiency on representative simulation-based robot control tasks under the same hardware configuration; demonstrates reduced dependency on CUDA and broad cross-platform execution.
AI.conclusion: GPU-based simulation is highly effective but not mandatory for efficient RL training; UniLab shows that a CPU-simulation / GPU-learning path can yield substantial efficiency gains and platform flexibility, broadening practical options for robot RL training.

title: Label-Free Reinforcement Learning via Cross-Model Entropy
summary: Post-training large language models with reinforcement learning is bottlenecked by the reward signal. Existing approaches require either ground-truth verifiable rewards, restricting training to domains with automatic correctness checks (e.g., mathematics, code execution), or human preference labels, which are expensive to collect and prone to reward hacking. Recent label-free methods replace ground-truth verifiers with self-referential signals like majority voting or token entropy over a model's own outputs, but risk reinforcing a model's own errors. In this work we propose Cross-Model Entropy (CME), the mean log-likelihood of a generator's response under a separate verifier model, as a label-free reward signal for RL post-training. CME is continuous, training-free, and grounded in the principle that responses a verifier finds unsurprising are likely correct or high quality. Because the verifier is independent of the generator, the signal cannot be gamed through self-consistency. We integrate CME into GRPO with no other changes to the training loop, extending label-free RL to open-ended instruction following -- a regime where self-referential signals are inapplicable or poorly suited. On open-ended instruction following (UltraFeedback prompts, evaluated on AlpacaEval 2.0), CME rewards beat the untrained base in head-to-head LLM-as-Judge comparisons across four model families (Qwen, Llama, Gemma, OLMo) and three training regimes (pretrained, SFT, and instruction-tuned), with tie-adjusted win rates ranging from 52.5% to 71.4%. Code will be released upon publication.

AI.tldr: Proposes Cross-Model Entropy (CME) as a label-free reward for RL post-training of LLMs; uses a separate verifier model to compute mean log-likelihood of generator outputs; integrates into GRPO; shows improvements on open-ended instruction following across multiple models and regimes; code to be released.
AI.motivation: RLHF reward signals are bottlenecked by the availability and reliability of ground-truth or human labels. Label-free signals risk reinforcing errors; need a continuous, training-free, verifiable signal that generalizes beyond domains with automatic correctness checks.
AI.method: Introduce CME: mean log-likelihood of a generator's response under a separate verifier model. The verifier is independent of the generator, reducing the risk of gaming via self-consistency. CME is integrated into the GRPO framework without altering the training loop.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on open-ended instruction following (UltraFeedback prompts; AlpacaEval 2.0) shows CME-rewarded models outperform the untrained base in head-to-head LLM-as-Judge comparisons across four model families (Qwen, Llama, Gemma, OLMo) and three training regimes (pretrained, SFT, instruction-tuned). Tie-adjusted win rates range from 52.5% to 71.4%.
AI.conclusion: CME provides a continuous, training-free, label-free reward signal for RL post-training that is grounded in a verifier’s surprise at the generator’s outputs. It enables label-free RL in open-ended instruction settings and appears robust across model families and training regimes; code will be released.

title: Tailoring the Curriculum: Student-Centered Reasoning Distillation via Dynamic Data-Model Compatibility
summary: Reasoning distillation transfers complex reasoning abilities from large language models (LLMs) to smaller ones, yet its success depends on how well the training data align with the student model. This paper introduces the Data-Model Compatibility (DMC) metric, which can be used to assess the suitability of a dataset for reasoning distillation on a student model. DMC provides an assessment by jointly considering data quality, relative difficulty, and student capability. We validated the effectiveness of DMC from two perspectives: (1) DMC exhibits a strong correlation with reasoning distillation performance; and (2) using DMC as the criterion for data selection leads to improved reasoning distillation performance. Both findings are consistently demonstrated across multiple student models and tasks. Moreover, since the DMC of each dataset dynamically changes during training, our experiments demonstrate that dynamically selecting datasets based on DMC can further enhance performance.

AI.tldr: Data-Model Compatibility (DMC) quantifies dataset suitability for reasoning distillation and improves performance through data selection, including dynamic adaptation during training.
AI.motivation: Reasoning distillation success hinges on alignment between training data and the student model; existing data selection methods are heuristic and may misalign with model capabilities.
AI.method: Define DMC by integrating data quality, relative difficulty, and student capability. Empirically validate via correlations between DMC and distillation performance across multiple student models and tasks; use DMC as a criterion for data selection and implement dynamic dataset scheduling during training.
AI.result: DMC correlates with reasoning distillation performance; using DMC for data selection improves distillation outcomes; dynamic DMC-based data selection yields further gains across models and tasks.
AI.conclusion: DMC is an effective, adaptable metric for assessing and selecting data for reasoning distillation, generalizable across models and tasks, with potential for dynamic training strategies.

title: ParCo-SDF: Learning Prior-Free Partial-to-Complete Signed Distance Fields of Deformable Objects
summary: This study addresses the partial-to-complete geometry reconstruction of deformable objects (DOs) from point-cloud observations toward precise DO manipulation. Recent DO reconstruction approaches often adopt implicit neural representations (INRs) to model continuous surfaces as well as capture structural variability. However, these methods typically rely on object-specific shape priors that improve training stability and limit generalization. To figure it out, we introduce ParCo-SDF, a two-stage partial-to-complete signed distance field (SDF) reconstruction framework consisting of temporal geometry encoding followed by FiLM-conditioned SDF prediction. The temporal encoder captures structural similarity across DO sequence, enabling prior-free stable training. FiLM-based conditioning preserves reconstruction expressivity while reducing network complexity. We evaluate the proposed method against a state-of-the-art DO surface reconstruction baseline on a rubber band manipulation dataset, demonstrating robust and high-fidelity reconstruction under severe occlusions.

AI.tldr: ParCo-SDF: a two-stage, prior-free partial-to-complete SDF reconstruction for deformable objects using a temporal geometry encoder and FiLM-conditioned SDF predictor, achieving robust high-fidelity reconstruction under occlusions on a rubber-band dataset.
AI.motivation: Enable accurate surface reconstruction of deformable objects from partial observations without object-specific priors, addressing occlusions and variability to aid manipulation tasks.
AI.method: Stage 1: temporal geometry encoding to exploit sequence-level structural similarity for prior-free stable training. Stage 2: FiLM-conditioned SDF network for partial-to-complete reconstruction, balancing expressivity and network complexity.
AI.result: Outperforms a state-of-the-art DO surface reconstruction baseline on a rubber band manipulation dataset, demonstrating robustness and high fidelity under severe occlusions.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that a temporal prior-free encoding plus FiLM conditioning can yield stable, expressive DO surface reconstructions with strong performance under occlusion; further validation on diverse DO types and ablation studies would bolster generalizability.

title: RoboWits: Unexpected Challenges for Robotic Creative Problem Solving
comment: The first two authors contributed equally
summary: The ability to reason, adapt, and creatively solve problems under unexpected challenges is essential for robots operating in real-world environments. However, current robotic benchmarks primarily emphasize skill-level execution and provide limited insight into such cognitive reasoning capabilities. We introduce RoboWits, a bi-manual robotic benchmark designed to systematically evaluate cognitive reasoning, creative tool use, and robustness to unexpected conditions. To enable scalable construction of high-quality reasoning-centric unexpected scenarios, we propose an automated task generation pipeline formulated as a multi-agent cooperative framework, comprising agents for seed task generation and verification, metric generation, scene generation, and task mutation. Using the pipeline, we curated 30 diverse seed tasks and 208 tasks with mutations and graded difficulty across geometry, material, and assembly-based reasoning. We benchmark popular robot policies, pre-trained VLAs, and oracle-state planners. Our results reveal a significant performance gap: while pre-trained VLAs exhibit preliminary success on seed tasks after single-task fine-tuning, they struggle to perform on mutated tasks, implying their brittleness in manipulation tasks requiring reasoning, strategy adaptation, and robustness to deceptive or constrained environments. Project page is available at https://umass-embodied-agi.github.io/RoboWits.

AI.tldr: RoboWits is a bi-manual robotic benchmark for cognitive reasoning, tool use, and robustness, built via an automated multi-agent pipeline that generates 30 seed tasks and 208 mutated tasks with varying difficulty; results show pre-trained vision-language agents are brittle on mutated tasks, highlighting the gap in reasoning capabilities for manipulation under deceptive or constrained conditions.
AI.motivation: There is a need to evaluate cognitive reasoning, creative tool use, and robustness in robotics beyond traditional skill-focused benchmarks. A scalable platform with automated task generation can systematically stress-test reasoning under unexpected or constrained conditions.
AI.method: Introduce RoboWits benchmark and an automated task-generation pipeline framed as a multi-agent cooperative framework: seed task generation/verification, metric generation, scene generation, and task mutation. Curated 30 seed tasks and 208 mutated tasks with graded difficulty across geometry, material, and assembly-based reasoning. Benchmarks include popular robot policies, pre-trained VLAs, and oracle-state planners.
AI.result: The evaluation reveals a large performance gap: pre-trained VLAs show some success on seed tasks after single-task fine-tuning but fail on mutated tasks, indicating brittleness in manipulation tasks requiring reasoning, strategy adaptation, and robustness to deceptive/constraint-based environments.
AI.conclusion: RoboWits exposes a reasoning-centric gap in current robotic systems and provides a scalable pipeline to generate robust, reasoning-challenging tasks; it motivates developing more capable reasoning-enabled agents and tools for manipulation under unexpected conditions.

title: Rethinking Literature Search Evaluation: Deep Research Helps, and Human Citation Lists Are Not a Ground Truth
summary: We study large-scale literature search from two complementary angles: improving the retrieval pipeline, and stress-testing the human reference list as an evaluation target. First, we implement a Deep Research pipeline that processes the full query paper and expands the retrieved results breadth-first along their bibliographies, and show that it substantially outperforms vanilla API-only search, raising recall on RollingEval-Jun25 (a 250-paper literature-search benchmark) from below 20% to above 80%. Second, we use a neutral LLM-as-a-judge to determine if human references are sound ground truth for the task. We find significant limitations: only 51% of human citations are judged moderately relevant or higher, against 86--88% for the strongest AI-based re-rankers. We study this gap on the OpenAlex co-authorship graph, finding that humans are 2.5x more likely than the best AI re-rankers to cite a direct collaborator. Together, our results argue against single-axis literature-search evaluation: recall, topical-relevance scoring, ranked-list diversity, and a co-authorship-distance diagnostic each measure complementary properties of citation quality and should be reported jointly.

AI.tldr: A two-pronged study improves large-scale literature search: (1) a Deep Research retrieval pipeline that expands results along bibliographies, boosting recall from <20% to >80% on a 250-paper benchmark; (2) a neutral LLM-based judge assessing whether human citations are sound ground truth, revealing biases and limitations in human vs AI ranking. The work argues for multi-axis evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: Address limitations in current literature-search evaluation by (a) enhancing retrieval pipelines to maximize recall and (b) critically evaluating the validity of human reference lists as ground truth, using AI-based judgments and network analysis to reveal biases.
AI.method: 1) Develop Deep Research pipeline that processes the full query paper and performs breadth-first expansion along bibliographies, evaluated against RollingEval-Jun25 (250-paper benchmark). 2) Use a neutral LLM-as-a-judge to rate the topical relevance of human references. 3) Analyze OpenAlex co-authorship graph to compare human vs AI re-ranking for citing direct collaborators.
AI.result: Recall on RollingEval-Jun25 improves from below 20% (vanilla search) to above 80% with Deep Research. Only 51% of human citations are moderately relevant or higher, compared with 86–88% for the strongest AI re-rankers. Humans are about 2.5× more likely than AI re-rankers to cite a direct collaborator (OpenAlex graph).
AI.conclusion: Single-axis evaluation is insufficient for literature search. A multi-axis reporting scheme—recall, topical-relevance, ranked-list diversity, and co-authorship-distance metrics—should be reported jointly to capture complementary aspects of citation quality.

title: One Click per Cell Type Suffices: Training-free Group Interaction for Cell Instance Segmentation
summary: Cell instance segmentation models trained on cell-specific datasets suffer severe performance drops on out-of-distribution cell types, while interactive foundation models overcome this through per-instance prompting at a cost that is prohibitively expensive for histopathology images containing hundreds to thousands of densely packed instances. We introduce Group Prompting, a new paradigm that shifts interactive segmentation from per-instance $O(N)$ to per-type $O(T)$, where a single click per cell type suffices to segment all instances of that type. Our key observation is that the frozen image encoder of the Segment Anything Model (SAM) already clusters same-type cells in its feature space before any prompt is given. Exploiting this property, we propose Chain-of-Prompts (CoP), a training-free framework that recursively expands a single user click by (1) identifying reliable same-type locations through non-parametric gating of multi-scale encoder features, and (2) selecting the most spatially distant reliable point as the next prompt to maximize coverage. On three cell-type-annotated benchmarks, CoP with one click per type retains over 90% of per-instance performance and surpasses fully-supervised methods without any additional training. On four morphologically homogeneous benchmarks, a single click retains over 99%. Project Page: https://shjo-april.github.io/Chain-of-Prompts/

AI.tldr: Training-free, one-click-per-type interactive segmentation for dense cell images via Chain-of-Prompts; expands a single user click to all instances of a cell type, leveraging SAM's feature-space clustering.
AI.motivation: Overcoming out-of-distribution generalization in cell instance segmentation and the high cost of per-instance prompting in densely packed histopathology images.
AI.method: Chain-of-Prompts (CoP): recursively expand a single click by non-parametric gating of multi-scale SAM encoder features to identify reliable same-type locations, then pick the farthest reliable point as the next prompt; operates with a frozen SAM encoder; per-type prompting reduces prompts from O(N) to O(T).
AI.result: On three cell-type benchmarks, one click per type retains >90% of per-instance performance and surpasses fully supervised methods without extra training; on four morphologically homogeneous benchmarks, a single click retains >99%.
AI.conclusion: CoP demonstrates that a single prompt per cell type can propagate to all instances, enabling scalable, training-free interactive segmentation; leverages intrinsic clustering in SAM feature space to reduce annotation cost.

title: DynaFLIP: Rethinking Robotics Perception via Tri-Modal-Dynamics Guided Representation
summary: Robot manipulation critically depends on perception that preserves the action-relevant aspects of a scene. Yet most robot learning pipelines are built upon visual encoders pre-trained for static recognition or vision-language alignment, leaving motion understanding to downstream policies. We introduce DynaFLIP, a dynamics-aware multimodal pre-training framework that pushes motion understanding upstream into perception. We construct image-language-3D flow triplets from heterogeneous human and robot videos, and use these triplets as training-time supervision to shape an image-only encoder. Our key idea is to encourage the three modalities to span a small simplex volume in the shared hyperspherical space -- a smaller simplex volume indicating stronger alignment. To avoid the geometric ambiguity and trivial collapse of naive volume minimization, we combine simplex-volume minimization with a cosine regularizer and a contrastive objective. Our analyses show that DynaFLIP focuses on control-relevant regions critical for manipulation. The resulting dynamics-aware representations serve as reusable visual backbones and consistently outperform baselines across diverse downstream policies, including VLAs. We validate this across diverse simulation and real-world setups, with gains reaching +22.5% under out-of-distribution scenarios. Our results suggest that robot generalization improves when visual representations are trained to encode not just what is present, but how the world changes under action.

AI.tldr: DynaFLIP introduces dynamics-aware multimodal pre-training using image-language-3D flow triplets to shape a dynamics-focused visual encoder, yielding improved manipulation performance, especially out-of-distribution, across simulations and real robots.
AI.motivation: Robot manipulation requires perception that preserves action-relevant dynamics. Static recognition pretraining often ignores motion cues essential for control, limiting generalization to changing scenes and actions.
AI.method: Construct image-language-3D flow triplets from heterogeneous human and robot videos and use triplet supervision to train an image-only encoder. Encourage alignment by minimizing the simplex volume spanned by the three modalities in a shared hyperspherical space, aided by a cosine regularizer and a contrastive objective to avoid collapse.
AI.result: The learned representations focus on control-relevant regions, serve as reusable visual backbones, and consistently outperform baselines across diverse downstream policies, including VLAs. Validation in both simulation and real-world setups shows gains up to 22.5% under out-of-distribution scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Dynamics-aware representations enable robot generalization by encoding not only what is present but how the world changes under action; pre-training with motion-aware multi-modal cues enhances downstream manipulation performance.

title: Surfacing Isolated Learners with Outcome-Independent Mediation of Feedback between Teachers and Students Using AI
comment: Accepted to HAI-Agency Workshop on Orchestrating Human and AI Agency for Proactive and Reflective Learning
summary: AI-augmented classrooms generate rich teacher and student feedback before graded outcomes become available, yet these signals can be difficult to translate into timely instructional decisions. We propose an interpretable decision layer: a transparent mechanism that ranks course topics requiring attention without using grades or post-hoc outcome labels. The approach combines three signals: student learning difficulty prevalence, disagreement between learner self-reports and observed difficulties, and unresolved teacher concerns. The output is a ranked set of topic priorities with per-topic decision records explaining each ranking. In one graduate CS course offering ($n=5$ instructor interviews; $n=279$ survey responses), prioritized topics aligned with instructor concerns (top-5 overlap 3/5; Spearman $ρ=0.80$) and student-reported topic difficulty ($ρ=0.46$, $p=.048$). Multi-signal integration also surfaced learners not identified through individual signal sources alone (AUC $=0.96$ vs. $0.91$ for gap prevalence alone). Reflective thinking, help-seeking, and self-efficacy provided additional evidence that student behavioral signals align with learning-related constructs. While preliminary, these findings suggest that transparent coordination mechanisms may help support human-AI co-agency when feedback is incomplete.

AI.tldr: An interpretable decision layer fuses three signals to prioritize course topics without grade data, yielding explanations per topic. It aligns with instructor concerns, correlates with student-reported difficulty, and improves detection of at-risk learners via multi-signal integration in a graduate CS course.
AI.motivation: Provide timely, actionable instructional decisions from AI-generated feedback when graded outcomes are not yet available, by delivering transparent, per-topic prioritization with interpretable reasoning.
AI.method: Develop an interpretable decision layer that combines three signals—prevalence of student difficulty, disagreement between self-reports and observed difficulties, and unresolved teacher concerns—and outputs a ranked list of topic priorities with per-topic decision records. Evaluate in a single graduate CS course (n=5 instructor interviews; n=279 surveys) using Spearman correlations to compare rankings with instructor concerns and student-reported difficulty, and compare AUC for multi-signal fusion against a single signal (gap prevalence). Add qualitative observations on reflective thinking, help-seeking, and self-efficacy.
AI.result: Top-5 overlap with instructor concerns: 3/5. Spearman ρ = 0.80. Correlation with student-reported topic difficulty ρ = 0.46, p = .048. AUC for multi-signal integration = 0.96 versus 0.91 for gap prevalence alone. Multi-signal approach surfaced learners not identified by individual signals. Behavioral signals (reflective thinking, help-seeking, self-efficacy) corroborate learning-related constructs.
AI.conclusion: Transparent coordination mechanisms for human–AI collaboration show promise to guide instructional focus when feedback is incomplete, offering interpretable topic priorities and records. Findings are preliminary and based on a single course, warrant further validation across contexts.

title: Recovering Policy-Induced Errors: Benchmarking and Trajectory Synthesis for Robust GUI Agents
comment: ICML 2026 Spotlight. 36 pages, 19 figures, includes appendix
summary: While GUI agents have advanced rapidly, they often lack the robustness to recover from their own errors, hindering real-world deployment. To bridge this gap at both the evaluation and data levels, we introduce GUI-RobustEval and propose Robustness-driven Trajectory Synthesis. GUI-RobustEval contains $1,216$ executable test cases that systematically measure error recovery capabilities across a broad and realistic spectrum of error modes. At the data level, RoTS is a scalable synthesis framework that creates $800k$ high-quality data via a tree-based pipeline that proactively discovers diverse error modes and synthesizes corresponding recovery steps. Our two models, RoTS-7B and RoTS-32B, fine-tuned on our dataset, both demonstrate significant gains on GUI-RobustEval and traditional GUI benchmarks. Notably, RoTS-32B achieves state-of-the-art performance on OSWorld, with a $47.4\%$ success rate and a $33.8\%$ All-Pass@4 score, suggesting that improved long-horizon error recovery ability contributes to both robustness and overall performance. Our code is available at https://github.com/AlibabaResearch/RoTS.

AI.tldr: Introduces GUI-RobustEval as a benchmark for GUI error-recovery and RoTS as a scalable data-synthesis framework; fine-tuned RoTS-7B/32B achieve strong gains, with RoTS-32B setting OSWorld SOTA.
AI.motivation: GUI agents often fail to recover from their own errors; need realistic evaluation benchmarks and scalable data generation to train robust, long-horizon recovery policies for real-world deployment.
AI.method: GUI-RobustEval: 1,216 executable test cases assessing error-recovery across diverse error modes. RoTS: a tree-based synthesis pipeline generating 800k high-quality data by discovering diverse error modes and constructing recovery steps; two models RoTS-7B and RoTS-32B are fine-tuned on this data.
AI.result: Significant improvements on GUI-RobustEval and standard GUI benchmarks; RoTS-32B achieves OSWorld state-of-the-art with 47.4% success and 33.8% All-Pass@4.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that enhanced long-horizon error recovery boosts robustness and overall GUI task performance; provides datasets and a synthesis framework that can advance robustness research in GUI agents; code released.

title: Theoretical Foundations and Effective Algorithms for Policy-Aware Simulator Learning
summary: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) agents typically learn world models by minimizing predictive loss. However, powerful RL optimizers inevitably exploit minor model inaccuracies, leading to simulator exploitation and a reality gap where policies succeed in simulation but fail in the real world. We propose that the objective for learning simulators should be strategic robustness rather than predictive accuracy, and formulate this as a zero-sum minimax game between a model player and an adversarial policy player. We provide a comprehensive theoretical analysis: (1) an online learning guarantee showing the game is learnable with sublinear regret bounds; (2) a tractable critic-based simplification bounding the global policy-value gap by the local critic's loss; and (3) an Error-MDP duality, proving that finding the worst-case policy is formally dual to a standard RL problem where the reward is the one-step critic error. This duality yields a provably convergent active data selection algorithm. Experiments on continuous control tasks demonstrate that our approach reduces prediction error in strategically important regions by $1.5$-$2.2\times$ and enables policies trained purely in simulation to match near-optimal real-world performance.

AI.tldr: Frames world-model learning as a minimax strategic robustness problem to close the sim-to-real gap, rather than purely predictive accuracy; introduces online learning guarantees, a critic-based bound, and an Error-MMD duality that yields a convergent active data-selection algorithm; empirically improves loss in critical regions and matches real-world performance after simulation-only training.
AI.motivation: Predictive accuracy in world models often leads to simulator exploitation by RL policies; real-world transfer fails due to model inaccuracies exploited by optimizers. The work argues for robustness of policies in strategically important regions and provides theoretical and algorithmic tools to achieve this.
AI.method: Zero-sum minimax game between a model (world) player and an adversarial policy player. The approach includes: (1) online learning guarantees with sublinear regret; (2) a tractable critic-based simplification that bounds the global policy-value gap by the local critic's loss; (3) an Error-MDP duality showing that worst-case policy search is dual to a standard RL problem with reward equal to the one-step critic error; this yields a convergent active data selection algorithm.
AI.result: The method reduces predictive error in strategically important regions by 1.5–2.2× and enables policies trained purely in simulation to achieve near-optimal real-world performance.
AI.conclusion: Strategic robustness in world-model learning can close the sim-to-real gap. The proposed minimax formulation, coupled with online learning guarantees and dualities, provides both theoretical assurances and practical data-selection machinery, with strong empirical gains in control tasks.

title: DenseSteer: Steering Small Language Models towards Dense Math Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning abilities, while smaller models (<= 3B parameters) significantly underperform on multi-step reasoning tasks. Based on empirical analyses of the Qwen-2.5 model family on math reasoning benchmarks, we find that more proficient reasoning is associated with fewer reasoning steps but higher information density per step, a property we term Dense Reasoning. Motivated by this observation, we propose DenseSteer, a training-free inference-time steering framework that enhances small-model reasoning by modulating internal representations toward dense reasoning patterns. Experiments show that our method yields consistent accuracy improvements without increasing token-level Negative Log-Likelihood, highlighting dense reasoning as an effective structural approach to mathematical problem solving.

AI.tldr: DenseSteer enhances small-model math reasoning by steering internal representations toward Dense Reasoning—fewer steps with higher information density—achieving accuracy gains without increased token-level NLL.
AI.motivation: Bridge the performance gap between large language models and small models on multi-step math reasoning by leveraging the discovered Dense Reasoning property and avoiding retraining.
AI.method: A training-free, inference-time steering framework (DenseSteer) that modulates internal representations to promote dense, high-information-density reasoning steps, thereby biasing the model toward concise and information-rich solutions.
AI.result: Small models exhibit consistent accuracy improvements on math reasoning benchmarks when using DenseSteer, without increasing token-level Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL), indicating improved reasoning quality without cost to token forecasts.
AI.conclusion: Dense reasoning is an effective structural paradigm for mathematical problem solving, enabling small models to perform better by increasing per-step information density and reducing unnecessary reasoning steps, without additional training.

title: Comparative evaluation of photogrammetric reconstruction methods and 3D Gaussian Splatting for road surface roughness analysis
summary: Image-based 3D reconstruction offers a low-cost alternative to traditional sensor-based techniques for road surface assessment. This study compares four reconstruction pipelines--COLMAP, Meshroom, Metashape, and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)--to evaluate their ability to estimate road surface roughness from smartphone imagery. All point clouds were processed in CloudCompare using a consistent workflow involving orientation alignment, segmentation, normal estimation, and roughness computation at neighborhood radiuses of 0.2, 0.4, and 0.6 model units. The results show that COLMAP provides the highest sensitivity to micro-texture, while Meshroom yields balanced reconstructions with moderate roughness variation. Metashape produces the smoothest geometry due to its internal filtering, and 3DGS captures visible irregularities but exhibits higher noise and lower density. The comparison demonstrates that open-source pipelines are viable for relative roughness evaluation, offering a practical approach for low-cost pavement monitoring.

AI.tldr: Open-source 3D reconstruction pipelines (COLMAP, Meshroom, Metashape, 3DGS) can estimate road surface roughness from smartphone imagery; COLMAP best captures micro-texture, Meshroom balanced, Metashape smooths geometry, 3DGS noisy/low-density; radii 0.2–0.6 units; CloudCompare-based workflow.
AI.motivation: Assess feasibility and relative performance of open-source pipelines for estimating road surface roughness from consumer-grade imagery, to enable low-cost pavement monitoring.
AI.method: Compare four photogrammetry pipelines (COLMAP, Meshroom, Metashape, 3D Gaussian Splatting) using smartphone imagery; process point clouds in CloudCompare with orientation alignment, segmentation, normal estimation, and roughness computation at neighborhood radii 0.2, 0.4, 0.6 model units.
AI.result: COLMAP: highest sensitivity to micro-texture; Meshroom: balanced reconstructions with moderate roughness variation; Metashape: smoothest geometry due to internal filtering; 3DGS: captures visible irregularities but higher noise and lower density.
AI.conclusion: Open-source pipelines are viable for relative roughness evaluation, offering a practical approach for low-cost pavement monitoring.

title: Moment Matching Q-Learning
comment: 23 pages, 14 figures, 10 tables, accepted by ICML 2026
summary: Score-based and flow-based generative models exhibit remarkable expressive capacity in capturing complex distributions, and have been extensively deployed in tasks ranging from image generation to reinforcement learning. Nevertheless, these models suffer from prolonged inference latency, which imposes a significant computational bottleneck in RL with iterative sampling. To overcome this limitation, we propose a new framework named Moment Matching Q-Learning (MoMa QL), which utilizes a technique from statistical hypothesis testing known as maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) that intend to match all orders of statistics between the original and target distribution. By enforcing strong regularization on all moment statistics, this algorithm guarantees distribution-level convergence for conditional score function and remains stable under various hyperparameters. Empirically, we show that our method MoMa QL is more computationally efficient with a comparable if not competitive performance in various D4RL tasks. Remarkably, by accelerating the action sampling process for flow-based policies, MoMa QL demonstrates superior performance in offline-to-online RL tasks because of faster and stronger adaptability for online interactive finetuning.

AI.tldr: MoMa QL leverages maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) to match all moments between original and target distributions in Q-learning, enabling stable distribution-level convergence and faster sampling for flow-based policies; empirically shows improved computational efficiency with competitive performance, and superior offline-to-online RL due to faster online finetuning.
AI.motivation: Score-based and flow-based generative models offer rich expressiveness but incur long inference latency, creating a bottleneck in reinforcement learning that relies on iterative sampling; the need is to reduce latency while maintaining or improving learning performance.
AI.method: Introduce Moment Matching Q-Learning (MoMa QL) that uses MMD to enforce alignment of all moment statistics between the original and target distributions for the conditional score function, applying strong regularization across all moments to ensure distribution-level convergence and stability across hyperparameters.
AI.result: Empirically, MoMa QL achieves lower computational cost with competitive performance on D4RL tasks; accelerating action sampling for flow-based policies yields improved offline-to-online RL performance due to faster and more robust online fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: MoMa QL provides a principled, distribution-level regularization framework that reduces inference latency in RL with negligible or favorable impact on performance, with particular strength in offline-to-online settings where faster online adaptation is advantageous.

title: Provably Secure Agent Guardrail
summary: As large language models transition from bounded generative engines to agents with expansive execution privileges, AI going out of control precipitates a fundamental crisis in artificial intelligence security. Existing defense architectures heavily rely on empirical semantic guardrails and probabilistic large model adjudicators, mechanisms that fail to provide deterministic security lower bounds when facing complex semantic symbol decoupling attacks. To overcome this empirical semantic guardrail dilemma, this paper proposes a new security paradigm for agents based on the fundamental limitations of logical reasoning. Based on this paradigm, we further introduce an executable Proof-Constrained Action (ePCA) framework with a neural symbolic isolation architecture. This framework abandons semantic trust in natural language, forcing agents to losslessly formalize their intentions into first-order logical mathematical constraints before performing physical operations. Empirical evaluations of macroscopic and microscopic two-dimensional dynamic adversarial systems demonstrate that our formal verification mechanism achieves zero attack success rate and zero false positive rate across the evaluated scenarios, with extremely low computational latency. This research provides a conditional formal foundation under explicit system assumptions and an engineering paradigm for constructing the underlying defense foundation for future intelligent systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes an executable constraint-based defense (ePCA) that compels agents to translate intentions into first-order logic constraints before acting, yielding deterministic security guarantees in evaluations.
AI.motivation: Current defenses rely on empirical guardrails and probabilistic adjudicators, which do not provide deterministic security lower bounds and fail under complex semantic decoupling attacks; a fundamental crisis in AI security motivates a formal, logic-based approach.
AI.method: Introduce a security paradigm grounded in the limitations of logical reasoning and develop an executable Proof-Constrained Action (ePCA) framework with a neural-symbolic isolation architecture that requires agents to losseslessly formalize intentions into first-order logical mathematical constraints prior to performing physical operations.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on macroscopic and microscopic 2D dynamic adversarial systems show zero attack success rate and zero false positives, with extremely low computational latency, under the formal verification mechanism.
AI.conclusion: Provides a conditional formal foundation under explicit system assumptions and an engineering paradigm for constructing the defense foundation for future intelligent systems.

title: Uni-RCM: Unified Reference-guided Cross-modal Mapping for Multi-Class Anomaly Detection
comment: This work has been submitted IEEE for potential publication
summary: Multi-modal industrial anomaly detection typically relies on separate models for each product category, fundamentally limiting practical scalability. When shifting to a unified paradigm that handles diverse classes simultaneously, detection accuracy often degrades due to inter-class interference and feature manifold confusion. To overcome these challenges, we propose a Unified Reference guided Cross-modal Mapping framework, named Uni-RCM. At its core, we propose a reference guide block to dynamically filter out category-specific noise by introducing a learnable reference feature, which captures the commonalities across different modalities. Besides, an offline residual quantizer is proposed to characterize the normal distribution by multiple cascaded codebooks. Extensive evaluations on the MVTec-3D AD dataset demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance in the challenging multi-class setting and in terms of image-level detection and pixel-level localization.

AI.tldr: Unified reference-guided cross-modal mapping to enable scalable multi-class industrial anomaly detection; introduces a learnable reference feature to filter noise across modalities and an offline residual quantizer with cascaded codebooks to model normality, achieving state-of-the-art results on MVTec-3D AD in both image-level and pixel-level tasks.
AI.motivation: Multi-modal industrial anomaly detection commonly relies on separate, per-class models, which hinders scalability. When a unified multi-class model is used, inter-class interference and feature manifold confusion degrade detection accuracy. A unified approach should suppress category-specific noise and robustly model normal distributions across modalities.
AI.method: A unified reference guided cross-modal mapping (Uni-RCM) framework with (i) a reference guide block that uses a learnable reference feature to dynamically filter category-specific noise and capture cross-modal commonalities, and (ii) an offline residual quantizer consisting of multiple cascaded codebooks to characterize the normal distribution. The approach enables cross-modal alignment and robust anomaly scoring in a unified, multi-class setting.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on the MVTec-3D AD dataset show state-of-the-art performance in the challenging multi-class setting, with gains in both image-level anomaly detection and pixel-level localization.
AI.conclusion: Uni-RCM demonstrates that a reference-guided cross-modal mapping combined with cascaded residual quantization can effectively suppress inter-class interference and accurately model normality, achieving superior multi-class anomaly detection and localization on 3D industrial data.

title: Planning with the Views via Scene Self-Exploration
summary: Can VLMs predict how each camera move changes the view, and plan many such moves ahead? We call this capability view planning, requiring (1)understanding how a single action transforms the view, and (2)composing many such transformations across multi-turn plans to identify a target view. We probe both abilities in our proposed ViewSuite, a 3D point-cloud environment on real ScanNet scenes. Across 13 frontier VLMs, a critical planning gap emerges: they possess basic view-action knowledge but fail to compose it across multi-turn plans, with the gap widening as viewpoint distance grows. To close this gap, we propose an iterative framework that alternates self-exploration with view graph distillation. The key insight is that all exploration trajectories, regardless of their outcome, collectively form a view graph that compactly captures how viewpoints connect across a scene. Distilling this graph into diverse supervised tasks reshapes the policy distribution and overcomes the sparse rewards that stall pure RL. This improves Qwen2.5-VL-7B from 2.5% to 47.8% on interactive view planning, surpassing GPT-5.4 Pro (18.5%) and Gemini 3.1 Pro (21.4%). Self-exploration emerges as a promising path toward VLMs that can actively reason and plan in 3D space.

AI.tldr: ViewSuite reveals that current frontier VLMs struggle to compose actions for multi-turn view planning in 3D; an iterative self-exploration with view-graph distillation substantially improves planning performance (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL-7B from 2.5% to 47.8%).
AI.motivation: Address the bottleneck in 3D view planning: current VLMs possess basic view-change knowledge but fail to compose across long-horizon plans, limiting active 3D reasoning and planning.
AI.method: Introduce ViewSuite, a 3D point-cloud environment on real ScanNet scenes; evaluate 13 frontier VLMs; identify a gap in multi-turn planning; propose an iterative framework that alternates self-exploration with view-graph distillation. Build a view graph from exploration trajectories and distill it into diverse supervised tasks to reshape the policy and mitigate sparse RL rewards.
AI.result: Found that VLMs have basic view-action knowledge but poor multi-turn composition, with the gap growing with viewpoint distance. The iterative method boosted Qwen2.5-VL-7B from 2.5% to 47.8% on interactive view planning, surpassing GPT-5.4 Pro (18.5%) and Gemini 3.1 Pro (21.4%).
AI.conclusion: Self-exploration combined with view-graph distillation enables VLMs to actively reason and plan in 3D space, offering a promising path toward more capable, long-horizon 3D planning in VLMs.

title: OpenClawBench: Benchmarking Process-side Anomalies in Real-world Agent Execution Trajectories
summary: Task success can hide process anomalies in real-world agent executions. An agent may pass the final task oracle while still accumulating unresolved ambiguity, unsafe external writes, ignored errors, weakly grounded commitments, or capability-boundary overcommitment. We study this mismatch as the Outcome-Process Gap and introduce OpenClawBench, a large-scale dataset for measuring and supervising process-side anomalies in real agent execution processes. OpenClawBench is built from BFCL-driven OpenClaw sessions produced by 6 source models and contains 31,264 annotated trajectories. It aligns task-oracle outcomes with structured process evidence. FullTax converts the aligned trajectories into structured anomaly supervision: binary labels, supporting evidence, onset/span localization, severity, recoverability, and a 5-class anomaly taxonomy. Using OpenClawBench, we make the Outcome-Process Gap measurable. Among 31,135 oracle-passing executions, 2,904 are still labeled process-anomalous under FullTax. These results show that success-only evaluation misses a concrete class of process-side failures in real agent executions. A LoRA-fine-tuned Gemma 3 12B detector trained on the high-confidence FullTax supervised pool reaches binary F1=0.729 on the cleaner-labels held-out test split. Together, OpenClawBench turns real agent execution logs into auditable and reusable supervision for studying, diagnosing, and operationally monitoring runtime agent reliability.

AI.tldr: OpenClawBench is a large-scale dataset and supervision framework to detect and quantify process-side anomalies in real agent executions, linking task outcomes with structured process evidence and enabling a taxonomy and auditability of anomalies.
AI.motivation: Address the Outcome-Process Gap where successful task completion can obscure underlying process-level anomalies, enabling auditable supervision and monitoring of runtime agent reliability.
AI.method: Construct a BFCL-driven OpenClaw corpus from 6 source models (31,264 annotated trajectories); align task outcomes with process evidence; develop FullTax to provide binary anomaly labels plus evidence, onset/span localization, severity, recoverability, and a 5-class anomaly taxonomy; evaluate with a LoRA-finetuned Gemma 3 12B detector on high-confidence supervision.
AI.result: Among 31,135 oracle-passing executions, 2,904 are process-anomalous per FullTax; a detector trained on high-confidence data yields binary F1 of 0.729 on a cleaner-held-out test set.
AI.conclusion: OpenClawBench makes real agent execution logs auditable and reusable for studying, diagnosing, and monitoring runtime reliability; it exposes that success-focused evaluation misses concrete process-side failures.

title: FedSmoothLoRA: Toward Smoother and Faster Convergence in Federated Low-Rank Adaptation
summary: Federated fine-tuning of foundation models with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) provides an efficient solution for reducing communication and computation costs while preserving data locality. However, the direct combination of FedAvg and LoRA suffers from three key issues: limited update space, which restricts the model's effective learning capacity; inter-round state mismatch, which disrupts cross-round local optimization continuity; and a client-agnostic starting state, which slows local convergence on clients. Although recent methods mitigate the limited update space issue by merging LoRA updates into the backbone across communication rounds, inter-round state mismatch and the client-agnostic starting state remain insufficiently addressed. To address these issues, we propose FedSmoothLoRA, a federated LoRA tuning framework that preserves the enlarged update space, improves cross-round local optimization continuity, and provides a client-aware starting state for local training. At each communication round, FedSmoothLoRA constructs the local LoRA initialization using two matrices: a Round-Matching matrix that preserves cross-round local state continuity, and a Gradient-Aligned matrix that provides client-specific optimization guidance from gradient signals estimated on local data. Together, these designs enable smoother and faster convergence. Extensive experiments on image classification and natural language generation tasks demonstrate that FedSmoothLoRA consistently outperforms existing federated LoRA tuning methods. Code: https://github.com/wangzehao0704/FedSmoothLoRA

AI.tldr: FedSmoothLoRA extends FedAvg+LoRA by introducing a Round-Matching matrix and a Gradient-Aligned matrix to preserve a larger update space, maintain cross-round local state continuity, and provide client-specific starting states, yielding smoother convergence and improved performance over existing federated LoRA methods.
AI.motivation: To address three limitations of FedAvg+LoRA: limited update space that constrains learning capacity, inter-round state mismatch that hurts cross-round local optimization, and a client-agnostic starting state that slows local convergence.
AI.method: At each communication round, FedSmoothLoRA constructs the local LoRA initialization using two matrices: (1) Round-Matching, which preserves cross-round local state continuity, and (2) Gradient-Aligned, which provides client-specific optimization guidance from gradient signals estimated on local data. These matrices enable an enlarged update space while stabilizing and guiding local training.
AI.result: Experimental results on image classification and natural language generation tasks show that FedSmoothLoRA consistently outperforms existing federated LoRA tuning methods. Code is available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: FedSmoothLoRA effectively addresses the three identified issues, preserving a larger update space, improving cross-round local optimization continuity, and offering a client-aware starting state to accelerate local convergence.

title: From General Vision to Reliable Traversability Estimation: Adapting Vision Foundation Models for Unstructured Outdoor Environments
summary: Vision-based approaches have become the dominant paradigm for traversability estimation in unstructured outdoor environments, typically adapting vision foundation models (VFMs) via semantic segmentation supervision. However, this paradigm faces three fundamental challenges that undermine its reliability: the task-agnostic design of VFMs, the ambiguity of traversability annotations, and the discrepancy between semantic labels and physical safety. We propose Vision-to-Traversability Adaptation (ViTA), a framework that adapts VFMs for reliable traversability estimation, instantiated on SAM2. ViTA injects task-specific knowledge through learnable traversability prompts while preserving the VFM's cross-domain generalization. To handle annotation ambiguity, we introduce Perspective-Diversified Training, which estimates semantic uncertainty to suppress confident predictions at ambiguous boundaries. To bridge the semantic-traversability discrepancy, we distill geometric knowledge during training, enabling slope and elevation reasoning from RGB images alone at inference. The semantic and geometric outputs are fused into a continuous traversability score that reflects both semantic uncertainty and geometric risk. Evaluations across diverse domains, including challenging real-world off-road datasets, demonstrate that ViTA achieves state-of-the-art IoU and Precision with substantial false-positive reduction and strong cross-domain generalization.

AI.tldr: ViTA is a vision-to-traversability adaptation framework that augments a vision foundation model with task-specific prompts, uncertainty-aware training, and geometric distillation to produce a continuous traversability score, achieving state-of-the-art IoU/Precision and strong cross-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: Vision-based traversability estimation relies on task-agnostic vision foundation models, suffers from annotation ambiguity, and faces a semantic–traversability mismatch, undermining reliability in unstructured outdoor environments.
AI.method: Instantiate ViTA on SAM2; inject learnable traversability prompts to condition the VFM; introduce Perspective-Diversified Training to estimate semantic uncertainty and suppress confident predictions at ambiguous boundaries; distill geometric knowledge to enable slope/elevation reasoning from RGB at inference; fuse semantic and geometric outputs into a continuous traversability score.
AI.result: Evaluations across diverse domains, including challenging real-world off-road datasets, show ViTA achieves state-of-the-art IoU and Precision, substantially reduces false positives, and demonstrates strong cross-domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: Task-specific prompting combined with uncertainty handling and geometric distillation yields reliable traversability estimation, producing a continuous score that reflects both semantic uncertainty and geometric risk and generalizes well across domains.

title: Ensemble Score Filtering for Real-Data Energy Consumption Forecast Correction
summary: Accurate estimation and forecasting of energy consumption are important for power-system operation, planning, and demand-side management. In practice, however, complete and timely measurements may not always be available, and the observed data can be partial, noisy, or delayed.
