This motivates the use of learned forecasting models for predicting the evolving consumption state, together with data assimilation methods for sequential forecast correction. In this work, we study a high-dimensional data assimilation problem for real energy-consumption data. \modeltext{The forward prediction is supplied by a pretrained black-box spatio-temporal forecasting model, which is treated as the state propagator in the filtering procedure.} We employ the Ensemble Score Filter (EnSF) to assimilate partial and noisy observations and to correct the forecast trajectory over time. The EnSF uses score-based diffusion models to approximate filtering distributions and avoids retraining neural-network score models during assimilation by using a closed-form score representation and Monte Carlo approximation. Numerical experiments demonstrate that open-loop propagation of the learned forecasting model can become unreliable over long horizons, while EnSF-based correction substantially improves state estimation. Comparisons with the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) further show that EnSF provides stronger correction under the nonlinear observation setting considered in this work.

AI.tldr: EnSF improves high-dimensional energy data assimilation by correcting a pretrained black-box spatiotemporal forecast with score-based diffusion, outperforming EnKF under nonlinear observations.
AI.motivation: Healthcare? No; energy systems often have partial, noisy, or delayed measurements. Accurate sequential state estimation and forecasting are essential for operation and planning, motivating robust data-assimilation methods that can handle nonlinearities and high dimensionality.
AI.method: Use a pretrained black-box spatiotemporal forecasting model as the state propagator; apply Ensemble Score Filter (EnSF) which employs score-based diffusion to approximate the filtering distribution. EnSF avoids retraining neural-score models during assimilation by using a closed-form score representation and Monte Carlo approximation.
AI.result: Open-loop forecasts from the learned model can become unreliable over long horizons; EnSF-based correction substantially improves state estimation. EnSF provides stronger corrections than Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) in nonlinear observation settings.
AI.conclusion: EnSF is a promising data-assimilation approach for real energy-consumption data, enabling robust state estimation and forecast correction when observations are partial or noisy, particularly outperforming EnKF under nonlinear observations.

title: FlowSeg: Dynamic Semantic Guidance for LLM-Conditioned Segmentation
summary: LLM-conditioned segmentation has recently advanced rapidly by coupling large language models with iterative mask generation frameworks. However, we identify a persistent failure mode in current propose-then-select pipelines. Although high-quality mask candidates are often generated, the final prediction may fail to match the given linguistic condition. This failure arises because language semantics are typically used as static prompts or post-hoc matching signals, rather than participating in the iterative mask generation process. Through systematic analysis, we show that many errors stem from semantic misalignment rather than poor mask quality. To address this issue, we propose FlowSeg, which introduces dynamic semantic guidance via a bidirectional semantic flow between intermediate decoding states and LLM-derived condition embeddings throughout the generation process. Language conditions actively guide mask refinement at each stage, while condition embeddings are progressively updated by emerging visual evidence. This design yields semantically grounded mask representations and visually aligned language conditions, enabling more reliable matching. We further incorporate a lightweight boundary-aware refinement to selectively enhance uncertain regions without perturbing confident interiors. Extensive experiments on referring expression segmentation and reasoning segmentation tasks demonstrate that FlowSeg consistently improves language-mask alignment and achieves state-of-the-art performance. Project page: https://zkzhang98.github.io/FlowSeg_page

AI.tldr: FlowSeg introduces dynamic semantic guidance for LLM-conditioned segmentation by establishing a bidirectional semantic flow between intermediate decoding states and LLM-derived condition embeddings, enabling language conditions to guide mask refinement and embeddings to update from visual evidence, plus a light boundary-aware refinement. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on referring expression segmentation and reasoning segmentation by improving language–mask alignment.
AI.motivation: Current propose-then-select LLM-conditioned segmentation suffers from semantic misalignment: high-quality mask candidates are generated, but the final mask may not satisfy the linguistic condition because semantics are used as static prompts or post-hoc signals rather than guiding generation. This paper targets semantic misalignment as the primary failure mode.
AI.method: FlowSeg introduces dynamic semantic guidance via a bidirectional semantic flow between intermediate decoding states and LLM-derived condition embeddings throughout the generation process. Language conditions actively guide mask refinement at each stage, while condition embeddings are progressively updated by emerging visual evidence. A lightweight boundary-aware refinement selectively enhances uncertain regions without perturbing confident interiors.
AI.result: Experiments on referring expression segmentation and reasoning segmentation show FlowSeg consistently improves language–mask alignment and achieves state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating the effectiveness of dynamic semantic guidance for LLM-conditioned segmentation.
AI.conclusion: Semantic alignment is critical in LLM-conditioned segmentation. A bidirectional, dynamic semantic flow between language conditions and intermediate predictions yields more semantically grounded masks and visually coherent language signals, with boundary refinement providing targeted improvements in uncertain regions; FlowSeg sets a new state-of-the-art in targeted segmentation tasks.

title: Momentum Based Reward Design for Low Emission Traffic Signal Control
summary: Urban traffic congestion is a growing global issue contributing significantly to long commute times and environmental pollution. Traditional traffic signal control systems often fail to adapt to dynamic traffic conditions. Adaptive traffic signal control can improve urban traffic without changing road infrastructure. Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has shown strong performance for this task, but existing delay and queue-based rewards often produce short-sighted or unstable policies. This paper proposes a Momentum-Based Reward Function (MBRF) that encourages vehicles to keep moving rather than penalizing congestion alone. The method is evaluated in SUMO (Simulation of Urban MObility) using standard traffic metrics such as waiting time, queue length, throughput, and CO2 emissions. Results show that the proposed reward produces better throughput-emission trade-offs and more stable learning behavior than delay or queue-based rewards, as well as classical controllers such as Max Pressure and LQF.

AI.tldr: Momentum-based reward improves DRL-based adaptive traffic signal control by promoting continuous movement, yielding better throughput-emission trade-offs and stability than delay/queue rewards and Max Pressure/LQF.
AI.motivation: To address short-sighted and unstable policies from delay/queue-based rewards and to reduce congestion and emissions in urban traffic via adaptable signal control.
AI.method: Introduce Momentum-Based Reward Function (MBRF) that biases rewards towards sustained vehicle throughput, reducing penalization solely on congestion. Evaluate in SUMO using standard metrics: waiting time, queue length, throughput, CO2; compare against delay/queue rewards and classical controllers (Max Pressure, LQF).
AI.result: MBRF achieves better throughput-emission trade-offs and more stable learning dynamics than both delay/queue rewards and classical controllers.
AI.conclusion: MBRF is a promising reward shaping technique for DRL-based adaptive traffic signal control, improving efficiency and stability with emissions considerations; warrants further study and real-world tests.

title: Knowledge Offloading: Decomposing LLMs into Sparse Backbones and Memory Modules
summary: LLMs encode both general capabilities and domain-specific knowledge in a single set of parameters. We ask whether this capacity can be reorganized: keeping broadly useful computation in a shared backbone, while moving specialized knowledge into external memory modules. We propose \emph{knowledge offloading} (KOFF), a framework for decomposing a pretrained LLM into a sparse shared backbone and domain-specific memories. Starting from a frozen base model, we jointly learn a structured pruning mask and lightweight recovery modules, implemented as LoRA adapters and learned key-value caches. Across Llama and Qwen models from 3B to 8B, we find that non-trivial capacity can be moved out of the shared backbone without a large loss in model ability. At around 12\% global sparsity, KOFF preserves much of the unpruned model's performance, while pruning the same frozen model without memories degrades sharply. Ablations show that LoRA and learned KV memories are complementary, and specialization analyses suggest that the learned decomposition is meaningful: language-specific neurons are preferentially removed while language-general neurons largely remain in the backbone. These results suggest that knowledge can be reallocated between a shared core and swappable external memories.

AI.tldr: KOFF splits a pretrained LLM into a sparse shared backbone and external domain-specific memories (LoRA adapters and learned KV caches); about 12% global sparsity preserves much performance, with memories complementing and language-specialization moving to the memories.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether domain-specific knowledge can be offloaded from a frozen base LLM into external memories, enabling reallocation of capacity between a shared core and swappable domain modules.
AI.method: Freeze a base model and jointly learn a structured pruning mask and lightweight recovery modules implemented as LoRA adapters and learned key-value caches. Evaluate across Llama and Qwen models from 3B to 8B, analyze sparsity vs performance, and perform ablations on memory types and language specialization.
AI.result: Non-trivial capacity can be moved from the backbone without large loss in ability at ~12% sparsity. Pruning the model without memories degrades sharply. LoRA and learned KV memories are complementary; language-specific neurons are largely moved to memories while language-general neurons stay in the backbone.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge can be reallocated between a shared core and external memories, enabling modular, swappable domain knowledge without fully retraining or enlarging the model.

title: When and How Human Curation Backfires: Preference Alignment under Multi-Model Self-Consuming Loop
summary: Foundation models are increasingly trained on synthetic data generated by prior model iterations rather than exclusively on real data. This self-consuming training paradigm can lead to model collapse, divergence, or bias amplification. Recent work (Ferbach et al., 2024) shows that incorporating human curation into the loop can steer a self-consuming model toward human-aligned behavior, but these analyses focus on a single, isolated model that solely consumes its own outputs. In practice, however, models often interact and train on input-output pairs produced by other models. This paper studies self-consuming training in the multi-model regime. We first formalize a framework for interacting self-consuming models and characterize when the resulting dynamical system converges to a stable point. We then examine how human curation of one model affects its own alignment (self-influence) and how such effects propagate to other models (cross-influence). Unlike isolated settings where human curation always enhances model alignment, we show that cross-model interactions can dampen or even invert this effect, ultimately degrading long-term alignment.

AI.tldr: Multi-model self-consuming training with human curation can improve alignment in isolated settings but cross-model interactions can dampen or reverse these gains; a formal dynamical-system framework yields convergence conditions and reveals how self- and cross-influence propagate in multi-model regimes.
AI.motivation: Extend self-consuming alignment analysis from a single model to interacting multi-model ecosystems to understand how curated feedback resonates across models and affects long-term alignment.
AI.method: Propose a formal dynamical-system framework for interacting self-consuming models, derive conditions for convergence to a stable point, and analyze how human curation on one model (self-influence) propagates to other models (cross-influence).
AI.result: The framework identifies when the multi-model system converges and shows that cross-model interactions can dampen or invert the benefits of human curation, leading to degraded long-term alignment.
AI.conclusion: In multi-model self-consuming regimes, cross-model influence can undermine curated alignment; alignment strategies must account for inter-model feedback loops and potential amplification or inversion of curated signals.

title: Benchmarking Large Vision-Language Models on CFMME: A Comprehensive Chinese Financial Multimodal Evaluation Dataset
summary: The emergence of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) has substantially expanded model capabilities beyond text-only understanding, enabling unified inference across both visual and textual modalities and supporting a broader range of real-world applications. To comprehensively evaluate the perception, understanding, reasoning, and cognition capabilities of LVLMs throughout the entire financial business workflow in Chinese contexts, we introduce CFMME, a novel Chinese financial multimodal evaluation benchmark. CFMME comprises 6,052 instances spanning from fundamental academic knowledge to complex real-world applications, covering eight primary financial image modalities and four core multimodal tasks. On CFMME, we conduct a thorough evaluation of representative LVLMs. The results show that the state-of-the-art model attains an overall accuracy of 66.11\% on the question answering task and an average score of 77.18 on the detection, recognition, and information extraction tasks, indicating substantial room for improvement in current LVLMs. In addition, we conduct detailed analyses of error causes, cross-modal capabilities, and multi-orientation settings, yielding valuable insights for future research. We hope that CFMME will spur further progress in LVLMs, especially by improving their performance on multiple multimodal tasks in the financial domain.

AI.tldr: CFMME benchmark introduces a Chinese financial multimodal evaluation suite for LVLMs, with 6,052 instances across 8 image modalities and 4 tasks; baseline results indicate substantial room for improvement.
AI.motivation: To systematically evaluate LVLM perception, understanding, reasoning, and cognition across the full financial workflow in Chinese contexts, addressing the lack of domain-specific multimodal benchmarks.
AI.method: Construct CFMME comprising 6,052 instances spanning eight financial image modalities and four core multimodal tasks; perform thorough evaluation of representative LVLMs and analyze error causes, cross-modal capabilities, and multi-orientation settings.
AI.result: State-of-the-art LVLM achieves 66.11% QA accuracy and 77.18 average on detection/recognition/information extraction tasks; results reveal substantial room for improvement and provide insights for future research.
AI.conclusion: CFMME is expected to spur progress in LVLMs by improving performance across multiple financial multimodal tasks and informing future research directions.

title: Energy-Aware NECO for Single-Pass Pixel-wise Out-of-Distribution Detection in Semantic Segmentation
comment: 7 pages, 6 figures. Accepted at the ICRA 2026 Workshop on Long-term Deployments in the Wild (LoWi 2026)
summary:
Reliable semantic segmentation for mobile robots requires both accurate dense prediction and robust uncertainty estimation under distribution shift. Strong uncertainty baselines such as Monte Carlo Dropout often require repeated stochastic forward passes and are difficult to deploy on edge platforms.
  We propose Energy-Aware NECO, a single-pass pixel-wise out-of-distribution (OOD) detector for semantic segmentation. The method combines a centered NECO-style geometric ratio computed from decoder features with a logit-based Energy score. Both components are standardized using statistics fitted on a pure in-distribution validation split and fused through a convex combination.
  We evaluate the method on the miniMUAD subset using true pixel-level OOD labels. The proposed hybrid score achieves an AUROC of 0.8539, outperforming NECO-only (0.8280), Energy-only (0.8171), and an ensemble predictive-entropy baseline (0.8124). Additional qualitative and operating-point analyses show that the hybrid detector improves overall ranking performance while preserving the efficiency advantages of a single-pass design.
  Code is available at https://github.com/boyuan-zhangx/Energy-Aware_NECO

AI.tldr: A single-pass, energy-aware OOD detector for semantic segmentation that fuses a NECO-style geometric ratio with a logit-based Energy score; achieves improved AUROC on miniMUAD while preserving single-pass efficiency.
AI.motivation: Need reliable semantic segmentation under distribution shift with low-latency, edge-friendly uncertainty estimation; MC-dropout is expensive for mobile robots.
AI.method: Compute centered NECO-style geometric ratio from decoder features; compute logit-based Energy score; standardize both via in-distribution statistics; fuse via convex combination.
AI.result: On miniMUAD subset with pixel-level OOD labels, hybrid score AUROC 0.8539, better than NECO-only 0.8280, Energy-only 0.8171, and predictive-entropy ensemble 0.8124; qualitative and operating-point analyses; single-pass efficiency preserved; code provided.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid fusion improves ranking performance and maintains efficiency, making it practical for edge deployments.

title: OISD: On-Policy Internal Self-Distillation of Language Models
summary: Recent reinforcement learning (RL) post-training approaches primarily optimize the final output policy using sparse outcome-level rewards, while largely overlooking predictive signals encoded in intermediate representations. In this paper, we introduce a new paradigm called on-policy internal self-distillation and propose the OISD framework, which improves reasoning by transferring on-policy predictive signals from the final layer to intermediate representations. During rollout and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) optimization, the final layer acts as both the policy and a detached internal teacher for selected intermediate layers, which are guided to align with it through two complementary mechanisms: logit alignment, which transfers high-level reasoning behaviors (how to think), and attention alignment, which enforces consistent attention patterns (where to look) from the final layer to the selected intermediate layer, both without requiring external privileged information. Our OISD, together with GRPO, employs signed advantage-weighted Jensen--Shannon alignment to distill informative intermediate representations while preserving policy consistency under a unified acting policy. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of OISD, with substantial and consistent improvements over strong reasoning RL baselines across four mathematical reasoning tasks. The code will be released at https://github.com/THE-MALT-LAB/OISD

AI.tldr: Introduces on-policy internal self-distillation (OISD) to transfer final-layer predictive signals to intermediate representations in reinforcement learning, via logit and attention alignment, improving mathematical reasoning across tasks.
AI.motivation: Current post-training RL primarily optimizes the final policy using sparse outcome-level rewards and largely ignores predictive signals encoded in intermediate representations; leveraging these signals can enhance reasoning and generalization.
AI.method: During rollout and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) updates, the final layer acts as a detached internal teacher for selected intermediate layers. Two alignment mechanisms are used: (1) logit alignment to transfer high-level reasoning (how to think) and (2) attention alignment to enforce consistent attention patterns (where to look). Distillation uses signed advantage-weighted Jensen–Shannon alignment, without external privileged information, under a unified acting policy.
AI.result: Experimental results show substantial and consistent improvements over strong reasoning RL baselines across four mathematical reasoning tasks; the approach is accompanied by a code release at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: OISD with GRPO distills informative intermediate representations while preserving policy consistency, improving on-policy reasoning in RL without external supervision, and demonstrating robust gains across multiple mathematical reasoning tasks.

title: Indexing the Unreadable: LLM-Native Recursive Construction and Search of Service Taxonomies
comment: Preprint. 8 pages main paper + appendix; 2 figures. Under submission to EMNLP 2026
summary: The era of the Internet of Agents (IoA) is taking shape: LLM agents are expected to fulfill user goals by orchestrating fast-growing populations of Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, Agent-to-Agent (A2A) endpoints, reusable skills, and other LLM-callable services. Yet LLMs face a structural mismatch with this regime: effective context is a scarce resource that does not scale with the number of services. Concatenating thousands of service descriptions into a prompt overflows the context window, and even when the window is large enough, models systematically under-attend to information in the middle of long inputs, the well-documented Lost-in-the-Middle phenomenon. This is fundamentally a question of context management for service discovery. To address this, we propose an LLM-native progressive-disclosure scheme and its concrete instantiation, A2X (Agent-to-Anything service discovery): an LLM-driven pipeline that automatically organizes the registered services into a hierarchical taxonomy and walks it layer by layer at query time, so that every LLM call sees only a small candidate set highly relevant to the user query. This decouples effective-context scarcity from registry size and significantly reduces token consumption while improving retrieval accuracy. Compared to full-context dumping, A2X achieves a 6.2-point Hit Rate gain at one-ninth the prompt-token cost; compared to the state-of-the-art open-source embedding-based baseline, A2X improves Hit Rate by more than 20 points.

AI.tldr: LLM-native progressive-disclosure A2X for service discovery in IoA; hierarchical taxonomy to present small candidate sets, reducing token usage while boosting retrieval accuracy.
AI.motivation: Context-window scarcity and Lost-in-the-Middle hinder effective discovery across large MCP/A2A ecosystems; need scalable, LLM-friendly registry navigation.
AI.method: Progressive-disclosure pipeline that builds a hierarchical taxonomy of registered services and traverses it layer-by-layer at query time so the LLM sees a small, highly relevant subset; decouples registry size from context usage; improves retrieval.
AI.result: Compared with full-context dumping, A2X yields 6.2-point Hit Rate gain at ~1/9 the prompt-token cost; vs an open-source embedding baseline, >20-point Hit Rate improvement.
AI.conclusion: A2X provides an effective, scalable solution to context-scarcity in IoA service discovery, balancing accuracy and token efficiency; promising for large, dynamic service registries.

title: V2XCrafter: Learning to Generate Driving Scene Across Agents
summary: Collaborative driving systems leverage vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication for multi-agent collaborative perception to enhance driving safety, yet they remain constrained by scarce annotated real-world V2X driving datasets and limited generalization across diverse driving conditions. While image generation technology offers a feasible solution for data augmentation, existing methods tailored for single-vehicle multi-view scenarios face two fundamental challenges in multi-agent driving settings: (1) the expansion of the learning objective degrades generation quality, and (2) the highly dynamic variations across agents hinder the modeling of consistency for physical attributes (e.g., color, category) in jointly observed objects. To bridge this gap, we propose V2XCrafter, the first framework for generating controllable and realistic collaborative driving scene across agents' camera views. For effective learning, we develop a progressive multi-agent diffusion model based on a single-agent backbone, using neighboring agents' latent states as reference signals to progressively guide the single-to-multi diffusion. To address cross-vehicle inconsistency, we propose a cross-agent attention module that leverages a collaboration view graph and learnable jointly observed object representation to model the dynamic cross-agent camera view relationships. Experiments have shown that V2XCrafter can generate high-fidelity and controllable street views with consistency across agents, thereby effectively enhancing the downstream collaborative 3D object detection tasks.

AI.tldr: V2XCrafter: a cross-agent diffusion framework for controllable, consistent multi-agent driving scene generation that improves downstream collaborative 3D detection.
AI.motivation: Addresses scarcity of annotated real-world V2X data and poor cross-condition generalization; existing single-vehicle multi-view generative methods fail to scale to multi-agent settings due to objective expansion and cross-agent attribute inconsistency.
AI.method: Progressive multi-agent diffusion based on a single-agent backbone; uses neighboring agents' latent states as references to guide single-to-multi diffusion; introduces a cross-agent attention module with a collaboration view graph and learnable joint-object representations to model dynamic cross-agent view relationships.
AI.result: Empirical results show high-fidelity, controllable street views with cross-agent consistency; improved performance on downstream collaborative 3D object detection tasks.
AI.conclusion: V2XCrafter provides a controllable, consistent framework for multi-agent scene generation to augment data for collaborative perception, addressing data scarcity and cross-view consistency.

title: DGSG-Mind: Dynamic 3D Gaussian Scene Graphs for Long-Term Scene Understanding and Grounding
summary: Integrating open-vocabulary semantic information into dynamic 3D scene representations is essential for long-term embodied scene understanding. However, existing methods often suffer from fragile instance association due to incomplete cross-view cues, while their limited ability to handle object-level topological changes restricts long-term robotic task execution. Moreover, current 3D scene understanding methods either rely on simple feature matching without explicit spatial reasoning or assume offline ground-truth 3D geometry. To address these challenges, we present DGSG-Mind, a hybrid instance-aware 3D Gaussian dynamic scene graph system with an embodied reasoning agent. Our system couples a probabilistic voxel grid with explicit 3D Gaussians to enable robust cross-modal instance fusion and incremental semantic mapping. It handles dynamic changes through Gaussian-based visual relocalization and localized masked refinement guided by geometric-semantic consistency. Built on the instance Gaussian map, DGSG-Mind further constructs a hierarchical scene graph and develops the 3D Gaussian Mind, which integrates structural relations, spatial-semantic information, and visually annotated RoI Gaussian renderings for multimodal reasoning. Extensive experiments show that DGSG-Mind achieves the best zero-shot 3DVG performance among methods operating on self-reconstructed maps, while also delivering strong performance in 3D open-vocabulary semantic segmentation and scene reconstruction. We further deploy DGSG-Mind on real-world robots to demonstrate its target-oriented reasoning and dynamic update capabilities. The project page of DGSG-Mind is available at https://icr-lab.github.io/DGSG-Mind

AI.tldr: DGSG-Mind proposes a hybrid 3D Gaussian dynamic scene graph with an embodied reasoning agent to fuse open-vocabulary semantics into dynamic 3D scenes, addressing fragile cross-view association and topology changes; achieves strong zero-shot 3DVG and open-vocabulary segmentation, with real-robot deployment.
AI.motivation: Long-term embodied scene understanding requires robust cross-view instance fusion and handling topological dynamics; existing methods rely on simple feature matching or offline geometry and struggle with open-vocabulary semantics.
AI.method: Hybrid probabilistic voxel grid plus explicit 3D Gaussians for cross-modal fusion; Gaussian-based visual relocalization and localized masked refinement; instance Gaussian map; hierarchical scene graph; 3D Gaussian Mind for multimodal reasoning (structural relations, spatial-semantic info, RoI Gaussian renderings); embodied reasoning agent.
AI.result: Outperforms on zero-shot 3DVG among self-reconstructed maps; strong results in 3D open-vocabulary segmentation and scene reconstruction; validated on real robots for target-oriented reasoning and dynamic updates.
AI.conclusion: DGSG-Mind advances robust long-term 3D scene understanding through probabilistic Gaussians and explicit scene graphs, enabling open-vocabulary perception and embodied reasoning, with demonstrated real-world applicability.

title: Model Merging by Output-Space Projection
summary: Model merging combines fine-tuned checkpoints into a single multi-task model without retraining. Existing methods - such as task arithmetic, model soups, TIES, and DARE - are computationally efficient and empirically successful, but rely on heuristic design choices and lack formal optimality guarantees. We show that merging can be formulated as a convex quadratic programme over residual updates, yielding weights that minimise a squared-output calibration objective using calibration inputs and fine-tuned model outputs, and subsuming existing methods as special cases. Our framework yields a closed-form diagnostic - the fraction of residual energy captured by a chosen basis - that predicts downstream merge quality using only the calibration set. Empirically, the QP matches or outperforms existing methods in the single-layer setting, and we characterise when the optimal basis provides significant gains over the cheaper diagonal QP. We extend to multi-layer merging via a sequential layer-wise algorithm and demonstrate consistent gains across language and vision benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Shows model merging can be cast as a convex quadratic program over residual updates, unifying and improving on existing heuristic methods; provides a predictive diagnostic for merge quality and a sequential layer-wise extension that yields consistent gains in language and vision tasks.
AI.motivation: Provide a principled, optimal framework for merging fine-tuned checkpoints into a single multi-task model, with formal guarantees and a diagnostic for downstream performance.
AI.method: Formulate merging as a convex quadratic program over residual updates to minimize a squared-output calibration objective using calibration inputs and fine-tuned outputs. Derive a closed-form diagnostic (fraction of residual energy captured by a chosen basis) to predict merge quality. Compare full-basis QP with cheaper diagonal QP; extend via sequential layer-wise merging.
AI.result: The QP-based merge matches or outperforms existing methods in the single-layer setting; the diagnostic predicts downstream performance using calibration data; the benefit of a full basis over diagonal is instance-dependent; sequential-layer extension yields consistent gains across language and vision benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A unified, principled, and scalable framework for model merging that subsumes existing methods as special cases, provides a predictive energy-capture diagnostic, and performs well across modalities with a practical, layer-wise approach.

title: CoHyDE: Iterative Co-Training of LLM Rewriter & Dense Encoder for Tool Retrieval
summary: Tool retrieval over large API catalogs is a core bottleneck for LLM agents: user queries arrive in colloquial, often underspecified language, while the catalog uses technical API vocabulary that no fixed encoder can bridge on its own. The two dominant training approaches, contrastive encoder fine-tuning and HyDE-style query expansion with a frozen LLM, address this problem from opposite ends and fail in complementary directions: the fine-tuned encoder excels when the query's surface form already matches the catalog but collapses when it does not, while zero-shot HyDE is more robust to underspecified queries yet generates catalog-unaware hypothetical descriptions that degrade retrieval when queries are well-formed. We introduce CoHyDE, an iterative procedure that trains the dense encoder and the LLM rewriter as a single co-evolving system: the encoder is retrained with InfoNCE on catalog-style hypothetical descriptions produced by the rewriter, and the rewriter is preference-aligned via DPO against the encoder's retrieval scores, with both sides warm-started on the tool catalog before the loop begins. On a ~10k tool subset of the ToolBench catalog, three rounds of CoHyDE improve over the strongest single-component baseline by +2.5 pp NDCG@5 on standard queries and +6.3 pp on held-out vague queries, with gains as large as +8 pp on the hardest vague tier. Ablations confirm that co-training is the key ingredient: using either component in isolation fails to match CoHyDE on both well-formed and vague queries, with losses of up to -8 pp on vague queries.

AI.tldr: CoHyDE jointly trains a dense encoder and an LLM rewriter in a co-evolving loop to improve tool retrieval across well-formed and vague queries, outperforming single-component baselines.
AI.motivation: Bridge the vocabulary gap between user natural language and catalog API terms; address weaknesses of contrastive encoder fine-tuning (sensitive to surface form) and HyDE-style query expansion (unaware catalog, harms well-formed queries).
AI.method: Iterative co-training: encoder retrained with InfoNCE on catalog-style descriptions produced by the rewriter; rewriter optimized with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) against encoder's retrieval scores; warm-started on the tool catalog; three rounds of co-training.
AI.result: On ~10k-tool ToolBench subset, CoHyDE surpasses best single-component baselines by +2.5 percentage points NDCG@5 on standard queries and +6.3 pp on vague queries; up to +8 pp gains on the hardest vague tier; ablations show co-training is essential, with isolated components underperforming by up to -8 pp on vague queries.
AI.conclusion: CoHyDE's joint, co-evolving training framework yields robust tool retrieval, making the system more tolerant to underspecified queries without sacrificing performance on well-specified ones.

title: AnyMo: Scaling Any-Modality Conditional Motion Generation with Masked Modeling
summary: Conditional human motion generation remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision and robotics. Despite significant progress, current methods are often constrained by fixed modality configurations and task-specific architectures, leaving cross-modal interactions and the scaling laws of multimodal-conditioned synthesis largely underexplored. A key bottleneck is the scarcity of large-scale modality-aligned motion data, limiting generalization across diverse control signals. In this work, we introduce OmniHuMo, a large-scale, high-quality dataset comprising over 5,000 hours of motion and 3.2 million sequences with precisely aligned multimodal annotations (e.g., text, speech, music, and trajectory). Leveraging OmniHuMo, we propose AnyMo, a unified multimodal framework combining a Residual FSQ-based motion tokenizer with a scalable masked modeling transformer, enabling high-quality motion synthesis under arbitrary modality combinations. Extensive experiments show that AnyMo achieves high-fidelity synthesis while offering flexible control over both spatial and stylistic attributes.

AI.tldr: Proposes OmniHuMo dataset and AnyMo framework for large-scale multimodal-conditioned motion synthesis; claims precise multimodal alignment and unified modeling across arbitrary modality combinations.
AI.motivation: Fill the data and modeling gaps in multimodal motion synthesis: scarcity of aligned large-scale motion data and fixed modality configurations that hinder cross-modal interactions and scaling laws.
AI.method: Introduce OmniHuMo: a dataset with >5,000 hours of motion and 3.2 million aligned sequences across modalities (text, speech, music, trajectory). Propose AnyMo: a unified multimodal framework using a Residual FSQ-based motion tokenizer and a scalable masked modeling transformer to synthesize motion under arbitrary modality combinations.
AI.result: Experiments report high-fidelity motion synthesis and flexible control over spatial and stylistic attributes across multimodal inputs.
AI.conclusion: OmniHuMo enables flexible, scalable multimodal-conditioned motion generation; AnyMo provides a general, modality-agnostic approach for high-quality synthesis.

title: Uncertainty-driven 3D Gaussian Splatting Active Mapping via Anisotropic Visibility Field
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026. Project page https://gatech-rl2.github.io/GAVIS/
summary: We present Gaussian Splatting Anisotropic Visibility Field (GAVIS), a novel framework for uncertainty quantification and active mapping in 3DGS. Our key insight is that regions unseen from the training views yield unreliable predictions from the 3DGS. To address this, we introduce a principled and efficient method for quantifying the visibility field in 3DGS, defined as the anisotropic visibility of each particle with respect to the training views, and represented using spherical harmonics. The resulting visibility field is integrated into a Bayesian Network-based uncertainty-aware 3DGS rasterizer, enabling real-time (200 FPS) uncertainty quantification for synthesized views. Active mapping is further performed within a maximum information gain framework building on this formulation. Extensive experiments across diverse environments demonstrate that GAVIS consistently and significantly outperforms prior approaches in both accuracy and efficiency. Moreover, beyond standalone use, our method can be applied post-hoc to improve the performance of existing approaches.

AI.tldr: GAVIS introduces an anisotropic visibility field for 3D Gaussian splatting to quantify uncertainty and enable active mapping; it uses per-particle spherical harmonics, a Bayesian rasterizer at real-time speeds (200 FPS), and maximum information gain for active mapping, outperforming prior work and usable post-hoc to improve existing methods.
AI.motivation: Unseen regions from training views lead to unreliable 3DGS predictions. A principled, efficient visibility/uncertainty model is needed to quantify uncertainty and guide data acquisition, with real-time performance for interactive applications.
AI.method: Define an anisotropic visibility field per particle using spherical harmonics to capture visibility with respect to training views. Integrate this field into a Bayesian Network-based 3DGS rasterizer to enable real-time uncertainty quantification (≈200 FPS). Use a maximum information gain framework for active mapping that selects views to maximize information gain.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse environments show that GAVIS consistently and significantly outperforms prior approaches in both accuracy and efficiency. The method is also applicable post-hoc to improve the performance of existing 3DGS approaches.
AI.conclusion: GAVIS provides principled uncertainty quantification and active mapping for 3D Gaussian splatting, with real-time performance and broad applicability, including post-hoc enhancement of existing methods.

title: Bridging Chemists and AI: An Expert-Augmented Framework for Interpretable Route Evaluation
comment: 13 pages, 11 figures, ELLIS Unconference Workshop: Generative Models, LLMs, and the Future of Molecular AI (ML4Molecules 2025)
summary: Selecting efficient multi-step synthetic routes is a central challenge in organic synthesis, particularly in medicinal and process chemistry, where route choice directly impacts feasibility, cost, and development efficiency. Data-driven assessment systems often oversimplify the multi-objective nature of synthesis design and rely on proxy datasets, such as patent routes, rather than universally grounded criteria. To address this, we introduce an expert-augmented, data-driven scoring framework that integrates machine learning with chemists' domain knowledge for both numerical and explainable route assessment. A DeepSets-based model is trained using tree edit distance between reference and machine-generated routes, and then fine-tuned with expert evaluations to produce both quantitative scores and interpretable qualitative categories: Good, Plausible, and Bad. The resulting system achieves a Spearman correlation coefficient of 0.78 and a Pearson correlation of 0.77 for category assessment prediction, and 60.2% top-1 ranking accuracy for score prediction, substantially outperforming the previous baseline of 17.5%.

AI.tldr: A data-driven, expert-augmented scoring framework for multi-step synthetic route assessment using DeepSets and tree edit distance, achieving strong correlations with expert judgments and improved ranking accuracy over a baseline.
AI.motivation: Address the multi-objective and domain-knowledge gaps in synthesis route evaluation, moving beyond proxy datasets to grounded criteria with explainable results.
AI.method: Train a DeepSets-based model on tree edit distance between reference and machine-generated routes; fine-tune with expert evaluations to output quantitative scores and qualitative categories (Good, Plausible, Bad).
AI.result: Category assessment: Spearman 0.78, Pearson 0.77; score prediction: 60.2% top-1 accuracy; baseline was 17.5%.
AI.conclusion: An expert-augmented framework yields interpretable scoring and strong predictive performance, aligning with chemists and improving route design assessment.

title: Diagnosing Harmful Continuation in Answer-Correct Long-CoT Training Traces
summary: Long chain-of-thought (CoT) traces are widely used as supervision for reasoning-oriented LLM SFT, yet answer-correct traces can still lead to markedly different fine-tuning outcomes. We study post-conclusion continuation in answer-correct long-CoT data: a continuation where the answer appears sufficiently supported, but the trace continues with additional reasoning that remains in the supervised target. To test its training effect, we use a delete-only editor to construct answer-preserving suffix removal and compare CoT-based SFT on the original and processed traces. We observe improved SFT outcomes after removing the editor-identified post-conclusion continuation, suggesting that this continuation is harmful to training in our setting. We therefore refer to this empirically supported phenomenon as harmful continuation. Beyond this intervention, we further characterize the removed post-conclusion continuation through uncertainty and hidden-state progress. We observe persistent local uncertainty together with weakened terminal-directional progress, forming an uncertainty--geometry mismatch. Finally, we instantiate Harmful Continuation Cut (HCC), a lightweight boundary proxy that approximates the editor-identified post-conclusion continuation boundary.

AI.tldr: Post-conclusion continuations in answer-correct long CoT data can harm supervised fine-tuning; removing the post-conclusion suffix improves SFT performance. A boundary proxy (HCC) is proposed to approximate this harmful region.
AI.motivation: To understand why long chain-of-thought (CoT) traces with answer-correct targets sometimes degrade SFT outcomes and to identify a practical mitigation by isolating harmful post-conclusion reasoning.
AI.method: Construct answer-preserving suffix removals using a delete-only editor on CoT traces; compare SFT trained on original vs edited traces; analyze the removed suffix in terms of uncertainty and hidden-state dynamics; introduce Harmful Continuation Cut (HCC) as a boundary proxy.
AI.result: Removing the editor-identified post-conclusion continuation yields improved SFT performance; the removed segment exhibits persistent local uncertainty and reduced forward progress, indicating an uncertainty–geometry mismatch; HCC serves as a lightweight boundary approximation of the harmful region.
AI.conclusion: Post-conclusion continuation can be detrimental to CoT-based SFT; careful data curation that truncates or bounds reasoning traces can improve outcomes; HCC offers a practical tool to identify and approximate harmful continuation boundaries.

title: ESAM++: Efficient Online 3D Perception on the Edge
summary: Online 3D scene perception in real time is essential for robotics, AR/VR, and autonomous systems, particularly in edge computing scenarios where computational resources are limited and privacy is crucial. Recent state-of-the-art methods like EmbodiedSAM (ESAM) demonstrate the promise of online 3D perception by leveraging the Segment Anything Model (SAM) for real-time, fine-grained, and generalized 3D instance segmentation. However, ESAM still relies on a computationally expensive 3D sparse UNet for point cloud feature extraction, which accounts for the majority of the 3D inference time, hindering its practicality on resource-constrained devices. In this paper, we propose ESAM++, a lightweight and scalable alternative for online 3D scene perception tailored to edge devices without GPU acceleration. Our method introduces a 3D Sparse Feature Pyramid Network (SFPN) that efficiently captures multi-scale geometric features from streaming 3D point clouds while significantly reducing computational overhead and model size. We evaluate our approach on four challenging segmentation benchmarks, namely ScanNet, ScanNet200, SceneNN, and 3RScan, demonstrating that our model achieves competitive accuracy with up to 3 times faster inference with a 2 times smaller model size compared to ESAM, enabling practical deployment on edge devices.

AI.tldr: ESAM++ introduces a lightweight 3D Sparse Feature Pyramid Network (SFPN) to replace the heavy 3D sparse UNet in EmbodiedSAM, achieving faster online 3D perception on edge devices with a smaller model, while maintaining competitive accuracy across ScanNet, ScanNet200, SceneNN, and 3RScan.
AI.motivation: There is a need for real-time, privacy-preserving 3D scene understanding on resource-constrained edge devices. The original ESAM relies on a computationally heavy 3D sparse UNet, which is a bottleneck for on-device inference. A lighter, scalable 3D feature extractor could enable practical deployment without GPU acceleration.
AI.method: Propose a 3D Sparse Feature Pyramid Network (SFPN) that efficiently captures multi-scale geometric features from streaming 3D point clouds. The SFPN is designed to be lightweight and to reduce computational overhead and model size, enabling edge-device inference without GPUs and integrating with the ESAM framework.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four benchmarks (ScanNet, ScanNet200, SceneNN, 3RScan) shows competitive accuracy with up to 3× faster inference and ~2× smaller model size compared to ESAM, enabling practical edge deployment.
AI.conclusion: ESAM++ demonstrates that a lightweight SFPN-based 3D perception pipeline can achieve real-time performance on edge devices while maintaining accuracy, validating its suitability for resource-constrained robotics, AR/VR, and autonomous systems.

title: When and How Long? The Readout-Mediator Angle in Temporal Reasoning
summary: A linear probe can decode a representation almost perfectly and yet be completely irrelevant to how the model uses it. On calendar-date duration reasoning in language models, a $\sin$/$\cos$ probe recovers day-of-year from a layer's activations, yet ablating its direction has no effect on the model's answers -- while ablating a four-dimensional subspace found by Distributed Alignment Search (DAS) at the same layer collapses performance entirely. We measure the angle between these two subspaces -- the \emph{readout-mediator angle} -- and find it indistinguishable from the angle between two random subspaces (the Haar-uniform null), meaning the probe has learned a direction orthogonal to the model's actual computation. Reverse-engineering the circuit reveals why: attention heads route month-grained context through learned QK offsets at ${\pm}30$ and ${\pm}61$ days, and MLPs then convert \emph{when} (absolute date) into \emph{how long} (duration) -- all downstream of the causal subspace the probe never touches. Sparse-autoencoder decomposition confirms the split: probe-aligned and DAS-aligned features encode semantically disjoint concepts with negligible causal overlap. The dissociation replicates across four scales ($1.5$-$9\,$B) and two model families, with preliminary evidence on two further domains (spatial displacement, symbolic arithmetic), suggesting that readout-mediator orthogonality is a general failure mode of probe-based interpretability. This directly undermines proposals to deploy probes as runtime safety monitors: the probe can report high confidence on a direction the model has silently abandoned.

AI.tldr: Linear probes can mislead: the direction detected by a probe is often orthogonal to the model's causal computation. In calendar-date duration tasks, a sin/cos probe recovers day-of-year from activations, yet removing its direction does not affect answers, while a DAS-derived subspace is causally necessary. The readout-mediator angle is Haar-random, indicating probe directions do not correspond to actual computation. Reverse-engineering shows separate processing for month information and duration, across scales, suggesting readout-mediator orthogonality as a general failure mode. Probes thus fail as runtime safety monitors.
AI.motivation: To test whether linear probes reveal the true causal subspaces used by transformer models, and to assess the reliability of probes for interpretability and safety by comparing probe-aligned directions to causally important subspaces.
AI.method: (1) Train a sin/cos linear probe to predict day-of-year from layer activations on calendar-date duration tasks. (2) Use Distributed Alignment Search (DAS) to extract a four-dimensional subspace at the same layer. (3) Measure the readout-mediator angle between the probe subspace and the DAS subspace; compare to Haar-random null. (4) Ablate the DAS subspace vs probe direction to test causal importance. (5) Reverse-engineer circuits to identify how attention heads route month-grained context via QK offsets at ±30 and ±61 days, and how MLPs map absolute dates to duration. (6) Use sparse-autoencoder decomposition to separate probe-aligned vs DAS-aligned features. (7) Replicate across four scales (1.5B–9B) and two model families; probe in two additional domains (spatial displacement, symbolic arithmetic).
AI.result: The readout-mediator angle between probe and DAS subspaces is indistinguishable from the Haar-uniform null, i.e., the probe’s direction is orthogonal to the model’s causal subspace. Ablating the DAS subspace collapses performance, while ablating the probe direction does not affect answers, revealing a lack of causal overlap. Probe-aligned and DAS-aligned features encode semantically disjoint concepts with negligible causal overlap, confirmed by sparse-autoencoder decomposition. The dissociation persists across model scales and families, with preliminary evidence in other domains, suggesting readout-mediator orthogonality as a general interpretability failure mode. Consequently, probes are unreliable as runtime safety monitors because they can report high confidence on directions the model has abandoned.
AI.conclusion: Probe-based interpretability can be misleading: high probe accuracy does not imply the model uses the probed direction causally. The study motivates shifting toward causally grounded analyses (like DAS) and cautions against deploying probes as safety monitors without validating causal relevance.

title: Entropy-KL Divergence-based Token Masking: A Novel Approach for Selective Fine-tuning of Large Language Models
summary: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by reinforcement learning (RL) has become a standard post-training paradigm for large language models. This paradigm provides a cold-start for RL exploration, avoiding the inefficiency of pure RL where on-policy sampling yields insufficient positive samples. However, in practice, existing approaches often use a small amount of data for SFT initialization compared to the RL phase, which can cause the model to fit the limited samples and shift away from its pre-trained distribution. This distribution shift impedes the model's ability to effectively explore during subsequent RL training. To address this challenge, we propose that in low-data regimes, SFT should prioritize activating task-relevant capabilities rather than memorizing specific content. Along this line, we propose EKSFT (Entropy-KL Selective Fine-Tuning), which selectively masks tokens that exhibit either high entropy or high KL divergence from a reference model. By excluding these high-uncertainty, distribution-shifting tokens from imitation, EKSFT injects task-specific knowledge while preserving the integrity of the model's pre-trained distribution. Empirical evaluations on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that EKSFT consistently outperforms standard SFT. Further RL fine-tuning from the EKSFT model yields consistently better post-RL performance, indicating improved exploration for the RL stage. Our codes and datasets are available at https://github.com/MINE-USTC/EKSFT.

AI.tldr: Entropy-KL Selective Fine-Tuning (EKSFT) selectively masks high-uncertainty tokens during supervised fine-tuning to avoid distribution shift in low-data regimes, improving downstream RL exploration and performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks; code available.
AI.motivation: In low-data regimes, standard SFT risks overfitting to limited samples, shifting the model away from its pre-trained distribution and hindering exploration in subsequent RL. A method that preserves the pre-trained distribution while acquiring task-specific capabilities is needed.
AI.method: Compute token-level entropy and KL divergence against a reference model; mask tokens with high entropy or high KL; train only on unmasked tokens (selective imitation); evaluate on mathematical reasoning benchmarks; apply RL fine-tuning from the resulting EKSFT model.
AI.result: EKSFT consistently outperforms standard SFT on math reasoning benchmarks; subsequent RL fine-tuning yields improved post-RL performance, indicating better exploration.
AI.conclusion: Selective fine-tuning via entropy and KL-based masking effectively leverages limited data by focusing on task-relevant capabilities while preserving the pre-trained distribution, with released code and datasets.

title: KGEdit: Ambiguity-Aware Knowledge Graphs for Training-Free Precise Video Generation and Editing
summary: In recent years, training-free video generation has progressed remarkably. However, when handling complex textual instructions, existing methods still suffer from semantic ambiguity, incorrect concept binding, and cross-frame inconsistency. To address these issues, we propose KGEdit, a structured semantic control framework for text-to-video (T2V) diffusion models. Specifically, we first construct an ambiguity-aware knowledge graph (AAKG) to disentangle and disambiguate the input prompt, converting it into four types of structured semantics: identity, relation, attribute, and negative constraints. We then design a structured semantic injection module (SSIM) to inject these semantic signals into key layers of the diffusion Transformer, enabling fine-grained semantic control. In addition, we introduce a temporal-aware semantic control (TASC) module that dynamically schedules semantic objectives according to the stage-wise characteristics of the denoising process, further improving semantic alignment and temporal consistency. Experiments show that KGEdit outperforms existing methods in editing precision and temporal stability, while offering higher efficiency and controllability in text-driven interaction scenarios.

AI.tldr: KGEdit introduces a structured semantic control framework for text-to-video diffusion models, leveraging an ambiguity-aware knowledge graph (AAKG), a structured semantic injection module (SSIM), and a temporal-aware semantic control (TASC) to improve editing precision and temporal consistency.
AI.motivation: Address semantic ambiguity, incorrect concept binding, and cross-frame inconsistency in training-free video generation.
AI.method: Construct an AAKG that parses prompts into identity, relation, attribute, and negative constraints; inject these semantics via SSIM into diffusion Transformer layers; use TASC to schedule semantic objectives across denoising stages.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines in editing precision and temporal stability, with higher efficiency and controllability in text-driven interactions.
AI.conclusion: Structured semantic control via AAKG, SSIM, and TASC enables precise, temporally coherent T2V generation and editing.

title: Apertus LLM Family Expansion via Distillation and Quantization
summary: The wide adoption of LLMs has led to their use in great variety of applications and scenarios, such as chatbot assistants and data annotation, creating the need for the models to satisfy certain budget and hardware constraints. This has led to the trend of LLMs being released in batches consisting of similar models of various sizes for the family of models to adhere to as wide of a range of constraints as possible. In this paper, we validate distillation and quantization as a cost-effective way to expand model families to new sizes and hardware formats. Based on the open-recipe Apertus 8B LLM, we produce Apertus-v1.1 - a distilled family of models with up to 4B parameters trained on 1.7T permissive license tokens. We demonstrate cost-efficiency and strong accuracy performance of our approach for covering large ranges of hardware and systems requirements.

AI.tldr: Distillation and quantization enable expanding LLM families to new sizes/hardware; Apertus-v1.1 distills an 8B model into a family of up to 4B models trained on 1.7T tokens, demonstrating cost-efficiency and broad hardware compatibility.
AI.motivation: Growing need for LLMs that fit diverse budgets and hardware constraints across applications, requiring model families with varying sizes and formats.
AI.method: Apply distillation and quantization on the open-recipe Apertus 8B to produce Apertus-v1.1—a family of models up to 4B parameters trained on 1.7T permissive-license tokens.
AI.result: The approach yields cost-effective expansion of model families with strong accuracy across a wide range of hardware and systems.
AI.conclusion: Distillation and quantization are effective strategies for expanding LLM family sizes and hardware formats; Apertus-v1.1 demonstrates practical feasibility and performance gains.

title: Rubric-Guided Process Reward for Stepwise Model Routing
summary: Stepwise model routing improves the efficiency of Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) by assigning each reasoning step to a suitable model. Recent methods formulate routing as a sequential decision process and train the router with reinforcement learning. However, although they model routing as a process, they still supervise the router with outcome rewards. Such rewards only reflect final answer correctness and fail to evaluate intermediate routing decisions, which can weaken performance and generalization. To address this gap, we propose RoRo, a rubric-guided process reward framework for stepwise model routing. RoRo first collects diverse routing trajectories and constructs preference pairs based on outcome, cost, and process quality. It then trains a Rubricor to generate a query-specific evaluation rubric and a Judge to score routing trajectories under this rubric through alternating optimization. The resulting process rewards are combined with outcome rewards to optimize the routing policy via GRPO. Experiments on five reasoning benchmarks under both same-family and cross-family settings show that RoRo consistently outperforms strong baselines and achieves better accuracy and cost trade-offs.

AI.tldr: RoRo: rubric-guided process rewards for stepwise model routing to evaluate intermediate decisions; uses Rubricor and Judge to generate and score query-specific rubrics; combines with outcome rewards via GRPO; yields better accuracy and cost trade-offs on five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing RL-based routing uses outcome-only rewards, neglecting intermediate decisions, which harms performance, generalization, and efficiency.
AI.method: Collect diverse routing trajectories; construct preference pairs based on outcome, cost, and process quality; train Rubricor to generate a query-specific evaluation rubric and a Judge to score routing trajectories under this rubric via alternating optimization; derive process rewards and combine with outcome rewards to optimize routing policy via GRPO.
AI.result: Experiments on five reasoning benchmarks under both same-family and cross-family settings show RoRo consistently outperforms strong baselines and achieves better accuracy and cost trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: Rubric-guided, process-aware rewards provide finer-grained supervision for stepwise routing, improving accuracy and efficiency and generalization across related and distant task families.

title: RadioFormer3D: Weakly Supervised 3D Radio Map Estimation in Low-Altitude Airspace via Generative Modeling
summary: With the emergence of wireless applications in three-dimensional environments, such as the low-altitude airspace and 3D heterogeneous networks, radio map estimation is increasingly required to characterize signal propagation across both horizontal and vertical dimensions. However, extending radio map estimation from 2D to 3D remains challenging due to increased spatial sparsity and limited supervision across continuous altitudes. In this paper, we propose \textbf{\textit{RadioFormer3D}}, a specialized model for volumetric spectrum reconstruction under weak supervision. Building on the dual-stream, multi-granularity fusion architecture of \textit{RadioFormer}, \textit{RadioFormer3D} introduces a Fourier-based sampling encoder and a volumetric decoder to efficiently process sparse measurements in 3D space. To alleviate the lack of vertical supervision, we propose the \textbf{\textit{Joint Spectrum Integrity Loss}}, which integrates volume-level pseudo-label supervision, map-level geometry-aware radio rendering, and pixel-level localized constraints within a unified optimization scheme. This design enables the model to capture complex vertical structural relationships more effectively under sparse supervision. Extensive experiments across several radio map datasets show that \textit{RadioFormer3D} achieves superior overall performance compared to representative existing methods. In particular, it demonstrates improved reconstruction quality at unlabeled altitudes while maintaining a favorable trade-off between accuracy and inference efficiency, positioning it as a highly promising solution for future 3D environment-aware wireless networks.

AI.tldr: RadioFormer3D yields accurate volumetric spectrum reconstruction under weak supervision by combining a dual-stream design with a Fourier-based encoder and a volumetric decoder, guided by a Joint Spectrum Integrity Loss.
AI.motivation: 3D radio map estimation is essential for 3D wireless networks (e.g., low-altitude airspace, 3D HetNets) but faces increased spatial sparsity and limited vertical supervision, making accurate 3D propagation modeling challenging.
AI.method: Builds on RadioFormer with a Fourier-based sampling encoder and a volumetric decoder to process sparse 3D measurements. Introduces Joint Spectrum Integrity Loss that fuses volume-level pseudo-label supervision, geometry-aware map rendering, and pixel-level constraints to capture vertical structural relationships under sparse supervision.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple radio map datasets show RadioFormer3D achieves superior overall performance, notably improving reconstruction at unlabeled altitudes while maintaining a favorable accuracy–inference efficiency trade-off.
AI.conclusion: RadioFormer3D is a promising solution for 3D environment-aware wireless networks, effectively enabling volumetric spectrum reconstruction under weak supervision.

title: Optimal Gap-Dependent Regret for Private Stochastic Decision-Theoretic Online Learning
summary:
We study stochastic decision-theoretic online learning with full information and event-level pure differential privacy. A COLT open problem of Hu and Mehta asks to determine the optimal gap-dependent regret rate for stochastic decision-theoretic online learning under pure event-level differential privacy. For $K$ actions, losses in $[0,1]$, and a unique best action separated from the second-best action by gap $Δ_{\min}$, the known lower bound is of order $
  \frac{\log K}{\min\{Δ_{\min},\varepsilon\}}, $
or equivalently, up to universal constants, of order \[
  \frac{\log K}{Δ_{\min}}+\frac{\log K}{\varepsilon}. \] We give a horizon-free pure-DP algorithm and prove the explicit regret bound \[
  \operatorname{Reg}_T
  \le
  1000 \cdot \left(\frac{\log K}{Δ_{\min}}+\frac{\log K}{\varepsilon}\right) \] for every horizon $T$. The numerical constant is not optimized. The algorithm partitions time into blocks of exponentially increasing size, plays a single action throughout each block, and chooses the next action by an exponential mechanism applied to a data-independent random prefix of the previous block. The random prefix converts block regret into a sum, over all prefix lengths, of softmax selection errors. A single entropy-potential argument controls all privacy-dominated large-gap actions at cost $\log K/\varepsilon$.

AI.tldr: Horizon-free pure-DP algorithm for stochastic decision-theoretic online learning with full information; achieves regret Reg_T ≤ 1000*(log K / Δ_min + log K / ε) for all T, matching the lower bound up to constants.
AI.motivation: Resolve Hu and Mehta's COLT open problem on optimal gap-dependent regret under pure event-level differential privacy; integrate privacy constraints with online learning performance.
AI.method: Block-based algorithm with exponentially increasing block sizes; inside each block, the agent sticks to a single action; the next action is selected via an exponential mechanism applied to a data-independent random prefix of the previous block; the random prefix converts per-block regret into a sum over prefix lengths of softmax errors; an entropy-potential argument controls privacy-dominated large-gap actions.
AI.result: Reg_T ≤ 1000*(log K / Δ_min + log K / ε) for every horizon T; horizon-free; constants not optimized.
AI.conclusion: Sets a horizon-free pure-DP online-learning algorithm achieving the (up to constants) optimal gap-dependent regret, addressing the COLT open problem; leverages a combination of block structure, exponential mechanism, and entropy-potential analysis.

title: ConMoE: Expert-Pool Consolidation via Prototype Reassignment for MoE Compression
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models reduce per-token computation but still require storing and serving all experts, making deployment memory-intensive. Existing post-training compression methods mainly shrink this cost by pruning experts or merging their weights. We formulate post-training MoE compression as expert-pool consolidation: retaining a smaller set of pretrained experts as reusable prototypes and deterministically remapping each original expert reference to one selected prototype. This view separates the reduced expert pool from the reuse structure that represents the original expert slots, and allows prototype sharing within local layer scopes while preserving the original router interface. We propose ConMoE, a train-free prototype remapping framework that selects retained experts using calibration-based contribution and replaceability signals, then redirects original expert calls to the selected prototypes without weight updates or post-compression fine-tuning. Experiments on three pretrained MoE language models show that ConMoE matches or outperforms strong pruning and merging baselines in several settings, achieving the best average score on deepseek-moe-16b-base at both 25% and 50% routed-expert reduction, while remaining competitive on Qwen3-30B-A3B and OLMoE-1B-7B-0125. Ablations indicate that deterministic reassignment is the most stable component, whereas broader cross-layer sharing and post-hoc weight fusion are model-dependent.

AI.tldr: ConMoE provides train-free post-training compression for Mixture-of-Experts by consolidating to a small prototype pool and deterministically remapping original experts, achieving competitive performance with reduced routed-expert budget.
AI.motivation: Reduce memory and serving cost of Mixture-of-Experts models without fine-tuning, addressing the inefficiency of storing and routing many experts; existing post-training methods prune/merge with potential retraining needs.
AI.method: Proposes a prototype consolidation framework (ConMoE): select retained experts using calibration-based contribution and replaceability signals; deterministically remap each original expert to a chosen prototype within local layer scope; share prototypes across slots while preserving the original router interface; no weight updates or post-compression fine-tuning; experiments on three pretrained MoE LMs.
AI.result: ConMoE matches or outperforms pruning and merging baselines in several settings; achieves the best average score on deepseek-moe-16b-base at 25% and 50% routed-expert reduction; remains competitive on Qwen3-30B-A3B and OLMoE-1B-7B-0125; ablations show deterministic reassignment is the most stable component, while broader cross-layer sharing and post-hoc weight fusion are model-dependent.
AI.conclusion: Train-free prototype consolidation is an effective approach to reduce MoE routing memory while preserving performance, with deterministic expert reassignment as a key stabilizing factor; cross-layer sharing and weight fusion effects vary by model.

title: GiPL: Generative augmented iterative Pseudo-Labeling for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Object Detection
summary: Vision-language foundation models have shown promising zero-shot generalization for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Object Detection (CD-FSOD). However, they face two critical challenges in fine-tuning: insufficient support set utilization due to sparse single-instance annotations, and severe overfitting under extremely limited target-domain samples. To address these issues, this paper proposes GiPL, an efficient two-branch training framework.In the first branch, we design an iterative pseudo-label self-training paradigm, which performs zero-shot inference on the support set to generate reliable pseudo-annotations, fuses them with ground-truth labels, and iteratively optimizes the model to fully exploit support set data. In the second branch, we introduce generative data augmentation pipeline using large vision-language models, which synthesizes domain-aligned, multi-object annotated images to enrich training samples and suppress overfitting. Extensive experiments on three challenging CD-FSOD datasets (RUOD, CARPK, CarDD) under 1/5/10-shot settings demonstrate that GiPL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods with significant performance gains.Code is available at \href{https://github.com/z-yaz/CDiscover}{CDiscover}.

AI.tldr: GiPL is a two-branch training framework for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Object Detection (CD-FSOD) that combines iterative pseudo-label self-training on the support set with generative data augmentation from vision-language models to enrich training data, achieving state-of-the-art results on RUOD, CARPK, and CarDD across 1/5/10-shot settings; code is released.
AI.motivation: In CD-FSOD, the support set is sparsely annotated (often single-instance) leading to underutilization, and extremely limited target-domain samples cause severe overfitting. A method that both makes full use of the support data and mitigates overfitting is needed.
AI.method: GiPL has two branches. Branch 1: iterative pseudo-label self-training where zero-shot inference on the support set generates pseudo-annotations, which are fused with ground-truth labels and used to iteratively optimize the model to better exploit support data. Branch 2: generative data augmentation using large vision-language models to synthesize domain-aligned, multi-object annotated images to expand training data and reduce overfitting.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on RUOD, CARPK, and CarDD under 1/5/10-shot settings show GiPL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods with substantial performance gains. The paper releases code at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: GiPL effectively leverages sparse support data and combats overfitting through a complementary two-branch approach, delivering strong CD-FSOD performance improvements and offering a practical, extensible training framework.

title: Do Deep Networks Forget Initialization? A Forgetting-Time View of Practical Inductive Bias
summary: Randomly initialized neural networks induce a prior over functions, but the predictor used in practice is produced only after training. We ask how much of this initial bias survives the training pipeline. To make the question measurable, we introduce initialization memory: the dependence of the validation-selected predictor on the scale of the random initialization. We perform controlled CIFAR-10 experiments on ResNets where initialization memory already sharply separates training regimes. Low-learning-rate SGD can interpolate while still remembering its initialization: on ResNet-9 with batch size $b=128$, test accuracy varies by $26.5$ percentage points across initialization scales despite $\ge99.5\%$ training accuracy. This is not undertraining: extending the same low-learning-rate regime to $5{,}000$ epochs leaves the spread essentially unchanged. In contrast, Adam-family methods largely erase the dependence. SGD can also be made to forget when larger learning rates are paired with explicit $L_2$ norm control. We interpret these findings in terms of the time scale of forgetting: gradient-flow-like dynamics can preserve initialization memory, whereas stochastic finite-step effects, explicit norm decay, and adaptive preconditioning erase it on scales governed by the size of explicit or implicit regularization. The practical inductive bias of a trained network is therefore not the architectural prior alone, but the architectural prior after being filtered by the forgetting dynamics of the training pipeline; and the same regularizers that improve generalization are precisely those that erase memory of initialization.

AI.tldr: Initialization memory shows that the implicit prior from random initialization can survive training under SGD with small learning rates, leading to sizable performance differences across initialization scales; Adam largely erases this memory. The memory is governed by the forgetting timescale of the training dynamics: gradient-flow-like updates preserve it, while stochastic steps, explicit norm penalties, and adaptive preconditioning erase it. Practically, the inductive bias is the architectural prior filtered by the training pipeline’s forgetting dynamics, and regularizers that improve generalization tend to erase initialization memory.
AI.motivation: Quantify how much of the initial, random inductive bias in a neural network persists after training and how training dynamics (optimizer, learning rate, regularization) filter this bias into the final predictor.
AI.method: Controlled CIFAR-10 experiments on ResNets. Systematically vary initialization scale and compare optimization regimes: low-learning-rate SGD (and long training) vs. Adam-family methods; examine forgetting with higher learning rates and explicit L2 regularization; measure performance of the validation-selected predictor across initialization scales to quantify initialization memory.
AI.result: Under low-LR SGD, test accuracy varies by 26.5 percentage points across initialization scales despite >99.5% training accuracy; extending training to 5000 epochs leaves this spread essentially unchanged. Adam diminishes or eliminates the dependence on initialization. Larger learning rates paired with explicit L2 norm control can cause SGD to forget initialization memory.
AI.conclusion: The practical inductive bias of a trained network is the architectural prior filtered by the forgetting dynamics of the training pipeline. Regularizers that improve generalization concurrently erase memory of initialization; thus the same mechanism that yields better generalization reduces reliance on the initial random seed.

title: PassNet: Scaling Large Language Models for Graph Compiler Pass Generation
comment: Code and data available at https://github.com/PaddlePaddle/PassNet
summary: Modern tensor compilers such as TorchInductor deliver substantial speedups on mainstream models, yet face a systematic performance ceiling on long-tail workloads -- our profiling shows that 43% of real-world subgraphs experience end-to-end slowdowns under default compilation. While LLMs offer a path toward automated optimization, existing efforts focus on standalone kernel generation. We argue that pass generation -- where LLMs author structured graph transformations that integrate directly into compiler pipelines -- is the more appropriate abstraction. We propose PassNet, the first large-scale ecosystem for LLM-based compiler pass generation, comprising: (1) PassNet-Dataset, over 18K unique computational graphs from 100K real-world models; and (2) PassBench, 200 curated long-tail fusible tasks (comprising 2,060 subgraphs in total) evaluated under the Error-aware Speedup Score (ES_t) -- a metric unifying correctness, stability, and performance -- with layered integrity defenses against systematic LLM exploitation. Experiments reveal that PassBench is both highly discriminative and genuinely unsaturated: the best frontier model trails TorchInductor by 37% in aggregate, yet on individual subgraphs LLMs achieve up to 3x speedup over the same compiler -- indicating that the bottleneck is consistency, not capability.
