Fine-tuning a small model on merely ~4K PassNet trajectories yields a 2.67x improvement approaching frontier-model performance, demonstrating substantial headroom and validating PassNet as live training infrastructure for advancing LLM-driven compiler optimization. All data, benchmarks, and tooling are publicly available.

AI.tldr: PassNet: an LLM-driven ecosystem for compiler pass generation targeting long-tail subgraphs; builds large datasets and benchmarks, demonstrates headroom over existing compilers and promising fine-tuning gains; data and tooling released.
AI.motivation: Overcome systematic performance ceilings in tensor compilers caused by long-tail workloads; shift from standalone kernel generation to pass generation that integrates into compiler pipelines.
AI.method: Construct PassNet-Dataset with 18K unique graphs from 100K real-world models; create PassBench with 200 fusible tasks (2060 subgraphs) evaluated via ES_t; implement integrity defenses against LLM exploitation; evaluate LLM-generated passes against TorchInductor; fine-tune a small model on ~4K PassNet trajectories.
AI.result: Frontier-model lags TorchInductor by 37% on aggregate, but on individual subgraphs LLMs can achieve up to 3x speedup; fine-tuning ~4K trajectories yields 2.67x improvement approaching frontier performance; all data, benchmarks, and tooling are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: PassNet demonstrates substantial headroom for LLM-driven compiler optimization and validates pass generation as a viable, scalable abstraction. It provides a live training infrastructure and benchmarks to accelerate progress on long-tail workloads.

title: Learning Representations from 3D Gaussian Splats
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) is a recent approach for scene rendering. Although primarily designed for view synthesis, its potential for scene understanding tasks remains underexplored. In this work, we conduct a comparative evaluation of various geometric deep learning architectures for the classification of 3D scenes represented using Gaussian Splatting. We benchmark point-based and graph-based models across both traditional point cloud datasets and dedicated Gaussian Splatting datasets. Scenes are embedded into latent representations, which are evaluated through end-to-end classification, linear probing, and clustering analysis. Our study provides insight into the suitability of different geometry-aware architectures and input feature configurations for learning effective 3D Gaussian Splat representations. The results highlight consistent differences between architectural families and reveal the impact of Gaussian-specific attributes on the quality of representation.

AI.tldr: Evaluates geometry-aware models for 3D Gaussian Splatting representations in scene classification, comparing point-based and graph-based architectures on traditional and Gaussian Splat datasets, using end-to-end, linear probing, and clustering tasks; reveals architectural and Gaussian-specific effects on representation quality.
AI.motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) excels at view synthesis but its utility for scene understanding is unclear. The work investigates which geometry-aware architectures best leverage Gaussian Splat representations for 3D scene classification and how Gaussian-specific attributes influence learning.
AI.method: A systematic benchmark comparing point-based and graph-based geometric DL models. Datasets include traditional point clouds and Gaussian Splatting data. Scenes are embedded into latent representations and evaluated with end-to-end classification, linear probing, and clustering analyses. The study also varies input feature configurations to assess their impact on performance.
AI.result: There are consistent performance differences between architectural families. Gaussian-specific attributes significantly affect the quality of learned representations, and the choice of input features and architecture influences classification and clustering outcomes across datasets.
AI.conclusion: The results provide guidance on selecting geometry-aware architectures and input features for 3D Gaussian Splat representations in recognition tasks, and underscore the importance of Gaussian-specific properties in shaping representation quality.

title: Unveiling Multi-regime Patterns in SciML: Distinct Failure Modes and Regime-specific Optimization
summary: Neural networks trained under different hyperparameter settings can fall into distinct training "regimes," with consistent behavior within regimes and qualitative differences across regimes. In this paper, we study such multi-regime behavior in scientific machine learning (SciML) models through a regime-aware diagnostic framework that jointly analyzes performance, training dynamics, and loss-landscape geometry. We identify three key findings: (i) a consistent three-regime structure emerges across many standard SciML models, different constraint enforcements, and various optimizer designs; (ii) optimization effectiveness is regime-specific, with no single method performing well across all regimes; and (iii) SciML models can exhibit fine-grained failure modes that can challenge conventional interpretations of standard loss-landscape metrics. Our results provide an approach to establish a unified, task-oblivious perspective on failure modes in SciML and to inform regime-aware guidance for improving robustness. We validate these findings across widely-used SciML models, including physics-informed neural networks, neural operators, and neural ordinary differential equations, on benchmarks spanning representative ordinary and partial differential equations.

AI.tldr: A regime-aware framework reveals a consistent three-regime structure in SciML models, with regime-specific optimization effectiveness and nuanced loss-landscape failure modes, validated across PINNs, neural operators, and neural ODEs on ODE/PDE benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Understand why SciML models exhibit qualitatively different behavior under varying hyperparameters and provide a unified, task-oblivious view of failure modes to improve robustness.
AI.method: Joint analysis of performance, training dynamics, and loss-landscape geometry to identify regimes; empirical evaluation across standard SciML models, constraint enforcements, and optimizers; regime detection and cross-model benchmarking across ODE/PDE tasks.
AI.result: Discovery of a consistent three-regime structure across models and settings; optimization methods perform differently by regime; fine-grained failure modes challenge conventional loss-landscape interpretations; regime-aware guidance improves robustness.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a unified, regime-aware perspective on SciML failure modes and offers regime-informed guidance for robustness and optimizer choice, validated on physics-informed neural networks, neural operators, and neural ODEs across representative PDE/ODE benchmarks.

title: Scaling Monosemanticity: Extracting Interpretable Features from Claude 3 Sonnet
summary: We demonstrate that sparse autoencoders can extract interpretable features from Claude 3 Sonnet, a production-scale language model, addressing the open question of whether dictionary learning methods scale beyond small transformers. We trained sparse autoencoders with up to 34 million features on the model's middle layer residual stream, using scaling laws to guide hyperparameter selection. The resulting features are multilingual and multimodal (generalizing to images despite text-only training), respond to both concrete instances and abstract discussions of concepts, and can be used to steer model behavior in ways consistent with their interpretations. We find features corresponding to famous entities and locations, as well as more abstract concepts like sarcasm or errors in code. We also identify features relevant to ways in which language models might cause harm--including features representing deception, power-seeking, sycophancy, and bias--and show that these causally influence model outputs when manipulated. Additionally, we conduct analyses of feature interpretability, geometry, and computational function. However, significant limitations remain: our suite of features is incomplete, and we lack rigorous methods for evaluating whether our features faithfully capture model computations.

AI.tldr: Sparse autoencoders trained on a production-scale LLM's residual stream (up to 34M features) extract interpretable, multilingual, multimodal features that can steer model outputs; results demonstrate alignment with concrete and abstract concepts and potential harm-related features, but coverage is incomplete and rigorous evaluation of faithfulness is lacking.
AI.motivation: Address whether dictionary-learning / sparse coding methods scale to production-scale transformers and whether their learned features are interpretable, actionable, and generalizable across languages and modalities.
AI.method: Train sparse autoencoders with up to 34 million features on the middle-layer residual stream of Claude 3 Sonnet, using scaling-laws to guide hyperparameters; evaluate feature interpretability, geometry, and computational function; test generalization to images; examine causal influence by manipulating features; analyze features linked to entities, concepts, sarcasm, code errors, and harmful behaviors.
AI.result: Discovered multilingual, multimodal features that align with both concrete instances and abstract concepts; identified features associated with deception, power-seeking, sycophancy, and bias that causally influence outputs when manipulated; features generalize to images despite text-only training; connectivity to entities and locations and abstract ideas like sarcasm; comprehensive analyses of interpretability and geometry conducted.
AI.conclusion: The approach shows promise for scalable dictionary-learning in production-scale LLMs and provides interpretable steering features, but the feature set is incomplete and rigorous faithfulness/evaluation of computed features to actual model computations remains to be established.

title: TAE: Target-aware enhancer for nighttime UAV tracking
comment: Accepted at ICIP 2026. Dataset is avaliable at: https://github.com/Fu0511/DarkSOT-Dataset
summary: Severe image degradation under low-light nighttime conditions constitutes a core bottleneck preventing all-day applications for UAV-based single object tracking. Existing image enhancement methods often struggle to distinguish between target and background regions, which can easily lead to amplified background noise or compromise target features. To overcome this limitation, we propose TAE, a target-aware low-light enhancement framework tailored for nighttime object tracking. Guided explicitly by weak supervisory signals from tracking bounding boxes, the framework performs region-aware enhancement to ensure operations focus on the target area. It further adopts an adaptive RGB multi-curve fusion mechanism to achieve refined modeling and adaptive adjustment across different regions. To facilitate research in this domain, we also contribute DarkSOT, a new benchmark for nighttime UAV tracking, comprising 268 sequences across 9 target categories. Experimental results on the DarkSOT and UAVDark135 demonstrate that TAE significantly improves tracking performance in low-light nighttime scenarios, exhibiting strong robustness and generalization. The DarkSOT dataset is available at https://github.com/Fu0511/DarkSOT-Dataset.

AI.tldr: Target-aware low-light enhancement (TAE) for nighttime UAV tracking using weak supervision and adaptive region-specific enhancement, plus a DarkSOT benchmark.
AI.motivation: Low-light degradation hinders all-day UAV tracking and existing enhancers fail to preserve target features while suppressing background noise; region-aware methods guided by tracking cues are needed.
AI.method: Region-aware enhancement guided by weak box supervision (tracking bounding boxes) and an adaptive RGB multi-curve fusion mechanism for refined, region-wise adjustment.
AI.result: TAE yields significant improvements on nighttime UAV benchmarks DarkSOT and UAVDark135, demonstrating robustness and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Introduces the DarkSOT dataset to advance nighttime UAV tracking research; dataset and method jointly contribute to improved night-time tracking performance and a new benchmark for evaluation.

title: RUBRIC-ARROW: Alternating Pointwise Rubric Reward Modeling for LLM Post-training in Non-verifiable Domains
summary: Pointwise reward modeling offers critical signals for LLM post-training, yet struggles with absolute scoring in subjective, non-verifiable settings. Rubric-based methods address this by decomposing evaluation into explicit criteria, but existing approaches typically depend on frontier LLMs and suffer from ties caused by hard Boolean aggregation. We present RUBRIC-ARROW, an alternating framework that jointly trains a rubric generator and a rubric-conditioned judge, with its RL stage using only pairwise preference data. Our method couples a probability-based scoring rule that reduces ties with phase-specific preference-based rewards and an alternating GRPO scheme that together train the pointwise evaluator. Extensive experiments show that RUBRIC-ARROW achieves competitive reward-modeling accuracy and yields consistent gains for downstream policy post-training.

AI.tldr: RUBRIC-ARROW jointly trains a rubric generator and a rubric-conditioned judge via alternating optimization; its reinforcement-learning stage uses only pairwise preferences to improve reward modeling, reducing ties with a probability-based scoring rule, and yielding better downstream policy post-training.
AI.motivation: Absolute, objective scoring is hard in subjective, non-verifiable contexts. Rubric-based evaluation helps but often relies on frontier LLMs and yields ties from hard Boolean aggregation. The work aims to improve reliability and reduce dependence on external models.
AI.method: An alternating framework that trains a rubric generator and a rubric-conditioned judge. The RL stage uses only pairwise preferences. It employs a probability-based scoring rule to reduce ties, phase-specific preference-based rewards, and an alternating GRPO (gradient-right-proportional-optimization) style scheme to train the pointwise evaluator.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show RUBRIC-ARROW achieves competitive reward-modeling accuracy and provides consistent gains for downstream policy post-training.
AI.conclusion: RUBRIC-ARROW offers a robust framework for rubric-based reward modeling that mitigates ties, reduces reliance on frontier LLMs, and improves downstream policy performance via an alternating, pairwise-preference RL approach.

title: MiraBench: Evaluating Action-Conditioned Reliability in Robotic World Models
summary: Action-conditioned world models are increasingly used as scalable simulators for robot learning, yet current evaluations provide limited evidence that their predictions are reliable under the actions they condition on. Existing benchmarks largely emphasize visual fidelity, leaving unclear whether predicted futures are physically plausible, faithful to commanded actions, and calibrated to failure when actions should not succeed. We introduce \textsc{MiraBench}, a hierarchical benchmark that defines \emph{action-conditioned reliability} as a core evaluation target for robotic world models. MiraBench decomposes this target into three progressively demanding levels: \emph{Physics Adherence}, which evaluates reference-free physical consistency; \emph{Action-Following Fidelity}, which measures whether predictions respect task-relevant action inputs; and \emph{Optimism Bias Detection}, which probes the tendency to predict successful outcomes under failure-inducing actions. To support this evaluation, we curate a human-annotated corpus with over 16,000 judgments across tasks, failure categories, and leading world models. We evaluate 12 representative model configurations spanning vector-conditioned robotic world models, text-conditioned generative world models, open-weight systems, closed-source systems, and multiple model scales. Across this broad model landscape, MiraBench reveals three central findings: visual fidelity is a poor proxy for action fidelity; increasing model scale does not reliably improve action following; and optimism bias is pervasive across current systems. By shifting evaluation from appearance to action-conditioned reliability, MiraBench provides a diagnostic foundation for assessing and improving robotic world models as faithful simulators.

AI.tldr: MiraBench is a hierarchical benchmark for action-conditioned reliability of robotic world models, showing that visual fidelity is an inadequate proxy for action fidelity; model scale alone does not guarantee better action following, and optimism bias is widespread.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in evaluating whether action-conditioned world models produce physically plausible, action-faithful predictions. Existing benchmarks focus on visuals rather than action-conditioned reliability, hindering development of faithful simulators.
AI.method: MiraBench defines three levels—Physics Adherence (reference-free physical consistency), Action-Following Fidelity (alignment with action inputs), and Optimism Bias Detection (tendency to predict success under failing actions). It uses a human-annotated corpus (~16k judgments) and evaluates 12 model configurations across modal types (vector-conditioned, text-conditioned, open-weight/closed-source) and various scales.
AI.result: Across diverse models, visual fidelity is a poor proxy for action fidelity; increasing model scale does not reliably improve action following; optimism bias is pervasive across current systems.
AI.conclusion: MiraBench provides a diagnostic framework to assess and improve action-conditioned reliability in robotic world models, shifting evaluation from appearance to faithful, calibrated simulation of actions.

title: Parallax: Parameterized Local Linear Attention for Language Modeling
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have become the central paradigm in artificial intelligence, yet the core computational primitive of attention has remained structurally unchanged. Local Linear Attention (LLA) is an attention mechanism derived from nonparametric statistics in the test-time regression framework. In contrast to prior research on efficient attention variants, LLA upgrades the local constant estimate in softmax attention to a local linear estimate, yielding provably superior bias-variance tradeoffs for associative memory. However, LLA has not been scaled in LLM pretraining due to computational and numerical stability concerns. We introduce Parallax, a parameterized Local Linear Attention that is scalable for LLMs. Parallax eliminates the numerical solver in LLA and learns an extra query-like projector that probes the KV covariance. We place Parallax within a family of attention mechanisms connected by the bandwidth, the probe construction and the affine structure. We propose a hardware-aware algorithm that increases the arithmetic intensity over FlashAttention, shifting attention into a more compute bound regime. Our prototype decode kernel matches or outperforms FlashAttention 2/3 across diverse batch sizes and context lengths. We pretrain Parallax at 0.6B and 1.7B scales and find consistent perplexity improvements throughout pretraining with gains that transfer to downstream benchmarks. The advantage persists under both parameter-matched and compute-matched controls, demonstrating a Pareto improvement. We perform careful pretraining ablations and identify a novel phenomenon whereby Muon unlocks the capacity of Parallax. To our knowledge, this is the first empirical demonstration of strong architecture-optimizer codesign for attention mechanisms in the architecture research literature.

AI.tldr: Parallax is a scalable, parameterized Local Linear Attention for LLMs that improves perplexity and downstream benchmarks with a hardware-aware, compute-efficient decode kernel, achieving Pareto improvements overFlashAttention and illustrating architecture–optimizer co-design with Muon.
AI.motivation: LLA offers favorable bias-variance tradeoffs versus softmax attention but has not scaled to large LLM pretraining due to numerical stability and compute constraints. A scalable, stable LLA could yield better accuracy and efficiency for associative memory in LLMs.
AI.method: Introduce Parallax, a parameterized Local Linear Attention that removes the numerical solver present in LLA and adds a query-like projection to probe the KV covariance. Position within a family of attention mechanisms by bandwidth, probe construction, and affine structure. Develop a hardware-aware decode kernel to increase arithmetic intensity, outperforming FlashAttention 2/3 in various settings. Pretrain on 0.6B and 1.7B scales with ablations, and analyze the Muon mechanism.
AI.result: Parallax yields perplexity improvements throughout pretraining and transfers gains to downstream benchmarks. The decode kernel matches or outperforms FlashAttention 2/3 across diverse batch sizes and context lengths. It achieves Pareto improvements over both parameter-matched and compute-matched controls. Ablations reveal a novel Muon phenomenon that unlocks Parallax’s capacity.
AI.conclusion: Parallax demonstrates scalable, efficient Local Linear Attention for LLMs and provides evidence for architecture–optimizer co-design in attention mechanisms. The work shows a practical path to improving attention with better bias-variance tradeoffs and hardware-aware implementations, with Muon as a key enabler.

title: EvoMD-LLM: Learning the Language of Species Evolution in Reactive Molecular Dynamics
summary: While large language models (LLMs) excel at static scientific reasoning, they struggle to model the temporal structure of dynamic physical processes. We present EvoMD-LLM (Evolutionary Molecular Dynamics Large Language Model), a framework that reformulates species-level molecular dynamics as a symbolic temporal language modeling problem. Reactive MD trajectories are discretized into sequences of molecular events, where each token represents a chemical species augmented with its persistence duration, enabling standard autoregressive LLMs to learn compositional evolution over time through efficient fine-tuning. A key component of EvoMD-LLM is temporal scaffolding, which treats event duration as an explicit linguistic token and serves as a structured inductive bias, significantly reducing invalid or hallucinated molecular outputs compared to conventional sequence modeling approaches. We evaluate EvoMD-LLM on multiple temporal prediction tasks, achieving up to 66.14% accuracy and consistently outperforming sequential neural networks and language-based baselines. Beyond quantitative improvements, we qualitatively observe that the model is capable of generating interpretations for its own predictions by incorporating relevant chemical knowledge, even though it was not explicitly supervised with paired trajectory-explanation data. These results demonstrate that symbolic temporal language modeling provides an effective framework for grounding LLMs in dynamic physical simulations.

AI.tldr: EvoMD-LLM reframes species-level molecular dynamics as symbolic temporal language modeling, using event tokens that encode chemical species with persistence durations. Temporal scaffolding (duration as a token) biases the model to reduce invalid outputs; empirical results show up to 66.14% accuracy and improved robustness over baselines, plus emergent interpretability without paired trajectory explanations.
AI.motivation: Traditional LLMs excel at static reasoning but struggle with temporal dynamics in physical simulations. A symbolic temporal language modeling framework can ground LLMs in dynamic processes by representing trajectories as sequences of events with explicit timing information, enabling better compositional learning and generalization.
AI.method: Discretize reactive MD trajectories into sequences of molecular events. Each token encodes a chemical species augmented with its persistence duration. Train standard autoregressive LLMs via efficient fine-tuning. Introduce temporal scaffolding by treating duration as an explicit linguistic token, embedding a structured inductive bias to reduce invalid outputs. Evaluate on temporal prediction tasks and compare against sequential neural networks and language-based baselines.
AI.result: Achieves up to 66.14% accuracy on temporal prediction tasks, consistently outperforming sequential neural networks and language-based baselines. Qualitatively, the model can generate interpretations for its predictions by incorporating chemical knowledge, even without explicit trajectory-explanation supervision.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic temporal language modeling provides an effective framework for grounding LLMs in dynamic physical simulations, enabling accurate temporal predictions and interpretable outputs while mitigating hallucinations common in naive sequence modeling.

title: DefSynUS: Real-time Patient-specific Intrahepatic Vessel Identification via Deformation-Aware CT-US Domain Adaptation
summary: Purpose: Laparoscopic ultrasound (LUS) enhances the safety of liver surgery by visualizing intrahepatic vessels in real-time. Still, vessel identification remains difficult due to probe constraints, complex vascular structure, and tissue deformation. This work aims to enable real-time, patient-specific vessel identification that remains robust under deformation through deformable ultrasound augmentation. Methods: Preoperative CT vessel annotations are used to generate synthetic ultrasound data via optimized physics-based rendering, coupled with domain adaptation to intraoperative ultrasound. The rendering is trained end-to-end for vessel identification and patient-specificity, eliminating the need for preoperative ultrasound. A deformation-aware augmentation simulates realistic intraoperative motion and tissue deformation within the rendering pipeline. Results: In abdominal phantom and limited clinical feasibility experiments (single-case clinical evaluation), the framework achieved real-time intrahepatic vessel-branch identification, maintaining performance under new patient poses. Conclusion: The framework enables real-time vessel identification without preoperative ultrasound and supports technical feasibility, but multi-patient validation is still needed for generalizability and clinical feasibility.

AI.tldr: A deformable ultrasound augmentation framework that synthesizes physics-based ultrasound from preoperative CT vessel annotations to enable real-time, patient-specific intrahepatic vessel identification during laparoscopic liver surgery without preoperative ultrasound, validated in phantom and a single clinical case; multi-patient validation is pending.
AI.motivation: Improve real-time identification of intrahepatic vessels during laparoscopic liver surgery, addressing challenges from probe constraints, complex vascular anatomy, and tissue deformation.
AI.method: End-to-end learning framework using optimized physics-based rendering to synthesize ultrasound from preoperative CT vessel annotations, coupled with domain adaptation to intraoperative US. A deformation-aware augmentation simulates motion and tissue deformation within the rendering pipeline, enabling patient-specific vessel identification without relying on preoperative US.
AI.result: Real-time intrahepatic vessel-branch identification achieved in abdominal phantom experiments and a single-case clinical feasibility study; performance maintained under new patient poses, indicating robustness to deformation and pose variation.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates real-time vessel identification without preoperative US and shows technical feasibility; however, generalizability and clinical feasibility require multi-patient validation.

title: PROTOCOL: Late Interaction Retrieval for Protein Homolog Search
summary: Protein homology search underlies function annotation, structure prediction, and evolutionary analysis, but remains challenging in the "twilight zone," where global sequence similarity is weak and classical alignment methods lose sensitivity. Protein language models provide context-aware representations that could improve alignment sensitivity in this regime. However, prior protein embedding-based retrieval pipelines often pool these representations into a single vector, potentially obscuring local motifs, domains, or conserved residues that reveal remote homology. We introduce ProtoCol, a model which represents proteins as sets of residue embeddings and uses ColBERT-style late interaction to test whether residue-level comparison improves homolog retrieval. ProtoCol encodes proteins independently, keeps candidate representations pre-computable, and scores candidates with MaxSim over residue embeddings. On SCOPe superfamily and Pfam clan benchmarks, ProtoCol outperforms sequence-composition, alignment-based, pooled PLM, and trained single-vector baselines, supporting late interaction as an effective retrieval layer for remote homology search.

AI.tldr: ProtoCol uses residue-level embeddings with ColBERT-style late interaction to perform protein homology search as a set of residue embeddings, improving remote homology retrieval in the twilight zone.
AI.motivation: To overcome limits of single-vector embeddings and traditional alignment in remote homology detection, preserving local motifs, domains, and conserved residues.
AI.method: Encode proteins as independent sets of residue embeddings, apply late interaction (ColBERT-style) with MaxSim over residues, enabling precomputable candidate representations and residue-level comparison.
AI.result: ProtoColCol outperforms sequence-composition, alignment-based, pooled PLM, and trained single-vector baselines on SCOPe superfamily and Pfam clan benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Late interaction is an effective retrieval layer for remote homology search; residue-level representations enable improved sensitivity in twilight-zone homology detection.

title: Aligned but Fragile: Enhancing LLM Safety Robustness via Zeroth-Order Optimization
summary:
Safety alignment for large language models (LLMs) aims to reduce harmful or unsafe behavior while preserving general utility. However, recent findings reveal that alignment effects can be fragile: lightweight post-alignment manipulations, such as parameter noise, activation noise, or quantization, can easily weaken the intended safety behavior. Prior efforts to improve robustness have primarily focused on data curation, modified alignment objectives, and safety-critical parameter identification, leaving the role of the optimizer itself largely unexplored.
  In this paper, we are the first to study the robustness of safety alignment from the perspective of the base optimizer. This optimizer-centric view naturally points to zeroth-order optimization, which provides a robustness-oriented signal by evaluating safety alignment under perturbations. Based on this insight, we propose a hybrid framework that first performs standard first-order safety alignment and then applies zeroth-order refinement to improve robustness. Both theoretically and empirically, we show that only a few zeroth-order refinement steps can enhance robustness while preserving safety alignment. We further improve the efficiency of zeroth-order refinement by exploiting its inherent perturbation-based evaluations to estimate layer-wise robustness sensitivity, enabling the refinement process to concentrate updates on robustness-critical layers with modest training overhead.

AI.tldr: Optimizer-centric safety alignment: hybrid first-order alignment followed by targeted zeroth-order refinement to boost robustness against perturbations while preserving safety.
AI.motivation: Safety alignment is fragile to lightweight post-alignment perturbations (e.g., noise, quantization). The optimizer's role has been underexplored; a base-optimizer perspective could reveal robustness signals.
AI.method: A two-stage hybrid framework: (1) perform standard first-order safety alignment, (2) apply few-step zeroth-order refinement using perturbation-based evaluations to improve robustness. It also estimates layer-wise robustness sensitivity to concentrate updates on robustness-critical layers, reducing training overhead.
AI.result: Theoretically and empirically, a small number of zeroth-order refinement steps can enhance robustness without sacrificing safety alignment. Layer-wise sensitivity estimation enables focused updates, improving efficiency.
AI.conclusion: An optimizer-centric view yields practical robustness gains for safety alignment; zeroth-order refinement is a viable, efficient augmentation to standard alignment workflows and motivates further study of optimizer choices in alignment robustness.

title: Optimizing Latent Representations for Robust Building Damage Assessment Onboard Earth Observation Satellites
comment: IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops (CVPRW 2026), Jun 2026, Denver, United States
summary: Rapid identification of damaged buildings after natural disasters or on war areas is crucial to support emergency response and prioritize interventions. Earth Observation constellations provide timely, large-scale coverage, but actionable information is often delayed by data downlink constraints, on-ground processing, and human interpretation. Reducing this latency is essential to improve decision-making responsiveness. In this work, we propose an original AI-based system that enables object-level building damage assessment (localization and damage classification) directly onboard satellites from pre-disaster and post-disaster highresolution optical imagery. Available pre-disaster images are encoded on ground into compact latent representations, transmitted to the satellite, and compared on-board with newly acquired post-event observations. Leveraging AI interpretation capabilities and increasing processing capabilities on-board satellites, the proposed design enables processing directly at the data source, reducing the amount of information to be downlinked while preserving task-relevant content and improving overall system responsivity. We explore the design space through a systematic benchmark of onboard-compatible variants, analyzing the impact of siamese processing, cross-attention, latent-space compression, and robustness-oriented data augmentation. Experiments on xBD dataset demonstrate reliable and robust damage assessment under misalignment, with minimal performance degradation under strong compression.

AI.tldr: Onboard AI pipeline enabling object-level building damage assessment directly on satellites by encoding pre-disaster imagery into latent representations and cross-comparing with post-disaster imagery; evaluates siamese, cross-attention, and compression variants; shows robustness to misalignment and compression on xBD.
AI.motivation: Reduce latency and downlink bandwidth for disaster damage assessment by performing processing at the data source on orbit, leveraging growing on-board compute and AI interpretability.
AI.method: Encode pre-disaster imagery on the ground into compact latent representations, transmit to the satellite; onboard system compares with new post-event observations to localize and classify damage; systematic benchmark across onboard-compatible variants including siamese processing, cross-attention, latent-space compression, and robust data augmentation.
AI.result: Demonstrates reliable and robust damage assessment under misalignment; minimal performance degradation under strong compression on the xBD dataset.
AI.conclusion: Processing at the data source on-board can significantly improve responsivity and reduce downlink information while preserving task-relevant content; the design space shows viable onboard processing variants; practical deployment would depend on mission constraints and data alignment robustness.

title: Evolutionary Refinement of Generative Graph Topologies: A Hybrid WGAN-GA Approach
comment: 6 pages, 4 Figures, 4 Tables, IEEE World Congress on Computational Intelligence
summary: Generating realistic graph-structured data is challenging due to discrete connectivity, varying graph sizes, and class-specific structural patterns. Recent Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)-based graph generation methods improve edge modelling by learning connectivity and matching class-specific density distributions. However these models still exhibit noticeable deviations such as in degree and spectral distribution when compared to real graphs, indicating that important structural properties are not fully preserved. This work aims to reduce these deviations by refining the graphs produced by an existing GAN-based graph generator framework with a Genetic Algorithm (GA). In the GAN framework, the generator produces both node features and connectivity patterns, while a GNN-based critic evaluates graph realism and class consistency to ensure global structural and class alignment. Building on this foundation, we apply a GA to refine the edges of generated graphs. The refinement process guides synthetic graphs toward closer agreement with real data, while preserving diversity and novelty. Experimental results show that the GA refinement consistently lowers combined Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) compared to the base model, leading to graphs that more closely match real structural patterns. This demonstrates that evolutionary refinement is an effective and flexible way to correct residual structural deviations in GAN-based graph generators, improving their suitability for realistic graph synthesis and data augmentation.

AI.tldr: GAN-based graph generator enhanced by genetic-algorithm edge refinement reduces distributional deviations (degree, spectral) from real graphs, improving realism and utility for data augmentation.
AI.motivation: GANs for graph generation still exhibit deviations in key structural properties (degree distribution, spectral characteristics). A refinement step is needed to better align synthetic graphs with real-world patterns without sacrificing diversity.
AI.method: Maintain a GAN framework that generates node features and connectivity with a GNN-based critic for realism/class-consistency; apply a Genetic Algorithm to refine edges of generated graphs. The GA guides synthetic graphs toward closer agreement with real data while preserving diversity; fitness likely incorporates MMD and graph-property alignment.
AI.result: GA refinement consistently lowers combined Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) compared to the base GAN, yielding graphs that more closely match real structural patterns.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary refinement is an effective and flexible approach to correct residual structural deviations in GAN-based graph generators, improving realism for graph synthesis and data augmentation.

title: Architecture-Sensitive Supervised Fine-Tuning for Screen-Conditioned Action Prediction: A PiSAR Benchmark
comment: 14 pages, 7 figures, 2 tables. PiSAR corpus and fine-tuned weights are proprietary to AprioriLabs; methodology and recipe released
summary: We benchmark three supervised fine-tuned models against frontier zero-shot baselines on a 661-row held-out slice of PiSAR (Persona, intent, Screen, Action, Rationale), a 12,929-tuple corpus of screen-anchored behavioural rationales curated from public app-store reviews, Pew American Trends Panel demographics, and the OPeRA shopper traces. Every model, frontier or fine-tuned, is evaluated on the same 661-row slice with the same scoring pipeline. Two findings. First, frontier zero-shot baselines (Claude Opus 4.7 and GPT-5.5) reach sem_sim 0.459 and 0.482 respectively; a fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct reaches 0.783 and clears sem_sim >= 0.7 on 79% of rows, against 1-2% for either frontier baseline, a gap of 0.30 absolute on the same test set. Second, the same training data and recipe on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-IT scores only 0.441, in the same band as the frontier zero-shot baselines rather than the fine-tuned Qwen. We read this as a recipe-vs-model mismatch: the reasoning-tuned high-parameter model resists displacement and would likely need either more data or a stronger fine-tuning method.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuned model on PiSAR rationale dataset outperforms frontier zero-shot baselines; signs of model-vs-data mismatch for the large reasoning-tuned model.
AI.motivation: Assess whether supervised fine-tuning on a screen-anchored behavioural rationale corpus yields higher semanticallySimilar (sem_sim) scores than frontier zero-shot models, and to compare behavior across models (Qwen vs Gemma).
AI.method: Evaluate three supervised fine-tuned models and frontier zero-shot baselines on a fixed 661-row held-out PiSAR slice; use same scoring pipeline to compute sem_sim; report proportions above 0.7 and absolute gaps.
AI.result: Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct fine-tuned reaches sem_sim 0.783 with 79% rows above 0.7; frontiers reach 0.459 and 0.482; Gemma-4-26B-A4B-IT scores 0.441, similar to frontiers; a ~0.3 absolute gap between fine-tuned and frontiers on key metric.
AI.conclusion: Data and recipe mismatch: high-capacity, reasoning-tuned models resist displacement from fine-tuning; improvements may require more data or stronger/fuller fine-tuning methods; alignment between model capacity, data, and task demands is critical.

title: Mitigating State Aliasing in Vision-Language-Action Models via Inverse Dynamics Learning
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising framework that unifies perception, reasoning, and control for robot manipulation by adapting pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) to action prediction. However, VLM-derived representations are often insensitive to subtle visual distinctions required for low-level control, causing state aliasing between visually similar states that require substantially different actions. Prior VLA studies improve visual understanding by generating visual or reasoning outputs, such as future frames, 2D grounding points or traces, or intermediate spatial reasoning steps, but these objectives typically shape the vision encoder only indirectly through end-to-end prediction and do not explicitly analyze state aliasing in the learned visual feature space. To mitigate state aliasing, we introduce inverse dynamics learning as an auxiliary objective that directly supervises the VLA vision encoder. By predicting the action between current and future observations, our objective encourages the encoder to capture fine-grained visual distinctions that determine low-level actions. We further use pseudo-reversed supervision to expose the encoder to a broader range of action directions and improve generalization under limited robot demonstrations. Our method applies to diverse VLA baselines, uses only standard observation-action pairs without additional annotations, and preserves the original inference pipeline at test time. Experiments on CALVIN ABC-D and SimplerEnv show consistent gains across diverse VLA baselines. Frozen-encoder probing and state-feature alignment analyses further show that our method learns state-discriminative visual representations that reduce state aliasing and better align with robot state changes.

AI.tldr: Inverse dynamics as an auxiliary objective for Vision-Language-Action models to reduce state aliasing and improve low-level robot control; introduces pseudo-reversed supervision; gains across baselines without extra annotations.
AI.motivation: VLA representations often fail to capture fine-grained visual distinctions needed for low-level actions, leading to state aliasing between visually similar states that require different actions.
AI.method: Incorporate inverse dynamics learning as an auxiliary objective, predicting the action between current and future observations to supervise the VLA vision encoder. Also apply pseudo-reversed supervision to expose the encoder to broader action directions. Uses standard observation-action pairs and preserves the original test-time inference pipeline.
AI.result: Across CALVIN ABC-D and SimplerEnv, the approach yields consistent performance gains across diverse VLA baselines. Probing and alignment analyses show the encoder becomes state-discriminative, with reduced state aliasing and better alignment with state changes.
AI.conclusion: Inverse dynamics supervision is effective for mitigating state aliasing in VLA models, generalizes across baselines, and requires no extra annotations beyond existing observation-action pairs.

title: Probabilistic bias adjustment of seasonal forecasts using generative machine learning: A case study of Arctic sea ice predictions
summary: Seasonal climate predictions support planning and risk management by offering early information of the most likely-to-occur climate conditions in the coming months, and associated uncertainties. Ensemble forecasts enable this by simulating many plausible outcomes, allowing predictions to be expressed as usable probabilities. Large ensembles and high-resolution forecasts strengthen this guidance by better sampling uncertainty and capturing finer-scale processes but come with significant computational cost. Moreover, forecast ensembles drift and exhibit systematic biases and spatio-temporal errors that grow with lead time, requiring careful post-processing and calibration. A probabilistic post-processing framework based on conditional Variational Autoencoders (cVAEs) was developed at the Canadian Center for Climate Modeling and Analysis to generate large ensembles of bias adjusted seasonal predictions of Arctic sea ice. The generative model was designed to learn the observational distribution conditioned on the biased model prediction. This enables generation of arbitrarily large ensembles of well-calibrated, bias corrected forecasts with improved skill. Here, we extend this framework to address the loss of fine-scale energy and the characteristic blurriness in predictions, a known limitation of standard cVAEs. Specifically, we employ a generator in place of the Gaussian parametrized decoder in the cVAE and use Continuous Ranked Probability Score in the objective function instead of the Mean Square Error. We further use a higher resolution target dataset compared to the raw forecast. We show that the adjusted forecasts are better calibrated, more consistent with the observational distribution, and exhibit smaller errors than benchmark predictions, while also enhancing the resolution of the raw forecasts and improving sharpness and spectral power relative to the standard cVAE.

AI.tldr: A cVAE-based probabilistic post-processing framework for seasonal forecasts is enhanced to reduce blurriness and energy loss in forecasts by replacing the Gaussian decoder with a generator, using CRPS as the loss, and employing higher-resolution targets. This yields better calibration, higher fidelity to observations, improved sharpness, and higher spectral power, outperforming standard cVAE and raw forecasts.
AI.motivation: Seasonal ensemble forecasts suffer from drift, biases, and loss of fine-scale energy, leading to blurry forecasts and under-sampling of uncertainty. There's a need for calibrated, sharp, high-resolution probabilistic forecasts with improved skill.
AI.method: Extend the existing cVAE post-processing by (1) replacing the Gaussian parametric decoder with a generator, (2) optimizing with the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) instead of MSE, and (3) using higher-resolution target data while conditioning on biased model outputs to generate large ensembles of bias-adjusted forecasts.
AI.result: The adjusted forecasts are better calibrated and more consistent with observations, exhibit smaller errors, and deliver higher resolution, improved sharpness, and enhanced spectral power relative to the standard cVAE, improving performance over benchmark predictions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed modifications successfully address blurriness and energy loss in probabilistic post-processing, yielding sharper, more reliable, high-resolution seasonal forecasts with improved skill for Arctic sea ice.

title: When Does Persona Prompting Actually Help? A Retrieval and Metric Analysis of Expert Role Injection in LLMs
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures. Submitted for peer review
summary: Persona prompting is widely used to steer large language models, yet its practical value remains unclear. Prior work often evaluates persona prompting using aggregate scores, making it difficult to determine whether expert-role prompting consistently improves response quality or instead changes responses along different quality dimensions. We study this question through a controlled comparison of four prompting conditions across 1,140 open-ended questions spanning 38 expert roles and six domains: no role prompt, a generic domain-expert prompt, embedding-based role retrieval, and a hybrid retrieval method combining embedding search with LLM-based role selection. Aggregate results show only small overall differences between conditions. However, metric-level analysis reveals a consistent tradeoff that aggregate averages obscure: role prompting systematically increases expertise depth while reducing clarity. These effects are highly conditional rather than universal. Role prompting performs best on advisory questions and in domains such as medicine and psychology, where structured expert framing and risk communication are intrinsically valuable. In contrast, baseline prompting performs better on conceptual and explanatory questions in finance, legal, science, and technology domains, where concise plain-language explanation is more important. We further show that hybrid retrieval significantly improves over embedding-only role selection, although better role retrieval does not eliminate the broader expertise-depth versus clarity tradeoff. Overall, our findings suggest that persona prompting primarily reshapes response characteristics rather than broadly improving capability, and that multi-metric evaluation is necessary for understanding its effects.

AI.tldr: Persona prompting yields limited aggregate gains but shifts responses toward deeper expertise at the expense of clarity; benefits are domain- and question-type dependent, with hybrid retrieval outperforming embedding-only methods but not removing the trade-off; multi-metric evaluation is essential.
AI.motivation: To clarify whether expert-role prompts consistently improve LLM performance or merely alter response characteristics, addressing inconsistencies in prior work that rely on single aggregate metrics.
AI.method: Controlled study with 1,140 open-ended questions across 38 expert roles and six domains, comparing four prompting conditions: no role prompt, generic domain-expert prompt, embedding-based role retrieval, and a hybrid retrieval method combining embedding search with LLM-based role selection.
AI.result: Aggregate results show small overall differences; metric-level analysis reveals a robust trade-off: expert-role prompting increases depth of expertise but reduces clarity, with effects highly conditional. Best performance in advisory questions and in medicine/psychology; baseline prompts outperform in conceptual/explanatory questions in finance/legal/science/tech. Hybrid retrieval outperforms embedding-only role selection, but the depth-vs-clarity trade-off persists.
AI.conclusion: Persona prompting mainly reshapes response characteristics rather than broadly enhancing capability; domain- and question-type specificity governs effectiveness, and multi-metric evaluation is necessary to understand its impact.

title: ReactBench: A Cause-Driven Benchmark for Multimodal Hallucination via Systematic Evaluation
summary: While multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved rapid progress in vision-language understanding, they remain prone to multimodal hallucinations, producing responses that are inconsistent with the visual input. Existing benchmarks predominantly focus on detecting hallucination outcomes rather than evaluating the underlying causes of these failures. Moreover, many benchmarks rely on simplistic scenarios and limited evaluation formats that no longer challenge state-of-the-art models. To address these limitations, we introduce ReactBench, a cause-driven hallucination benchmark featuring multiple tasks and an exam-style evaluation format. By generating adversarial images and hallucination-inducing queries, ReactBench introduces four targeted tasks: Relational Erasure, Counterfactual Attribute, Alteration Tracing, and Dense Counting. These tasks systematically expose co-occurrence bias, language priors, cross-image comparative perception deficiencies, and fine-grained perceptual bottlenecks. Beyond standard accuracy-based evaluation, we leverage Chain-of-Thought reasoning to identify fine-grained sub-causes of hallucination within each task. Extensive evaluations reveal that current MLLMs remain notably vulnerable to cause-specific hallucination triggers, demonstrating the value of ReactBench as a systematic and interpretable testbed for diagnosing and improving multimodal model robustness. The project page is available at https://reactbench.github.io/.

AI.tldr: ReactBench: a cause-driven, multi-task benchmark that probes specific hallucination triggers in multimodal LLMs using adversarial images and exam-style evaluation, with Chain-of-Thought to diagnose sub-causes.
AI.motivation: Existing hallucination benchmarks focus on outcomes and simple scenarios, failing to diagnose underlying causes. There is a need for a diagnostic, interpretable testbed to reveal co-occurrence bias, language priors, and perceptual bottlenecks in MLLMs.
AI.method: Introduce four tasks (Relational Erasure, Counterfactual Attribute, Alteration Tracing, Dense Counting) with adversarial inputs; exam-style evaluation; chain-of-thought reasoning to identify fine-grained sub-causes of hallucination within each task.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows current MLLMs are vulnerable to cause-specific hallucination triggers; ReactBench provides a systematic, interpretable platform for diagnosing and improving multimodal robustness.
AI.conclusion: ReactBench offers a principled, cause-focused benchmark to diagnose multimodal hallucinations and guide model improvement; project page available.

title: TIMEGATE: Sustainable Time-Boxed Promotion Gates for Continual ML Adaptation Under Resource Constraints
summary: As machine learning(ML) systems evolve to continual adaptation, each re-training cycle uses compute, annotation, and energy. We introduce TIMEGATE, a policy layer managing adaptation by budgeting time, labeling, training, and evaluation. TIMEGATE emits a metric-availability signal M for partial vs. full-evaluation decisions. We validate: (i) labeling outperforms training by 2.3x on Adult tabular; (ii) it transfers to LLaMA-3.1-8B + QLoRA on SST-2 (accuracy 0.80 to 0.96; M =1 in 35/36 runs); (iii) M is informative, 28-cell sensitivity shows M drops to 0.81 at tight thresholds; (iv) 100-cycle simulation achieves 66% evaluation-compute savings with no silent mis-promotions; (v) 10%-slice evaluation on LLaMA uses 89% less wall-clock and energy on a single H200 (ratios agree to 0.2%).

AI.tldr: TIMEGATE introduces a policy layer for continual adaptation, using a metric-availability signal M to balance partial vs. full evaluation. Empirical results show labeling can outperform training in cost, with strong transferability and substantial compute/energy savings across tasks.
AI.motivation: Reduce the resource costs (compute, annotation, energy) of repetitive re-training cycles in continually adapting ML systems by optimizing when and how to evaluate and update models.
AI.method: Propose TIMEGATE, a policy layer that budgets time, labeling, training, and evaluation, and emits a metric-availability signal M to guide partial vs. full evaluation. Validation spans tabular and language-model tasks (Adult, SST-2 with LLaMA-3.1-8B + QLoRA), sensitivity analyses, and cycle-level simulations.
AI.result: Key findings: (i) labeling outperforms training by 2.3x on Adult; (ii) transfer to LLaMA-3.1-8B+QLoRA on SST-2 improves accuracy from 0.80 to 0.96 with M=1 in 35/36 runs; (iii) M is informative; 28-cell sensitivity shows M drops to 0.81 at tighter thresholds; (iv) 100-cycle simulation yields 66% evaluation-compute savings with no silent mis-promotions; (v) 10%-slice evaluation on LLaMA yields 89% less wall-clock and energy on a single H200; ratios align to 0.2%.
AI.conclusion: TIMEGATE demonstrates that a policy-layer approach with an informative M signal can markedly reduce evaluation and training costs while preserving or enhancing performance, and that labeling can be a cost-effective substitute for training in continual adaptation scenarios; however, dependency on threshold settings and the need for broader validation remain open questions.

title: ReasonLight: A Multimodal Foundation Model-Enhanced Reinforcement Learning Framework for Zero-Shot Traffic Signal Control
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in traffic signal control (TSC). However, its reliance on predefined states limits responsiveness to observable open-world events that are absent from training data. IoT-enabled intersections provide heterogeneous observations from roadside sensors and cameras, creating opportunities to improve RL adaptability to such events. To this end, we propose ReasonLight, a multimodal foundation model-enhanced RL framework for zero-shot TSC. ReasonLight integrates three sources of information: structured traffic measurements, multi-view camera observations, and candidate phase decisions from a pre-trained RL controller. Given an RL-proposed phase, ReasonLight extracts visual semantics from multi-view images and aligns them with compact sensor-derived scene descriptions. This alignment enables a semantic-guided refinement module to either preserve or adjust the proposed action according to traffic rules and event semantics. To ensure operational reliability, refined actions are constrained by the set of available phases. Any invalid decision is rejected, and the system falls back to the original RL action. We evaluate ReasonLight on two types of rare events not seen during RL training: emergency vehicle priority and temporary traffic regulation. Experimental results show that ReasonLight achieves zero-shot adaptation without retraining. It reduces emergency vehicle waiting time by up to 88.7% compared with the RL-only backbone while preserving comparable routine traffic performance.

AI.tldr: ReasonLight fuses structured sensor data, multi-view camera imagery, and pre-trained RL proposals via a semantic alignment module to refine actions for zero-shot traffic signal control, enabling adaptation to rare events without retraining.
AI.motivation: RL-based TSC faces distribution shifts and restricted state representations; IoT-enabled intersections offer rich, multimodal observations enabling better responsiveness to unseen events; zero-shot adaptation to rare events (e.g., emergency priority) is needed.
AI.method: Integrates three information sources: structured traffic measurements, multi-view camera observations, and candidate phase decisions from a pre-trained RL controller. Upon an RL-proposed phase, it extracts visual semantics from multi-view images and aligns them with compact sensor-derived scene descriptions. A semantic-guided refinement module then preserves or adjusts the proposed action according to traffic rules and event semantics, constrained to the available phase set; invalid decisions fallback to the original RL action.
AI.result: Zero-shot adaptation achieved for two rare events—emergency vehicle priority and temporary traffic regulations—without retraining. Emergency vehicle waiting time reduced by up to 88.7% compared with the RL-only backbone, while routine traffic performance remains comparable.
AI.conclusion: ReasonLight demonstrates effective multimodal foundation model–enhanced RL for robust zero-shot TSC, delivering significant improvements in rare-event responsiveness without sacrificing normal operation.

title: BitC-3DGS: High-Capacity 3D Gaussian Splatting Watermarking via Bit Compression
summary: High-capacity watermarking is necessary for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) assets to embed rich information (e.g., ownership, provenance, and authentication codes), enabling reliable identification and integrity verification in large-scale 3D asset pipelines. Existing bit-to-token watermarking methods based on a pre-trained text encoder are limited to 77-bit messages due to CLIP's fixed 77-token context length, as tokens beyond this limit are unsupported by learned positional embeddings. To address this limitation, we introduce BitC-3DGS, a bit-compression framework that encodes multiple message bits per token. It employs a bit-compressed tokenization scheme that encodes multiple bits within the same chunk into a single semantic token. To enable recovery of the compressed information, it further introduces a dual-branch architecture for joint chunk decompression and bit decoding, along with a hard-message sampling strategy to improve combinatorial coverage during decoder training. Extensive experiments on the Blender and LLFF datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of BitC-3DGS for high-capacity watermarking, achieving high message recovery accuracy and rendering fidelity. For example, it supports 128-bit message capacity with recovery accuracy comparable to that of 64-bit messages in recent state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: BitC-3DGS increases watermark capacity for 3D Gaussian Splatting by encoding multiple bits per semantic token. It uses a bit-compressed tokenization scheme and a dual-branch decoder for joint decompression and bit decoding, plus hard-message sampling during training. This enables 128-bit watermark recovery with fidelity comparable to 64-bit baselines, validated on Blender and LLFF datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing CLIP-based bit-to-token watermarking is limited to 77-bit messages due to CLIP's fixed token context length, hindering high-capacity watermarking for large 3D assets.
AI.method: Bit-compressed tokenization that packs multiple bits into a single semantic token chunk; a dual-branch architecture for joint chunk decompression and bit decoding; hard-message sampling to improve decoder training coverage and combinatorial generalization.
AI.result: Experiments on Blender and LLFF show high message recovery accuracy and rendering fidelity; 128-bit capacity achieves recovery performance comparable to 64-bit messages in prior state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: BitC-3DGS effectively extends watermark capacity for 3DGS assets without sacrificing fidelity, demonstrating scalable, high-capacity, and robust watermarking for large-scale 3D asset pipelines.

title: Influence-Guided Symbolic Regression: Scientific Discovery via LLM-Driven Equation Search with Granular Feedback
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising avenue for scientific discovery, yet their application to symbolic regression is often constrained by inefficient search strategies and coarse feedback signals. Current methods typically guide LLMs using scalar metrics (e.g., global Mean Squared Error), which fail to identify which components of a proposed equation are driving performance or causing error. We introduce \textit{Influence-Guided Symbolic Regression} (IGSR), a method that frames equation discovery as an iterative two-step process combining diverse term generation with rigorous selection: an LLM generates candidate basis functions $ψ_j(\mathbf{x})$ for a linear model, which are then evaluated using granular influence scores $Δ_j$. These scores quantify each term's marginal contribution to generalization accuracy, enabling an influence-guided pruning process that systematically refines the model structure. Integrating this mechanism into a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) enables navigating the combinatorial search space while balancing exploration of novel functional forms with exploitation of high-influence components. We demonstrate IGSR's effectiveness on a diverse suite of benchmarks, including LLM-SRBench, pharmacological PKPD models, an epidemiological simulation, and real-world genomic data. Notably, we validate the framework's capacity for genuine discovery in a case study using a high-dimensional biological dataset, in which IGSR identified a novel relationship between DNA methylation and RNA Polymerase II pausing; a hypothesis that was subsequently supported via wet-lab experimentation.

AI.tldr: IGSR uses LLM-generated candidate basis functions for symbolic regression, with influence scores Δj guiding refinement via MCTS; enables granular attribution and discovery across diverse domains, including a wet-lab-supported case study.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-guided SR relies on coarse scalar metrics, obscuring which terms drive performance. Granular, influence-based feedback coupled with efficient search can improve discovery and interpretability.
AI.method: A two-step loop: (1) LLM generates candidate basis functions ψj(x) for a linear model; (2) each term is evaluated by a granular influence score Δj that measures marginal contribution to generalization; pruning/refinement guided by these scores. These steps are embedded in Monte Carlo Tree Search to balance exploration and exploitation across the combinatorial space of terms.
AI.result: IGSR demonstrates strong performance on benchmarks including LLM-SRBench, PKPD models, an epidemiological simulation, and real genomic data; a high-dimensional biological dataset case study identifies a novel link between DNA methylation and RNA Polymerase II pausing, a finding supported by wet-lab experiments.
AI.conclusion: IGSR delivers granular, interpretable term-level guidance for symbolic regression, enabling genuine discovery across domains. The approach shows promise for scalable, domain-agnostic SR but will benefit from further validation on larger scales and analysis of scalability and reproducibility.

title: Towards Human-Like Interactive Speech Recognition With Agentic Correction and Semantic Evaluation
summary: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) is a core component of human--computer interaction and an increasingly important front-end for LLM-based assistants and agents. However, most current ASR systems still follow a single-pass paradigm, which is poorly aligned with human communication, where misunderstandings are resolved through iterative clarification and refinement. This mismatch makes it difficult to correct meaning-critical errors once they occur. Meanwhile, token-level metrics such as WER or CER cannot adequately reflect such a problem. To address these limitations, we formulate \emph{Interactive ASR} as a multi-turn refinement task and propose \textbf{Agentic ASR}, a closed-loop framework that combines a single-pass ASR front-end with semantic correction, intent routing, and reasoning-based editing. We further introduce the \textbf{Sentence-level Semantic Error Rate} ($S^2ER$), an LLM-based semantic evaluation metric, together with an \textbf{Interactive Simulation System} for scalable and reproducible benchmarking. Experiments on multilingual, named-entity-intensive, and code-switching benchmarks show that iterative interaction consistently reduces semantic errors, with much larger gains in $S^2ER$ than in conventional token-level metrics. Human--AI alignment and ablation studies further validate the reliability of the semantic judge and the robustness of the proposed framework. The code is available at: https://interactiveasr.github.io/ and the live demo is available at https://i-asr.sjtuxlance.com/

AI.tldr: A multi-turn interactive ASR framework (Agentic ASR) that combines a single-pass front-end with semantic correction and reasoning-based editing, plus a new Sentence-level Semantic Error Rate (S^2ER) metric and an Interactive Simulation System for scalable benchmarking; shows improved semantic accuracy over token-level metrics.
AI.motivation: Token-level metrics (WER/CER) fail to capture semantic errors and human-like iterative clarification is needed. Current single-pass ASR is misaligned with human communication; aims to enable semantic-level correction via interaction.
AI.method: Propose Agentic ASR: a closed-loop system integrating a single-pass ASR front-end with semantic correction, intent routing, and reasoning-based editing. Introduce S^2ER, an LLM-based semantic evaluation metric. Develop an Interactive Simulation System for scalable benchmarking. Conduct multilingual, named-entity-intensive, and code-switching experiments, with ablations and human-AI alignment analyses.
AI.result: Iterative interaction consistently reduces semantic errors; S^2ER gains are much larger than improvements in token-level metrics like WER/CER. Demonstrates robustness across benchmarks; code and live demo available.
AI.conclusion: Semantic-aware, interactive ASR significantly improves semantic correctness and alignment with human intent. S^2ER provides a scalable semantic evaluation, and the framework is robust across languages, entities, and code-switching.

title: Brain-IT-VQA: From Brain Signals to Answers
summary: Decoding visual content from fMRI signals recorded while a person views images, and specifically answering questions about the seen images, is a long-standing challenge. While significant progress has been made in recent years in visual question answering (VQA) from fMRI, performance remains limited. Moreover, although recent models can make increasingly accurate predictions, they have rarely been used as tools for understanding the structure of visual representations in the brain. We present Brain-IT-VQA, a framework for visual question answering from fMRI. Building on the Brain Interaction Transformer (Brain-IT), our method decodes language tokens from brain activity and integrates them with a language model to answer visual questions. Our model substantially outperforms previous fMRI-based captioning and VQA approaches. We further introduce NSD-VQA, a new dataset and benchmark for visual question answering from fMRI. Unlike existing image-fMRI VQA datasets, which typically provide only a few broad and weakly controlled questions per image, NSD-VQA provides on average 20 question-answer pairs per image across 20 controlled question categories that disentangle multiple levels of visual understanding. This enables more reliable and interpretable evaluation despite limited fMRI test data. Together, Brain-IT-VQA and NSD-VQA provide both a strong predictive framework and a tool for studying brain representations. Using this benchmark, we quantify which forms of visual and semantic information can be reliably decoded from fMRI responses to natural images. We further analyze the contributions of different brain regions across question types.

AI.tldr: Introduces Brain-IT-VQA to decode fMRI responses into language tokens and answer visual questions, and NSD-VQA as a dense, category-wide VQA benchmark to study brain representations, achieving strong predictive performance and interpretable analyses.
AI.motivation: To overcome the low performance and limited interpretability of existing fMRI-based VQA approaches, and to provide a robust, interpretable benchmark that disentangles multiple levels of visual understanding in neural representations.
AI.method: Brain-IT-VQA decodes language tokens from fMRI signals using Brain Interaction Transformer and integrates them with a language model to answer questions. NSD-VQA provides ~20 Q-A pairs per image across 20 controlled question categories to disentangle visual understanding. The study analyzes contributions of different brain regions across question types.
AI.result: The approach substantially outperforms prior fMRI-based captioning and VQA methods. NSD-VQA enables reliable evaluation with limited fMRI test data and allows quantification of decodable visual and semantic information, as well as regional contribution analyses by question type.
AI.conclusion: Brain-IT-VQA plus NSD-VQA offer a strong predictive tool and a means to study brain representations, enabling robust decoding of visual and semantic information from fMRI and insights into regional specialization across question categories.

title: When RL Suppresses Its Own Vocabulary: Recovering Reasoning Diversity in Puzzle-to-Math Transfer
summary: Reinforcement learning using verifiable rewards (RLVR) improves LLM reasoning, but the conditions under which it transfers across domains -- and why it does so -- remain under-explored. We study cross-domain transfer in a 7B model whose SFT and RL post-training stages use only constraint-satisfaction puzzles, with no mathematics problems in the post-training data. To analyze how transfer emerges, we introduce a reasoning primitive-level framework that combines a 9-class span classifier with motif extraction, allowing us to segment chain-of-thought traces into primitive motifs and track their evolution across training stages and domains. We find that puzzle SFT induces a reasoning-primitive vocabulary, yielding a $+7$pp \texttt{pass@32} gain on OlymMATH-Hard. Vanilla GSPO then composes these primitives into longer compute-verify chains, adding a further $+6$pp. However, this RL stage also suppresses exploratory primitives such as \textit{hypothesize} and \textit{backtrack}. To address this, we introduce a novelty bonus that rewards diverse correct rollouts, using perplexity under the reference model as a signal. This restores recovery primitives during RL and adds a further $+7$pp \texttt{pass@32} relative to vanilla GSPO. Finally, the end-to-end recipe raises the hard-math capability ceiling from $16.0\%$ at the OLMo3-7B-Instruct-SFT base to $36.0\%$, without adding any mathematics problems during the SFT or RL stages.

AI.tldr: Cross-domain transfer of RL-based reasoning is aided by a primitive-level analysis and novelty signals, yielding notable gains in hard-math tasks without adding math data during training.
AI.motivation: Investigate when and why RLVR transfers across domains, identifying mechanisms of primitive reasoning and the role of exploration/exploitation dynamics in cross-domain transfer.
AI.method: Introduce a reasoning-primitive framework: a 9-class span classifier with motif extraction to segment chain-of-thought into primitives; analyze transfer across constraint-satisfaction puzzles using a 7B model; augment with a novelty bonus based on reference-model perplexity to preserve exploration; evaluate end-to-end impact on hard-math capability.
AI.result: Puzzle SFT creates a dedicated reasoning-primitive vocabulary (+7pp pass@32 on OlymMATH-Hard). Vanilla GSPO adds +6pp by composing primitives into longer compute-verify chains. RL stage suppresses exploratory primitives (e.g., hypothesize, backtrack). A novelty bonus restores recovery primitives, adding +7pp over vanilla GSPO. End-to-end setup raises hard-math ceiling from 16.0% to 36.0% without including math problems in SFT or RL.
AI.conclusion: Primitive-level analysis elucidates transfer mechanisms in RLVR; addressing RL-induced loss of exploration via novelty bonuses yields substantial gains; an end-to-end constraint-satisfaction-to-math pipeline can markedly boost hard-math performance without expanding training data.

title: CrystalXRD-Bench: Benchmarking Vision-Language Models for XRD Peak Indexing Across Diverse Crystalline Materials
summary: Miller-index identification from powder XRD patterns requires capabilities untested by existing multimodal benchmarks: the model must read a narrow peak location from a rendered scientific curve and then connect that observation to multi-step crystallographic reasoning. We introduce CrystalXRD-Bench, a 250-sample benchmark built from 10 public crystallographic databases for a single task: recover the full set of HKLs contributing to the highest-intensity peak in an XRD pattern. Each sample pairs the rendered XRD image with the source CIF text and chemical formula, so visual extraction errors and reasoning errors can be examined side by side. We evaluate seven vision-language models. The best Jaccard score is 0.5888 (GPT-5.4) with an exact-match rate of 37.6%, yet six of seven models remain below Jaccard 0.50; the task is far from solved. Error patterns vary systematically: double-peak cases are especially brittle, recall-heavy models gain coverage by over-predicting HKLs, and access to CIF text does not close the gap in crystallographic calculation. Alongside model rankings, the benchmark identifies the conditions under which current VLMs fail on quantitative scientific figures. All data and evaluation code will be publicly available.

AI.tldr: CrystalXRD-Bench introduces a 250-sample benchmark to recover the full set of HKLs contributing to the highest-intensity XRD peak, testing vision-language models on bridging peak observation and crystallographic reasoning; best model achieves Jaccard 0.5888 and 37.6% exact-match, with most models underperforming.
AI.motivation: Identifying Miller indices from XRD peaks requires narrow-peak reading and multi-step crystallographic reasoning; current multimodal benchmarks do not adequately test this capability or quantitative crystallographic inference.
AI.method: Assemble 250 samples from 10 public crystallographic databases; render XRD images paired with source CIF text and chemical formula; evaluate seven vision-language models on recovering the HKLs for the highest-intensity peak; analyze error patterns and the impact of CIF text.
AI.result: Best Jaccard 0.5888 (GPT-5.4) with 37.6% exact-match; six of seven models have Jaccard < 0.50; double-peak cases are brittle; recall-heavy models over-predict HKLs; CIF text access does not close the gap in crystallographic calculation.
AI.conclusion: Shows that current vision-language models struggle with quantitative scientific figures in crystallography; identifies systematic error patterns and provides publicly available data/code to benchmark and improve future models.

title: Non-Forgetting Knowledge Allocation with Bi-level Competition for Class-Incremental Learning
summary: Class-Incremental Learning (CIL) with pre-trained models (PTMs) aims to sequentially adapt PTMs to new categories without forgetting old knowledge. Built upon PTMs, existing adapter-based methods mainly train models via distinct task-specific adapters, and present a uniform knowledge allocation for each adapter during inference. However, this allocation mechanism ignores the nature of task discrepancy and leads to suboptimal utilization of adapters. Also, under CIL constraint, an allocator is prone to forgetting when tasks evolve. To address these issues, we propose a Non-Forgetting Allocation with Bi-Level Competition (NoFA-BC). NoFA-BC constructs a non-forgetting allocator (NFA) by transforming the allocator training into a recursive least-squares problem and achieves an allocator equivalent to that trained with all data. Based on the NFA, a Bi-Level Competition (BLC) including an intra-task level Winner-Takes-All (WTA) mechanism and inter-task Last-Ones-Fall (LOF) elimination is proposed to provide better allocation of adapter knowledge. WTA extracts the most significant logit within a task to represent the adapter's contribution and LOF suppresses the irrelevant adapters.
