With BLC, participation ratio of each adapter can be tailored for each input. Moreover, a Stability Enhancement (SE) process is incorporated to further improve the performance of old tasks.

AI.tldr: NoFA-BC introduces a non-forgetting allocator and bi-level competition to allocate adapters for class-incremental learning with pre-trained models, enabling per-input adapter participation and improved stability.
AI.motivation: In CIL with PTMs, uniform adapter allocation across tasks fails to capture task discrepancy and leads to suboptimal adapter utilization and forgetting. A memory-efficient allocator that remains stable across evolving tasks is needed.
AI.method: Transform allocator training into a recursive least-squares problem to create a non-forgetting allocator (NFA) that mimics training with all data. Build a Bi-Level Competition (BLC) with intra-task Winner-Takes-All (WTA) to select the most significant logit within a task, and inter-task Last-Ones-Fall (LOF) to suppress irrelevant adapters. Allow per-input adapter participation ratios. Add a Stability Enhancement (SE) to boost old-task performance.
AI.result: The abstract claims improved allocation of adapter knowledge and per-input adaptation via BLC, along with enhanced stability for old tasks. No numerical results are provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: NoFA-BC offers a principled framework combining NFA, BLC, and SE to improve adapter utilization and mitigate forgetting in CIL with PTMs. Experimental validation would be required to quantify gains.

title: Stochastic Lifting for Generating Trajectories of Stochastic Physical Systems
summary: Many stochastic physical systems evolve smoothly over time in the sense that the distribution of states changes regularly across time steps. The transition from current state to the next state can often be modeled as the combination of a smooth map and an explicit source of randomness. Stochastic Lifting exploits this structure by attaching an independent, high-dimensional random label to each state transition in the training data and fitting a transition map from the current state and label to the next state using a standard regression loss. The labels act as auxiliary coordinates that let the model represent multiple plausible next states from similar current states, avoiding collapse to a mean prediction in the finite-sample size regime. At inference, fresh labels are sampled at each time step and the learned map is rolled forward autoregressively, generating diverse trajectories with a single network evaluation per time step.

AI.tldr: A stochastic lifting method uses random labels attached to state transitions to learn a multimodal transition mapping; at inference, sample labels and autoregressively roll out diverse trajectories with one network evaluation per step.
AI.motivation: Model multimodal next-state distributions and avoid mean collapse under finite data by introducing high-dimensional auxiliary randomness that can represent multiple plausible futures without increasing inference cost.
AI.method: Attach independent random labels to each transition in training data; train a regression model that maps (current state, label) to next state using a standard regression loss; at inference, sample new labels per time step and roll forward autoregressively, requiring one network evaluation per step.
AI.result: Enables generation of diverse trajectories from a single trained model, preserving diversity and avoiding collapse, with efficient, low-cost inference.
AI.conclusion: Stochastic Lifting provides a scalable framework for capturing stochastic, multimodal dynamics by augmenting states with latent randomness, enabling diverse, plausible futures with minimal inference overhead.

title: VitalAgent: A Tool-Augmented Agent for Reactive and Proactive Physiological Monitoring over Wearable Health Data
summary: Wearable devices enable continuous monitoring of physiological signals such as ECG and PPG, but existing mHealth systems are largely limited to task-specific prediction pipelines or reactive question answering over static summaries. They lack the ability to support temporal reasoning, persistent physiological context, and proactive monitoring over long-term signal streams. We propose VitalAgent, a tool-augmented agentic framework for ECG/PPG-based mHealth that supports both reactive question answering and proactive monitoring. VitalAgent is built on a longitudinal physiological memory and a tool-augmented reasoning interface that enables dynamic computation over raw signals. We further introduce VitalBench, a longitudinal physiological monitoring benchmark dataset comprising 1,862 QA pairs for reactive question answering and 90.2 hours of continuous ECG/PPG recordings for proactive monitoring, covering cardiac, physical activity, and stress-related tasks. Experiments demonstrate that VitalAgent achieves over 30% improvement over prompt-based and ReAct baselines in reactive evaluation and supports proactive alert monitoring over long-term physiological signals, highlighting the importance of dynamic tool use and long-term physiological monitoring.

AI.tldr: VitalAgent: a tool-augmented, memory-enabled framework for ECG/PPG-based mHealth enabling reactive QA and proactive long-term monitoring; plus VitalBench benchmark with QA pairs and long recordings; results show >30% improvement over baselines.
AI.motivation: Addresses lack of temporal reasoning, persistent physiological context, and proactive monitoring in current mHealth systems; need for long-term signal stream analysis.
AI.method: Develop VitalAgent with longitudinal physiological memory and tool-augmented reasoning to perform dynamic computation on raw signals; construct VitalBench with 1,862 QA pairs and 90.2 hours of ECG/PPG; evaluate against prompt-based and ReAct baselines.
AI.result: VitalAgent achieves over 30% improvement in reactive QA; enables proactive alert monitoring over long-term signals; demonstrates importance of tool use and long-term memory.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of unified reactive and proactive mHealth using memory and tools; VitalBench provides a benchmark for long-term physiological monitoring; suggests design principles for future mHealth AIs.

title: CogniVerse: Revolutionizing Multi-Modal Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Cognitive Reflection and Geometric Reasoning
summary: Multi-modal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (MMRAG) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for enhancing Multimodal Large Language Models in knowledge-intensive question answering by integrating external visual, textual, and structural knowledge. However, existing MMRAG frameworks suffer from critical limitations, including noisy and irrelevant retrieval, cross-modal semantic misalignment, lack of adaptive reasoning, and incoherent generation across local and global contexts. We introduce \textbf{CogniVerse}, a novel MMRAG framework that addresses these challenges through a cognitive-inspired, mathematically rigorous approach. Drawing from human-like reasoning, CogniVerse integrates three synergistic components: (1) a Cognitive Reflection Module that dynamically assesses retrieval necessity and filters relevant multi-modal content, reducing noise and computational overhead; (2) a Multi-modal Retrieval Module that aligns embeddings in a Riemannian manifold using information geometry and refines knowledge graphs via spectral graph theory, ensuring precise and coherent retrieval; and (3) a Hierarchical Generation Module that employs an optimal transport-based loss to balance token-level accuracy and global semantic coherence. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CogniVerse significantly outperforms state-of-the-art systems in both accuracy and coherence, while reducing retrieval latency.

AI.tldr: CogniVerse is a cognitive-inspired MMRAG framework with three synergistic modules—Cognitive Reflection Module, Multi-modal Retrieval Module with Riemannian-geometry alignment and spectral graph refinement, and Hierarchical Generation Module with an optimal-transport loss—achieving higher accuracy and coherence with lower retrieval latency.
AI.motivation: To address core limitations of existing MMRAG systems: noisy/irrelevant retrieval, cross-modal semantic misalignment, lack of adaptive reasoning, and incoherent generation across local/global contexts.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Cognitive Reflection Module dynamically assesses the necessity of retrieval and filters relevant multi-modal content to reduce noise/computation; (2) Multi-modal Retrieval Module aligns embeddings in a Riemannian manifold using information geometry and refines knowledge graphs via spectral graph theory to ensure precise, coherent retrieval; (3) Hierarchical Generation Module uses an optimal transport–based loss to balance token-level accuracy with global semantic coherence.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show CogniVerse significantly outperforms state-of-the-art systems in accuracy and coherence while reducing retrieval latency.
AI.conclusion: CogniVerse demonstrates that a cognitively-inspired, mathematically rigorous framework can elevate MMRAG performance by improving retrieval quality, alignment, and generation coherence, with potential applicability to broader multimodal reasoning tasks.

title: Auditing Training Data in Generative Music Models via Black-Box Membership Inference
comment: The paper has been accepted for presentation at the workshop ArtSec 2026: Workshop on Artwork Security and Provenance in the Age of AI
summary: Recent advances in text-to-music generation enable high-fidelity synthesis of structured musical audio, raising growing concerns about data provenance, consent, and training transparency. These models are typically trained on large-scale corpora with little disclosure, leaving no practical mechanism to verify whether a particular audio sample was included in training. In this paper, we investigate black-box membership inference for generative music models, aiming to determine whether a candidate music sample was used during training, given only query access to the deployed system. Our key insight is that training membership induces systematically stronger semantic and structural alignment between a candidate sample and the model's generation conditioned on its caption. We query the target model with the associated caption and measure the relationship between the candidate audio and the generated output in a learned feature space. To capture features that separate members from non-members, we construct paired examples consisting of each track and its caption-conditioned generation from shadow models, and train a music auditor to classify membership. The auditor captures alignment patterns characteristic of training membership and generalizes to unseen target models in a fully black-box setting without access to model parameters or training metadata. Across multiple state-of-the-art music generators, our method achieves up to 98.6% accuracy, with false-positive and false-negative rates as low as 1.9% and 1.0%, demonstrating that reliable training-data auditing is feasible in realistic deployment scenarios.

AI.tldr: A black-box membership inference method for text-to-music generators using a shadow-model auditor to detect whether a sample was in training, achieving high accuracy in a fully black-box setting.
AI.motivation: Rising concerns about data provenance, consent, and training transparency in text-to-music systems; need practical mechanisms to verify if a sample was used during training.
AI.method: Query a target model with the sample's caption, obtain generated output, and measure semantic/structural alignment between the candidate audio and caption-conditioned generation in a learned feature space. Build paired examples from tracks and their caption-conditioned generations using shadow models, train a music auditor to classify membership, enabling black-box generalization to unseen targets.
AI.result: The approach achieves up to 98.6% accuracy on multiple state-of-the-art music generators, with false-positive rates around 1.9% and false-negative rates around 1.0%, indicating reliable training-data auditing in realistic deployments.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasible, effective training-data auditing in a fully black-box setting for generative music models; the auditor captures alignment patterns indicative of training membership and generalizes across target models.

title: The Curse of Helpfulness: Inverse Scaling Law in Robustness to Distractor Instructions via DistractionIF
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in agentic and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, where they must execute user-specified tasks over externally provided reference text. In practice, such context is often unstructured and contaminated with benign but instruction-like semantic noise, such as editorial comments and system traces, which should be treated strictly as data. We introduce DistractionIF, a benchmark designed to evaluate robustness against such distractor instructions in reference text. Across a broad range of models, we observe a consistent inverse scaling phenomenon: larger models are often less robust, with performance dropping by up to 30 points as scale increases. Mechanistically, our perplexity analysis reveals that scaling erodes the probabilistic boundary between robust and distracted behaviors, making models increasingly prone to over-interpreting noise as instructions. To address this, we demonstrate that reinforcement learning, specifically Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), can restore this boundary, improving robustness by up to 15.5% without compromising general instruction-following capability. Our findings highlight a critical instruction-following robustness gap in reference-grounded tasks and establish reinforcement learning as a promising path for enforcing strict data-instruction separation at scale.

AI.tldr: DistractionIF benchmarks how distractor instructions in reference text affect LLM robustness in RAG setups; finds larger models are less robust (inverse scaling); GRPO RL can restore robustness without hurting instruction-following.
AI.motivation: LLMs in agentic/RAG contexts must follow user tasks while ignoring benign, instruction-like noise in external reference text; real-world data is unstructured and contaminated with non-instructional signals.
AI.method: Introduce DistractionIF benchmark to evaluate robustness to distractor instructions; test across a wide range of models; analyze perplexity to study the boundary between robust and distracted behavior; apply Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) reinforcement learning to enforce separation between data and instruction signals and measure robustness vs. general instruction-following.
AI.result: Observed consistent inverse scaling: larger models exhibit reduced robustness, with performance degradation up to 30 points as scale increases. Perplexity analysis indicates scaling blurs the probabilistic boundary between robust and distracted behaviors, making models over-interpret noise as instructions. GRPO training restores this boundary, improving robustness by up to 15.5% without compromising general instruction-following capability.
AI.conclusion: There exists a critical robustness gap in reference-grounded tasks; LLMs become more noise-prone with scale. Reinforcement learning, specifically GRPO, is a promising approach to enforce strict data-instruction separation at scale and enhance robustness in RAG systems.

title: Learning Context-Conditioned Predicate Semantics via Prototype Feedback
summary: In scene graph generation, a central challenge is modeling polysemous predicates whose meanings shift across contexts. Prior approaches address this issue by decomposing predicates into multiple static prototypes or retrieving semantically similar exemplars. However, these strategies keep predicate representations static and cannot reorganize semantics to reflect image-specific evidence, leading to systematic confusions in ambiguous contexts. We propose AlignG, which learns context-conditioned predicate semantics via prototype feedback. AlignG infers context-conditioned predicate semantics from the relation candidates within each image and feeds the adapted semantics back to recalibrate relation representations. The learning objective anchors this adaptation to global semantic centers, preventing semantic drift while still allowing selective reorganization when the scene provides consistent relational cues. Experiments on VG-150 and GQA-200 show consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines, with F@100 improvements of +1.4 on VG-150 and +2.7 on GQA-200 under SGDet. We further visualize per-image prototype similarity shifts and observe coherent context-dependent reorganization where prototypes selectively merge or separate predicates according to scene evidence. The code is available at https://github.com/Namgyu97/AlignG-SGG.pytorch.

AI.tldr: AlignG introduces context-conditioned predicate semantics for scene graph generation via prototype feedback, achieving state-of-the-art gains on VG-150 and GQA-200.
AI.motivation: Polysemous predicates have meanings that shift with context; static predicate representations (prototypes) and exemplar-based retrieval fail to adapt to image-specific evidence, leading to systematic confusions in ambiguous contexts.
AI.method: AlignG learns context-conditioned predicate semantics from relation candidates within each image, then feeds the adapted semantics back to recalibrate relation representations. The learning objective anchors this adaptation to global semantic centers to prevent semantic drift while allowing reorganization when scene cues are consistent.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on VG-150 and GQA-200 shows consistent improvements over strong baselines, with F@100 gains of +1.4 on VG-150 and +2.7 on GQA-200 under SGDet. Qualitative analysis visualizes per-image prototype similarity shifts, revealing coherent context-dependent reorganization where prototypes merge or separate predicates according to scene evidence. Code is available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: AlignG effectively models context-conditioned predicate semantics and dynamically reorganizes predicate representations per image, yielding improved SGDet performance and interpretable semantic adaptation.

title: Inferring the Size of Large Language Models From Popular Text Memorization
summary: The parameter counts of the most widely used large language models (LLMs) are often withheld by their developers, leaving model size -- a primary reference point for interpreting capabilities and costs -- largely undisclosed. We propose a black-box method to infer conservative lower bounds on LLM size from generated text outputs alone, requiring nothing beyond the ability to submit text fragments and observe next-token predictions. Our approach is grounded in a key observation: popular, widely-circulated texts -- such as classical literature, religious texts, and foundational documents -- are present in virtually every large-scale pretraining corpus, and how accurately a model predicts the next word across text fragments of varying length is a reliable signal of how much it has memorized them, which in turn is fundamentally limited by its total parameter count. We aggregate this memorization signal across a diverse corpus of texts and fragment lengths into a single accuracy profile vector per model, and build two complementary inference methods on top of it: a pairwise statistical test that determines which of two models is larger, and a scaling-law estimator that extracts a one-dimensional latent index from these vectors via Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to map the aggregated signal to a parameter count. Validated on a broad set of open-weight models, both methods produce accurate and reliable lower bounds. When applied to popular closed-weight models, our framework recovers internal product hierarchies and reveals a clear divergence in industry scaling strategies: while some developers yield significantly higher bounds indicative of large generational parameter growth, others operate under strict parameter ceilings, demonstrating that hidden design choices can be systematically probed even under strict API limitations.

AI.tldr: A black-box method to infer conservative lower bounds on LLM size from generated text; uses memorization of widely circulated texts to derive a scaling-law based size estimator and a pairwise test, validated on open-weight and closed-weight models.
AI.motivation: Parameter counts are often undisclosed, hindering interpretation of capabilities and costs. A non-invasive method to bound size with API-only access is valuable for benchmarking and policy.
AI.method: Construct an accuracy profile by measuring how well models predict next tokens for fragments of widely memorized texts across varying lengths. Build two tools: (1) a pairwise statistical test to decide which model is larger, and (2) a PCA-based scaling-law estimator that maps the aggregated memorization signal to a parameter-count latent index and then to a lower-bound estimate.
AI.result: Lower bounds are accurate and reliable across a broad set of open-weight models. For closed-weight models, the method recovers internal hierarchies and reveals divergent industry scaling strategies, indicating some developers use larger parameter growth while others impose ceilings.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables conservative, model-size estimation using only text generation outputs, enabling size comparisons and leakage of design choices under API limitations without direct access to model parameters.

title: Xetrieval: Mechanistically Explaining Dense Retrieval
summary: Explaining why dense retrievers assign high relevance scores remains challenging because retrieval decisions are made through opaque high-dimensional embeddings. Existing explanations often focus on surface signals, such as lexical matches, token alignments, or post-hoc textual rationales, and thus provide limited insight into the latent factors that shape dense retrieval behavior at the embedding level. We propose \textit{Xetrieval}, an embedding-level mechanistic framework for explaining dense retrieval. \textit{Xetrieval} first introduces a lightweight reasoning internalizer that approximates Chain-of-Thought reasoning directly in the embedding space with a single forward pass, enriching sentence embeddings with reasoning-oriented information while avoiding expensive autoregressive generation. It then decomposes these reasoning-enhanced embeddings into sparse, human-interpretable features, each associated with a coherent natural language description. By aggregating sparse feature overlaps across multiple document-side views, \textit{Xetrieval} provides feature-level explanations of individual retrieval decisions. Experiments on diverse retrievers and benchmarks show that \textit{Xetrieval} uncovers coherent interpretable features, yields stronger pair-level intervention effects, and supports task-level feature steering. The project page and source code are available at https://hihiczx.github.io/Xetrieval .

AI.tldr: Xetrieval is an embedding-space mechanistic framework that explains dense retrieval by internalizing reasoning into sentence embeddings and extracting sparse, human-interpretable features linked to language descriptions, enabling feature-level explanations and task steering.
AI.motivation: Dense retrievers operate on high-dimensional embeddings, and existing explanations focus on surface signals; there is a need to reveal latent embedding-level factors that drive retrieval decisions.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight reasoning internalizer that approximates Chain-of-Thought reasoning directly in the embedding space with a single forward pass, enriching sentence embeddings with reasoning-oriented information; decompose into sparse, human-interpretable features with natural-language descriptions; aggregate feature overlaps across document-side views to provide feature-level explanations of individual retrieval decisions.
AI.result: Experiments across diverse retrievers/benchmarks show coherent interpretable features, stronger pair-level intervention effects, and support for task-level feature steering; project page and source code available.
AI.conclusion: Xetrieval offers an embedding-level, mechanistic explanation framework for dense retrieval, enabling interpretation, intervention, and steering by exposing latent factors through sparse, interpretable features.

title: DiffSpot: Can VLMs Spot Fine-Grained Visual Differences in Web Interfaces?
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have made strong progress on high-level image-text alignment, yet their ability to perceive subtle visual differences remains limited. We study this problem in rendered web interfaces, where localized visual changes are both a diagnostic test of fine-grained perception and a practical requirement for GUI agents and design tools. We introduce \textbf{DiffSpot}, a code-driven benchmark for open-ended spot-the-difference on web interfaces. DiffSpot constructs controlled image pairs by mutating a single CSS property of a target element in self-contained HTML, re-rendering the page, and recording the changed property, element, and mutation magnitude. A grounding gate retains only pairs whose rendered pixel difference is confined to the target element. The benchmark contains 4{,}400 pairs, including 3{,}900 has-diff pairs balanced across 13 CSS-property operators and three difficulty tiers, plus 500 no-diff pairs for hallucination control. Evaluating 13 frontier VLMs zero-shot, we find that even the best model identifies only $40.7\%$ of true changes, with Hard-tier Recall below $23\%$ for every model. DiffSpot further shows that difficulty is strongly property-dependent: across CSS operators, neither pixel magnitude nor CLIP distance reliably predicts Recall.

AI.tldr: DiffSpot: a code-driven benchmark for open-ended spot-the-difference on web interfaces; current VLMs show limited fine-grained perception with low recall, highly dependent on CSS properties; simple metrics fail to predict recall.
AI.motivation: Evaluate fine-grained visual perception of vision-language models on GUI-like content; reveal limitations and guide development of GUI agents and design tools.
AI.method: Generate pairs by mutating a single CSS property of a target element in self-contained HTML, re-render, and record the mutation details. Apply a grounding gate to confine changes to the target element. Assemble 4,400 pairs (3,900 has-diff across 13 CSS-property operators and 3 difficulty tiers; 500 no-diff). Evaluate 13 frontier VLMs zero-shot.
AI.result: Best model recalls 40.7% of true changes; Hard-tier recall <23% for all models. Difficulty is strongly property-dependent; plain pixel magnitude or CLIP distance do not reliably predict recall across operators.
AI.conclusion: DiffSpot reveals substantial gaps in VLMs’ fine-grained perception on web interfaces and provides a controlled benchmark to drive improvements, highlighting the need for property-aware evaluation and higher-resolution perceptual capabilities.

title: Traditional machine learning vs. deep learning from dynamic graph representations of proteins' 3D folds in the task of protein structure classification
comment: Main paper: 16 pages, 4 figures, and 1 table; Supplementary information: 13 pages, 9 figures
summary: Protein structure classification (PSC) uses supervised learning to predict a protein's CATH/SCOP(e) class from the protein's sequence or 3D structural feature(s). We already modeled 3D structures as (static) protein structure networks (PSNs), demonstrating the competitiveness of PSN-based features to sequence or direct (i.e. non-network) 3D structural features in the PSC task. More recently, we demonstrated the power of features extracted from dynamic PSNs over features extracted from static PSNs (and thus by transitivity over sequence and direct 3D structural features) in the same task. That dynamic PSN approach used traditional machine learning (ML), combining manual (pre-engineered) features with an off-the-shelf classifier. Here, we evaluate whether automatic deep learning (DL) from the dynamic PSNs yields improvements. Our evaluation on 72 datasets spanning ~44,000 CATH- or SCOPe-labeled dynamic PSNs reveals that in terms of PSC accuracy, traditional ML and DL are (close to) tied for a large majority of the datasets, while DL is on average 10+ times slower. We are the first to evaluate traditional ML vs. DL in the dynamic PSN-based PSC task.

AI.tldr: DL from dynamic PSNs matches traditional ML in PSC accuracy across 72 datasets (~44k dynamic PSNs), but is ~10× slower on average.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether end-to-end deep learning on dynamic protein structure networks improves PSC performance over traditional ML with engineered features, extending prior work showing dynamic PSNs are predictive for PSC.
AI.method: Benchmark study across 72 CATH/SCOPe-labeled dynamic PSN datasets (~44k instances). Compare end-to-end DL models trained on dynamic PSNs to baseline traditional ML models using pre-engineered dynamic-PSN features and standard classifiers; assess PSC accuracy and computational cost.
AI.result: DL and traditional ML are (close to) tied in PSC accuracy on many datasets; DL shows no consistent accuracy advantage but is substantially slower (≈10×).
AI.conclusion: End-to-end DL on dynamic PSNs provides little to no accuracy gain for PSC at a high computational cost; traditional ML remains competitive and practical. This work provides the first direct DL vs ML comparison for dynamic PSN-based PSC.

title: MINDGAMES: A Live Arena for Evaluating Social and Strategic Reasoning in Multi-Agent LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as interactive agents, yet their capacity for social and strategic reasoning over extended interaction remains poorly understood. Existing evaluations rely on static vignettes or single-game benchmarks that cannot capture the sustained, multi-faceted reasoning that real-world multi-agent settings demand. We introduce Mindgames, a multi-game arena and evaluation platform for LLM agents that operationalizes complementary reasoning demands relevant to ``theory of mind'': belief attribution under hidden information, opponent modeling through repeated strategic interaction, cooperative inference under knowledge asymmetries, and sustained deception in social deduction. Built on TextArena, Mindgames provides a unified interaction interface, TrueSkill-based rating, and full trajectory logging across four game environments. We instantiate Mindgames through a 2025 competition cycle hosted at a major AI conference, which assessed 944 submitted agents from 76 teams across four games: Colonel Blotto, Iterated Prisoner's Dilemma, Codenames, and Secret Mafia. Our analysis surfaces both agent-level and evaluation-level limitations: brittle rule adherence remains a major bottleneck, top-performing systems repeatedly rely on explicit structural scaffolding, and leaderboard validity differs sharply across environments. In particular, failure-heavy environments can reward robustness to opponent errors as much as strategic ability, with Secret Mafia exhibiting a pronounced error-survival confound in this cycle. We release a dataset of 29,571 multi-agent games with turn-level observations, actions, and rewards, together with MG-Ref, a deterministic offline tournament protocol that scores new agents against a frozen reference pool of top-ranked, low-error Stage~II submissions under the same error-attribution lens used in this analysis.

AI.tldr: Mindgames is a unified, multi-game arena to evaluate LLM agents on social/strategic reasoning across four games, releasing a large multi-agent dataset and a deterministic offline tournament protocol (MG-Ref). Findings show brittleness to rules, reliance on scaffolding, and uneven leaderboard validity across environments; Secret Mafia reveals error-survival confounds.
AI.motivation: To move beyond static prompts and single-game benchmarks, capturing extended theory-of-mind-like reasoning in multi-agent, interactive settings and enabling robust, comparable evaluation of LLM agents.
AI.method: Build Mindgames on TextArena with four games (Colonel Blotto, Iterated Prisoner's Dilemma, Codenames, Secret Mafia), TrueSkill-based ratings, full trajectory logging, and an offline MG-Ref protocol. Hosted a 2025 competition with 944 agents from 76 teams.
AI.result: Identified limitations: brittle rule adherence; top systems rely on explicit scaffolding; leaderboard validity varies by environment. Robustness to opponent errors can dominate in failure-prone settings; Secret Mafia shows notable error-survival confound. Produced a dataset of 29,571 multi-agent games and the MG-Ref protocol for offline benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: Mindgames provides a scalable, standardized evaluation platform for multi-agent social reasoning in LLMs and exposes current weaknesses in models' strategic and cooperative capabilities, while supplying datasets and protocols to drive future improvements and fair benchmarking.

title: AgentCVR: Active Multi-Agent Cross-Video Reasoning via Script-Simulated Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cross-Video Reasoning (CVR) has emerged as a critical frontier in multimodal intelligence, requiring models to retrieve, align, and aggregate evidence distributed across multiple videos. Current Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) often struggle with CVR, as simple single-pass strategies encode multiple videos into a shared compressed context, potentially obscuring rare but critical evidence. In this paper, we propose AgentCVR, a multi-agent framework that treats CVR as an active evidence-acquisition task. AgentCVR employs a Master Agent to iteratively coordinate specialized Visual and Audio Agents for targeted evidence extraction. To ensure efficient training, we introduce Script-Simulated RL, which optimizes the agent's policy with LLM-generated semantic scripts and a lightweight text-based simulator, bypassing costly multimodal inference during online exploration. Experimental results on a comprehensive CVR benchmark show that AgentCVR outperforms single-pass baselines and achieves comparable performance to state-of-the-art closed-source systems, particularly in complex cross-video alignment and localization. To ensure reproducibility, our code is available at https://github.com/wang-jh24/AgentCVR.

AI.tldr: AgentCVR introduces a multi-agent framework for Cross-Video Reasoning (CVR) where a Master Agent coordinates specialized Visual and Audio Agents for targeted evidence extraction, trained via Script-Simulated RL using LLM-generated semantic scripts and a lightweight text-based simulator to avoid expensive online multimodal inference. It achieves strong CVR performance and reproducible code on a benchmark.
AI.motivation: CVR requires retrieving, aligning, and aggregating evidence across multiple videos. Single-pass encoding into a shared context risks obscuring rare but critical evidence, motivating active, modular evidence acquisition.
AI.method: A Master Agent orchestrates specialized Visual and Audio Agents to perform targeted evidence extraction. Training uses Script-Simulated RL: LLM-generated semantic scripts guide policy learning with a lightweight text-based simulator, bypassing costly multimodal inference during exploration.
AI.result: AgentCVR outperforms single-pass baselines and rivals state-of-the-art closed-source systems on CVR benchmarks, excelling in complex cross-video alignment and localization.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the effectiveness of a multi-agent evidence-acquisition approach for CVR, with good reproducibility supported by publicly available code at the provided GitHub URL.

title: BlockBatch: Multi-Scale Consensus Decoding for Efficient Diffusion Language Model Inference
summary: Diffusion language models (dLLMs) generate text by iteratively denoising multiple token positions in parallel, offering an attractive alternative to strictly autoregressive decoding. In practice, however, block-wise dLLM inference exposes a difficult granularity trade-off: small blocks preserve local conditioning but require many denoising steps, whereas large blocks expose more parallelism but can make premature commitments and accumulate cache error. Existing acceleration methods typically choose a single block size per request, leaving the complementarity among block sizes unused. We show that block size itself is a useful branching dimension. Different block sizes induce related but non-identical KV-cache trajectories: branches often share an initial prefix, bifurcate at semantically decisive positions, and later agree on syntactically lightweight tokens. Motivated by this structure, we propose BlockBatch, a training-free online inference framework that executes multiple block-size branches for the same request inside a batched forward pass. BlockBatch coordinates these branches through confidence-gated token merging, leader-based synchronization, and periodic full-sequence refreshes that re-anchor local block updates to a globally consistent KV state. Across 3 representative dLLMs and 4 datasets, BlockBatch reduces denoising NFEs by 26.6\% on average and achieves a 1.33$\times$ average end-to-end speedup over Fast-dLLM while preserving accuracy. These results identify block-size diversity as a practical and previously underexplored axis for branch-parallel dLLM inference.

AI.tldr: BlockBatch enables training-free online inference by running multiple block-size branches in parallel during diffusion LLM inference, exploiting block-size diversity to improve efficiency while maintaining accuracy; it achieves ~26.6% denoising NFEs reduction and ~1.33x end-to-end speedup on 3 dLLMs across 4 datasets.
AI.motivation: Block-wise diffusion LLMs face a granularity trade-off: small blocks offer local conditioning but many denoising steps; large blocks offer more parallelism but risk premature decisions and cache error. Existing methods pick a single block size, squandering the potential of diverse blocks as independent branching trajectories.
AI.method: BlockBatch performs multiple block-size branches for the same request within a single batched forward pass, using confidence-gated token merging across branches, leader-based synchronization to align updates, and periodic full-sequence refreshes to re-anchor local updates to a globally consistent KV cache. The approach is training-free.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 3 representative dLLMs and 4 datasets shows a 26.6% average reduction in denoising NFEs and a 1.33x average end-to-end speedup over Fast-dLLM, with accuracy preserved.
AI.conclusion: Block-size diversity is a practical and underexplored axis for branch-parallel dLLM inference; leveraging multiple block-size branches within a single inference can yield substantial efficiency gains without degrading quality.

title: DeepSurvey: Enhancing Analytical Depth and Citation Reliability in Automated Survey Generation
summary: As scientific literature grows rapidly, automated survey generation has become a key capability for AI scientists and human researchers. However, existing systems suffer from limited analytical depth due to reliance on abstracts and isolated paper processing, and unreliable citations from imprecise retrieval and post-hoc grounding, producing superficial surveys and may mislead researchers. We present DeepSurvey, an agentic system that addresses both. To enhance depth, DeepSurvey extracts structured keynotes from full-text papers, models cross-paper relationships through clustering and comparative analysis, and integrates code-repository analysis to recover implementation-level details. To fortify reliability, it combines citation-graph expansion with hybrid filtering for topic-focussed retrieval, enforces evidence-constrained citation assignment, and deploys multi-granularity agentic refinement to validate citation-claim alignment. Experiments show that DeepSurvey achieves the highest content score (8.644/10) and citation quality (12.3% and 9.3% recall and precision gains over the strongest baseline), generalizes more robustly across domains (0.14 vs 0.22 to 0.69 CS-to-non-CS drop), and is preferred over human-written surveys by domain experts (83.3% overall quality, 100% content depth).

AI.tldr: DeepSurvey is an agentic system that produces deeper, more reliable survey content by extracting structured keynotes from full texts, cross-paper analysis, and code-repo grounding, plus robust citation retrieval with evidence constraints and refinement.
AI.motivation: As literature grows, surveys must be more thorough and trustworthy; abstracts and isolated processing yield shallow, unreliable surveys; the work aims to improve depth and citation reliability.
AI.method: Full-text keynotes extraction; cross-paper clustering and comparative analysis; code-repo analysis for implementation details; citation-graph expansion; hybrid topic-focused retrieval; evidence-constrained citation assignment; multi-granularity agentic refinement for citation-claim alignment.
AI.result: Highest content score (8.644/10); citation quality gains (12.3% recall, 9.3% precision) over strongest baseline; better generalization across domains (CS-to-non-CS drop 0.14–0.22 to 0.69); domain experts prefer it (83.3% overall quality, 100% content depth).
AI.conclusion: DeepSurvey demonstrates superior content depth and reliability, with stronger cross-domain generalization and favorable expert reception.

title: MARTIAN: A Rendering Framework for Aerial Mars Imagery from HiRISE Orbital Data
summary: Aerial navigation on Mars requires vision-based pipelines that are robust to the diverse illumination conditions and terrain morphology of the Martian surface. A key bottleneck for training and evaluating such methods is the scarcity of large-scale, annotated aerial datasets. We present MARTIAN, an open-source Blender-based rendering framework that leverages real HiRISE orbital map products to synthesize realistic aerial views of the Martian terrain under controllable lighting conditions and at varying altitudes. MARTIAN generates observations with accurate pose annotations, directly addressing the scarcity of training data for vision-based navigation on Mars. The framework has been validated through its deployment in concurrent work on map-based localization systems for Ingenuity and future Mars rotorcraft, where synthetically trained deep image matchers were successfully evaluated on real Mars imagery. MARTIAN is publicly available at: https://github.com/nasa-jpl/martian.

AI.tldr: MARTIAN is a Blender-based rendering framework that synthesizes realistic Martian aerial views from real HiRISE maps with pose annotations to address data scarcity for vision-based Mars navigation; open-source and validated via downstream localization work with deep image matchers on real Mars imagery.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of large-scale, annotated aerial datasets for Martian vision navigation under diverse illumination and terrain; need for realistic, controllable synthetic data to train and evaluate vision systems for Mars rotorcraft.
AI.method: MARTIAN uses real HiRISE orbital map products within Blender to render aerial views of Martian terrain at varying altitudes and lighting, generating observations with accurate pose annotations and providing an open-source framework.
AI.result: The framework has been deployed in concurrent work on map-based localization for Ingenuity and future Mars rotorcraft, where synthetically trained deep image matchers were evaluated on real Mars imagery; MARTIAN is publicly available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: MARTIAN offers a practical solution to data scarcity for Mars vision tasks by enabling realistic, annotated synthetic data generation with controlled conditions, fostering training and evaluation of navigation systems; the code is open-source for reproducibility.

title: SigmaMedStat: Temporal Signal Modeling for ICU False Alarm Reduction
summary: Alarm fatigue in intensive care units (ICUs) is a well documented patient safety crisis. Clinical monitors generate 350 or more alarms per patient per day, out of which 72-99% are clinically irrelevant. Staff desensitization to non-actionable alarms increases the risk of missed true emergencies. This paper presents SigmaMedStat, a machine learning system that evaluates the trustworthiness of physiological alarm signals before clinical action is taken. Four approaches were evaluated on the PhysioNet/Computing in Cardiology Challenge 2015 dataset of 498 four-channel ICU alarm recordings. Primary contribution is a temporal modeling framework that splits each 60 second recording into six consecutive 10-second chunks, and this in turn generates Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) scalograms per chunk, encodes each chunk with a shared EfficientNet-B0 encoder, and passes the resulting feature sequence to a two-layer Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. Five-fold stratified cross-validation yields a mean AUC of 0.822 +/- 0.016 (95% CI: [0.790,0.853]), compared to 0.641 for a static EfficientNet baseline trained on the full 60-second window. Ablation studies confirm that temporal chunking and multi-channel signal fusion both contribute independently to classification performance. Per-alarm type analysis reveals that Ventricular Flutter is the most accurately classified alarm type (AUC 0.820) while Asystole remains the hardest (AUC 0.722). Error analysis identifies 65 false negatives and 85 high-confidence misclassifications as the primary failure modes. All code and results are publicly available at https://github.com/Arun-K-Ram/sigmamedstat.

AI.tldr: SigmaMedStat is a temporal ML framework that classifies ICU alarm signals for trustworthiness. It slices 60s recordings into six 10s chunks, computes CWT scalograms per chunk, encodes with EfficientNet-B0, and models the sequence with a two-layer LSTM. It achieves mean AUC 0.822 on PhysioNet/CinC 2015 data, outperforming a static 60s baseline (AUC 0.641). Ablation confirms benefits from chunking and multi-channel fusion. Ventricular Flutter is best among alarms (AUC 0.820), Asystole worst (0.722). 65 false negatives and 85 high-confidence misclassifications identified. Code/results: GitHub.
AI.motivation: Address alarm fatigue and high false-alarm rate in ICUs by predicting the trustworthiness or actionability of physiological alarms, enabling clinicians to focus on true emergencies and reduce missed events.
AI.method: Temporal modeling framework: 60-second recordings split into six consecutive 10-second chunks; compute Continuous Wavelet Transform scalograms for each chunk; encode each chunk with a shared EfficientNet-B0; feed the resulting feature sequence into a two-layer LSTM; multi-channel signal fusion; use five-fold stratified cross-validation on PhysioNet/CinC 2015 dataset (498 four-channel ICU alarm recordings).
AI.result: Mean AUC 0.822 ± 0.016 (95% CI: 0.790–0.853) across folds, vs 0.641 for a static 60-second EfficientNet baseline; ablation shows temporal chunking and multi-channel fusion independently boost performance; per-alarm type: Ventricular Flutter AUC 0.820; Asystole AUC 0.722; 65 false negatives and 85 high-confidence misclassifications.
AI.conclusion: Temporal chunking and multi-channel fusion substantially improve alarm signal trustworthiness classification on ICU data. The approach is validated on a standard dataset, with publicly available code and results.

title: UI-KOBE: Knowledge-Oriented Behavior Exploration for Lightweight Graph-Guided GUI Agents
summary: Recent advances in mobile GUI agents have shown strong potential for automating mobile tasks, but most effective systems still depend on large vision-language models for screenshot understanding and long-horizon planning. Small GUI agents that can be deployed directly on mobile devices are more attractive for practical use, offering lower inference cost and better protection of sensitive on-device information. However, due to limited model capacity, such lightweight agents remain unreliable when planning and executing GUI tasks end-to-end from screenshots alone. We propose Knowledge-Oriented Behavior Exploration (\textbf{UI-KOBE}), a framework that improves lightweight mobile GUI agents with reusable app-specific graph knowledge. UI-KOBE first autonomously explores a mobile application and constructs an app knowledge graph, where nodes represent distinct UI states and edges represent executable transitions. At runtime, a lightweight GUI agent uses the graph as external guidance: given a user task and the current screenshot, it identifies the current graph node and selects among self-loop actions, neighboring transitions, task completion, or fallback free actions associated with that node. By supporting runtime decisions with app-specific graph guidance, UI-KOBE reduces the burden of end-to-end GUI planning and helps lightweight models perform mobile GUI tasks more effectively, offering a practical step toward efficient, interpretable, and privacy-conscious on-device GUI agents.

AI.tldr: UI-KOBE uses an app-specific knowledge graph to guide lightweight on-device GUI agents, reducing end-to-end planning and improving reliability and privacy.
AI.motivation: Small mobile GUI agents are limited by model capacity and cannot reliably plan end-to-end from screenshots alone; on-device, privacy-preserving approaches with interpretable guidance are needed.
AI.method: Autonomously explore the app to build a knowledge graph where nodes are distinct UI states and edges are executable transitions. At runtime, the agent maps the current screenshot to a graph node and selects actions from self-loop options, neighboring transitions, task completion, or fallback actions guided by the graph.
AI.result: Expected improvements in reliability, efficiency, and interpretability of on-device GUI task execution, with reduced dependence on large vision-language models.
AI.conclusion: Graph-guided, app-specific knowledge enables practical, efficient, and privacy-conscious lightweight mobile GUI agents.

title: SuperVoxelGPT: Adaptive and Ordered 3D Tokenization for Autoregressive Shape Generation
summary:
Autoregressive multimodal large language models (MLLMs) enable 3D generation but struggle to scale to high-resolution shapes due to
  inadequate 3D tokenizations. Compact set-based representations discard deterministic spatial ordering, leading to ambiguous sequence
  prediction, while uniform or octree-based voxel grids preserve ordering at the cost of severe redundancy and excessively long
  sequences. This structural trade-off limits stable and efficient autoregressive 3D generation.
  We present SuperVoxelGPT, a representation-first framework that resolves this tension through adaptive and deterministically ordered
  supervoxel tokenization. Given a prompt, we first predict a coarse geometric saliency distribution and construct a shape-adaptive
  supervoxel partition using saliency-guided centroidal Voronoi tessellation, allocating fine-grained cells to complex regions and
  larger cells to smooth regions. Conditioned on the text and ordered supervoxel layout, we introduce a SuperVoxelVAE and fine-tune a
  pretrained MLLM to autoregressively generate supervoxel tokens.
  Experiments on Trellis-500K show that SuperVoxelGPT reduces token sequence length to 12.8% of uniform voxel tokenization while
  achieving state-of-the-art generation quality and an average 10$\times$ speedup over prior methods.

AI.tldr: Adaptive supervoxel tokenization for autoregressive 3D generation (SuperVoxelGPT): shape-adaptive, ordered tokens via saliency-guided Voronoi tessellation; a SuperVoxelVAE plus an MLLM fine-tune enable 12.8% token sequence length and ~10x speedup with competitive quality on Trellis-500K.
AI.motivation: Addresses the tokenization bottleneck in autoregressive 3D generation by reconciling the need for deterministic token ordering with high-resolution detail. Existing compact representations lose ordering or cause long sequences with voxel grids; a representation-first approach aims to enable efficient, stable autoregressive generation at high fidelity.
AI.method: Predict coarse geometric saliency; construct a shape-adaptive supervoxel partition using saliency-guided centroidal Voronoi tessellation (allocating finer cells to complex regions, larger cells to smooth regions). Condition on text and ordered layout; train a SuperVoxelVAE and fine-tune a pretrained multimodal LLM to autoregressively generate supervoxel tokens.
AI.result: On Trellis-500K, reduces token sequence length to 12.8% of uniform voxel tokenization; achieves state-of-the-art generation quality and ~10× speedup over prior methods.
AI.conclusion: A representation-first framework effectively resolves the trade-off between ordering and redundancy in 3D tokenizations, enabling efficient high-resolution autoregressive 3D generation with improved quality.

title: KLAS: Using Similarity to Stitch Neural Networks for Improved Accuracy-Efficiency Tradeoffs
summary: Given the wide range of deployment targets, flexible model selection is essential for optimizing performance within a given compute budget. Recent work demonstrates that stitching pretrained models within a model family enables cost-effective interpolation of the accuracy-efficiency tradeoff space. Stitching transforms intermediate activations from one pretrained model into another, producing a new interpolated stitched network. Such networks provide a pool of deployment options along the accuracy-efficiency spectrum. However, existing stitching approaches often yield suboptimal tradeoffs and lack generalizability, as they primarily rely on heuristics to select stitch configurations. We argue that constructing improved accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs requires explicitly capturing and leveraging the similarity between pretrained models being stitched. To this end, we introduce KLAS, a novel stitch selection framework that automates and generalizes stitch selection across model families by leveraging KL divergence between intermediate representations. KLAS identifies the most promising binary stitches from the $O(k^2n^2)$ possibilities for $k$ pretrained models of depth $n$. Through comprehensive experiments, we demonstrate that KLAS improves the accuracy-efficiency curve of stitched models at the same finetuning cost as baselines. KLAS achieves up to $1.21\%$ higher ImageNet-1K top-1 accuracy at the same computational cost, or maintains accuracy with a $1.33\times$ reduction in FLOPs.

AI.tldr: KLAS automates stitch selection across pretrained model families by using KL divergence of intermediate representations to identify promising binary stitches, yielding better accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs at equal fine-tuning cost or with reduced FLOPs.
AI.motivation: Deployment under tight compute budgets requires flexible, high-quality accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs. Existing stitching methods rely on heuristics and lack generalizability across model families.
AI.method: Compute KL divergence between intermediate activations to measure model similarity; search the stitch space O(k^2 n^2) for k pretrained models of depth n and select the most promising binary stitches; integrate stitched networks without extra fine-tuning cost; evaluate across ImageNet-1K and compare against baselines.
AI.result: Achieves up to 1.21% absolute gain in ImageNet-1K top-1 accuracy at the same computational cost, or enables a 1.33× reduction in FLOPs with maintained accuracy; improves the accuracy-efficiency curve for stitched models and generalizes across model families.
AI.conclusion: KLAS provides a principled, automatic, and generalizable stitch-selection framework that improves the accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs of stitched networks without increasing fine-tuning costs.

title: Opt-Verifier: Unleashing the Power of LLMs for Optimization Modeling via Dual-Side Verification
summary: Building mathematical optimization models is critical in operations research (OR), while it requires substantial human expertise. Recent advancements have utilized large language models (LLMs) to automate this modeling process. However, existing works often struggle to verify the correctness of the generated optimization models, without checking the rationality of the constraints and variables or the validity of solutions to the generated models. This hampers the subsequent verification and correction steps, and thus it severely hurts the modeling accuracy. To address this challenge, we propose a novel LLM-based framework with Dual-side Verification (Opt-Verifier) from both structure and solution perspectives, thereby improving the modeling accuracy. The structure-side verification ensures that the modeling structure of the generated optimization models aligns with the original problem description, accurately capturing the problem's constraints and requirements. Meanwhile, the solution-side verification interprets and evaluates the solutions' validity, confirming that the optimization models are logically and mathematically sound. Experiments on popular benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves over 20\% improvement in accuracy.

AI.tldr: A dual-side verification framework (Opt-Verifier) for LLM-generated optimization models that checks structure and solutions, achieving >20% accuracy gains on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLM-based optimization modeling often lacks verification of constraints/variables and solution validity, which undermines modeling accuracy and hinders subsequent verification and correction steps.
AI.method: Propose Opt-Verifier with Dual-side Verification from structure and solution perspectives. Structure-side verification ensures the modeling structure aligns with the problem description and captures constraints; solution-side verification interprets and evaluates the solutions for logical and mathematical validity.
AI.result: Experiments on popular benchmarks show over 20% improvement in accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The dual-side verification framework improves modeling accuracy by validating both structure and solutions, enabling more reliable and verifiable optimization models.

title: OccamToken: Efficient VLM Inference with Training-Free and Budget-Adaptive Token Pruning
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) rely on long visual token sequences for visual understanding, making the prefill stage expensive in both computation and memory. Most existing pruning methods follow an absolute-ranking paradigm, assigning importance scores to visual tokens and retaining a fixed top-K subset. In this work, we argue that this paradigm is fundamentally brittle: attention sinks distort token importance rankings, while image redundancy and query-dependent visual evidence make fixed token budgets unreliable across inputs. We propose OccamToken, a training-free framework that replaces absolute token ranking with register-anchored relative evidence testing. Instead of asking which tokens are globally important, OccamToken evaluates whether a visual token provides information beyond a register-based reference. Our key insight is that register tokens naturally absorb low-information attention patterns, making them a stable reference for identifying genuinely informative visual evidence. Based on this principle, OccamToken performs both image-adaptive redundancy pruning and query-adaptive relevance pruning through dynamic thresholds derived from register attention. Across LLaVA-NeXT, LLaVA-v1.5, and Qwen3-VL, OccamToken consistently improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off without additional training. Notably, on LLaVA-NeXT, it reduces 2,880 visual tokens to approximately 40 while preserving over 93% of the original accuracy, enabling stable visual token compression even in the extreme 1.4% retention regime.

AI.tldr: Training-free OccamToken replaces absolute top-K token ranking with register-anchored relative evidence testing to prune visual tokens in vision-language models, achieving substantial compression with minimal accuracy loss across multiple models.
AI.motivation: Absolute-ranking pruning is brittle due to attention sinks and input-dependent redundancy; a stable, adaptive pruning criterion that respects query and image evidence is desired without retraining.
AI.method: Introduce register tokens as a reference; evaluate whether each visual token provides information beyond register-based evidence; use dynamic thresholds derived from register attention to perform image-adaptive redundancy pruning and query-adaptive relevance pruning; no training required.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in accuracy-efficiency across LLaVA-NeXT, LLaVA-v1.5, and Qwen3-VL; on LLaVA-NeXT, token count drops from 2,880 to ~40 with >93% of original accuracy retained, enabling stable compression even at 1.4% retention.
AI.conclusion: OccamToken offers a robust, training-free token pruning paradigm that mitigates brittleness of absolute ranking and yields strong accuracy-efficiency gains across diverse VLMs.

title: Robust Frequency-Calibrated Virtual EEG Channel Generation from Four Frontal Electrodes for Wearable EEG Augmentation
summary: Low-channel wearable electroencephalography (EEG) is attractive for long-term monitoring, but four frontal electrodes provide only a sparse and spatially biased view of distributed scalp activity. We present FAVC-Net, a compact frequency-calibrated virtual-channel network that generates 13 unmeasured EEG channels from Fp1, Fp2, F7, and F8. The model combines shared multi-scale source encoding, source-state embeddings, target-conditioned signed source-block mixing, GATv2-based attention refinement, attention-consistent skip fusion, and weak Welch power spectral density calibration. Rather than treating sparse-to-dense EEG generation as a purely waveform-matching task, the framework jointly emphasizes amplitude fidelity, spectral allocation, channel-frequency texture, and robustness to corrupted wearable inputs. On the PRED+CT dataset, FAVC-Net achieved the best joint waveform-spectral operating point among neural and interpolation baselines. Its time-domain gains were modest, whereas log-spectral distance and PSD KL divergence were reduced by 30.09% and 37.98% relative to the strongest non-FAVC comparator. Under wearable-like source perturbations, the model preserved spectral fidelity and resisted spectral collapse. These results support virtual EEG channel generation as a dual-domain augmentation problem, while emphasizing that generated posterior and parietal channels should be interpreted as frequency-calibrated representations derived from sparse frontal measurements rather than as independent physical recordings.

AI.tldr: FAVC-Net is a compact, frequency-calibrated network that synthesizes 13 EEG channels from 4 frontal electrodes, achieving superior spectral fidelity and robustness on wearable EEG data compared to baselines, with modest time-domain gains.
AI.motivation: To enable long-term, low-channel EEG monitoring by densifying sparse frontal measurements while preserving amplitude and spectral integrity, addressing spatial bias and vulnerability to input corruption.
AI.method: A multi-stage architecture incorporating shared multi-scale source encoding, source-state embeddings, target-conditioned signed source-block mixing, GATv2-based attention refinement, attention-consistent skip fusion, and weak Welch PSD calibration to map 4 frontal inputs to 13 virtual channels, optimized for amplitude fidelity, spectral allocation, and channel-frequency texture in a dual-domain (time and frequency) framework.
AI.result: On the PRED+CT dataset, FAVC-Net achieved the best joint waveform-spectral operating point among neural and interpolation baselines. Time-domain gains were modest; log-spectral distance and PSD KL divergence improved by 30.09% and 37.98% over the strongest non-FAVC comparator. Under wearable-like perturbations, spectral fidelity was preserved and spectral collapse resisted.
AI.conclusion: Generated posterior and parietal channels should be interpreted as frequency-calibrated representations derived from sparse frontal measurements, rather than independent physical recordings; virtual channels serve as dual-domain augmentations rather than true denser EEG recordings.

title: Battery-Sim-Agent: Leveraging LLM-Agent for Inverse Battery Parameter Estimation
summary: Parameterizing high-fidelity "digital twins" of batteries is a critical yet challenging inverse problem that hinders the pace of battery innovation. Prevailing methods formulate this as a black-box optimization (BBO) task, employing algorithms that are sample-inefficient and blind to the underlying physics. In this work, we introduce a new paradigm that reframes the inverse problem as a reasoning task, and present Battery-Sim-Agent, the first framework to deploy a Large Language Model (LLM) agent in a closed loop with a high-fidelity battery simulator. The agent mimics a human scientist's workflow: it interprets rich, multi-modal feedback from the simulator, forms physically-grounded hypotheses to explain discrepancies, and proposes structured parameter updates. On a systematically constructed benchmark suite spanning diverse battery chemistries, operating conditions, and difficulty levels, our agent significantly outperforms strong BBO baselines like Bayesian optimization in identifying accurate parameters. We further demonstrate the framework's capability in complex long-horizon degradation fitting tasks and validate its practical applicability on real-world battery datasets. Our results highlight the promise of LLM-agents as reasoning-based optimizers for scientific discovery and battery parameter estimation.

AI.tldr: An LLM-driven agent (Battery-Sim-Agent) closes loop with a high-fidelity battery simulator to infer model parameters; it outperforms Bayesian optimization across diverse benchmarks and enables long-horizon degradation fitting on real data.
AI.motivation: Parameterizing high-fidelity battery digital twins is a key inverse problem; traditional black-box optimization is sample-inefficient and physics-blind, slowing battery innovation.
AI.method: Introduce Battery-Sim-Agent, the first framework that uses an LLM agent in a closed loop with a high-fidelity simulator. The agent interprets rich simulator feedback, forms physically-grounded hypotheses to explain discrepancies, and proposes structured parameter updates. Evaluated on a benchmark suite spanning battery chemistries, operating conditions, and difficulty levels; extended to long-horizon degradation fitting and real-world datasets.
AI.result: The agent significantly outperforms strong BBO baselines (e.g., Bayesian optimization) in identifying accurate parameters. Demonstrates capability in complex degradation fitting and validates practical applicability on real-world data.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based agents can serve as reasoning-based optimizers for scientific discovery and efficient battery parameter estimation, offering a promising direction to accelerate battery development.

title: Geometry-Guided Modeling of Foundation Features Enables Generalizable Object Shape Deformation Learning
summary: Monocular 3D shape recovery is fundamental to geometric understanding, yet achieving robust generalization across arbitrary viewpoints and unseen object categories remains a significant challenge. In this paper, we present a generalizable deformation learning framework that reconstructs 3D objects by explicitly deforming a category-level shape template to match the target observation. To address complex shape variations between the template and the target, we introduce a geometry-guided feature modeling mechanism. This process first enriches foundation features with template topology to yield a geometry-aware representation, which is then explicitly correlated with the target observation to guide precise deformation. Furthermore, to bridge the disparity between the fixed template and arbitrary target views, we propose a view-adaptive feature aggregation module. This module leverages multi-view template features and their corresponding camera poses to enrich the canonical template representation, ensuring robust feature alignment regardless of the target's perspective. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in handling large shape variations and diverse viewpoints, exhibiting strong generalization to novel categories and effectively supporting downstream real-world dexterous robotic manipulation tasks. Project homepage: https://GODeform.github.io/

AI.tldr: GODeform introduces a generalizable monocular 3D shape reconstruction framework that deforms a category-level template to match observations, using geometry-guided features and view-adaptive aggregation to handle large shape variations and unseen viewpoints, with strong cross-category generalization and robotics applicability.
AI.motivation: The task requires robust 3D reconstruction from a single image across arbitrary viewpoints and unseen object categories. A template-to-target deformation approach leverages category topology, while geometry-aware representations and view-adaptive aggregation address structural variation and view discrepancy.
AI.method: 1) Geometry-guided feature modeling: enriches foundation features with template topology to form a geometry-aware representation, then explicitly correlates it with the target observation to guide precise deformation. 2) View-adaptive feature aggregation: uses multi-view template features and their camera poses to enrich the canonical template, enabling robust feature alignment across target perspectives. 3) Deform a fixed category-level template to match the target observation, enabling generalization to novel categories and viewpoints.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in handling large shape variations and diverse viewpoints, with strong generalization to novel categories and positive impact on downstream dexterous robotic manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The framework (GODeform) provides a generalizable deformation-based solution for monocular 3D shape recovery by fusing geometry-guided, template-aware representations with view-aware aggregation, achieving robust cross-category and cross-view generalization and enabling practical robotic applications.

title: Causal Label Recovery in Payment Networks
summary:
Fraud detection models in payment networks train on chargeback labels that are systematically biased. Every label must survive three sequential gates: authorization (declined transactions generate no labels), issuer reporting (unreported fraud is invisible), and delay (pending chargebacks are missing at training time). Labels that do arrive may be corrupted by first-party misuse or issuer misclassification. A companion paper [arXiv:2605.27557] proved that these four impairments impose a minimax lower bound on detection performance. This paper asks: can that bound be achieved?
  We formalize the observation pipeline as a sequential missing-data problem with three propensity stages and a corruption layer, and construct the Sequential Triply Robust (STR) estimator. The STR corrects for all four impairments simultaneously and achieves the semiparametric efficiency bound -- no estimator can have lower asymptotic variance. It is sequentially triply robust: at each gate, consistency requires only that either the propensity model or the outcome regression is correctly specified, not both. We provide corruption correction via noise-rate-adjusted pseudo-labels, empirical Bayes shrinkage to stabilize inverse-propensity weights for small issuers, a plug-in variance estimator yielding valid confidence intervals, and a Bernstein concentration inequality for finite-sample guarantees.
  On the operational side, we derive the optimal training delay -- the maturity window that minimizes the sum of label-quality loss and model staleness -- and prove that the STR permits training on data that is days old rather than months old, decoupling model freshness from the chargeback maturity cycle. The STR provably dominates naive chargeback-based training in mean squared error for any sample size.

AI.tldr: Sequential Triply Robust (STR) estimator corrects three-stage label missingness and a corruption layer in fraud-detection training data, achieving the semiparametric efficiency bound and improved training data strategy.
AI.motivation: Labels in payment network fraud detection are biased by three sequential gates (authorization, reporting, delay) plus corruption; previous work showed a minimax bound on detection performance; aim to achieve this bound in practice.
AI.method: Formalize the observation pipeline as a sequential missing-data problem with three propensity stages and a corruption layer; construct STR estimator that is sequentially triply robust; incorporate corruption correction via noise-rate-adjusted pseudo-labels, empirical Bayes shrinkage for stability of inverse-propensity weights, plug-in variance estimator, and Bernstein concentration for finite-sample guarantees.
AI.result: STR achieves the semiparametric efficiency bound (lowest possible asymptotic variance); it is sequentially triple robust (consistency if either propensity model or outcome regression is correct at each gate); provides operational benefits including optimal training delay that balances label quality and model staleness; enables training on data days old instead of months; STR dominates naive chargeback-based training in mean squared error for any sample size.
AI.conclusion: The STR framework provides a theoretically optimal and practically robust solution for training fraud detection models under sequential missingness and label corruption, with provable finite-sample guarantees and actionable guidance on data freshness.

title: ParaTool: Shifting Tool Representations from Context to Parameters
summary: Tool calling extends large language models (LLMs) by enabling grounded interaction with external executable interfaces, thereby supporting environment-coupled problem solving. However, mainstream in-context learning (ICL) approaches typically incorporate detailed tool documentation and usage examples directly into the context. This results in substantial inference overhead and heightened risks of hallucination as the context length grows. Conversely, while tuning-based methods improve general tool-calling capabilities, they often fail to effectively internalize the specific details of previously seen tools, thereby retaining a dependency on in-context documentation. To address these limitations, we propose ParaTool, a framework that projects each tool into a dedicated, loadable set of parameters. By equipping a dynamic integration of these parameterized tools, the LLM can perform tool calling without relying on in-context documents or examples. Specifically, our approach consists of three stages: (1) parametric tool pre-training encapsulates the knowledge of different tools into independent parameter modules; (2) soft tool selection employs a gating network to dynamically weigh and aggregate relevant tool parameters; and (3) parametric tool fine-tuning jointly updates tool parameters to align the training and inference processes. Experiments on Stable ToolBench and BFCL demonstrate that ParaTool significantly outperforms strong ICL-based baselines, achieving superior performance while reducing computational complexity.

AI.tldr: ParaTool proposes parameterizing tools as independent, loadable parameter modules and uses a gating-based soft selection to dynamically combine them, enabling tool calling without in-context documentation or exemplars. It achieves superior performance and reduced compute compared with ICL-based baselines.
AI.motivation: To alleviate the high inference overhead and hallucination risk of including full tool documentation and usage examples in the prompt, and to overcome tuning-methods' failure to internalize tool specifics, by internalizing tool knowledge into trainable parameters.
AI.method: Three-stage approach: (1) parametric tool pre-training to encode each tool into a dedicated parameter module; (2) soft tool selection via a gating network that weighs and aggregates relevant tool parameters; (3) parametric tool fine-tuning to jointly update tool parameters so training and inference align.
AI.result: Empirical results on Stable ToolBench and BFCL show ParaTool outperforms strong ICL-based baselines and reduces computational complexity.
AI.conclusion: Parameterized tool integration enables effective, docs-free tool calling, improving performance and efficiency, with potential for broader environment-coupled problem solving.

title: SAFE-Pruner: Semantic Attention-Guided Future-Aware Token Pruning for Efficient Vision-Language-Action Manipulation
summary: Real-time inference of vision-language-action (VLA) models is essential for robotic control. While visual token pruning has shown strong potential for accelerating inference, most existing methods mainly base pruning decisions on shallow-layer cues and risk discarding visual information required by deep layers. To address this issue, we propose SAFE-Pruner, a plug-and-play pruning framework that incorporates attention cues of future layers into pruning decisions. Specifically, we identify semantic attention consistency, the tendency that VLA models concentrate their attention probability mass on the same semantic entity across execution steps. Based on this observation, we design a forward-looking strategy to forecast the token saliency in deep layers, which prevents the premature removal of critical tokens and leads to more stable acceleration. We further introduce an adaptive subtask division strategy to detect abrupt attention shifts, thereby improving forecasting accuracy and pruning reliability. Extensive experiments in simulation and real-world settings demonstrate that our method achieves up to 1.89x speedup with a minimal degradation in success rate of less than 1.7%, while outperforming state-of-the-art methods by up to 1.9%.

AI.tldr: SAFE-Pruner introduces forward-looking token pruning for vision-language-action models by forecasting deep-layer token saliency from future-layer attention cues and an adaptive subtask division. It achieves up to 1.89x speedup with minimal impact on success rate (<1.7%) and up to 1.9% improvement over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Real-time inference for vision-language-action (VLA) systems is critical for robotic control. Existing visual token pruning relies on shallow-layer cues and can discard tokens later found essential by deeper layers, hindering performance.
AI.method: SAFE-Pruner is a plug-and-play pruning framework that uses attention cues from future layers to guide pruning. It relies on the semantic attention consistency across execution steps to forecast deep-layer token saliency, preventing premature token removal. It also introduces an adaptive subtask division strategy to detect abrupt attention shifts, improving forecasting accuracy and pruning reliability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show up to 1.89x acceleration with less than 1.7% degradation in success rate, and up to 1.9% improvement over state-of-the-art methods in comparison.
