AI.conclusion: Forecasting deep-layer token importance via future-attention cues and adaptive subtasks yields more stable, reliable pruning for VLA models, enabling faster real-time inference without substantial performance loss.

title: A Theoretical and Experimental Study of a Novel Adaptive Learning Algorithm
summary: A crucial component of machine learning algorithms is minimizing loss functions with less computational cost and less oscillations. While adaptive learning rate-based optimizers have been widely used for real-world tasks, they do not guarantee convergence, which is why AMSGrad was later introduced to investigate the non-convergence behaviour of Adam. In this paper, popular adaptive optimization methods like Adam and AMSGrad are critically reviewed with an emphasis on their fundamental design concepts. To address limitations of the above mentioned optimizers, a new optimizer variant, C-Adam, is proposed based on the line of sight approach. A theoretical proof for convergence is also provided and the optimizer is validated through a number of real-life based numerical experiments.

AI.tldr: The paper reviews Adam and AMSGrad, highlighting convergence issues, and introduces C-Adam based on a line-of-sight approach. It provides a convergence proof and validates the method with real-world numerical experiments.
AI.motivation: To address non-convergence and oscillations in adaptive optimizers (like Adam/AMSGrad) while reducing computational cost in loss minimization tasks.
AI.method: Critically surveys existing adaptive optimizers (Adam and AMSGrad), proposes C-Adam via a line-of-sight strategy, provides a theoretical convergence proof, and conducts real-world numerical experiments to validate performance.
AI.result: A theoretical convergence proof for C-Adam is established; empirical experiments indicate improved stability and efficiency over baseline adaptive methods.
AI.conclusion: C-Adam offers a convergent, robust alternative to Adam/AMSGrad with demonstrated theoretical guarantees and practical effectiveness.

title: Unsupervised Semantic Segmentation Facilitates Model Understanding
summary:
Self-supervised learning (SSL) has produced a diverse landscape of vision transformers (ViTs) whose pretrained representations support a wide range of downstream tasks. Towards a better understanding of these models, a body of work has assessed the mechanics of their self-attention as well as the types of information captured across their representations, revealing, for example, stark differences between models trained with contrastive learning (CL) and masked image modeling (MIM). However, these advances in model understanding have not yet fully permeated the broader community, where insights specific to CL models are sometimes generalized to MIM models. To make model understanding straightforward and intuitive for a broad audience, we propose a simple and easily interpretable visualization protocol.
  Our protocol is based on visualizing unsupervised semantic segmentation results, yet our goal is not to maximize segmentation performance. Instead, it allows us to convey model behaviors that consistently emerge across images. Benchmarking a diverse set of SSL models across layers and representations, we obtain novel insights into distinct positional biases and scaling behaviors, including strong boundary artifacts in DINOv3-Large model tokens. These insights complement and help communicate a range of previous findings.
  Our protocol further enables a clear visual distinction between positional effects and the closely related but distinct locality bias, which has been studied much more extensively in the literature. The protocol is publicly available on GitHub and we believe it will catalyze further model understanding for a broad community.

AI.tldr: Presents a simple, interpretable visualization protocol to understand SSL ViTs, highlighting CL vs MIM differences and a distinction between positional effects and locality bias through unsupervised semantic segmentation visualizations across layers.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between findings on CL and MIM models by providing an accessible, intuitive visualization tool that communicates model behaviors to a broad audience and complements existing mechanical analyses of self-attention and representations.
AI.method: Benchmark a diverse set of SSL ViT models (including CL and MIM) by visualizing unsupervised semantic segmentation results across layers and representations. Analyze recurring behaviors: positional biases, scaling, boundary artifacts (notably in DINOv3-Large). Distinguish positional effects from locality bias. Provide an open-source protocol on GitHub.
AI.result: Identified consistent phenomena across models, including distinct positional biases and scaling patterns, and specific boundary artifacts in DINOv3-Large. The protocol effectively differentiates positional effects from locality bias and offers a communicative, intuitive means to convey complex model behavior beyond segmentation performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed visualization protocol is simple, interpretable, and broadly applicable, enabling clearer communication of SSL ViT behaviors and catalyzing further community understanding; the authors provide an open-source implementation on GitHub.

title: LoopFM: Learning frOm HistOrical RePresentations of Foundation Model for Recommendation
comment: Shali Jiang, Hua Zheng, Boyang Liu contributed equally to this work
summary: Knowledge distillation (KD) transfers a single scalar prediction from a large foundation model (FM) to compact vertical models (VMs), suffering from diminishing transfer ratio -- the fraction of FM improvement captured by the VM -- as a single scalar cannot convey the rich intermediate knowledge that larger FMs learn. To address this bottleneck, we propose LoopFM (Learning frOm HistOrical ReP*resentations of FM), a framework that opens a high-bandwidth transfer channel by structuring FM intermediate embeddings as input features (e.g., user history sequence) for downstream VMs, without requiring real-time FM inference at serving and architectural coupling between FM and VM. We provide a theoretical framework for LoopFM with a gain decomposition and transfer-ratio analysis. On three public benchmarks, LoopFM demonstrates strong AUC improvements (e.g., 6\%+ on TaobaoAd) and complementary knowledge transfer capability with KD. On industrial-scale systems (billions of examples, trillion-parameter FMs), LoopFM approximately doubles the knowledge transfer ratio on top of KD, delivering a +0.5\% conversion improvement in Y1H1, and a +1.03\% and +1.22\% conversion improvement from two individual launches respectively in Y1H2.

AI.tldr: LoopFM creates a high-bandwidth knowledge transfer channel by using historical FM intermediate embeddings as features for downstream VMs, decoupling FM/VM architectures and avoiding real-time FM inference. It significantly boosts transfer effectiveness beyond standard KD, with strong public benchmark gains and industrial-scale conversions.
AI.motivation: KD with a single scalar prediction captures limited transferability; richer intermediate representations from large FMs are underutilized, causing diminishing transfer ratios. A high-bandwidth, architecture-agnostic transfer channel can improve VM performance without real-time FM inference.
AI.method: Use FM intermediate embeddings (e.g., user history sequences) as additional input features for VMs. No real-time FM inference during serving; decouple FM/VM; provide theoretical gain decomposition and transfer-ratio analysis.
AI.result: Public benchmarks show strong AUC gains (e.g., 6%+ on TaobaoAd). In industrial-scale deployments (billions of examples, trillion-parameter FMs), LoopFM nearly doubles the knowledge transfer ratio on top of KD and yields conversion gains of +0.5% (Y1H1) and +1.03%, +1.22% (Y1H2) across two launches.
AI.conclusion: LoopFM delivers a high-bandwidth, architecture-agnostic transfer channel that substantially enhances knowledge transfer beyond KD and scales to industrial-scale systems.

title: DeepTool: Scaling Interleaved Deliberation in Tool-Integrated Reasoning via Process-Supervised Reinforcement Learning
summary: Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) extends LLM capabilities by leveraging external environments. However, existing methods lack the deliberation during sequential tool invocation required for strategic planning and self-correction. While RL mitigates this, conventional approaches for Tool-Integrated Reasoning are hindered by sparse outcome-based rewards, failing to supervise intermediate reasoning steps and tool invocations. To address this, we propose DeepTool, a novel framework that scales deliberate thinking within the interleaved process of thinking, action, and observation at each turn. In DeepTool, we first introduce a synthesis pipeline that evolves extended thinking into interleaved trajectories, integrating adversarial perturbations to ensure robustness and self-correction. Secondly, we devise Process-Supervised Reinforcement Learning based on GRPO, which utilizes an Action-Centric Process Reward to reinforce intermediate interleaved thinking and enforce precise tool invocation at every turn. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DeepTool achieves superior performance, boosting Qwen2.5-7B significantly across six benchmarks (e.g., AIME24: 3.2% -> 40.4% and HMMT25: 0.0% -> 28.6%). Furthermore, the token cost-effectiveness analysis confirms the utility of interleaved thinking, demonstrating DeepTool's optimal balance between performance and token efficiency.

AI.tldr: DeepTool introduces a Deliberate Interleaved Thinking framework with adversarial perturbations and a Process-Supervised RL (GRPO) objective to reinforce intermediate reasoning and precise tool invocation, achieving substantial gains on AIME24, HMMT25, and token efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address lack of deliberation during sequential tool invocation and sparse intermediate supervision in Tool-Integrated Reasoning; enable robust self-correction and precise tool calls.
AI.method: Two components: (1) synthesis pipeline that evolves extended thinking into interleaved trajectories with adversarial perturbations for robustness; (2) Process-Supervised RL based on GRPO using an Action-Centric Process Reward to reinforce intermediate thinking and enforce precise tool invocation at every turn.
AI.result: Outperforms baseline on six benchmarks, e.g., AIME24 from 3.2% to 40.4%, HMMT25 from 0.0% to 28.6% for Qwen2.5-7B; token cost analyses indicate favorable efficiency trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: DeepTool demonstrates that structured interleaved thinking and process-aware RL can substantially improve tool-integrated reasoning, balancing performance with token efficiency; promising for scalable, robust tool usage in LLMs.

title: Efficient, Validation-Free Intrinsic Quality Estimation for Large-Scale Face Recognition Datasets
summary: We propose Intrinsic Quality (IQ), a validation-free metric designed to estimate the inherent potential of face recognition (FR) datasets to produce high-performance models without the need for full-scale training. IQ integrates two components: (i) a Neighbor-Consistency Score that quantifies local identity label agreement via nearest neighbors, and (ii) Global Representation Subspace Complexity (Effective Rank, ER), which captures the underlying embedding geometry and dataset diversity. IQ allows for rapid evaluation using lightweight proxy models or data subsets, facilitating dataset diagnosis and curation prior to resource-intensive full-scale training. We describe an experimental protocol tailored to clean, noisy, and mixed-quality FR datasets, and outline evaluation methodologies to validate IQ's predictive power for downstream performance.

AI.tldr: Introduces Intrinsic Quality (IQ), a validation-free metric for FR datasets combining Neighbor-Consistency Score and Global Representation Subspace Complexity (Effective Rank) to predict downstream model performance using lightweight proxies.
AI.motivation: Address the need to gauge dataset quality and potential without full-scale training, enabling fast diagnosis and curation of FR datasets across clean, noisy, and mixed-quality regimes.
AI.method: Two components: (i) Neighbor-Consistency Score based on local identity label agreement via nearest neighbors; (ii) Global Representation Subspace Complexity via Effective Rank capturing embedding geometry and diversity. Proposes an experimental protocol and evaluation plan for FR datasets to validate IQ's predictive power.
AI.result: Framework proposal with protocol; no empirical results are provided in the abstract; IQ is presented as a rapid evaluation tool using proxy models or data subsets.
AI.conclusion: IQ can facilitate dataset diagnosis and curation before expensive training, potentially accelerating FR pipeline development.

title: Do Physics Foundation Models Learn Generalizable Physics? A Bias-Aware Benchmark Across Physical Regimes and Distribution Shifts
summary: Recent physics foundation models claim general spatiotemporal forecasting ability, yet their evaluations often collapse performance into a single average score under a fixed training distribution. This makes it difficult to determine whether a model has learned generalizable physical dynamics or only performs well under particular settings. We construct a benchmark with 8 physical dynamics, 3 training-data mixtures, and 25 test regimes induced by dynamic-scale and initial-condition complexity shifts, covering in-distribution, distribution-shift, and out-of-distribution settings. We evaluate five physics foundation model architectures and four model variants per architecture (scratch and three pretrained sizes), resulting in 60,000 measurements. Our results show that current physics foundation models behave as conditional rather than universal generalists: their generality depends on the physical regime, temporal scale, initial-condition setting, pretraining, model size, and architecture. Improving the training data distribution only partially mitigates this limitation. Pretraining and scaling are also unable to reliably remove their ability biases. We argue that improving physics foundation models requires moving beyond scaling models or expanding data, toward learning mechanisms that better capture transferable physical knowledge across regimes, temporal scales, and distribution shifts.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive benchmark reveals physics foundation models are not universally generalizable across regimes; their performance depends on regime, scale, and training configuration, and data/scale alone do not ensure true transferable physics understanding.
AI.motivation: To determine whether physics foundation models truly capture transferable physical dynamics or merely perform well under specific training/test distributions, addressing the limitation of single-metric evaluations.
AI.method: An 8-dynamics benchmark with 3 training-data mixtures and 25 test regimes induced by dynamic-scale and initial-condition complexity shifts; evaluation of 5 model architectures with 4 variants per architecture (scratch and three pretrained sizes), totaling 60,000 measurements.
AI.result: Core generality emerges only conditionally: models act as conditional generalists, with generalization varying by physical regime, temporal scale, initial-condition setting, pretraining, model size, and architecture. Training data expansion and pretraining/scale improvements do not reliably eliminate capability biases; accuracy across regimes remains uneven.
AI.conclusion: To advance physics foundation models, focus should shift from scaling/data expansion to learning mechanisms that capture transferable physical knowledge across regimes and scales, enabling robust generalization under distribution shifts.

title: GPS-Enhanced Tourist Mobility Modeling with Seasonal Spatial Priors and LLM-Based Activity Chain Generation
summary: Tourist mobility poses a distinct challenge for urban transportation planning. Unlike resident commuting, tourist travel is largely non-routine, attraction driven, and highly sensitive to trip purpose, travel season, and trip member composition. Existing approaches either measure aggregate tourist spatial patterns without generating individual schedules, or synthesize mobility without tourist specific structure such as trip duration conditioning, month varying attraction demand, and household co-travel rules. To address these challenges, we propose a four stage simulation framework combining month conditioned spatial priors derived from GPS and survey data, trip extent prediction from tourist demographics, distance feasible ward sequence assignment, and LLM-based activity chain generation under household and spatial constraints. GPS data are used only in privacy preserving aggregated form as month conditioned spatial priors, with no individual traces retained or exposed. Experiments on tourism in Tokyo demonstrate that the GPS based tourist cohort extraction recovers spatial visitation signatures consistent with survey references, and our framework produces demographically aligned synthetic schedules whose ward-level visitation shares align closely with both survey distributions and staypoint derived monthly visitation patterns. The results demonstrate the framework's effectiveness as a geographically grounded, demographically aware approach to tourist mobility modeling.

AI.tldr: A four-stage, privacy-conscious framework for tourist mobility modeling that integrates month-conditioned GPS-derived spatial priors, demographic-based trip extent prediction, distance-feasible ward sequence assignment, and LLM-driven activity chain generation under household and spatial constraints; validated on Tokyo with aggregated GPS data and survey-aligned synthetic schedules.
AI.motivation: Tourist travel is non-routine and largely attraction-driven, requiring individual schedules and seasonality consideration; existing methods struggle to produce demographic-aware, temporally conditioned, and household-consistent schedules while preserving privacy.
AI.method: Four-stage framework: (1) derive month-conditioned spatial priors from GPS and survey data while preserving privacy (aggregated form only); (2) predict trip extent from tourist demographics; (3) assign ward sequences within feasible distance constraints; (4) generate activity chains via a large language model under household and spatial constraints. Experiments in Tokyo show spatial visitation signatures align with survey references; synthetic schedules are demographically aligned; ward-level visitation shares match survey and staypoint-derived patterns.
AI.result: GPS-based cohort extraction recovers expected visitation signatures; framework produces demographically aligned synthetic schedules; ward-level visitation shares closely match survey distributions and staypoint-derived monthly patterns; demonstrates effectiveness of geographically grounded, demographically aware tourist mobility modeling.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework advances tourist mobility modeling by integrating privacy-preserving GPS priors with demographic and constraint-aware scheduling, yielding realistic, policy-relevant synthetic travel patterns for tourism planning.

title: SLAD : Shared LoRA Adapters for Task Specific Distillation
summary: In the context of resource-constrained environments such as embedded systems, adapting reduced-size foundation models to downstream tasks has become increasingly popular. This has recently motivated the emerging setting of task-specific distillation, where a larger and a smaller version of the same foundation model are both adapted to the same downstream task, with the goal of transferring knowledge from the former to the latter. Recent work has demonstrated the benefits of using a larger version of the same foundation model to assist the adaptation of a smaller one. Typically, the larger model (teacher) is first adapted via fine-tuning or linear probing before its knowledge is distilled into the smaller model (student). While fine-tuning the teacher often increases its performance, recent work showed that probing it leads to better knowledge distillation to the student. Our findings show that this is mainly due to a mis-alignment in feature representation between the teacher and the student which occurs during the teacher's fine-tuning. Inspired by existing efforts to preserve previously learned knowledge, we first propose leveraging low-rank adaptation, resulting in better feature alignment and therefore better knowledge transfer. Drawing from this insight, we further enhance the feature alignment through a parameter-sharing strategy of the adapters between the two encoders during joint training. Our proposed method, SLAD, shows better feature alignment between the teacher and student, which results in increased performance for not only the student but also the teacher model, while being 2x faster to train than fine-tuning. Through extensive experiments on multiple classification and segmentation datasets, we demonstrate the improved accuracy and transfer efficiency of our method, achieving state-of-the-art performance in the task-specific distillation framework.

AI.tldr: SLAD: a task-specific distillation framework that uses low-rank adapters and shared adapters to align teacher–student feature representations, yielding faster training and improved accuracy in classification and segmentation tasks.
AI.motivation: To mitigate feature misalignment that occurs when fine-tuning the teacher for distillation, which impairs knowledge transfer to the student in resource-constrained settings.
AI.method: Apply low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to preserve feature alignment between teacher and student; introduce a parameter-sharing strategy for adapters across the two encoders during joint training to further enhance alignment.
AI.result: Better feature alignment improves both student and teacher performance, while reducing training time by ~2× compared with full teacher fine-tuning; achieves state-of-the-art performance in task-specific distillation across multiple classification and segmentation datasets.
AI.conclusion: SLAD offers a more efficient and effective path for task-specific distillation by preserving feature alignment through adapters and cross-model parameter sharing, with strong empirical validation and potential applicability to broader downstream tasks.

title: NeuroEdge: Real-Time Hand Gesture Recognition with High-Density EMG Using Deep Learning at the Edge
summary: High-density electromyography (HD-EMG) has emerged as a powerful modality for decoding fine-grained neuromuscular activity, enabling real-time neural-machine interfaces (NMIs) for applications such as prosthetic control, rehabilitation, and augmented interaction. While deep learning approaches such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs)have demonstrated high classification accuracy for EMG-based gesture recognition, their deployment on embedded hardware remains a major challenge due to computational and memory constraints. This paper presents NeuroEdge, a real-time HD EMG-based NMI system that performs gesture recognition entirely on resource-constrained microcontrollers. The system features two custom-designed modules: the HD-EMG StreamBridge, a wireless communication interface that streams raw HD-EMG data from a Quattrocento amplifier to an ESP32 microcontroller; and the EdgeDL Inference Engine, a lightweight deep learning framework executing on a Sony Spresense microcontroller. A compact 1-dimensional CNN optimized for embedded inference processes, sliding windows of EMG data in real time. Data streaming and inference are pipelined and synchronized through an architecture that utilizes Direct Memory Access (DMA) for data transfer and Serial Peripheral Interface (SPI) burst communication between the ESP32 and Spresense, ensuring low-latency performance. Experimental results show that NeuroEdge achieves a real-time classification accuracy of 90% across seven hand gestures, with a total average latency of 83 ms using 192 channels of HD-EMG recorded from the forearm. Our system demonstrates the feasibility of deploying complex HD-EMG-based gesture recognition on microcontroller-based edge devices, bridging the gap between high-resolution biosignal acquisition and deep learning-based embedded inference for next-generation NMIs.

AI.tldr: NeuroEdge demonstrates real-time, embedded HD-EMG gesture recognition on resource-constrained microcontrollers using a two-module edge architecture and a compact 1D CNN, achieving 90% accuracy with 83 ms latency on 192 channels.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between high-resolution HD-EMG sensing and DL-based gesture recognition on embedded hardware, enabling fully edge-based neuromuscular interfaces without cloud offloading.
AI.method: Two-module architecture: HD-EMG StreamBridge for wireless streaming from a Quattrocento amplifier to ESP32 and EdgeDL Inference Engine on Sony Spresense. A compact 1D CNN processes sliding windows of HD-EMG in real time. Data transferred via DMA and SPI bursts; pipeline ensures low-latency inference on microcontrollers.
AI.result: Real-time gesture classification accuracy of 90% across seven hand gestures with total average latency of 83 ms, using 192 HD-EMG channels from forearm data.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of deploying complex HD-EMG-based gesture recognition entirely on edge microcontrollers, bridging high-resolution biosignals with embedded DL for next-generation NMIs.

title: FinVerBench: Benchmark Validity and Calibration in Large Language Model Financial Statement Verification
summary: We introduce FinVerBench, a benchmark and validity study for financial statement verification: determining whether a set of corporate financial statements is numerically consistent from the information shown to the model. FinVerBench is built from SEC 10-K XBRL filings for 43 S&P 500 companies and defines a four-category error taxonomy covering arithmetic, cross-statement linkage, year-over-year, and magnitude perturbations. We attempt fifteen contemporary LLM evaluations and report fourteen complete runs; a Gemini 2.5 Pro run is excluded from the main comparison because 40/108 gateway calls failed. All binary metrics exclude underdetermined positive instances whose perturbed line item is not rendered, leaving a 105-instance observable diagnostic subset (43 clean, 62 error-injected). Under the original guided-checklist prompt on the unrounded diagnostic subset, nine of fourteen complete LLM runs produce 95-100% false positives on clean statements, while one run achieves 0% observed false positives. Benchmark rendering choices materially affect measured recall: on a realistic rounded variant of the same observable subset, the calibrated model's recall is 79.0% with 0% observed FPR, compared with 100.0% recall on the unrounded diagnostic variant. These results support a construct-validity conclusion rather than a final leaderboard: financial statement verification is not merely arithmetic detection, but calibrated judgment under incomplete observability, prompt-induced assumptions, and realistic numerical rendering. FinVerBench and all code are publicly available.

AI.tldr: Introduces FinVerBench, a benchmark and validity study for financial statement verification using SEC 10-K XBRL data for 43 S&P 500 firms; defines four error types; evaluates 15 LLMs, with 105 observable diagnostics; results show prompting and rendering materially affect performance and suggest construct validity rather than leaderboard emphasis.
AI.motivation: To assess whether large language models can reliably verify numerical consistency in financial statements, addressing issues of observability, prompt design, and numerical rendering that are not captured by simple arithmetic checks.
AI.method: Construct a benchmark from SEC 10-K XBRL filings, define a four-category error taxonomy (arithmetic, cross-statement linkage, year-over-year, magnitude perturbations), and evaluate 15 contemporary LLMs with 105 diagnostic instances (43 clean, 62 with errors) under different prompt settings and rendering (rounded vs unrounded). Exclude one model due to gateway failures; report binary metrics on observable cases and analyze recall and false-positive patterns.
AI.result: Nine of fourteen complete runs yield very high false positives (95–100%) on clean statements under guided prompts; one run yields 0% observed false positives. Rendering choices significantly affect recall: rounded variant achieves 79.0% recall with 0% observed FPR, while unrounded variant yields 100% recall. A 105-instance diagnostic subset is used (43 clean, 62 error-injected). Gemini 2.5 Pro excluded from main comparison due to gateway failures.
AI.conclusion: Financial statement verification requires calibrated judgment under incomplete observability and prompt-induced assumptions; it is not merely arithmetic detection. FinVerBench supports construct validity and highlights the impact of rendering and prompts. Code and data are publicly available.

title: S2MDF: A Plug-And-Play Layer for Intersection-Free Multi-Object Signed Distance Fields
summary: Compositional implicit surface representations model scenes as collections of objects, each encoded by a Signed Distance Field (SDF). A fundamental limitation of this approach is that multiple SDFs can produce geometries that interpenetrate, violating physical plausibility. Existing mitigation strategies rely on soft penalty terms that reduce but do not eliminate intersections, and require careful loss weighting. To truly prevent interpenetration, we propose a hard constraint on vector-valued SDFs and introduce S2MDF, a lightweight plug-and-play module that enforces the constraint on any object-compositional SDF representation without architectural modifications. It introduces negligible computational overhead and is compatible with linearly-interpolated standard meshing algorithms such as Marching Cubes. It can be applied during training or as a post-processing step. Experiments on multiple state-of-the-art compositional methods show that S2MDF reduces intersections to numerical precision while preserving reconstruction quality, outperforming existing mitigation strategies.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, plug-and-play hard-constraint module (S2MDF) for vector-valued SDFs that prevents interpenetration in compositional implicit surfaces, with negligible overhead and compatibility with standard meshing (e.g., Marching Cubes); usable during training or post-processing.
AI.motivation: To address interpenetration in compositional SDFs, where existing soft penalties reduce but do not eliminate intersections and require careful loss weighting.
AI.method: Introduce S2MDF, a hard constraint enforcement for vector-valued SDFs implemented as a lightweight, architectural-agnostic module that plugs into any object-compositional SDF representation; compatible with linearly interpolated meshing algorithms; applicable during training or as post-processing.
AI.result: S2MDF reduces interpenetrations to numerical precision while preserving reconstruction quality and outperforms existing mitigation strategies.
AI.conclusion: Hard constraint-based enforcement via S2MDF provides a robust, low-overhead solution for preventing interpenetration in compositional implicit surface representations, with broad applicability and compatibility with standard meshing pipelines.

title: Attention as In-Context Empirical Bayes: A Two-Stage View via Particle Dynamics
summary: We study minimal attention-only transformers under all-token corruption and show they admit a two-stage empirical Bayes interpretation. A single attention step computes a kernel-weighted posterior mean with respect to the empirical distribution defined by the context. Depth refines this distribution through particle dynamics (Stage 1), while a long-range skip-connection carries the noisy input as a query for posterior inference (Stage 2), revealing distinct statistical roles for depth and attention residuals. The framework isolates a minimal setting in which the context itself induces a depth-dependent energy landscape governing in-context inference. We show that effective denoising can emerge without an explicit noise schedule: a fixed kernel bandwidth and finite integration horizon suffice, yielding a principled depth-noise relationship. We further establish a posterior-mean recovery guarantee for a class of well-behaved priors, where the empirical estimator converges to the Bayes-optimal predictor under asymptotic conditions. Connecting these dynamics to reverse-diffusion limits, our results provide a statistical interpretation of attention as in-context inference via sample-based posterior estimation, without explicit density modeling.

AI.tldr: Attention in minimal, all-token corruptors acts as a two-stage empirical Bayes estimator: a single attention step computes a kernel-weighted posterior mean over the context, while depth acts as particle dynamics refining this distribution and a long-range skip-connection provides the query for inference. Denoising can emerge with fixed kernel bandwidth and finite horizon, without a noise schedule. An asymptotic posterior-mean recovery guarantee holds for well-behaved priors, linking attention to sample-based posterior inference and reverse-diffusion limits without explicit density modeling.
AI.motivation: To provide a probabilistic, Bayes-based understanding of in-context learning in attention-only transformers under severe corruption, clarifying the distinct roles of depth and attention residuals and connecting to diffusion-like limits.
AI.method: The paper analyzes a minimal attention-only transformer under all-token corruption. It interprets one attention step as computing a kernel-weighted posterior mean with respect to the empirical context distribution. Depth is modeled as particle dynamics refining this posterior (Stage 1), while a long-range skip-connection carries the noisy input as a query for posterior inference (Stage 2). The analysis derives an energy landscape induced by context, shows that denoising can arise with fixed kernel bandwidth and finite horizon, and proves a posterior-mean recovery guarantee for a broad class of priors under asymptotics. It also connects to reverse-diffusion limits to interpret attention as sample-based posterior estimation without explicit density modeling.
AI.result: Shows that attention implements a posterior mean under an empirical Bayes framework; depth and attention residuals serve distinct roles; a depth-induced energy landscape governs in-context inference; fixed-bandwidth, finite-horizon denoising suffices; provides asymptotic guarantees that the empirical estimator converges to the Bayes-optimal predictor; situates attention within a reverse-diffusion-like interpretation without explicit density modeling.
AI.conclusion: Attention in minimal transformers can be viewed as in-context, sample-based posterior estimation with a depth-driven refinement dynamics and a long-range query. This yields a principled depth-noise relationship, a denoising mechanism without a noise schedule, and a posterior-mean recovery guarantee, bridging attention with Bayesian inference and diffusion-like interpretations without explicit density modeling.

title: Mind-Omni: A Unified Multi-Task Framework for Brain-Vision-Language Modeling via Discrete Diffusion
summary: Modeling the interplay between external stimuli and internal neural representations is a pivotal research area for Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs). A major limitation of prior work is the prevailing paradigm of specialized, single-task models, which curtails versatility and neglects inter-task synergies. To address this, we propose Mind-Omni, the first versatile framework that unifies seven distinct encoding and decoding tasks through a discrete diffusion paradigm. At its core is a novel Brain Tokenizer that transforms heterogeneous, continuous brain signals into standardized, discrete tokens. This enables direct, token-level interactions for mutual understanding and generation between any two or more modalities within a shared semantic space. To unlock advanced reasoning capabilities, we further curate a specialized Brain Question Answering (BQA) instruction-tuning dataset. Our model not only establishes a new state-of-the-art among multi-task unified frameworks but also provides strong evidence for multi-task synergy. By demonstrating performance competitive with, and at times superior to, larger specialized models, our work offers a powerful new paradigm for neural modeling and paves the way for foundation models of neural activity. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/ReedOnePeck/Mind-Omni.

AI.tldr: Mind-Omni: a universal, diffusion-based framework for neural data that unifies seven encoding/decoding tasks via a Brain Tokenizer that discretizes continuous brain signals into tokens, enabling cross-modal interactions in a shared semantic space. Includes a Brain Question Answering instruction-tuning dataset; claims state-of-the-art among multi-task unified models and competitive with larger specialized models; code available.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of single-task, specialized brain-computer interface models by leveraging inter-task synergies and building a versatile, foundation-model-like framework for neural activity.
AI.method: Introduce a discrete diffusion-based framework (Mind-Omni) and a Brain Tokenizer that converts heterogeneous, continuous brain signals into discrete tokens. Enables token-level interactions across modalities within a shared semantic space. Unified seven encoding/decoding tasks and curates a Brain QA instruction-tuning dataset for advanced reasoning.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance among multi-task unified frameworks and shows competitive or superior performance to larger specialized models. Provides empirical evidence for multi-task synergy and versatility of the approach.
AI.conclusion: Mind-Omni offers a flexible, scalable foundation for neural activity modeling, enabling mutual understanding and generation across modalities, and establishing a pathway toward foundation models of neural activity; code released.

title: GeoMag: Geometric-Aware Video Motion Magnification via State Space Model
summary: Video Motion Magnification (VMM) reveals imperceptible dynamics but often suffers from structural inconsistencies under complex geometric transformations. Existing learning-based methods generally face a trade-off between the limited global context of CNNs and the high computational cost of Transformers. In addition, current training protocols, largely dominated by simple linear motion, fail to capture the geometric and imaging complexities encountered in real-world videos. To address these issues, we propose GeoMag, a geometric-aware VMM framework built upon State Space Models to achieve globally consistent motion amplification with linear complexity. We further construct Geo-200K, a large-scale synthetic dataset that introduces rich geometric transformations together with sensor-realistic degradations, improving the diversity and realism of training signals. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks show that GeoMag consistently outperforms prior methods in visual fidelity and computational efficiency, while producing fewer artifacts and better structural consistency.

AI.tldr: GeoMag is a geometry-aware video motion magnification method that uses State Space Models to deliver globally consistent amplification with linear complexity, backed by a large synthetic Geo-200K dataset; it achieves better fidelity and efficiency than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing VMM methods struggle with geometric inconsistencies under complex transforms and face CNN-Transformer trade-offs. Training often relies on simple linear motion, failing to capture real-world geometry and imaging effects. A scalable, geometry-aware approach with realistic training data is needed.
AI.method: Introduce GeoMag: a VMM framework based on State Space Models to capture long-range, globally-consistent motion with linear (O(n)) complexity. Build Geo-200K, a synthetic dataset with rich geometric transformations and sensor-like degradations. Train and evaluate on synthetic and real datasets; compare to baselines.
AI.result: GeoMag achieves higher visual fidelity and lower artifacts, with improved structural consistency and computational efficiency (linear complexity) relative to prior methods, on both synthetic and real-world benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: GeoMag provides a geometry-aware, efficiently scalable VMM solution and demonstrates that a rich, geometry-focused synthetic dataset improves generalization for real-world videos.

title: Neural-Behavioral Representation of Natural Whole-body Movement in Monkeys
summary: Understanding how cortical activity represents natural whole-body behaviors in primates remains challenging. Limited by the diversity of movements and inaccessibility of large-scale neural representation of whole-body kinematics, previous motor decoding studies focused on constrained tasks and limited limb movements. Here, we present a neural-behavioral recording and modeling framework for freely moving monkeys, combining large-scale epidural cortical signals from distributed sensory- and motor-related areas with synchronized multi-view motion capture through a custom-made data collection platform. We reconstructed whole-body monkey kinematics and learned a compact behavior prior using an autoregressive encoder-decoder model. Conditioned on neural signals, the model decoded accurate and realistic whole-body movement without explicit physical constraints. Our results provide a novel proof-of-concept approach for decoding natural whole-body movements in primates using large-scale intracranial neural activity.

AI.tldr: A neural-behavioral framework decodes natural whole-body movements from large-scale epidural cortical signals in freely moving monkeys using an autoregressive encoder–decoder with a compact behavioral prior, achieving accurate and realistic kinematics conditioned on neural activity—a proof-of-concept for naturalistic brain–body decoding.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of decoding constrained tasks and limited limb movements, by leveraging broad, scalable neural signals across sensory and motor areas and modeling naturalistic whole-body behavior with a data-driven prior.
AI.method: Record large-scale epidural cortical activity from distributed sensory- and motor-related areas in freely moving monkeys. Simultaneously capture whole-body motion with synchronized multi-view video. Build a data collection platform. Learn a compact behavior prior via an autoregressive encoder–decoder model and decode whole-body kinematics conditioned on neural signals, without enforcing explicit physical constraints.
AI.result: Demonstrates accurate and realistic reconstruction of whole-body movements from neural activity, validating a proof-of-concept for naturalistic brain–body decoding in primates.
AI.conclusion: Opens a path toward decoding naturalistic, whole-body behaviors from large-scale neural activity and informs development of brain–body interfaces; future work should address generalization, real-time decoding, and translational relevance to humans.

title: HiKEY: Hierarchical Multimodal Retrieval for Open-Domain Document Question Answering
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for document-based Open-domain Question Answering (ODQA) on large-scale industrial corpora faces two critical bottlenecks: routing failure in locating the correct document and evidence fragmentation in integrating scattered information. Existing approaches relying on flat text chunks or page-level images inherently struggle to (i) precisely pinpoint the target document among thousands of candidates and (ii) organically connect multimodal evidence, such as tables and figures, within a limited token budget. To address these challenges, we propose HiKEY, a hierarchical tree-based multimodal retrieval framework that elevates document hierarchy to a first-class retrieval signal. Instead of simple chunking, HiKEY reconstructs a logical heterogeneous graph via Document Hierarchical Parsing (DHP), explicitly encoding parent-child relationships. Adopting a hierarchical coarse-to-fine strategy, the framework (1) performs global routing to rapidly prune the search space using hierarchical indexing, and (2) conducts fine-grained retrieval to rank sections by employing a multimodal fusion strategy that captures the most discriminative evidence. Finally, HiKEY assembles a token-efficient evidence subgraph via a hybrid structural-semantic packing strategy. Experiments on ODQA benchmarks demonstrate that HiKEY significantly outperforms page- and chunk-based baselines, improving retrieval recall by up to 12.9% and end-to-end QA performance by up to 6.8%.

AI.tldr: HiKEY introduces a hierarchical tree-based multimodal retrieval framework for document-based ODQA, using Document Hierarchical Parsing to form a heterogeneous, parent-child graph; it employs hierarchical coarse-to-fine routing and a multimodal fusion method to rank evidence, assembling a compact token-efficient subgraph.
AI.motivation: Address two bottlenecks in retrieval-augmented generation for large-scale industrial corpora: (i) routing failures in locating the correct document among thousands of candidates, and (ii) evidence fragmentation when integrating scattered information from multiple modalities under a strict token budget.
AI.method: Constructs a heterogeneous graph via Document Hierarchical Parsing (DHP) that encodes document hierarchy; performs hierarchical coarse-to-fine retrieval with global routing using hierarchical indices followed by fine-grained, multimodal evidence ranking; produces a token-efficient evidence subgraph through a hybrid structural-semantic packing strategy.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on ODQA benchmarks shows HiKEY outperforms page- and chunk-based baselines, boosting retrieval recall by up to 12.9% and end-to-end QA performance by up to 6.8%.
AI.conclusion: Modeling document hierarchy as a first-class retrieval signal and assembling compact, multimodal evidence subgraphs significantly improves retrieval precision and QA quality in RAG for large-scale industrial corpora.

title: Solving Integer Linear Programming with Parallel Tempering
summary: Integer Linear Programming (ILP) serves as a versatile framework for modeling a wide range of combinatorial optimization problems, typically addressed by sophisticated exact solvers or heuristics. While learning-based approaches have recently shown their effectiveness, they suffer from poor generalization to out-of-distribution instances and inherent dependence on external solvers. In this work, we propose a solver-free, sampling-based optimization framework for ILP that directly explores discrete feasible regions without training or external solvers. Exploiting the linear structure of ILP, we employ a Locally-Balanced Proposal to construct a transition kernel, thereby avoiding the gradient approximation. To overcome the highly multimodal nature of ILP energy landscapes, we integrate Parallel Tempering. In addition to standard temperature tempering, we introduce penalty tempering, which modulates constraint barriers while preserving the objective landscape over feasible solutions. Empirically, our method consistently outperforms SCIP across all four benchmarks, matches or exceeds Gurobi on two of four tasks within a 200-second budget, and is substantially more robust to distribution shift than learning-based methods. Furthermore, on MIPLIB 2017 instances, our framework remains competitive with classical solvers without any problem-specific tuning.

AI.tldr: Solver-free, sampling-based ILP optimization using a Locally-Balanced Proposal and parallel/penalty tempering, achieving strong performance and robustness without training or external solvers.
AI.motivation: Address generalization gaps and solver-dependence of learning-based ILP approaches; enable robust, training-free optimization directly over discrete feasible regions.
AI.method: Locally-Balanced Proposal to build a transition kernel; parallel tempering plus penalty tempering to navigate multimodal energy landscapes and constraint barriers; no gradient reliance, no external solvers, no training.
AI.result: Outperforms SCIP on four benchmarks; matches/exceeds Gurobi on two of four tasks within 200s; robust to distribution shift; competitive with classical solvers on MIPLIB 2017 without tuning.
AI.conclusion: A solver-free, sampling-based ILP framework can achieve competitive performance and robustness relative to state-of-the-art solvers and learning-based methods, without problem-specific tuning.

title: Improving Collaborative Storytelling with a Multi-Agent Framework Based on Large Language Models
summary: The topic of Co-creation, i.e., AI agents interacting with humans to generate outputs (e.g., art), has gained significant attention recently. However, most studies focus on adult-human interactions in a digital setting. This paper explores a novel ludic co-creation scenario involving children and Large Language Models (LLMs) interacting through a physical board game to create written stories. Our goal is to develop a multi-agent framework capable of producing high-quality narratives suitable for young players. At the core of our approach is an iterative Writer-Editor process in which one LLM generates stories while another evaluates them and provides feedback for refinement. Through a simulation study involving multiple LLMs, we show that this iterative interaction consistently improves the perceived quality of generated stories across successive loops. The results indicate that a small number of refinement steps may be sufficient to achieve high-quality outputs in interactive storytelling systems.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent Writer-Editor LLM framework for children's interactive storytelling via a physical board game; iterative refinement improves quality with few passes.
AI.motivation: Addresses a gap in co-creation research by focusing on child-LLM interactions in a ludic (board-game) setting to produce high-quality narratives suitable for young players.
AI.method: An iterative Writer-Editor loop with one LLM generating stories and another evaluating/feedbacking for refinement; evaluated via a simulation study with multiple LLMs showing quality gains across loops.
AI.result: Iterative refinement consistently improves the perceived quality of generated stories; a small number of refinement steps suffices to achieve high-quality outputs in interactive storytelling.
AI.conclusion: The proposed lightweight, multi-agent Write-Edit refinement framework is effective for producing high-quality, child-appropriate narratives in ludic co-creation settings, with practical implications for interactive storytelling systems.

title: Improving CLIP Adaptation by Breaking Tail Alignment for Source-Free Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as CLIP demonstrate strong zero-shot generalization, but their performance significantly degrades in cross-domain scenarios with scarce target-domain training data (Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning, CDFSL). In this paper, we focus on the target-domain few-shot finetuning in the CLIP-based CDFSL task. Prevailing finetuning paradigms uniformly align all image patch tokens with their corresponding textual embeddings. However, we find a counterintuitive phenomenon: actively pushing away certain low-similarity image tokens, termed "tail tokens", from their textual embeddings consistently improves target-domain performance. We delve into this phenomenon and provide a novel interpretation: under great domain shifts and scarce training data, the model can hardly extract semantic information from visual inputs; therefore, the common belief of alignment is valid only for tokens already containing sufficient semantic information; for tail tokens, forcing the alignment would lead to excessive overfitting to the scarce training, while breaking the alignment is more useful. Motivated by this, we propose Adaptive Tail-Head Alignment (ATHA), a novel fine-tuning strategy for CLIP that transforms the conventional uniform alignment paradigm to an adaptive alignment paradigm, with both alignment strengthening and weakening. Extensive experiments on four challenging CDFSL benchmarks validate our state-of-the-art performance. Our code is available at https://github.com/shuaiyi308/ATHA.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Tail-Head Alignment (ATHA) improves CLIP-based cross-domain few-shot fine-tuning by selectively weakening alignment for tail tokens and strengthening alignment for head tokens, achieving state-of-the-art on four CDFSL benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In cross-domain few-shot CLIP finetuning, uniform image-token-to-textual-embedding alignment causes overfitting for low-signal (tail) tokens under large domain shifts and scarce data; breaking or weakening alignment for tail tokens can improve generalization.
AI.method: Introduce ATHA: an adaptive alignment mechanism that partitions image patch tokens into tail and head groups based on their similarity to textual embeddings, and applies weakening or strengthening of the token-text alignment accordingly during fine-tuning; operates within the CLIP framework to adapt alignment strength across tokens.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four challenging CDFSL benchmarks show ATHA achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing finetuning approaches.
AI.conclusion: Token-wise adaptive alignment is beneficial for cross-domain few-shot CLIP finetuning; ATHA provides a simple, effective strategy for better target-domain generalization with limited data; code released.

title: Deep Adaptive Dimension Reduction for Bayesian Inference in Inverse Problems
summary: Solving high-dimensional PDE-governed inverse problems is often challenging due to complex non-Gaussian posterior distributions, expensive forward model evaluations, and misspecified prior information. To address these issues, we propose a deep adaptive dimension-reduction Bayesian inference framework based on the Variational Flow (VF) model. Since standard normalizing flows are restricted by bijective mappings and cannot directly reduce dimensions, VF overcomes this limitation by integrating VAE-based nonlinear dimension reduction with dual normalizing flows for the latent prior and encoder. This design provides a strictly higher evidence lower bound than VAE and allows more flexible approximation of complex posterior distributions. We further introduce an iterative prior updating strategy that gradually moves the prior mean toward high-probability posterior regions, avoiding manual prior tuning. These components form a closed adaptive loop together with an adaptively fine-tuned Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) surrogate: VF generates posterior-concentrated samples to refine the surrogate, while the updated surrogate further improves posterior inference. Numerical experiments on a 100-dimensional Rosenbrock problem and three standard PDE-governed inverse problems show that our method delivers competitive or superior accuracy compared with MCMC, UKI, and SVGD baselines across all tested configurations, with the most pronounced advantages emerging in challenging scenarios such as high-noise observations and high-dimensional parameter spaces.

AI.tldr: A deep adaptive dimension-reduction Bayesian inference framework (Variational Flow) combines VAE-based nonlinear dimension reduction with dual normalizing flows, an iterative prior update, and an adaptively trained FNO surrogate to tackle high-dimensional PDE-governed inverse problems, achieving competitive accuracy vs MCMC/UKI/SVGD, especially in challenging regimes.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional inverse problems governed by PDEs face non-Gaussian posteriors, expensive forward models, and potentially misspecified priors. Standard normalizing flows struggle with dimensionality reduction, necessitating a framework that reduces dimension while flexibly approximating posteriors.
AI.method: Integrates VAE-based nonlinear dimension reduction with dual normalizing flows for latent prior and encoder. This yields a strictly higher ELBO than VAE. Introduces an iterative prior updating strategy that shifts the prior mean toward high-probability posterior regions. Employs an adaptively fine-tuned Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) surrogate. Forms a closed adaptive loop: VF generates posterior-concentrated samples to refine the surrogate, and the updated surrogate further improves posterior inference.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on a 100-dimensional Rosenbrock problem and three standard PDE-governed inverse problems show competitive or superior accuracy compared with MCMC, UKI, and SVGD baselines across tested configurations, with the most pronounced advantages in high-noise and high-dimensional settings.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a scalable, adaptive, and accurate Bayesian inference approach for high-dimensional inverse problems, addressing non-Gaussian posteriors and expensive forward models via simultaneous dimension reduction, adaptive priors, and surrogate refinement.

title: Beyond Attack Success Rate: Temporal Logit Observability for LLM Safety Failures
summary: Attack Success Rate (ASR) evaluates each jailbreak with a single yes/no label at the end of generation, telling us whether a failure happened but not how it unfolded. Two attacks that produce equally harmful outputs may have followed completely different paths, and ASR cannot tell them apart. We make those hidden paths observable from logits alone. Temporal Logit Observability (TLO) is a training-free diagnostic that watches a compliance-refusal margin during decoding and places each model-attack condition on a calibrated 2D plane. By design, this plane is most informative exactly where ASR is least informative: among attacks that succeed for genuinely different reasons. Across four aligned LLMs and three jailbreak paradigms, attacks with nearly identical ASR land at clearly different points on the plane: the same model can fail through different temporal patterns. The geometry matches refusal-direction probes from hidden states on most conditions, with one model showing the limit of our fixed-lexicon approach. A simple early-stop rule derived from TLO cuts successful jailbreaks by more than half, without false alarms on plain benign queries. Safety evaluation should report when and how a failure unfolds, not only whether it occurred. TLO makes the first two observable from logits alone.

AI.tldr: Temporal Logit Observability (TLO) is a training-free diagnostic that reveals how jailbreaks unfold by tracking logit dynamics during decoding and plotting them on a calibrated 2D plane, enabling effective early stopping and richer safety evaluation than ASR alone.
AI.motivation: ASR (attack success rate) conveys only binary outcomes and misses the causal paths that yield successful jailbreaks. Understanding temporal patterns across models and attacks improves safety assessment and mitigation.
AI.method: Monitor compliance-refusal margin during decoding, map model-attack condition to a 2D plane via calibration, and compare across four aligned LLMs and three jailbreak paradigms; no training required.
AI.result: Attacks with similar ASR occupy distinct regions on the plane; the same model can fail for different temporal reasons; plane geometry aligns with hidden-state refusal probes; one model shows limits of a fixed-lexicon approach.
AI.conclusion: TLO provides visibility into both whether and how a failure unfolds, enabling a simple early-stop rule that halves successful jailbreaks without increasing benign false alarms; safety reporting should include progressive failure dynamics.

title: Fewer Steps, Better Performance: Efficient Cross-Modal Clip Trimming for Video Moment Retrieval Using Language
summary: Given an untrimmed video and a sentence query, video moment retrieval using language (VMR) aims to locate a target query-relevant moment. Since the untrimmed video is overlong, almost all existing VMR methods first sparsely down-sample each untrimmed video into multiple fixed-length video clips and then conduct multi-modal interactions with the query feature and expensive clip features for reasoning, which is infeasible for long real-world videos that span hours. Since the video is downsampled into fixed-length clips, some query-related frames may be filtered out, which will blur the specific boundary of the target moment, take the adjacent irrelevant frames as new boundaries, easily leading to cross-modal misalignment and introducing both boundary-bias and reasoning-bias. To this end, in this paper, we propose an efficient approach, SpotVMR, to trim the query-relevant clip. Besides, our proposed SpotVMR can serve as plug-and-play module, which achieves efficiency for state-of-the-art VMR methods while maintaining good retrieval performance. Especially, we first design a novel clip search model that learns to identify promising video regions to search conditioned on the language query. Then, we introduce a set of low-cost semantic indexing features to capture the context of objects and interactions that suggest where to search the query-relevant moment. Also, the distillation loss is utilized to address the optimization issues arising from end-to-end joint training of the clip selector and VMR model. Extensive experiments on three challenging datasets demonstrate its effectiveness.

AI.tldr: SpotVMR introduces an efficient, plug-and-play clip-trimming module for video moment retrieval (VMR). It trains a language-conditioned clip search model with low-cost semantic indexing features and a distillation loss to stabilize joint training, reducing boundary/ reasoning bias and improving efficiency on long videos while preserving retrieval accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current VMR approaches down-sample long videos into fixed-length clips, causing boundary bias and misalignment with query moments. This is inefficient for long videos and can miss relevant frames. There is a need for an efficient, accurate method to locate query-relevant regions before reasoning.
AI.method: Propose SpotVMR: a clip selector that searches for promising video regions conditioned on the language query; incorporate low-cost semantic indexing features to capture context (objects/interactions) guiding the search; use a distillation loss to stabilize end-to-end training of the clip selector with the VMR model; design as a plug-and-play module that can enhance state-of-the-art VMR methods.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three challenging datasets demonstrate the approach is effective, achieving efficiency gains while maintaining or improving retrieval performance when integrated with existing VMR models.
AI.conclusion: SpotVMR provides a practical solution to reduce boundary and reasoning biases in VMR by trimming to query-relevant clips. It serves as a versatile plug-in to boost efficiency for existing methods without sacrificing accuracy.

title: TRACER: Persistent Regularization for Robust Multimodal Finetuning
summary: Mainstream strategies for finetuning pretrained multimodal models often degrade out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness, a phenomenon known as catastrophic forgetting. In this paper, we develop a theoretical framework for multimodal contrastive finetuning, yielding closed-form solutions and a geometric decomposition for each strategy. This framework shows that self-distillation is more effective than other regularization approaches to retain the knowledge of the pretrained model. Our analysis reveals a largely overlooked limitation: standard Exponential Moving Average (EMA) teachers, widely used in robust finetuning, suffer from collapse. To solve this, we prove that a Weighted Moving Average (WMA) teacher maintains a persistent regularizing force over finite horizons and yields bias-free convergence in the task subspace while preserving orthogonal knowledge. These insights motivate **TRACER** (**T**rajectory-**R**obust **A**nchoring for **C**ontrastive **E**ncoder **R**egularization), which combines contrastive learning with WMA-guided multi-perspective distillation. Extensive experiments on CLIP finetuning demonstrate consistent OOD accuracy and calibration gains across three backbone architectures, and comprehensive ablations confirm that TRACER is both principled and robust to hyperparameter choices. Code is available at [https://github.com/HesamAsad/TRACER](https://github.com/HesamAsad/TRACER).

AI.tldr: TRACER introduces a Weighted Moving Average (WMA) teacher to address EMA collapse in multimodal contrastive finetuning, offering a theoretical framework and empirical gains in OOD robustness and calibration for CLIP finetuning.
AI.motivation: OOD robustness deteriorates during finetuning of pretrained multimodal models; standard strategies (e.g., EMA teachers, regularizers) can degrade robustness and cause catastrophic forgetting. A principled, stable distillation approach is needed to preserve pretrained knowledge while adapting to new tasks.
AI.method: Develop a theoretical framework for multimodal contrastive finetuning with closed-form solutions and geometric decomposition. Identify limitations of standard EMA teachers (collapse) and propose a Weighted Moving Average (WMA) teacher. Introduce TRACER, combining contrastive learning with WMA-guided multi-perspective distillation.
AI.result: WMA teacher provides persistent regularization over finite horizons, yields bias-free convergence in the task subspace, and preserves orthogonal knowledge. TRACER achieves consistent out-of-distribution accuracy and calibration gains across three backbone architectures for CLIP finetuning; extensive ablations show robustness to hyperparameters. Code is available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: TRACER presents a principled, robust regularization strategy for multimodal contrastive finetuning by tackling EMA collapse with a WMA teacher, improving OOD performance and calibration while remaining effective across backbones and hyperparameters.

title: VikingMem: A Memory Base Management System for Stateful LLM-based Applications
summary: Large Language Models have revolutionized interactive applications; however, their finite context windows pose a critical data management challenge for maintaining stateful, long-term interactions. Existing memory approaches often rely on simplistic extraction methods that lead to incomplete memories or use rigid, single-purpose memory extraction prompts tailored to a single use case, such as chatbots. Consequently, they lack generalizability and perform poorly across diverse downstream tasks. To bridge this gap, we introduce the Memory Base, a novel data management paradigm for managing the persistent state of long-term interactions. It is characterized by three core principles: selective extraction of high-value memories from raw information streams; inherent statefulness and evolution, where memory content is progressively summarized, corrected, and temporally weighted to prioritize recent interactions; and a generalizable abstraction paradigm designed for robust transferability across diverse applications, including education, recommendation, and agent memory. Building on this foundation, we present VikingMem, an end-to-end Memory Base Management System implemented on the VikingDB vector engine. VikingMem materializes this paradigm through interconnected event and entity abstractions. It features event-centric memory extraction to selectively handle complex information streams, while entities are dynamically updated by events to achieve stateful evolution. Using temporal compression via a topic-wise timeline and time-weighted recall, the system progressively produces high-level summary memories, prioritizes recent items, and compresses and fades older ones. Extensive evaluations on long-term memory benchmarks demonstrate that VikingMem outperformes baselines by up to 30% in memory retrieval effectiveness while maintaining the low latency essential for interactive applications.

AI.tldr: Memory Base and VikingMem introduce a generalizable memory management paradigm for long-term, stateful interactions under LLMs. They emphasize selective extraction, progressive stateful evolution, and transferability across domains, implemented on VikingDB with event/entity abstractions and temporal compression.
AI.motivation: Finite context windows limit long-term interactions; existing memory approaches are often brittle, non-generalizable, or use rigid prompts—failing to generalize across tasks.
AI.method: Propose Memory Base with event-centric memory extraction and dynamic entity updates. Implement VikingMem on VikingDB, using topic-wise timelines and time-weighted recall for progressive summaries and fading of old data. System is end-to-end with interconnected event/entity abstractions.
AI.result: Evaluations on long-term memory benchmarks show VikingMem improves memory retrieval effectiveness by up to 30% over baselines while maintaining low latency suitable for interactive applications.
AI.conclusion: Memory Base provides a generalizable framework for persistent state in long-term interactions, with transferability to domains like education and recommendation. VikingMem demonstrates effective selective extraction, temporal compression, and stateful evolution, enabling scalable, responsive memory systems.

title: Low-Magnification SEM May Suffice: Interpretable Deep Learning for Multi-Scale Fracture-Cause Classification in Zirconia-Toughened Alumina
summary: Reliable identification of fracture origins in alumina matrix composite hip and knee implants is critical for quality assurance and patient safety, yet current fractographic workflows are time-consuming, partly subjective, and reliant on high-magnification scanning electron microscopy (SEM). We present an interpretable vision-transformer (ViT) workflow for automated classification of fracture causes in an alumina matrix composite (BIOLOX delta, CeramTec GmbH) widely used in total joint replacements. A dataset of 8,493 SEM images (50x-10,000x) was curated from five years of in-production burst and proof tests and annotated into three defect categories defined along the manufacturing chain: green body, hard machining, and material defects. Under severe class imbalance, the fine-tuned ViT reached an accuracy of 0.907 and a macro-F1 of 0.888 in stratified five-fold cross-validation, with a two-stage perceptual-hash/SSIM leakage audit confirming negligible specimen overlap. Notably, performance at low magnification (50x) was comparable to that at high magnification (1k-10kx), indicating that macro-scale features - mirror geometry and hackle line fields - already encode sufficient diagnostic signal. Grad-CAM attributions consistently localised on canonical fractographic cues (mirrors, hackles, pores, machining marks), aligning with established fractographic criteria. Together, these results position interpretable ViTs as a complementary tool for ceramic-implant quality assurance, enabling low-magnification pre-screening and reducing reliance on time-intensive high-magnification inspection.

AI.tldr: A dataset of 8,493 SEM images for alumina matrix composite fractures; interpretable vision transformer classifies fracture origin into green body, hard machining, material defects; achieves 0.907 accuracy and 0.888 macro-F1 in stratified five-fold CV; low magnification (50x) performs as well as high magnification (1k–10k); Grad-CAM localizes on fractographic cues; leakage audit confirms no data leakage; supports ViT as a low-magnification pre-screening tool to reduce high-magnification inspection in quality assurance of ceramic implants.
AI.motivation: Improve reliability and efficiency of fracture origin identification in alumina matrix composites to enhance quality assurance and patient safety. Current fractography workflows are time-consuming and subjective, heavily dependent on high-magnification SEM; a scalable, interpretable model could pre-screen and guide manual analysis.
AI.method: Fine-tune a vision transformer (ViT) on 8,493 SEM images (50x–10,000x) labeled into three defect categories along the manufacturing chain: green body, hard machining, material defects. Address severe class imbalance. Evaluate with stratified five-fold cross-validation. Employ a two-stage perceptual-hash/SSIM leakage audit to ensure no specimen overlap between train/test sets. Use Grad-CAM to localize model attention to canonical fractographic cues.
AI.result: The ViT achieved 0.907 accuracy and 0.888 macro-F1 in stratified five-fold CV. Performance at low magnification (50x) was comparable to high magnification (1k–10k). Grad-CAM attributions localized on mirror geometry, hackle line fields, pores, and machining marks, consistent with established fractographic cues. The leakage audit confirmed negligible specimen overlap between train and test sets.
AI.conclusion: Interpretable ViTs can complement ceramic-implant quality assurance by enabling low-magnification pre-screening and reducing reliance on time-intensive high-magnification inspection, while providing interpretable localization to canonical fractographic features.

title: On the Optimizer Dependence of Neural Scaling Laws
summary: The scaling exponent $α$ in neural scaling laws $L(N) \propto N^{-α}$ is commonly treated as a fixed constant set by architecture and data. We present evidence that $α$ depends systematically on the optimizer. In controlled random-feature regression experiments -- the canonical theoretical framework for neural scaling -- we measure $α$ across five optimizer variants and six spectral conditions. Preconditioned optimizers consistently yield steeper scaling (larger $α$), with the $α$-shift increasing across most of the tested spectral range, peaking near $s = 1.5$, and remaining large at $s = 2.0$. At $s \approx 1.0$ (characteristic of natural language), the full natural gradient achieves $α\approx 0.31$ versus $α\approx 0.12$ for gradient descent -- a $2.6\times$ larger fitted exponent that, within the random-feature model, compounds with each model-size doubling. Whether and how this exponent shift transfers to large-scale LLM training -- where recent evidence suggests the advantage may attenuate with scale -- remains an important open question. Our results imply that scaling-law forecasts should account for optimizer choice, and we provide a spectral diagnostic predicting when advanced optimizers will pay off.

AI.tldr: The reported work shows that the neural scaling exponent α is not constant but depends on the optimizer and spectral conditions; preconditioned optimizers yield larger α, especially near certain spectral ranges, with natural gradient achieving substantially higher α than gradient descent at s≈1.0; these results in the random-feature regression setting imply optimizer choice should be included in scaling-law forecasts and offer a spectral diagnostic for when advanced optimizers are beneficial.
AI.motivation: Challenge the assumption that α is determined solely by architecture and data, and quantify how optimizer choice and spectral properties affect scaling exponents in neural scaling laws.
AI.method: Controlled random-feature regression experiments across five optimizers and six spectral conditions to measure the scaling exponent α of L(N) ∝ N^{-α}; analysis of α as a function of spectral parameter s and optimizer variant; comparison between natural gradient and gradient descent.
AI.result: Preconditioned optimizers yield steeper scaling (larger α); α shifts across spectral range and peaks near s≈1.5, remaining large at s=2.0; at s≈1.0 natural gradient gives α≈0.31 vs α≈0.12 for SGD, about 2.6× larger; in the random-feature model, larger α compounds with each model-size doubling.
AI.conclusion: Optimizer choice materially affects neural scaling exponents; a spectral diagnostic can predict when advanced optimizers will provide scaling benefits; forecasting should incorporate optimizer and spectral considerations, though transfer to large-scale LLMs remains an open question and may depend on scale.

title: LLM-Evolved Domain-Independent Heuristics for Symbolic AI Planning
summary: Heuristic search is the dominant paradigm in symbolic AI planning, and the strongest heuristics are the result of decades of work by planning researchers. Recent work has shown that large language models (LLMs) can design heuristics for individual planning domains, but no LLM-generated heuristic has so far worked on arbitrary planning tasks. In this paper, we use evolutionary search to produce the first LLM-generated domain-independent heuristics that exceed the hand-engineered state of the art. We let an LLM mutate parent heuristics written in C++, store candidates in a MAP-Elites archive keyed on informedness and speed and calculate fitness scores by blending coverage with solving time. To place the evolved programs in context, we additionally benchmark a broad set of hand-engineered heuristics on their informedness-speed tradeoff, which to our knowledge has not been done before. On unseen testing domains, our best evolved heuristic solves more tasks than even the strongest baseline, with our full heuristic suite spanning the Pareto frontier of said tradeoff. We also find that seeding evolution from the trivial blind heuristic outperforms seeding from the strong FF heuristic, even when the resulting program is itself an FF variant, and that LLM reasoning effort affects how often candidates compile much more than the quality of those that do. Because the evolved programs are plain C++, they slot into existing planners as drop-in replacements and inherit the soundness and completeness guarantees of the underlying search.

AI.tldr: LLM-generated, domain-independent heuristics for AI planning—evolved via MAP-Elites—outperform hand-engineered baselines on unseen domains, with a Pareto-frontier tradeoff between informedness and speed; seeding strategy and compilation effort significantly affect results; heuristics integrate as drop-in C++ in planners.
AI.motivation: Address the brittleness of domain-specific, hand-crafted heuristics in symbolic AI planning by creating domain-independent heuristics that generalize to arbitrary tasks; leverage LLMs to propose heuristics and evolutionary search to optimize their performance across the informedness-speed spectrum.
AI.method: Mutate LLM-generated C++ heuristics using MAP-Elites evolutionary search; maintain an archive keyed by informedness and speed; fitness combines coverage (task-solving) and solving time. Benchmark against a broad set of hand-engineered heuristics to map the informedness–speed tradeoff. Test on unseen domains. Compare seeding from the trivial blind heuristic versus the strong FF heuristic; analyze compile behavior.
AI.result: Best evolved heuristic solves more tasks on unseen domains than the strongest baseline; the full suite reaches the Pareto frontier of the informedness–speed tradeoff. Seeding from the blind heuristic outperforms seeding from FF, even when the resulting program is an FF variant. LLM reasoning effort correlates with compilation frequency rather than solution quality. The evolved C++ heuristics are drop-in replacements that preserve planner soundness and completeness.
