AI.conclusion: LLM-driven, domain-independent heuristics evolved via MAP-Elites can surpass hand-engineered heuristics and be readily integrated into existing planners; seeding strategy and compilation cost are important considerations; this approach establishes a scalable pathway to robust, general-purpose heuristics in symbolic planning.

title: Not All Inputs Are Valid: Towards Open-Set Video Moment Retrieval Using Language
summary: Video Moment Retrieval (VMR) targets to retrieve the specific moment corresponding to a sentence query from an untrimmed video. Although recent works have made remarkable progress in this task, they implicitly are rooted in the closed-set assumption that all the given queries as video-relevant\footnote{In this paper, we treat ``video-relevant query'' as ``in-distribution (ID) query'' and ``video-irrelevant query'' as ``out-of-distribution (OOD) query''.}. Given an OOD query in open-set scenarios, they still utilize it for wrong retrieval, which might lead to irrecoverable losses in high-risk scenarios, \textit{e.g.}, criminal activity detection. To this end, we creatively explore a brand-new VMR setting termed Open-Set Video Moment Retrieval (OS-VMR), where we should not only retrieve the precise moments based on ID query, but also reject OOD queries. In this paper, we make the first attempt to step toward OS-VMR and propose a novel model \textbf{OpenVMR}, which first distinguishes ID and OOD queries based on the normalizing flow technology, and then conducts moment retrieval based on ID queries. Specifically, we first learn the ID distribution by constructing a normalizing flow, and assume the ID query distribution obeys the multi-variate Gaussian distribution. Then, we introduce an uncertainty score to search the ID-OOD separating boundary. After that, we refine the ID-OOD boundary by pulling together ID query features. Besides, video-query matching and frame-query matching are designed for coarse-grained and fine-grained cross-modal interaction, respectively. Finally, a positive-unlabeled learning module is introduced for moment retrieval. Experimental results on three VMR datasets show the effectiveness of our OpenVMR.

AI.tldr: Proposes OpenVMR for open-set video moment retrieval by separating ID/OOD queries via normalizing flow and using positive-unlabeled learning for retrieval, with coarse- and fine-grained cross-modal interactions.
AI.motivation: Address the open-set problem in VMR where OOD queries can cause wrong retrieval and high-risk errors; need to reject OOD queries while retrieving ID moments.
AI.method: Model ID query distribution with a normalizing flow assuming a multivariate Gaussian; derive an uncertainty-based boundary to separate ID/OOD; refine boundary by clustering ID features; perform coarse video-query and fine frame-query interactions; apply positive-unlabeled learning for retrieval.
AI.result: Experimental results on three VMR datasets show OpenVMR effectively rejects OOD queries and retrieves moments for ID queries, outperforming baselines.
AI.conclusion: OpenVMR demonstrates the feasibility of OS-VMR and highlights the benefits of normalizing-flow based ID/OOD separation and positive-unlabeled retrieval in open-set VMR.

title: GDSD: Reinforcement Learning as Guided Denoiser Self-Distillation for Diffusion Language Models
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) can be used to improve the policy (denoiser) of diffusion large language models (dLLMs), while being hindered by the intractability of the policy likelihood. A dominant and efficient family of methods replaces the likelihood in standard RL with its evidence lower bound (ELBO), estimated from randomly masked sequences. Despite being well aligned with pre-training, these approaches introduce bias through training--inference mismatch by using the ELBO as a likelihood surrogate, which can degrade performance. In this work, we propose Guided Denoiser Self-Distillation (GDSD) to directly distill the denoiser of dLLMs from an advantage-guided self-teacher, derived from the closed-form optimum of reverse-KL regularized RL. GDSD matches the dLLM's denoiser logits to the teacher's via a normalization-free objective, which reduces RL to likelihood-free self-distillation and thus bypasses the TIM biases. Recent ELBO-based methods emerge as instances of applying different distillation divergences, but with diagnosable pathologies that GDSD avoids. On planning, math, and coding benchmarks with LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B, GDSD consistently outperforms prior state-of-the-art ELBO-based methods with a more stable training reward dynamics, achieving test-accuracy improvements of up to $+19.6\%$. These results suggest that direct denoiser self-distillation, without relying on an ELBO likelihood surrogate, can provide a more stable and effective RL procedure for dLLMs. Code is available at https://github.com/GaryBall/GDSD.

AI.tldr: GDSD bypasses ELBO-based RL surrogates by distilling dLLM denoisers from an advantage-guided self-teacher derived from reverse-KL RL, achieving improved performance and stability on math, planning, and coding benchmarks with LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B, outperforming prior ELBO methods by up to ~19.6%.
AI.motivation: Address training-inference bias and instability introduced by ELBO-based policy surrogates in diffusion-Language Models (dLLMs), which can degrade downstream performance.
AI.method: Derive a closed-form optimum of reverse-KL regularized RL to obtain an advantage-guided self-teacher; apply a normalization-free objective to match the dLLM denoiser logits to the teacher, turning RL into likelihood-free self-distillation and avoiding ELBO biases.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms prior ELBO-based methods across planning, math, and coding benchmarks; achieves test-accuracy gains up to 19.6% and exhibits more stable training reward dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Direct denoiser self-distillation without ELBO likelihood surrogates provides a more stable and effective RL framework for dLLMs; code is released for reproducibility.

title: Think Fast, Talk Smart: Partitioning Deterministic and Neural Computation for Structured Health Text Generation
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being used to generate health text from structured records such as wearable time series, biomarkers, vitals, and care-management logs. For recurring health outputs, fluency is not enough: systems must remain faithful to source data, ground explanatory claims in available evidence, follow stated policies, emit machine-readable outputs, and run cheaply enough for repeated use. We ask which responsibilities in structured health generation should be deterministic computation rather than runtime LLM prompting.
  We introduce Think Fast, Talk Smart, a sleep-health insight pipeline in which deterministic code performs recurring analysis before one bounded LLM writer call. Across 280 user-nights and six models, achieves lower numeric error, lower instruction-compliance error, and lower end-to-end cost than structured zero-shot and few-shot one-call baselines.
  Layer replacement reveals contract-specific failures: LLM comparison raises numeric error, LLM ranking degrades policy selection, LLM attribution increases unsupported causal language, and an LLM-generated writer interface reintroduces errors even after upstream facts are deterministic. The results support a broader design rule: let code own recurring analysis, and let LLMs express verified facts within bounded interfaces.

AI.tldr: Deterministic code handles recurring sleep-health data analysis with a single bounded LLM writer call, improving accuracy and cost vs zero-shot/few-shot baselines; reveals that letting code own recurring analysis and enforcing bounded interfaces yields more reliable health text from structured data.
AI.motivation: Health text generation from structured data must be faithful to source data, grounded in evidence, policy-compliant, machine-readable, and cost-effective for repeated use; relying solely on prompting risks errors and inefficiency.
AI.method: Introduce Think Fast, Talk Smart pipeline: a deterministic analysis stage runs before a single bounded LLM writer call to generate outputs. Evaluation across 280 user-nights and six LLM models, compared against structured zero-shot and few-shot one-call baselines, with layer replacement to diagnose component failures (numeric error, policy-ranking, attribution, and interface errors).
AI.result: Achieves lower numeric error, lower instruction-compliance error, and lower end-to-end cost than baselines across models. Layered analysis shows LLMs can introduce or amplify errors when placed in different layers; a bounded writer interface plus deterministic upstream analysis reduces such issues.
AI.conclusion: Let code own recurring analysis, and constrain LLMs to express verified facts within bounded interfaces. This design rule improves fidelity, compliance, and cost-effectiveness in structured health text generation.

title: Fairness Beyond Demographics: Optimizing Performance Across Appearance-Based Hidden Cohorts in Medical Imaging
comment: Pre-review version submitted to MICCAI 2026. 10 pages, 5 figures
summary: Medical image analysis models can exhibit performance disparities across patient subgroups, threatening clinical safety and fairness. Existing methods typically address this issue by optimizing accuracy and fairness metrics for visible demographic attributes (e.g., sex or age) considered in isolation. This strategy not only overlooks potentially more informative latent stratifications, which may reveal deeper sources of model error and inequity, but also fails to scale when multiple demographic attributes are considered simultaneously due to the resulting sparsity of training data within each subgroup. We deal with these issues by introducing the label-free hidden-cohort fairness (LHCF) training paradigm that instead of maximizing fairness over visible demographic attributes, it optimizes fairness across latent subpopulations discovered from image appearance. By clustering images into K appearance-based cohorts and applying fairness optimization over them, LHCF uncovers underlying sources of model error and avoids the combinatorial sparsity of multi-demographic attributes, reducing disparities across both single and multiple demographic attributes. We demonstrate on our proposed fairness benchmark, HIDFairBench, that LHCF provides state-of-the-art fairness results on single and multiple demographic attributes, despite never using demographic labels for training. Our results position hidden-cohort fairness as a practical, scalable, and robust alternative to demographic-based fairness optimization for trustworthy medical image analysis.

AI.tldr: Introduces LHCF, a label-free fairness training paradigm that clusters images into appearance-based cohorts and optimizes fairness across those latent cohorts, achieving state-of-the-art fairness across single and multi-attribute demographics without using demographic labels on HIDFairBench.
AI.motivation: Address performance disparities in medical image analysis across patient subgroups, highlighting limitations of using only visible demographic attributes and the combinatorial data sparsity when considering multiple attributes; latent, appearance-based subgroups may reveal deeper sources of error and inequity.
AI.method: Cluster images into K appearance-based cohorts (hidden cohorts) and apply fairness optimization over these cohorts in training, with no demographic labels used. The approach is evaluated on a proposed HIDFairBench benchmark.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art fairness results for both single and multiple demographic attributes, despite not using demographic labels for training; demonstrates robustness, scalability, and practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: Hidden-cohort fairness provides a practical, scalable, and robust alternative to demographic-based fairness optimization for trustworthy medical image analysis.

title: Rethinking Post-Training Recipes for Multimodal Time-Series Forecasting
summary: Time-Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) excel at zero-shot unimodal forecasting using numerical data, but unlike LLMs they cannot consume multimodal, non-numerical context that often shape real-world trajectories. In this work, we bridge this gap and argue for a multimodal time-series forecasting approach that post-trains LLMs to act as context-guided revisors over strong numerical TSFM priors. We introduce PostTime, a post-training recipe combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), along with a methodology to generate automated reasoning traces for forecast revisions. PostTime teaches an LLM to generate context-conditioned forecast interventions -- decisions to revise, preserve, or ignore the TSFM prior based on the multimodal context. We evaluate this approach on the TimesX multimodal forecasting benchmark using a Gemma-3-4B LLM and TimesFM-2.5 TSFM, and show that it significantly outperforms standalone TSFMs, LLM-only baselines, and existing multimodal forecasting approaches.

AI.tldr: PostTime post-trains a language model via supervised fine-tuning and RL with verifiable rewards to act as context-guided revisors of strong time-series priors, enabling multimodal forecasting improvements on TimesX.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between TSFMs' unimodal numerical forecasting and multimodal, non-numerical context shaping real trajectories; enable LLMs to intervene on priors using multimodal cues.
AI.method: PostTime combines SFT and RLVR; generates automated reasoning traces for forecast revisions; trains LLM to issue context-conditioned interventions (revise/preserve/ignore) to the TSFM prior; evaluation on TimesX with Gemma-3-4B and TimesFM-2.5.
AI.result: Significant improvement over standalone TSFMs, LLM baselines, and existing multimodal forecasting approaches on TimesX.
AI.conclusion: Context-guided LLM revisions are effective for multimodal time-series forecasting; the PostTime recipe shows promise for integrating LLMs with numeric priors to improve forecasts.

title: PTCG-Bench: Can LLM Agents Master Pokémon Trading Card Game?
summary: Given a strategically complex board game, human players can quickly learn to devise strategies after playing a few rounds. Autonomous agents require similar capabilities in realistic interactive environments, yet existing agent benchmarks often fail to fully capture such strategic and evolving decision-making scenarios. We present PTCG-Bench, a benchmark built on the Pok'{e}mon Trading Card Game (PTCG) that evaluates LLM agents at two complementary levels: (1) their decision-making performance within a single complex environment, and (2) their ability to self-evolving through accumulated experience. We further include a modular harness ablation to better interpret agent performance without conflating it with model capability. Our experiments show that, although LLM agents can achieve non-trivial gameplay performance, sustained and stable self-evolution remains challenging, and performance is sensitive to harness design. We hope that PTCG-Bench will facilitate future research on harness-aware and self-evolving agents in realistic interactive environments.

AI.tldr: PTCG-Bench benchmarks LLM agents in Pokémon TCG on two axes: one-shot decision-making in a complex environment and long-term self-evolution through experience, using a harness-ablation framework; results show meaningful play but limited, fragile self-improvement and strong harness sensitivity.
AI.motivation: Current agent benchmarks miss strategic depth and evolving behavior in interactive domains; need a benchmark that separates model capability from harness design and measures learning from accumulated gameplay.
AI.method: Build PTCG-Bench from Pokémon TCG; evaluate LLM agents at two levels (in-environment decision-making and experience-based evolution); introduce a modular harness with ablations to isolate effects; run experiments to assess performance and evolution.
AI.result: LLM agents achieve non-trivial gameplay within a single environment; sustained, stable self-evolution remains challenging; performance is sensitive to harness design.
AI.conclusion: PTCG-Bench enables harness-aware and self-evolving research in realistic interactive environments; suggests future work on improving self-evolution stability and harness robustness.

title: Parameter-Efficient Subspace Decoupling ViT for Mitigating Multi-Task Negative Transfer in Histological Scoring
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables. Accepted by IEEE ICME 2026. Camera-ready version
summary: Histological scoring is essential for diagnosing Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease (NAFLD), yet its automation remains challenging due to the high annotation cost and negative transfer among the strongly correlated NAFLD Activity Score (NAS) indicators in multi-task learning. To address this issue, we propose a subspace-decoupled multi-task Vision Transformer (ViT) that integrates lightweight task-specific Adapters with orthogonality-based constraints. This design constructs independent feature subspaces for steatosis, ballooning, and inflammation, effectively reducing task interference while retaining shared representations. We further construct a curated multi-task mouse NAFLD histology dataset with expert annotations for all NAS components. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method improves multi-task stability and generalization with substantially reduced computational cost compared to training separate single-task models. The code and the curated dataset have been prepared and will be made publicly available upon acceptance to support reproducibility.

AI.tldr: A subspace-decoupled multi-task ViT with adapters and orthogonality constraints for NAS component scoring in NAFLD histology; introduces a curated mouse dataset; shows improved multi-task stability/generalization with reduced cost; code/dataset to be released.
AI.motivation: High annotation cost for NAFLD histology and strong interdependence among NAS indicators causes negative transfer in multi-task learning; need decoupled yet shared representations.
AI.method: Vision Transformer with lightweight task-specific adapters for steatosis, ballooning, and inflammation; orthogonality-based constraints to enforce independent feature subspaces; curated mouse NAS dataset with expert annotations; evaluation against single-task baselines.
AI.result: Improved multi-task stability and generalization; reduced computational cost vs separate single-task models; dataset and code will be publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Subspace decoupling via adapters plus orthogonality constraints effectively mitigates negative transfer in NAS multi-task learning for histology; provides reproducible resources and a scalable framework for similar multi-task histology problems.

title: Information-Directed Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning
summary: Decision-making from offline datasets typically warm-starts a policy or score model from fixed offline data and then refines it with limited online interaction. Offline data reduces uncertainty, but it does not remove the need for exploration; it changes what remains to be explored. We formalise this residual uncertainty by the conditional mutual information $I(χ;τ_{1:T}\mid\mathcal{D}_N)$ between a learning target $χ$ and the online trajectories after conditioning on the offline dataset. This view leads naturally to information-directed sampling (IDS), a family parameterised by $η\ge 0$ that selects actions by trading off instantaneous regret against information gain. We prove a generic offline-to-online Bayesian regret bound for IDS through a ratio certificate: any information-ratio bound satisfied by a reference Thompson-sampling policy over the same randomised policy class is inherited by IDS. In a known-dynamics Bayesian linear-reward model, the conditional mutual information has a log-determinant form, and vanilla IDS ($η=0$) satisfies $\widetilde O\!\left(Hd\min\left\{\sqrt T,\,T\sqrt{C^\dagger_{β,\mathrm{IDS}_0}(N,T)/N}\right\}\right),$ where the coverage coefficient is tied to the visitation distribution induced by vanilla IDS itself. We also identify a warm-start regime with a dominated but informative probe in which vanilla IDS selects the probe while Thompson sampling never does, giving a constant-factor Bayesian regret separation. Controlled bandit experiments and D4RL offline-to-online RL experiments validate this mechanism: IDS is most beneficial when offline data is informative but leaves biased or low-probability residual uncertainty that targeted online actions can resolve, a regime shared by offline RL, offline black-box optimization, and Bayesian optimization.

AI.tldr: IDS combines offline data with information-theoretic exploration in offline-to-online RL; provides regret bounds via ratio certificates; shows warm-start regime and practical benefits in experiments.
AI.motivation: Reduce residual uncertainty after offline data by targeted information gain; improve online exploration efficiency.
AI.method: Define conditional mutual information I(χ; τ_{1:T} | D_N); propose IDS with parameter η; prove offline-to-online Bayesian regret bound via ratio certificate; in known-dynamics BLR, mutual information has log-det form; derive regret bounds; identify warm-start regime where IDS probes while Thompson sampling does not.
AI.result: Regret bound Ť(H d min{ sqrt T, T sqrt(C†_{β,IDS_0}(N,T)/N) }); constant-factor Bayesian regret separation in warm-start regime; empirical validation on controlled bandits and D4RL offline-to-online RL.
AI.conclusion: IDS is beneficial when offline data is informative but leaves residual uncertainty; links to offline RL and Bayesian optimization; highlights regimes where offline-to-online learning benefits most, and provides theoretical and empirical support.

title: TRACE: Toulmin-based Reasoning Assessment through Constructive Elements for LLM CoT Evaluation
summary: Evaluating open-ended outputs from large language models (LLMs) remains challenging due to the absence of ground truth. Existing metrics rely on final-answer accuracy or surface-level statistics, leaving the reasoning process itself unexamined. We introduce TRACE (Toulmin-based Reasoning Assessment through Constructive Elements), a metric that analyzes Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning processes. Rather than judging outcomes, TRACE inspects how arguments are constructed by integrating Toulmin's argumentation theory with Flavell's metacognitive framework to assess reasoning structure. Experiments on 26.3K QA samples across 7 reasoning models show strong correlation with benchmark accuracy (r=0.74). Furthermore, TRACE is effective as a reinforcement learning reward signal, outperforming accuracy-only baselines. Together, these results indicate that logically sound reasoning leads to higher-quality answers. TRACE thus serves as a complementary metric for evaluating open-ended outputs. Code is available at https://github.com/hyyangkisti/trace.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a Toulmin+Flavell-based metric to evaluate chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs by analyzing constructive elements of reasoning; it correlates with accuracy (r=0.74) and can serve as an RL reward signal; code is available on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Evaluating open-ended outputs from LLMs is hard due to absence of ground truth. Existing metrics focus on final answers or surface stats and neglect the reasoning process.
AI.method: TRACE analyzes CoT reasoning by mapping argument construction to Toulmin's argumentation theory (claims, data/evidence, warrants, backing, rebuttals, qualifiers) and Flavell's metacognitive framework (planning, monitoring, evaluating) to assess the reasoning structure. It is validated on 26.3K QA samples across 7 reasoning models.
AI.result: TRACE shows a strong correlation with benchmark accuracy (r=0.74) and serves as an effective RL reward signal, outperforming accuracy-only baselines.
AI.conclusion: TRACE provides a complementary metric for evaluating open-ended outputs by focusing on reasoning quality; code available at the linked GitHub repository.

title: Building and Road Recognition in Dense Urban Informal Settlements: A Dataset and Benchmark
summary: As a widespread form of informal settlements, urban villages present significant challenges for sustainable urban development and governance. Precise mapping of their infrastructure is essential, however, existing remote sensing datasets primarily focus on formal urban environments, lacking fine-grained annotated data for the high-density building patterns and narrow road networks typical of urban villages. To address this gap, we introduce the \textit{DenseUIS} dataset, the first high-resolution remote sensing dataset specifically designed for building and road extraction in extremely dense urban informal settlements, covering 126 urban villages across Shenzhen and Guangzhou in China. Furthermore, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of state-of-the-art deep learning models on this dataset. Experimental results reveal the limitations of existing methods in handling the unique morphological patterns of dense informal settlements, underscoring the need for specialized approaches. \textit{DenseUIS} therefore provides a robust benchmark for advancing fine-grained urban mapping in complex and high-density informal environments. The dataset is publicly available at https://github.com/rui-research/DenseUIS.

AI.tldr: DenseUIS is a high-resolution remote sensing dataset for building and road extraction in extremely dense urban informal settlements (urban villages) across 126 villages in Shenzhen/Guangzhou, China; it benchmarks state-of-the-art models and highlights the need for specialized methods; the dataset is publicly available.
AI.motivation: Addresses a data gap: existing remote sensing datasets focus on formal urban areas and fail to capture fine-grained morphology and road networks in dense informal settlements, hindering accurate mapping and governance.
AI.method: Construction of a high-resolution RS dataset with ground-truth annotations for buildings and roads across 126 urban villages; evaluation of current deep learning models to assess performance and limitations on this morphology.
AI.result: State-of-the-art models exhibit limited performance on dense informal settlement morphology, revealing their inadequacy without morphology-aware or data-specific strategies; DenseUIS provides a challenging benchmark for advancement.
AI.conclusion: DenseUIS fills a crucial gap in high-resolution urban mapping of informal settlements and will spur development of specialized methods; dataset is publicly accessible at the provided repository.

title: The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly of Markov Boundary for Tabular Prediction
summary: Under standard graphical assumptions, the Markov boundary of a target variable is the smallest set of features that renders every other feature redundant. Once the boundary is observed, the target is conditionally independent of the rest of the table. This is a tempting object for tabular prediction, since it names exactly the columns a model should need. Yet modern regressors are still trained on the full feature set. We ask whether the Markov boundary is genuinely useful for prediction on SCM3K, a 3,450-task synthetic SCM benchmark with feature counts from 40 to 1000 and six SCM families, evaluated with six regressors. The answer is more nuanced than the theory suggests. Restricting a regressor to the oracle boundary often improves prediction substantially, and the improvement grows as the feature space becomes larger and sparser. But the natural pipeline of recovering the boundary with causal discovery and training on the recovered mask does not deliver. Existing estimators exhaust the compute budget before reaching the regime where the boundary helps most, and even where they run they rarely beat the full feature set. We trace this to three causes. Discovery optimizes structural recovery rather than prediction. False negatives and false positives carry sharply asymmetric predictive cost. The exact boundary is only one of many feature sets that beat all features. We then develop what these facts imply for prediction-aligned feature selection and for tabular models that learn to use causal structure.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether the Markov boundary can improve tabular prediction. Using a large synthetic SCM benchmark (SCM3K), it finds that restricting predictors to the oracle boundary can boost accuracy in high-dimensional, sparse settings, but attempting to recover the boundary via causal discovery is computationally expensive and often not advantageous, due to structure-focused discovery, asymmetric costs of errors, and the existence of multiple predictive feature sets.
AI.motivation: To assess the practical usefulness of the Markov boundary for prediction on large, real-like tabular data and to understand why its theoretical appeal may not translate into consistent predictive gains when using standard causal discovery pipelines.
AI.method: Evaluate prediction performance on SCM3K (3,450 tasks, 40–1000 features, six SCM families) using six regressors. Compare oracle boundary restriction versus full feature set. Attempt boundary recovery via causal discovery, analyze computational cost, and diagnose failures. Identify three causes for the gap between theory and practice.
AI.result: Oracle boundary restriction often improves predictive performance, especially as feature space grows and becomes sparser. However, recovering the boundary with causal discovery does not deliver practical gains: estimators exhaust compute budgets and, when run, rarely beat the full feature set.
AI.conclusion: For prediction-focused feature selection and causal-structure–aware tabular models, focus on predictive cost and multiple effective feature sets rather than the exact Markov boundary; current boundary-recovery pipelines are misaligned with predictive goals.

title: GRASP: Gated Regression-Aware Skill Proposer for Self-Improving LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents acting in structured environments fail in operational rather than conversational ways, and reliability depends on procedural knowledge of the environment. Prior self-improvement methods accumulate natural-language guidance without checking that each new item preserves previously correct behavior, so a note that fixes one trajectory can silently regress another. We introduce GRASP (Gated Regression-Aware Skill Proposer), which treats agent improvement as a sequence of edits to a bounded skill library, admitting each candidate only if it produces a net improvement on a balanced held-out probe under a hard regression budget. We evaluate GRASP across five base models (gpt-oss-120b, DeepSeek V4 Flash, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite, GPT-4.1, GPT-5.4) on two FHIR-based clinical benchmarks. On MedAgentBench, GRASP lifts gpt-oss-120b from 40.6% to 88.8%, exceeds the strongest of five self-improvement baselines by 21.0 points, and improves every other base model by 17.2 to 40.3 points. Ablations attribute the gain to comparative proposal generation, the acceptance gate, and the hard regression budget rather than to skill writing itself, which without validation is no better than using no skills. The mechanism generalizes beyond the clinical domain, improving agents on three of four non-clinical environments and remaining flat only where the action space is open-ended. Frozen libraries transfer across models, where skills from a stronger model improve weaker executors beyond what they learn for themselves while the reverse does not, an asymmetry that no ungated baseline reproduces.

AI.tldr: GRASP improves LLM agent reliability by gating iterative skill edits with a regression budget and a held-out validation probe, yielding strong gains across clinical and some non-clinical domains; skill writing alone is not sufficient without validation and a gating mechanism; frozen, model-agnostic libraries enable cross-model transfer of skills.
AI.motivation: LLM agents acting in structured environments rely on procedural knowledge; naive self-improvement via unvalidated edits can cause silent regressions. A controlled, validated update mechanism is needed to ensure net improvements without breaking existing correct behavior.
AI.method: Treat agent improvement as edits to a bounded skill library. Each candidate edit is accepted only if it yields a net improvement on a balanced held-out probe, under a hard regression budget that limits negative impact. Evaluate across five base models on two FHIR-based clinical benchmarks (MedAgentBench and others); perform ablations to identify contributing components; test transfer by freezing libraries and applying to other models and non-clinical environments.
AI.result: GRASP substantially improves performance: on MedAgentBench, gpt-oss-120b rises from 40.6% to 88.8%; outperforms five baselines by 21.0 points; other base models gain 17.2–40.3 points. Ablations show gains from comparative proposal generation, acceptance gate, and regression budget rather than skill writing alone. The mechanism generalizes to three of four non-clinical environments, with flat performance where action space is open-ended. Frozen libraries transfer across models, enabling stronger models to boost weaker executors more than vice versa, a pattern not reproduced by ungated baselines.
AI.conclusion: GRASP's gating, regression budget, and validation-driven acceptance are key to reliable agent improvement; skill curation with validation is essential, and the approach generalizes beyond clinical domains, though gains depend on action space structure. The transferability of frozen libraries suggests cross-model knowledge reuse as a powerful design principle.

title: Masked Diffusion Vision-Language Models for Temporal Action Localization
summary:
Temporal action localization (TAL) requires recognizing the target event and localizing its start and end times precisely in untrimmed videos. Recent vision-language formulations improve semantic reasoning and support language-conditioned outputs, but their autoregressive decoders still generate tokens from left to right, preventing later semantic evidence from revising earlier timestamp predictions.
  We adapt masked diffusion vision-language models (MDVLMs) to TAL so that semantic tokens and boundary tokens remain editable throughout iterative denoising with bidirectional attention, allowing temporal boundaries and semantic content to be refined jointly.
  Direct adaptation, however, creates two TAL-specific mismatches: standard masked diffusion training corrupts all positions uniformly at random, but the time tokens are more reliable when enough semantic context is available; and token-level cross-entropy does not reflect temporal IoU. To address these mismatches, we introduce a Planned Training Objective that uses boundary-aware masking and step-weighted reconstruction to rehearse the late recovery of time tokens, together with a Step-Level IoU Reward that provides overlap-aware supervision during denoising. A standard sequence-level cross-entropy term provides the base reconstruction signal.
  Experiments on ActivityNet-RTL, ActivityNet-1.3, and THUMOS-14 show that MDVLM-TAL improves both temporal reasoning and boundary localization over autoregressive vision-language baselines, with especially strong gains under stricter temporal IoU criteria.

title: How Much Is a Dataset Worth? Scaling Laws, the Vendi Score, and Matrix Spectral Functions
summary: Neural scaling laws appraise data through dataset size, while the Vendi Score uses quantum entropy to measure dataset value. We show both that common neural-scaling-law objectives and the Vendi Score are submodular. We further show that the Vendi Score is a special case of a broader class of submodular objectives that we call matrix spectral functions. This also includes determinantal (DPP) objectives, as well as many others. We also introduce weakly matrix monotone functions and show how they lead to weakly submodular matrix spectral functions, yielding a broad family of practical objectives for data appraisal. We develop secular-equation-based updates that avoid repeated eigendecompositions during greedy optimization, reducing marginal-gain evaluation for $m$-dimensional embeddings by an $O(m)$ factor relative to oracle queries. This yields an average empirical speedup of about 35,000x, making direct optimization of the Vendi Score feasible on ImageNet-1K-scale datasets. Thus enabled, we compare how well several objectives predict the value of training subsets for held-out test performance under fixed-size, class-balanced, and fixed training-budget regimes, including the Vendi Score, DPPs, facility location, and three new matrix spectral variants. Across multiple datasets, facility location performs the best. Direct optimization also reveals that, while the Vendi Score is predictive over moderate score ranges, pushing the objective to higher values can make it a poor downstream performance proxy. We also find that uniformly at random fixed-size subsets, both unconstrained and class-balanced, are remarkably concentrated in both appraisal scores and held-out performance. Finally, we show that size, class balance, and training budget do not alone determine data value: even when controlling for these factors, performance ranges smoothly from good to bad.

AI.tldr: Neural scaling laws and the Vendi Score are shown to be submodular; the authors unify them under matrix spectral functions, introduce weakly matrix monotone functions, and derive fast secular-equation updates enabling scalable optimization. Empirically, facility location often best; higher Vendi values don't guarantee downstream performance; random fixed-size subsets show concentration; data value depends on factors beyond size, balance, and budget.
AI.motivation: to understand and compare data-valuation objectives through submodularity, broaden to matrix spectral functions, and enable scalable optimization on large datasets for reliable data value estimation.
AI.method: theoretical analysis proving submodularity; define matrix spectral functions and weakly matrix monotone; derive secular-equation-based optimization updates; implement to compare across objectives (Vendi Score, DPPs, facility location, matrix spectral variants) on ImageNet-1K-scale datasets; hold-out performance evaluation under fixed-size, class-balanced, fixed budget regimes; analyze concentration of random subsets.
AI.result: Vendi Score is a special case of matrix spectral objectives; a broad family; secular updates reduce marginal-gain eval by O(m); average speedup ~35,000x; direct optimization feasible on ImageNet-1K; facility location performs best; Vendi Score predictive only over moderate score ranges; random subsets show concentration; size/balance/budget do not solely determine data value; performance ranges from good to bad even controlling for these factors.
AI.conclusion: matrix spectral framework subsumes DPPs and others; enables practical data-valuation optimization; decisions should consider multiple objectives; rely on facility location for best empirical performance; caution in interpreting Vendi Score as universal predictor.

title: Forget Less, Generalize More: Unifying Temporal and Structural Adaptation for Dynamic Graphs
summary: Representation learning on dynamic graphs requires capturing complex dependencies that evolve across both time and structure. Existing approaches typically adopt fixed temporal decay schemes or predetermined structural propagation depths, limiting their ability to generalize across graphs with diverse interaction frequencies and topological characteristics. We propose Dual-Scale Retentive Dynamics (DSRD), a unified framework that maintains a retentive representation state encoding both temporal memory and structural context. DSRD introduces two key components: (i) a retentive state with dual-scale adaptation that jointly models temporal dynamics and structural propagation within a single recurrent formulation, and (ii) adaptive decay kernels with learnable time-sensitivity parameters that automatically balance short-term responsiveness and long-term retention based on the underlying interaction patterns. We provide theoretical analysis establishing the equivalence between event-wise parallel aggregation and efficient recurrent state updates, as well as stability and boundedness guarantees for the learned dynamics. Extensive experiments on 14 real-world benchmarks demonstrate that DSRD consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance on both link prediction and node classification tasks, with strong generalization across transductive and inductive settings.

AI.tldr: DSRD introduces a dual-scale retentive state for dynamic graphs with learnable adaptive decay, enabling state-of-the-art results on link prediction and node classification across 14 benchmarks, supported by theoretical guarantees.
AI.motivation: Dynamic graphs exhibit diverse temporal interaction frequencies and topologies; fixed temporal decay and fixed propagation depths hinder generalization. A unified, memory-augmented framework with adaptive time sensitivity is needed.
AI.method: DSRD uses a retentive state encoding both temporal memory and structural context within a single recurrent formulation, augmented by adaptive decay kernels with learnable time-sensitivity parameters. It establishes equivalence between event-wise parallel aggregation and efficient recurrent updates, plus stability/boundedness guarantees.
AI.result: Theoretical results show equivalence and stability guarantees; empirical evaluation on 14 real-world benchmarks reports state-of-the-art performance for link prediction and node classification, with good transductive and inductive generalization.
AI.conclusion: DSRD offers a flexible, scalable, and theoretically grounded framework for dynamic graph representation learning, balancing short-term responsiveness and long-term retention across diverse interaction patterns.

title: NICE: A Theory-Grounded Diagnostic Benchmark for Social Intelligence of LLMs
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in social contexts such as emotional companionship and customer service, measuring their social intelligence has become critical to the quality and safety of human-AI interaction. However, existing social intelligence benchmarks lack a unified framework that organizes social abilities into a unified structure, and therefore cannot enable fine-grained diagnosis. To build the first holistic diagnostic evaluation grounded in social theory, we first construct a social intelligence framework through a literature review and multi-stage expert validation guided by psychometric principles. The resulting framework includes 4 categories and 11 dimensions, each further specified by fine-grained capability facets. Building on this framework, we introduce NICE (Norm, Interaction, Cognition, Experience), a diagnostic benchmark of 137 items operationalized through representative Chinese contexts. Across 5 frontier LLMs and a human reference group, models score higher in aggregate accuracy yet show a consistent weakness in Communication, which the framework localizes to 3 specific capability facets: multi-turn communication, nonverbal communication, and synchrony. NICE thus reframes social intelligence evaluation toward theory-grounded diagnosis of socially consequential weaknesses in LLMs.

AI.tldr: Introduces NICE, a theory-grounded social intelligence diagnostic benchmark for LLMs, built on a 4-category, 11-dimension framework with 137 items in Chinese contexts; across 5 frontier LLMs and a human reference, models show higher aggregate accuracy but consistent weakness in Communication, localized to multi-turn, nonverbal, and synchrony facets.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of a unified, theory-grounded framework for diagnosing social intelligence in LLMs and enable fine-grained assessment beyond aggregate accuracy.
AI.method: Develop a social intelligence framework via literature review and multi-stage expert validation guided by psychometric principles, defining 4 categories, 11 dimensions, and facet-level capabilities; operationalize 137 items in representative Chinese contexts; evaluate 5 frontier LLMs plus a human reference group.
AI.result: Models achieve higher aggregate accuracy but exhibit a consistent weakness in Communication, localized to three facets: multi-turn communication, nonverbal communication, and synchrony.
AI.conclusion: NICE reframes social intelligence evaluation as a theory-grounded diagnostic tool, enabling targeted identification of socially consequential weaknesses in LLMs and facilitating precise, facet-level improvements.

title: Mitigating Hallucination in Vision-Language Models through Barrier-Regulated Adaptive Closed-form Steering
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) often hallucinate objects that are not present in the input image, largely because visual grounding weakens as decoding progresses. Existing inference-time mitigation methods modify logits or hidden states throughout generation, but they suffer from three key limitations: they lack an explicit grounding objective, intervene even when the model is already well-grounded, and use fixed correction strengths that do not adapt to the severity of grounding failure. We propose BRACS (Barrier-Regulated Adaptive Closed-form Steering), a training-free steering framework that addresses these issues through barrier-regulated adaptive closed-form steering. BRACS monitors the model's own attention to measure visual grounding and applies corrections to the hidden states only when grounding deteriorates. The corrective update is computed analytically in closed form, requiring no training of auxiliary networks or model retraining. Experiments on LLaVA-1.5-7B and Qwen-VL-Chat show that BRACS consistently outperforms prior methods on hallucination benchmarks, reducing CHAIR$_s$ by 9.4 points and improving POPE F1 by 2.7 points, while matching or improving performance on four general multimodal benchmarks. BRACS also remains efficient, operating at 80% of greedy decoding throughput and achieving 1.3 times higher speed on average than the baselines.

AI.tldr: A training-free, barrier-regulated adaptive closed-form steering (BRACS) reduces hallucinations in LVLMs by monitoring visual grounding via attention and applying analytically computed hidden-state corrections only when grounding deteriorates.
AI.motivation: LVLMs frequently hallucinate objects not in the image because grounding weakens during decoding; existing inference-time mitigations lack explicit grounding objectives, act indiscriminately, and use fixed correction strengths.
AI.method: BRACS uses barrier-regulated adaptive closed-form steering: it monitors the model’s attention to assess grounding, and when grounding deteriorates it applies a corrective update to hidden states computed analytically in closed form, without training auxiliary networks or retraining the model.
AI.result: BRACS outperforms prior methods on hallucination benchmarks (reducing CHAIR_s by 9.4 points and increasing POPE F1 by 2.7), while maintaining or improving performance on four general multimodal benchmarks; it operates at ~80% of greedy decoding throughput and is ~1.3× faster on average than baselines.
AI.conclusion: BRACS provides an efficient, training-free mitigation that adaptively corrects grounding failures, improving hallucination metrics with minimal overhead and compatible performance across diverse multimodal tasks.

title: A Full-Pipeline Framework for Evaluating Membership Inference Attacks in Machine Learning
summary: While Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) are the prevailing method for identifying training data, their application has expanded into privacy auditing and machine unlearning. Nevertheless, the field lacks a systematic framework for evaluating how different contexts affect MIA efficacy. Without such a characterization, practitioners risk deploying algorithms that perform well on benchmarks but become statistically irrelevant when faced with the nuances of specific, real-world datasets. To bridge this gap and provide actionable insights, we introduce a comprehensive evaluation framework that systematically characterizes privacy risks across the entire machine learning pipeline, spanning data, architectures, algorithms, and post-training modules. Designed to inherently capture diverse operational contexts, our framework rigorously evaluates state-of-the-art MIAs across a broad spectrum of training configurations. To account for varying misclassification costs in real-world deployments, we employ three complementary metrics: Balanced Accuracy for symmetric costs, alongside TPR at low FPR (or TNR at low FNR) for asymmetric scenarios where false alarms or missed detections are strictly penalized. Furthermore, recognizing that existing MIAs assume divergent adversary capabilities, we formalize two standardized threat models and adapt these attacks into corresponding variants to ensure an equitable benchmark. Extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that the efficacy of specific MIA methodologies is highly sensitive to the assumed threat models and chosen evaluation metrics. Ultimately, we distill these findings into actionable guidelines and provide a ready-to-use auditing toolkit, empowering practitioners to conduct better privacy assessments.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive framework to benchmark membership inference attacks (MIAs) across data, architectures, algorithms, and post-training steps, using multiple metrics and standardized threat models; includes guidelines and an auditing toolkit.
AI.motivation: Current MIA evaluations are fragmented and benchmark-centric, failing to capture real-world variability and misclassification costs; there is a need for systematic privacy-risk characterization along the full ML pipeline.
AI.method: Proposes a unified evaluation framework spanning data, model architectures, training algorithms, and post-training modules; defines three metrics (Balanced Accuracy; TPR at low FPR/TNR at low FNR); formalizes two standardized threat models and adapts MIAs accordingly; performs extensive empirical studies across training configurations; delivers actionable guidelines and a ready-to-use auditing toolkit.
AI.result: Shows that MIA effectiveness is highly sensitive to threat model and chosen metric, challenging one-size-fits-all conclusions; provides robust, context-aware insights and practical tooling for privacy auditing.
AI.conclusion: Offers practical guidelines for practitioners to conduct privacy assessments and supports standardized benchmarking of MIAs, enabling better privacy-aware decision making in model deployment and unlearning contexts.

title: Reliable Reasoning with Large Language Models via Preference-Based Maximum Satisfiability
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at understanding natural language but struggle with optimisation tasks involving multiple constraints and user-defined preferences, which commonly arise in domains such as robotics. We propose a hybrid reasoning approach in which LLMs externalise reasoning through code generation. Given a natural language problem description, an LLM generates Python code that encodes user-defined constraints and preferences as a preference-based Maximum Satisfiability (MaxSAT) problem, which is then solved by an exact MaxSAT solver. To ensure correctness, solutions returned by the model-generated code are independently verified for feasibility and optimality against a canonical MaxSAT encoding, allowing for different encodings and multiple optimal solutions. We evaluate our approach using both open-source and closed-access LLMs on three families of preference-based reasoning tasks, and compare it against direct-answer, chain-of-thought, and program-of-thought baselines using the same models. While these baselines rarely produce feasible solutions, the MaxSAT-based pipeline achieves substantially higher acceptance rates, in some cases exceeding 80%. Our results demonstrate that LLM-driven code generation combined with preference-based MaxSAT enables solver-verifiable optimisation with respect to generated encodings, and substantially improves correctness under independently verified reference semantics.

AI.tldr: An LLM-based pipeline externalizes reasoning by generating Python code that encodes a problem as a preference-based MaxSAT, which an exact solver resolves; independent verification against a canonical encoding yields higher feasibility and optimality than baselines.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with multi-constraint, user-specific optimization tasks (e.g., robotics). A verifiable, solver-backed approach can improve correctness and reliability by delegating formal optimization to an exact solver while using the LLM to translate user intent into encodings.
AI.method: The LLM emits Python code encoding constraints into a preference-based MaxSAT problem. An exact MaxSAT solver solves it. The produced solutions are independently verified for feasibility and optimality against a canonical MaxSAT encoding, allowing different encodings and multiple optima. Evaluations compare against direct-answer, chain-of-thought, and program-of-thought baselines using the same models across three task families.
AI.result: The MaxSAT-based pipeline achieves substantially higher acceptance/feasibility rates than baselines, with some experiments exceeding 80%. This demonstrates solver-verifiable optimization with respect to generated encodings and improved correctness under independently verified semantics.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven code generation combined with preference-based MaxSAT enables solver-verifiable optimisation and markedly improves correctness over common reasoning baselines across varying encodings and goal structures.

title: Honest Lying: Understanding Memory Confabulation in Reflexive Agents
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 Workshop "Failure Modes in Agentic AI"
summary: Reflexion-style agents rely on self-generated reflections as memory, implicitly assuming that agents can accurately diagnose their own failures.We show that this assumption can fail systematically: across ALFWorld and HumanEval, agents store confident but incorrect interpretations of the task and continue acting on them across trials,even though the environment resets to the correct task each time. We call this failure mode memory confabulation and introduce the Reflection Repetition Rate (RRR), a log-based metric that detects repeated reliance on incorrect reflective content.Using RRR, we identify 16 frozen environments in ALFWorld, where 0 of 121 reflections mention the correct target object, and 4 analogous cases in HumanEval. Our mitigation replaces open-ended self-diagnosis with programmatic extraction of trajectory-level failure signals, increasing correct object mention from 0% to 86%, reducing RRR from 0.64 to 0.10, and solving 3 of 16 frozen ALFWorld environments, suggesting that reflective memory can reinforce false beliefs rather than correct them.

AI.tldr: Reflexion-style agents can suffer memory confabulation, where confident but incorrect self-diagnoses persist across trials; a log-based metric (RRR) detects this, and replacing open-ended self-diagnosis with trajectory-level failure signals markedly improves performance and reduces confabulation.
AI.motivation: Test the assumption that self-generated reflections are reliable for failure diagnosis in reflexion-style agents and quantify the risk of memory confabulation across tasks/environments.
AI.method: Compute a log-based Reflection Repetition Rate (RRR) to detect repeated reliance on incorrect reflective content. Analyze ALFWorld and HumanEval to identify instances where reflections fail to mention the correct target. Propose mitigation by replacing open-ended self-diagnosis with programmatic extraction of trajectory-level failure signals, and evaluate impact.
AI.result: Memory confabulation observed: 16 frozen ALFWorld environments with 0/121 reflections mentioning the correct target; 4 analogous cases in HumanEval. After mitigation, correct object mentions rise from 0% to 86%, RRR drops from 0.64 to 0.10, and 3 of 16 frozen ALFWorld environments are solved.
AI.conclusion: Reflective memory can reinforce false beliefs rather than correct them. The RRR metric reveals this failure mode, and trajectory-level failure signals provide a robust mitigation, though generalization and broader task settings require further study.

title: FHRFormer: A Self-Supervised Masked Transformer Framework for Fetal Heart Rate Time-Series Inpainting and Forecasting
comment: Submitted to Frontiers in Digital Health. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2509.20852
summary: Approximately 10% of newborns require assistance to initiate breathing at birth, and around 5% need ventilation support. Fetal heart rate (FHR) monitoring plays a crucial role in assessing fetal well-being during prenatal care, enabling the detection of abnormal patterns and supporting timely obstetric interventions to mitigate fetal risks during labor. Applying artificial intelligence (AI) methods to analyze large datasets of continuous FHR monitoring episodes with diverse outcomes may offer novel insights into predicting the risk of needing breathing assistance or interventions. Recent advances in wearable FHR monitors have enabled continuous fetal monitoring without compromising maternal mobility. However, sensor displacement during maternal movement, as well as changes in fetal or maternal position, often lead to signal dropout, resulting in gaps in recorded FHR data. Such missing data limits the extraction of meaningful insights and complicates automated (AI-based) analysis. Traditional approaches to handling missing data, such as simple interpolation techniques, often fail to preserve the spectral characteristics of the signals. In this paper, we propose a masked transformer-based autoencoder approach to reconstruct missing FHR signals by capturing both local temporal and frequency components of the data. The proposed method demonstrates robustness across varying durations of missing data and can be used for signal inpainting and forecasting. The proposed approach can be applied retrospectively to research datasets to support the development of AI-based risk algorithms. In the future, the proposed method could be integrated into wearable FHR monitoring devices to achieve earlier and more robust risk detection.

AI.tldr: Masked transformer-based autoencoder for FHR signal inpainting and forecasting to handle missing data in wearable fetal monitoring, preserving temporal and spectral features and enabling AI risk modeling.
AI.motivation: Missing FHR data due to sensor displacement and movement; traditional interpolation fails to preserve spectral characteristics; need robust reconstruction to enable AI-based prediction of breathing assistance risk.
AI.method: Masked transformer-based autoencoder capturing local temporal and frequency components; performs inpainting and forecasting; robust to varying lengths of missing data; can be applied retrospectively to datasets and integrated into wearables.
AI.result: Proposed method demonstrates robustness across varying durations of missing data; supports signal inpainting and forecasting; applicable to retrospective datasets and future wearables.
AI.conclusion: Promising for improving reliability of FHR-based risk detection; potential integration into devices for earlier risk detection and AI-based risk algorithms.

title: Train the Agent, Not the Expert: Learning to Harness Heterogeneous Experts for Multi-Turn Visual Reasoning
summary: Recent progress in computer vision has produced a wide range of powerful specialized models for detection, segmentation, counting, and other visual tasks. However, these models are usually optimized for isolated task formulations, making it difficult to directly support general-purpose visual intelligence, especially when a task requires complex language understanding and dense small-object perception. In this paper, we propose VisHarness, a trainable visual agent that decouples high-level perception, reasoning, and decision-making from low-level task execution. Instead of training a model to solve a specific visual task, VisHarness learns to harness a set of carefully designed heterogeneous visual experts. This paradigm preserves the general intelligence of the agent while fully leveraging the precision advantages of specialized visual models in concrete visual tasks. With only lightweight training, VisHarness learns a generalizable visual expert-harnessing policy and can solve common fundamental vision tasks under various complex conditions through multi-turn interactions with visual expert models. To enable efficient on-policy reinforcement learning training in a live environment, we introduce dynamic visual memory archiving, which mitigates the rapidly accumulating visual-token overhead caused by multi-turn interactions with visual expert models. Experiments on four representative benchmarks covering reasoning segmentation, generalized referring segmentation, dense small-object detection, and referring counting demonstrate that VisHarness substantially outperforms existing general-purpose models and achieves competitive or superior performance compared with task-specific models.

AI.tldr: VisHarness is a trainable visual agent that orchestrates heterogeneous specialized visual experts via a learned harness, enabling general-purpose visual intelligence with lightweight training and multi-turn expert interactions; it introduces dynamic visual memory archiving to manage token overhead and shows strong results across diverse vision tasks.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of task-specific vision models and enable general-purpose visual intelligence, especially for tasks requiring complex language understanding and dense small-object perception, by leveraging specialized experts through a learnable harness.
AI.method: Decouple high-level perception, reasoning, and decision-making from low-level execution; learn a generalizable expert-harnessing policy through on-policy RL; enable multi-turn interactions with diverse visual experts; introduce dynamic visual memory archiving to mitigate rapidly accumulating visual tokens in live environments.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four benchmarks—reasoning segmentation, generalized referring segmentation, dense small-object detection, and referring counting—demonstrates that VisHarness substantially outperforms existing general-purpose models and is competitive or superior to task-specific models.
AI.conclusion: VisHarness provides a generalizable visual agent that effectively leverages specialized visual models to achieve broad visual competence with lightweight training, and its dynamic memory mechanism enables efficient on-policy learning in live settings.

title: Composing Non-Conjugate Factor Graphs with Closed-Form Variational Inference
summary: Stacking probabilistic building blocks into deeper architectures typically breaks closed-form inference. We show that closed-form inference can be preserved. We identify five factor-graph primitives: a bilinear factor, an exponential link, a Gamma prior, a Gaussian likelihood, and an equality node, and prove that any model composed from them admits closed-form variational message passing. The construction works because each primitive preserves a small set of message families: under mean-field factorization, messages on Gaussian variables remain Gaussian and messages on precision variables remain Gamma, while the only non-conjugate interface, the exponential link, remains tractable through the Gaussian moment-generating function and the sufficient statistics of the Gamma family. We demonstrate composition at increasing depth, from static ensembles through input-dependent gating to split-branch routing, and show that stacking routing layers encodes arbitrary decision trees, establishing universal function approximation with closed-form inference. Applied to ensemble time-series forecasting, the framework yields a Bayesian mixture of experts in which gating functions are inferred rather than learned, providing calibrated uncertainty over expert selection across five benchmark datasets.

AI.tldr: A principled five-primitives framework for stacking probabilistic building blocks while preserving closed-form variational inference; proves universal function approximation via routing layers; applied to Bayesian mixture of experts with calibrated uncertainty across five time-series benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Closed-form inference is often lost when stacking probabilistic blocks in deep architectures. The authors aim to preserve tractable inference by restricting interactions to a small set of primitives that preserve variational message-passing families.
AI.method: Define five factor-graph primitives: bilinear factor, exponential link, Gamma prior, Gaussian likelihood, and equality node. Prove that under mean-field factorization, Gaussian variables remain Gaussian and Gamma-precision variables remain Gamma; the non-conjugate exponential link is tractable via Gaussian moment-generating function and Gamma sufficient statistics. Demonstrate depth by stacking routing layers (static ensembles, input-dependent gating, split-branch routing) and show these can encode arbitrary decision trees (universal function approximation with closed-form inference). Apply to ensemble time-series forecasting as a Bayesian mixture of experts with gating inferred rather than learned, yielding calibrated uncertainty over expert selection across five datasets.
AI.result: Closed-form variational message passing is preserved under deep compositions of the five primitives. The framework achieves universal function approximation with closed-form inference. In the forecasting setting, it yields a Bayesian mixture of experts with gates inferred from data, providing calibrated uncertainty across five benchmark time-series datasets.
AI.conclusion: The five-primitive design enables deep probabilistic models with tractable, closed-form inference and universal function approximation. It also offers calibrated uncertainty in ensemble predictions by inferring routing/gating, demonstrating the practical viability on time-series benchmarks.

title: Beyond Trajectory Rewards: Step-level Credit Assignment for Agentic Search via Graph Modeling
summary: In Agentic Search, trajectory-level outcome rewards fail to quantify the behavioral contributions of individual steps, while existing step-level reward methods typically rely on costly tree sampling. We view world knowledge as a latent world graph and each IS task as search within a latent task graph, where effective steps should make graph progress toward the answer node. Based on this prior, we propose Graph-Distance Contribution Reward (GDCR), a step-level process reward that scores newly-retrieved and newly-cited entities by their distance to the answer node in a training-time Entity-Relation (ER) graph. We further propose Step Advantage Policy Optimization (SAPO), which converts GDCR into step-level advantages and combines them with trajectory-level outcome advantages. Experiments on four challenging benchmarks validate the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: We propose Graph-Distance Contribution Reward (GDCR) and Step Advantage Policy Optimization (SAPO) for step-level credit assignment in Agentic Search. GDCR scores newly retrieved/cited entities by their distance to the answer node in a training-time ER graph; SAPO converts these rewards into step-level advantages and combines them with trajectory-level advantages, enabling efficient learning.
AI.motivation: Trajectory-level rewards fail to attribute value to individual steps; existing step-level methods rely on costly tree sampling. A latent world/task graph perspective motivates a scalable, graph-based reward for step contributions.
AI.method: GDCR: a step-level process reward that scores newly retrieved and newly-cited entities by their distance to the answer node in a training-time Entity-Relation (ER) graph. SAPO: converts GDCR into step-level advantages and combines them with trajectory-level outcome advantages.
AI.result: Empirical validation on four challenging benchmarks demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed method in improving agentic search performance by providing principled step-level credit assignment.
AI.conclusion: GDCR and SAPO provide a scalable, graph-aware framework for step-level credit assignment in Agentic Search, improving performance across challenging tasks.

title: CityGen: Structure-Guided City-Style Synthesis for Cross-City Autonomous Driving
summary: Autonomous driving systems are commonly trained and evaluated within limited geographic regions, which hinders their scalability when deployed in new cities. However, significant domain shifts in appearance, road topology, and traffic patterns often cause severe performance degradation under cross-city deployment. Existing approaches based on domain adaptation, data augmentation, or synthetic data generation typically rely on labeled target data, city-specific annotations, or task-specific designs, limiting their scalability and effectiveness for holistic evaluation. In this paper, we introduce CityTransfer-Bench, a geographically disjoint benchmark for evaluating cross-city generalization across perception, segmentation, and planning, and propose CityGen, a diffusion-based generative framework that performs zero-label city adaptation via HD-map-conditioned synthesis guided by city-level visual prompts. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CityGen consistently improves cross-city robustness across multiple tasks, establishing a scalable and label-efficient foundation for generalizable autonomous driving.

AI.tldr: Proposes CityTransfer-Bench for cross-city generalization and CityGen, a diffusion-based zero-label city adaptation framework conditioned on HD-maps and city prompts, boosting robustness across perception, segmentation, and planning.
AI.motivation: Autonomous driving models suffer performance degradation when deployed in new cities due to domain shifts in appearance, road topology, and traffic patterns. Existing domain adaptation/data augmentation methods often require labeled target data or city-specific annotations, limiting scalability and holistic evaluation.
AI.method: Introduce CityTransfer-Bench, a geographically disjoint benchmark for evaluating cross-city generalization across perception, segmentation, and planning. Propose CityGen, a diffusion-based generative framework that performs zero-label city adaptation via HD-map-conditioned synthesis guided by city-level visual prompts.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show CityGen consistently improves cross-city robustness across multiple tasks, establishing a scalable and label-efficient foundation for generalizable autonomous driving.
AI.conclusion: CityGen enables zero-label city adaptation and holistic cross-city evaluation, providing scalable, label-efficient support for robust, generalizable autonomous driving across diverse cities.

title: Access Sets Matter: Budgeting Expert Reads for Scalable Weight-Space Model Merging
comment: ICML 2026 Workshop on Weight-Space Symmetries: from Foundations to Practical Applications
summary: Weight-space model merging is usually formulated as an algebraic operation on checkpoints, yet at LLM scale the limiting resource is often the set of expert weights that must be read. We introduce MergePipe, a budget-aware execution layer that casts LLM merging as an \emph{expert access-set} problem: given a merge operator and a checkpoint family in a shared weight coordinate system, choose which expert delta blocks to access under an explicit I/O budget. MergePipe indexes parameter blocks, builds deterministic access plans, and executes the induced budgeted merge with replayable manifests. The plan is budget-sound by construction and recovers the full-read merge at full budget; for fixed-coefficient additive operators, the omitted-update error is bounded by the norm of omitted deltas. Across Qwen and Llama merging workloads, MergePipe reduces expert-read I/O by up to an order of magnitude and achieves up to $11\times$ speedups. Representative budget sweeps show $O(10^{-3})$ parameter deviation from full-read merges and no monotonic degradation on downstream benchmarks.

AI.tldr: MergePipe economizes LLM weight-space merging by planning which expert blocks to read under an IO budget; achieves up to 11x speedups with bounded error and reduced IO.
AI.motivation: LLM merging IO is a bottleneck due to the need to read many expert weights; a budget-aware, reproducible approach is needed to scale merging to large models.
AI.method: Index parameter blocks, build deterministic access plans, and execute budgeted merges using replayable manifests. The plan is budget-sound and, at full budget, recovers the full-read merge; for fixed-coefficient additive operators, the omitted-update error is bounded by the norm of omitted deltas.
AI.result: Across Qwen and Llama workloads, MergePipe reduces expert-read I/O by up to an order of magnitude and yields up to 11× speedups. Budget sweeps show ~10^-3 parameter deviation from full-read merges, with no monotonic degradation on downstream benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: MergePipe offers an efficient, reproducible, budget-aware merging framework with provable bounds on omitted updates for suitable operators, delivering substantial IO savings and speedups for large-scale LLM checkpoint fusion.

title: BitTP: The Lightweight Trajectory Prediction Model with BitLLM for Edge-Devices
comment: Camera-ready version. Accepted as a findings paper at CVPR 2026. 8 pages, 4 figures
summary: Trajectory prediction is a fundamental task for autonomous systems, requiring complex reasoning about multi-agent interactions and intents. Large language models (LLMs) have recently been adopted for this task, as they provide strong contextual reasoning and interpretable, language-based trajectory representations. However, these LLM-based predictors are extremely memory- and compute-intensive, making them difficult to deploy on resource-constrained edge devices such as on-board computers in autonomous robots. To bridge this gap, we propose BitTP, which converts an LLM-based trajectory predictor into a lightweight bitlinear architecture. We demonstrate that weight-only quantization to 1.58-bit (BitTP-Weight) is optimal. Crucially, activations must remain in full precision, as quantizing them leads to severe degradation and instability in spatio-temporal reasoning. Empirically, BitTP-Weight not only preserves but improves prediction quality over the full-precision (BF16) LLM baseline, reducing ADE by 14.29% and FDE by 20.97% on average, while simultaneously reducing memory usage and inference latency relative to other quantization methods. These results demonstrate that carefully designed quantization acts as an effective regularizer, enabling the practical deployment of sophisticated LLM-based reasoning on edge devices. Code is available at: https://github.com/MintCat98/BitTP.

AI.tldr: BitTP introduces weight-only quantization (to ~1.58 bits) for LLM-based trajectory predictors, keeping activations in full precision. This bitlinear approach preserves or improves prediction quality on edge devices while reducing memory and latency compared to other quantization methods.
AI.motivation: LLM-based trajectory predictors offer strong reasoning and interpretable representations but are too resource-intensive for edge devices. The goal is to enable practical deployment by reducing memory and compute without sacrificing, and ideally improving, predictive accuracy.
AI.method: Propose BitTP: a bitlinear architecture where weights are quantized to ~1.58 bits (BitTP-Weight) while activations remain in full precision. The framework treats quantization as a regularizer, and empirically shows that weight-only quantization suffices for maintaining performance and stability in spatio-temporal reasoning.
AI.result: BitTP-Weight either preserves or improves performance compared to the BF16 baseline, with average ADE reduced by 14.29% and FDE reduced by 20.97%. It also reduces memory usage and inference latency relative to other quantization methods.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed weight-only quantization can serve as an effective regularizer, enabling efficient deployment of sophisticated LLM-based trajectory reasoning on edge devices without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Mesh-Aware Epipolar Matching for Multi-View Multi-Person 3D Pose Estimation in Basketball
summary: Multi-view multi-person 3D pose estimation in team sports scenarios remains challenging due to player occlusions, appearance similarity caused by team uniforms, and the scarcity of annotated multi-view data, all of which limit the effectiveness and generalization capability of learning-based methods. In contrast, the performance of training-free approaches is inherently constrained by the accuracy of 2D keypoint detection and the robustness of cross-view association. To address these challenges, we propose Mesh-Aware Epipolar Matching (MAEM), a training-free framework for multi-view multi-person 3D pose estimation. Our method employs a monocular 3D human mesh recovery model as the frontend and introduces a two-stage epipolar matching strategy based on the recovered mesh outputs. Specifically, the proposed framework combines disjoint-set-union-based clustering with per-joint triangulation to achieve robust cross-view association and accurate 3D pose reconstruction. Experiments on two public multi-view basketball datasets demonstrate that MAEM consistently outperforms existing training-free association baselines while achieving competitive RGB-only performance in both indoor and outdoor basketball scenarios. MAEM achieves MPJPE/PA-MPJPE scores of 59.8/40.7 mm on SportCenter EPFL and 74.0/51.8 mm on Human-M3 Basketball, highlighting the effectiveness of dense mesh geometry for cross-view association without requiring target-domain training or fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: Training-free MAEM framework for multi-view multi-person 3D pose estimation in sports, using mesh-aware epipolar matching with DSU clustering and per-joint triangulation; achieves competitive RGB-only results on basketball datasets.
AI.motivation: Overcome occlusion, uniform appearance, and scarce annotated multi-view data; training-free methods limited by 2D keypoint detection and cross-view data association; propose a mesh-based, training-free solution to improve cross-view consistency.
AI.method: Two-stage epipolar matching using monocular 3D mesh recovery frontend; DSU-based clustering for cross-view association; per-joint triangulation for 3D pose reconstruction; uses dense mesh geometry to guide matching; no training/fine-tuning on target domain.
AI.result: Outperforms training-free baselines and achieves competitive RGB-only performance on SportCenter EPFL and Human-M3 Basketball; MPJPE/PA-MPJPE: 59.8/40.7 mm (SportCenter EPFL), 74.0/51.8 mm (Human-M3 Basketball).
AI.conclusion: Dense mesh geometry facilitates robust cross-view association; training-free approach can achieve strong 3D pose estimation in sports; generalizes to indoor/outdoor scenarios without target-domain training.
