title: Gradient Perturbation: Learning to Perturb Gradients for Adaptive Training
summary: Deep neural network training involves both forward propagation (from features through logits to loss) and backward propagation (from loss through gradients to parameter updates). While perturbations along the forward chain, including feature perturbation, logit perturbation, and label perturbation, have been extensively studied, the backward chain's gradient perturbation has received little systematic investigation. In this paper, we establish a unified framework for gradient perturbation, revealing that existing methods such as Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM), gradient clipping, and gradient noise injection can all be interpreted as imposing specific forms of gradient perturbation. Analogous to the recently proposed Logit Perturbation Learning (LPL), we conjecture that amplifying the gradient norm for a class acts as positive augmentation (enhancing learning), while dampening it acts as negative augmentation (suppressing overfitting). Based on these observations, we propose Learning to Perturb Gradients (LPG), which adaptively perturbs logit-level gradients at the class level to achieve category-aware training. We also establish theoretical connections between gradient perturbation bounds and generalization guarantees via PAC-Bayesian analysis. Experiments on balanced classification, long-tail classification, and noisy label learning demonstrate that LPG consistently outperforms existing methods and can be combined with them as a plug-in module.

AI.tldr: A unified gradient perturbation framework is proposed; introduces LPG to adaptively perturb class-level gradients; shows theoretical PAC-Bayes connections and empirical gains; can be used as a plug-in with existing methods.
AI.motivation: Backward-pass gradient perturbations have been underexplored. The work aims to unify perturbations across forward and backward chains, and to leverage gradient perturbations for better generalization, including category-aware training.
AI.method: Recast existing perturbation techniques (e.g., SAM, gradient clipping, gradient noise) as forms of gradient perturbation in the backward chain. Propose Learning to Perturb Gradients (LPG) to adaptively perturb logit-level gradients at the class level, enabling category-aware learning. Draw connections to Logit Perturbation Learning (LPL) and provide PAC-Bayesian analysis to relate gradient perturbation bounds to generalization.
AI.result: Empirical results show LPG consistently outperforms existing gradient perturbation methods on balanced, long-tail, and noisy-label classification. LPG can be integrated as a plug-in with other methods, providing class-aware improvements across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Gradient perturbation offers a principled regularization mechanism for deep networks. LPG delivers category-aware gradient perturbations with theoretical generalization guarantees and strong empirical gains, and can be combined with other perturbation methods as a plug-in.

title: NaRA: Noise-Aware LoRA for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Diffusion LLMs
summary: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) have emerged as a promising non-autoregressive generative paradigm. Given the prohibitive computational cost of full fine-tuning, Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) has become the standard approach. However, existing PEFT methods (e.g., LoRA), originally tailored for autoregressive models, rely on static parameters that are agnostic to the noise level. Consequently, they ignore the intrinsic dynamics of the diffusion process, where input distributions and generation difficulty shift significantly along the denoising trajectory, rendering them suboptimal for dLLMs. To address this, we propose Noise-aware Low-Rank Adaptation (NaRA), which introduces a low-rank core matrix generated by a lightweight, globally shared hypernetwork conditioned on the noise level. This design enables the update matrices to vary continuously along the diffusion process while keeping parameter and latency overhead negligible. We provide a theoretical justification for the proposed NaRA framework and empirically demonstrate consistent improvements over noise-agnostic baselines across commonsense reasoning, mathematical reasoning, and code generation benchmarks. Our code is available at https://github.com/generaldi/NaRA.

AI.tldr: NaRA introduces noise-aware low-rank adapters for diffusion LLMs, using a hypernetwork conditioned on the denoising step to generate a dynamic, low-rank update that varies along the diffusion trajectory, achieving gains with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing PEFT methods like LoRA are static and ignore diffusion-time dynamics. Diffusion LLMs exhibit evolving input distributions and generation difficulty along the denoising process, necessitating noise-aware adaptation.
AI.method: A lightweight globally shared hypernetwork conditioned on noise level generates a low-rank core matrix; this core yields update matrices that vary continuously with the diffusion step, enabling dynamic adaptation while keeping parameter and latency overhead small.
AI.result: The approach is theoretically justified and yields consistent empirical improvements over noise-agnostic baselines across commonsense reasoning, mathematical reasoning, and code generation benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: NaRA effectively aligns PEFT with diffusion dynamics, offering dynamic, noise-conditioned updates with minimal overhead and favorable empirical performance; code is publicly available.

title: SwInception -- Local Attention Meets Convolutions
comment: International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence, 2024
summary: Sparse vision transformers have gained popularity as efficient encoders for medical volumetric segmentation, with Swin emerging as a prominent choice. Swin uses local attention to reduce complexity and yields excellent performance for many tasks but still tends to overfit on small datasets. To mitigate this weakness, we propose a novel architecture that further enhances Swin's inductive bias by introducing Inception blocks in the feed-forward layers. The introduction of these multi-branch convolutions enables more direct reasoning over local, multi-scale features within the transformer block. We have also modified the decoder layers in order to capture finer details using fewer parameters. We demonstrate a performance improvement on eleven different medical datasets through extensive experimentation. We specifically showcase advancements over the previous state-of-the-art backbones on benchmark challenges like the Medical Segmentation Decathlon and Beyond the Cranial Vault. By showing that the existing inductive bias in Swin can be further improved, our work presents a promising avenue for enhancing the capabilities of sparse vision transformers for both medical and natural image segmentation tasks. Code and pre-trained weights can be accessed at https://github.com/Eiphodos/SwInception.

AI.tldr: Enhances Swin Transformer with Inception-style multi-branch feed-forward blocks and a refined decoder to boost medical volumetric segmentation, achieving consistent gains across 11 datasets and state-of-the-art benchmarks; code released.
AI.motivation: Swin-like sparse transformers reduce complexity via local attention but tend to overfit on small medical datasets. Strengthening inductive bias toward local multi-scale features can improve generalization and segmentation quality with fewer parameters.
AI.method: Incorporate Inception blocks (multi-branch convolutions) into the feed-forward networks of Swin Transformer layers to enable direct reasoning over local multi-scale features; modify decoder layers to better recover fine details with fewer parameters; train and evaluate on 11 medical segmentation datasets, including Medical Segmentation Decathlon and Beyond the Cranial Vault; compare against prior state-of-the-art backbones.
AI.result: Reported performance improvements across all evaluated datasets, with advancements over previous state-of-the-art backbones on benchmark challenges such as Medical Segmentation Decathlon and Beyond the Cranial Vault.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that Swin’s inductive bias can be further strengthened through multi-branch local convolutions, offering a promising direction for sparse vision transformers in both medical and natural image segmentation; code and pre-trained weights are publicly available.

title: On-Policy Replay for Continual Supervised Fine-Tuning
summary: Continual supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is the de facto recipe for adapting large language models (LLMs) to a stream of downstream tasks, but it suffers from catastrophic forgetting of earlier capabilities. Recent work shows that on-policy signals -- training on the model's own outputs -- reduce forgetting more reliably than off-policy supervision. Existing on-policy methods route this signal through a new training objective (e.g., self-distillation losses with a teacher copy), inheriting an extra forward pass, schedule sensitivity, and stylistic drift from the teacher.We instead route the on-policy signal through the training data source. Our method, On-Policy Replay (OPR), rolls out the most recent checkpoint on a small budget of historical prompts, filters the generations by a task reward, and replays the surviving (prompt, model response) pairs as ordinary SFT examples. There is no teacher, no auxiliary loss, and no on-the-fly distillation. Across three 7--8B instruction-tuned backbones (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Qwen3-8B, Llama3.1-8B-Instruct) on the TRACE continual-learning benchmark, OPR consistently reduces forgetting; on the sharpest stress test (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Sequential SFT BWT -13.93), OPR lifts BWT to -0.65 at a 10% replay budget and to -2.29 at a 1% budget -- a 46% reduction in |BWT| over a tuned Vanilla Replay baseline, with 42--46% reductions observed across all three backbones. We give a KL-shrinkage interpretation that places OPR and prior on-policy distillation methods on a single axis, and we present a counterintuitive finding that explains why Vanilla Replay is already a strong baseline: low-score replay is uniformly worse than Vanilla Replay, demonstrating that the active ingredient in OPR is the on-policy distribution, not the response quality alone.Our code is available at https://github.com/Yancey2024/OnPolicyReplay.

AI.tldr: OPR is a simple, data-driven on-policy replay method for continual SFT that reuses recent model outputs generated on historical prompts, filtered by a task reward, to form standard training data without teachers or auxiliary losses, yielding strong forgetting reduction across 7–8B models on TRACE.
AI.motivation: Mitigate catastrophic forgetting in continual supervised fine-tuning of LLMs by leveraging on-policy signals without introducing extra losses or distillation, and without teacher models.
AI.method: Roll out the most recent checkpoint on a small set of historical prompts, generate responses, filter generations by a task-specific reward, and replay surviving (prompt, response) pairs as ordinary SFT data. No teacher, no auxiliary loss, no on-the-fly distillation.
AI.result: OPR consistently reduces forgetting across three 7–8B instruction-tuned backbones on TRACE. In the strongest stress test (Sequential SFT BWT = -13.93), OPR improves BWT to -0.65 with 10% replay budget and to -2.29 with 1% budget (≈46% reduction in |BWT| vs. Vanilla Replay); 42–46% reductions observed across all backbones. A KL-shrinkage interpretation links OPR to prior on-policy distillation, and low-score replay is inferior to Vanilla Replay, highlighting that the active ingredient is the on-policy distribution rather than response quality alone.
AI.conclusion: OPR offers a simple, effective, and resource-efficient on-policy data replay mechanism that outperforms vanilla baselines without teachers or extra losses. It frames on-policy data within the standard SFT paradigm and provides a theoretical lens (KL shrinkage) to unify on-policy methods. Code is available for replication.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Transfer Learning for Cross-Building Energy Forecasting: Toward Robust and Scalable District-Level Energy Management
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables. Accepted at BALANCES'26 (6th ACM International Workshop on Big Data and Machine Learning for Smart Buildings and Cities), Banff, Alberta, Canada, June 22, 2026. This is the author's accepted manuscript; final published version DOI will be activated after June 22, 2026
summary: Scaling data-driven energy forecasting to district level requires models that can be re-used across buildings with minimal target-domain data and honest uncertainty estimates. We present an uncertainty-aware transfer learning (TL) framework for cross-building energy forecasting based on the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT), evaluated on a newly released high-resolution real sub-meter dataset: an educational building at Aalborg University, Denmark (source) and the multi-typology NEST building at EMPA, Switzerland (target). We introduce the Transfer Robustness Index (TRI), an architecture-agnostic metric for quantifying generalization quality across domain gaps. A four-strategy layer-freezing ablation shows that Probe-Only fine-tuning, updating only 455 output-layer parameters out of 806K, achieves the best transfer quality (TRI = 3,097), outperforming full fine-tuning and suggesting that TFT encoders learn transferable temporal representations. Monte Carlo Dropout yields a prediction interval coverage probability of 93.2%, close to the nominal 95% target. A data-scarcity analysis further shows monotonic improvement with increasing target-domain data, providing practical guidance for district energy deployment.

AI.tldr: Transfer-learning with Temporal Fusion Transformer for cross-building energy forecasting, introducing Transfer Robustness Index (TRI); layer-freezing ablations show updating only final layer yields best transfer; MC dropout yields ~93% PICP; dataset from Aalborg University and NEST EMPA; data-scarcity analysis shows monotonic gains with more target data.
AI.motivation: Enable data-driven district-scale energy forecasting with minimal target-domain data and honest uncertainty estimates; quantify cross-domain generalization with a building-to-building transfer index.
AI.method: Uncertainty-aware TL framework using TFT; four-strategy layer-freezing ablations; construct TRI; use MC Dropout for predictive intervals; evaluate transfer from a single source building to a different-target building across typologies.
AI.result: Probe-Only fine-tuning (updating 455 of 806K parameters) achieves best transfer quality with TRI = 3,097; full fine-tuning performs worse; MC Dropout yields PICP = 93.2% (near the 95% target); data-scarcity analysis shows monotonic improvement as target-domain data increases.
AI.conclusion: TFT encoders learn transferable temporal representations; targeted, lightweight fine-tuning is effective for cross-building transfer; TRI provides an architecture-agnostic measure of generalization; practical guidance for deploying district energy forecasting with limited target data.

title: EVL-ECG: Efficient ECG Interpretation With Multi-Aspect Heterogeneous Knowledge Distillation
comment: Accepted at the SD4H Workshop at ICML 2026. 11 pages, 3 figures
summary: High-fidelity ECG interpretation is increasingly reliant on massive foundation models, yet their deployment in clinical edge-care remains hindered by extreme computational demands. While knowledge distillation (KD) is a promising solution, traditional methods fail to capture the complex spatio-temporal dependencies of ECG signals when transferring knowledge across heterogeneous architectures. In this paper, we propose EVL-ECG, a framework specifically designed for cross-architecture distillation of cardiac diagnostic logic. EVL-ECG introduces three ECG-aware innovations: (1) Multi-Head Cross-Attention Alignment, which harmonizes architectural discrepancies to preserve fine-grained morphological features; (2) Optimal Transport-based Visual Feature Matching, utilizing optimal transport to maintain global structural relationships across ECG leads despite mismatched token representations; and (3) Geometric Intra-Architecture Relation Matching, which distills the latent diagnostic reasoning of the teacher model. Evaluations across ECG benchmarks demonstrate that EVL-ECG yields improvements of up to 2.4% AUC and 1.1% clinical accuracy over existing baselines. Notably, EVL-ECG establishes an efficient 2B-parameter ECG foundation model, suitable for resource-constrained clinical environments.

AI.tldr: A cross-architecture knowledge distillation framework for ECG (EVL-ECG) with three ECG-aware innovations—Multi-Head Cross-Attention Alignment, Optimal Transport-based Visual Feature Matching, and Geometric Intra-Architecture Relation Matching—achieving modest performance gains and enabling a compact 2B-parameter ECG foundation model for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity ECG interpretation relies on large foundation models whose heavy computations hinder edge deployment; traditional KD across heterogeneous architectures often fails to preserve ECGs' complex spatio-temporal dependencies and diagnostic reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce EVL-ECG with three ECG-aware innovations: (1) Multi-Head Cross-Attention Alignment to harmonize architecture-specific representations and preserve fine-grained morphological features; (2) Optimal Transport-based Visual Feature Matching to maintain global lead relationships across mismatched token representations; (3) Geometric Intra-Architecture Relation Matching to distill latent diagnostic reasoning from the teacher.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on ECG benchmarks show improvements up to 2.4% in AUC and 1.1% in clinical accuracy over baselines; demonstrates feasibility of a 2B-parameter ECG foundation model suitable for resource-constrained clinical environments.
AI.conclusion: EVL-ECG provides an effective cross-architecture KD framework for ECG, enabling competitive diagnostic performance with a compact, edge-friendly foundation model; future work could explore broader datasets, ablations, and cost/latency analyses.

title: Convex Basins in Single-Index Model Loss Landscapes: Applications to Robust Recovery under Strong Adversarial Corruption
summary: We study the problem of robustly learning Gaussian Single Index Models (SIMs) in the presence of heavy-tailed noise and a constant fraction of adversarially corrupted covariates and responses. Prior work on robust recovery has considered settings such as linear regression (Pensia et al., JASA 2024), strictly monotonic link functions (Awasthi et al., NeurIPS 2022), and phase retrieval (Buna and Rebeschini, AISTATS 2025). However, these techniques do not extend to generic asymmetric non-monotonic link functions such as \textsc{GeLU} and \textsc{Swish}, which arise naturally as scalar primitives in modern gated neural architectures. We close this gap by giving the first robust recovery algorithm with near-linear sample and time complexity for generic non-monotonic link functions, thereby establishing the first robust recovery guarantees for a broad family of nonlinear SIMs for which \textit{no guarantees were previously known}. Our central contribution is a new structural understanding of the Gaussian squared-loss landscape under adversarial contamination. Crucially, we prove that for a broad class of nonlinear non-monotonic SIMs, a dimension-independent, constant-radius convex basin exists around the ground truth and is efficiently reachable via robust spectral initialization even under adversarial contamination. Prior works fail to establish both guarantees simultaneously, thereby either breaking down under adversarial contamination or failing to handle generic non-monotonic link functions. Together, these structural insights yield a principled warm start for robust gradient descent that provably converges to a final estimation error of $O(σ\sqrtε)$ in $\tilde{O}(nd)$ time with $\tilde{O}(d)$ samples, where $ε$ is the contamination fraction.

AI.tldr: First robust recovery algorithm for Gaussian Single Index Models with heavy-tailed noise and adversarially corrupted data, enabling non-monotonic link functions (e.g., GeLU, Swish) with near-linear sample/time complexity and estimation error O(σ√ε).
AI.motivation: There is a gap in robust learning: existing results cover linear regression, monotone links, or phase retrieval, but not generic non-monotonic, asymmetric links relevant to modern neural architectures. The goal is to obtain robust guarantees for a broad nonlinear SIM class under practical corruption models.
AI.method: Structural analysis of the Gaussian squared-loss landscape under adversarial contamination; show a constant-radius, dimension-independent convex basin around the ground truth that is reachable via robust spectral initialization even with outliers; use a robust initialization followed by robust gradient descent; achieve near-linear time and sample complexity with contamination parameter ε.
AI.result: Algorithm attains final estimation error O(σ√ε) in ~O(nd) time using ~O(d) samples, robust to ε-fraction corruption; applicable to non-monotonic link functions such as GeLU and Swish; provides the first robust guarantees for a broad family of nonlinear SIMs.
AI.conclusion: Extends robust recovery to nonlinear SIMs with non-monotonic links by revealing a favorable loss landscape and providing a practical initialization and optimization pipeline; closes a gap in theory for robust learning in modern neural-inspired models and establishes near-linear complexity guarantees under contamination.

title: Citation-Closure Retrieval and Per-Rule Attribution for Real-World Regulatory Compliance Question Answering
summary: Deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) for regulatory compliance demands rigorous traceability via comprehensive citations across multi-tiered authority structures. Unlike traditional multi-hop or legal QA, this task requires structured procedural lookups and evidence-set closure rather than entity resolution or case-law reasoning. Existing RAG systems struggle here due to flattened citation edges, fragmented retrieval expansions, and fragile post-hoc attribution. We formalize Regulatory Compliance QA with RegOps-Bench, a novel benchmark featuring an Operational Knowledge Graph derived from complex national R\&D regulations. To address these bottlenecks, we propose RefWalk, a unified framework driven by a shared topic anchor. RefWalk traverses cross-document citations, fuses multi-view candidates via max-based aggregation, and enforces per-rule attribution to explicitly map claims to sources. We establish a strong baseline with substantial improvements in retrieval recall and citation accuracy. Finally, a contrastive evaluation on a U.S. health compliance dataset (HIPAA) reveals that existing systems exhibit saturation on flat-structure rules, underscoring the need for RegOps-Bench. Our code is available at https://github.com/yeongjoonJu/RefWalk.

AI.tldr: Proposes RegOps-Bench, an Operational Knowledge Graph benchmark for regulatory compliance QA, and RefWalk, a citation-aware retrieval framework that uses a shared topic anchor to traverse cross-document references, fuse candidates with max-based aggregation, and enforce per-rule attribution; shows improved retrieval recall and citation accuracy, with a HIPAA-based contrastive evaluation highlighting limitations of flat-structure systems; code released.
AI.motivation: Regulatory compliance QA demands rigorous traceability and explicit source attribution across multi-tier authorities. Traditional retrieval systems suffer from flattened citation edges, fragmented retrieval, and fragile post-hoc attribution, motivating a dedicated benchmark and a structured, attribution-focused method.
AI.method: RegOps-Bench provides an Operational Knowledge Graph derived from complex national R&D regulations. RefWalk traverses cross-document citations via a shared topic anchor, aggregates multiple candidate views using max-based scoring, and enforces per-rule attribution to map each claim to its sources.
AI.result: Establishes a strong baseline with substantial gains in retrieval recall and citation accuracy. A contrastive HIPAA evaluation reveals current systems saturate on flat-structure rules, underscoring the need for RegOps-Bench.
AI.conclusion: RegOps-Bench and RefWalk address a critical gap in regulatory QA by enabling traceable, source-backed reasoning; the approach improves recall and attribution and is validated on HIPAA data; code is available at the authors’ repository.

title: Genetically Aligned Patient Representations Improve Hematological Diagnosis
comment: Accepted for publication at the 29th International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer Assisted Intervention - MICCAI 2026
summary: Multimodal alignment of histopathology encoders with transcriptomic and genomic data has been shown to significantly improve performance in downstream diagnostic tasks. Hematological cytology is unique in that visual single-cell evaluation is often paired with cytogenetics and molecular genetics for blood cancer diagnosis. In this study, we present a framework to align single white blood cell images with chromosomal aberrations (karyotype) and somatic mutations from targeted gene panels. Our training strategy follows a two-stage approach: (i) self-supervised, vision-only pretraining of a transformer aggregator using an iBOT head on a cohort of over 1500 patients, and (ii) genetic alignment via supervised contrastive loss on acute myeloid leukemia patients. Our genetically aligned patient encoder improves hematological diagnostic tasks, outperforming slide-level histopathology foundation models. Additionally, the model provides off-the-shelf retrieval capabilities for diseases and genetic alterations. Incorporating genetic data into patient encoders increases the quality of patient representations, providing a framework that aligns with clinical diagnostic workflows and paves the way for future multimodal hematology-specific AI. The code and model weights are available at https://github.com/marrlab/GenBloom.

AI.tldr: A two-stage multimodal learning framework that aligns single-cell histology images with genetic alterations (karyotype and targeted mutations) using vision-only self-supervised pretraining followed by supervised contrastive genetic alignment, improving hematological diagnostic tasks and enabling retrieval of diseases and genetic alterations.
AI.motivation: Bridge histopathology and genomics in hematology to enhance diagnostic accuracy and align with clinical workflows.
AI.method: Stage 1: Self-supervised vision-only pretraining of a transformer aggregator with an iBOT head on >1500 patients. Stage 2: Genetic alignment via supervised contrastive loss on AML patients to align image representations with chromosomal aberrations and somatic mutations.
AI.result: Genetically aligned patient encoder improves hematological diagnostic tasks over slide-level histopathology foundation models and enables off-the-shelf retrieval by disease and genetic alteration. Code and model weights available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates the feasibility of integrating imaging and genetic data in hematology, offering improved patient representations and practical retrieval capabilities, with potential to extend to future multimodal hematology AI workflows.

title: Quotient DAGs for Off-Policy Evaluation:Forward-Flow Importance Sampling and Exact Slate Propensities
summary: Off-policy evaluation estimates how a target policy would perform using data collected by a different behavior policy, which is crucial when online testing is costly or risky, such as in recommendation or healthcare. Standard importance sampling reweights each logged trajectory, but it can treat details of the generation process as meaningful even when the evaluation target ignores them: for example, an autoregressive slate recommender may generate an ordered sequence of items while the reward and downstream estimator depend only on the unordered slate. This creates nuisance variance and a computational gap, since exact unordered slate propensities require summing over all generation orders. We introduce a quotient-DAG view that merges histories equivalent for evaluation and assigns weights using target-to-behavior forward-flow ratios on the merged graph. For slate recommendation under a set-sufficient next-item interface, this yields Forward-DP, a subset-DAG dynamic program that computes exact unordered propensities without factorial enumeration. The resulting propensity primitive enables practical propensity-based evaluation and model selection for context-dependent autoregressive slate loggers.

AI.tldr: A quotient-DAG framework computes exact unordered propensities for off-policy evaluation in autoregressive slate recommendations by merging histories and using forward-flow ratios; Forward-DP computes a subset-DAG DP to avoid factorial enumeration, enabling practical OPE and model selection.
AI.motivation: Off-policy evaluation in complex autoregressive slate loggers suffers from nuisance variance and a computational gap because evaluation targets depend on unordered slates while data is generated as ordered sequences; exact unordered propensity computation would require factorial enumeration of orders.
AI.method: Introduce a quotient-DAG view that merges histories equivalent for evaluation and assigns weights using target-to-behavior forward-flow ratios on the merged graph. For slate recommendation under a set-sufficient next-item interface, derive Forward-DP, a subset-DAG dynamic program that computes exact unordered propensities without factorial enumeration.
AI.result: Enables practical propensity-based evaluation and model selection for context-dependent autoregressive slate loggers by providing an exact, efficient propensity computation primitive.
AI.conclusion: The quotient-DAG approach provides a principled and scalable solution to off-policy evaluation in ordered-to-unordered slate generation, addressing nuisance variance and computational bottlenecks and enabling effective OPE-based model selection.

title: Why Specialist Models Still Matter: A Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Paradigm for Medical Artificial Intelligence
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026. 12 pages main text, 16 pages appendix
summary: The impressive performance of generalist large language models (LLMs) such as GPT and Claude in healthcare raises a critical question: will domain-specific medical specialist models become obsolete? We argue that the future of medical artificial intelligence (AI) lies not in building monolithic medical foundation models, nor in replacing human expertise, but in orchestrating collaboration among generalist LLMs, domain-specific specialist models, and clinicians. We propose HetMedAgent, a heterogeneous medical multi-agent framework that enables conflict-aware evidence fusion, uncertainty-based clinician intervention triggering, and adaptive threshold calibration. Experiments on three real-world clinical decision-making tasks demonstrate that the synergy between generalist LLMs and domain-specific specialist models significantly outperforms using either type of model alone, validating the irreplaceable value of specialist models in modality-specific analysis. HetMedAgent represents a shift from building medical LLMs or foundation models to multi-agent collaboration, achieving a balance between general reasoning capabilities and domain-specific precision.

AI.tldr: HetMedAgent proposes a heterogeneous multi-agent framework that combines generalist LLMs, domain-specific models, and clinicians to improve clinical decision-making, using conflict-aware evidence fusion, uncertainty-based clinician intervention, and adaptive threshold calibration; experiments show multi-agent synergy outperforms single-model baselines.
AI.motivation: Generalist LLMs achieve strong performance in healthcare but may lack domain-specific precision; domain-specific specialist models remain valuable for modality-specific analysis. The paper argues for orchestrating collaboration among generalist LLMs, specialists, and clinicians instead of pursuing monolithic medical foundation models.
AI.method: Introduce HetMedAgent, a heterogeneous medical multi-agent system with: (1) conflict-aware evidence fusion across agents, (2) uncertainty-based intervention triggering to involve clinicians, (3) adaptive threshold calibration to balance automation and human input. Evaluate on three real-world clinical decision-making tasks against baselines.
AI.result: Empirical results show that synergy between generalist LLMs and domain-specific specialists significantly outperforms either type alone, validating the irreplaceable value of specialist models in modality-specific analysis and demonstrating robust gains across three tasks.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from building medical LLMs/foundation models to multi-agent collaboration achieves a balance between broad reasoning and domain-specific precision, underscoring the importance of integrating heterogeneous agents and clinician input for future medical AI.

title: FRUC: Feedforward Dynamic Scene Reconstruction from Uncalibrated Collaborative Driving Views
summary: We present FRUC, a feed-forward 3D Gaussian splatting framework for dynamic scene reconstruction from uncalibrated collaborative driving views. Existing multi-agent reconstruction frameworks are often hindered by rigid prerequisites, demanding precise spatial calibration and slow per-scene optimization. In this paper, we rethink this task by conceptualizing a distributed multi-vehicle network as a spatio-temporally unstructured ego-centric multi-camera system, where the core challenge lies in enhancing ego-centric occluded geometry through collaboration without degrading the ego's accurately observed visible geometry, while preserving reconstruction efficiency. For efficient reconstruction, FRUC is built upon a visual grounded geometric Transformer backbone to enable one-shot, calibration-free inference from a flexible number of multi-vehicle views. To achieve non-destructive geometric supplementation under uncalibrated cross-agent misalignment, FRUC first introduces an ego-centric causal occlusion field that explicitly derives occlusion evolution as latent priors by modeling agent-wise spatio-temporal correlations. Guided by these occlusion priors, it further formulates cross-agent integration as a deterministic residual denoising process via zero-initialized injection, turning challenging cross-agent fusion into bounded residual learning for robust collaborative blind-spot completion. Through extensive evaluations on the real-world V2XReal and UrbanIng-V2X datasets, FRUC is shown to be a new state-of-the-art for the scene reconstruction of dynamic collaborative driving environments, significantly outperforming existing methods in both rendering quality and efficiency.

AI.tldr: Calibration-free, one-shot 3D scene reconstruction for dynamic driving from uncalibrated, ego-centric multi-vehicle views via a causal occlusion prior and residual denoising.
AI.motivation: Overcome rigid calibration prerequisites and slow per-scene optimization in multi-agent 3D reconstruction; enable robust ego-centric collaboration to improve occluded geometry while preserving observed geometry and efficiency.
AI.method: FRUC uses a feed-forward 3D Gaussian splatting framework with a visual grounded geometric Transformer backbone for calibration-free, one-shot inference from multiple vehicles. It introduces an ego-centric causal occlusion field to model agent-wise spatio-temporal correlations and formulates cross-agent fusion as a deterministic residual denoising process via zero-initialized injection.
AI.result: On real-world V2XReal and UrbanIng-V2X datasets, FRUC achieves state-of-the-art scene reconstruction for dynamic collaborative driving, outperforming existing methods in rendering quality and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: FRUC enables robust, calibration-free cooperative reconstruction of dynamic driving scenes by leveraging occlusion priors and a residual denoising framework for safe and efficient multi-vehicle collaboration.

title: K-FinHallu: A Hallucination Detection Benchmark for Multi-Turn RAG in Korean Finance
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have advanced financial automation through Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), yet hallucinations remain a critical barrier to deployment in high-stakes environments. Existing benchmarks focus on single-turn, English-centric tasks, leaving the multi-turn dynamics and linguistic-regulatory nuances of the Korean financial domain unaddressed. We introduce K-FinHallu, the first benchmark for hallucination detection in multi-turn Korean financial RAG. We construct multi-turn dialogues from authentic Korean financial documents and inject hallucinations under a proposed hierarchical taxonomy based on context answerability that explicitly accounts for justified abstention. Benchmarking frontier and open-source LLMs as hallucination detectors, we find that even the strongest models struggle with fine-grained financial diagnostics and refusal behavior. While fine-tuning an 8B model on our training split yields performance competitive with frontier LLMs, justified abstention remains the weakest axis across all evaluated models.

AI.tldr: First multi-turn Korean financial RAG hallucination benchmark (K-FinHallu); reveals frontier and open LLMs struggle with fine-grained hallucinatory detection and justified abstention; fine-tuning an 8B model narrows the gap but abstention remains the weak axis.
AI.motivation: Addresses multi-turn dynamics and linguistic-regulatory nuances in the Korean financial domain, where hallucinations in high-stakes decision-making are critical. Existing benchmarks are English-centric and single-turn, leaving Korean financial settings under-evaluated.
AI.method: Construct multi-turn dialogues from authentic Korean financial documents; inject hallucinations using a hierarchical taxonomy based on context answerability, explicitly accounting for justified abstention; benchmark frontier and open-source LLMs as detectors; fine-tune an 8B model on the training split and compare performance.
AI.result: Strongest models struggle with fine-grained financial diagnostics and refusal behavior; an 8B model fine-tuned on the training split achieves performance competitive with frontier LLMs; justified abstention remains the weakest axis across all models.
AI.conclusion: K-FinHallu provides a domain-specific benchmark to evaluate hallucination detection and abstention in Korean financial RAG, highlighting the need for better abstention strategies and continued model improvement to mitigate high-stakes risks.

title: Benchmarking Positional Encoding Strategies for Transformer-Based EEG Foundation Models
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a widely used non-invasive technique for measuring brain activity in brain-computer interface (BCI) applications. Supervised EEG decoding models often struggle to generalize across tasks, subjects, and datasets, motivating transformer-based EEG foundation models trained with self-supervised learning. Since transformers are permutation-invariant, they require explicit positional information. Unlike textual tokens, EEG electrodes are spatially distributed across the scalp, raising the question of how electrode positions should be encoded in transformer-based EEG models. In this study, we benchmark five positional encoding strategies within the CBraMod backbone and evaluate them under linear probing and fine-tuning protocols on motor imagery classification and emotion recognition. Our results show that no single strategy consistently outperforms across tasks. Spherical Positional Encoding (SPE) yields strong representations for motor imagery but underperforms on emotion recognition, while Asymmetric Conditional Positional Encoding (ACPE) demonstrates more consistent performance across tasks. These findings suggest that the optimal positional encoding strategy is task-dependent, with no universal solution across EEG decoding scenarios.

AI.tldr: Benchmarked five positional encoding strategies for EEG transformer models within the CBraMod backbone across motor imagery and emotion recognition tasks. No universal winner; SPE excels for motor imagery, ACPE offers more consistent performance; encoding strategy should be task-dependent.
AI.motivation: Transformers require positional information, but EEG electrodes are spatially distributed. The goal is to determine how to encode electrode positions to improve generalization across tasks/datasets.
AI.method: Evaluate five positional encoding strategies in the CBraMod EEG backbone under linear probing and full fine-tuning for two tasks: motor imagery classification and emotion recognition.
AI.result: No single positional encoding consistently outperforms others across tasks. Spherical Positional Encoding (SPE) yields strong representations for motor imagery but underperforms on emotion recognition, whereas Asymmetric Conditional Positional Encoding (ACPE) provides more consistent performance across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Positional encoding effectiveness in EEG transformers is task-dependent; there may be no universal encoding strategy suitable for all EEG decoding scenarios.

title: EarlyTom: Early Token Compression Completes Fast Video Understanding
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026. 16 pages, 8 figures, 8 tables. Project page: https://viridisgreen.github.io/EarlyTom
summary: Video large language models (Video-LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in video understanding tasks. However, their practical deployment is still hindered by the inefficiency introduced by processing massive amounts of visual tokens. Although recent approaches achieve extremely low token retention ratios while maintaining accuracy comparable to full-token baselines, most of them perform compression only at the late stage of prefilling, leaving the efficiency of the vision encoder unoptimized. In this paper, we first show that vision encoding contributes a large portion to the time-to-first-token (TTFT). Therefore, instead of compressing visual tokens only after the vision encoder, performing compression inside the encoder still leaves substantial room for exploration. Based on this insight, we propose EarlyTom, a training-free token compression framework that performs early-stage visual token compression inside the vision encoder, enabling significantly better TTFT reduction and higher throughput. In addition, we introduce a decoupled spatial token selection strategy that improves the overall compression effectiveness. EarlyTom reduces TTFT by up to 2.65x and FLOPs by up to 61% on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU for the LLaVA-OneVision-7B model, while maintaining accuracy comparable to the full-token baseline. These improvements substantially enhance the practicality of deploying Video-LLMs in real-world production scenarios.

AI.tldr: EarlyTom is a training-free token compression framework that compresses visual tokens inside the vision encoder to reduce TTFT and FLOPs in Video-LLMs, plus a decoupled spatial token selection. It achieves up to 2.65x TTFT reduction and up to 61% FLOPs reduction on LLaVA-OneVision-7B with accuracy on par with full-token baselines.
AI.motivation: Vision encoding contributes a large portion of TTFT; compressing tokens only after prefilling leaves encoder-side inefficiencies unaddressed. Optimizing token use inside the encoder could substantially improve throughput.
AI.method: Introduce early-stage visual token compression within the vision encoder; add a decoupled spatial token selection strategy to improve compression effectiveness; maintain training-free operation to avoid additional optimization costs.
AI.result: TTFT reduced by up to 2.65x and FLOPs reduced by up to 61% on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU for the LLaVA-OneVision-7B model, with accuracy comparable to the full-token baseline.
AI.conclusion: EarlyTom substantially enhances practical deployment of Video-LLMs by accelerating vision encoding without retraining; it achieves significant throughput gains while preserving accuracy, though generalization across diverse models/datasets warrants further validation.

title: Learning to Perturb Hidden Representations for Generalizable Deep Learning
summary: Deep neural networks process data through a cascade of representations: input features, hidden activations, logits, and loss. While perturbations at the input, logit, and label levels have been systematically studied, the intermediate hidden activations, which constitute the bulk of the network's computation, have received no unified perturbation analysis. In this paper, we establish a unified framework for hidden activation perturbation, revealing that Dropout, Manifold Mixup, adversarial feature perturbation, and related methods all impose specific forms of activation perturbation but with class-agnostic or random strategies. We conjecture that expansive perturbation (increasing activation norm) acts as positive augmentation, while contractive perturbation (decreasing activation norm) acts as negative augmentation, and that the perturbation layer determines whether the effect resembles input-level augmentation (shallow layers) or logit-level manipulation (deep layers). We propose Learning to Perturb Activations (LPA), which adaptively perturbs activations at a selected hidden layer with class-level perturbations learned via PGD. We further provide theoretical analysis connecting activation perturbation to flat minima and perturbation amplification through layers. Experiments on balanced classification, long-tail classification, and domain generalization demonstrate that LPA consistently outperforms existing methods and provides complementary benefits to logit perturbation methods such as LPL.

AI.tldr: Unified activation-perturbation framework for hidden representations in DNNs; introduces Learning to Perturb Activations (LPA) with class-level PGD perturbations; theory connects activation perturbation to flat minima and cross-layer amplification; empirically improves balanced, long-tail, and domain generalization tasks and complements logit perturbation methods.
AI.motivation: Hidden activations carry the bulk of computation and have lacked a unified perturbation analysis. Existing work focuses on input, logit, or label perturbations; a unified activation-perturbation framework can reconcile Dropout, Manifold Mixup, and adversarial feature perturbations while offering principled understanding and potential performance gains.
AI.method: Propose a unified activation perturbation framework; categorize perturbations as expansive (increase activation norm) or contractive (decrease norm); identify a perturbation layer that controls whether effects resemble input-level or logit-level perturbations. Introduce Learning to Perturb Activations (LPA), which adaptively perturbs activations at a chosen hidden layer with class-level perturbations learned via PGD. Provide theoretical analysis linking activation perturbation to flat minima and to perturbation amplification through layers.
AI.result: LPA consistently outperforms existing methods on balanced classification, long-tail classification, and domain generalization; it provides complementary benefits to logit perturbation methods such as LPL.
AI.conclusion: Activation perturbations offer a viable augmentation mechanism for DNNs. The perturbation layer determines the qualitative effect, and adaptive, class-level perturbations via PGD yield robust gains across tasks. Theoretical connections to flat minima and perturbation amplification support the framework and suggest directions for combining activation and logit perturbations.

title: LFQ: Logit-aware Final-block Quantization for Boosting the Generation Quality of Low-Bit Quantized LLMs
summary: As large language models continue to scale, low-bit weight-only post-training quantization (PTQ) offers a practical solution to their memory-efficient deployment. Although block-wise PTQ is capable of matching the full-precision (FP) baseline on basic language modeling and understanding, its quality is degraded for generative tasks -- especially at longer responses and extended chains of thought, which is critical in boosting task accuracy. We attribute this shortfall to two factors: (i) the omission of the unembedding layer (the LM head) in block-wise optimization and (ii) the reliance on the mean squared error (MSE) objective. Both factors cause the token probability distribution of the quantized model to misalign with that of the FP model, yielding notable accuracy drops on text generation benchmarks. To rectify the discrepancy, we introduce Logit-aware Final-block Quantization (LFQ), a simple yet effective enhancement to block-wise PTQ that quantizes the final Transformer block by minimizing the cross-entropy between the logits of the FP model and those of its quantized counterpart. By aligning token probabilities at the logit level in the final block, LFQ consistently improves the accuracy of complex generation tasks over state-of-the-art block-wise PTQ across diverse model families, while maintaining parity with FP baselines on language modeling and understanding.

AI.tldr: LFQ improves block-wise PTQ for LLMs by quantizing the final Transformer block to minimize cross-entropy between FP and quantized logits, aligning token probabilities and boosting generation quality while maintaining FP parity on LM/understanding.
AI.motivation: Block-wise PTQ often degrades generative performance due to misalignment of token distributions, caused by omitting the LM head in optimization and relying on MSE; a logit-level alignment in the final block can mitigate this.
AI.method: Introduce Logit-aware Final-block Quantization (LFQ) by quantizing only the final Transformer block and optimizing a cross-entropy loss between the FP model's logits and the quantized model's logits for that block.
AI.result: LFQ yields consistent generation-quality improvements over state-of-the-art block-wise PTQ across diverse model families, while preserving FP parity on language modeling and understanding tasks.
AI.conclusion: Aligning token probabilities at the logit level in the final block is an effective strategy to remedy generation degradation in block-wise PTQ, enabling robust low-bit deployment of LLMs.

title: VisualThink-VLA: Visual Intermediate Reasoning for Effective and Low-Latency Vision-Language-Action Policies
summary: Recent work has begun to equip vision-language-action (VLA) policies with explicit intermediate reasoning. In embodied control, however, textual chain-of-thought is a poor fit: irrelevant or weakly textual information can interfere with action prediction, while autoregressive text decoding adds too much latency for real-time closed-loop execution. We present VISUALTHINK-VLA, a visual intermediate-reasoning framework for accurate, low-latency VLA policies. Our bootstrapping philosophy is to guide action with effective visual thinking: VISUALTHINK-VLA bootstraps action prediction through a compact visual-evidence interface that preserves spatial precision while avoiding decoding overhead. Besides, to further improve performance and efficiency, VISUALTHINK-VLA adopts a tailored selective routing mechanism to learn the visual evidence tokens, enabling low-latency inference while preserving high-capacity specialization. We also introduce VisualEvidence-Kit, a supervision-and-audit resource centered on a VisualEvidence-Agent that constructs a 754.7k VLA instructions VisualEvidence-Set for route supervision and counterfactual faithfulness tests. Across multiple benchmarks and real-robot evaluation, VISUALTHINK-VLA achieves the highest success rate on most benchmarks while reducing the multi-second latency of reasoning-augmented baselines to the sub-second regime. For example, on BridgeData V2, it reduces step latency from 8.377,s with ECoT to 0.367,s, achieving a 22.8 times speedup.

AI.tldr: Introduces VISUALTHINK-VLA, a visual intermediate-reasoning approach for VLA policies that avoids text-based reasoning to achieve low latency and high accuracy; uses a compact visual-evidence interface, selective routing, and a large VisualEvidence-Set dataset.
AI.motivation: Textual chain-of-thought is unsuitable for real-time embodied control due to latency and interference; need spatially precise, low-latency visual reasoning.
AI.method: A compact visual-evidence interface preserves spatial precision while reducing decoding overhead; a tailored selective routing mechanism learns visual evidence tokens for fast inference; VisualEvidence-Kit provides supervision and audit via a VisualEvidence-Agent that constructs a 754.7k VLA instructions VisualEvidence-Set for route supervision and counterfactual faithfulness tests.
AI.result: Achieves the highest success rates on most benchmarks; reduces reasoning latency from multi-second regimes to sub-second levels; on BridgeData V2, latency drops from 8.377 s (with ECoT) to 0.367 s, a 22.8× speedup.
AI.conclusion: Presents an effective and efficient alternative to text-based CoT for VLA in embodied control, with strong empirical gains and a practical supervision toolkit.

title: AsymVLM: Asymmetric Token Pruning for Efficient Vision-Language Model Inference
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) process thousands of visual tokens per image alongside comparatively few text tokens, yet existing compression methods treat both modalities uniformly. We observe that the two modalities have fundamentally different properties: vision tokens are spatially redundant and dominate prefill, while text tokens are causally dependent and accumulate during decoding. Based on this asymmetry, we propose and empirically evaluate AsymVLM, which applies aggressive pruning to vision tokens before prefill using a learned importance scorer with per-sample adaptive budgeting, and temporal threshold-based eviction to text tokens only when they exceed a fixed budget. Our experiments indicate that AsymVLM achieves the highest FLOPs savings (up to 54%) among state-of-the-art methods while outperforming existing approaches by 2--3% on document and chart understanding tasks where visual information is spatially localized and query-specific, and maintaining competitive accuracy on holistic benchmarks. In text-dominated scenarios, our eviction strategy substantially outperforms standard LLM cache compression methods by adapting to the short-context nature of VLM.

AI.tldr: AsymVLM introduces modality-aware token pruning for Vision-Language Models: aggressive vision-token pruning before prefill with a learned importance scorer and per-sample budgets, plus fixed-budget, temporal eviction of text tokens during decoding. This yields substantial FLOPs savings with competitive accuracy, excelling in document/chart understanding and in text-dominated scenarios.
AI.motivation: Vision tokens are spatially redundant and dominate prefill, while text tokens are causally dependent and accumulate during decoding. Uniform compression across modalities loses these asymmetries, limiting efficiency and accuracy in VLMs, especially for spatially localized visual queries.
AI.method: Two-tier approach: (1) vision tokens: aggressive pruning before prefill using a learned importance scorer with per-sample adaptive budgeting; (2) text tokens: temporal threshold-based eviction applied only when the token budget is exceeded, retaining the remaining tokens. Evaluation on document/chart understanding tasks and holistic benchmarks against state-of-the-art compression methods and LLM cache compression.
AI.result: FLOPs savings up to 54%, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by 2–3% on document/chart understanding tasks where visual information is spatially localized and query-specific; competitive accuracy on holistic benchmarks. In text-dominated scenarios, the eviction strategy surpasses standard LLM cache compression by adapting to short-context VLM.
AI.conclusion: AsymVLM demonstrates that exploiting the intrinsic asymmetry between vision and text tokens yields superior efficiency-accuracy trade-offs in VLMs, particularly for spatially localized visual information; the approach also adapts well to text-dominated regimes and surpasses existing compression baselines.

title: From XXLTraffic to EvoXXLTraffic: Scaling Traffic Forecasting to Sensor-Evolving Networks
summary: Existing traffic forecasting benchmarks assume a fixed sensor set, but real road-sensor networks grow continuously as the road network changes year by year. We introduce the XXLTraffic dataset family, which spans up to 27 years of California PeMS and Transport for NSW data. The fixed-sensor subsets of XXLTraffic support extremely long forecasting with multi-year gaps and standard hourly / daily long-horizon forecasting. We extend it to EvoXXLTraffic, a sensor-evolving reorganization that exposes per-year active sensors, yearly traffic-flow matrices, and yearly graph snapshots across nine PeMS districts, with growth ratios ranging from +305% to over +10,000%. We define a yearly streaming forecasting protocol on EvoXXLTraffic in which each calendar year is a continual task, and benchmark a wide range of representative baselines drawn from static spatio-temporal GNNs, naïve online schemes, evolving-graph continual methods, and retrieval / test-time methods. We find that our ultra-large evolutionary dataset better reflects the real world, and many state-of-the-art (SOTA) results no longer work. Our dataset complements existing benchmarks by enabling more realistic forecasting under ultra-long evolutionary road networks.

AI.tldr: Introduces XXLTraffic and EvoXXLTraffic, ultra-long and evolving traffic forecasting datasets spanning up to 27 years with sensor growth; benchmarks diverse baselines and reveals SOTA fragility under evolving sensor networks.
AI.motivation: Real road networks steadily grow and reconfigure; fixed-sensor benchmarks fail to capture long-horizon forecasting under sensor evolution. A dataset that encodes annual sensor activity and network changes enables realistic evaluation of forecasting methods.
AI.method: Assemble multi-year data from California PeMS and NSW; define fixed-sensor subsets for long-horizon tasks; construct EvoXXLTraffic with per-year active sensors, yearly traffic-flow matrices, and yearly graph snapshots across nine PeMS districts; establish a yearly streaming forecasting protocol where each year is a continual task; benchmark baselines spanning static spatio-temporal GNNs, naive online methods, evolving-graph continual methods, and retrieval/test-time approaches.
AI.result: The ultra-large, evolution-aware dataset better reflects real-world forecasting; sensor growth (e.g., +305% to >10,000%) challenges existing SOTA methods, causing performance degradation and prompting need for methods robust to changing sensor sets.
AI.conclusion: XXLTraffic and EvoXXLTraffic complement existing benchmarks by enabling realistic, ultra-long forecasting under evolving road networks and stimulus further research into robust, long-horizon forecasting under dynamic sensor configurations.

title: DocRetriever: A Plug-and-Play Framework for Multimodal Document Retrieval with Comprehensive Benchmark
summary: Multimodal documents contain diverse elements, such as tables, figures, and layouts, which can complicate retrieval tasks. While current approaches typically combine dense visual embedding models with supervised rerankers to achieve high-precision retrieval, they face inherent limitations. First, the coarse-grained nature of dense embeddings tends to obfuscate explicit semantics, failing to leverage structurally salient information. Second, supervised reranking models suffer from generalization bottlenecks, as their performance heavily relies on domain-specific training data. Furthermore, existing benchmarks often lack diverse assessment dimensions and comprehensive relevance annotations, limiting reliable evaluation. To address these challenges, we propose DocRetriever, a plug-and-play framework. It enhances visual retrieval via a layout-aware sparse embedding technique, enabling effective hybrid encoding without the overhead of optical character recognition (OCR). We also introduce a generalizable reranker that leverages reasoning-augmented demonstrations and optimized sampling to improve accuracy in few-shot settings. Finally, we construct a new benchmark, MultiDocR, to enable more rigorous evaluation. Experiments across diverse benchmarks validate DocRetriever's superiority over state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: DocRetriever introduces a plug-and-play framework for multimodal document retrieval that uses layout-aware sparse embeddings to enable hybrid encoding without OCR, a generalizable few-shot reranker based on reasoning-augmented demonstrations and optimized sampling, and a new benchmark MultiDocR for more robust evaluation. It claims superior performance over state-of-the-art methods across diverse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current dense visual embeddings with supervised rerankers struggle to preserve explicit structural semantics and generalize across domains. OCR-free, layout-aware representations are needed to leverage document structure, while benchmarks often lack diversity and comprehensive relevance annotations.
AI.method: 1) A layout-aware sparse embedding technique enabling hybrid encoding without OCR. 2) A generalizable reranker using reasoning-augmented demonstrations and optimized sampling for few-shot accuracy. 3) A new benchmark MultiDocR with diverse assessment dimensions and thorough relevance annotations.
AI.result: Experiments across diverse benchmarks show DocRetriever outperforms state-of-the-art methods, indicating improved accuracy and robustness in visual document retrieval without OCR and with better few-shot generalization.
AI.conclusion: DocRetriever provides a modular plug-and-play solution addressing semantic representation gaps and generalization bottlenecks in multimodal document retrieval, complemented by a rigorous new benchmark for evaluation; future work could extend to broader modalities, efficiency, and analysis of component contributions.

title: SCOPE: A Lightweight-training LLM Framework for Air Traffic Control Readback Monitoring
summary: Pilot readback of Air Traffic Control (ATC) voice instructions is a primary safeguard against miscommunication in air transportation. However, readback anomalies remain implicated in approximately 80% of aviation incidents. This vulnerability is further exacerbated by rising traffic volume and elevated cognitive workload, thereby motivating automated readback monitoring by machine. Traditional rule-based and machine learning approaches struggle to generalize across the highly variable and evolving phraseology of air traffic controller-pilot communications. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have opened a new avenue through their strong reasoning and generalization capabilities, existing approaches still face deployment and computational barriers in practice. In this work, we propose Semantic reasoning for Communication via Open-set Plug-in with Examples (SCOPE), a novel lightweight-training LLM framework that advances both the efficiency and accuracy of machine-based ATC readback monitoring. The core idea is to couple a plug-in open-set classifier with a carefully designed in-context learning mechanism on top of a frozen LLM. Extensive experiments on the semi-synthetic communication dataset show that SCOPE attains superior accuracy while delivering the low-latency response required for operational environments. Under a few-shot setting, SCOPE achieves 91.05% accuracy in open-set detection and corrects 96.63% of anomalous readbacks, thereby outperforming the strongest available baselines while providing explanations for its decisions. These findings demonstrate the potential of our framework as a practical pathway toward interpretable and controllable ATC readback monitoring.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, open-set plug-in with in-context learning on a frozen LLM (SCOPE) for ATC readback monitoring, achieving strong few-shot open-set detection and anomaly correction with low latency.
AI.motivation: ATC readbacks are critical safety checks; miscommunication contributes to aviation incidents; traditional rule-based/ML systems struggle to generalize amid evolving phraseology and growing traffic; there is a need for interpretable, real-time automated monitoring.
AI.method: SCOPE couples a plug-in open-set classifier with an in-context learning mechanism on top of a frozen LLM. It uses lightweight training data to enable open-set detection and explanations, while maintaining low latency suitable for operational settings.
AI.result: In few-shot experiments on a semi-synthetic dataset, SCOPE achieves 91.05% accuracy in open-set detection and corrects 96.63% of anomalous readbacks, outperforming strong baselines and providing explanations for its decisions.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates a practical pathway toward interpretable and controllable ATC readback monitoring, balancing accuracy, generalization, and latency; real-world validation and deployment considerations remain important.”

title: Croissant Tasks: A Metadata Format for Reproducible Machine Learning Evaluations
summary: Reproducibility is fundamental to the scientific method, yet remains a critical challenge in machine learning. Contributing factors include underspecified execution details and brittle software environments. Human-centric remedies, such as checklists and manual verification, help but require intensive effort and fail to scale. To address this, we introduce Croissant Tasks: a declarative, machine-actionable metadata format that abstracts low-level implementation details into high-level specifications. This format enables conceptual reproducibility: verifying claims via independent, agent-generated implementations rather than brittle source code replication. We contribute: (1) the Croissant Tasks specification, formally decoupling task problem from solution; (2) an automated LLM pipeline that retrofits existing benchmarks into this format; and (3) empirical validation showing autonomous agents can ingest these specifications to generate functional, accurate reproduction pipelines from scratch. We envision this format as a new foundation for automated and conceptual reproducibility in machine learning.

AI.tldr: Croissant Tasks offer a declarative, machine-actionable metadata format that separates the task from the solution to enable automated, concept-based reproducibility in ML. It includes a formal specification, an automated LLM-based pipeline to retrofit benchmarks, and empirical validation where autonomous agents generate functional reproduction pipelines from specs.
AI.motivation: Reproducibility in ML is hindered by underspecified execution details and brittle environments, and manual checklists do not scale. A machine-actionable, high-level specification could enable scalable, conceptual reproducibility.
AI.method: Introduce Croissant Tasks as a formal specification decoupling task definition from solution; develop an automated LLM-based pipeline to retrofit existing benchmarks into this format; empirically validate that autonomous agents can ingest specs and generate functional reproduction pipelines from scratch.
AI.result: Proposed Croissant Tasks specification; an automated LLM-driven retrofit pipeline; empirical demonstration that agents can create accurate reproduction pipelines from specifications.
AI.conclusion: Croissant Tasks could become a foundational tool for automated and conceptual reproducibility in machine learning, enabling scalable verification of claims via agent-generated implementations.

title: GenEraser: Generalizable Video Object Removal via Balanced Text-Mask Guidance and Decoupled Locator-Preserver
summary: Video object removal frequently struggles to simultaneously eliminate target objects and their associated physical effects (e.g., smoke, reflections, light, and ripples) in out-of-domain scenarios due to complex spatiotemporal ambiguities. While existing methods primarily rely on spatial masks, they often fail to capture weakly correlated effects, and the potential of explicit textual guidance remains underexplored. Furthermore, a fundamental optimization conflict exists in removal models between high-level semantic generalization and precise pixel-level background preservation. To address these challenges, we propose GenEraser, a novel framework for generalized and high-fidelity video object and effect removal. First, we introduce a Multi-Conditional Mixture-of-Experts (MC-MoE) paired with Bipartite Text guidance to fully exploit the multimodal priors of Diffusion Transformers, significantly enhancing the identification of complex effects. Second, a Learnable Deep ``CFG'' Fusion mechanism (LD-CFG) is developed to adaptively balance the relative dominance of mask and textual conditions across diverse scenarios. Finally, we propose a Decoupled Expert Architecture, comprising a Locator and a Preserver, to mitigate the inherent trade-off between semantic generalization and pixel alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our GenEraser surpasses recent state-of-the-art approaches, achieving significant quantitative improvements (e.g., $2.16$ dB and $1.44$ dB on the ROSE Benchmark and VOR-Eval, respectively) while maintaining exceptionally robust generalization in open-world scenarios. https://cyqii.github.io/GenEraser.github.io/

AI.tldr: GenEraser proposes a generalized, high-fidelity video object and effect removal framework using MC-MoE with bipartite text guidance, a learnable CFG fusion, and a decoupled Locator–Preserver architecture, achieving significant PSNR gains on ROSE and VOR-Eval and strong open-world generalization.
AI.motivation: Current methods struggle to remove objects and their associated effects in out-of-domain scenes due to spatiotemporal ambiguities and reliance on masks; there is a fundamental trade-off between semantic generalization and pixel-level preservation, and explicit textual guidance is underexplored.
AI.method: Introduce a Multi-Conditional Mixture-of-Experts (MC-MoE) paired with Bipartite Text guidance to leverage multimodal priors of Diffusion Transformers; a Learnable Deep CFG Fusion (LD-CFG) to adaptively balance mask vs textual conditions; a Decoupled Expert Architecture with a Locator and a Preserver to separate semantic localization from pixel-level restoration.
AI.result: Reported improvements of 2.16 dB on the ROSE Benchmark and 1.44 dB on VOR-Eval, with robust generalization in open-world scenarios, surpassing recent state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: GenEraser advances generalized, high-fidelity video object and effect removal; future work could explore efficiency, broader effect types, and more diverse datasets to further assess generalization and robustness.

title: Singularity-aware Optimization via Randomized Geometric Probing: Towards Stable Non-smooth Optimization
summary: Deep learning optimization relies heavily on the assumption of smooth loss landscapes, a condition systematically violated by modern architectures due to non-smooth components such as ReLU activations and quantization operators. In such non-smooth regimes, adaptive optimizers such as Adam suffer from gradient chattering, violent oscillations caused by conflicting signals within the Clarke subdifferential, leading to poor convergence and suboptimal generalization. To address this, we introduce Singularity-aware Adam (S-Adam), a novel optimizer that stabilizes training by dynamically modulating step sizes based on local geometric instability. Our key contribution is the Local Geometric Instability (LGI) metric, a computationally efficient estimator of the Clarke subdifferential diameter derived from the variance of randomized directional derivatives. S-Adam incorporates an adaptive damping mechanism exp(-$λ$$ρ$) that decelerates updates in high-instability regions while preserving fast convergence in smooth basins. We provide a rigorous convergence analysis using differential inclusions, proving that S-Adam converges almost surely to ($δ$,$ε$)-Clarke stationary points at the optimal O(1/$\sqrt(T)$) rate. Empirical evaluations on Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) and high-noise small-batch learning demonstrate that S-Adam consistently outperforms AdamW and Prox-SGD, achieving accuracy gains of up to 6 percent on CIFAR-100 and 3 percent on TinyImageNet while effectively mitigating gradient oscillations.

AI.tldr: S-Adam stabilizes optimization in non-smooth DL settings by using a Local Geometric Instability (LGI) metric to adapt step sizes, with a damping factor that slows updates in unstable regions. It converges almost surely to Clarke stationary points at O(1/√T) and shows empirical gains over AdamW and Prox-SGD on QAT and high-noise tasks.
AI.motivation: Non-smooth architectures (ReLU, quantization) induce gradient chattering and unstable training. Adam-style optimizers struggle in this regime; a geometry-aware mechanism is needed to damp oscillations and improve convergence.
AI.method: Introduce LGI, a computationally efficient estimator of the Clarke subdifferential diameter derived from variance of randomized directional derivatives. Integrate an adaptive damping exp(-λ ρ) to modulate step sizes in high-instability regions. Convergence analysis via differential inclusions to Clarke stationary points. Empirical validation on QAT and noisy small-batch learning.
AI.result: Theoretical: almost-sure convergence to (δ, ε)-Clarke stationary points at O(1/√T). Empirical: S-Adam surpasses AdamW and Prox-SGD, with accuracy gains up to 6% on CIFAR-100 and 3% on TinyImageNet, and reduced gradient oscillations.
AI.conclusion: S-Adam provides stability in non-smooth optimization with convergence guarantees and practical performance gains. LGI offers a scalable instability proxy suitable for adaptive optimizers in non-smooth DL contexts.

title: Certified Policy Optimisation for Nested Causal Bandits via PAC-Bayes Risk
summary: Critical sequential decisions are rarely single-timescale: a strategic decision causally shapes the context in which every subsequent tactical choice is made; standard bandit and reinforcement-learning theory does not capture this causal coupling between timescales. We formalise the problem class as Nested Contextual Causal Bandits (NCCBs), a hierarchical SCM where each level's action sets the next level's context distribution, and propose Nested Causal Thompson Sampling (NCTS), which draws one mechanism-factorised belief per episode and acts recursively under it. Our main theoretical result is a causal PAC-Bayesian excess-risk bound that certifies any candidate deployment policy from historic data alone, off-policy and anytime, answering the deployment question: can we trust this agent here, and at what risk? Experiments on a hierarchical SCM show that, against a matched RFF-GP joint regression on the same function class, the factorised SCM-mechanism posterior transfers significantly better zero-shot under exogenous distribution shifts, the recursive meta-to-inner commit significantly dominates the joint-commit alternative in distribution, and the certificate significantly contracts as offline data accumulates. Combining these results, we establish progressive certified handover, a safe-deployment method: each timescale flips from a legacy controller to NCTS when gains can be certified, independently of the others.

AI.tldr: Introduces Nested Contextual Causal Bandits (NCCBs) and Nested Causal Thompson Sampling (NCTS) for multi-timescale decisions, with a causal PAC-Bayesian excess-risk bound enabling offline, off-policy certification and a progressive safe deployment strategy across timescales.
AI.motivation: Standard bandit/RL theory fails to capture causal coupling across timescales where higher-level actions shape subsequent contexts; a formal model and deployment guarantees are needed for safe, sequential decisions.
AI.method: Formalizes NCCBs as a hierarchical structural causal model (SCM) where each level's action sets the next level's context distribution; proposes NCTS that samples mechanism-factorised beliefs per episode and acts recursively; proves a causal PAC-Bayesian excess-risk bound for deployment policies based on offline data; conducts experiments against a matched RFF-GP joint regression, comparing factorised posterior transfer and recursive meta-to-inner commitments.
AI.result: The bound certifies deployment policies from historical data off-policy and anytime; factorised SCM-mechanism posteriors transfer significantly better zero-shot under exogenous distribution shifts; recursive meta-to-inner commit dominates joint-commit across distributions; offline data accumulation tightens the certificate; experiments show improved safe transfer and robust performance under shifts.
AI.conclusion: Proposes progressive certified handover: a safe-deployment method where each timescale can flip from a legacy controller to NCTS once gains are certifiably safe, independently across timescales.

title: Towards Consistent Video Geometry Estimation
summary: This work presents ViGeo, a feed-forward foundation model for recovering spatially dense and temporally consistent geometry from video sequences. Built upon a plain transformer architecture without task-specific architectural modifications, ViGeo supports streaming, full-sequence, and long-video inference within a unified model. The key design is dynamic chunking attention, which exposes the model to both bidirectional and causal temporal contexts during training and allows it to adapt its attention pattern at test time without retraining. To improve supervision quality, we further introduce a completion-based data refinement framework. This framework trains a video depth completion teacher that conditions on sparse and noisy annotations and exploits video/multi-view context to produce dense, temporally coherent, and geometrically reliable training targets. Beyond depth and point maps, ViGeo also predicts surface normals within the same framework. Trained solely on public datasets, ViGeo achieves state-of-the-art performance across online, offline, and long-video depth estimation, surface normal estimation, and video point map estimation.

AI.tldr: ViGeo is a plain-transformer based video geometry model with dynamic chunking attention and a data-refinement training framework. It achieves state-of-the-art results on depth, surface normals, and video point maps across online, offline, and long-video settings, while supporting streaming and long sequences with no task-specific architectural changes.
AI.motivation: To obtain spatially dense and temporally consistent 3D geometry from video without task-specific model modifications. The work also tackles limited supervision by introducing a completion-based teacher that leverages sparse/noisy annotations and multi-view context to produce dense, coherent training targets.
AI.method: A plain transformer backbone with dynamic chunking attention that blends bidirectional and causal temporal contexts and adapts its attention pattern at test time.
