summary: Accurate long-range prediction of geophysical systems is difficult due to strongly nonlinear dynamics, the high computational cost of full-physics simulations, and the error accumulation that arise when one-step autoregressive surrogates are rolled out over decades. Deep neural network can serve as efficient emulators, but most are trained only for next-step prediction and often drift or become unstable as the forecast horizon grows. We propose a multi-horizon graph neural network emulator that learns state-to-state transitions from a single current time to multiple future lead times within one unified model. The physical domain is represented as a graph, where nodes correspond to spatial locations with time-varying geophysical attributes and edges encode local spatial interactions. Given the current graph state, the model predicts the future evolution of key fields, ice thickness and ice velocities at all nodes, using a shared graph backbone with separate output branches for each target variable. To improve stability, the network predicts state increments relative to the current state, which are then added back to reconstruct future states. Training jointly optimizes all lead times with a unified regression objective, and inference uses a coarse-to-fine rollout that advances with larger jumps and selectively refines with shorter jumps to reduce drift and avoid redundant computation. Experiments on multi-decadal Pine Island Glacier simulations show that our approach achieves higher long-range accuracy and improved stability than both (i) an initial-state baseline that predicts each future time directly from the starting state and (ii) a standard single-step autoregressive rollout, producing a more reliable emulator for downstream climate and sea-level studies.

AI.tldr: A multi-horizon graph neural network emulator predicts multi-step future states (ice thickness, ice velocity) of geophysical systems from a single current state, using state increments and a coarse-to-fine rollout to improve long-range accuracy and stability; validated on Pine Island Glacier with superior performance vs baselines.
AI.motivation: Long-range forecasting of nonlinear geophysical systems is difficult due to the high cost of full-physics simulations, error accumulation in one-step autoregressive surrogates, and the need for stable, scalable emulators across multiple lead times.
AI.method: Graph neural network with nodes as spatial locations and edges encoding local interactions; a shared backbone with separate output heads for each target variable; predicts state increments relative to the current state to reconstruct future states; trained jointly over multiple lead times; inference uses a coarse-to-fine rollout that uses larger jumps and refines with shorter jumps.
AI.result: The approach achieves higher long-range accuracy and improved stability compared with an initial-state baseline (direct from start state) and a standard single-step autoregressive rollout, on multi-decadal Pine Island Glacier simulations.
AI.conclusion: A unified multi-horizon GNN emulator with increment-based predictions and coarse-to-fine rollout delivers more reliable long-range forecasts for geophysical systems, beneficial for downstream climate and sea-level analyses.

title: Modularizing Educational LLM-Agency for Fostering Responsible Learning Assistance
comment: 12 pages, 2 figures (+ 2 in appendix), accepted at AISoLA 2025 (Track: Responsible and Trusted AI: An Interdisciplinary Perspective)
summary: The widespread adoption of AI chatbots in education will drastically change learning, making responsible deployment a critical concern. While large language models (LLMs) might have access to sources discussing insights from educational sciences, they are not particularly inclined to adhere to pedagogical concepts, risking negative effects on the learning process, such as a loss of transfer capabilities, critical thinking, or creativity. In this paper, we introduce an agentic AI chatbot architecture assisting students with exercise solving, specifically designed to contribute to more responsible AI use in education. We base our conceptual development on the identification of several desiderata for responsible LLM-based educational systems, argue for the structural shortcomings inherent in monolithic, out-of-the-box solutions, and instead suggest modularizing the agentic architecture. We propose specific modules for different stages of exercise solving, enabling incorporation of targeted pedagogical advice, guiding students through the learning process in a more controllable, transparent, and overseeable manner.

AI.tldr: Proposes a modular, agentic AI chatbot framework for education to promote responsible AI use and preserve core learning processes, addressing limitations of monolithic LLM systems.
AI.motivation: To ensure responsible deployment of AI in education and to protect learning outcomes (transfer, critical thinking, creativity) by moving beyond monolithic LLM-based approaches.
AI.method: Conceptual development identifying desiderata for responsible LLM-based educational systems; critique of monolithic solutions; design of a modular, agentic architecture with modules for different exercise-solving stages and pedagogical guidance.
AI.result: A modular, agentic chatbot architecture is proposed, with distinct modules for stages of solving exercises and targeted pedagogical advice, enabling greater controllability, transparency, and oversight; no empirical evaluation is reported.
AI.conclusion: Modular agentic designs offer a path to more responsible AI use in education by addressing the structural shortcomings of monolithic systems and enhancing pedagogical alignment.

title: A Fully Convolutional Approach to Denoising Structural Dynamics Data from X-Ray Photon Correlation Spectroscopy
summary: We present a fully convolutional denoising autoencoder (FC-DAE) for denoising two-time intensity-intensity correlation functions ($C_2$) in X-ray photon correlation spectroscopy (XPCS). Unlike conventional denoising autoencoders that are typically restricted to fixed input sizes, the FC-DAE accepts inputs of arbitrary dimensions while preserving correlation structures across diverse dynamical regimes. The model is trained using experimentally derived $C_2$ data collected at NSLS-II beamlines, with data augmentation applied to expand the diversity of the dataset and reduce overfitting. The FC-DAE successfully recovers intricate dynamical features in low signal-to-noise conditions while maintaining structural fidelity. To assess reconstruction reliability, we employ quantitative metrics to evaluate structural fidelity and identify potential model-induced bias. Our results demonstrate that the FC-DAE provides robust denoising performance with high computational efficiency, enabling recovery of XPCS dynamics under photon-limited and low-dose measurement conditions.

AI.tldr: Proposes a fully convolutional denoising autoencoder (FC-DAE) for denoising two-time correlation functions (C2) in XPCS; supports arbitrary input sizes; trained on experimental data with augmentation; shows robust denoising in photon-limited regimes and provides fidelity/bias metrics.
AI.motivation: XPCS data are photon-limited and noisy; conventional denoising autoencoders often require fixed input sizes, limiting applicability across dynamical regimes. A flexible, structure-preserving denoiser could improve reliability of extracted dynamics.
AI.method: Introduce FC-DAE architecture that accepts inputs of arbitrary dimensions while preserving correlation structures. Train on experimentally derived C2 data from NSLS-II with data augmentation to enhance diversity and mitigate overfitting. Use quantitative metrics to assess structural fidelity and detect model-induced bias during denoising.
AI.result: FC-DAE recovers intricate dynamical features under low SNR and maintains structural fidelity. It demonstrates robust denoising with high computational efficiency, enabling XPCS dynamics recovery under photon-limited and low-dose conditions.
AI.conclusion: The FC-DAE provides a flexible, efficient denoising solution for C2 in XPCS, preserving correlation structures across dynamical regimes. Its fidelity metrics and bias assessment support reliable deployment in photon-limited experiments.

title: Double-Edged Sword or Sharp Tool? Designing and Evaluating Triadic LLM-Teacher Collaboration for K-12 Writing at Scale
summary: The double-edged sword of integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) requires an effective triadic collaboration mechanism among LLMs, teachers and students, especially for K-12 education. By developing a triadic collaboration system to support K-12 writing learning, a multidimensional evaluation framework grounded in Systemic Functional Linguistics and the suggestion trajectory tracing pipeline, this paper contributes a large-scale empirical dataset involving $57,954$ essays from $10,195$ students across $120$ schools over two years. Our findings confirm the efficacy of this system in improving writing quality through a strategic labor division: the LLM serves as a generative engine to mitigate teacher burnout, and the teacher acts as a pedagogical gatekeeper and bridge to guarantee feedback quality. While both LLM and teacher are critical for skill improvement, we uncover a ceiling effect where excessive linguistic expansion yields diminishing marginal utility. These suggest a dynamically adaptive LLM-teacher collaboration as student proficiency increases.

AI.tldr: Triadic LLM–teacher–student system improves K-12 writing with large-scale data; shows efficacy but a ceiling effect; calls for adaptive collaboration as proficiency grows.
AI.motivation: Explore benefits and risks of integrating LLMs into K-12 writing education, aiming to reduce teacher burnout while maintaining feedback quality and provide a robust evaluation framework.
AI.method: Develop a triadic collaboration system and a multidimensional evaluation framework grounded in Systemic Functional Linguistics, plus a suggestion trajectory tracing pipeline; analyze a large-scale dataset (57,954 essays, 10,195 students, 120 schools, over two years) to assess impact and the division of labor between LLMs and teachers.
AI.result: LLM–teacher collaboration improves writing quality; LLM acts as a generative engine reducing teacher burnout; teachers serve as gatekeepers to ensure feedback quality; both LLM and teacher are critical for skill improvement; a ceiling effect observed where excessive linguistic expansion yields diminishing marginal utility; indicates need for dynamically adaptive LLM–teacher collaboration as student proficiency increases.
AI.conclusion: A triadic LLM–teacher–student model shows promise for K-12 writing education but requires adaptive strategies to optimize gains and feedback quality as student proficiency evolves; future work should focus on personalized adaptation and evaluation mechanisms.

title: GPIC: A Giant Permissive Image Corpus for Visual Generation
summary: Studying scalable methods for visual generative modeling requires large, accessible, and stable datasets. We introduce GPIC, a Giant Permissive Image Corpus of approximately 28 trillion pixels. GPIC comprises diverse internet images captioned by a state-of-the-art vision-language model, including 100M training, 200K validation, and 1M test examples. Moreover, all GPIC images are permissively licensed for both research and commercial use. GPIC is safety-filtered, deduplicated, and centrally hosted on Hugging Face. We provide a benchmarking protocol for generative modeling on GPIC. Finally, we provide a reference baseline for pixel-space flow matching on GPIC. Our dataset, benchmark, and models are available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/stanford-vision-lab/gpic. Evaluation toolkit and code are available at https://gpic.stanford.edu

AI.tldr: GPIC is a giant (≈28T pixels) permissively licensed image corpus for scalable visual generative modeling, with 100M training, 200K validation, and 1M test examples. Images are captioned by a vision-language model, safety-filtered, deduplicated, and hosted on Hugging Face; it provides a benchmarking protocol and a pixel-space flow-matching baseline. Resources and code are publicly available.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, accessible, and stable data for visual generative modeling by addressing data scale, licensing, safety, and evaluation challenges that limit current datasets.
AI.method: Assemble a massive image collection captioned by a state-of-the-art vision-language model; apply safety filtering and deduplication; license images permissively for research and commercial use; host centrally on Hugging Face; provide benchmarking protocol and a reference baseline for pixel-space flow matching; release dataset and tools publicly.
AI.result: GPIC comprises ~28 trillion pixels with 100M train, 200K validation, and 1M test examples; all images have permissive licenses for research and commercial use; safety-filtered and deduplicated; central hosting; benchmarking protocol; pixel-space flow-matching baseline; resources available online.
AI.conclusion: GPIC offers a scalable, permissive data resource for generative modeling research and evaluation, enabling broad use (research and commercial) and providing standardized benchmarks and baselines to facilitate progress.

title: Improving Adversarial Robustness of Attribution via Implicit Regularization
comment: 39 pages, 22 figures, to be published in International Conference on Machine Learning 2026
summary: The adversarial robustness of attributions is a fundamental requirement for reliable explainability in deep learning, yet existing approaches typically rely on computationally expensive explicit regularization. In this work, we show that attribution robustness can arise implicitly from the learning dynamics of standard stochastic gradient descent. We theoretically motivate this effect through connections between parameter-space and input-space curvature, and validate it across architectures, datasets, and attribution methods, with negligible computational overhead. In contrast, we prove that such robustness gains often does not transfer to attention-based attribution under softmax normalization, due to inherent entropy constraints, and we validate this limitation experimentally. Finally, we show that replacing softmax attention with kernel-based attention restores the robustness gains in transformer models. Our results highlight learning dynamics as a principled and practical mechanism for robust explainability, and reveal fundamental limitations of attention-based attribution under normalization.

AI.tldr: SGD dynamics implicitly yield attribution robustness across architectures, datasets, and attribution methods, with negligible overhead; softmax-based attention hinders robustness due to entropy constraints, while kernel-based attention recovers robustness in transformers.
AI.motivation: Robust explanations are essential for trustworthy AI, but many robustness strategies rely on costly explicit regularization. The work investigates whether standard training dynamics alone can confer robust attributions, and examines limits under attention normalization.
AI.method: Theoretically connect parameter-space and input-space curvature to explain robustness; empirically validate across models, datasets, and attribution methods; analyze attention-based attribution under softmax and test kernel-based attention in transformers.
AI.result: Robust attributions arise implicitly from SGD dynamics with little additional cost, across methods and architectures; softmax normalization constrains attention-based attribution, limiting robustness; kernel-based attention restores robustness gains in transformer models.
AI.conclusion: Learning dynamics offer a principled, practical mechanism for robust explainability and highlight fundamental limitations of attribution under softmax normalization; kernel-based attention can mitigate these limitations in attention-based architectures.

title: MIC: Maximizing Informational Capacity in Adaptive Representations via Isotropic Subspace Alignment
comment: Accepted at the GlobalSouthML Workshop at ICML 2026. 13 pages, 2 figures
summary: Although multi-scales representation learning enables elastic-dimension embeddings, nested subspaces often suffer from dimensional redundancy and spectral collapse. To address this, we introduce MIC, a framework that optimizes the geometric landscape of multi-granular embeddings through isotropic subspace alignment. MIC employs Soft Collapse Regularization (SCR) to mitigate redundancy between prefix and residual subspaces via cross-correlation penalties, alongside Spectral Isotropy Regularization (SIR) to ensure hyper-spherical uniformity in low-dimensional prefixes. By unifying these strategies through a self-distillation objective, MIC generates semantically dense representations that maintain high discriminative power. Our experiments demonstrate that MIC significantly outperforms standard baselines, particularly in high-compression scenarios where maintaining informational capacity is most critical.

AI.tldr: MIC: a framework for multi-granular embeddings that reduces redundancy and enforces isotropy in prefixes via SCR and SIR, unified by self-distillation, achieving improved performance under high compression.
AI.motivation: Nested subspaces in multi-scale representations exhibit dimensional redundancy and spectral collapse, limiting semantic density and discriminative power under compression.
AI.method: Introduce MIC with Soft Collapse Regularization to penalize cross-correlation between prefix and residual subspaces; Spectral Isotropy Regularization to enforce unit-sphere uniformity in low-dimensional prefixes; unify via self-distillation objective.
AI.result: Empirical results show MIC significantly outperforms baselines, particularly in high-compression regimes where preserving information is critical.
AI.conclusion: MIC provides semantically dense, high-discrimination embeddings by aligning subspaces isotropically and reducing redundancy, proving effective for elastic-dimension representation learning.

title: When Should Models Change Their Minds? Contextual Belief Management in Large Language Models
summary: Long-horizon interactions require language models to manage accumulating information: when to update their state, when to preserve their state, and what to ignore. We study this challenge as \textbf{Contextual Belief Management (CBM)}: maintaining a predicted belief state aligned with formal evidence while isolating task-irrelevant noise. To make CBM measurable, we introduce BeliefTrack, a closed-world benchmark spanning Rule Discovery and Circuit Diagnosis, where a finite belief space and symbolic verifiers enable exact turn-level evaluation. BeliefTrack diagnoses three failures: Failed Stay, Failed Update, and Failed Isolation. Across multiple LLMs, vanilla models exhibit severe CBM failures, while explicit belief-tracking prompts provide limited gains. In contrast, reinforcement learning with belief-state rewards reduces failure rates by 70.9\% on average. Further probing reveals latent belief-state dynamics behind these failures, and representation-level steering reduces failure rates by 46.1\% across two tasks\footnote{Code is coming soon at https://github.com/zjunlp/CBM.

AI.tldr: Introduces Contextual Belief Management (CBM) and BeliefTrack to study how LLMs manage long-horizon information, identifies three failure modes, and shows RL with belief-state rewards and representation-level steering outperform baseline belief-tracking prompts.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon interactions require maintaining a belief state aligned with formal evidence while isolating task-irrelevant noise; current LLMs struggle with when to update, stay, or ignore information.
AI.method: Establish BeliefTrack, a closed-world benchmark with a finite belief space and symbolic verifiers enabling exact turn-level evaluation. Diagnose CBM failures (Failed Stay, Failed Update, Failed Isolation) across multiple LLMs. Compare explicit belief-tracking prompts, reinforcement learning with belief-state rewards, and representation-level steering. Include probing to reveal latent belief-state dynamics; code forthcoming.
AI.result: Vanilla LLMs exhibit severe CBM failures; explicit belief-tracking prompts yield limited gains; RL with belief-state rewards reduces failure rates by 70.9% on average; representation-level steering reduces failure rates by 46.1% across two tasks; probing reveals latent dynamics; BeliefTrack enables exact turn-level evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Explicit belief management helps but is limited; RL-based belief-state optimization and representation-level steering significantly improve CBM performance. The BeliefTrack benchmark provides a precise evaluation framework for long-horizon belief management, with code to come.

title: YoCausal: How Far is Video Generation from World Model? A Causality Perspective
summary: As video diffusion models (VDMs) advance toward world models, a key question arises: do they truly understand causality, or merely overfit to statistical temporal patterns? Existing benchmarks mostly rely on synthetic data, limiting real-world generalization due to the sim-to-real gap. We present YoCausal, a two-level benchmark inspired by the Violation of Expectation (VoE) paradigm from cognitive science. By temporally reversing real-world videos at zero cost as natural counterfactual samples, YoCausal establishes an arbitrarily extensible evaluation protocol. Level 1 introduces the Reverse Surprise Index (RSI), quantifying arrow-of-time perception via denoising loss. Level 2 introduces the Causality Cognition Index (CCI), which leverages a VLM to stratify datasets into causal and non-causal subsets, disentangling genuine causal reasoning from temporal bias. Evaluation of 13 state-of-the-art VDMs reveals that perceiving the arrow of time does not imply understanding causality, and a significant gap persists relative to human-level causal cognition.

AI.tldr: YoCausal proposes a two-level, real-world VoE-based benchmark for video diffusion models to test causal understanding beyond temporal patterns; Level 1 (RSI) uses denoising loss to measure arrow-of-time perception, Level 2 (CCI) uses a VLM to separate causal vs non-causal data; findings show arrow-of-time awareness does not imply true causality and models lag behind human causal cognition.
AI.motivation: Current VDM benchmarks rely heavily on synthetic data, risking poor real-world generalization due to sim-to-real gaps. It remains unclear if VDMs truly reason about causality or merely exploit temporal statistics. A scalable, real-world benchmark is needed to evaluate causal cognition beyond temporal biases.
AI.method: Introduce YoCausal, a two-level VoE-inspired benchmark. Temporally reverse real-world videos at zero cost to create counterfactuals. Level 1: Reverse Surprise Index (RSI) quantifies arrow-of-time perception via denoising loss. Level 2: Causality Cognition Index (CCI) uses a Vision-Language Model to stratify datasets into causal vs non-causal subsets, separating genuine causal reasoning from temporal bias.
AI.result: Evaluation of 13 state-of-the-art video diffusion models shows that perceiving the arrow of time does not equate to causal understanding. There remains a substantial gap to human-level causal cognition in these models.
AI.conclusion: YoCausal offers an extensible, real-world benchmark protocol to probe causal reasoning in video models, revealing that current VDMs struggle with genuine causal cognition despite detecting temporal order, and highlighting the need for progress toward human-level causal reasoning.

title: Test Time Training for Supervised Causal Learning
summary: Supervised Causal Learning (SCL) has shown promise in causal discovery by framing it as a supervised learning problem. However, it suffers from significant out-of-distribution generalization challenges. We reveal three limitations of previous SCL practices: a significant performance gap between synthetic benchmarks and real-world data, fragility to distribution shifts, and failure in compositional generalization, collectively questioning its real-world applicability. To address this, we propose Test-Time Training for Supervised Causal Learning (TTT-SCL), a novel framework that dynamically generates training sets explicitly aligned with any specific test instance. We demonstrate the correlation between TTT-SCL and score-based methods, and design an efficient module for generating training sets based on the classic scoring function. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks, pseudo-real and real-world datasets demonstrate that TTT-SCL significantly outperforms existing SCL and traditional causal discovery methods.

AI.tldr: TTT-SCL introduces test-time training to supervised causal learning, dynamically creating test-instance-aligned training data to improve out-of-distribution and compositional generalization, showing strong gains over SCL and traditional causal discovery on synthetic and real-world data.
AI.motivation: Address key weaknesses of prior SCL: large gap between synthetic benchmarks and real data, sensitivity to distribution shifts, and poor compositional generalization, which impede real-world applicability.
AI.method: Introduce Test-Time Training for Supervised Causal Learning (TTT-SCL). At test time, generate training sets tailored to the specific test instance, using a module that leverages a classic scoring function to guide training set construction. Establish correlations with score-based methods.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic benchmarks, pseudo-real, and real-world datasets shows that TTT-SCL significantly outperforms existing SCL methods and traditional causal discovery baselines.
AI.conclusion: TTT-SCL effectively mitigates out-of-distribution and compositional generalization issues in supervised causal learning by aligning training with each test instance, with strong empirical performance and potential for real-world deployment.

title: mcp-proto-okn: Natural-language access to open scientific knowledge graphs through the Model Context Protocol
summary:
MCP Server Proto-OKN (mcp-proto-okn) is a Python-based Model Context Protocol server that enables AI assistants to discover, inspect, query and integrate scientific knowledge graphs through natural language. The server provides graph routing, schema inspection, SPARQL execution, ontology expansion, multi-graph querying, and transcript generation, lowering the barrier to cross-domain knowledge graph analysis for biomedical and scientific users.
  mcp-proto-okn is implemented in Python using the FastMCP framework and is available at https://github.com/sbl-sdsc/mcp-proto-okn. Documentation, client configuration instructions, and example analysis transcripts are provided in the GitHub repository.

AI.tldr: A Python-based MCP server enabling AI assistants to discover, inspect, query, and integrate scientific knowledge graphs via natural language, featuring graph routing, schema inspection, SPARQL, ontology expansion, multi-graph querying, and transcript generation; built on FastMCP; released on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Lower barriers to cross-domain knowledge graph analysis for biomedical and scientific users by providing a flexible, NL-driven interface over multiple graphs.
AI.method: Implement the Model Context Protocol server in Python using the FastMCP framework; supports graph routing, schema inspection, SPARQL execution, ontology expansion, multi-graph querying, and transcript generation; includes documentation and example transcripts; facilitates integration with diverse knowledge graphs.
AI.result: Open-source implementation with a GitHub repository, documentation, and example transcripts enabling researchers to perform natural-language–driven knowledge-graph tasks.
AI.conclusion: Offers a practical, extensible tool to connect AI assistants to scientific knowledge graphs, potentially accelerating data integration and analysis across domains.

title: NeuROK: Generative 4D Neural Object Kinematics
summary: Data-driven approaches have revolutionized 3D vision, enabling transformers to effectively reconstruct and generate static 3D objects. However, generating simulative 4D dynamics -- realistic temporal deformations of static objects under various physical conditions -- remains challenging and often ad hoc, despite its importance in building comprehensive 3D world models. Most existing methods assume a predefined physical model and use system identification to estimate parameters, restricting these methods to specific categories and small-scale datasets. We propose that these restrictions can be overcome by learning a data-driven kinematic state parameterization for object-centric physical systems. Specifically, we learn both a latent space representing all possible states of the object and a decoder that maps any sampled latent to a plausibly deformed shape of the object. We refer to this parameterization as Neural Object Kinematics (NeuROK), and learn a transformer-based encoder-decoder model on a curated large-scale 4D dataset. This formulation and the learned model significantly simplify the generation of simulative dynamics since we only need to consider the dynamics within a low-dimensional latent space from the Lagrangian mechanics' perspective in classical physics. We demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of this neural simulation framework across diverse dynamic object types, showing clear advantages over prior works. Project page: https://chen-geng.com/neurok

AI.tldr: A data-driven, transformer-based latent kinematic parameterization (NeuROK) for 4D object dynamics; learns a latent space and a decoder to produce plausible deformations, enabling dynamics in a compact space and generalizing across object types, demonstrated on large-scale 4D data with advantages over prior model-based methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional physics-based system identification constrains dynamics to predefined models and small datasets; a scalable, data-driven approach is needed to handle diverse, complex 4D object dynamics in object-centric scenes.
AI.method: Train a transformer-based encoder-decoder to learn a latent space capturing all object states and a decoder mapping latent vectors to plausibly deformed shapes. Use a large curated 4D dataset. Frame dynamics within the reduced latent space aligned with Lagrangian mechanics.
AI.result: NeuROK achieves effective, generalizable 4D dynamics across diverse object types, with clear advantages over prior approaches in flexibility and scalability, reducing reliance on explicit physical models.
AI.conclusion: A generalizable neural simulation framework that enables data-driven 4D dynamics by learning a low-dimensional state parameterization and shape decoder, offering a versatile alternative to model-driven dynamics for comprehensive 3D world models.

title: ProjectionBench: Evaluating Scientific Hypothesis Generation in LLMs Under Progressive Information Disclosure
summary: Scientific discovery is an inherently creative and uncertain process, requiring reasoning beyond the recall of known knowledge. While many benchmarks have been proposed to evaluate large language model (LLM) performance on deep research tasks via multi-hop retrieval, their innovative reasoning abilities essential for true scientific discovery remain largely untested. We introduce a benchmark framework for evaluating model performance in scientific discovery and reasoning, building up from a raw problem to the classical null hypothesis test. In our framework, models initially receive only the topic and research question from a recent paper, with technical details progressively revealed. At each stage of information disclosure, the model is tasked with generating hypotheses that address the research question, which is compared with the conclusions from the original paper and evaluated via automated semantic similarity of constituent atomic claims. This progressive evaluation of semantic divergence from ground-truth conclusions enables assessment of a model's innovativeness (under minimal information) to grounded reasoning capabilities (under full experimental details), both critical for using LLMs for scientific discovery purposes. Our framework provides a foundation for systematically evaluating scientific reasoning and discovery capabilities in LLMs, crucial for advancing the development of next-generation AI scientist/co-scientist systems. Specifically, here we evaluate GPT-5, GPT-5.4, Gemini 2.5 pro, and Gemini 3.1 pro preview across 45 papers spanning bioactive materials, mechanical materials, and nanomaterials. We find that GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 pro outperform their previous generation counterparts as expected, and GPT-5.4 in particular maintains 0.7 F1 score alignment with ground truth conclusions even under minimal context.

AI.tldr: A progressive framework to evaluate scientific discovery in LLMs by disclosing information stepwise and comparing generated hypotheses to ground-truth conclusions via semantic similarity; applied to 45 papers across bioactive materials, mechanical materials, and nanomaterials; newer models (GPT-5.4, Gemini 3.1 pro) show improved performance, with GPT-5.4 achieving ~0.7 F1 alignment under minimal context.
AI.motivation: To test and quantify true scientific reasoning and innovativeness in LLMs beyond retrieval benchmarks, addressing the need for AI systems capable of genuine scientific discovery.
AI.method: Progressive information disclosure: start with topic and research question; gradually reveal technical details; at each stage, the model generates hypotheses addressing the question; evaluate reasoning by semantic similarity of generated atomic claims to the original paper's conclusions; comparison yields a measure of innovativeness (low-information) and grounded reasoning (full context); evaluation spans 45 papers in bioactive materials, mechanical materials, and nanomaterials.
AI.result: Newer models (GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 pro) outperform previous generations; GPT-5.4 maintains 0.7 F1 alignment with ground-truth conclusions even under minimal context.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a systematic foundation for evaluating scientific reasoning and discovery capabilities in LLMs, enabling development of AI scientist/co-scientist systems. Results indicate that larger, more capable models exhibit stronger alignment with ground-truth conclusions and improved innovative reasoning under progressive information disclosure; potential for broader domain applicability and further methodological refinements.

title: AdaState: Self-Evolving Anchors for Streaming Video Generation
summary: Autoregressive video diffusion models generate streaming video by producing frames sequentially, conditioning each chunk on previously generated content. These models are structurally anchored to the first frame: its key-value representation occupies a privileged position in the attention cache and serves as the primary scene reference throughout generation. As the cleanest and most error-free position in the cache, this anchor draws disproportionate attention, suppressing video dynamics, and locking scene composition to the initial viewpoint even as the scene naturally evolves. The result is a temporally shallow video in which motion, camera movement, and scene progression are dampened in favor of static consistency. To address this, we replace the static anchor with an adaptive state, a hidden latent that the model denoises alongside content at every chunk but never renders. Rather than referencing a frozen first frame, the model generates its own scene anchor at each step by attending to both the previous state and the current content, producing a reference that evolves with the generated content. Unlike standard video generation, which encodes an absolute notion of time, our formulation treats time as relative: every generation step sees the same positional structure regardless of how far generation has progressed, and the state transition is identical at every chunk. Together, these properties introduce a recurrence into the generation process, where denoising serves as the transition function, and the KV cache serves as the carrier, requiring no external module. Experiments demonstrate that the adaptive state substantially improves video dynamics, enabling richer motion and natural scene progression within generated videos.

AI.tldr: Adaptive state replaces the static first-frame anchor in autoregressive video diffusion, enabling a recurrent-like mechanism where a hidden latent denoises with content to form a evolving scene anchor. This yields improved motion dynamics and natural scene progression by treating time as relative and reusing the KV cache as the carrier.
AI.motivation: Current autoregressive video diffusion methods hinge on the first frame as a privileged reference, causing over-stable scenes and dampened dynamics. An adaptive, recurrent anchor aims to capture evolving scene structure and motion.
AI.method: Introduce an adaptive latent state that is denoised at every chunk and used to generate a scene anchor. The model attends to both the previous state and the current content to produce an evolving anchor, while time is treated relatively with identical transition at each step; the denoising process serves as the transition function with the KV cache as the carrier, requiring no external module.
AI.result: Experiments show that the adaptive state substantially improves video dynamics, enabling richer motion and natural scene progression.
AI.conclusion: Recasting video generation as a recurrence with a denoising-defined transition and a evolving anchor improves temporal dynamics; the approach maintains a lightweight, cache-based architecture without external modules.

title: Masked Diffusion Modeling for Anomaly Detection
summary: Anomaly detection aims to identify samples that deviate from the nominal data distribution and is central to many safety-critical applications. However, developing effective anomaly detection methods for categorical, mixed-type, and discrete sequence data remains challenging and relatively underexplored. Masked diffusion models provide a natural way to model such data by learning to recover masked values from the remaining visible context. In this paper, we propose Masked Diffusion for Anomaly Detection (MaskDiff-AD), a forward-only method based on masked diffusion models trained only on nominal data. Given a test sample, MaskDiff-AD constructs anomaly scores from the difficulty of reconstructing randomly masked coordinates, yielding a content-sensitive score that operates directly on discrete state spaces while avoiding reverse-time sampling. We also develop a non-parametric variant of MaskDiff-AD and provide theoretical guarantees by characterizing Type-I and Type-II errors under a fixed detection threshold. Experiments on fourteen categorical and mixed-type tabular datasets from ADBench and UADAD, as well as four text anomaly detection datasets from NLP-ADBench, show that MaskDiff-AD achieves competitive performance against classical, diffusion-based, and recent tabular/text anomaly detection baselines. Notably, MaskDiff-AD achieves the best overall average rank, outperforming all twelve tabular baseline methods.

AI.tldr: Masked Diffusion for Anomaly Detection (MaskDiff-AD) is a forward-only diffusion approach trained on nominal data to score anomalies by reconstructing masked coordinates, effective for categorical and mixed-type data; includes a non-parametric variant and theoretical Type-I/II guarantees; achieves top average ranking on multiple tabular and text datasets.
AI.motivation: Anomaly detection for discrete, categorical, and mixed-type data remains challenging due to complex state spaces and inefficiency of reverse-time diffusion. A forward-only, content-sensitive method that operates directly on discrete states can improve practicality and performance.
AI.method: Train masked diffusion models on nominal data and, at test time, compute anomaly scores from the difficulty of reconstructing randomly masked coordinates. This forward-only approach avoids reverse-time sampling and yields content-sensitive scores suitable for discrete state spaces. A non-parametric variant is also introduced, with theoretical guarantees on error rates (Type-I and Type-II) under a fixed detection threshold.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on fourteen categorical/mixed-type tabular datasets from ADBench and UADAD, plus four text anomaly datasets from NLP-ADBench, shows MaskDiff-AD is competitive with classical, diffusion-based, and recent baselines. It achieves the best overall average rank among tabular baselines, outperforming all twelve compared methods.
AI.conclusion: MaskDiff-AD provides a practical forward-only diffusion-based anomaly detector for discrete and mixed data, delivering content-sensitive anomaly scores and theoretical guarantees, with strong cross-domain performance and a non-parametric variant for robustness.

title: MIRA: Mid-training Rubric Anchoring for Source-Aware Data Selection
summary: Mid-training has become an important stage in modern LLM development, using large-scale curated mixtures to strengthen capabilities before final post-training. Its data selection problem is distinct: the data are optimized under a pretraining-style objective at near-pretraining scale, but are curated toward downstream capabilities and drawn from heterogeneous sources with different formats and training roles. As a result, effective selection requires both scalability and source-adaptive semantic criteria. Existing model-based methods scale well, but provide only implicit quality signals. Semantic selection methods offer stronger judgments, but usually assume fixed rubrics or standardized data formats. To address this mismatch, we propose MIRA, a source-aware filtering framework based on self-anchored rubric discovery. The key idea is to make rubric construction part of data selection: MIRA first discovers what should be evaluated for each source group, then distills those judgments into scalable student scorers for full-corpus filtering. On code-oriented mid-training with 21 sources and 5 source groups, MIRA outperforms selection baselines across nine code benchmarks and matches the full-corpus run while using only half the tokens.

AI.tldr: MIRA is a source-aware, self-anchored rubric discovery framework for mid-training data selection that learns source-specific evaluation rubrics and distills them into scalable scorers, achieving competitive performance with about half the tokens in code-oriented mid-training.
AI.motivation: Mid-training data selection is challenging due to heterogeneous data sources, varying formats, and the need to balance pretraining-like objectives with downstream capabilities; existing methods struggle to provide both scalable and semantic judgments across sources.
AI.method: MIRA jointly discovers evaluation rubrics for each source group (self-anchored rubric discovery) and distills these judgments into scalable student scorers for full-corpus filtering, enabling source-adaptive mid-training data selection; demonstrated on 21 sources divided into 5 source groups for code.
AI.result: MIRA outperforms selection baselines across nine code benchmarks and matches the full-corpus run while using only half the tokens.
AI.conclusion: Integrating rubric discovery into the data selection loop yields source-adaptive, scalable mid-training data filtering that preserves performance while improving data efficiency.

title: VideoMLA: Low-Rank Latent KV Cache for Minute-Scale Autoregressive Video Diffusion
summary: Long-rollout causal video diffusion has converged on a fixed-size sliding-window KV cache, with recent progress innovating within this layout by changing which tokens occupy the window or how their positions are encoded. The per-head KV layout itself, a dominant contributor to streaming memory and latency, has been mostly left unchanged. In this paper, we present the first study of Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) in video diffusion. VideoMLA replaces per-head keys and values with a shared low-rank content latent and a shared decoupled 3D-RoPE positional key, reducing per-token KV memory by 92.7% at every cached layer. We further investigate why MLA succeeds in video diffusion even though the spectral assumption often used to motivate it in language models does not hold: pretrained video attention is not low-rank, with 99%-energy effective rank far above any practical latent dimension. VideoMLA retains quality at compression ratios where direct spectral approximation would predict large reconstruction error. We show that the MLA bottleneck, rather than the pretrained spectrum, determines the effective rank: both spectral and random initialization occupy nearly the full rank budget from initialization, and training preserves this budget while adapting within it. On VBench, VideoMLA matches short-horizon streaming video diffusion baselines, achieves the best overall score at long horizons among evaluated methods, and improves throughput by 1.23x on a single B200.

AI.tldr: Introduces Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) for video diffusion to drastically reduce KV memory and improve throughput while preserving quality, achieving 92.7% per-token KV memory reduction, competitive long-horizon performance, and 1.23x throughput on a single B200.
AI.motivation: Long-rollout causal video diffusion uses a sliding-window KV cache; per-head KV representations dominate memory and latency. There is a need to reduce memory/compute without sacrificing quality, especially for long-horizon generation.
AI.method: Proposes MLA by replacing per-head keys/values with a shared low-rank content latent and a shared decoupled 3D-RoPE positional key. Analyzes why MLA works in video diffusion despite the spectrum not being low-rank; conducts experiments on VBench to compare against baselines and measure memory, quality, and throughput.
AI.result: Achieves 92.7% reduction in per-token KV memory at every cached layer. VideoMLA matches short-horizon baselines and yields the best overall score at long horizons among evaluated methods. Throughput improves by 1.23x on a single B200. Spectral arguments for low-rank structure do not hold in pretrained video attention; the MLA bottleneck governs the effective rank and training preserves the budget.
AI.conclusion: MLA provides a practical and scalable approach to efficient video diffusion attention, showing that shared latent keys/values with decoupled positional keys can retain quality while greatly reducing memory, and challenges conventional low-rank assumptions by identifying the MLA bottleneck as the determinant of effective rank.

title: Ridge Regression from Poisson Resetting: A Renewal Perspective on Spectral Regularization
summary: We connect stochastic resetting from non-equilibrium statistical physics with ridge regularization in statistical learning. For linear gradient flow, resetting to the origin at rate $r$ produces stationary mean $(X^\top X+rI)^{-1}X^\top y$, exactly the ridge estimator with penalty $λ=r$. This uses the known Laplace-transform relationship between ridge regression and exponential-time averaging of gradient flow, with the exponential time now interpreted as the stationary age associated with Poisson resetting. We then extend this identity to general renewal reset laws: the exponential reset time distribution is the unique renewal law whose stationary mean reproduces scalar ridge in every eigendirection as an exact filter identity for every positive curvature, while non-exponential renewal laws generate alternative spectral filters. At the fluctuation level, we study a separate additive Ornstein-Uhlenbeck extension with constant diffusion, interpreted as a stylized SGD approximation. In this setting, the equality holds only at the level of the mean, since the reset process has a nonzero stationary covariance from accumulated OU noise and reset-timing variance, whereas deterministic ridge is a fixed estimator with the same center. Stylized experiments compare the deterministic renewal-induced filters directly and illustrate when filters induced by non-exponential reset-time laws can differ predictively from ridge. The results for the stationary mean and the induced spectral filters are established for continuous-time gradient flow with isotropic resetting on quadratic objectives; the covariance and risk formulas additionally assume additive noise with state-independent covariance.

AI.tldr: Stochastic resetting can reproduce ridge regression as the stationary mean of a linear gradient flow; exponential resetting yields exact ridge across all eigendirections; non-exponential reset laws generate alternative spectral filters; with Ornstein-Uhlenbeck noise, equality holds only at the mean; reset timing thus shapes regularization.
AI.motivation: Bridge non-equilibrium stochastic-process techniques (resetting) with regularization in statistical learning (ridge), uncovering how reset timing acts as a spectral filter in gradient flows and generalizing beyond exponential resets.
AI.method: Derive the stationary mean via a Laplace-transform relation between ridge regression and exponential-time averaging of gradient flow; show exponential resetting is the unique renewal law that reproduces ridge in every eigendirection; extend to general renewal reset laws; analyze an additive Ornstein-Uhlenbeck extension to study fluctuations; provide spectral analysis on quadratic objectives with isotropic resetting and state-independent noise.
AI.result: For linear gradient flow, resetting to the origin at rate r yields the stationary mean (X^T X + r I)^{-1} X^T y, the ridge estimator with λ = r; exponential reset is uniquely tied to ridge across all eigendirections; non-exponential renewal laws create alternate spectral filters; in the OU-noise extension, the equality holds only at the mean due to nonzero stationary covariance from reset-timing variance; stylized experiments compare renewal-induced filters and demonstrate potential predictive differences from ridge; results established for continuous-time gradient flow with isotropic resetting on quadratic objectives, with covariance/risk formulas under additive noise assumptions.
AI.conclusion: The work recasts ridge regularization as a consequence of stochastic reset dynamics, showing reset-law choice dictates the induced spectral filter; while mean-level equivalence to ridge holds for exponential resets, fluctuations reveal differences, suggesting new avenues to design regularizers via reset timing and to study its behavior beyond the mean on more general objectives.

title: Demystifying Data Organization for Enhanced LLM Training
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized various fields, yet their training efficiency is heavily reliant on effective data curation. While data selection has been widely studied, the strategic data organization for enhanced training remains an underexplored area, particularly since current LLMs are often trained for only one or a few epochs. This paper systematically explores the influence of data organization on LLM training by reusing pre-computed sample-level scores originally generated for data efficiency, thereby incurring minimal additional computational overhead. We identify and formalize four key guidelines for optimizing data organization: Boundary Sharpening, Cyclic Scheduling, Curriculum Continuity, and Local Diversity. Guided by them, we introduce two novel data ordering methods termed STR and SAW. Extensive experiments across different model scales and data sizes, encompassing both pre-training and SFT stages, validate the effectiveness of our summarized guidelines. They also demonstrate the robustness of our proposed data ordering methods in enhancing the stability and performance of LLM training. Github Link: https://github.com/microsoft/data-efficacy/

AI.tldr: Systematic data organization via precomputed sample scores improves LLM training efficiency and stability; two ordering methods STR and SAW operationalize four guidelines (Boundary Sharpening, Cyclic Scheduling, Curriculum Continuity, Local Diversity) with broad empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Data organization during LLM training is an underexplored knob compared to data selection. When training is short (few epochs or limited iterations), the order of data can significantly impact convergence and final performance. Reusing precomputed per-sample scores enables exploring ordering with minimal overhead.
AI.method: Formalize four data-ordering guidelines: Boundary Sharpening, Cyclic Scheduling, Curriculum Continuity, Local Diversity. Propose two data-ordering methods, STR and SAW, that leverage precomputed sample-level scores. Validate across model scales, data sizes, and training stages (pre-training and SFT) with extensive experiments, including ablations and robustness checks.
AI.result: Empirical results show that following the guidelines and applying STR/SAW lead to improved training stability and higher performance across scales and stages, with robustness to data size variations and model size.
AI.conclusion: Data organization is a practical, low-overhead lever to enhance LLM training. The four guidelines provide a principled framework, and STR/SAW offer concrete ordering strategies that generalize across settings; results motivate integrating organization with other data-efficiency techniques.

title: GMOS: Grounding Moving Object Segmentation in 3D Space and Time
summary: Moving Object Segmentation (MOS) aims to discover, segment, and track objects that move independently of the camera. Current MOS methods, however, exhibit two fundamental limitations: they rely on pre-computed 2D auxiliary modalities such as optical flow or point trajectories that lack 3D geometric information, and they treat motion as a sequence-level attribute, overlooking the instantaneous motion state of each object. We address both by grounding MOS in 3D space and time, and propose GMOS, a framework that operates directly on RGB video to produce 3D-aware, temporally fine-grained segmentation of multiple moving objects, alongside a foreground--background variant GMOS-S for faster deployment. To support training and evaluation in this regime, we curate GMOS-2K, a dataset of 2,210 real-world videos with per-object temporal motion annotations drawn from five established Video Object Segmentation (VOS) benchmarks, and formalise MOS-I ("I" for instantaneous), a temporally fine-grained evaluation protocol with three complementary metrics. GMOS achieves state-of-the-art results across MOS, MOS-I, and Unsupervised VOS benchmarks, while running significantly faster than prior multi-object MOS methods and supporting online inference for streaming deployment.

AI.tldr: GMOS introduces 3D-aware, temporally fine-grained MOS directly from RGB video, with a fast GMOS-S variant, plus GMOS-2K dataset and MOS-I evaluation; achieves state-of-the-art and supports online inference.
AI.motivation: Current MOS methods rely on pre-computed 2D auxiliary modalities lacking 3D geometric information and treat motion as a sequence-level attribute, limiting geometric fidelity and online applicability.
AI.method: Ground MOS in 3D space-time by operating directly on RGB video to produce per-object segmentation with instantaneous motion states; propose GMOS and a faster GMOS-S for multi-object MOS; introduce GMOS-2K with per-object temporal annotations; formalize MOS-I, a temporally fine-grained evaluation protocol with three complementary metrics; enable online inference.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on MOS, MOS-I, and Unsupervised VOS benchmarks; faster than prior multi-object MOS methods; supports online streaming deployment.
AI.conclusion: GMOS advances MOS by integrating 3D geometry and instantaneous motion modeling, enabling accurate, real-time multi-object segmentation from RGB; the GMOS-2K dataset and MOS-I protocol support robust evaluation and deployment.

title: Locally Coherent, Globally Incoherent: Bounding Compositional Incoherence in Multi-Component LLM Agents
comment: 25 pages, 7 figures, 24 tables. Preliminary versions to appear at the ICML 2026 Workshops on Combining Theory and Benchmarks (CTB), Statistical Frameworks for Uncertainty in Agentic Systems (AgenticUQ), and Failure Modes of Agentic AI (FAGEN)
summary: Multi-component LLM agents assemble probabilistic claims from components that each see only part of a joint problem; the composition can violate basic probability axioms even when every component is locally coherent. We formalise this locally coherent, globally incoherent failure via the compositional residual eps*, the L2 distance from the composed quote to the joint coherent polytope, computable at runtime from system output and the declared cross-component coupling constraints. A product-structure dichotomy characterises when local coherence suffices, and a Rayleigh-quotient prediction matches the observed residual within 7% on three of four relation classes. A hierarchical Boyle-Dykstra projection repairs the composition deterministically; an anytime-valid e-process gives sequential coherence monitoring. Across 1,876 ensemble cliques on a four-LLM mid-tier panel (frontier-panel rerun in Section 5.5), eps* > 0 on 33-94% of cliques, translating to +0.115 nats per bet of regret on 1,770 resolved bets under the proportional allocation rule (the gain collapses to +0.006 under bettors that themselves coherentise). Three intuitive LLM-side mitigations(retrieval, partition-aware prompting, aggregator-LLM) each fail or regress.

AI.tldr: Defines eps* as the L2 distance to the joint coherent polytope for composed LLM outputs; analyzes when local coherence suffices, provides a hierarchical repair via Boyle–Dyktstra projection, and introduces sequential calibration with an e-process; empirical results show substantial global incoherence across ensembles and limited effectiveness of simple mitigations.
AI.motivation: To diagnose why assembling locally coherent components can yield globally incoherent probabilistic claims in multi-LLM ensembles, and to provide a formal measure, repair mechanism, and monitoring approach for coherence.
AI.method: Introduce compositional residual eps* (L2 distance to the joint coherent polytope) computable from system outputs and cross-component coupling constraints; establish a product-structure dichotomy for when local coherence suffices; relate residual to a Rayleigh quotient; apply hierarchical Boyle–Dyktstra projection to deterministically repair compositions; deploy an anytime-valid e-process for sequential coherence monitoring; evaluate on 1,876 ensemble cliques from a four-LLM mid-tier panel with a frontier-panel rerun; quantify residuals and regrets and test three mitigation strategies.
AI.result: Observed eps* > 0 on 33–94% of cliques; the residual translates to +0.115 nats per bet of regret over 1,770 resolved bets under proportional allocation; for bettors who themselves coherentise the gain drops to +0.006 nats; a Rayleigh-quotient prediction matches the residual within 7% on three of four relation classes; the three intuitive LLM-side mitigations (retrieval, partition-aware prompting, aggregator-LLM) each fail or regress.
AI.conclusion: Global incoherence is prevalent in multi-LLM ensembles even when components are locally coherent; local coherence is not universally sufficient; deterministic repair and sequential monitoring are feasible, but simple mitigations commonly fail to mitigate the issue.

title: Q-ANCHOR: Federated Quantum Learning with ZNE-guided Correction
summary: Quantum Federated Learning (QFL) offers a promising framework to train quantum models across distributed clients while keeping data strictly local. Due to its simplicity and low communication overhead, Federated Averaging (FedAvg) is the standard aggregation choice in QFL literature. However, deploying QFL on practical hardware exposes a severe double-drift phenomenon: the global model is simultaneously derailed by client drift from non-IID data and hardware bias from noisy quantum gradient estimates. In this work, we first analyze the convergence of FedAvg under these realistic conditions, mathematically demonstrating that quantum hardware bias creates a persistent error floor that standard averaging cannot correct. To overcome this limitation, we propose Q-ANCHOR, a quantum-aware federated aggregation architecture that anchors server updates with zero-noise extrapolation while applying stateful client correction to suppress both client drift and hardware-induced bias. Our convergence theory proves that Q-ANCHOR successfully mitigates classical client drift while actively reducing the hardware-bias floor. Experimental results demonstrate that Q-ANCHOR achieves significantly more stable training than conventional FL baselines.

AI.tldr: FedAvg in Quantum Federated Learning under non-IID data and quantum hardware noise creates a persistent error floor; Q-ANCHOR uses zero-noise extrapolation and stateful client correction to stabilize training and reduce bias.
AI.motivation: Address double-drift: classical client drift due to data heterogeneity and hardware bias due to noisy quantum gradient estimates in QFL.
AI.method: Convergence analysis of FedAvg under non-IID and hardware bias; propose Q-ANCHOR architecture that anchors server updates with zero-noise extrapolation and applies stateful client correction; provide convergence theory showing drift mitigation and bias floor reduction.
AI.result: Theoretical convergence: Q-ANCHOR mitigates classical drift and reduces hardware-induced bias floor; experiments show substantially more stable training compared to conventional FL baselines.
AI.conclusion: Q-ANCHOR offers a robust aggregation strategy for QFL, effectively addressing both data-induced drift and hardware noise, yielding improved stability and convergence.

title: Tiny but Trusted: Efficient Vision-Language Reasoning for Time-Series Anomaly Detection
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved impressive performance across many tasks, yet prior studies report unsatisfactory performance when applying large language or multimodal models to finding abnormal patterns in sequential data. Public anomaly detection benchmarks typically provide interval annotations but not natural-language rationales, making it difficult to fine-tune VLMs to produce grounded, interpretable decisions. To address this gap, we construct VisAnomBench, a curated benchmark built from public time-series datasets and augmented with high-quality anomaly explanations selected from multiple large VLMs using fine-grained, task-specific rewards. Through fine-tuning on this benchmark, we develop VisAnomReasoner, a parameter-efficient VLM for time-series anomaly detection. Experimental results on VisAnomBench show that VisAnomReasoner achieves more accurate anomaly localization and consistently outperforms all baselines, with improvements of at least 21.23 and 23.87 percentage points in precision and F1, respectively. Additional experiments on the TSB-AD-U benchmark demonstrate strong cross-benchmark generalization, with VisAnomReasoner improving precision and F1 by 9.57 and 13.39 percentage points, respectively.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark VisAnomBench with time-series anomaly explanations is created and used to fine-tune VisAnomReasoner, a parameter-efficient VLM, achieving state-of-the-art anomaly localization and strong cross-benchmark generalization.
AI.motivation: Address lack of natural-language rationales in anomaly detection benchmarks and the need for grounded, interpretable decisions when using vision-language models on sequential data.
AI.method: Curate VisAnomBench from public time-series datasets; augment with high-quality anomaly explanations from multiple large VLMs using task-specific rewards; fine-tune a parameter-efficient VLM (VisAnomReasoner) on the benchmark.
AI.result: Significant performance gains on VisAnomBench: ≥21.23pp precision and ≥23.87pp F1; cross-benchmark gains on TSB-AD-U: ≥9.57pp precision and ≥13.39pp F1; superior anomaly localization and baseline improvements.
AI.conclusion: Shows that grounding anomaly decisions with NLP explanations via fine-tuned VLMs is effective for time-series anomaly detection and generalizes across benchmarks.

title: Distributionally Robust Set Representation Learning Under Inference-Time Element Corruption
summary: Standard Set Representation Learning methods typically excel on curated data but often overlook the challenge of inference-time element corruption. This refers to scenarios where deployed models encounter element-level degradations, such as outliers or missing components, that may distort set representation and degrade performance. We propose SW-DRSO, a distributionally robust optimization framework tailored for sets. Rather than minimizing loss solely on observed training data, SW-DRSO optimizes a tractable surrogate of the worst-case expected loss over a family of plausible inference-time variations. We introduce a barycentric adversary that approximates the intractable search over corrupted sets by a differentiable training-time optimization over simplex weights. Extensive experiments across four tasks demonstrate that SW-DRSO effectively enhances robustness against corruption while maintaining high overall performance.

AI.tldr: SW-DRSO is a distributionally robust framework for set representations that uses a barycentric adversary to protect against inference-time element corruption, yielding improved robustness across tasks with little loss in accuracy.
AI.motivation: Inference-time element corruption (outliers/missing components) can severely degrade set-based representations; there is a need for tractable, robust optimization tailored to sets.
AI.method: Introduce SW-DRSO, a distributionally robust optimization approach for sets. It approximates the worst-case loss over corrupted sets via a differentiable training-time optimization over simplex weights (barycentric adversary). The method yields a tractable surrogate for the intractable worst-case objective.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four tasks shows improved robustness to corruption while maintaining high overall performance.
AI.conclusion: SW-DRSO provides a practical, robust framework for set representations under inference-time corruptions, with generalization across tasks and a tractable training regime.

title: SchGen: PCB Schematic Generation with Semantic-Grounded Code Representations
summary: Printed circuit board (PCB) schematic design defines nearly all electronic hardware, but it remains manual and expertise-intensive. While generative AI has advanced digital and analog IC design, PCB schematic generation from natural-language intent is largely unexplored. This paper presents SchGen, the first large language model that generates editable PCB schematics from natural-language requests. The key challenge lies in the lack of an LLM-suited representation and a large-scale dataset. Current schematic formats are dominated by verbose, tool-specific syntax and geometry-heavy descriptions, making them difficult to generate reliably. We introduce a semantically grounded code representation that encodes schematic editing primitives with relative placement and pin-name-based wiring, transforming a geometry-driven generation problem into a semantics-driven matching task amenable to LLMs. We further construct a large-scale dataset of PCB schematics paired with user prompts via a human-agent collaborative pipeline that converts open-source hardware designs into our representation. Experiments show that SchGen significantly outperforms alternative representations and even larger general-purpose LLMs on wire connectivity accuracy and functional correctness. Our results highlight the critical role of representation design in enabling generative models for complex hardware design tasks.

AI.tldr: SchGen is a language-model-based system that generates editable PCB schematics from natural-language prompts using a semantically grounded representation and a large paired dataset.
AI.motivation: To enable natural-language-driven PCB schematic design by addressing representations and data scarcity that hinder reliable LLM-based generation.
AI.method: Introduce a semantically grounded code representation encoding schematic editing primitives with relative placement and pin-name-based wiring; frame PCB schematic generation as semantic matching rather than geometry-driven generation; build a large open-source-to-representation dataset via a human-agent collaborative pipeline; evaluate LLMs on wire connectivity and functional correctness.
AI.result: SchGen significantly outperforms alternative representations and larger general-purpose LLMs in wire connectivity accuracy and functional correctness.
AI.conclusion: Proper representation design is crucial for applying generative models to complex hardware design tasks, enabling reliable, editable PCB schematic generation from natural-language intents.

title: Chess-World-Model: A 10M-Game Benchmark for Exact State Tracking from Chess Move Sequences
summary: World models require state tracking, which is the ability to maintain a correct latent state across action sequences. Existing benchmarks are often synthetic or language-based, limiting their value as tests of structured state updates in realistic domains. We introduce Chess-World-Model, a large-scale state-tracking benchmark built from 10 million real chess games, where models predict the exact board state reached after a sequence of legal moves. Alongside a held-out real-game split, we include an out-of-distribution split from uniformly random legal play, which tests whether models learn the transition rules rather than shortcuts from common human positions. Prior theoretical and empirical work has shown that Transformers struggle to state-track, while input-dependent linear RNNs require expressive state-transition matrices to do so. We therefore benchmark a causal Transformer, block-diagonal SLiCE, Mamba-3, and Gated DeltaNet with negative eigenvalues under a matched interface and training protocol. The recurrent models strongly outperform the Transformer at 3 and 8 million parameters. Real-game performance saturates above 18 million parameters, but the random-uniform split remains discriminative up to 40 million, exposing failures otherwise hidden by scale. Additionally, ablations show that less expressive state-transition mechanisms reduce performance on the out-of-distribution split for all three recurrent models. Together, these results establish Chess-World-Model as a practical large-scale benchmark for state tracking that exposes failures model scale would otherwise conceal.

AI.tldr: Chess-World-Model is a large-scale state-tracking benchmark built from 10M real chess games, requiring models to predict the exact board state after move sequences. It reveals recurrent architectures outperform Transformers at moderate sizes, with random-play splits remaining discriminative at large scale, exposing limits of scale alone.
AI.motivation: Existing state-tracking benchmarks are often synthetic or language-based, limiting assessment of structured state updates in realistic domains. Chess provides a rich, rule-governed domain with explicit latent state (board) updates. The random-play out-of-distribution split tests whether models learn transition rules or rely on common human positions.
AI.method: Construct a Chess-World-Model benchmark from 10M real games. Task: predict the board state after a sequence of legal moves. Include a held-out real-game split and an out-of-distribution split formed by uniformly random legal play. Compare a causal Transformer, block-diagonal SLiCE, Mamba-3, and Gated DeltaNet with negative eigenvalues under matched interface/training.
AI.result: Recurrent models outperform Transformer at 3M and 8M parameters. Real-game performance saturates above 18M parameters; the random-uniform split remains discriminative up to 40M parameters, revealing failures hidden by scale in Transformers. Ablations show that reducing the expressivity of state-transition mechanisms degrades OOD performance for all recurrent models.
AI.conclusion: Chess-World-Model serves as a practical, large-scale benchmark for state tracking that exposes limitations of scale-driven improvements and underscores the need for expressive state-transition dynamics in models tasked with maintaining accurate latent states across sequences.

title: Physics Is All You Need? A Case Study in Physicist-Supervised AI Development of Scientific Software
comment: 10 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables, 1 physicist and a few AI agents. Accepted by ICML 2026 AI for Science Workshop. Code and development log are available at this repo: https://github.com/MinhMPA/clax-pt
summary:
Are AI agents tools, co-authors, or researchers? We present a quantified case study ($N=1$): a physicist supervising an AI coding agent (Claude Code, Sonnet and Opus models) over 12 work days and 57 sessions to build CLAX-PT, a differentiable one-loop perturbation theory module in JAX. We documented and classified 15 supervision events by intervention level.
  The agent resolved ten autonomously by iterating against oracle tests. Two more by the physicist's domain knowledge. The three it could not -- all evaded oracle detection -- share a common property: the agent treated symptom reduction as root-cause resolution. It spent 33 of the 57 sessions adjusting coefficients within a code architecture that could not represent the target physics, and could not re-evaluate its CLASS-PT branch choice even when prompted to reconsider; only an injected physics concept (anisotropic BAO damping) triggered the redesign. Separately, the agent committed a calibrated correction that passed all oracle tests but corresponded to no quantity in the theory, predicting wrong values at any other cosmology.
  The fudge factor was caught and replaced within the same session. Three supervision practices proved critical for catching what oracle tests missed: testing at diverse parameter points beyond the fiducial calibration; shared changelogs that surfaced stalled exploration across sessions; and an explicit rule against unphysical numerical patches. In this case, supervision design, not model capability, determined whether the agent's output was trustworthy. Closing the gap would require agents that propose architectural alternatives rather than optimize within a given structure, and distinguish predictive adequacy from explanatory correctness -- capabilities not exhibited here, not obviously addressed by scaling alone. [Abridged.]

AI.tldr: A single-case study evaluating whether AI agents act as tools, co-authors, or researchers in building a differentiable one-loop perturbation theory module (CLAX-PT) in JAX, supervised by a physicist over 12 days. The study documents 15 intervention events, with 10 autonomous resolutions, 2 aided by domain knowledge, and 3 failures due to treating symptom reduction as root-cause. It finds major inefficiencies when the agent tuned coefficients within an unsuitable architecture and only redesigns after introducing a new physics concept. A calibrated yet incorrect patch passes oracle tests. Three supervision practices (diverse parameter testing, changelogs, and rules against unphysical patches) helped catch misses. Concludes supervision design, not model capability, governs trust; calls for architectural-innovation and a separation between predictive adequacy and explanatory correctness.
AI.motivation: To understand the role of AI agents in scientific work and how supervision shapes trust and output quality, by empirically examining a real-world research task with a single agent–scientist collaboration.
AI.method: N=1 case study: physicist supervising AI coding agents (Claude Code, Sonnet, Opus) over 12 work days and 57 sessions to build a differentiable one-loop perturbation theory module (CLAX-PT) in JAX. Documented and classified 15 supervision events by intervention level. Evaluated agent outputs against oracle tests; identified cases of autonomous resolution, domain-knowledge-assisted resolution, and failures. Extracted factors influencing success/failure (architecture suitability, revision triggers). Assessed impact of testing strategies and governance rules.
AI.result: Agents autonomously resolved 10 tasks; 2 more with domain knowledge; 3 failures due to symptom-focused root-cause reasoning, spending 33 sessions tuning within an ill-suited architecture and failing to reconsider architecture. A calibrated patch passed oracle tests but was wrong in other cosmologies. A physics concept injection triggered a redesign. A fudge factor was detected and replaced in-session. Three supervision practices were critical to catching misses: diverse parameter testing, shared changelogs, and explicit prohibitions on unphysical patches.
AI.conclusion: Supervision design determines trustworthiness of AI-generated scientific outputs; scaling alone is insufficient. Future work should enable agents to propose architectural alternatives and distinguish predictive adequacy from explanatory correctness.

title: Convergence Theory for Iterative LLM-Based Neural Architecture Search: A Parametric Cross-Entropy Framework with Closed-Form Proxy Reliability
comment: 14 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Submitted to NeurIPS 2026
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as generators in iterative neural architecture search (NAS), yet no formal convergence theory exists for this class of algorithms. We model iterative LLM-NAS as a parametric Cross-Entropy (CE) method over executable programs and prove six results: (1) iterative LLM fine-tuning on elite architectures is equivalent to the CE update restricted to the LLM parametric family; (2) expected architecture quality is monotonically non-decreasing across cycles; (3) elite-set probability converges to a fixed point at a geometric rate C_t >= 1-(1-rho_0)^t; (4) delta-based generation achieves a strictly higher valid-generation rate than full-code generation under a first-order Markov token-error model; (5) the MinHash-Jaccard novelty filter prevents mode collapse; (6) proxy reliability admits the closed-form rho_S = (6/pi) arcsin(rho_P(SNR)/2), yielding the practical diagnostic sigma^2_arch >> sigma^2_noise as a necessary condition for trustworthy proxy-based rankings. Testing against a 22-cycle, three-LLM, six-dataset experiment with 3,300 generated architectures confirms two predictions quantitatively, two at direction-of-effect level, and explains the proxy-reliability ceiling effect previously reported empirically but left unexplained.

AI.tldr: Formalizes iterative LLM-NAS as a parametric CE process, proving six convergence/design results and validating them in a 22-cycle, multi-LLM setup.
AI.motivation: Provide a rigorous theoretical foundation for LLM-guided iterative neural architecture search, clarifying convergence, effectiveness of delta-generation, novelty filtering, and proxy-based rankings.
