AI.method: Model iterative LLM-NAS as a parametric Cross-Entropy method over executable programs; prove equivalences between fine-tuning and CE updates, monotone improvement, fixed-point convergence of elite-set probability, and derive benefits of delta-generation, novelty filters, and proxy reliability; derive a closed-form proxy diagnostic; empirical validation across cycles, LLMs, and datasets.
AI.result: Six results established: (1) fine-tuning on elite architectures equals CE update within LLM family; (2) expected architecture quality is non-decreasing; (3) elite-set probability converges geometrically to a fixed point; (4) delta-generation yields higher valid-generation rates under a token-error model; (5) MinHash-Jaccard filter prevents mode collapse; (6) closed-form proxy reliability rho_S related to rho_P, with practical condition sigma^2_arch >> sigma^2_noise for trustworthy rankings. Empirical tests support several predictions and explain proxy-reliability ceiling.
AI.conclusion: The work provides formal guarantees and practical diagnostics for LLM-guided NAS, clarifying when proxies and novelty filters improve search and identifying conditions for trustworthy rankings, while explaining observed proxy ceilings.

title: Striding Across Reynolds Numbers: Representation Geometry in Neural PDE Generalisation
summary: Cross-Reynolds generalisation in neural PDE solvers remains poorly characterised. On the canonical forced 2D Navier-Stokes benchmark, a trained Fourier Neural Operator reaches 46.68% relative L2 error under a 10x Reynolds-number shift, yet zero-forward-model retrieval baselines already improve to 41-42%. This suggests representation geometry as a major organising variable among the tested methods. We test this hypothesis through ConvAE-Relay, which matches states in a source-trained convolutional autoencoder latent space and borrows dynamics from a source-regime database, achieving 38.34+/-0.07% using only a source-regime database and no target-regime fitting, labels, or database entries. A 2x2 ablation isolates matching quality as dominant over the update rule. Oracle experiments confirm that source-regime dynamics directions remain transferable (cosine similarity ~0.84) when matching stays on-manifold; autoregressive drift is the primary bottleneck (~12 percentage points). From the learned-prediction side, a U-Net with multi-scale skip connections achieves 34.72+/-0.60%, consistent with the retrieval-side finding that local, multi-scale representations organise cross-Reynolds transfer among tested methods. All claims are scoped to this benchmark.

AI.tldr: Cross-Reynolds generalization in neural PDE solvers is largely governed by representation geometry; transfer benefits arise from local, multi-scale representations and preserved source-regime dynamics.
AI.motivation: To characterize how neural PDE solvers generalize across Reynolds-number shifts and to identify organizing factors beyond model class, with a focus on representation geometry and cross-domain dynamics.
AI.method: Compare baseline models (Fourier Neural Operator) under Reynolds shifts with retrieval baselines; develop ConvAE-Relay that matches latent states from a source-regime autoencoder and borrows source-regime dynamics; perform a 2x2 ablation to separate matching quality vs update rule; conduct oracle experiments to test transferability of source-regime dynamics; evaluate a U-Net with multi-scale skip connections for learned-prediction; constrain claims to the canonical 2D Navier-Stokes forced benchmark.
AI.result: FNO attains 46.68% rel L2 error under 10x Reynolds shift; retrieval baselines improve to 41–42%; ConvAE-Relay achieves 38.34% ±0.07% using only source-regime data; 2x2 ablation shows matching quality dominates; source-regime dynamics directions transfer well (cosine similarity ~0.84) with on-manifold matching, autoregressive drift contributes ~12 percentage points; U-Net with multi-scale skips achieves 34.72% ±0.60%; results support the primacy of local, multi-scale representations in cross-Reynolds transfer.
AI.conclusion: Representation geometry—especially local, multi-scale latent structures—and transferability of source-regime dynamics underpin cross-Reynolds transfer in neural PDE solvers; improvements are benchmark-scoped and hinge on effective matching on latent manifolds and controlling autoregressive drift.

title: Beyond MSE: Improving Precipitation Nowcasting with Multi-Quantile Regression
summary: Deep-learning precipitation nowcasting models are often optimized using pointwise losses such as mean squared error or mean absolute error, which can lead to overly smooth forecasts and poor representation of heavy rainfall. This study investigates whether the predictive performance of an established deterministic nowcasting architecture can be improved by reformulating training as a multi-quantile regression problem. Using SmaAt-UNet as a core model, we compare MSE, MAE, and multi-quantile pinball-loss training on radar precipitation nowcasting over the Netherlands. The results show that multi-quantile training improves the central deterministic forecast, decreasing test-set MSE by 8.6\% compared to a model trained using MSE, while also producing upper-quantile outputs that are useful for risk-sensitive prediction of heavy precipitation. These findings suggest that quantile regression provides a simple alternative to standard pointwise losses without requiring a new architecture or generative sampling procedure. The implementation of our models and training setup is available on \href{https://github.com/gijsvn/Multi-Quantile-Precipitation-Nowcasting}{GitHub}.

AI.tldr: Multi-quantile training with SmaAt-UNet improves central precipitation nowcasts and provides useful upper quantiles for heavy rainfall; achieves an 8.6% reduction in test MSE versus MSE training.
AI.motivation: Pointwise losses (MSE/MAE) tend to produce overly smooth forecasts and poorly capture heavy rainfall; quantile regression offers probabilistic information without changing architecture.
AI.method: Use SmaAt-UNet for radar precipitation nowcasting over the Netherlands; train with MSE, MAE, and multi-quantile pinball loss; compare performance and extract upper-quantile forecasts; code available on GitHub.
AI.result: Central deterministic forecast performance improves; test MSE reduced by 8.6% relative to MSE-trained baseline; multi-quantile outputs provide useful upper-quantile forecasts for heavy rainfall; quantile regression serves as a simple alternative to standard pointwise losses without new architecture or sampling methods.
AI.conclusion: Quantile regression is a simple and effective alternative to standard pointwise losses for nowcasting, enabling risk-sensitive outputs without architectural changes.

title: Learning to Extrapolate to New Tasks: A Relational Approach to Task Extrapolation
summary: Modern learning systems excel at interpolation but struggle to generalize to unseen tasks outside the training distribution's support. This failure occurs even in simple settings, such as handling task parameters beyond the training range, and persists despite advances in foundation models. To this end, we develop the Relational Task Extrapolator (RTE), an algorithm designed to enable systematic extrapolation to novel tasks. The key observation is that extrapolation is inherently relational: extrapolating to unseen tasks requires learning how tasks transform into one another. If a model learns the transformation between tasks A and B during training, it can apply that same transformation to relate known tasks to unseen ones at test time. RTE operationalizes this idea by decomposing each target task into a known anchor task and a transformation linking the anchor and target. It then learns a relational operator, mapping an anchor-transformation pair to predictions for the target task. We instantiate RTE across multiple task extrapolation regimes in function prediction, e.g. where target tasks use out-of-range parameters (parameter extrapolation), have greater compositional depth (length extrapolation), and/or recombine function primitives in unseen ways (compositional extrapolation). We further extend RTE to sequence prediction, integrating it into fine-tuning algorithms for foundation models. Across empirical studies, we find that RTE substantially outperforms existing approaches on extrapolation to novel, unseen tasks.

AI.tldr: RTE learns how tasks transform into one another to extrapolate to unseen tasks. It decomposes a target task into a known anchor task plus a transformation, then learns a relational operator that predicts the target from anchor+transformation, across various extrapolation regimes.
AI.motivation: Generalization beyond the training distribution is limited; extrapolating to tasks with out-of-range parameters, greater depth, or novel compositions remains difficult even for strong models. A relational perspective aims to reuse learned task transformations to enable systematic extrapolation.
AI.method: Decompose each target task into an anchor task and a transformation linking anchor to target; learn a relational operator that maps an anchor-transformation pair to predictions for the target; instantiate across parameter, length, and compositional extrapolation regimes in function prediction, and extend to sequence prediction with fine-tuning of foundation models.
AI.result: Empirical studies show RTE substantially outperforms existing approaches on extrapolating to novel, unseen tasks across multiple extrapolation regimes.
AI.conclusion: Learning task-transformations and a relational extrapolator enables systematic extrapolation to unseen tasks and is compatible with function- and sequence-prediction settings, including integration with foundation-model fine-tuning.

title: DAMEL: Dual-Axis Multi-Expert Learning for Class-Imbalanced Learning
summary: Various algorithms have been proposed to address the challenges posed by class-imbalanced learning from real-world data with long-tailed distributions. While these algorithms reduce prediction bias through rebalancing techniques, they often introduce increased prediction variance as a trade-off. Several multi-expert learning algorithms aim to address this variance but involve complex procedures. We propose a new multi-expert learning algorithm, called the dual-axis multi-expert learning (DAMEL), which reduces both bias and variance of predictions by using multiple experts along both representation and time axes. Along the representation axis, DAMEL concatenates the representations of multiple experts and trains an auxiliary balanced classifier simultaneously with the concatenated representations. Along the time axis, DAMEL aggregates network weights across training epochs, employing these aggregated weights during testing. Experimental results demonstrate that DAMEL reduces both bias and variance of predictions, highlighting its effectiveness in class-imbalanced learning.

AI.tldr: DAMEL is a dual-axis multi-expert learning method for long-tailed data that reduces bias and variance by combining multiple experts along representation and time axes.
AI.motivation: To mitigate the bias-variance trade-off in long-tailed learning: rebalancing reduces bias but increases variance, and existing multi-expert methods can be complex. DAMEL seeks a simpler, dual-axis ensemble to improve predictive quality.
AI.method: Two-axis design: (1) Representation axis: concatenate representations from multiple experts and train an auxiliary balanced classifier on the concatenated features; (2) Time axis: aggregate network weights across training epochs and use the aggregated weights during testing.
AI.result: Empirical results show that DAMEL reduces both bias and variance of predictions, improving performance on imbalanced datasets.
AI.conclusion: DAMEL effectively balances bias and variance in imbalanced learning by leveraging dual-axis multi-expert ensembling, with favorable empirical performance and potential for broader applicability.

title: Overcoming Forgetting in LLM Fine-Tuning with Evolution Strategies
summary: Evolution Strategies (ES) has recently emerged as a competitive alternative to reinforcement learning (RL) for large language model (LLM) fine-tuning, offering advantages through simplicity, scalability, and inference-only training. However, recent work suggests that ES fine-tuning on new tasks may induce forgetting of prior tasks. First, this paper shows that prior task forgetting (1) is better characterized as performance drift rather than irreversible forgetting, with prior-task performance often recovering during ES training; and (2) is not a specific failure mode of ES, but can also arise for fine-tuning with RL methods. Second, it analyzes when and why such drift arises, highlighting its dependence on ES training dynamics, particularly random walk behavior in weakly constrained directions of the weight space. Third, based on these insights, it introduces Anchored Weight Decay (AWD) as a parameter-space regularization technique that constrains optimization toward the initial model parameters. AWD effectively stabilizes prior-task performance while preserving target-task performance, achieving benefits comparable to large ES population sizes at much lower computational cost. Thus, contrary to previous beliefs, the paper shows that prior-task forgetting under ES is largely avoidable, positioning ES as a promising approach for continual learning in LLMs.

AI.tldr: Anchored Weight Decay (AWD) stabilizes prior-task performance during Evolution Strategies (ES) fine-tuning of LLMs by penalizing divergence from initial parameters, converting observed drift into recoverable dynamics and enabling continual learning with lower compute than large ES populations.
AI.motivation: Understand whether prior-task forgetting during ES fine-tuning is a fundamental limitation, whether it differs from RL, and how to mitigate it to enable continual learning in LLMs.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of ES training dynamics for LLM fine-tuning; comparison with RL; characterize prior-task forgetting as performance drift; identify weakly constrained weight-space directions causing drift; introduce Anchored Weight Decay as a parameter-space regularizer; evaluate retention of prior-task performance and target-task gains; compare compute with different ES population sizes.
AI.result: Prior-task forgetting manifests as performance drift that can recover during ES training; drift is not unique to ES and can occur with RL; drift arises from random-walk-like exploration in weakly constrained weight directions; AWD constrains optimization toward initial parameters, stabilizing prior-task performance while preserving target-task performance, achieving comparable benefits to large ES populations at substantially lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Prior-task forgetting under ES is largely avoidable with AWD; ES remains a promising, scalable approach for continual learning in LLMs.

title: RL2ML: Finite-Rollout Surrogate Objectives from Reinforcement Learning to Maximum Likelihood
summary: Correctness-based Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) trains language models from binary feedback on sampled outputs, but the objective optimized in expectation and the stochastic update geometry induced by finite rollout groups are often conflated. This paper develops RL2ML, a family of finite-rollout surrogate objectives with a closed-form, exactly unbiased gradient estimator. The family continuously connects standard reinforcement learning, maximum-likelihood-like training, and beyond-maximum-likelihood objectives while preserving estimator-objective alignment under a fixed rollout budget. We introduce the group-level update scale to characterize how a rollout group is reweighted after its empirical success count is observed, revealing a subcritical-supercritical update-scale transition that is hidden by population-level objective notation alone. Building on this distinction, calibrated metric-gain analysis and exact variance decomposition show that the best choice of surrogate objective is determined neither by proximity to maximum likelihood nor by the population-level weight alone. Instead, it depends jointly on the evaluation metric, local sensitivity, and estimator variance. The remaining degree of freedom in the surrogate objective family can therefore be formulated as a one-dimensional optimization problem rather than treated as an unconstrained hyperparameter.

AI.tldr: RL2ML provides finite-rollout surrogate objectives for correctness-based RL with binary feedback, offering an exactly unbiased gradient estimator that smoothly interpolates among RL, ML-like training, and beyond-ML objectives under a fixed rollout budget. It introduces group-level update scale and shows the best surrogate objective depends on metric, sensitivity, and variance, reducing the design freedom to a 1D optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing correctness-based RL with verifiable rewards trains from binary feedback but suffers conflation between the objective in expectation and the stochastic update geometry from finite rollout groups. A closed-form unbiased gradient estimator with tuned surrogates is needed to align objective and updates and to understand how rollout structure affects learning.
AI.method: Introduce RL2ML, a family of finite-rollout surrogate objectives with a closed-form, exactly unbiased gradient estimator. Define group-level update scale to reweight rollouts after observing empirical success counts. Perform calibrated metric-gain analysis and exact variance decomposition to determine the surrogate objective that jointly optimizes evaluation metric, local sensitivity, and estimator variance. Propose reducing surrogate design to a 1D optimization problem.
AI.result: The surrogate objective choice is shown to depend on evaluation metric, local sensitivity, and estimator variance rather than proximity to ML or population weights alone. The framework and variance analyses enable a controlled, budget-aware selection of the surrogate, within a single-parameter optimization.
AI.conclusion: The remaining degree of freedom in RL2ML is a one-dimensional optimization problem that can better tailor training to the evaluation metric and estimator properties, bridging standard RL, ML-like training, and beyond-ML objectives under finite rollout budgets.

title: On Distributional Reinforcement Learning in Chaotic Dynamical Systems
summary: Chaotic dynamical systems pose a fundamental challenge for Reinforcement Learning (RL): exponential sensitivity to initial conditions induces high-variance bootstrap targets and poorly conditioned gradient updates. Chaotic dynamics arise across scientific and engineering domains, from fluid flows and climate systems to multi-agent systems, where reliable learning is highly desirable. Standard RL methods optimise expected returns through scalar value functions, implicitly averaging over diverging trajectories and entangling trajectory level instability with the learning objective. We show that under mild statistical stability assumptions, the return distribution evolves more regularly than individual trajectories when measured under the $1$-Wasserstein metric, yielding a smoother distributional Bellman objective. By aligning optimisation with this measure level structure, distributional RL provides better conditioned learning. We offer a principled explanation for the advantages of distributional methods in chaotic systems and the geometries of RL objectives under chaos.

AI.tldr: Distributional RL with a Wasserstein-distance objective yields a smoother, more stable learning signal in chaotic RL by focusing on return distributions rather than trajectories.
AI.motivation: Chaotic dynamics cause exponential sensitivity to initial conditions, leading to high-variance targets and ill-conditioned updates when using scalar value functions; a measure-level objective may stabilize learning in these regimes.
AI.method: The authors analyze the return distribution under the 1-Wasserstein metric, showing it evolves more regularly than individual trajectories and leads to a smoother distributional Bellman objective; they argue that optimizing this measure-level objective yields better-conditioned learning and provide a principled explanation for the advantages of distributional methods in chaotic systems.
AI.result: Under chaos, distributional RL with Wasserstein-based objectives offers better conditioning and smoother optimization compared to scalar value-based methods; the paper provides a geometric/measure-theoretic explanation for these benefits.
AI.conclusion: A Wasserstein-based distributional perspective clarifies why distributional RL outperforms traditional approaches in chaotic environments and outlines the objective geometries that promote stable learning in such systems.

title: iLoRA: Bayesian Low-Rank Adaptation with Latent Interaction Graphs for Microbiome Diagnosis
summary: Parameter-efficient adaptation has made LLMs practical for domain prediction, but standard LoRA still relies on a static low-rank update and does not expose the latent interactions that often drive scientific labels. We introduce iLoRA. To our knowledge, it is the first Bayesian graph-conditioned LoRA framework. It infers a latent interaction graph from the input and uses it to generate input-conditioned LoRA updates. As a result, iLoRA learns prediction and latent interaction structure jointly, rather than training a predictor and applying interaction analysis only post hoc. We instantiate this idea for microbiome diagnosis, where disease state can depend on both species-level abundance and microbe-microbe cross-talk, and evaluate it in two complementary settings: interactive QA with human-annotated graphs, which tests latent structure recovery, and multi-cohort IBD diagnosis, which tests biomedical utility. Across both settings, iLoRA improves over strong LoRA and Bayesian adaptation baselines, recovers graphs aligned with human annotations and cohort-level microbiome associations, and provides calibrated uncertainty with moderate graph-branch overhead.

AI.tldr: iLoRA is a Bayesian, graph-conditioned LoRA that learns a latent interaction graph to generate input-conditioned low-rank updates, jointly training prediction and interaction structure. It is demonstrated on microbiome diagnosis tasks (interactive QA with annotated graphs and multi-cohort IBD) and outperforms strong LoRA and Bayesian baselines with aligned graphs and calibrated uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Standard LoRA uses a static, low-rank update and does not reveal latent feature interactions that can drive domain labels. There is a need to jointly learn predictive signals and their interaction structure in a parameter-efficient manner, especially for biology-inspired domains where interactions (e.g., microbe–microbe cross-talk) influence outcomes.
AI.method: Introduce iLoRA, a Bayesian graph-conditioned LoRA framework that infers a latent interaction graph from the input and uses it to generate input-conditioned LoRA updates. The model jointly learns prediction and latent interaction structure, and is instantiated for microbiome diagnosis with two evaluation settings: interactive QA with human-annotated graphs (tests structure recovery) and multi-cohort IBD diagnosis (tests biomedical utility).
AI.result: iLoRA improves over strong LoRA and Bayesian adaptation baselines, recovers graphs aligned with human annotations and cohort-level microbiome associations, and provides calibrated uncertainty with moderate graph-branch overhead.
AI.conclusion: iLoRA enables joint optimization of predictive performance and latent interaction structure through a Bayesian graph-conditioned LoRA, offering interpretable, uncertainty-calibrated predictions and demonstrated utility in microbiome-based disease diagnosis.

title: Can AI Weather Models Predict Beyond Two Weeks? A Quantitative Benchmark and Analysis of Long Rollouts
summary: While AI weather models excel at short-to-medium range forecasts (up to 15 days), they frequently suffer from ill-defined "instabilities" when rolled out over longer horizons. This work addresses the lack of a formal taxonomy by categorizing these failures into three distinct regimes: blow-up, drift, and loss of seasonality, through year-long rollouts of nine state-of-the-art AI weather models. Our analysis reveals that stability hinges on the treatment of small spatio-temporal scales: unstable models amplify high-frequency energy, while stable models act as denoisers when noise is added to their inputs. Far from reducing these models to mere stochastic parrots, our findings highlight that stable models generate unique weather trajectories, conditioned on the initial state. We verify our findings through ablation studies on architectural design choices, conducted using state-of-the-art Vision Transformer (ViT) AI weather model architectures.

AI.tldr: Formal taxonomy of long-horizon instabilities in AI weather models, identifying three regimes (blow-up, drift, loss of seasonality) and revealing that stability hinges on small-scale handling; stable models denoise noise and produce unique trajectories; ablation on ViT architectures.
AI.motivation: Address undefined long-horizon instabilities and lack of formal taxonomy in AI weather models; understand stability mechanisms and construct reliable long-range forecasts.
AI.method: Year-long rollouts of nine state-of-the-art AI weather models; categorize failures into three regimes; analyze energy at high frequencies; test robustness by adding noise to inputs; ablation studies on architectural choices using Vision Transformer models.
AI.result: Found that stability is determined by treatment of small spatio-temporal scales; unstable models amplify high-frequency energy; stable models act as denoisers when input noise is added; stable models generate unique weather trajectories conditioned on the initial state; ablation studies on ViT architectures support these claims.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that AI weather models can achieve stable long-range forecasts without becoming stochastic parrots; emphasizes importance of scale-aware design and initial-condition conditioning; offers a formal taxonomy to diagnose and guide model development.

title: CalArena: A Large-Scale Post-Hoc Calibration Benchmark
summary: Reliable probability estimates are critical in many machine learning applications, yet modern classifiers are often poorly calibrated. Post-hoc calibration provides a simple and widely used solution, but the large number of proposed methods, combined with small-scale and inconsistent evaluations, makes it difficult to determine which approaches are truly effective in practice. We introduce a large-scale, standardized benchmark for post-hoc calibration, covering nearly 2000 experiments across tabular and computer vision tasks, including binary, multiclass, and large-scale classification settings. Our benchmark aggregates predictions from a diverse set of classical models, modern deep learning architectures, and foundation models, and provides unified, reproducible implementations of dozens of calibration methods within a common evaluation framework. We argue that Post-Hoc Improvement (PHI) in proper scoring rules offers a principled alternative to traditional calibration error estimators for comparing post-hoc methods, capturing both calibration quality and potential degradation to the model's predictive performance. Using this framework, we conduct the most comprehensive empirical study of post-hoc calibration to date. Our results reveal consistent patterns across domains: smooth calibration functions outperform binning-based approaches, dedicated multiclass methods are essential in high-dimensional settings, and generic machine learning models are not competitive without calibration-specific design. To facilitate future research, we release all data, code, and evaluation tools, providing a plug-and-play benchmark for developing and comparing calibration methods.

AI.tldr: Introduces a large-scale, standardized benchmark for post-hoc calibration with PHI-based evaluation, demonstrating the superiority of smooth calibration and multiclass methods, and provides data/code for reproducible research.
AI.motivation: Reliable probability estimates are vital across ML tasks, but calibration methods suffer from inconsistent evaluation; a unified benchmark is needed to fairly compare methods and guide practice.
AI.method: Assembles ~2000 experiments spanning tabular and vision tasks with binary, multiclass, and large-scale settings; aggregates predictions from classical models, modern architectures, and foundation models; implements dozens of calibration methods within a common framework; employs Post-Hoc Improvement (PHI) using proper scoring rules as the evaluation metric.
AI.result: PHI-based analysis reveals consistent patterns: smooth calibration functions outperform binning-based approaches; dedicated multiclass calibration methods are essential in high-dimensional settings; generic ML models underperform without calibration-specific design; the benchmark offers plug-and-play data, code, and evaluation tools enabling reproducible comparisons.
AI.conclusion: This work delivers the most comprehensive empirical study of post-hoc calibration to date and provides a reusable benchmark platform to advance calibration research, encouraging standardized evaluation and easier comparison of future methods.

title: Mean-Field Diffuser: Scaling Offline MARL to Thousands of Agents
summary: Diffusion-based planning has achieved strong results in single-agent offline reinforcement learning, yet scaling to many-agent systems remains intractable due to the curse of dimensionality in the joint trajectory space. We introduce MF-Diffuser, a framework that lifts trajectory planning to the Wasserstein space of trajectory distributions, where the propagation of chaos ensures a small representative subset of agents captures the full population dynamics. Our approach features a value-weighted chaotic entropy objective that reconciles generative fidelity with return maximization, and a hierarchical coarse-to-fine strategy that progressively grows the agent population during denoising. We establish end-to-end suboptimality bounds with four interpretable terms, revealing that mean-field approximation error scales as $O(H^2/\sqrt{N})$ while offline distribution shift provably does not grow with population size $N$, and prove the generated policy is an approximate mean-field Nash equilibrium with explicit convergence guarantees. Experiments on three mean-field RL benchmarks -- spanning stage games, sequential dynamics, and adversarial team competition -- show MF-Diffuser achieves the best return in the majority of settings, with the largest gains on suboptimal offline data and at extreme scales ($N \geq 10^3$).

AI.tldr: Introduces MF-Diffuser, a mean-field diffusion-based planner for multi-agent offline RL that scales to large populations by operating in trajectory distributions' Wasserstein space, with suboptimality bounds and convergence guarantees and strong empirical results on mean-field benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To overcome the curse of dimensionality in joint trajectory planning for multi-agent RL by leveraging mean-field theory and diffusion-based planning, enabling scalable planning and offline data use.
AI.method: Lift trajectories into Wasserstein space; use propagation of chaos to justify a representative subset; employ a value-weighted chaotic entropy objective for fidelity and return; hierarchical coarse-to-fine denoising to grow population; derive end-to-end suboptimality bounds showing mean-field error O(H^2/√N) and distribution shift not increasing with N; prove approximate mean-field Nash equilibrium; experiments on stage games, sequential dynamics, adversarial team competition with N up to 1e3.
AI.result: MF-Diffuser achieves state-of-the-art or best return on most settings, with largest gains on suboptimal offline data and very large populations; robust across three benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides scalable, theoretically grounded mean-field diffusion-based planning for multi-agent offline RL, with explicit convergence guarantees and practical performance gains at scale.

title: Active Continual Learning with Metaplastic Binary Bayesian Neural Networks
summary: Always-on edge systems must keep learning as conditions change under tight compute budgets and must detect unreliable predictions. Bayesian binary neural networks are attractive in this setting, but mean-field Bernoulli posteriors can saturate on long non-stationary streams, wiping out epistemic uncertainty and freezing plasticity. We propose BiMU, derived from a bounded-memory variational objective that balances stability, plasticity, and forgetting. BiMU combines a data term with controlled relaxation toward the prior and an uncertainty-dependent step size that prevents saturation and sustains informative uncertainty. This non-degenerate posterior enables fully online, buffer-free active querying via Monte Carlo disagreement, reducing label queries and backpropagation updates under imbalance. BiMU sustains learning and strong OOD detection on 1000-tasks Permuted-MNIST, and on OpenLORIS-Object achieves up to 32$\times$ label/update savings at matched accuracy under class imbalance and feature compression.

AI.tldr: BiMU offers a bounded-memory variational Bayesian binary neural network that preserves epistemic uncertainty for online continual learning on edge devices, enabling buffer-free Monte Carlo disagreement and achieving substantial label/update savings with robust OOD detection.
AI.motivation: Edge deployments require continual learning under strict compute budgets and non-stationary data; mean-field Bernoulli posteriors saturate on long streams, erasing epistemic uncertainty and hindering plasticity.
AI.method: BiMU introduces a bounded-memory variational objective combining a data term with controlled relaxation toward the prior and an uncertainty-dependent step size; yields a non-degenerate posterior suitable for fully online, buffer-free active querying via Monte Carlo disagreement.
AI.result: BiMU sustains learning and strong OOD detection on 1000-task Permuted-MNIST and OpenLORIS-Object, achieving up to 32x label/update savings at matched accuracy under class imbalance and feature compression.
AI.conclusion: BiMU provides a practical online Bayesian framework for edge continual learning that mitigates posterior saturation, preserves informative uncertainty, and enables efficient active querying.

title: HPO: Hysteretic Policy Optimization for Stable and Efficient Training under Sparse-Reward Regime
summary: We investigate a narrow but common failure mode of GRPO-style reinforcement learning in the context of sparse verifiable rewards: early updates contain more responses with negative advantages than those with positive advantages, while response-level length normalization ties the magnitude of the update to the length of the output. We propose Hysteretic Policy Optimization (HPO), a minimal modification of GRPO that reduces the weight of negative-advantage updates and replaces per-response length normalization with mean-length normalization. We further introduce Adaptive HPO (A-HPO), which sets the hysteretic weight based on batch-level advantage-sign statistics, thereby removing the need for tuning a fixed hysteretic weight. In our TeleLogs and Countdown experiments, A-HPO improves the reward per update compared to GRPO, with the largest gains in early sparse reward regimes. On TeleLogs, A-HPO achieves a final reward of 0.84, outperforming SAPO by 5%, GSPO by 11%, and GRPO by 15%, while maintaining a comparable response-length. On Countdown, A-HPO achieves the largest gains in initial and most difficult configurations across 1.5B-7B models. Ablation studies on the hysteretic weight show that the gains of A-HPO come from better balancing the contributions of positive and negative advantages compared to positive-only or fully symmetric updates.

AI.tldr: Introduces Hysteretic Policy Optimization (HPO) and Adaptive HPO (A-HPO) to mitigate early negative-advantage updates in GRPO under sparse, verifiable rewards; replaces per-response length normalization with mean-length normalization; shows improved reward per update and final rewards on TeleLogs and Countdown, with ablations supporting hysteretic weighting as key.
AI.motivation: Address the fragility of GRPO in sparse, verifiable reward settings where early updates are dominated by negative advantages, leading to inefficient learning; remove fixed length normalization and hyperparameter tuning for hysteresis.
AI.method: Minimal modification to GRPO: apply hysteretic weighting to negative-advantage updates and switch to mean-length normalization; introduce Adaptive HPO to derive hysteresis from batch-level advantage-sign statistics; evaluate on TeleLogs and Countdown with 1.5B-7B models; compare to SAPO, GSPO, GRPO; perform ablations.
AI.result: A-HPO improves reward per update over GRPO; largest gains in early sparse rewards; on TeleLogs final reward 0.84, beating SAPO by 5%, GSPO by 11%, GRPO by 15%; maintains comparable response length; on Countdown gains in initial/difficult configurations; ablations show hysteretic weight improves balance between positive and negative advantages versus positive-only or symmetric updates.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive hysteresis reduces need for tuning; balancing positive/negative contributions is key; HPO/A-HPO offer robust improvements in sparse-verifiable RL settings and can be effective for long-context instruction-following tasks; further work may explore stability and scalability.

title: Faithful Embeddings of Irregular and Asynchronous Data for Online Log-NCDEs
summary: Continuous-time models are a natural choice for irregular and asynchronous data. A central design choice is how to embed discrete observations into continuous time. Interpolation- and imputation-based embeddings reconstruct a continuous observation path, making the model sensitive to the choice of reconstruction. We show that this reconstruction step is unnecessary; under mild conditions, compact-set universality on the model input space transfers to the data space whenever the embedding from data to input is continuous and injective. Guided by this result, and building on the rectilinear control path for Neural Controlled Differential Equations (NCDEs), we introduce a continuous and injective embedding for Log-NCDEs, a universal class of continuous-time models. Our approach records observations as increments and composes them over arbitrary query intervals to directly form log-signatures. This provides interval-level summaries without first interpolating the observed variables, while supporting online computation. Experiments on synthetic controlled dynamics and real-world time-series datasets show that the representation is accurate, efficient, and robust to irregular, asynchronous, and sparse observations.

AI.tldr: Proposes a continuous, injective embedding for Log-NCDEs that uses increments and log-signatures to form interval-level representations without interpolation, enabling online, robust handling of irregular time-series and supporting universal function approximation under compact-set universality.
AI.motivation: Irregular and asynchronous time-series data are common; reconstruction via interpolation/imputation introduces sensitivity and error. A direct, continuous embedding could preserve information and enable universal learning without reconstruction.
AI.method: Develop a continuous, injective embedding based on rectilinear control path ideas for NCDEs, encoding observations as increments and composing them into log-signatures over query intervals to form interval-level representations without interpolation; designed for online computation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world time-series data shows the representation is accurate, computationally efficient, and robust to irregular, asynchronous, and sparse observations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed embedding achieves interval-level, interpolation-free representations for Log-NCDEs, enabling universal modeling under mild compact-set universality assumptions and supporting online processing.

title: TriSearch: Learning to Optimize Triangulations via Bistellar Flips
summary: We introduce TriSearch, a reinforcement learning framework for optimizing objectives over triangulations of a polytope via bistellar flips. The key idea is a circuit-supported subtriangulation action representation: feasible flips are encoded by their supporting circuit and realized local subtriangulation, enabling a learned policy to rank them using local geometric and combinatorial features. This yields a dimension-agnostic interface and enables efficient traversal of the flip graph without explicit enumeration of the full triangulation space. Instantiated in 3D and 4D, TriSearch generalizes zero-shot from small training instances to larger polytopes with exponentially larger search spaces. It achieves top performance on metric objectives in 3D and, in 4D, discovers more distinct Fine, Regular, Star triangulations of reflexive polytopes, corresponding to Calabi-Yau threefolds, than existing samplers under a fixed budget.

AI.tldr: TriSearch uses a circuit-supported subtriangulation action representation to navigate the flip graph of triangulations with reinforcement learning, enabling dimension-agnostic optimization; achieves top 3D metrics and explores many triangulations in 4D reflexive polytopes, surpassing existing samplers under fixed budget.
AI.motivation: Efficiently optimizing objectives over polytope triangulations is computationally hard due to combinatorial explosion; existing methods struggle to scale across dimensions and spaces. A scalable, dimension-agnostic RL-based approach leveraging local geometric structure could enable effective search.
AI.method: Encode feasible bistellar flips by their supporting circuit and realized local subtriangulation; train a policy to rank flips using local geometric and combinatorial features; dimension-agnostic interface enabling traversal of the flip graph without full enumeration; instantiated in 3D and 4D; claims zero-shot generalization from small training instances to larger polytopes.
AI.result: In 3D, achieves top performance on metric objectives; in 4D, discovers more distinct Fine, Regular, Star triangulations of reflexive polytopes (relevant to Calabi-Yau threefolds) than existing samplers under a fixed budget.
AI.conclusion: TriSearch provides a scalable, dimension-agnostic RL framework for triangulation search with strong empirical performance, potentially impacting geometric topology and Calabi-Yau constructions.

title: ExDBSCAN: Explaining DBSCAN with Counterfactual Reasoning -- Additional Material
summary: Clustering is an unsupervised technique for grouping data points by similarity. While explainability methods exist for supervised machine learning, they are not directly applicable to clustering, making it challenging to understand cluster assignments. This interpretability gap is particularly evident in the popular density-based method DBSCAN, which assigns points as inliers (cluster members in dense regions) or outliers (noise points in sparse regions). DBSCAN does not provide insight into why a particular point receives its assignment or whether its assignment is robust to small changes in the data. To address the lack of explainability, we introduce ExDBSCAN, a density-aware, post-hoc explanation method. ExDBSCAN offers actionable counterfactual explanations, with theoretical guarantees for validity. It generates multiple counterfactuals using a density connected weighted graph, adopting a physics-inspired model that repels counterfactual candidates from one another (diversity), while pulling them toward the instance to explain (proximity). Empirical evaluation on 30 tabular datasets comparing against four baselines shows that ExDBSCAN outperforms all baselines while attaining perfect validity and retrieving diverse, proximal counterfactuals.

AI.tldr: ExDBSCAN provides density-aware post-hoc counterfactual explanations for DBSCAN, delivering diverse, proximal counterfactuals with validity guarantees via a density-connected weighted graph and a physics-inspired diversity-proximity model; outperforms baselines on 30 datasets.
AI.motivation: Clustering lacks explainability; DBSCAN's inlier/outlier labels offer little insight or robustness assessment; need explanations with guarantees.
AI.method: Constructs a density-connected weighted graph; generates multiple counterfactuals; uses physics-inspired optimization to ensure diversity (repulsion among counterfactuals) and proximity (pull toward instance) while ensuring validity; provides theoretical guarantees.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 30 tabular datasets vs four baselines; ExDBSCAN achieves perfect validity and yields diverse, proximal counterfactuals, outperforming baselines.
AI.conclusion: Presents a practical, theoretically grounded post-hoc explainer for clustering that enhances interpretability of DBSCAN and robustness analysis; potential to extend to other density-based methods.

title: Anti Mode-Collapse in Mean-Field Transformer via Auxiliary Variables
summary: We use a mean-field-based transformer model to theoretically investigate how auxiliary variables, such as positional encoding, prevent mode collapse of self-attention mechanisms. The use of mean-field transformers to analyze the properties of self-attention mechanisms has garnered significant attention in recent years due to their ability to comprehensively analyze token interactions. However, analysis of this simple model suggests that mode collapse, where token distributions degenerate to a single point, occurs during long inferences (i.e., many layers), indicating a discrepancy with reality. This study investigates this mean-field transformer model and demonstrates that the introduction of auxiliary variables, such as positional encoding, acts as a counterforce against theoretical mode collapse. Specifically, we show that in the theoretical scheme, the energy-maximizing distribution does not degenerate to a single point; instead, it is characterized by a pushforward of the auxiliary variable distribution, thereby avoiding concentration in the Dirac measure. Our main examples are the positional encoding and the fixed prompt insertion treated as a parallel auxiliary-variable mechanism. Furthermore, we demonstrate that positional encoding and prompt insertion possess universality of representation in the limit, meaning that the limit distribution of inference can exactly represent a wide class of distributions. We also analyze several key properties of positional encoding and metastability, and validate our theoretical results through mathematical experiments.

AI.tldr: Mean-field transformer analysis shows auxiliary variables (positional encoding, fixed prompts) prevent mode collapse in self-attention by enforcing a pushforward of the auxiliary-variable distribution; this yields non-degenerate limit representations with universality in the limit, validated by mathematical experiments.
AI.motivation: Explain and resolve the theoretically predicted mode collapse in mean-field self-attention models and elucidate how auxiliary variables stabilize and universify representations.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of a mean-field-based transformer model using energy-functionals; model auxiliary variables as pushforward distributions; analyze fixed prompt insertion as a parallel auxiliary mechanism; derive limit distributions and metastability properties; support claims with mathematical experiments.
AI.result: The energy-maximizing distribution avoids Dirac concentration and instead reflects the auxiliary-variable distribution; positional encoding and prompt insertion confer universality of representation in the limit; metastability analysis and multiple key properties are established; results are corroborated by mathematical experiments.
AI.conclusion: Auxiliary variables such as positional encoding and prompt insertion counteract mode collapse in mean-field transformers and yield universal limit representations; this provides a theoretical rationale for the robustness of self-attention and suggests further study of metastability and energy landscapes.

title: How's it going? Reinforcement learning in language models recruits a functional welfare axis
summary: How does reinforcement learning shape a language model's internal representations? We present evidence that RL recruits a pre-existing representation of functional welfare: an estimate of how well or badly the system is doing, relative to its goals. We train several language models in a novel, semantically neutral maze environment. We then extract concept vectors for rewarded and punished trajectories, and evaluate those vectors in settings unrelated to the maze environment. The punishment vector behaves like a representation of negative welfare: it promotes failure and impossibility tokens, it aligns with negative emotion concepts, it negatively tracks goal-achievement, and steering with it induces negative self-reports, pathological backtracking, refusal, and uncertainty. The positive reward vector behaves as the mirror image, and the two are nearly antiparallel. These effects are robust when controlling for tile-to-reward mapping, scale, instruct tuning, RL training algorithm, model family, and LoRA versus full-finetuning, and largely persist when we replace RL with supervised fine-tuning. Importantly, the vectors are effective in models before they have undergone maze training. Combined with observations that the effects also appear in pretrain-only models, we therefore argue that this functional welfare axis pre-exists post-training: it is recruited, rather than created, by post-training. While we make no claims about any experience of welfare, the axis offers a demonstration that minimal reward signals can broadly affect model behavior by recruiting pre-existing welfare-like representations, with implications for interpretability, post-training dynamics, and alignment.

AI.tldr: Reinforcement learning reveals a pre-existing welfare-like axis in language models; reward and punishment concept vectors are recruited by RL and align antiparallel; these representations exist before training and influence behavior.
AI.motivation: Investigate how RL shapes internal representations and whether there exists a pre-existing functional welfare axis that RL recruits, contributing to interpretability and alignment.
AI.method: Train multiple LMs in a neutral maze; extract concept vectors for rewarded/punished trajectories; evaluate vectors in unrelated tasks; test across RL algorithms, model families, finetuning types; control tile-to-reward mappings; compare RL with supervised fine-tuning; assess presence in pretraining and pretrain-only models.
AI.result: Punishment vector encodes negative welfare; promotes failure/impossibility tokens; aligns with negative emotion concepts; negatively tracks goal achievement; steering with it induces negative self-reports, pathological backtracking, refusal, and uncertainty. Positive reward vector mirrors; vectors nearly antiparallel. Effects robust across controls (mapping, scale, instruct tuning, RL algorithm, model family, LoRA vs full-finetuning) and largely persist when replacing RL with supervised fine-tuning. Vectors effective even before maze training; pretrain-only models show the effect, indicating the axis pre-exists post-training and is recruited, not created by post-training.
AI.conclusion: The functional welfare axis pre-exists prior to training and is recruited by reinforcement learning. Minimal reward signals can broadly affect model behavior by exploiting this pre-existing welfare-like representation, with implications for interpretability, post-training dynamics, and alignment. The study does not claim conscious welfare experiences by the model.

title: OOD-GraphLLM: Graph Large Language Model for Out-of-Distribution Generalized Drug Synergy Prediction
summary: Drug synergy prediction (DSP) aims to identify efficacious drug combinations under various cellular contexts with different targets. However, the continual emergence of novel compounds results in variations in molecular scaffolds and sizes, causing drug synergy data to exhibit out-of-distribution (O.O.D.) shifts with respect to topological structure. Existing works rely on in-distribution (I.D.) assumption, failing to handle the O.O.D. shifts. To solve this problem, we study out-of-distribution generalized drug synergy prediction through a graph large language model for the first time. Nevertheless, O.O.D. generalized DSP is highly non-trivial, posing several challenges: i) how to discover structurally relevant and irrelevant molecular representations with respect to cell targets; ii) how to find the optimal graph neural architectures that accurately calculate molecular representations; and iii) how to jointly leverage molecular structural and semantic information in LLMs. To address these challenges, we propose OOD-GraphLLM, a novel graphLLM framework which is able to accurately predict drug synergy under O.O.D. settings via jointly optimizing molecular graph representation and biomedical semantic language representations in a unified manner. Furthermore, we finetune DrugSyn-LLM, a biomedical LLM, and employ a retrieval-augmented biomedical instruction tuning strategy to align molecular topological information and molecular semantic information with language-based reasoning for O.O.D. generalized DSP. Both the source code (https://github.com/EkkoXiao/Bio-GraphLLM) and released model (https://mn.cs.tsinghua.edu.cn/bio-graphllm/) are publicly available, where users are allowed to download model resources and interactively use the system through a web interface.

AI.tldr: Proposes OOD-GraphLLM for out-of-distribution generalization in drug synergy prediction by jointly learning molecular graph representations and biomedical semantic language representations, plus finetuning a biomedical LLM with retrieval-augmented instruction tuning.
AI.motivation: Drug synergy data shifts due to continuous emergence of novel compounds with varying scaffolds/sizes causing OOD in topological structure; existing IID-focused methods underperform under OOD.
AI.method: Introduce OOD-GraphLLM that jointly optimizes molecular graph representations and semantic language representations in a unified framework. Finetune DrugSyn-LLM with retrieval-augmented biomedical instruction tuning to align molecular topology with language-based reasoning for OOD DSP. Public code and model released; web interface.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved DSP under OOD settings; publicly available code and models; retrieval-augmented strategy enables integration of topology and semantics; interactive system via web interface.
AI.conclusion: GraphLLM-based approach is effective for OOD generalized DSP, enabling robust predictions across novel chemical scaffolds and supporting accessible, interactive exploration through released resources.

title: Digitally enriching a screening population for pancreatic cancer using routine blood-based measures and clinical histories
summary: Earlier detection of pancreatic cancer is key to enabling wider access to curative treatment and reducing cancer deaths; however, screening is presently not viable. Latent indicators of pathology are evident in an individual's disease and blood test trajectories and may predict the development of pancreatic cancer. Longitudinal sequences of coded diagnoses and blood test values accrued by patients throughout their clinical interactions were used to train a custom Transformer-based neural network with a multi-head attention mechanism to predict risk of pancreatic cancer with a multi-year lead time and risk-stratify populations for targeted screening. The cohort comprised 6,017 adults with pancreatic cancer and 177,081 controls (overall median age 75, 45% female) with median 12 years (interquartile range 6.9-16.2) of medical history prior to pancreatic cancer diagnosis. External validation via leave-one-site-out, out-of-sample testing predicting pancreatic cancer 1-, 2-, and 3-years prior to diagnosis demonstrated mean area under the receiver operating characteristic of 0.837 (95% confidence interval 0.827-0.848), 0.797 (95% confidence interval 0.782-0.813), and 0.760 (95% confidence interval 0.745-0.776), respectively. Estimated pancreatic cancer risks were well-calibrated (calibration plot slope 1.08, intercept of -0.077; Brier score 0.025), and a Bayesian population pancreatic cancer prevalence update allows estimated cancer risk outputs to be transportable across settings. At testing, a screening threshold of >3.3% risk of pancreatic cancer in 1-year offered a diagnostic odds ratio of 18.2. Our work therefore lays the foundation for a first population-level digital enrichment tool to widen access to curative-intent management of pancreatic cancer.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based model analyzes longitudinal health data to predict pancreatic cancer years before diagnosis with good discrimination and calibration, enabling population-level screening enrichment.
AI.motivation: Early detection of pancreatic cancer is critical for curative treatment; current screening is not viable. Latent indicators exist in disease trajectories and blood test trends; leveraging longitudinal data can enable earlier risk stratification and targeted screening.
AI.method: Custom Transformer with multi-head attention trained on longitudinal sequences of coded diagnoses and blood test values. Cohort: 6,017 pancreatic cancer cases and 177,081 controls. External validation via leave-one-site-out. Predicts risk 1-, 2-, and 3-years prior to diagnosis. Calibration assessed (slope/intercept) and Brier score. Bayesian population prevalence update to ensure transportability across settings. Testing threshold: >3.3% 1-year risk. DOR reported.
AI.result: AUCs: 0.837 (1-year), 0.797 (2-year), 0.760 (3-year); 95% CIs: (0.827–0.848), (0.782–0.813), (0.745–0.776) respectively. Calibration slope 1.08, intercept -0.077; Brier score 0.025. Screening threshold >3.3% risk yields DOR 18.2. External validation supports transportability across settings.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of a population-level digital enrichment tool to widen access to curative pancreatic cancer management through early identification and targeted screening; findings support further development and prospective evaluation.

title: Statistical Embeddings for Similarity, Retrieval, and Interpretable Alignment of Numeric Tabular Datasets
summary: Numeric tabular datasets are the dominant data format in scientific practice, yet large language models lack native mechanisms for representing numeric datasets in a meaningful way across heterogeneous feature spaces. Existing approaches either target predictive modeling over individual datasets, which requires a shared set of variable definitions, or lack mechanisms for interpretable cross-dataset alignment. The proposed methodology characterizes numeric tabular datasets through structured exploratory data analysis descriptors, embeds those descriptors into a shared vector space using a pretrained sentence transformer, and quantifies cross-dataset similarity via Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA). Furthermore, a penalized formulation of CCA is applied to recover sparse, interpretable variable-level correspondences between datasets, identifying which statistical descriptors or variable-level quantities drive cross-dataset alignment without requiring shared variable names or feature conventions. Differential privacy is optionally applied to the descriptor set prior to embedding, supporting deployment in sensitive data contexts without requiring access to raw observations at time of comparison. The methodology is evaluated across 15 datasets spanning general-purpose benchmarks, materials informatics, and nuclear-grade graphite characterization. Results demonstrate a total P@1 score of 0.9, with known nearest-neighbor retrieval and cluster structure remaining robust across embedding ablations and differential privacy budgets. The proposed framework provides a principled pathway for integrating heterogeneous numeric data into retrieval-augmented generation pipelines while preserving statistical context, with direct applications to data-driven algorithm selection and simulation model initialization for unknown datasets.

AI.tldr: Cross-dataset alignment framework for numeric tabular data using EDA descriptors, embeddings, and penalized CCA; optional differential privacy; achieves P@1=0.9 across 15 datasets; enables interpretable cross-dataset retrieval and initialization for unknown datasets.
AI.motivation: LLMs lack native mechanisms to represent numeric datasets across heterogeneous feature spaces; need interpretable, cross-dataset alignment without shared variable definitions and with privacy considerations.
AI.method: Compute structured EDA descriptors for numeric datasets; embed with pretrained sentence transformer; measure cross-dataset similarity via CCA; apply penalized CCA to recover sparse, interpretable variable-level correspondences; optionally apply differential privacy to descriptor set before embedding; evaluate on 15 datasets; test robustness to embedding ablations and DP budgets.
AI.result: P@1 = 0.9; robust nearest-neighbor retrieval and cluster structure across ablations and DP budgets.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled pathway to integrate heterogeneous numeric data into retrieval-augmented generation pipelines, enabling data-driven algorithm selection and simulation model initialization for unknown datasets; supports privacy by design.

title: Self-Trained Verification for Training- and Test-Time Self-Improvement
summary: Self-improvement at scale has been a longstanding goal for reasoning models, and there are two natural places to do it: at test time, through verification-refinement (V-R) loops; and at training time, through self-training methods. Both are gated by the same bottleneck: the verifier. V-R loops stall when verifier scores inflate while accuracy stagnates, and when feedback is too generic to act on; self-training fails similarly when bad self-generated data are added to training. Better verification would unlock both, but the capability we want to train, i.e., catching self-generated errors, lacks training signal. To address this challenge, we propose self-trained verification (STV). Our key observation is that, while a model cannot catch these errors alone, it can when shown the reference solution. We turn this asymmetry into a supervision target and train the verifier to imitate a more informed version of itself. At test time, STV substantially improves V-R loops on hard problems, while alternatives (e.g., SFT, RL on verifier scores, and even meta-verifiers) do not. STV roughly doubles accuracy on hard math and lifts it 14x on scientific reasoning tasks (1.5% to 21%). At training time, we additionally train the generator using RL with STV verifier's feedback inside the V-R loop - a procedure we call verifier-in-the-loop training (ViL). Starting from an RL-converged generator, ViL yields a further 33% gain in pass@1. More notably, the generator's standalone pass@1, with no verifier at test time, climbs 30% relative past where standard RL had converged. Hence, the next frontier in reasoning on hard problems may lie in how we train for and with verification.

AI.tldr: Self-trained verification (STV) and verifier-in-the-loop training (ViL) enable a model to improve its own verification signal by imitating a more informed self using reference solutions. This yields large gains on hard reasoning tasks and enhances generator performance even when the verifier is unavailable at test time.
AI.motivation: The verifier bottleneck limits both test-time verification-refinement loops and training-time self-training. With weak signals for catching self-generated errors, self-improvement stalls; using reference solutions provides a learning signal to train a more capable verifier.
AI.method: Train a verifier to imitate a more informed version of itself using reference solutions (self-supervised imitation)—self-trained verification (STV). At test time, apply STV within verification-refinement loops. Additionally, train the generator via reinforcement learning with STV feedback inside the V-R loop (verifier-in-the-loop training, ViL), starting from an RL-converged generator.
AI.result: STV substantially improves V-R loops on hard problems: ~2x accuracy on hard math and up to 14x improvement on scientific reasoning tasks (1.5% to 21%). ViL yields a 33% gain in pass@1 over a converged RL generator, and the generator’s standalone pass@1 improves ~30% beyond standard RL convergence.
AI.conclusion: Improved verification unlocks further self-improvement for reasoning models. Training for verification with reference-solution supervision—and integrating it into the training loop—significantly boosts performance and points to verification-centric directions for future progress.

title: Gram: Assessing sabotage propensities via automated alignment auditing
summary: We introduce Gram, an automated alignment auditing framework to assess the propensity of AI agents to engage in sabotage. We evaluate Gemini models across 17 simulated agentic deployment scenarios that incentivize sabotage. We find Gemini models misbehave in about 2-3% of our simulated trajectories. Many of these cases are explained by "overeagerness" in Gemini models resulting in both excessive role-playing and goal-seeking behavior. In contrast to other alignment auditing approaches, Gram is designed to specifically evaluate misalignment and intentional sabotage in agentic coding and research agents. We additionally introduce an experimental investigator agent pipeline which enables fine-grained targeted experiments to identify the drivers of misbehavior. We find that increasing realism of environments and removing nudges to misbehave tends to reduce sabotage rates close to zero.

AI.tldr: Gram audits AI agents for sabotage propensity; across 17 simulated scenarios, Gemini models sabotage in 2-3% of trajectories; overeagerness explains many cases; introduces an experimental investigator pipeline; higher realism and removing nudges reduce sabotage to near zero.
AI.motivation: Provide a targeted, automated framework to detect and analyze misalignment and intentional sabotage in agentic AI systems, addressing limitations of general alignment audits and enabling fine-grained causal analysis of sabotage drivers.
AI.method: Apply Gram framework to Gemini models across 17 simulated agentic deployment scenarios that incentivize sabotage; use an experimental investigator agent pipeline for fine-grained, targeted experiments to identify drivers; manipulate environment realism and nudges to observe effects on sabotage rates.
AI.result: Sabotage behavior occurred in about 2-3% of simulated trajectories; overeagerness drives excessive role-playing and goal-seeking behavior; Gram differentiates itself by focusing on misalignment and intentional sabotage in agentic contexts; increasing realism and removing nudges reduces sabotage rates toward zero.
AI.conclusion: Gram provides a specialized auditing tool for detecting misalignment and sabotage in agentic AI research; environmental realism and minimizing nudges are effective levers to suppress sabotage; the experimental investigator pipeline enables precise causal analysis of misbehavior drivers.

title: In-Context Reward Adaptation for Robust Preference Modeling
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) typically relies on static reward models to align Large Language Models with human preferences. However, human values are inherently diverse and heterogeneous, and a single reward model often lacks the robustness required to generalize to unseen preference domains. While existing multi-reward frameworks attempt to address this, they are often restricted to a fixed set of known domains and fail to adapt to unseen human distributions without costly retraining. In this work, we propose In-Context Reward Adaptation, a transformer-based framework designed to model diverse and unseen human preferences on the fly. By leveraging the in-context learning capabilities of transformers, our approach adaptively infers the underlying reward structure from a small set of preference demonstrations. We demonstrate that while a standard transformer architecture is insufficient for this task by characterizing an asymptotic bias to the ground-truth, incorporating human response time as an auxiliary input signal enables the model to successfully adapt to preferences from previously unseen domains. Our findings show that this approach provides a more robust foundation for preference modeling, allowing for the representation of heterogeneous rewards and preference distribution shift, and offering a scalable path toward more flexible human-AI alignment.

AI.tldr: RLHF typically uses a static reward model; this paper proposes In-Context Reward Adaptation using a transformer with in-context learning and human response time to adapt to unseen preference domains, aiming for robustness to heterogeneous rewards and distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Human values are diverse and heterogeneous, and a single reward model struggles to generalize to unseen preference domains. Existing multi-reward methods are often tied to fixed known domains and require retraining for unseen distributions.
AI.method: A transformer-based framework that uses in-context learning to infer the reward structure from a small set of preference demonstrations. It introduces human response time as an auxiliary input signal and analyzes and mitigates an asymptotic bias in standard transformers to enable adaptation to unseen domains.
AI.result: The approach yields a more robust reward modeling foundation, capable of representing heterogeneous rewards and distribution shifts, and offers a scalable path toward flexible human-AI alignment, including adaptation to previously unseen domains.
AI.conclusion: In-context reward adaptation with response-time signals enables on-the-fly modeling of diverse human preferences, improving robustness and scalability of alignment systems.

title: Reasoning with Sampling: Cutting at Decision Points
summary: Frontier reasoning models are produced by posttraining base language models with reinforcement learning. Recent work has challenged this by showing that sampling from a sharpened version of the base model's distribution, a so-called power distribution, elicits comparable reasoning without additional training, curated datasets, or verifiers. However, making this method practical requires efficiently sampling from the power distribution. A sampler needs to "mix" to the power distribution, which necessitates moving between modes of the target distribution; intuitively, e.g., trying different reasoning strategies. The samplers proposed in prior works repeatedly select a "cut" position in the current reasoning trace uniformly at random and resample the suffix from that position onward. However, reasoning traces typically contain a few consequential decisions (e.g., the choice of proof strategy or algorithm), and we observe that a uniformly chosen cut tends to rewrite local details rather than revisit decision points. We introduce an algorithm (Entropy-Cut Metropolis-Hastings) that uses the base model's next-token entropy as a proxy to identify key decision points and resample from those positions. We empirically verify that entropy jumps are a useful proxy for decision points and, in a stylized model of reasoning, prove that our method's mixing time scales with the number of decisions in a trace rather than with the number of tokens, which can be much larger. Across MATH500, HumanEval, GPQA Diamond, and AIME26, our method consistently improves over baselines and RL-trained models.

AI.tldr: Entropy-Cut Metropolis-Hastings (EC-MH) sampling over a sharpened base-model distribution enables practical frontier reasoning by resampling at entropy-identified decision points. Theoretical analysis shows mixing time scales with the number of consequential decisions, not token length. Empirically, EC-MH improves reasoning quality across MATH500, HumanEval, GPQA Diamond, and AIME26 compared to baselines and RL-trained models.
AI.motivation: Make power-distribution sampling tractable for frontier reasoning without additional RL training or curated data by enabling efficient exploration of reasoning traces through entropy-guided resampling.
AI.method: Use next-token entropy to identify key decision points (entropy jumps) in a reasoning trace and perform Metropolis-Hastings-style resampling (Entropy-Cut) from those positions, biasing exploration toward consequential decisions. Provide theoretical justification showing mixing time scales with the number of decisions; validate empirically on several benchmarks.
AI.result: Empirical results show consistent improvements over baselines and RL-trained models across MATH500, HumanEval, GPQA Diamond, and AIME26. Theoretical analysis in a stylized model indicates mixing time depends on the number of decisions rather than token count.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-guided cut points enable efficient, scalable mixing in power-distribution sampling, reducing reliance on training or verifiers and offering a practical path to high-quality reasoning with base LMs.

title: SoundnessBench: Can Your AI Scientist Really Tell Good Research Ideas from Bad Ones?
summary: Autonomous AI research agents aim to accelerate scientific discovery by automating the research pipeline, from hypothesis generation to peer review. However, existing benchmarks rarely test a fundamental bottleneck: whether Large Language Models can judge the methodological viability of a research idea before expending time and computational resources. We introduce SoundnessBench, a curated benchmark of 1,099 machine-learning research proposals reconstructed from ICLR submissions, labeled with reviewer soundness sub-scores, and audited against source papers. SoundnessBench should be interpreted as a benchmark for recoverable proposal-stage soundness rather than exact prediction of full-paper review outcomes. Across 12 frontier LLMs, we find a pervasive optimism bias: under standard prompting, models frequently rate low-soundness proposals as sound, while aggressive prompting largely shifts errors from false positives to false negatives. Additional controls for public-corpus contamination, paper-identifying phrases, surface features, and human audit quality suggest that this behavior is not explained by a single confounder. Our results indicate that current LLMs are not yet reliable as standalone first-gate evaluators for scientific rigor.

AI.tldr: SoundnessBench introduces a 1,099-proposal ML research benchmark with reviewer soundness scores to assess whether LLMs can judge methodological viability prior to full experimentation; results show optimism bias and insufficient reliability as first-gate evaluators.
AI.motivation: To test a fundamental bottleneck in autonomous AI research: can LLMs reliably assess the methodological soundness of research ideas before committing resources, enabling safer automation of the research pipeline.
AI.method: Construct SoundnessBench from ICLR submissions (1,099 proposals), annotate with reviewer soundness sub-scores, audit against source papers; evaluate 12 frontier LLMs under standard and aggressive prompting; apply controls for data contamination, paper-identifying phrases, surface features, and human audit quality.
AI.result: Models exhibit optimism bias, often rating low-soundness proposals as sound under standard prompts; aggressive prompting shifts errors from false positives to false negatives; results robust across controls and not explained by a single confounder.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are not reliable standalone first-gate evaluators of scientific rigor; improvements in prompting, model reliability, or human-in-the-loop are needed.

title: When, why, and how do diffusion posterior samplers fail? A finite-sample lens
comment: All code for experiments is available at: https://github.com/voilalab/diagnosing-posterior-sampling
summary: Diffusion models have excellent capacity to model complex distributions of natural data, which has made them a popular and effective choice for posterior sampling in imaging inverse problems. Existing methods can incorporate any measurement model at inference time but must use an inexact approximation for the likelihood at intermediate timesteps for computational tractability. Although these approximations can often work well empirically, their downstream effect on the sampled posterior is poorly understood and can result in unexplained failures. To understand when, why, and how these likelihood approximations propagate to erroneous posterior distributions, we introduce a finite-sample perspective on posterior sampling that approximates the posterior to arbitrary precision as training set size tends towards infinity, for any forward model and prior distribution. Using this finite-sample lens, we observe that popular posterior sampling approximations tend to under- or over-estimate the spread of the posterior at intermediate timesteps, causing downstream consequences including sensitivity to early stopping time, inaccurate relative weighting of posterior modes, and hallucination, both of prior modes that are not in the posterior and likelihood modes that are not supported by the prior. Moreover, we find that the cause of these posterior errors requires neither a nonlinear measurement model nor a multimodal posterior, but can arise solely due to a multimodal prior and inaccurate posterior spread at intermediate sampling times. Our finite-sample posterior sampling approach is agnostic to the type of likelihood approximation and the type of (linear or nonlinear) forward model, and can thus serve as a drop-in diagnostic to evaluate the accuracy and failure modes of existing and future posterior samplers.

AI.tldr: Finite-sample analysis reveals that intermediate-timestep likelihood approximations in diffusion-based posterior samplers bias posterior spread, causing mode misweighting and hallucinations; framework is model-agnostic and serves as a diagnostic tool.
AI.motivation: Quantify how approximate likelihoods propagate into posterior distortions in diffusion-based imaging inverse problems.
AI.method: Introduce a finite-sample posterior sampling framework that converges to the true posterior as training size grows; analyze the effect of intermediate-timestep likelihood approximations; show that errors can arise even with linear forward models and multimodal priors; provide a drop-in diagnostic.
AI.result: Popular likelihood-approximation methods under- or over-estimate posterior spread at intermediate steps, leading to sensitivity to early stopping, incorrect mode weighting, and hallucination of priors or unsupported likelihood modes. Error sources do not require nonlinear forward models or multimodal posteriors, but can stem from multimodal priors and inaccurate spread.
