The framework is agnostic to likelihood approximations and forward models and can diagnose current/future samplers.
AI.conclusion: The finite-sample posterior sampling approach furnishes a robust diagnostic for evaluating and understanding posterior-sampling methods, highlighting failure modes and guiding improvements across diffusion-based samplers.

title: Fairness-Aware Federated Learning with Trajectory Shapley Value
comment: Accepted for publication at the 24th European Control Conference (ECC 2026)
summary: Federated learning is an emerging distributed paradigm that addresses the challenges posed by heterogeneous, privacy-sensitive data. It enables multiple clients to train a model collaboratively by aggregating their local updates at a server. However, conventional aggregation schemes typically use fixed weights that fail to reflect unequal and time-varying client contributions, leading to biased and unstable learning. To improve fairness and stability, we propose the Trajectory Shapley Value (TSV), a contribution metric that evaluates how each client influences the optimization trajectory of the global model using a validation-based, temporally consistent utility. Building on TSV, we design FedTSV, an adaptive aggregation method that converts per-round evaluations into dynamic client weights, allowing the server to respond to heterogeneous and adversarial participation in real time. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that FedTSV accelerates convergence, improves robustness, and yields more equitable contribution assessments, thereby providing a principled foundation for fairness-aware federated optimization.

AI.tldr: Trajectory Shapley Value (TSV) measures each client's contribution to the optimization trajectory using a validation-based, temporally consistent utility; FedTSV uses TSV to adapt per-round weights for robust, fair federated aggregation, improving convergence and resilience to heterogeneous/adversarial participation.
AI.motivation: Fixed, static aggregation weights fail to capture unequal and time-varying client contributions, leading to biased updates and unstable learning in federated settings with heterogeneity and potential adversaries.
AI.method: Define Trajectory Shapley Value (TSV) as a Shapley-like contribution metric over the optimization trajectory under a validation utility; compute per-round TSV and convert to dynamic client weights; implement FedTSV that adjusts server aggregation weights in real time based on TSV, enabling adaptive, fairness-aware aggregation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on benchmark datasets shows FedTSV accelerates convergence, enhances robustness to heterogeneous/adversarial participation, and provides more equitable contribution assessments.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a principled framework for fairness-aware federated optimization by tying client contributions to their impact on the optimization trajectory and translating this into dynamic aggregation weights.

title: Efficient Test-Time Finetuning of LLMs via Convex Reconstruction and Gradient Caching
summary: Test-time finetuning (TTFT) is a rapidly evolving paradigm that adapts a language model to each prompt by retrieving related sequences, updating the model on them, and then evaluating the prompt. However, TTFT is only practical if it is fast: selection and finetuning both happen per query, making each a direct bottleneck. Existing methods trade speed for quality: fast retrieval is often redundant, while stronger diversity-aware selection adds prohibitive per-query cost. We introduce HullFT, a geometric approach to TTFT that addresses both bottlenecks. Given a query, HullFT first represents the query embedding as a sparse convex combination of few training sequences, using efficient projection-free Frank-Wolfe optimization. This yields a support set that is inherently relevant and diverse. We then convert the fractional convex weights into an exact integer multiset for finetuning through a geometric integerization procedure. The resulting multiplicities naturally create repeated examples, which we exploit with Gradient Reuse to amortize forward-backward computation across repeated finetuning steps. Our experiments show that HullFT improves the quality-efficiency tradeoff over current state-of-the-art TTFT methods, achieving lower bits-per-byte at substantially lower total runtime.

AI.tldr: HullFT is a fast test-time finetuning approach leveraging a Frank-Wolfe-based sparse representation to select a diverse, relevant subset of training sequences, followed by integerization and Gradient Reuse to amortize computation, yielding superior speed-quality tradeoffs over prior TTFT methods.
AI.motivation: TTFT bottlenecks arise from per-query retrieval and finetuning; existing methods trade speed for quality (fast retrieval is often redundant; stronger diversity incurs high per-query cost). A method that achieves both fast and diverse, high-quality selections is needed.
AI.method: Represents the query embedding as a sparse convex combination of a small number of training sequences using projection-free Frank-Wolfe optimization to obtain a diverse, relevant support set. Converts fractional convex weights to an exact integer multiset via a geometric integerization procedure for finetuning. Reuses gradients and forward-backward computations across repeated finetuning steps (Gradient Reuse) to amortize costs.
AI.result: Experiments show HullFT improves the quality-efficiency tradeoff compared to state-of-the-art TTFT methods, achieving lower bits-per-byte at substantially lower total runtime.
AI.conclusion: HullFT demonstrates that a geometry-guided, sparse, and gradient-reuse-enabled TTFT pipeline can deliver faster per-query finetuning with competitive or improved performance.

title: Evolving Features vs Evolving Entire Trees with GP for Interpretable Survival Analysis
summary:
Survival analysis concerns the task of predicting the time until an event occurs. Often used in the medical field, survival analysis deals with incomplete (i.e., censored) data, for instance, from patients who did not experience the event during the duration of the study. For practical use, both accuracy and interpretability are important.
  Survival trees are easy-to-follow survival models that split the patient cohort recursively into discrete patient groups. Whilst survival trees can capture complex relationships, they typically need to grow large, threatening interpretability. Moreover, survival trees are often built using greedy approaches that may overlook globally optimal split combinations, limiting predictive performance.
  Shallow survival trees require expressive, higher-order feature combinations to achieve competitive accuracy. We therefore use genetic programming to multi-objectively evolve inherently inspectable feature sets and study how they interact with different tree induction strategies. We further introduce an evolutionary approach that jointly optimises the survival tree structure and the non-linear split logic.
  Our findings demonstrate that evolutionary feature construction improves predictive performance across different tree induction strategies on two real-world datasets and two different survival tree depths. Full joint evolution has the overall highest potential to propose multiple inherently inspectable shallow survival trees of good performance.

AI.tldr: Genetic programming is used to evolve feature constructions and jointly optimize survival tree structure and non-linear split rules, improving accuracy while preserving interpretability in survival analysis with censored data.
AI.motivation: Survival analysis requires accurate and interpretable models. Traditional survival trees are greedy and often large, hindering interpretability; shallow trees need expressive higher-order features. An evolutionary approach could yield both performance gains and inherently inspectable models.
AI.method: Apply multi-objective genetic programming to simultaneously evolve non-linear feature combinations and survival-tree induction strategies. Perform full joint evolution of feature construction and tree structure with policies to balance accuracy and interpretability. Evaluate on two real-world survival datasets across two tree depths.
AI.result: Evolutionary feature construction improves predictive performance across tree induction strategies. Full joint evolution yields the highest potential, enabling multiple inherently interpretable shallow survival trees with competitive performance.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary methods are promising for survival trees, delivering improved accuracy while maintaining interpretability, especially when jointly evolving features and tree structure.
